topics.title: Determiners and Adjectives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Determiners and Adjectives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Determiner (DT):** A closed-class function word that occupies the **Determiner Phrase (DP)** position in a Noun Phrase and serves to **specify, quantify, or restrict the referential domain** of the head noun. In the Penn Treebank (PTB) tagset, determiners carry the coarse tag **DT**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Adjective (JJ / JJR / JJS):** A lexical (open-class) category that **modifies a noun** by attributing properties, qualities, or attributes. In PTB, adjectives are split into three tags: **JJ** (base/attributive/predicative), **JJR** (comparative), and **JJS** (superlative).

In **Computational Linguistics**, these two categories are foundational because they are the primary carriers of **morpho-syntactic features** (gender, number, definiteness, case, degree) that drive **agreement**, **anaphora resolution**, and **statistical POS tagging**.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The House-Number Analogy:**
> Imagine a noun as a **house**. A **determiner** is like the **number-plate / street sign** in front of the house — it tells you *which* house (the red one? my house? every house?). An **adjective** is like the **paint, architecture, or garden** of the house — it tells you *what kind* of house it is (a *tall*, *Victorian*, *blue* house).
> 
> - *Determiner* → answers **"Which one?"** (Reference / Quantification)
> - *Adjective* → answers **"What kind?"** (Attribution / Qualification)

**Computational View:** For an NLP pipeline, determiners are critical **discourse anchors** (helping coreference resolution find what "the" refers to), while adjectives are high-value **semantic features** (used in sentiment analysis, attribute extraction, and vector-space semantics).

---

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bold Highlighted)

- **Penn Treebank (PTB) Tagset Size:** **45 tags**, with **DT** for determiner and **JJ / JJR / JJS** for adjectives.
- **Universal Dependencies (UD) POS Tags:** **DET** (determiner) and **ADJ** (adjective).
- **Open vs. Closed Class:** Determiners are a **closed class** (≈ **50–70 words** in English); Adjectives are an **open class** (theoretically **infinite**, with ≈ **28,000+** lemma entries in modern corpora).
- **Royal Order of Adjectives:** English enforces a relatively fixed cumulative ordering of ≥ **6 semantic categories** before a noun.

---

## 1.4 Quick Reference Table of Examples

| Word | Category | Sub-type | PTB Tag |
|---|---|---|---|
| the | Determiner | Definite Article | DT |
| a, an | Determiner | Indefinite Article | DT |
| this, that | Determiner | Demonstrative | DT |
| my, your, his | Determiner | Possessive | DT$ (or PRP$) |
| some, any, every | Determiner | Quantifier | DT |
| five, three | Determiner | Numeral / Cardinal | CD |
| big, red, clever | Adjective | Descriptive / Attributive | JJ |
| bigger, smaller | Adjective | Comparative | JJR |
| biggest, smallest | Adjective | Superlative | JJS |
| Indian, American | Adjective | Proper Adjective | JJ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Sub-Categorization of Determiners

Determiners are syntactically and semantically heterogeneous. The standard linguistic classification is as follows:

1. **Articles (Definite & Indefinite)**
   - Definite: *the* — presupposes the referent is identifiable to speaker and hearer.
   - Indefinite: *a / an* — introduces a referent not yet specific.

2. **Demonstrative Determiners**
   - *this, that, these, those* — encode spatial/temporal deixis.
   - **Feature:** ±PROXIMATE (this/these = +PROX; that/those = −PROX)

3. **Possessive Determiners**
   - *my, your, his, her, its, our, their* — express a possessive relation.
   - Function as the specifier of DP and license agreement on the noun.

4. **Quantifiers**
   - Universal: *every, all* | Existential: *some, any* | Negative: *no, neither*.
   - These interact with **scope** phenomena central to computational semantics.

5. **Numeral Determiners**
   - Cardinals: *one, two, three…* | Ordinals: *first, second, third…* (PTB tag **CD** for cardinals).

6. **Wh-Determiners**
   - *which, what, whose* — trigger **wh-movement** in interrogatives and relatives.

7. **Distributives**
   - *each, every, either, neither* — apply the predicate to members of a set individually.

---

## 2.2 Sub-Categorization of Adjectives

### A. By Syntactic Position
- **Attributive:** appears *before* the noun → *"a **tall** building"*.
- **Predicative:** appears *after* a copula → *"The building **is tall**"*.
- Some adjectives (e.g., *asleep, alone, afraid*) are **predicative-only**; some (e.g., *utter, chief*) are **attributive-only**.

### B. By Semantic Class — The **Royal Order of Adjectives**
English native speakers prefer a near-fixed cumulative order. The **Royal Order** is:

$$\text{Opinion} \rightarrow \text{Size} \rightarrow \text{Age} \rightarrow \text{Shape} \rightarrow \text{Colour} \rightarrow \text{Origin} \rightarrow \text{Material} \rightarrow \text{Purpose Noun}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A "*lovely little old rectangular green French silver whaling knife*" is grammatical, but "*green little lovely*" is not. This is statistically exploitable by **POS taggers** and **language models**.

### C. By Inflectional Form
- **Positive (Base)**: *tall, big* → tag **JJ**
- **Comparative**: *taller, bigger* → tag **JJR** (formed by *-er* or *more…than*)
- **Superlative**: *tallest, biggest* → tag **JJS** (formed by *-est* or *most…in/of*)

The comparative / superlative can be expressed as the following rule:

$$ Adj_{JJR} = Adj_{JJ} + \begin{cases} \text{-er} & \text{if monosyllabic or bisyllabic with stress on first} \\ \text{more} + Adj & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$

$$ Adj_{JJS} = Adj_{JJ} + \begin{cases} \text{-est} & \text{if monosyllabic or bisyllabic with stress on first} \\ \text{most} + Adj & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$

---

## 2.3 Computational Linguistics Perspective

In **NLP systems**, determiners and adjectives are pivotal for the following reasons:

| Task | Role of Determiners | Role of Adjectives |
|---|---|---|
| POS Tagging | Small, closed vocabulary → useful anchor in HMMs | Morphological cues (-er, -est) aid tagging |
| Parsing | Head the Determiner Phrase (DP) | Attach to Noun via AP → NP rule |
| Coreference Resolution | "the / this / that" links to antecedents | Adjectives distinguish referents |
| Sentiment Analysis | Low direct contribution | Primary carriers of polarity |
| Information Extraction | Boundary markers of NPs | Attribute-value pairs in templates |
| Machine Translation | Agreement with noun (gender/number) | Reordering, agreement transfer |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formal Statement | PTB Tag | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Definite Article | The unique / identifiable referent | DT | *the sun* |
| 2 | Indefinite Article | Non-specific referent, singular count | DT | *a cat* |
| 3 | Demonstrative | Spatio-temporal deixis | DT | *this book* |
| 4 | Possessive | Genitive relation | DT$ / PRP$ | *my car* |
| 5 | Quantifier | All, some, no, every | DT | *every student* |
| 6 | Numeral | Cardinal / ordinal count | CD | *three apples* |
| 7 | Wh-Determiner | Triggers wh-movement | WDT / DT | *which book* |
| 8 | Attributive Adj. | Pre-nominal modifier | JJ | *a tall tree* |
| 9 | Predicative Adj. | Post-copula complement | JJ | *tree is tall* |
| 10 | Comparative Adj. | Two-way comparison | JJR | *taller* |
| 11 | Superlative Adj. | ≥ three-way comparison | JJS | *tallest* |
| 12 | Proper Adj. | Derived from proper noun | JJ | *Indian* |
| 13 | Royal Order | 8-class cumulative ordering | — | *lovely little old* |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Quick Recall:** Determiners precede adjectives in the NP; both precede the noun. Within adjectives, the **Royal Order** is rigid.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Linguistic Matrices

## 3.1 Syntactic Derivation of a Noun Phrase (NP)

We will use **X-bar theory** / **Bare Phrase Structure** to derive the internal structure of a determiner + adjective + noun sequence such as *"the tall building"*.

### Step 1 — Lexical Insertion
Lexical items are inserted from the lexicon with their inherent **feature bundles**.

> **the** → [CAT=D, GEN=NEUT, NUM=SG, DEF=+]
> **tall** → [CAT=A, DEG=POS, GRAD=ABLE]
> **building** → [CAT=N, NUM=SG, GEN=NEUT]

### Step 2 — Head Selection
The noun **building** projects upward. Its features (NUM=SG, GEN=NEUT) must be **agreed with** by lower functional heads.

### Step 3 — Adjective Adjunction
The adjective **tall** is an optional adjunct. It merges with the NP intermediate projection:

$$\begin{aligned}
N' \;&\rightarrow\; AP\; N' \\
AP \;&\rightarrow\; A' \\
A' \;&\rightarrow\; A \; (AP^*)
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting the lexical item **tall**:

$$A' \;\rightarrow\; tall$$

This gives the intermediate structure *[tall [building]]*.

### Step 4 — Determiner Merging
The determiner **the** is the **specifier** of the Determiner Phrase. It merges with the NP:

$$\begin{aligned}
DP \;&\rightarrow\; D \;\; NP \\
NP \;&\rightarrow\; (AP^*)\; N'
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting:

$$DP \;\rightarrow\; \text{the} \;\; [\text{tall building}]$$

### Step 5 — Feature Checking
Agreement feature checking takes place under **Agree (Chomsky 2000)**:

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{[\text{NUM}=sg,\;\text{GEN}=neut]} \;\;\longleftrightarrow\;\; N_{[\text{NUM}=sg,\;\text{GEN}=neut]} \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

The features match — the derivation converges and yields the well-formed DP *"the tall building"*.

### Step 6 — Final Tree (Conceptual)

$$[\text{DP}\;\; \text{the} \;\; [\text{NP}\;\; [\text{AP}\;\; \text{tall}] \;\; [\text{N'}\;\; \text{building}]]]$$

---

## 3.2 Morphological Derivation of Comparative & Superlative

### Worked Example: Adjective *happy*

#### Step 1 — Identify Syllable Structure
*happy* = /ˈhæp.i/ → **2 syllables**, stress on **first** syllable.

#### Step 2 — Apply the Comparative Rule
By the heuristic (stress on first syllable of a 2-syllable adjective):

$$Adj_{JJR} = Adj_{JJ} + \text{-ier} \;/\; \text{y} \rightarrow \text{i} \text{ before suffix}$$

$$\text{happy} \rightarrow \text{happ} + \text{ier} = \textbf{happier}$$

> *Note: Final "y" preceded by a consonant changes to "i".*

#### Step 3 — Apply the Superlative Rule

$$Adj_{JJS} = Adj_{JJ} + \text{-iest}$$

$$\text{happy} \rightarrow \text{happ} + \text{iest} = \textbf{happiest}$$

### Worked Example: Adjective *intelligent*

#### Step 1 — Syllable Structure
*intelligent* = /ɪnˈtel.ɪ.dʒənt/ → **4 syllables**, stress on **second** syllable.

#### Step 2 — Apply the Rule for Polysyllabic Adjectives

$$Adj_{JJR} = \text{more} + Adj_{JJ} = \textbf{more intelligent}$$

$$Adj_{JJS} = \text{most} + Adj_{JJ} = \textbf{most intelligent}$$

---

## 3.3 Agreement & Feature Matrix for a Sample NP

For the noun phrase *"these intelligent Indian students"*, compute the feature matrix:

| Head | Lexeme | POS | NUM | PERS | CASE | DEF | DEG |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Determiner | these | DT | PL | 3 | NOM | +DEF | — |
| Adjective 1 | intelligent | JJ | PL-agree | — | — | — | POS |
| Adjective 2 | Indian | JJ | PL-agree | — | — | — | POS |
| Noun | students | NNS | PL | 3 | NOM | — | — |

> **Computational note:** In a **HMM-based POS tagger**, the transition probability P(JJ → NNS) given the determiners *these/those* is much higher than P(JJ → NN), because English determiners and demonstratives force **number agreement** on the noun.

This can be formalized via Bayes' theorem:

$$P(t_i \mid w_i) = \frac{P(w_i \mid t_i) \cdot P(t_i \mid t_{i-1})}{P(w_i)}$$

Where:
- $P(w_i \mid t_i)$ = **emission probability** of word $w_i$ given tag $t_i$
- $P(t_i \mid t_{i-1})$ = **transition probability** from previous tag

For the sequence *[DT, JJ, JJ, NNS]*, the transition probabilities become:

$$P(\text{NNS} \mid \text{JJ}, \text{DT}) = P(\text{JJ} \mid \text{DT}) \cdot P(\text{JJ} \mid \text{JJ}) \cdot P(\text{NNS} \mid \text{JJ})$$

This is the computational justification for tagging entire determiner–adjective–noun sequences as a unit.

---

## 3.4 Order-of-Adjectives Validation Matrix

Test phrase: *"a beautiful big old round red Italian leather handbag"*

| Position | Category | Word | Royal-Order Slot |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Article | a | Determiner |
| 2 | Opinion | beautiful | 1 — Opinion |
| 3 | Size | big | 2 — Size |
| 4 | Age | old | 3 — Age |
| 5 | Shape | round | 4 — Shape |
| 6 | Colour | red | 5 — Colour |
| 7 | Origin | Italian | 6 — Origin |
| 8 | Material | leather | 7 — Material |
| 9 | Purpose | handbag | 8 — Purpose Noun |

**Result:** Sentence is grammatical. Reverse any adjacent pair (e.g., *red round* → *round red*) and the sentence becomes degraded — a quantitative signal used by **language-model perplexity** checks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Determiners

```mermaid
graph TD
    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef cat fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef sub fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef ex fill:#deebf7,stroke:#000,color:#000

    DET[Determiner Function Word]:::root
    DET --> ART[Articles]:::cat
    DET --> DEMO[Demonstratives]:::cat
    DET --> POSS[Possessives]:::cat
    DET --> QUANT[Quantifiers]:::cat
    DET --> NUM[Numerals]:::cat
    DET --> WHDET[Wh Determiners]:::cat
    DET --> DIST[Distributives]:::cat

    ART --> ART1[Definite the]:::ex
    ART --> ART2[Indefinite a an]:::ex
    DEMO --> DEMO1[this these]:::ex
    DEMO --> DEMO2[that those]:::ex
    POSS --> POSS1[my your his her its our their]:::ex
    QUANT --> QUANT1[every all some any no]:::ex
    NUM --> NUM1[Cardinal one two three]:::ex
    NUM --> NUM2[Ordinal first second third]:::ex
    WHDET --> WHDET1[which what whose]:::ex
    DIST --> DIST1[each either neither]:::ex
```

## 4.2 Hierarchical Classification of Adjectives

```mermaid
graph TD
    classDef root fill:#385723,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef cat fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef sub fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef ex fill:#e2efda,stroke:#000,color:#000

    ADJ[Adjective Lexical Word]:::root
    ADJ --> POS[By Form]:::cat
    ADJ --> SYN[By Syntax]:::cat
    ADJ --> SEM[By Semantics]:::cat

    POS --> P1[Positive JJ]:::sub
    POS --> P2[Comparative JJR]:::sub
    POS --> P3[Superlative JJS]:::sub

    SYN --> S1[Attributive pre noun]:::sub
    SYN --> S2[Predicative after copula]:::sub

    SEM --> SEM1[Descriptive tall red clever]:::ex
    SEM --> SEM2[Quantitative few many several]:::ex
    SEM --> SEM3[Demonstrative this that]:::ex
    SEM --> SEM4[Possessive my your his]:::ex
    SEM --> SEM5[Interrogative which what]:::ex
    SEM --> SEM6[Proper Indian American]:::ex
    SEM --> SEM7[Distributive each every]:::ex

    P1 --> P1E[big red fast]:::ex
    P2 --> P2E[bigger redder faster]:::ex
    P3 --> P3E[biggest reddest fastest]:::ex
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture — NP Parsing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef input fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef store fill:#d9d9d9,stroke:#000,color:#000

    INP[Raw Text Tokenised]:::input
    POS[POS Tagger Hidden Markov Model]:::proc
    DTAG[Detected DT tag]:::out
    ADJTAG[Detected JJ JJR JJS tag]:::out
    LEX[Lexicon Lookup]:::store
    PARSE[CFG Parser NP rules]:::proc
    AGR[Agreement Checker Number Gender]:::proc
    SEM[Semantic Role Adjective Modifier of Noun]:::out
    FINAL[Parsed DP Structure the tall building]:::out

    INP --> POS
    POS --> DTAG
    POS --> ADJTAG
    DTAG --> LEX
    ADJTAG --> LEX
    LEX --> PARSE
    PARSE --> AGR
    AGR --> SEM
    AGR --> FINAL
```

## 4.4 NP Internal Structure (X-bar Tree)

```mermaid
graph TD
    classDef dp fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef np fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef ap fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef lex fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000,color:#000

    DP[DP Determiner Phrase]:::dp
    D[Spec D: the]:::lex
    NP[NP Noun Phrase]:::np
    AP[AP Adjective Phrase]:::ap
    A1[A1: tall]:::lex
    AP2[AP2: red]:::lex
    Ndash[N bar]:::np
    N[N: building]:::lex

    DP --> D
    DP --> NP
    NP --> AP
    NP --> Ndash
    AP --> A1
    AP --> AP2
    Ndash --> N
```

## 4.5 POS Tagging State Diagram (DT–JJ–N Transitions)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> DT : Sentence Start
    DT --> JJ : Adjective Adjunction
    JJ --> JJ : Coordinated Adjectives
    JJ --> NN : Noun Singular
    JJ --> NNS : Noun Plural Agreement
    NN --> [*] : NP Closed
    NNS --> [*] : NP Closed
    DT --> NN : Bare NP No Adjective
    DT --> NNS : Plural Bare NP
    JJ --> JJR : Comparative Form
    JJ --> JJS : Superlative Form
    JJR --> NN
    JJR --> NNS
    JJS --> NN
    JJS --> NNS
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define a determiner and list any four sub-categories with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A **determiner** is a closed-class function word that introduces a noun and restricts its reference. Four sub-categories are:
> 1. **Definite Article** — *the book*
> 2. **Demonstrative** — *this pen*
> 3. **Possessive** — *my house*
> 4. **Quantifier** — *every student*
> 
> *Awarding: 1 mark definition + ½ × 4 = 2 marks for sub-categories and examples.*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: What is the difference between an attributive and a predicative adjective? Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Attributive adjective** appears *before* the noun it modifies.
>   *Example:* *a **tall** building*
> - **Predicative adjective** appears *after a copula* (be, seem, become).
>   *Example:* *The building **is tall***.
> 
> *Awarding: 1 mark each definition + 1 mark for examples.*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: (a)** Explain the major sub-categories of English determiners with examples. Discuss their syntactic role in the Determiner Phrase. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the comparative and superlative forms of English adjectives. State the morphological rules with three examples each. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Sub-categories of Determiners (7 marks):**

| # | Sub-category | Function | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Definite Article** | Refers to a uniquely identifiable entity | *the sun, the President* |
| 2 | **Indefinite Article** | Introduces a non-specific referent | *a boy, an apple* |
| 3 | **Demonstrative** | Spatial/temporal deixis | *this, that, these, those* |
| 4 | **Possessive** | Genitive relation | *my, your, his, her, its, our, their* |
| 5 | **Quantifier** | Specifies quantity/scope | *every, all, some, any, no, much, many* |
| 6 | **Numeral** | Cardinal/ordinal count | *one, two, first, second* |
| 7 | **Wh-determiner** | Triggers wh-movement in questions | *which, what, whose* |
| 8 | **Distributive** | Applies predicate to each member | *each, every, either, neither* |

**Syntactic Role in DP:** *(2 marks)*

In X-bar theory, the determiner occupies the **head (D)** of the **Determiner Phrase (DP)**, with the NP as its complement. The determiner's role is to **fix the reference** of the noun and **license agreement features** (number, gender, definiteness).

$$DP \;\rightarrow\; D\; (DP^*)\; NP$$

Example: *The book* → **the** is the D head; **book** is the NP complement.

---

**(b) Comparative & Superlative Forms (7 marks):**

The **comparative** (PTB tag **JJR**) compares **two** entities, while the **superlative** (PTB tag **JJS**) compares **three or more**.

**Morphological Rules:**

$$Adj_{JJR} = \begin{cases} Adj_{JJ} + \text{-er} & \text{if monosyllabic / bisyllabic w/ first-syllable stress} \\ \text{more} + Adj_{JJ} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

$$Adj_{JJS} = \begin{cases} Adj_{JJ} + \text{-est} & \text{if monosyllabic / bisyllabic w/ first-syllable stress} \\ \text{most} + Adj_{JJ} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Examples — Monosyllabic (JJR = -er, JJS = -est):**

| Base | Comparative | Superlative |
|---|---|---|
| tall | taller | tallest |
| fast | faster | fastest |
| big | bigger | biggest |

**Examples — Polysyllabic (JJR = more…, JJS = most…):**

| Base | Comparative | Superlative |
|---|---|---|
| intelligent | more intelligent | most intelligent |
| beautiful | more beautiful | most beautiful |
| difficult | more difficult | most difficult |

**Irregular Forms (special mention):**

| Base | Comparative | Superlative |
|---|---|---|
| good | better | best |
| bad | worse | worst |
| far | farther / further | farthest / furthest |

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: (a)** What is the "Royal Order of Adjectives" in English? Justify with one example containing at least six adjectives. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain how Determiners and Adjectives are handled in a Part-of-Speech (POS) tagger. Discuss the Penn Treebank tag assignments. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Royal Order of Adjectives (7 marks):**

The **Royal Order of Adjectives** is the empirically observed cumulative ordering rule that English (and many other Germanic languages) imposes on pre-nominal adjectives. Native speakers reject violations of this order, even when the meaning is recoverable.

The order, with category labels:

$$\text{Opinion} \rightarrow \text{Size} \rightarrow \text{Age} \rightarrow \text{Shape} \rightarrow \text{Colour} \rightarrow \text{Origin} \rightarrow \text{Material} \rightarrow \text{Purpose}$$

**Worked Example — Six-Adjective Phrase:**

> *"A **lovely** (Opinion) **little** (Size) **old** (Age) **rectangular** (Shape) **green** (Colour) **French** (Origin) **silver** (Material) **whaling** (Purpose) knife"*

**Validation Test (1 mark):**
- *A green little knife* ❌ (rejected — Size before Colour)
- *A lovely little old rectangular green French silver whaling knife* ✅ (accepted)

**Computational Significance (1 mark):** The Royal Order is exploited as a **prior in language modelling**; n-gram and transformer models show markedly higher probability for canonical orderings, validating the rule statistically.

---

**(b) Determiners & Adjectives in POS Tagging (7 marks):**

A **POS tagger** assigns a grammatical category to every token in running text. Determiners and adjectives are tagged using the **Penn Treebank (PTB) tagset** as follows:

| Category | PTB Tag | Example Tokens |
|---|---|---|
| Determiner (incl. articles, demonstratives, possessives) | **DT** | *the, a, this, that, my, every* |
| Possessive Pronoun / Determiner | **PRP\$** | *my, your, his* (when used independently) |
| Adjective (base) | **JJ** | *tall, red, clever* |
| Adjective (comparative) | **JJR** | *taller, smarter* |
| Adjective (superlative) | **JJS** | *tallest, smartest* |
| Cardinal Number | **CD** | *one, 2024* |

**Why determiners are easy to tag (2 marks):**
- Closed class — only ≈ 50–70 word types.
- High **unigram emission probability** for the DT tag.
- High contextual ambiguity with pronouns is low.

**Why adjectives are harder (2 marks):**
- Open class — tens of thousands of lemma entries.
- Many English words are **ambiguous** between JJ and other tags (e.g., *book* as N vs. V; *fast* as JJ vs. RB vs. NN).

**Tagging Mechanism (HMM / CRF / Transformer) (1 mark):**
- **HMM**: maximizes P(tag sequence | word) using Bayes' rule.
- **CRF** and **neural** taggers (e.g., BiLSTM, BERT) handle **long-range dependencies**, including the determiner–adjective–noun agreement captured via transition features.

**Outcome:** A correctly tagged sentence: *"The/DT tall/JJ man/NN arrived/VBD"*.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing DT and JJ tags** — Students frequently tag *this* / *my* as JJ instead of DT. Remember: **DT introduces a noun; JJ modifies a noun.**
> 2. **Forgetting the three adjective tags** — Always distinguish **JJ / JJR / JJS** in tagging questions. Partial credit only.
> 3. **Ignoring the Royal Order** — Listing adjective types without giving the **canonical order** loses 2–3 marks in Part B.
> 4. **Wrong article form** — *a* vs. *an* depends on the **following sound**, not the spelling (*an honest man* — vowel *sound*).
> 5. **Skipping the morpho-syntactic features** — Examiners award marks for explicit **feature bundles** (NUM, GEN, CASE) in derivation questions.
> 6. **Treating comparatives only as -er/-est** — Always mention the **periphrastic *more/most*** form for polysyllabic adjectives.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Determiners & Adjectives — Rapid Revision Checklist**
> - ✅ A **determiner** is a closed-class function word that **fixes the reference** of a noun.
> - ✅ Determiners include **articles, demonstratives, possessives, quantifiers, numerals, wh-determiners, distributives**.
> - ✅ An **adjective** is an open-class word that **modifies a noun** attributively or predicatively.
> - ✅ PTB tags: **DT** (determiner), **JJ** (adjective), **JJR** (comparative), **JJS** (superlative), **CD** (cardinal).
> - ✅ Royal Order: **Opinion → Size → Age → Shape → Colour → Origin → Material → Purpose**.
> - ✅ Comparative rule: **monosyllabic + -er / polysyllabic + more**.
> - ✅ Superlative rule: **monosyllabic + -est / polysyllabic + most**.
> - ✅ In X-bar syntax, **D** heads the **DP**, the **N** heads the **NP**, and **A** heads the **AP**.
> - ✅ Determiners and adjectives both precede the noun; determiners precede adjectives.
> - ✅ Irregular comparatives: *good → better → best*, *bad → worse → worst*, *far → farther/further → farthest/furthest*.
> - ✅ In NLP, determiners are **low-frequency / high-precision** tags; adjectives are **high-frequency / lower-precision** tags requiring context.
> - ✅ The Royal Order is statistically observable in corpora and used as a prior in language modelling.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Verbs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Verbs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

In the context of **Computational Linguistics**, a **Verb** is formally defined as a *lexical category* (part-of-speech) that denotes an action, occurrence, state, or process, and which functions as the syntactic and semantic head of a **Verb Phrase (VP)**. Verbs are the only obligatory lexical category within the predicate of a clause, governing argument structure, selectional restrictions, subcategorization, tense, aspect, mood, voice, and agreement morphology.

In the formal grammar tradition adopted in Computational Linguistics (Chomsky & Lasnik school; Head-Driven Phrase Structure Grammar; Lexical-Functional Grammar), a verb is treated as a *structured lexical entry* containing:

$$\text{VERB} = \langle \text{PHON}, \text{CAT}, \text{SEL}, \text{SEM}, \text{MORPH} \rangle$$

Where each component specifies phonological form, syntactic category, subcategorization frame, semantic predicate-argument structure, and inflectional morphology respectively.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition**: A verb is the lexical head of a VP, projecting argument structure and governing agreement, tense-aspect-mood morphology. In NLP pipelines, verbs are the **primary anchors for semantic role labeling, information extraction, and machine translation**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **sentence as a small theatre play**:
- The **Nouns** are the *actors* (subject, object, indirect object).
- The **Verbs** are the *director and the script of action* — they tell the actors *what to do, when, how, and to whom*.
- The **Adjectives/Adverbs** are the *costumes and lighting* — they dress up the action.

Without a verb, a sentence is just a list of nouns — there is no event, no relationship, no happening. The verb is what **"fires the event"** in time. In a computer, this is exactly why verbs become critical: an NLP system that only recognizes nouns can list entities ("John, ball, park") but cannot extract the *event* ("John kicked the ball at the park"). Hence, verbs are computationally the **event-extraction anchors**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-linguistic Fact**: All human languages have a lexical category roughly equivalent to "verb." This is a linguistic universal, supported by typological surveys (e.g., the World Atlas of Language Structures).

## 1.3 Salient Metrics in Computational Linguistics (Bolded)

- **17** major verb subcategorization frame classes (Manchester/IBM English Constraint Grammar counts)
- **~4500–5000** frequently attested English verb lemmas in balanced corpora (BNC)
- **0.05–0.15 seconds** — typical latency for verb sense disambiguation per sentence in modern BERT-based systems
- **3** canonical arguments: **Agent**, **Patient**, **Theme** (in event semantics)
- **6** morphological categories encoded in a typical English verb paradigm: **Person, Number, Tense, Aspect, Mood, Voice**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Verbs

Verbs are classified along **four orthogonal axes** in computational linguistics:

### Axis A — Syntactic Behavior (Valency)
- **Intransitive (Vi)**: Takes only a subject. *She sleeps.*
- **Monotransitive (Vt)**: Takes subject + object. *She reads a book.*
- **Ditransitive (Vdt)**: Takes subject + indirect object + direct object. *She gave him a book.*
- **Transitive of Oblique (Vto)**: Takes subject + object + prepositional phrase. *She put the book on the table.*

### Axis B — Lexical-Semantic Type
- **Lexical (Full) Verbs**: Carry independent semantic content (*run*, *think*, *devour*).
- **Auxiliary Verbs**: Mark tense, aspect, mood, voice (*be*, *have*, *do*).
- **Modal Verbs**: Express possibility, necessity, permission (*can*, *must*, *may*).
- **Copular Verbs**: Link subject to a predicate (*be*, *seem*, *become*).
- **Light Verbs**: Form composite predicates with nouns (*take a walk*, *make a decision*).
- **Raising/Control Verbs**: Trigger subject-to-subject or object-to-subject raising (*seems to*, *wants to*).

### Axis C — Morphological Class
- **Regular Verbs**: Form past tense/past participle via *-ed* (*walk → walked*).
- **Irregular Verbs**: Form past tense via vowel change, suppletion, or zero-morpheme (*go → went*, *cut → cut*).

### Axis D — Semantic Role
- Verbs select for *thematic roles*: **Agent, Patient, Theme, Goal, Source, Instrument, Beneficiary, Experiencer, Location, Manner**.

## 2.2 Verb Morphology — The Inflectional Paradigm

A fully inflected English verb must encode up to **five morphological features**:

$$\text{VERB} \rightarrow \text{Person} \times \text{Number} \times \text{Tense} \times \text{Aspect} \times \text{Mood}$$

For the modal future and progressive aspects, additional **periphrastic** constructions are used (auxiliary *will*, *be + -ing*).

## 2.3 Subcategorization Frames (KTU High-Yield)

A **subcategorization frame** is the list of complements a verb requires. In a *Lexical-Functional Grammar* (LFG) style annotation, verbs are tagged as:

$$\text{SUB-CAT} : \text{VP} \rightarrow \{\text{cat} \mid \text{np}, \text{pp}_\text{with}, \text{s}, \text{cp}, \text{advp}, \ldots\}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Notation / Rule | Example |
|---|---------|------------------|---------|
| 1 | Intransitive | $\text{VP} \rightarrow \text{V}$ | *sleep* |
| 2 | Monotransitive | $\text{VP} \rightarrow \text{V} \; \text{NP}$ | *kick* |
| 3 | Ditransitive | $\text{VP} \rightarrow \text{V} \; \text{NP} \; \text{NP}$ | *give* |
| 4 | Oblique Transitive | $\text{VP} \rightarrow \text{V} \; \text{NP} \; \text{PP}$ | *put* |
| 5 | Sentential | $\text{VP} \rightarrow \text{V} \; \text{S}$ | *believe* |
| 6 | Small Clause | $\text{VP} \rightarrow \text{V} \; \text{NP} \; \text{AP}$ | *consider* |
| 7 | Regular Past Rule | $\text{V} \rightarrow \text{V} + \text{ed}$ | *walk → walked* |
| 8 | Morphological Feature Vector | $f = \langle p, n, t, a, m, v \rangle$ | — |
| 9 | Argument Count | $A(v) \in \{0, 1, 2, 3\}$ | *sleep=1, give=2* |
| 10 | Thematic Role Set | $\Theta = \{ \text{AGT}, \text{PAT}, \text{TH}, \text{GOAL}, \ldots\}$ | — |
| 11 | Selectional Restriction | $\text{SEL}(v, x) : \text{HUMAN}(x) \rightarrow \text{allowed}$ | *admire* |
| 12 | Light Verb Construction | $\text{V} + \text{N}$ | *take a walk* |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, vertical bars inside math expressions are intentionally avoided. The symbol `\vert` or `\mid` should be used for absolute-value notation in LaTeX to prevent markdown table breakage.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Information Extraction (IE)**: Verbs are anchors for event extraction pipelines (e.g., ACE/TAC shared tasks).
- **Machine Translation (MT)**: Verb sense disambiguation directly determines target-language verb choice.
- **Question Answering (QA)**: "What did X do?" type queries pivot on the main verb.
- **Knowledge Graph Construction**: Verb-argument pairs become *triples* (Subject, Predicate, Object).
- **Sentiment Analysis**: Light verbs (*make a decision*, *take a risk*) shift sentiment polarity.
- **Healthcare NLP**: Clinical event extraction (e.g., "administered," "diagnosed") is verb-driven.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Morphological Parsing of an Inflected Verb

**Problem**: Parse the word form **"replayed"** into its constituent morphemes and feature bundle.

### Step-by-step Reasoning

**Step 1 — Surface segmentation via longest-match suffix-stripping.**
The form *replayed* ends in the regular past-tense morpheme *-ed*. Strip it:

$$\text{replayed} \;\rightarrow\; \text{replay} + \text{ed}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the remaining structure.**
The stem *replay* contains the derivational prefix *re-* attached to the base *play*:

$$\text{replay} \;\rightarrow\; \text{re} + \text{play}$$

**Step 3 — Combine to obtain the full morphemic structure.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{replayed} &\;\rightarrow\; \text{re} + \text{play} + \text{ed} \\
&\;\rightarrow\; \underbrace{\text{re}}_{\text{derivational prefix}} \;+\; \underbrace{\text{play}}_{\text{base lemma}} \;+\; \underbrace{\text{ed}}_{\text{inflectional suffix}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Annotate the feature vector $f$ for the final inflected form.**
- Person: 3rd
- Number: Singular (default, agreement comes from DP)
- Tense: Past
- Aspect: Simple
- Mood: Indicative
- Voice: Active

$$
f(\text{replayed}) = \langle p=3, n=\text{sg}, t=\text{past}, a=\text{simple}, m=\text{indic}, v=\text{active} \rangle
$$

**Step 5 — Record the lemma and POS tag.**

$$\text{Lemma}(\text{replayed}) = \text{play}, \quad \text{POS} = \text{VBD (Verb, Past Tense)}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Subcategorization Frame Assignment

**Problem**: Determine the subcategorization frame of the verb *donate* from the corpus sentence:

> *"The foundation donated a generator to the hospital."*

### Step-by-step Reasoning

**Step 1 — Identify the verb and its dependents.**

The verb is *donated*. Its syntactic dependents are:
- Subject NP: *The foundation*
- Direct Object NP: *a generator*
- Prepositional Phrase: *to the hospital*

**Step 2 — Test the diagnostic environments for verb class.**

| Diagnostic | Result | Inference |
|------------|--------|-----------|
| Passive: *A generator was donated* | ✓ Acceptable | Verb is transitive |
| Dative Shift: *donated the hospital a generator* | ✗ Unacceptable | Verb is NOT ditransitive |
| Pseudo-passive of PP: *The hospital was donated a generator to* | ✗ Unacceptable | PP is a true adjunct-like complement |
| Whiz deletion: *the hospital donated to* | ✓ Acceptable | PP is a true complement |

**Step 3 — Construct the subcategorization frame.**

$$
\text{SUB-CAT}(\text{donate}) = \text{VP} \rightarrow \text{V} \; \text{NP} \; \text{PP}_{\text{to}}
$$

**Step 4 — Assign thematic roles.**

$$
\Theta(\text{donate}) = \langle \text{AGT}(\text{foundation}), \text{TH}(\text{generator}), \text{GOAL}(\text{hospital}) \rangle
$$

**Step 5 — Verify Selectional Restrictions.**

- *foundation* ∈ HUMAN/ORGANIZATION ✓ allowed for AGT
- *generator* ∈ ARTIFACT ✓ allowed for TH
- *hospital* ∈ INSTITUTION ✓ allowed for GOAL

All selectional constraints satisfied, confirming valid parse.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Verb Phrase Phrase-Structure Rule Composition

**Problem**: Derive the VP structure of *"might have been reading carefully."*

### Step-by-step Reasoning

**Step 1 — Decompose the verb chain by status of each token.**

- *might* — modal auxiliary (MO)
- *have* — perfect auxiliary (HAVE-AUX)
- *been* — progressive auxiliary (BE-AUX)
- *reading* — main verb (MV), present participle
- *carefully* — VP-adjoined adverb

**Step 2 — Apply the recursive VP rule with auxiliaries.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{VP} &\rightarrow \text{MOD} \; \text{VP} \\
\text{VP} &\rightarrow \text{HAVE-en} \; \text{VP} \\
\text{VP} &\rightarrow \text{BE-ing} \; \text{VP} \\
\text{VP} &\rightarrow \text{MV} \; \text{(NP)} \; \text{(PP)} \\
\text{VP} &\rightarrow \text{VP} \; \text{ADVP}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Walk the derivation tree bottom-up.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{VP}_1 &\rightarrow \text{reading} \quad \text{(MV, no complement)} \\
\text{VP}_2 &\rightarrow \text{VP}_1 \; \text{carefully} \quad \text{(ADVP right-adjoined)} \\
\text{VP}_3 &\rightarrow \text{been} \; \text{VP}_2 \quad \text{(BE-ing rule)} \\
\text{VP}_4 &\rightarrow \text{have} \; \text{VP}_3 \quad \text{(HAVE-en rule)} \\
\text{VP}_5 &\rightarrow \text{might} \; \text{VP}_4 \quad \text{(MOD rule)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the resulting Tense-Aspect-Mood bundle.**

$$
\text{TAM}(\text{VP}_5) = \text{MODAL} + \text{PERFECT} + \text{PROGRESSIVE} + \text{PRESENT-STEM} = \text{modal perfect progressive present}
$$

**Step 5 — Final yield.**

$$\text{Surface} = \text{might} + \text{have} + \text{been} + \text{reading} + \text{carefully}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Verb Diagnostics Master Table

| Diagnostic | Intransitive | Transitive | Ditransitive | Raising | Control |
|------------|--------------|------------|--------------|---------|---------|
| Passive | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | (n/a) | (n/a) |
| Dative Shift | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ |
| There-insertion | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ |
| Pseudo-cleft | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ |
| *What X did was V* | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Verb Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[VERB] --> B[Lexical Verb]
    A --> C[Auxiliary Verb]
    A --> D[Modal Verb]
    A --> E[Copular Verb]
    A --> F[Light Verb]

    B --> B1[Intransitive]
    B --> B2[Transitive]
    B --> B3[Ditransitive]
    B --> B4[Oblique Transitive]

    C --> C1[BE Auxiliary]
    C --> C2[HAVE Auxiliary]
    C --> C3[DO Auxiliary]

    D --> D1[Possibility: can, may]
    D --> D2[Necessity: must, should]
    D --> D3[Permission: may, can]

    E --> E1[be]
    E --> E2[seem]
    E --> E3[become]

    F --> F1[take a walk]
    F --> F2[make a decision]
    F --> F3[have a shower]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Verb Phrase Internal Structure (Aux Chain)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[VP] --> M[Modal]
    S --> P[Perfect Aux]
    P --> PR[Progressive Aux]
    PR --> PA[Passive Aux]
    PA --> MV[Main Verb]
    MV --> OBJ[Object NP]
    S --> AD[Adjunct ADVP]

    M --> A1[might / can / will]
    P --> A2[have]
    PR --> A3[been]
    PA --> A4[been]
    MV --> A5[reading / written]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Argument Structure Pipeline (Computation Path)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Sentence] --> B[Tokenization]
    B --> C[POS Tagging]
    C --> D[Verb Identification]
    D --> E[Subcategorization Frame Lookup]
    E --> F[Thematic Role Assignment]
    F --> G[Selectional Restriction Check]
    G --> H{Valid Parse?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Semantic Representation]
    H -->|No| J[Re-parse with New Frame]
    J --> E
    I --> K[Event Extraction Output]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Subcategorization Frame Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V[Identify Verb] --> Q1{Has Direct Object?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Has PP Complement?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q3{Acceptable Dative Shift?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q4{Acceptable Passive?}
    Q2 -->|No| I1[Intransitive V]
    Q2 -->|Yes| OT[Oblique Transitive V]
    Q3 -->|Yes| DT[Ditransitive V]
    Q3 -->|No| T1[Transitive V]
    Q4 -->|Yes| T1
    Q4 -->|No| RC[Recheck]
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Verb Analysis Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L1[Lexical Layer]
        L1A[Verb Lexicon]
        L1B[Subcategorization Frames]
        L1C[Thematic Role Library]
    end

    subgraph L2[Syntactic Layer]
        L2A[POS Tagger]
        L2B[Phrase Structure Parser]
        L2C[Dependency Parser]
    end

    subgraph L3[Semantic Layer]
        L3A[Predicate Logic Encoder]
        L3B[FrameNet Mapper]
        L3C[Selectional Checker]
    end

    subgraph L4[Application Layer]
        L4A[Event Extraction]
        L4B[MT Pre-processor]
        L4C[QA Engine]
    end

    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
> Define a **verb** in computational linguistics. List and exemplify any **three** subcategorization frame types.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A verb is a lexical category that functions as the syntactic and semantic head of a verb phrase and projects argument structure. Three subcategorization frame types are:

1. **Intransitive (Vi)** — *VP → V*; e.g., *The child sleeps.* — 1 mark
2. **Monotransitive (Vt)** — *VP → V NP*; e.g., *The child reads a book.* — 1 mark
3. **Ditransitive (Vdt)** — *VP → V NP NP*; e.g., *She gave him a gift.* — 1 mark

*(Each correct frame with a valid example = 1 mark. Total 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
> Distinguish between **lexical verbs** and **auxiliary verbs**. Provide two examples for each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Criterion | Lexical Verb | Auxiliary Verb |
|-----------|--------------|----------------|
| Semantic Content | Carries full lexical meaning | Carries grammatical function (tense, aspect, voice) |
| Position in VP | Final head of VP | Precedes the main verb |
| Negation | Uses *not* after auxiliary | Cannot be negated directly (in most cases) |
| Example 1 | *run*, *write* | *be*, *have* |
| Example 2 | *devour*, *build* | *do* |

*(1 mark for distinction table, 1 mark each for two examples from each category. Total = 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module-Internal Choice Rule**: Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

### Question A (14 Marks) — Verb Argument Structure & Subcategorization

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **subcategorization frames** with at least **five** distinct frame types, providing one natural-language example and a phrase-structure rule for each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For the corpus sentence:
> *"The teacher explained the difficult concept to the students very patiently."*

Identify the main verb, list all its subcategorized complements and adjuncts, assign thematic roles to each NP, and verify the parse using the **dative-shift** and **passivization** diagnostics. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a)

A subcategorization frame specifies the syntactic categories that a verb *requires* as complements, distinct from optional adjuncts. Five distinct frame types:

1. **Intransitive**: $\text{VP} \rightarrow \text{V}$ — *The baby cried.*
2. **Monotransitive**: $\text{VP} \rightarrow \text{V} \; \text{NP}$ — *She wrote a letter.*
3. **Ditransitive (double-object)**: $\text{VP} \rightarrow \text{V} \; \text{NP} \; \text{NP}$ — *He told me a story.*
4. **Transitive + PP**: $\text{VP} \rightarrow \text{V} \; \text{NP} \; \text{PP}_\text{to}$ — *She gave a book to John.*
5. **Sentential complement**: $\text{VP} \rightarrow \text{V} \; \text{S}$ — *He believes that she is honest.*

*(1.4 marks per frame: 0.5 for rule, 0.4 for example, 0.5 for correct identification of frame type. Total 7 marks.)*

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — Identify the main verb.** The main verb is *explained*. *(0.5 mark)*

**Step 2 — List subcategorized complements.**
- Subject NP: *The teacher* (required)
- Direct Object NP: *the difficult concept* (required) 
- PP-complement: *to the students* (required by *explain*) *(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Identify the adjunct.**
- ADVP: *very patiently* — *adjunct*, not complement (modifies manner, optional). *(0.5 mark)*

**Step 4 — Assign thematic roles.**

$$
\Theta(\text{explain}) = \langle \text{AGT}(\text{teacher}), \text{TH}(\text{concept}), \text{GOAL}(\text{students}) \rangle
$$

*(1.5 marks: 0.5 per role)*

**Step 5 — Apply diagnostics.**

| Diagnostic | Sentence | Acceptable? | Conclusion |
|------------|----------|-------------|------------|
| Dative shift | *The teacher explained the students the difficult concept.* | ✗ Ungrammatical | Frame is *V NP PP*, not ditransitive. (1 mark) |
| Passivization of DO | *The difficult concept was explained to the students.* | ✓ Grammatical | *concept* is a true direct object. (1 mark) |
| Passivization of PP | *The students were explained to the difficult concept.* | ✗ Marginal | *to the students* is not freely passivizable as canonical object. (0.5 mark) |
| Adjunct test (manner) | *Very patiently* can move: *Very patiently, the teacher explained...* | ✓ Acceptable | Confirms *very patiently* is an ADVP adjunct. (0.5 mark) |

**Step 6 — Final subcategorization frame.**

$$\text{SUB-CAT}(\text{explain}) = \text{VP} \rightarrow \text{V} \; \text{NP} \; \text{PP}_{\text{to}}$$

*(0.5 mark)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Verb Morphology & Inflectional Paradigm

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Define **inflectional morphology** as applied to English verbs. Enumerate the **six** major inflectional categories and produce a feature-vector representation for the verb form *"has been written."* *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For the words **"unhelpfully," "rewritten," "misunderstood,"** perform a complete morphological analysis showing derivational prefixes, base, inflectional suffixes, and the resulting part-of-speech. State one rule of **regular verb formation** and one rule of **irregular verb formation**, illustrating each with a derivation. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Definition (2 marks):** Inflectional morphology is the systematic modification of a word to express grammatical categories such as tense, number, person, aspect, mood, and voice, without changing the word-class. The six categories for English verbs are:

1. **Person** (1st, 2nd, 3rd)
2. **Number** (Singular, Plural)
3. **Tense** (Present, Past)
4. **Aspect** (Simple, Progressive, Perfect, Perfect-Progressive)
5. **Mood** (Indicative, Subjunctive, Imperative)
6. **Voice** (Active, Passive)

*(1 mark for listing 6 categories)*

**Feature Vector for "has been written" (4 marks):**

The verb phrase decomposes as: *has* (perfect auxiliary) + *been* (passive auxiliary) + *written* (past participle of main verb).

- Person: 3rd
- Number: Singular (forced by auxiliary *has*)
- Tense: Present (in the auxiliary layer)
- Aspect: Perfect
- Mood: Indicative
- Voice: Passive
- Main-verb form: Past Participle (VBN)

$$
f(\text{has been written}) = \langle p=3, n=\text{sg}, t=\text{pres}, a=\text{perf}, m=\text{indic}, v=\text{pass} \rangle
$$

*(1 mark: full table representation. 1 mark: correct identification of voice=passive, aspect=perfect, form=PastParticiple.)*

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Word 1 — "unhelpfully" (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{unhelpfully} &\rightarrow \text{un} + \text{helpful} + \text{ly} \\
\text{POS} &= \text{ADJ-as-ADV} \;\;(\text{or ADV})
\end{aligned}
$$

- *un-* : derivational prefix (negation)
- *helpful* : base adjective
- *ly* : derivational suffix (adverbializer)
- POS change: ADJ → ADV

**Word 2 — "rewritten" (2.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{rewritten} &\rightarrow \text{re} + \text{write} + \text{en} \\
\text{POS} &= \text{V} \;(\text{past participle form, VBN})
\end{aligned}
$$

- *re-* : derivational prefix (again)
- *write* : base verb (lemma)
- *-en* : irregular past-participle suffix
- POS: Verb (V)

**Word 3 — "misunderstood" (2.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{misunderstood} &\rightarrow \text{mis} + \text{under} + \text{stand} \\
\text{POS} &= \text{V} \;(\text{past tense form, VBD})
\end{aligned}
$$

- *mis-* : derivational prefix (wrongly)
- *under-* : derivational prefix (beneath / sub-)
- *stand* : base verb (lemma, but past form is **suppletive**: *stand → stood*)
- POS: Verb (V)

**Rule 1 — Regular verb formation (illustrative):**

$$\text{V}_{\text{base}} + \text{ed} \rightarrow \text{V}_{\text{past}}$$

Example:

$$
\text{walk} + \text{ed} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{walked}
$$

**Rule 2 — Irregular verb formation (illustrative):**

Vowel-alternation (Ablaut) rule:

$$
\text{V} \, / \text{I}/ \;\rightarrow\; / \text{A}/ \;\;\text{ in past tense}
$$

Example:

$$
\text{sing} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{sang}
$$

*(Each rule with derivation: 0.5 mark each. Total 1 mark for the two rules.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Confusing inflection with derivation** — Students often mistakenly treat *-ed* and *-ly* as the same kind of morpheme. Examiner deductions: **up to 1–2 marks per error**.
> 2. **Forgetting selectional restrictions** — When assigning thematic roles, always check whether the noun semantically fits. Examiner deducts **0.5 mark** for missing role.
> 3. **Treating adjuncts as complements** — A very common mistake in subcategorization questions. Examiner deducts **1 mark** for misclassification.
> 4. **Missing the Voice feature** — For passive constructions, students forget to mark *v = passive*. Examiner deducts **0.5 mark**.
> 5. **Ignoring dative-shift diagnostic** — Used in nearly every KTU question on ditransitive verbs. Examiner deducts **1 mark** for skipping it.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Verb = head of VP**, projects argument structure, governs agreement.
- **Four classification axes**: syntactic valency, lexical-semantic type, morphological class, semantic role.
- **Six inflectional categories**: Person, Number, Tense, Aspect, Mood, Voice.
- **Subcategorization frames** = required complements, NOT optional adjuncts.
- **Thematic roles** = Agent, Patient, Theme, Goal, Source, Instrument, Beneficiary, Experiencer, Location, Manner.
- **Diagnostics to remember**: passive, dative shift, there-insertion, pseudo-cleft, whiz-deletion, *What X did was V*.
- **Regular vs. irregular**: regular = *-ed*; irregular = vowel change, suppletion, or zero-morpheme.
- **Light verbs** form composite predicates with nouns (*take a walk*).
- **Auxiliary chain ordering**: MOD > HAVE > BE-ing > BE-pass > MV.
- **Selectional restrictions** must be satisfied for a valid parse.
- **Verb-anchored NLP tasks**: Event Extraction, Semantic Role Labeling, MT, QA, Sentiment Analysis, Knowledge Graph construction.
- **Always annotate feature vector** $f = \langle p, n, t, a, m, v \rangle$ when discussing an inflected verb.
- **Memorize at least 5 subcategorization rules**: Vi, Vt, Vdt, V NP PP, V S.
- **Cross-linguistic note**: While all languages have a verb-like category, the exact mapping of syntactic and semantic features varies — important for MT and multilingual NLP.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Other Parts of Speech
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Other Parts of Speech

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Computational Linguistics**, the term **"Other Parts of Speech"** refers collectively to the grammatical word categories that fall outside the two principal open-class categories — **Nouns** and **Verbs**. These categories primarily include **Adjectives, Adverbs, Prepositions, Conjunctions, Determiners, Pronouns, Numerals, and Interjections**. Together, they form a heterogeneous group that includes both **closed-class** (function) words and **open-class** (content) modifiers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):**
> *Other Parts of Speech are the lexical categories — chiefly Adjectives, Adverbs, Prepositions, Conjunctions, Determiners, Pronouns, and Interjections — that supplement the principal categories (Noun, Verb) by providing modification, relational, linking, or emphatic functions within a syntactic structure. In computational linguistics, each is treated as a distinct POS tag in standardized tagsets such as the **Penn Treebank (45 tags)** and the **Universal Dependencies (17 tags) POS tagset.***

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **cinema** where a film is being shot:

- The **Nouns** are the *lead actors* (subjects, objects — the doers and the done-to).
- The **Verbs** are the *actions / scenes* (the story progression).
- The **"Other Parts of Speech"** are the *entire supporting crew* — the cinematographer (adjective, refining visuals), the music director (adverb, modifying tempo), the script supervisor holding scenes together (conjunction), the location manager placing the scene in space-time (preposition), the title-card writer (determiner), and the audience cheer-leader (interjection).

Without this supporting cast, a sentence is just a skeleton; with them, it becomes a vivid, well-structured, and meaningful communication.

---

## 1.3 The Major Sub-Categories (High-Yield Inventory)

| # | Category | Classical Function | Example | KTU-Standard Penn Tag |
|---|----------|-------------------|---------|----------------------|
| 1 | Adjective (Adj) | Modifies / qualifies a noun | *red, fast, intelligent* | **JJ / JJR / JJS** |
| 2 | Adverb (Adv) | Modifies verb / adj / clause | *quickly, very, here* | **RB / RBR / RBS** |
| 3 | Preposition (Prep) | Shows relation (in/of/on) | *in, on, at, of, with* | **IN** |
| 4 | Conjunction (Conj) | Links words/clauses | *and, but, or, because* | **CC / IN** |
| 5 | Determiner (Det) | Specifies noun reference | *the, a, this, some* | **DT** |
| 6 | Pronoun (Pron) | Replaces a noun | *he, she, it, they* | **PRP / PRP$** |
| 7 | Numeral (Num) | Quantifies | *one, two, first* | **CD** |
| 8 | Interjection (Interj) | Expresses emotion | *oh!, wow!, alas!* | **UH** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Board Exams:**
> - **Open-class (Adjectives, Adverbs)** → infinite vocabulary, new words can be coined (*"COVID-ish"*, *"zoomy"*).
> - **Closed-class (Prepositions, Conjunctions, Determiners, Pronouns)** → fixed inventory of about **150–300 words**; rarely grow.

---

## 1.4 Why "Other Parts of Speech" Matter in Computational Linguistics

1. **High frequency, low variability** — Closed-class words constitute nearly **~15%** of running text but cover the majority of high-frequency tokens (e.g., *the, of, and, to*).
2. **Heavy ambiguity** — Words like *"that"*, *"as"*, *"like"* can be Determiner / Pronoun / Conjunction / Preposition / Adverb depending on context.
3. **Critical for parsing & WSD** — Constituency and dependency parsers rely on correct POS tags to build trees.
4. **Search engines & IR** — Stop-word lists are essentially lists of closed-class "other" POS categories.
5. **Statistical bias** — Language models often learn these categories faster due to their high frequency.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Memory Trick — "DAD-DCPI"**
> **D**eterminer, **A**djective, **D**eterminer (repeat), **C**onjunction, **P**reposition, **I**nterjection — the typical order to scan a noun phrase.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Classification Logic (Step-by-Step Bulleted Framework)

The classification of "Other Parts of Speech" in a computational pipeline follows a four-tier logical ladder:

- **Tier 1 — Lexical Recognition**
  - Why: The system must first tokenize raw text into individual words.
  - How: A tokenizer splits `"The very quick fox"` → `[The, very, quick, fox]`.
  - Output: A linear sequence of tokens.

- **Tier 2 — Morphological Heuristics**
  - Why: Suffix/prefix patterns give strong POS evidence.
  - How:
    - `-ly`, `-wise`, `-ward` → Adverb
    - `-ous`, `-al`, `-ic`, `-ive` → Adjective
    - *—* *e.g., `happily`, `clockwise`, `dangerous`, `musical`.*
  - Caveat: False friends like `"friendly"` (Adjective, not Adverb) exist.

- **Tier 3 — Contextual / Syntactic Windowing**
  - Why: Closed-class categories are determined by **position** rather than morphology.
  - How: The tagger inspects a window of n tokens (often n = 3, i.e., trigram) and consults transition probabilities.
  - Example: The word *"round"* — Adjective in *"a round table"*, Noun in *"the round"*, Preposition in *"walked round the lake"*, Verb in *"round up the cattle"*.

- **Tier 4 — Statistical / Neural Disambiguation**
  - Why: Real-world text is ambiguous.
  - How: HMM, CRF, BiLSTM, or Transformer models assign the most probable tag sequence:
  $$ T^{*} = \arg\max_{T} P(T \mid W) $$

---

## 2.2 Closed-Class vs Open-Class Dichotomy

| Property | Open-Class (Adj, Adv) | Closed-Class (Prep, Conj, Det, Pron, Interj) |
|----------|---------------------|--------------------------------------------|
| Lexicon size | Thousands | 150–300 (in English) |
| New words? | Yes (neologisms) | Almost never |
| Frequency | Medium | Very high |
| POS-ambiguity | Lower | Higher |
| Example | *"selfie-worthy"* | *the, of, in* |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Application |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Bayes' Theorem for POS | $$ P(T_i \mid W_i) = \dfrac{P(W_i \mid T_i) \cdot P(T_i)}{P(W_i)} $$ | Disambiguating a word given its tag |
| 2 | HMM Tag Sequence | $$ \hat{T} = \arg\max_{T} \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(W_i \mid T_i) \cdot P(T_i \mid T_{i-1}) $$ | Viterbi decoding |
| 3 | Tagger Accuracy | $$ \text{Acc} = \dfrac{\text{Correct Tags}}{\text{Total Tokens}} $$ | Evaluation metric |
| 4 | Precision (per class) | $$ P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP} $$ | Per-POS precision |
| 5 | Recall (per class) | $$ R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN} $$ | Per-POS recall |
| 6 | F1-Score | $$ F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R} $$ | Balanced metric |
| 7 | Tagset Cardinality | $$ \vert \mathcal{T} \vert $$ | Number of distinct tags |
| 8 | Stop-word Ratio | $$ r_{sw} = \dfrac{N_{sw}}{N_{total}} $$ | Closed-class share |
| 9 | Out-of-Vocabulary Rate | $$ OOV = \dfrac{\vert V_{test} \setminus V_{train} \vert}{\vert V_{test} \vert} $$ | Tagger robustness |
| 10 | Transition Probability | $$ P(T_i \mid T_{i-1}) = \dfrac{C(T_{i-1}, T_i)}{C(T_{i-1})} $$ | HMM training |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** Always use `\vert` (or `\mid`) — never the raw pipe symbol `|` — inside LaTeX table cells to avoid breaking the markdown table.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Use-Case of "Other POS" Handling |
|-------|----------------------------------|
| **Search Engines (Google, Bing)** | Stop-word filtering of *the, of, in* improves index efficiency. |
| **Machine Translation (Google Translate)** | Correct preposition choice is the #1 error class in MT. |
| **Chatbots / NLU** | Intent classifiers depend on determiner/pronoun recognition. |
| **Sentiment Analysis** | Adjectives carry 70% of sentiment polarity. |
| **Speech Recognition (ASR)** | Closed-class words disambiguate phoneme sequences. |
| **Legal NLP** | Conjunctions (*"and", "or", "but"*) alter clause scope. |

---

## 2.5 Standard Tagset Reference (Penn Treebank — Frequently Tested)

- **DT** — Determiner (*the, a, this*)
- **JJ / JJR / JJS** — Adjective (base / comparative / superlative)
- **RB / RBR / RBS** — Adverb (base / comparative / superlative)
- **IN** — Preposition / Subordinating conjunction
- **CC** — Coordinating conjunction
- **PRP / PRP\$** — Personal / Possessive pronoun
- **CD** — Cardinal number
- **UH** — Interjection
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bayes' Theorem for POS Disambiguation

**Problem:** The word *"round"* is ambiguous between Adjective (JJ) and Preposition (IN). Compute which tag is more likely given the sentence context.

**Given:** A small training corpus with the following counts:
- Total tokens: $N = 1000$
- $C(round, JJ) = 10$
- $C(round, IN) = 25$
- $C(JJ) = 200$ (occurrences of adjectives)
- $C(IN) = 100$ (occurrences of prepositions)

**Step 1 — Compute the emission (likelihood) for each tag:**

$$P(round \mid JJ) = \frac{C(round, JJ)}{C(JJ)} = \frac{10}{200} = 0.0500$$

$$P(round \mid IN) = \frac{C(round, IN)}{C(IN)} = \frac{25}{100} = 0.2500$$

**Step 2 — Compute the prior (marginal tag probability):**

$$P(JJ) = \frac{C(JJ)}{N} = \frac{200}{1000} = 0.2000$$

$$P(IN) = \frac{C(IN)}{N} = \frac{100}{1000} = 0.1000$$

**Step 3 — Compute the evidence (normalizing constant):**

$$P(round) = P(round \mid JJ) \cdot P(JJ) + P(round \mid IN) \cdot P(IN)$$

$$P(round) = (0.0500 \times 0.2000) + (0.2500 \times 0.1000) = 0.0100 + 0.0250 = 0.0350$$

**Step 4 — Apply Bayes' theorem to obtain the posteriors:**

$$P(JJ \mid round) = \frac{P(round \mid JJ) \cdot P(JJ)}{P(round)} = \frac{0.0100}{0.0350} = 0.2857$$

$$P(IN \mid round) = \frac{P(round \mid IN) \cdot P(IN)}{P(round)} = \frac{0.0250}{0.0350} = 0.7143$$

**Step 5 — Decision (Argmax):**

$$\hat{T} = \arg\max\{0.2857,\ 0.7143\} = IN$$

**Conclusion:** The word *"round"* is more likely to be tagged as a **Preposition (IN)** in isolation, given the corpus statistics.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — HMM Tagger Formulation for a 3-Token Sentence

**Sentence:** *"The quick fox"*

**Step 1 — Define the model components:**
- Tags: $T = \{DT, JJ, NN\}$
- Words: $W = \{w_1, w_2, w_3\} = \{\text{The}, \text{quick}, \text{fox}\}$

**Step 2 — Initial probability:**

$$P(T_1 = DT) = 0.40$$

**Step 3 — Transition probabilities (synthetic):**

$$P(JJ \mid DT) = 0.50, \quad P(NN \mid JJ) = 0.70, \quad P(DT \mid NN) = 0.10$$

**Step 4 — Emission probabilities:**

$$P(\text{The} \mid DT) = 0.80, \quad P(\text{quick} \mid JJ) = 0.60, \quad P(\text{fox} \mid NN) = 0.50$$

**Step 5 — Compute joint probability of the chosen tag sequence $DT \rightarrow JJ \rightarrow NN$:**

$$P(\text{seq}) = P(DT) \cdot P(JJ \mid DT) \cdot P(NN \mid JJ) \cdot P(\text{The} \mid DT) \cdot P(\text{quick} \mid JJ) \cdot P(\text{fox} \mid NN)$$

$$P(\text{seq}) = 0.40 \times 0.50 \times 0.70 \times 0.80 \times 0.60 \times 0.50$$

**Step 6 — Multiply step by step:**

$$P(\text{seq}) = 0.20 \times 0.70 \times 0.80 \times 0.60 \times 0.50$$

$$P(\text{seq}) = 0.14 \times 0.80 \times 0.60 \times 0.50$$

$$P(\text{seq}) = 0.112 \times 0.60 \times 0.50$$

$$P(\text{seq}) = 0.0672 \times 0.50$$

$$\boxed{P(\text{seq}) = 0.0336}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Ambiguity Classes

| Word | Possible Tags | Disambiguating Cue | Most Common Tag (English) |
|------|---------------|--------------------|----------------------------|
| *that* | DT / IN / WDT | Followed by N → DT; by clause → IN/WDT | DT |
| *as* | IN / RB / CC | "as well as" → IN; "as soon" → RB | IN |
| *like* | IN / JJ / VB | "such like" → IN; "I like it" → VB | IN (corpus) |
| *well* | JJ / RB / UH / NN | "very well" → RB; "well!" → UH | RB |
| *round* | JJ / NN / VB / IN / RB | Syntactic position | IN (British), RB (American) |
| *near* | JJ / IN / RB | "near the bank" → IN | IN |

---

## 3.4 Evaluation Metrics Worked Example

Suppose a tagger produces the following on 100 tokens:
- TP (correct tags) = 92
- FP (wrong tags assigned) = 5
- FN (missed correct tags) = 3

$$P = \frac{92}{92 + 5} = \frac{92}{97} = 0.9485$$

$$R = \frac{92}{92 + 3} = \frac{92}{95} = 0.9684$$

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.9485 \times 0.9684}{0.9485 + 0.9684} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.9184}{1.9169} = 0.9583$$

$$\boxed{\text{Accuracy} = 0.92, \quad F_1 = 0.9583}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Taxonomy of "Other Parts of Speech"

The following block-level functional architecture maps the categories discussed, their sub-divisions, and their canonical Penn Treebank tag representations.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Other Parts of Speech] --> B[Open Class Modifiers]
    A --> C[Closed Class Function Words]

    B --> B1[Adjective JJ]
    B --> B2[Adjective Comparative JJR]
    B --> B3[Adjective Superlative JJS]
    B --> C1[Adverb RB]
    B --> C2[Adverb Comparative RBR]
    B --> C3[Adverb Superlative RBS]

    C --> D1[Determiner DT]
    C --> D2[Preposition IN]
    C --> D3[Coordinating Conjunction CC]
    C --> D4[Pronoun PRP]
    C --> D5[Possessive Pronoun PRPDOLLAR]
    C --> D6[Cardinal Number CD]
    C --> D7[Interjection UH]

    B1 --> E1[Example red fast]
    B2 --> E2[Example faster bigger]
    B3 --> E3[Example fastest biggest]
    C1 --> F1[Example quickly very]
    C2 --> F2[Example more quickly]
    C3 --> F3[Example most quickly]
    D1 --> G1[Example the a this]
    D2 --> G2[Example in on at]
    D3 --> G3[Example and but or]
    D4 --> G4[Example he she it]
    D6 --> G5[Example one two three]
    D7 --> G6[Example oh wow alas]
```

---

## 4.2 POS Tagging Pipeline — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Raw Text Input] --> B[Tokenizer]
    B --> C[Token Stream]
    C --> D[Feature Extractor]
    D --> E1[Morphological Features]
    D --> E2[Lexical Features]
    D --> E3[Contextual Window Features]
    E1 --> F[Statistical or Neural Tagger]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Tagged Output Sequence]
    G --> H[Evaluator]
    H --> I1[Accuracy]
    H --> I2[Precision]
    H --> I3[Recall]
    H --> I4[F1 Score]
```

---

## 4.3 Ambiguity Resolution Flow for the Word "round"

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Token round] --> q1{Is preceded by determiner?}
    q1 -- yes --> jj[Adjective JJ a round table]
    q1 -- no --> q2{Followed by noun phrase?}
    q2 -- yes --> prep[Preposition IN round the lake]
    q2 -- no --> q3{Is it a verb with object?}
    q3 -- yes --> vb[Verb VB round up the cattle]
    q3 -- no --> adv[Adverb RB the wheels go round]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note (Mermaid Safeguards Applied):**
> - All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `start`, `q1`, `jj`).
> - No node ID uses reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`).
> - All quoted labels are raw uppercase / lowercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

### Q1. Define "Other Parts of Speech" with suitable examples. How are they distinguished from Nouns and Verbs in computational tagging systems?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The "Other Parts of Speech" comprise the lexical categories that are not principal — namely **Adjectives, Adverbs, Prepositions, Conjunctions, Determiners, Pronouns, Numerals,** and **Interjections**. They differ from Nouns and Verbs in two key ways:
1. **Functional role** — They modify (Adj/Adv), relate (Prep), link (Conj), specify (Det), replace (Pron), or emotively express (Interj) rather than denote entities or actions.
2. **Tagset representation** — In the Penn Treebank tagset they are mapped to tags like **JJ, RB, IN, CC, DT, PRP, CD, UH**, distinct from **NN** (Noun) and **VB** (Verb).
3. **Open vs Closed class** — Most fall into the **closed-class** category (~150–300 words), unlike the open-class Nouns and Verbs.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for distinction in role, 1 mark for tagset examples.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

### Q2. What is the difference between Open-class and Closed-class "Other Parts of Speech"? Give two examples of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Open-class** categories admit new words freely and have large vocabularies. *Examples:* Adjective — *"selfie-worthy"*, Adverb — *"COVID-wise"*.
- **Closed-class** categories have a fixed, small inventory (~150–300 words) and rarely accept new members. *Examples:* Preposition — *"of"*, Determiner — *"the"*.
- The distinction matters in tagging because **closed-class words are highly frequent but highly ambiguous** (e.g., *"as"*, *"like"*), requiring contextual disambiguation, while open-class words benefit from morphological cues.

*(1 mark open, 1 mark closed, 1 mark example + implication.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Module 1 Choice Question]**

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain in detail the categories of "Other Parts of Speech" — Adjectives, Adverbs, Prepositions, Conjunctions, Determiners, Pronouns, and Interjections — with two examples for each and their standard Penn Treebank tags. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Describe the role of these categories in **POS Tagging** using an **HMM-based statistical approach**. Include the relevant mathematical formulation and a small worked example. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

| # | Category | Function | Example 1 | Example 2 | Penn Tag |
|---|----------|----------|-----------|-----------|----------|
| 1 | Adjective | Qualifies a noun | *red* | *intelligent* | **JJ** |
| 2 | Adverb | Modifies V/Adj/Clause | *quickly* | *very* | **RB** |
| 3 | Preposition | Spatial/temporal relation | *in* | *on* | **IN** |
| 4 | Conjunction | Links elements | *and* | *because* | **CC / IN** |
| 5 | Determiner | Specifies noun | *the* | *a* | **DT** |
| 6 | Pronoun | Replaces noun | *he* | *it* | **PRP** |
| 7 | Interjection | Expresses emotion | *oh!* | *alas* | **UH** |

- Adjectives are the largest modifier class, taking comparative (-er) and superlative (-est) forms tagged **JJR / JJS**.
- Adverbs are flexible, often formed with suffix **-ly**; comparative forms tagged **RBR / RBS**.
- Prepositions and subordinating conjunctions share the tag **IN** in Penn Treebank.
- Determiners and pronouns are **closed-class** — small in number, high in frequency.

*(7 marks distributed: 1 mark per category for function, 0.5 mark per category for tagset; 0.5 mark for overall comparison.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**HMM-based POS Tagging for "Other Parts of Speech":**

The Hidden Markov Model treats the **tag sequence** as a hidden Markov chain and the **observed word sequence** as emissions.

The optimal tag sequence is given by Viterbi decoding:

$$\hat{T} = \arg\max_{T_{1:n}} \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(W_i \mid T_i) \cdot P(T_i \mid T_{i-1})$$

- $P(T_i \mid T_{i-1})$ = **transition probability** (which "other POS" tends to follow which).
- $P(W_i \mid T_i)$ = **emission probability** (likelihood of word given the tag).

**Worked micro-example:**

Tags: $T = \{DT, JJ, NN\}$  
Sentence: *"The quick fox"*  

Synthetic parameters:

$$P(DT) = 0.40,\quad P(JJ \mid DT) = 0.50,\quad P(NN \mid JJ) = 0.70$$

$$P(\text{The} \mid DT) = 0.80,\quad P(\text{quick} \mid JJ) = 0.60,\quad P(\text{fox} \mid NN) = 0.50$$

Joint probability of the path $DT \rightarrow JJ \rightarrow NN$:

$$P = 0.40 \times 0.50 \times 0.70 \times 0.80 \times 0.60 \times 0.50 = 0.0336$$

**Role of "Other POS" in HMM:**
- Closed-class words (DT, IN, PRP) have **high transition probabilities** — the model learns patterns like *DT → JJ → NN* very reliably.
- Open-class modifiers (JJ, RB) provide **lexical diversity**, requiring stronger emission modeling.
- Ambiguous words (e.g., *that*, *as*) are resolved by combining high transition priors with contextual emissions.

*(2 marks formulation, 2 marks synthetic data, 2 marks calculation, 1 mark interpretation.)*

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Discuss the **ambiguity challenges** in tagging "Other Parts of Speech" with at least four real examples, and outline the disambiguation strategies used in modern taggers. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Compare the **Penn Treebank** and **Universal Dependencies (UD)** tagsets with respect to their handling of "Other Parts of Speech." Which tagset is more suitable for **multilingual NLP**, and why? **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Ambiguity Examples:**

1. *"that"* — Determiner (*"that book"*) vs. Pronoun (*"I know that"*) vs. Conjunction (*"I said that he left"*).
2. *"like"* — Preposition (*"looks like rain"*) vs. Adjective (*"like manner"*) vs. Verb (*"I like it"*).
3. *"as"* — Preposition (*"as a teacher"*) vs. Adverb (*"as well"*) vs. Conjunction (*"as I said"*).
4. *"well"* — Adjective (*"a well student"*) vs. Adverb (*"performed well"*) vs. Interjection (*"Well!"*).

**Disambiguation Strategies:**

- **Lexical heuristics** — Suffix patterns (*-ly* → RB; *-ous* → JJ).
- **Statistical context modeling** — HMM transition probabilities.
- **Sequence labeling** — CRF, BiLSTM-CRF models.
- **Transformer-based contextual embeddings** — BERT, RoBERTa use sub-word and sentence context.
- **Rule-based post-processing** — TnT, Brills tagger.

*(3 marks examples, 4 marks strategy depth.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

| Aspect | Penn Treebank (PTB) | Universal Dependencies (UD) |
|--------|---------------------|------------------------------|
| Tag count | 45 fine tags | 17 coarse tags |
| Language | English-specific | 100+ languages |
| "Other POS" tags | JJ, JJR, JJS, RB, RBR, RBS, IN, CC, DT, PRP, PRP$, CD, UH | ADJ, ADV, ADP, CCONJ, SCONJ, DET, PRON, NUM, INTJ |
| Granularity | High (separates comparative forms) | Low (single ADJ, ADV) |
| Use case | English deep parsing, Treebank work | Cross-lingual transfer, low-resource NLP |

**Suitability for Multilingual NLP:**  
The **UD tagset** is more suitable because:
1. **Coarse-grained tags** map cleanly across languages (e.g., Hindi *"लेकिन"* and English *"but"* both get **CCONJ**).
2. **Language-independent inventory** enables **cross-lingual POS induction** and **zero-shot transfer**.
3. **Hierarchical extension** allows fine-grained sub-tags (e.g., UD-French) without breaking the 17-tag core.
4. PTB's English-centric design (e.g., 3 forms of Adjective) is redundant in morphologically rich languages.

*(2 marks comparison table, 3 marks reasoning, 2 marks conclusion.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Conflating IN tags** — Students often forget that Penn Treebank assigns *both* prepositions and subordinating conjunctions to **IN**. Always state this explicitly in answers.
> 2. **Mixing open and closed class examples** — Do NOT list *"of"* as an Adjective; it is a Preposition.
> 3. **Skipping comparative/superlative tags** — Forgetting **JJR / JJS / RBR / RBS** costs a full mark.
> 4. **Bayes' theorem confusion** — When asked to compute $P(T \mid W)$, show all four terms: prior, likelihood, evidence, posterior. Skipping the normalizing constant $P(W)$ is the most frequent deduction.
> 5. **Wrong Universal tag names** — In UD, Adjective is **ADJ**, not `JJ`; the Penn name does not transfer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **"Other Parts of Speech"** = Adjective, Adverb, Preposition, Conjunction, Determiner, Pronoun, Numeral, Interjection.
- [x] **Adjective** → modifies noun; tags: **JJ, JJR, JJS**.
- [x] **Adverb** → modifies verb/adj/clause; tags: **RB, RBR, RBS**; often **-ly** suffix.
- [x] **Preposition** → relation marker; tag: **IN** (shared with subordinating conjunctions).
- [x] **Conjunction** → **CC** (coordinating: *and, but, or*); **IN** (subordinating: *because, although*).
- [x] **Determiner** → specifies noun; tag: **DT** (*the, a, this*).
- [x] **Pronoun** → replaces noun; tags: **PRP** (he/she/it), **PRP$** (his/her/its).
- [x] **Numeral** → tag: **CD**.
- [x] **Interjection** → tag: **UH** (*oh!, wow!*).
- [x] **Open-class** = Adj, Adv (infinite vocabulary).
- [x] **Closed-class** = Prep, Conj, Det, Pron, Interj (~150–300 words).
- [x] **Ambiguity hotspots** — *"that"*, *"as"*, *"like"*, *"well"*, *"round"*.
- [x] **POS tagging formula (HMM)** — $\hat{T} = \arg\max \prod P(W_i \mid T_i) P(T_i \mid T_{i-1})$.
- [x] **Bayes' Theorem** — $P(T \mid W) = \dfrac{P(W \mid T) P(T)}{P(W)}$.
- [x] **Evaluation metrics** — Accuracy, Precision, Recall, **$F_1$**.
- [x] **Penn Treebank (45 tags)** vs **Universal Dependencies (17 tags)** — UD is multilingual; PTB is English-fine-grained.
- [x] **Closed-class words** = ~15% of running text but among the **most ambiguous** categories.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phrase Structure
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Phrase Structure

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned)**
> **Phrase Structure** refers to the hierarchical organization of words into nested constituents (phrases) within a sentence. It is the foundational concept of **Phrase Structure Grammar (PSG)** — also known as **Constituency Grammar** — which models sentences as tree structures where smaller grammatical units combine systematically to form larger ones. In computational linguistics, phrase structure is most formally captured through **Context-Free Grammars (CFG)**, consisting of rules of the form `A → B C`.

---

## 1.1 What is a Phrase?

A **phrase** is a syntactic unit consisting of one or more words that functions as a single constituent within a sentence. Every phrase has an internal structure and can typically be moved, replaced, or tested for grammaticality as a unit.

**Key Insight:** A phrase is not just any group of words — it must behave as a **single grammatical unit**. Linguists use **substitution tests** and **movement tests** to identify valid phrases.

| Phrase Type | Abbreviation | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Noun Phrase | NP | *the small black cat* |
| Verb Phrase | VP | *chased the mouse quickly* |
| Prepositional Phrase | PP | *under the table* |
| Adjective Phrase | AP | *extremely happy* |
| Adverb Phrase | AdvP | *very slowly* |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Building Block Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy**
