> Think of a sentence as a **Lego structure**. Just as you cannot build a castle by gluing random Lego pieces together, you cannot build a sentence by stringing random words together. Each Lego brick has a **connection point** (its stud/anti-stud), and only bricks with matching connection points can snap together. Similarly, in phrase structure, words have **syntactic categories** (N, V, P, Adj) and **subcategorization features** that dictate which other words they can combine with. **Phrase Structure Rules are the assembly instructions** for the sentence.

---

## 1.3 The Concept of Constituency

A **constituent** is a group of words that functions together as a single unit within a larger phrase. The key property is that constituents are **non-overlapping** and **exhaustively cover** the sentence.

**Evidence for Constituency (Linguistic Tests):**

1. **Substitution Test**: Replace a word sequence with a single word (*"The small black cat slept"* → *"**It** slept"*). If the sentence remains grammatical, the replaced sequence is a constituent.
2. **Movement Test**: A constituent can be moved as a unit (e.g., fronting: *"The small black cat, I saw ___ yesterday"*).
3. **Coordination Test**: Two constituents of the same type can be joined with *and* / *or*.
4. **Standalone / Fragment Test**: A constituent often forms a grammatical answer to a question.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example from a Standard Computational Linguistics Curriculum**
> Sentence: *"The student read the book."*
> - **The student** → a Noun Phrase (NP) — substitute with *"She"* and the sentence still works.
> - **read the book** → a Verb Phrase (VP) — substitute with *"left"* and the sentence still works.
> - **the book** → an NP inside the VP.

---

## 1.4 Phrase Structure Rules (CFG Productions)

Phrase structure is captured by **production rules** (also called **rewrite rules** or **PS-rules**). The general form is:

$$A \rightarrow \beta$$

where **A** is a non-terminal symbol (a phrase category) and **β** is a sequence of terminal and/or non-terminal symbols.

> **Standard Worked Example — English Fragment Grammar**
>
> - S → NP VP
> - NP → Det N
> - NP → Det Adj N
> - NP → ProperN
> - VP → V
> - VP → V NP
> - VP → V NP PP
> - PP → P NP
> - Det → *the* | *a* | *that*
> - N → *book* | *student* | *mouse*
> - V → *read* | *chased* | *saw*
> - P → *with* | *under* | *on*
> - Adj → *small* | *black* | *clever*
> - ProperN → *John* | *Mary*

These rules generate sentences such as *"The small black cat chased the mouse under the table."*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

---

## 2.1 The Hierarchical Architecture of Phrase Structure

Phrase structure embodies three fundamental principles:

### Principle 1: **Hierarchy**
Sentences are not flat sequences — they have a tree structure in which constituents contain sub-constituents. The sentence *"The dog barked"* has the dog as a unit, distinct from *barked*.

### Principle 2: **Categorization**
Each word belongs to a **lexical category** (also called **part of speech** or **POS**). The major categories are:
- **N** (Noun) — *cat, book, idea*
- **V** (Verb) — *run, think, give*
- **P** (Preposition) — *in, on, with*
- **Adj** (Adjective) — *happy, tall, blue*
- **Adv** (Adverb) — *quickly, very, often*
- **Det** (Determiner) — *the, a, this*
- **Conj** (Conjunction) — *and, or, but*
- **Pron** (Pronoun) — *he, it, they*

### Principle 3: **Complementarity / Recursion**
A phrase can contain another phrase of the **same type** inside it. This is **recursion**, and it is what gives human language its **discrete infinity** — we can build sentences of unbounded length using a finite rule set.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recursion — The Defining Property of Human Language**
> The rule `NP → NP PP` allows an NP to contain an NP inside a PP, which contains an NP, and so on. This is what enables sentences like:
> *"The cat on the mat near the door of the house by the lake slept."*
> Noam Chomsky identified this as a defining feature of natural language grammar.

---

## 2.2 The X-Bar Theory Framework

A more abstract version of phrase structure is **X-bar theory**, which posits a uniform three-level structure for all phrases:

$$XP \rightarrow Spec \quad X'$$
$$X' \rightarrow X \quad Complement$$
$$X' \rightarrow X' \quad Adjunct^*$$

Where:
- **XP** = the maximal phrase (e.g., NP, VP)
- **X'** = the intermediate bar level
- **X** = the head word (N, V, A, P)
- **Spec** = the specifier (e.g., Determiner in NP)
- **Complement** = an obligatory sister of the head
- **Adjunct** = an optional sister of X' (recursive)

This framework reveals the **parallelism** between phrase types — all phrases have the same internal architecture.

---

## 2.3 Heads, Complements, and Modifiers

Every phrase has a **head** — the central word that determines the phrase's syntactic category and many of its semantic properties.

| Element | Definition | Example (in NP *"the red book on the table"*) |
|---|---|---|
| **Head** | The word giving the phrase its category | *book* (N) |
| **Specifier** | A sister of X' (often a Det) | *the* |
| **Complement** | An argument of the head | *—* (this noun has no complement) |
| **Modifier / Adjunct** | Optional information | *red* (Adj), *on the table* (PP) |

> **Key distinction:** **Complements are required by the head**; **adjuncts are optional**. The verb *put* requires an NP and a PP (*"put the book on the table"*) — these are complements. The adverb *quickly* can be added freely — it is an adjunct.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formal Representation | Key Property | Marks Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| Phrase Structure Rule | $A \rightarrow \beta$ | $A$ is a non-terminal, $\beta \in (V \cup \Sigma)^*$ | High |
| Sentence Rule | $S \rightarrow NP \ VP$ | Root of every parse tree | High |
| NP Rule | $NP \rightarrow Det \ N(\mid PP)^*$ | Recursive on the right | High |
| VP Rule | $VP \rightarrow V \ (NP)(PP)^*$ | Subcategorization-driven | High |
| Lexicon | Terminals mapped to categories | $w \rightarrow cat$ | Medium |
| Recursion | $X \rightarrow \alpha \ X \ \beta$ | Generates infinite language from finite rules | High |
| Derivation | Sequence of rule applications | Terminating in a string of terminals | High |
| Parse Tree | Visual derivation structure | Nodes = constituents, leaves = words | Very High |
| X-bar Schema | $XP \rightarrow Spec \ X'$ | Universal phrase structure | Medium |
| Subcategorization | Feature on lexical head | Restricts which complements a verb takes | High |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**
> Phrase structure underpins virtually every modern NLP pipeline: **syntactic parsers** (CKY, Earley, Recursive Neural Networks), **information extraction systems**, **machine translation** (e.g., synchronous CFG), **question answering**, and **grammar checkers**. Without a robust model of constituency, computers cannot reliably determine *who did what to whom* in a sentence — a problem known as **semantic role labeling**.

---

## 2.5 Formal Power of Phrase Structure Grammars

Phrase structure grammars are equivalent to **Context-Free Grammars (CFG)** in the **Chomsky hierarchy**.

**Chomsky Hierarchy (Relevant Levels):**

| Grammar Type | Recognizer | Power | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Regular Grammar | Finite State Automaton | Lowest | *a\*b\** |
| Context-Free Grammar | Pushdown Automaton | **PSG lives here** | *aⁿbⁿ* |
| Context-Sensitive Grammar | Linear Bounded Automaton | Higher | *aⁿbⁿcⁿ* |
| Unrestricted Grammar | Turing Machine | Highest | Any computable language |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Observation**
> English exhibits **context-free** phenomena (*"The cat the dog chased ran away"*) that **cannot be captured by regular grammars**. This empirical fact — first argued by Chomsky in 1956 — is why phrase structure (CFG) is the foundational representation in computational linguistics, not finite-state models.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations, Derivations & Analytical Workbench

---

## 3.1 Deriving a Sentence: A Complete Worked Example

We will derive the sentence: **"The small cat chased the mouse"** using the grammar from Section 1.4.

**Grammar (recap):**
- S → NP VP
- NP → Det Adj N
- VP → V NP
- Det → *the*
- Adj → *small*
- N → *cat* | *mouse*
- V → *chased*

**Step 1 — Initialize with the start symbol S**

We begin with the sentence symbol and expand it.

$$
S
$$

**Step 2 — Apply rule S → NP VP**

The root of any English sentence is an NP followed by a VP.

$$
S \Rightarrow NP \ VP
$$

**Step 3 — Expand NP using rule NP → Det Adj N**

The subject noun phrase is built up from a determiner, adjective, and noun.

$$
S \Rightarrow NP \ VP \Rightarrow Det \ Adj \ N \ VP
$$

**Step 4 — Expand VP using rule VP → V NP**

The verb phrase contains the verb and its direct object NP.

$$
S \Rightarrow Det \ Adj \ N \ VP \Rightarrow Det \ Adj \ N \ V \ NP
$$

**Step 5 — Expand the second NP using NP → Det N**

The object is a simple determiner + noun.

$$
S \Rightarrow Det \ Adj \ N \ V \ Det \ N
$$

**Step 6 — Replace all terminals (lexical insertion)**

We substitute the words using the lexical rules.

$$
S \Rightarrow \text{the} \ \text{small} \ \text{cat} \ \text{chased} \ \text{the} \ \text{mouse}
$$

> **Derivation Complete!** The string **"The small cat chased the mouse"** has been generated.

---

## 3.2 Leftmost vs. Rightmost Derivation

There are two conventional orderings for applying rules:

**Leftmost Derivation:** Always expand the leftmost non-terminal first.

**Rightmost Derivation:** Always expand the rightmost non-terminal first.

Both lead to the same string, but the **sequence of rule applications differs**. For our example:

| Step | Leftmost | Rightmost |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | S | S |
| 1 | NP VP | NP VP |
| 2 | Det Adj N VP | NP V NP |
| 3 | the Adj N VP | NP V Det N |
| 4 | the small N VP | NP V the N |
| 5 | the small cat VP | NP V the mouse |
| 6 | the small cat V NP | Det Adj N V the mouse |
| 7 | the small cat chased NP | the Adj N V the mouse |
| 8 | the small cat chased Det N | the small N V the mouse |
| 9 | the small cat chased the N | the small cat V the mouse |
| 10 | the small cat chased the mouse | the small cat chased the mouse |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** The order of expansion determines the **parse tree** shape. Most parsing algorithms (especially **CKY**) require derivations in a specific order (bottom-up, using the **rightmost derivation in reverse**).

---

## 3.3 Building the Parse Tree

The parse tree for our example sentence, with each rule mapping to a tree fragment:

```
                S
               / \
             NP   VP
            /|    |\
         Det Adj N  V   NP
          |   |  |  |   /  \
        the small cat chased Det  N
                              |   |
                            the mouse
```

**Tree Properties:**
- **Root:** S (the entire sentence)
- **Interior nodes:** Non-terminals (NP, VP, Det, Adj, N, V)
- **Leaves (terminals):** the, small, cat, chased, the, mouse
- **Yield (read off left to right):** the small cat chased the mouse
- **Height:** 4 (distance from root to deepest leaf)
- **Branching factor:** variable (binary or ternary depending on rule)

---

## 3.4 Ambiguity — A Central Problem in Phrase Structure

Phrase structure grammars admit **structural ambiguity** (also called **attachment ambiguity** or **parse ambiguity**), where one string has **multiple valid parse trees**.

**Classic Example:** *"I saw the man with the telescope."*

**Two valid parses:**

| Interpretation | Parse | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | `S → NP VP`, `VP → V NP PP`, `PP → P NP` | The **telescope** is the instrument of seeing |
| **2** | `S → NP VP`, `VP → V NP`, `NP → NP PP`, `PP → P NP` | The **man** is the one with the telescope |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Computational Implication**
> Ambiguity is **exponentially explosive**. A sentence with $n$ prepositional phrases can have up to $C_n$ (Catalan number) distinct parses:
> $$C_n = \frac{1}{n+1}\binom{2n}{n}$$
> For $n = 5$, $C_5 = 42$ parses. For $n = 10$, $C_{10} = 16796$ parses. This is why probabilistic parsers (PCFGs) and statistical disambiguation are essential in real NLP systems.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Common Phrase Structure Rule Patterns

| Rule Pattern | Linguistic Function | Example Instantiation | Recursive? |
|---|---|---|---|
| $S \rightarrow NP \ VP$ | Sentence skeleton | *"Cats sleep."* | No |
| $NP \rightarrow Det \ N$ | Simple NP with determiner | *"the book"* | No |
| $NP \rightarrow Det \ Adj^* \ N$ | NP with modifiers | *"the small red book"* | Yes (Adj) |
| $NP \rightarrow NP \ PP$ | PP-modification of NP | *"the book on the table"* | **Yes** |
| $NP \rightarrow NP \ Conj \ NP$ | Coordination | *"John and Mary"* | No |
| $VP \rightarrow V$ | Intransitive | *"He slept."* | No |
| $VP \rightarrow V \ NP$ | Transitive | *"She read a book."* | No |
| $VP \rightarrow V \ NP \ NP$ | Ditransitive | *"He gave her a gift."* | No |
| $VP \rightarrow VP \ PP$ | PP-modification of VP | *"walked to the store"* | **Yes** |
| $PP \rightarrow P \ NP$ | Prepositional phrase | *"in the house"* | No (NP inside can recurse) |
| $S \rightarrow S \ Conj \ S$ | Sentence coordination | *"He came and he left."* | **Yes** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

## 4.1 Phrase Structure Parse Tree (Mermaid)

Below is the rendered parse tree for the sentence **"The small cat chased the mouse"**:

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1["S"]
    NP1["NP (Subject)"]
    VP1["VP (Predicate)"]
    Det1["Det"]
    Adj1["Adj"]
    N1["N"]
    V1["V"]
    NP2["NP (Object)"]
    Det2["Det"]
    N2["N"]

    S1 --- NP1
    S1 --- VP1
    NP1 --- Det1
    NP1 --- Adj1
    NP1 --- N1
    VP1 --- V1
    VP1 --- NP2
    NP2 --- Det2
    NP2 --- N2

    Det1 -. "the" .-> W1((the))
    Adj1 -. "small" .-> W2((small))
    N1 -. "cat" .-> W3((cat))
    V1 -. "chased" .-> W4((chased))
    Det2 -. "the" .-> W5((the))
    N2 -. "mouse" .-> W6((mouse))

    style S1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style NP1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style VP1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style NP2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Phrase Structure Parsing Pipeline

The following block diagram illustrates the **end-to-end computational pipeline** of how phrase structure is used in a real NLP system:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Sentence] --> B[Tokenizer]
    B --> C[POS Tagger]
    C --> D[Lexicon Lookup]
    D --> E[Grammar Rule Set]
    E --> F[CFG Parser]
    F --> G{Ambiguity?}
    G -->|No| H[Single Parse Tree]
    G -->|Yes| I[Probabilistic Disambiguator]
    I --> J[PCFG or Neural Ranker]
    J --> K[Best Parse Tree]
    H --> L[Syntactic Representation]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Downstream NLP Tasks]
    M --> N1[Information Extraction]
    M --> N2[Machine Translation]
    M --> N3[Question Answering]
    M --> N4[Semantic Role Labeling]

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style L fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.3 X-Bar Theory Architecture Diagram

The uniform internal structure of all phrase types in X-bar theory:

```mermaid
graph TD
    XP["XP (Maximal Phrase)"]
    Spec["Specifier"]
    Xbar["X-bar (Intermediate)"]
    X["X (Head)"]
    Comp["Complement"]
    Adj1["Adjunct (optional)"]
    Adj2["Adjunct (optional)"]

    XP --- Spec
    XP --- Xbar
    Xbar --- X
    Xbar --- Comp
    Xbar --- Adj1
    Adj1 --- Adj2

    style XP fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style Xbar fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style X fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Examples of instantiation:**

| Phrase | Head (X) | Specifier | Complement | Adjunct |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| NP *"the red book"* | *book* (N) | *the* (Det) | — | *red* (Adj) |
| VP *"gave her a book"* | *gave* (V) | — | *her* (NP), *a book* (NP) | *yesterday* (PP) |
| AP *"very happy"* | *happy* (A) | *very* (Adv) | — | — |
| PP *"in the house"* | *in* (P) | — | *the house* (NP) | — |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define phrase structure grammar. With a suitable example, explain the concept of constituency.**

**Model Answer:**

Phrase Structure Grammar (PSG) is a formal linguistic framework that models the internal hierarchical structure of sentences by decomposing them into nested constituents (phrases) using rewrite rules of the form $A \rightarrow \beta$.

**Example:** In the sentence *"The clever student solved the puzzle"*, the words *"The clever student"* form a single Noun Phrase (NP) because:
- It can be replaced by a pronoun: *"She solved the puzzle"*
- It can be moved: *"The clever student, I believe, solved the puzzle"*
- It can be coordinated: *"The clever student and the teacher solved the puzzle"*

These tests confirm that *"The clever student"* is a **constituent** — a syntactic unit behaving as one. The phrase structure rules to derive this NP are:

$$S \rightarrow NP \ VP \qquad NP \rightarrow Det \ Adj \ N \qquad VP \rightarrow V \ NP$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Constituency** is the property of being a well-formed syntactic unit, evidenced by substitutability, movability, and coordinatability.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Explain the role of recursion in phrase structure grammar. Why is this property significant for natural language?**

**Model Answer:**

**Recursion** in phrase structure grammar refers to a rule in which a non-terminal symbol appears on both the left and right side of the same production. It allows a phrase to contain another phrase of the same type, and this can repeat indefinitely.

**Example Rules:**

$$NP \rightarrow NP \ PP \qquad VP \rightarrow VP \ PP \qquad S \rightarrow S \ Conj \ S$$

**Significance for natural language:**

1. **Discrete infinity:** A finite set of rules can generate an infinite number of grammatical sentences. The sentence *"The cat on the mat on the floor in the house by the lake..."* can be extended without bound.
2. **Productivity of language:** Speakers can produce and understand sentences they have never heard before — this is only possible through recursive generation.
3. **Theoretical claim:** As argued by **Chomsky (1957)**, recursion is a defining property that distinguishes natural language from finite-state (regular) systems. It places natural language firmly within the **context-free** class of formal languages in the Chomsky hierarchy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bottom line:** Without recursion, no finite grammar could capture the unbounded creativity of human language.

---

## PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) Discuss the major phrasal categories in English with examples. Explain how each category contributes to the construction of a sentence. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **phrasal category** (or **syntactic category**) groups words that can occupy the same structural position in a sentence. The major phrasal categories in English are:

**1. Noun Phrase (NP)**

The NP functions as a subject, object, or complement in a sentence. It typically contains a noun (head) optionally preceded by determiners, adjectives, and followed by prepositional modifiers.

- Rule: $NP \rightarrow Det \ (Adj)^* \ N \ (PP)^*$
- Example: *"The tall man in the black coat arrived."*

**2. Verb Phrase (VP)**

The VP contains the verb and its complements and modifiers. It expresses the action or state of the sentence.

- Rule: $VP \rightarrow V \ (NP) \ (PP)^* \ (AdvP)^*$
- Example: *"gave the child a toy yesterday"*

**3. Prepositional Phrase (PP)**

The PP consists of a preposition followed by an NP. It functions as an adjunct or complement, expressing location, time, manner, etc.

- Rule: $PP \rightarrow P \ NP$
- Example: *"on the roof"*, *"before sunset"*

**4. Adjective Phrase (AP)**

The AP modifies a noun and contains an adjective as its head, optionally with an intensifier.

- Rule: $AP \rightarrow (AdvP) \ Adj$
- Example: *"extremely intelligent"*, *"very tired"*

**5. Adverbial Phrase (AdvP)**

The AdvP modifies a verb, adjective, or clause, providing information about manner, frequency, or degree.

- Rule: $AdvP \rightarrow (AdvP) \ Adv$
- Example: *"quite remarkably fast"*

**Sentence construction:** These categories combine hierarchically. A declarative sentence in English is built from the **S → NP VP** template, where the NP is a noun phrase functioning as the subject, and the VP is a verb phrase containing the predicate. Modifiers (AP, PP, AdvP) attach to their respective heads, generating an infinitely varied but structurally well-defined set of sentences.

---

**(b) Using a context-free grammar, derive the parse tree for the sentence "The teacher gave the student a book". Show all steps of the leftmost derivation. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Grammar:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& S \rightarrow NP \ VP \\
& NP \rightarrow Det \ N \ \vert \ ProperN \\
& VP \rightarrow V \ NP \ NP \\
& Det \rightarrow \text{the} \\
& N \rightarrow \text{teacher} \ \vert \ \text{student} \ \vert \ \text{book} \\
& V \rightarrow \text{gave}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Leftmost Derivation (step by step):**

| Step | Rule Applied | Resulting String |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | — | $S$ |
| 1 | $S \rightarrow NP \ VP$ | $NP \ VP$ |
| 2 | $NP \rightarrow Det \ N$ | $Det \ N \ VP$ |
| 3 | $Det \rightarrow \text{the}$ | $\text{the} \ N \ VP$ |
| 4 | $N \rightarrow \text{teacher}$ | $\text{the teacher} \ VP$ |
| 5 | $VP \rightarrow V \ NP \ NP$ | $\text{the teacher} \ V \ NP \ NP$ |
| 6 | $V \rightarrow \text{gave}$ | $\text{the teacher gave} \ NP \ NP$ |
| 7 | $NP \rightarrow Det \ N$ | $\text{the teacher gave} \ Det \ N \ NP$ |
| 8 | $Det \rightarrow \text{the}$ | $\text{the teacher gave the} \ N \ NP$ |
| 9 | $N \rightarrow \text{student}$ | $\text{the teacher gave the student} \ NP$ |
| 10 | $NP \rightarrow Det \ N$ | $\text{the teacher gave the student} \ Det \ N$ |
| 11 | $Det \rightarrow \text{the}$ | $\text{the teacher gave the student the} \ N$ |
| 12 | $N \rightarrow \text{book}$ | $\text{the teacher gave the student the book}$ |

**Parse Tree:**

```
                       S
                      / \
                    NP    VP
                   / \    |
                 Det  N   V  NP  NP
                  |   |   |  / \ / \
                the teacher gave Det N Det N
                                |  |  |  |
                              the student the book
```

**Yield (left-to-right read of leaves):** the teacher gave the student the book ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a) What is structural ambiguity? Illustrate with two examples and explain how it arises in phrase structure. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Structural ambiguity** (also called **syntactic ambiguity** or **attachment ambiguity**) occurs when a single sentence has **more than one valid phrase structure parse tree**, leading to two or more distinct meanings derived purely from different constituent structures — not from lexical ambiguity.

**Example 1: PP-Attachment Ambiguity**

Sentence: *"I saw the man with the telescope."*

- **Parse A:** The telescope is the instrument of seeing.
  - $VP \rightarrow V \ NP \ PP$ (PP attaches to VP)
  - Meaning: I used a telescope to see the man.

- **Parse B:** The man possesses the telescope.
  - $VP \rightarrow V \ NP$, and $NP \rightarrow NP \ PP$ (PP attaches to NP)
  - Meaning: I saw a man who had a telescope.

**Example 2: Coordination Ambiguity**

Sentence: *"old men and women."*

- **Parse A:** $NP \rightarrow AP \ N \ Conj \ N$ → "old men" and "women" (only men are old)
- **Parse B:** $NP \rightarrow AP \ (N \ Conj \ N)$ → "old (men and women)" (both men and women are old)

**How it arises:**

Structural ambiguity arises when a grammatical rule allows a constituent to be **attached** to the structure in multiple positions. In CFG terms, two distinct derivation trees (with the same yield string) are both valid under the grammar. This is not a defect of the grammar — it is an inherent feature of natural language, and computational systems must use probabilistic or contextual information to choose the correct parse.

---

**(b) Construct a phrase structure grammar that generates the following sentences. Provide at least 5 rules and demonstrate generation of one example sentence. (7 Marks)**

**Sentences to generate:**
1. *The cat sleeps.*
2. *A dog barked.*
3. *The bird sang a song.*
4. *A cat chased the mouse.*
5. *The boy gave his mother a flower.*

**Model Answer:**

**Proposed Phrase Structure Grammar:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& S \rightarrow NP \ VP \\
& NP \rightarrow Det \ N \ \vert \ Det \ N \ PP \ \vert \ ProperN \\
& VP \rightarrow V \ \vert \ V \ NP \ \vert \ V \ NP \ NP \\
& PP \rightarrow P \ NP \\
& Det \rightarrow \text{the} \ \vert \ \text{a} \ \vert \ \text{his} \\
& N \rightarrow \text{cat} \ \vert \ \text{dog} \ \vert \ \text{bird} \ \vert \ \text{song} \ \vert \text{mouse} \ \vert \ \text{boy} \ \vert \ \text{mother} \ \vert \ \text{flower} \\
& V \rightarrow \text{sleeps} \ \vert \ \text{barked} \ \vert \ \text{sang} \ \vert \ \text{chased} \ \vert \ \text{gave} \\
& P \rightarrow \text{on} \ \vert \ \text{with} \ \vert \ \text{under} \\
& ProperN \rightarrow \text{John} \ \vert \ \text{Mary}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Demonstration — Generating "A cat chased the mouse":**

| Step | Rule | Result |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | — | $S$ |
| 1 | $S \rightarrow NP \ VP$ | $NP \ VP$ |
| 2 | $NP \rightarrow Det \ N$ | $Det \ N \ VP$ |
| 3 | $Det \rightarrow \text{a}$ | $\text{a} \ N \ VP$ |
| 4 | $N \rightarrow \text{cat}$ | $\text{a cat} \ VP$ |
| 5 | $VP \rightarrow V \ NP$ | $\text{a cat} \ V \ NP$ |
| 6 | $V \rightarrow \text{chased}$ | $\text{a cat chased} \ NP$ |
| 7 | $NP \rightarrow Det \ N$ | $\text{a cat chased} \ Det \ N$ |
| 8 | $Det \rightarrow \text{the}$ | $\text{a cat chased the} \ N$ |
| 9 | $N \rightarrow \text{mouse}$ | $\text{a cat chased the mouse}$ |

> **Yield:** A cat chased the mouse ✓ (matches the target sentence)

This grammar captures all five sentences because the rules permit intransitive, transitive, and ditransitive VPs, and the lexicon contains the required words.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Missing terminal replacements:** A frequent error is stopping the derivation before replacing all non-terminals (Det, N, V, etc.) with actual words. The derivation is **incomplete** unless it ends in a string of pure terminals.
> 2. **Forgetting the start symbol:** A derivation must begin with **S** (the sentence symbol). Starting with NP or VP is an automatic zero in many KTU evaluations.
> 3. **Conflating parse tree and derivation:** A derivation is a **sequence of rewrites**; a parse tree is a **tree structure**. Examiners often ask for both — provide them as separate items.
> 4. **Ambiguity oversight:** If a question involves an ambiguous sentence, students often give only one parse and lose 3–4 marks. Always check if PP-attachment, coordination, or scope ambiguity is possible.
> 5. **No recursion shown:** When the question asks for "phrase structure," students must demonstrate at least one **recursive rule** (e.g., $NP \rightarrow NP \ PP$). A non-recursive grammar gets partial credit only.
> 6. **Confusing NP and N:** NP is the **phrase**; N is the **lexical head inside the phrase**. Writing *"the cat is an N"* instead of *"the cat is an NP"* is a textbook mistake costing 1–2 marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Phrase structure** is the hierarchical decomposition of a sentence into nested constituents (NP, VP, PP, AP, AdvP).
- **Phrase Structure Grammar (PSG)** uses rewrite rules of the form $A \rightarrow \beta$ and is mathematically equivalent to a **Context-Free Grammar (CFG)**.
- The **basic English sentence template** is $S \rightarrow NP \ VP$.
- **Constituency tests** (substitution, movement, coordination, fragment) are used to identify valid phrases.
- **Recursion** is the property of rules like $NP \rightarrow NP \ PP$ that allow a finite grammar to generate an infinite language — it is the defining feature of human language.
- **X-bar theory** provides a uniform three-level template $XP \rightarrow Spec \ X' \rightarrow X \ Complement$ for all phrasal categories.
- Every phrase has a **head** that determines its category; phrases can have **specifiers**, **complements** (obligatory), and **adjuncts** (optional).
- **Structural ambiguity** arises when one string has multiple valid parse trees; PP-attachment is the most common type.
- The number of parses for $n$ PP-attachment ambiguities is the **Catalan number** $C_n = \frac{1}{n+1}\binom{2n}{n}$.
- Phrase structure sits at the **context-free** level of the **Chomsky hierarchy** — a level empirically necessary for natural language.
- Practical applications: **parsing**, **information extraction**, **machine translation**, **grammar checking**, **question answering**, **semantic role labeling**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phrase Structure Grammars
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Phrase Structure Grammars

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Phrase Structure Grammar (PSG)** is a formal, generative device introduced in mathematical linguistics to describe the syntactic structure of natural and artificial languages through a finite set of rewriting rules. Formally, it is a **4-tuple mathematical structure** expressed as:

$$G = (N, T, P, S)$$

where:
- $N$ = A finite, non-empty set of **Non-Terminal symbols** (also called *variables* or *syntactic categories*). These represent intermediate phrasal constituents that must be expanded further (e.g., $S, NP, VP, Det$).
- $T$ = A finite, non-empty set of **Terminal symbols** (also called *lexical items* or *words*) such that $N \cap T = \emptyset$ (the two sets are disjoint).
- $P$ = A finite set of **Production Rules** (or *rewriting rules*) of the general form $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$, where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are strings over $N \cup T$.
- $S$ = A distinguished **Start Symbol** belonging to $N$, representing the root of any well-formed derivation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The phrase structure grammar forms the theoretical backbone of **Generative Grammar** and **Context-Free Grammars (CFGs)**, which are cornerstones of both computational linguistics and compiler construction. Mastery of the 4-tuple notation $(N, T, P, S)$ is **mandatory** for all university examinations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Terminology Note:** Phrase Structure Grammars are also referred to as **Constituency Grammars** because they explicitly encode how smaller constituents (phrases) combine to form larger constituents.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Architectural Blueprint" View

Imagine that you are an **architect** designing a complex building. You do not directly pour cement; instead, you first design a **blueprint** that states:
1. *A "Building" consists of a "Foundation", a "Wall Structure", and a "Roof".*
2. *A "Wall Structure" consists of multiple "Bricks" cemented together.*
3. *A "Brick" is a primitive object that cannot be broken down further.*

A Phrase Structure Grammar works **identically**:
- The **Start Symbol** $S$ is your "Building".
- The **Non-terminals** $N$ (like $NP, VP$) are your "intermediate assemblies" (Wall, Roof).
- The **Terminals** $T$ (like `the`, `cat`, `sat`) are your "primitive bricks" — they cannot be expanded further.
- The **Production Rules** $P$ are your "blueprint instructions" that tell you how to assemble components.

> [!TIP]
> **The Key Insight:** PSGs do not just generate strings of words — they generate **hierarchical structures**. Two sentences with identical words but different tree structures can carry entirely different meanings (classic example: *"I saw the man with the telescope"*).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Boundaries

- **Standard Symbols Used in KTU Notation:** $V$ or $V_N$ for total vocabulary, $V_T$ for terminals, $V_N$ for non-terminals.
- **Cardinality Constraints:** $N \geq 1$, $T \geq 1$, $P \geq 1$, $S \in N$.
- **Convention:** Capital letters ($A, B, S$) denote non-terminals; lowercase letters ($a, b, c$) denote terminals; Greek letters ($\alpha, \beta, \gamma$) denote strings over $(N \cup T)$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of a Phrase Structure Grammar

A PSG operates on a string by **successive replacement** (derivation). The process is conceptually broken down into the following logical steps:

1. **Initialization:** The derivation begins with the start symbol $S$ placed on the working tape.
2. **Rule Selection:** At each step, exactly one production rule $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ is selected from $P$ such that the left-hand side $\alpha$ appears as a substring of the current sentential form.
3. **Substitution:** The substring $\alpha$ is replaced by $\beta$, producing a new sentential form.
4. **Termination Check:** The process halts when the sentential form contains **only terminal symbols**. The resulting string belongs to the language $L(G)$.
5. **Language Definition:** The language generated by $G$ is the set of all terminal strings derivable from $S$:

$$L(G) = \{ w \in T^{*} \mid S \Rightarrow^{*} w \}$$

where $\Rightarrow^{*}$ denotes **zero or more derivation steps** (the reflexive-transitive closure of the $\Rightarrow$ relation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Sentential Form vs. Sentence:** A *sentential form* is any string derivable from $S$ (it may still contain non-terminals). A *sentence* is a sentential form containing **only terminal symbols**.

## 2.2 The Chomsky Hierarchy — Classification of PSGs

Noam Chomsky classified grammars into four types based on restrictions on production rules. **This is a high-yield KTU topic.**

| Type | Name | Production Rule Form | Recognizer | Language Class |
|------|------|----------------------|------------|----------------|
| **Type 0** | Unrestricted / Recursively Enumerable | $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$, where $\alpha, \beta \in (N \cup T)^{*}$, $\alpha \neq \epsilon$ | Turing Machine | Recursively Enumerable |
| **Type 1** | Context-Sensitive | $\alpha A \beta \rightarrow \alpha \gamma \beta$, where $A \in N$, $\gamma \neq \epsilon$ | Linear Bounded Automaton | Context-Sensitive |
| **Type 2** | Context-Free Grammar (CFG) | $A \rightarrow \gamma$, where $A \in N$, $\gamma \in (N \cup T)^{*}$ | Pushdown Automaton | Context-Free |
| **Type 3** | Regular Grammar | $A \rightarrow aB$ or $A \rightarrow a$ (right-linear) | Finite Automaton | Regular |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** In the 2024 scheme, **Context-Free Grammars (Type 2)** receive the most attention because they balance expressive power (capable of capturing nested phrase structure) with computational tractability (polynomial-time parsing algorithms exist, e.g., CYK, Earley).

## 2.3 Derivation Strategies

There are two canonical strategies for choosing which non-terminal to expand at each step:

- **Leftmost Derivation ($\Rightarrow_{lm}$):** Always expand the **leftmost** non-terminal first. Used to construct a canonical parse tree for LL parsers.
- **Rightmost Derivation ($\Rightarrow_{rm}$):** Always expand the **rightmost** non-terminal first. Used to construct a canonical parse tree for LR parsers.

> [!TIP]
> **Why does this matter?** A grammar may produce **multiple distinct parse trees** for the same string (ambiguity). Leftmost and rightmost derivations provide a *deterministic canonical form* to compare candidate trees.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formal Expression | Notes |
|---|---------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | Grammar Definition | $G = (N, T, P, S)$ | Standard 4-tuple form |
| 2 | Language Generated | $L(G) = \{ w \in T^{*} \mid S \Rightarrow^{*} w \}$ | Strings of only terminals |
| 3 | Derivation (1-step) | $\alpha \Rightarrow \beta$ | One production applied |
| 4 | Derivation (multi-step) | $\alpha \Rightarrow^{*} \beta$ | Zero or more steps |
| 5 | General Production | $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ | Type 0 (unrestricted) |
| 6 | Context-Sensitive Rule | $\alpha A \beta \rightarrow \alpha \gamma \beta$ | $A$ replaced only in context $\alpha \_ \beta$ |
| 7 | Context-Free Rule | $A \rightarrow \gamma$ | $A \in N$, no context required |
| 8 | Right-Linear Rule | $A \rightarrow aB$ or $A \rightarrow a$ | Regular grammar form |
| 9 | String Length | $\vert w \vert$ | Number of terminal symbols |
| 10 | Ambiguity Check | Multiple parse trees for same $w$ | Indicates ambiguous grammar |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compiler Design:** Every mainstream compiler (GCC, Clang, javac) uses a **Context-Free Grammar** to specify the syntax of the programming language. The **YACC/Bison** tool generates parsers directly from CFG specifications.
- **Natural Language Processing (NLP):** PSGs form the foundation of syntactic parsers, machine translation pre-processing, and information extraction pipelines.
- **Bioinformatics:** Pattern grammars in computational biology use PSG variants to model RNA secondary structure and protein folding patterns.
- **Formal Verification:** Model checkers and theorem provers use grammar specifications to validate system behaviors and protocol correctness.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example: Building a PSG for Simple English

**Problem Statement:** Design a Phrase Structure Grammar that generates the mini-language consisting of sentences of the form *"the cat sat"* and *"the dog ran"*. Then derive the string *"the cat sat"* and show its parse tree.

### Step 1: Identify the Components

We need four components $(N, T, P, S)$:

- **Start Symbol:** $S$ (Sentence)
- **Non-Terminals:** $N = \{S, NP, VP, Det, N, V\}$
- **Terminals:** $T = \{\text{the}, \text{cat}, \text{dog}, \text{sat}, \text{ran}\}$

### Step 2: Enumerate the Production Rules

$$P = \{
S \rightarrow NP \; VP,
\; NP \rightarrow Det \; N,
\; VP \rightarrow V,
\; Det \rightarrow \text{the},
\; N \rightarrow \text{cat} \mid \text{dog},
\; V \rightarrow \text{sat} \mid \text{ran}
\}$$

The symbol $\mid$ denotes **alternation** — equivalent to writing two separate rules.

### Step 3: Perform the Leftmost Derivation for "the cat sat"

We apply productions one at a time, always substituting the leftmost non-terminal:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\Rightarrow NP \; VP \quad \text{(Rule: } S \rightarrow NP \; VP\text{)} \\
&\Rightarrow Det \; N \; VP \quad \text{(Rule: } NP \rightarrow Det \; N\text{)} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{the} \; N \; VP \quad \text{(Rule: } Det \rightarrow \text{the}\text{)} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{the} \; \text{cat} \; VP \quad \text{(Rule: } N \rightarrow \text{cat}\text{)} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{the} \; \text{cat} \; V \quad \text{(Rule: } VP \rightarrow V\text{)} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{the} \; \text{cat} \; \text{sat} \quad \text{(Rule: } V \rightarrow \text{sat}\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

After **6 derivation steps**, we reach a string of pure terminals, so *"the cat sat"* $\in L(G)$.

### Step 4: Verify the Language Generated

By enumerating all combinations:

$$
L(G) = \{ \text{the cat sat},\; \text{the cat ran},\; \text{the dog sat},\; \text{the dog ran} \}
$$

The cardinality is $\vert L(G) \vert = 4$.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Classifying a Given Grammar

**Problem:** Classify the following grammar under the Chomsky Hierarchy.

$$
S \rightarrow aS \mid aB, \quad B \rightarrow bB \mid b
$$

**Step-by-Step Analysis:**

- **Step 1 — Examine LHS of each rule:** All left-hand sides consist of a single non-terminal ($S$ or $B$). This is the **defining characteristic of Context-Free Grammars (Type 2)**.
- **Step 2 — Examine RHS of each rule:** All right-hand sides are of the form "a single terminal optionally followed by one non-terminal". Specifically, $S \rightarrow aS$ has form $A \rightarrow aB$ and $B \rightarrow b$ has form $A \rightarrow a$. This is the canonical form of a **Right-Linear Regular Grammar (Type 3)**.
- **Step 3 — Resolution:** Since the grammar satisfies both Type 2 and Type 3 conditions, we classify it by the **most restrictive** type that applies. **Classification: Type 3 (Regular Grammar).**

$$
\boxed{\text{This grammar is Regular (Type 3), which is a subset of Context-Free (Type 2).}}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Proving Language Membership

**Problem:** Show that the string $w = abba$ is in the language generated by $G$ where $P = \{ S \rightarrow aS \mid bA,\; A \rightarrow bA \mid a \}$.

**Derivation Trace (Leftmost):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\Rightarrow aS \quad \text{(apply } S \rightarrow aS\text{)} \\
&\Rightarrow abA \quad \text{(apply } S \rightarrow bA\text{)} \\
&\Rightarrow abbA \quad \text{(apply } A \rightarrow bA\text{)} \\
&\Rightarrow abba \quad \text{(apply } A \rightarrow a\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** After 4 steps, $S \Rightarrow^{*} abba$. Since $abba \in T^{*}$, we conclude $abba \in L(G)$.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Counting Derivation Length

**Problem:** Given a grammar where the only rule is $S \rightarrow aSb \mid \epsilon$, what is the number of derivation steps required to produce $w = a^{3}b^{3}$?

**Analysis:**

The grammar forces a strict palindrome-like structure: equal numbers of $a$'s and $b$'s, with all $a$'s preceding all $b$'s. The derivation pattern is:

$$
S \Rightarrow aSb \Rightarrow aaSbb \Rightarrow aaaSbbb \Rightarrow aaabbb
$$

For a string $a^{n}b^{n}$:
- We need $n$ applications of the rule $S \rightarrow aSb$.
- We need $1$ final application of the rule $S \rightarrow \epsilon$.

$$
\boxed{\text{Total derivation steps} = n + 1}
$$

For $n = 3$: total steps $= 3 + 1 = 4$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The PSG Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the functional architecture of a Phrase Structure Grammar, showing how the four components $(N, T, P, S)$ interact during the derivation process.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[START SYMBOL S] --> RuleSel[RULE SELECTION ENGINE]
    RuleSel --> ProductionSet[(PRODUCTION RULES P)]
    ProductionSet --> RuleSel
    RuleSel --> WorkingTape[CURRENT SENTENTIAL FORM]
    WorkingTape --> Check{Contains only terminals T star}
    Check -- No --> RuleSel
    Check -- Yes --> Accept[ACCEPTED STRING W]
    Accept --> LangSet[LANGUAGE L paren G paren]

    NPool[(NON TERMINALS N)] -.feeds.-> RuleSel
    TPool[(TERMINALS T)] -.feeds.-> ProductionSet
    SStart[START S belongs to N] -.initializes.-> Start
```

## 4.2 Parse Tree Diagram for "the cat sat"

The parse tree visualizes the hierarchical structure produced by the PSG derivation. Each internal node represents a non-terminal (a rule application), and each leaf node represents a terminal (a final word).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootNodeS["S"] --> nodeNP["NP"]
    rootNodeS --> nodeVP["VP"]
    nodeNP --> nodeDet["Det"]
    nodeNP --> nodeNoun["N"]
    nodeVP --> nodeVerb["V"]
    nodeDet --> termThe["the"]
    nodeNoun --> termCat["cat"]
    nodeVerb --> termSat["sat"]
```

**Tree Interpretation:**
- Root: $S$ (Sentence)
- Level 1: Split into $NP$ (Noun Phrase) and $VP$ (Verb Phrase)
- Level 2: $NP$ decomposes into $Det$ (Determiner) + $N$ (Noun); $VP$ decomposes into $V$ (Verb)
- Leaves: The terminal string `the cat sat`

## 4.3 Chomsky Hierarchy — Type Inclusion Lattice

The following diagram shows the strict subset relationships between grammar types in the Chomsky Hierarchy.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    type0["TYPE 0 UNRESTRICTED"] --> type1["TYPE 1 CONTEXT SENSITIVE"]
    type1 --> type2["TYPE 2 CONTEXT FREE"]
    type2 --> type3["TYPE 3 REGULAR"]
    type0 --> langRE["RECURSIVELY ENUMERABLE LANGUAGES"]
    type1 --> langCS["CONTEXT SENSITIVE LANGUAGES"]
    type2 --> langCF["CONTEXT FREE LANGUAGES"]
    type3 --> langREG["REGULAR LANGUAGES"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** An arrow from $A$ to $B$ means "$A$ is strictly more powerful than $B$" but also "$B$ is a proper subset of $A$." Every Regular language is also Context-Free; every Context-Free language is also Context-Sensitive; and so on.

## 4.4 Derivation Pipeline — Leftmost vs Rightmost

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    initS["S"] --> step1a["NP VP LMD"] & step1b["NP VP RMD"]
    step1a --> step2a["Det N VP LMD"] & step2b["Det N VP RMD after VP first"]
    step2a --> step3a["the N VP"]
    step3a --> step4a["the cat VP"]
    step4a --> finalStr["the cat sat"]
    step2b --> step3b["NP V"]
    step3b --> step4b["Det N V"]
    step4b --> finalStr
```

> [!TIP]
> **Valuable Insight:** Both **LMD (Leftmost Derivation)** and **RMD (Rightmost Derivation)** reach the same final string, but they traverse the parse tree in **opposite directions** — top-down vs. bottom-up. This duality is the foundation of LL vs. LR parser design in compiler theory.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Phrase Structure Grammar with its formal components.
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
A Phrase Structure Grammar (PSG) is a formal mathematical model used to define the syntax of a language by specifying how strings of symbols can be derived from a distinguished start symbol. Formally, it is a 4-tuple:
$$G = (N, T, P, S)$$
where $N$ is the set of non-terminals, $T$ is the set of terminals (with $N \cap T = \emptyset$), $P$ is a finite set of production rules of the form $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$, and $S \in N$ is the start symbol. The language generated is $L(G) = \{ w \in T^{*} \mid S \Rightarrow^{*} w \}$.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Context-Free and Context-Sensitive Grammars.
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Context-Free Grammar (Type 2) | Context-Sensitive Grammar (Type 1) |
|---------|------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| Production Form | $A \rightarrow \gamma$ | $\alpha A \beta \rightarrow \alpha \gamma \beta$ |
| LHS Structure | Single non-terminal | Non-terminal embedded in context |
| Generative Power | Strictly less | Strictly more |
| Recognizer | Pushdown Automaton | Linear Bounded Automaton |
| Example Rule | $S \rightarrow aSb$ | $aSb \rightarrow aaSbb$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Context-Free** with **Context-Sensitive** because of the naming. Remember: in CFG, the LHS is a **single non-terminal with no surrounding context**; in CSG, the LHS contains a non-terminal that is replaced **only when specific left and right contexts** are present.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive PSG Construction and Analysis
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]**

**Statement:**
Consider the following set of English sentences:
- *"She reads a book"*
- *"He writes a letter"*
- *"She reads a letter"*

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]: Design a Phrase Structure Grammar that generates all three sentences. Clearly identify $(N, T, P, S)$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify Terminals (Lexical Items).**
$$T = \{\text{She}, \text{He}, \text{reads}, \text{writes}, a, \text{book}, \text{letter}\}$$

**Step 2: Identify Non-Terminals (Syntactic Categories).**
$$N = \{S, NP, VP, Pronoun, Det, N, V\}$$

**Step 3: Construct Production Rules $P$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Rule 1: } S \rightarrow NP \; VP \\
&\text{Rule 2: } NP \rightarrow Pronoun \mid Det \; N \\
&\text{Rule 3: } VP \rightarrow V \; NP \\
&\text{Rule 4: } Pronoun \rightarrow \text{She} \mid \text{He} \\
&\text{Rule 5: } Det \rightarrow a \\
&\text{Rule 6: } N \rightarrow \text{book} \mid \text{letter} \\
&\text{Rule 7: } V \rightarrow \text{reads} \mid \text{writes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Start Symbol.** $S$ is the start symbol, $S \in N$.

**Summary:** $G = (N, T, P, S)$ as defined above. The grammar is **Context-Free (Type 2)** because all productions have a single non-terminal on the LHS.

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]: Derive the string *"She reads a book"* using a leftmost derivation. Show the corresponding parse tree.

**Model Solution:**

**Leftmost Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\Rightarrow NP \; VP &&\text{(Rule 1)} \\
&\Rightarrow Pronoun \; VP &&\text{(Rule 2)} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{She} \; VP &&\text{(Rule 4)} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{She} \; V \; NP &&\text{(Rule 3)} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{She} \; \text{reads} \; NP &&\text{(Rule 7)} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{She} \; \text{reads} \; Det \; N &&\text{(Rule 2)} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{She} \; \text{reads} \; a \; N &&\text{(Rule 5)} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{She} \; \text{reads} \; a \; \text{book} &&\text{(Rule 6)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total Derivation Steps:** 8.

**Parse Tree:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootS["S"] --> npN["NP"] & vpN["VP"]
    npN --> pronN["Pronoun"]
    pronN --> tShe["She"]
    vpN --> vN["V"] & npN2["NP"]
    vN --> tReads["reads"]
    npN2 --> detN["Det"] & nounN["N"]
    detN --> tA["a"]
    nounN --> tBook["book"]
```

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Alternative — Chomsky Classification and Language Properties
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Modified]**

**Statement:**
Consider the grammar $G$ with the following production rules:
$$S \rightarrow aSb \mid ab$$

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]: Classify $G$ under the Chomsky Hierarchy. Generate the first 4 strings in $L(G)$ and provide a general formula for the language.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Classification Analysis.**
- All LHS symbols are single non-terminals ($S$).
- RHS consists of terminals and at most one non-terminal.
- This is a **Context-Free Grammar (Type 2)** because the LHS is a single non-terminal.
- It is **not regular** because the production $S \rightarrow aSb$ has a non-terminal in the middle of the RHS, violating both right-linear and left-linear forms.

**Classification:** $G$ is a **Context-Free Grammar (Type 2)**.

**Step 2: Generating the First 4 Strings.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{String 1: } & S \Rightarrow ab &&\text{(apply } S \rightarrow ab\text{)} \\
\text{String 2: } & S \Rightarrow aSb \Rightarrow aabb &&\text{(apply } S \rightarrow aSb \text{ then } S \rightarrow ab\text{)} \\
\text{String 3: } & S \Rightarrow aSb \Rightarrow aaSbb \Rightarrow aaaabbb &&\text{(apply twice then base)} \\
\text{String 4: } & S \Rightarrow aSb \Rightarrow aaSbb \Rightarrow aaaSbbb \Rightarrow aaaabbbb &&\text{(apply thrice then base)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: General Formula.**
By induction, the rule $S \rightarrow aSb$ adds one $a$ to the left and one $b$ to the right, while $S \rightarrow ab$ provides the base case of 1 $a$ and 1 $b$. Therefore:

$$
L(G) = \{ a^{n} b^{n} \mid n \geq 1 \}
$$

This is the canonical **non-regular, context-free language**.

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]: Using the Pumping Lemma, prove that $L = \{ a^{n}b^{n} \mid n \geq 1 \}$ is not regular.

**Model Solution:**

**The Pumping Lemma for Regular Languages** states: If $L$ is regular, then there exists a constant $p$ (the pumping length) such that every string $s \in L$ with $\vert s \vert \geq p$ can be decomposed as $s = xyz$ satisfying:
- $\vert xy \vert \leq p$
- $\vert y \vert \geq 1$
- $xy^{i}z \in L$ for all $i \geq 0$

**Proof by Contradiction:**

**Step 1 — Assume for contradiction that $L$ is regular.** Then there exists a pumping length $p$.

**Step 2 — Choose the string $s = a^{p}b^{p}$.** Clearly $s \in L$ and $\vert s \vert = 2p \geq p$.

**Step 3 — Apply the decomposition.** By the Pumping Lemma, $s = xyz$ with $\vert xy \vert \leq p$ and $\vert y \vert \geq 1$.

**Step 4 — Observe the constraint.** Since $\vert xy \vert \leq p$ and $s$ starts with $p$ consecutive $a$'s, the substring $y$ must consist entirely of $a$'s. Therefore $y = a^{k}$ for some $k \geq 1$.

**Step 5 — Pump the string.** Consider $i = 2$, so we pump once more:
$$xy^{2}z = a^{p+k}b^{p}$$

**Step 6 — Check membership.** The pumped string $a^{p+k}b^{p}$ has $p+k$ $a$'s and $p$ $b$'s. Since $k \geq 1$, the counts are unequal, so:
$$a^{p+k}b^{p} \notin L$$

**Step 7 — Contradiction.** The Pumping Lemma requires $xy^{2}z \in L$, but we have shown it is not. This contradicts our assumption that $L$ is regular.

$$
\boxed{\therefore L = \{a^{n}b^{n} \mid n \geq 1\} \text{ is not regular.}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Base Case:** When generating strings, students often apply the recursive rule $S \rightarrow aSb$ repeatedly and forget to terminate with $S \rightarrow ab$. This produces an **infinite non-terminal string** that never becomes a sentence.
> 2. **Confusing LMD and Parse Tree:** Leftmost derivations and parse trees are **not the same thing**. A parse tree is a 2D structure; a derivation is a 1D sequence of sentential forms. Always draw both when required.
> 3. **Pumping Lemma Misapplication:** The pumped substring $y$ must contain at least one symbol ($\vert y \vert \geq 1$). Pumping with $i = 0$ to "remove" characters is also a valid test — use it if $i = 2$ gives you trouble.
> 4. **Omitting the Set Definitions:** When writing $G = (N, T, P, S)$, you **must** explicitly enumerate $N$ and $T$ as sets. Vague descriptions like "the non-terminals are phrase types" will cost 1-2 marks.
> 5. **Mixing up Chomsky Types:** A common error is classifying every CFG as Type 2 but missing that it might also be Type 3 (regular). Always check both — classify by the **most restrictive** type that fits.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PSG is a 4-tuple** $G = (N, T, P, S)$: Non-terminals, Terminals, Productions, Start symbol.
- **Terminals** are the "atomic" symbols (words) that cannot be expanded further.
- **Non-terminals** are intermediate phrasal categories (NP, VP, S) that must be rewritten.
- **Production rules** are the rewriting instructions; their form determines the grammar type.
- **Derivation** is the step-by-step application of rules starting from $S$ until only terminals remain.
- **A language** $L(G)$ is the **set of all terminal strings** reachable from $S$.
- **Chomsky Hierarchy** has 4 levels: Type 0 (unrestricted) > Type 1 (context-sensitive) > Type 2 (context-free) > Type 3 (regular). Each level is a **proper subset** of the one above.
- **Leftmost Derivation (LMD):** Always replace the leftmost non-terminal.
- **Rightmost Derivation (RMD):** Always replace the rightmost non-terminal.
- **Parse Tree:** A 2D tree showing hierarchical structure; leaves are terminals, internal nodes are non-terminals.
- **Ambiguity:** A grammar is ambiguous if some string has **two or more distinct parse trees**.
- **Pumping Lemma:** The standard tool to **prove a language is not regular**.
- **Real-World Tie-in:** Compiler parsers (YACC, Bison, ANTLR) are direct industrial applications of CFGs.
- **Key Language:** $L = \{a^{n}b^{n} \mid n \geq 1\}$ is the **canonical non-regular, context-free language** — expect it in exams.
- **Formula for derivation steps** in $S \rightarrow aSb \mid \epsilon$ generating $a^{n}b^{n}$: total steps = $n + 1$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Semantics and Pragmatics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Semantics and Pragmatics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Semantics — Formal Definition

**Semantics** is the branch of linguistics and logic concerned with the study of *meaning*. In the context of computational linguistics, semantics refers to the formal, context-independent representation of the literal meaning conveyed by linguistic expressions — words, phrases, sentences, and discourse. It answers the question: **"What does this expression logically denote or mean?"**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Canonical):** *Semantics* in Computational Linguistics is the systematic study of meaning encoded in language, encompassing (a) **lexical semantics** (meaning of individual lexical items), (b) **compositional semantics** (how meanings combine through syntactic structure), and (c) **truth-conditional semantics** (the conditions under which a proposition is true in a model).

### Intuition / Real-World Analogy

Imagine language as a **calculator**. Each word is a button with a fixed value (semantics = the printed values on buttons), and grammar is the wiring that connects them. Pressing buttons in a particular order (`"The cat chased the mouse"`) yields a numeric result — the sentence's meaning. The *internal logic* of the buttons and wiring is **semantics**. If someone winks and says *"It's cold in here"* — whether they literally mean the temperature or are politely asking you to close the window — that *extra layer of intended meaning*, dependent on context, is **pragmatics**.

## 1.2 Pragmatics — Formal Definition

**Pragmatics** is the study of how context contributes to meaning. It examines what speakers *imply*, *intend*, or *presuppose* beyond the literal semantic content. It addresses the question: **"What is the intended meaning in this particular situation?"**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition (Canonical):** *Pragmatics* is the sub-field of linguistics that interprets meaning in light of **speaker intent, situational context, shared knowledge, and conversational implicature** — the non-literal inferences made during communication.

### Key Distinction Table (KTU High-Yield)

| Aspect | Semantics | Pragmatics |
|---|---|---|
| Level of analysis | Literal / Truth-conditional | Intended / Contextual |
| Dependency on context | Context-free (in classical view) | Heavily context-dependent |
| Core question | What does it *mean*? | What does it *imply* / *impart*? |
| Tools | Predicate logic, Lambda calculus | Speech act theory, Maxims, Inference |
| Output | Proposition / Logical form | Illocutionary force / Implicature |

## 1.3 Why This Distinction Matters in NLP

Computational Linguistics must handle **both** layers:

- **Semantic Processing** is required for tasks like Machine Translation, Question Answering, and Information Extraction where *literal content equivalence* must be preserved.
- **Pragmatic Processing** is required for chatbots, sentiment analysis, dialogue systems, and figurative language understanding where *intent* overrides literal meaning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric:** In MT evaluation, the **BLEU score** measures n-gram overlap (semantic surface fidelity), while **METEOR** and human evaluation often penalize systems that fail to capture pragmatic intent.

## 1.4 The Semantics–Pragmatics Boundary: A Working Model

In modern CL frameworks (e.g., Montague Grammar, Type-Logical Semantics, Discourse Representation Theory), semantics and pragmatics are modeled as a **pipeline**:

$$\text{Surface Form} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Syntactic Structure} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Logical Form (Semantics)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Speech Act \& Implicature (Pragmatics)}$$

Where the **bold** boundary between logical form and speech act marks the **interface** — the most actively researched area in modern CL.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Lexical Semantics — Meaning at the Word Level

Lexical semantics studies the meaning of individual lexical items. The foundational concepts KTU examiners test are:

### 2.1.1 Sense vs. Reference

- **Reference:** The actual entity in the world that a word points to (e.g., the actual dog "Rex").
- **Sense:** The abstract concept / mental representation (e.g., "the idea of a domesticated canine mammal").

> [!NOTE]
> **Frege's Principle (1892):** Two expressions may have the same reference but different senses (e.g., *Morning Star* vs. *Evening Star* both refer to Venus).

### 2.1.2 Lexical Semantic Relations

| Relation | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Synonymy** | Same or near-same meaning | big $\approx$ large |
| **Antonymy** | Opposite meaning | hot / cold |
| **Hyponymy** | Subordinate (is-a) | rose $\subset$ flower |
| **Hypernymy** | Superordinate (umbrella term) | flower $\supset$ rose |
| **Meronymy** | Part-of relation | wheel $\subset$ car |
| **Polysemy** | Multiple related senses of one word | bank (river / financial) |
| **Homonymy** | Same form, unrelated meaning | bat (animal / sports equipment) |

### 2.1.3 Semantic Roles (Thematic Roles)

Verbs predicate events with participants filling specific semantic roles:

| Role | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Agent** | The intentional doer of action | *Ravi* opened the door |
| **Patient / Theme** | The entity undergoing change | *the door* was opened |
| **Instrument** | The means used | with *the key* |
| **Location** | Where the event occurs | in *the room* |
| **Goal / Source** | Direction endpoints | to *Chennai* / from *Delhi* |
| **Beneficiary** | For whom the action is performed | for *Asha* |
| **Manner** | How the action is performed | *quickly* |

## 2.2 Compositional Semantics — Building Sentence Meaning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frege's Principle of Compositionality (1892):** *The meaning of a complex expression is determined by the meanings of its constituent parts and the rules used to combine them.*

This is the **central pillar** of computational semantics. Formally:

$$[\![\phi \;\oplus\; \psi]\!] \;=\; f([\![\phi]\!],\;[\![\psi]\!])$$

Where:
- $[\![\cdot]\!]$ is the semantic interpretation function
- $\oplus$ is a syntactic combination operation
- $f$ is the semantic composition function (e.g., function application)

### 2.2.1 Predicate Logic as the Semantic Representation Language

Each syntactic category maps to a type in **typed lambda calculus** (Montague's insight):

| Syntactic Category | Semantic Type | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Noun (CN) | $\mathbf{e} \to \mathbf{t}$ | $x.\; \text{dog}(x)$ |
| Intransitive Verb (IV) | $\mathbf{e} \to \mathbf{t}$ | $x.\; \text{run}(x)$ |
| Transitive Verb (TV) | $(\mathbf{e} \to \mathbf{t}) \to \mathbf{e} \to \mathbf{t}$ | $y.\;x.\; \text{chase}(x, y)$ |
| Sentence (S) | $\mathbf{t}$ | $\text{run}(a)$ |

Where $\mathbf{e}$ = entity type, $\mathbf{t}$ = truth-value type.

### 2.2.2 Truth-Conditional Semantics

A sentence's meaning is the **set of worlds** in which it is true:

$$[\![\text{S}]\!]_M \;=\; \{\,w \in W : S \text{ is true in } w \text{ under model } M\,\}$$

This is the **Tarski-style** model-theoretic approach adopted universally in formal semantics.

## 2.3 Pragmatics — Theoretical Pillars

### 2.3.1 Speech Act Theory (Austin, 1962; Searle, 1969)

Every utterance performs **three simultaneous acts**:

| Act Type | Definition | Example: *"It's cold here"* |
|---|---|---|
| **Locutionary** | The literal utterance + meaning | Statement that temperature is low |
| **Illocutionary** | The intended function / force | Request to close the window |
| **Perlocutionary** | The effect on the hearer | Hearer feels compelled, closes window |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Searle's Taxonomy of Illocutionary Acts** (must memorize for KTU):
> 1. **Assertives** — state what is the case (claim, assert)
> 2. **Directives** — try to get hearer to do something (request, command)
> 3. **Commissives** — commit speaker to future action (promise, threat)
> 4. **Expressives** — express psychological state (apologize, congratulate)
> 5. **Declarations** — change the world via utterance (declare, pronounce)

### 2.3.2 Grice's Theory of Conversational Implicature

**H. P. Grice (1975)** proposed that conversation is governed by the **Cooperative Principle** and four **maxims**:

| Maxim | Sub-maxims |
|---|---|
| **Quantity** | (i) Make your contribution as informative as required. (ii) Do not make it more informative than required. |
| **Quality** | (i) Do not say what you believe false. (ii) Do not say what you lack evidence for. |
| **Relation** | Be relevant. |
| **Manner** | (i) Avoid obscurity. (ii) Avoid ambiguity. (iii) Be brief. (iv) Be orderly. |

When a speaker **flouts** a maxim, the hearer infers an **implicature** — meaning beyond the literal content.

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Flouting the Maxim of Quantity:**
> A: *"Where is John?"*
> B: *"Somewhere in Paris."*
> B flouts Quantity (Paris is a large city, not informative enough). Implicature: B does not know exactly where John is, or is reluctant to say.

### 2.3.3 Deixis

Words whose meaning is **fixed only relative to the context of utterance**:

| Type | Depends On | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Person deixis** | Speaker / hearer identity | I, you, we |
| **Place deixis** | Location of utterance | here, there |
| **Time deixis** | Time of utterance | now, then, yesterday |
| **Discourse deixis** | Prior / upcoming text | this, that (in discourse) |
| **Social deixis** | Social relation | sir, madam, *tu* / *vous* |

### 2.3.4 Presupposition

A **presupposition** is what a sentence assumes to be true in order for it to be felicitous:

- *"John stopped smoking."* presupposes *John used to smoke*.
- *"The king of France is bald."* presupposes *There is a king of France* (famously triggering Russell's and Strawson's analyses).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formal Statement |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Compositionality | $[\![\phi \oplus \psi]\!] = f([\![\phi]\!], [\![\psi]\!])$ |
| 2 | Truth-Conditional Meaning | $[\![\text{S}]\!]_M = \{w \in W : M, w \models S\}$ |
| 3 | Predicate Type | $\text{IV}: \mathbf{e} \to \mathbf{t}$, $\text{TV}: (\mathbf{e} \to \mathbf{t}) \to \mathbf{e} \to \mathbf{t}$ |
| 4 | Entailment | $S_1 \models S_2 \iff [\![S_1]\!] \subseteq [\![S_2]\!]$ |
| 5 | Contradiction | $S_1 \models \neg S_1$ |
| 6 | Implicature | $\text{Implicature} = \text{Utterance Meaning} - \text{Semantic Content}$ |
| 7 | Speech Act Triad | $\text{Utterance} = \text{Locution} + \text{Illocution} + \text{Perlocution}$ |
| 8 | Presupposition Trigger | $S$ presupposes $p$ iff asserting $\neg S$ still requires $p$ to be true |
| 9 | Deictic Anchoring | $\text{Meaning}(x) = f(x, \text{Speaker}, \text{Time}, \text{Place})$ |
| 10 | Gricean Maxim | $\text{Flout}(\text{Maxim}) \Rightarrow \text{Implicature}$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Application | Semantic Component | Pragmatic Component |
|---|---|---|
| **Machine Translation** | Word alignment, lexical choice | Discourse coherence, register |
| **Chatbots / Dialogue** | Intent classification | Politeness, turn-taking, humor |
| **Search Engines** | Query expansion, synonyms | User intent, query context |
| **Sentiment Analysis** | Polarity of adjectives | Sarcasm, irony, cultural framing |
| **Information Extraction** | Entity-Relation-Event extraction | Coreference, implicit arguments |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Example 1: Building Semantic Representation of a Sentence

**Sentence:** *"Every student read a book."*

We will derive its **predicate logical form (FOL)** step by step, applying **Montague-style compositional semantics**.

### Step 1 — Lexical Semantics Assignment

Assign each lexical item its semantic type and denotation:

| Word | Category | Semantic Type | Denotation |
|---|---|---|---|
| every | Det (general) | $(\mathbf{e} \to \mathbf{t}) \to ((\mathbf{e} \to \mathbf{t}) \to \mathbf{t})$ | $\lambda P.\,\lambda Q.\,\forall x\,(P(x) \to Q(x))$ |
| student | N | $\mathbf{e} \to \mathbf{t}$ | $\lambda x.\, \text{student}(x)$ |
| a | Det (existential) | $(\mathbf{e} \to \mathbf{t}) \to ((\mathbf{e} \to \mathbf{t}) \to \mathbf{t})$ | $\lambda P.\,\lambda Q.\,\exists x\,(P(x) \wedge Q(x))$ |
| book | N | $\mathbf{e} \to \mathbf{t}$ | $\lambda x.\, \text{book}(x)$ |
| read | TV | $(\mathbf{e} \to \mathbf{t}) \to \mathbf{e} \to \mathbf{t}$ | $\lambda Y.\,\lambda x.\,\exists y\,(Y(y) \wedge \text{read}(x, y))$ |

### Step 2 — Syntactic Combination (Bottom-Up)

We compose from leaves upward using **Function Application (FA)**.

**Step 2a:** Combine `a` with `book` (Det + N):
$$\text{Det-NP:} \;\; \lambda Q.\,\exists y\,(\text{book}(y) \wedge Q(y)) \; (\text{Type: } (\mathbf{e} \to \mathbf{t}) \to \mathbf{t})$$

**Step 2b:** Combine the above NP with `read` (NP + TV → VP):
$$\text{VP:} \;\; \lambda x.\,\exists y\,(\text{book}(y) \wedge \text{read}(x, y)) \; (\text{Type: } \mathbf{e} \to \mathbf{t})$$

**Step 2c:** Combine `every` with `student` (Det + N → NP):
$$\text{NP1:} \;\; \lambda Q.\,\forall x\,(\text{student}(x) \to Q(x)) \; (\text{Type: } (\mathbf{e} \to \mathbf{t}) \to \mathbf{t})$$

**Step 2d:** Combine NP1 with VP (NP + VP → S):
$$\text{S:} \;\; \forall x\,(\text{student}(x) \to \exists y\,(\text{book}(y) \wedge \text{read}(x, y)))$$

### Step 3 — Final Logical Form

The fully composed meaning of *"Every student read a book"*:

$$\boxed{\forall x\,(\text{student}(x) \to \exists y\,(\text{book}(y) \wedge \text{read}(x, y)))}$$

This reads: **For every x, if x is a student, then there exists some y such that y is a book and x read y.**

> [!NOTE]
> This is **Scope-Ambiguous** — `every` and `a` can swap, producing the alternative reading "There is one specific book such that every student read it" — a classic KTU exam question on quantifier scope.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Example 2: Deriving Implicature from a Flouted Maxim

**Dialogue:**
> A: *"Will you come to my party?"*
> B: *"I have an exam tomorrow."*

### Step 1 — Determine the Literal Content

B's utterance literally states: **B has an examination scheduled for the following day.**

This is semantically true regardless of any party context.

### Step 2 — Apply the Cooperative Principle

Assume both A and B are cooperative. B's response is **relevant** (Maxim of Relation satisfied if we look hard), but appears to **flout Maxim of Quantity / Relation** by *not* directly answering the yes/no question.

### Step 3 — Compute the Implicature

Grice's reasoning by the hearer:

1. B is cooperative.
2. B did not answer "yes" or "no."
3. If B could say "yes," they would (Maxim of Quantity).
4. Therefore B is unable / unwilling to attend.
5. The implied reason: the exam conflicts with the party.

### Step 4 — Formal Notation

$$ \text{Implicature}(B) = \text{Intent}(B) \setminus \text{Literal Content}(B) $$

Where:
- $\text{Literal Content}(B) = \exists e\,(\text{exam}(e) \wedge \text{tomorrow}(e))$
- $\text{Intent}(B) \approx \neg \text{will-accept-invitation}$

So the **conversational implicature** is: *"No, I cannot attend your party."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distinguish:** This is a **particularized implicature** (depends on context — B having an exam). A **generalized implicature** is one that arises regardless of context (e.g., *"Some of the students passed"* implicates *"Not all"*).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Example 3: Identifying the Speech Act

**Utterance (situation: a formal office, door open, AC on):**
> Colleague A: *"It is quite cold here, isn't it?"*

| Layer | Analysis |
|---|---|
| **Locutionary Act** | Producing a well-formed English sentence with literal meaning "the temperature is low" |
| **Illocutionary Act** | An indirect **Directive** — a polite request to close the door or turn up the temperature |
| **Perlocutionary Act** | Hearer interprets the request, closes the door |

The same utterance could be re-classified:

- In a doctor's clinic → **Assertive** (genuinely stating temperature).
- Spoken with a smile to a friend → **Expressive** (light complaint/joke).

This **context-dependent reclassification** is what pragmatics explains and what classical semantics *cannot*.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: The Semantics–Pragmatics Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Surface Utterance] --> B0[Tokenizer]
    B0 --> C0[Morphological Analyzer]
    C0 --> D0[Parser and Syntactic Tree]
    D0 --> E0[Lexical Semantics Layer]
    E0 --> F0[Compositional Semantics Engine]
    F0 --> G0[Logical Form FOL Representation]
    G0 --> H0[Pragmatic Context Resolver]
    H0 --> I0[Speech Act Classifier]
    I0 --> J0[Implicature and Presupposition Engine]
    J0 --> K0[Final Interpreted Meaning]

    E0 --- E0a[Word Sense Disambiguation]
    E0 --- E0b[Semantic Role Labeler]
    E0 --- E0c[Lexical Entailment Graph]
    H0 --- H0a[Deixis Resolver]
    H0 --- H0b[Coreference Resolver]
    H0 --- H0c[World Knowledge Lookup]
    I0 --- I0a[Searle Five Way Taxonomy]
    J0 --- J0a[Gricean Maxims Checker]
    J0 --- J0b[Presupposition Trigger Detector]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Searle's Taxonomy of Illocutionary Acts

```mermaid
graph TD
    SA[Speech Act] --> SA1[Locutionary Act]
    SA --> SA2[Illocutionary Act]
    SA --> SA3[Perlocutionary Act]

    SA2 --> IA1[Assertives Claim and Assert]
    SA2 --> IA2[Directives Request and Command]
    SA2 --> IA3[Commissives Promise and Threat]
    SA2 --> IA4[Expressives Apologize and Congratulate]
    SA2 --> IA5[Declarations Declare and Pronounce]

    IA1 --> IA1A[Examples: The sky is blue]
    IA2 --> IA2A[Examples: Please close the door]
    IA3 --> IA3A[Examples: I will finish by Friday]
    IA4 --> IA4A[Examples: I am sorry]
    IA5 --> IA5A[Examples: I now pronounce you married]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Grice's Maxims and Implicature Derivation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CP[Cooperative Principle] --> M1[Maxim of Quantity]
    CP --> M2[Maxim of Quality]
    CP --> M3[Maxim of Relation]
    CP --> M4[Maxim of Manner]

    M1 --> M1A[Sub Maxim One: Required Info]
    M1 --> M1B[Sub Maxim Two: Not Excess Info]

    M2 --> M2A[Sub Maxim One: No Falsehoods]
    M2 --> M2B[Sub Maxim Two: No Unsourced Claims]

    M3 --> M3A[Sub Maxim One: Be Relevant]

    M4 --> M4A[Sub Maxim One: Avoid Obscurity]
    M4 --> M4B[Sub Maxim Two: Avoid Ambiguity]
    M4 --> M4C[Sub Maxim Three: Be Brief]
    M4 --> M4D[Sub Maxim Four: Be Orderly]

    M1A --> OUT1[Flouting Yields Implicature]
    M3A --> OUT2[Flouting Yields Implicature]
    M4C --> OUT3[Flouting Yields Implicature]

    OUT1 --> IMP[Conversational Implicature]
    OUT2 --> IMP
    OUT3 --> IMP
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Compositional Semantics Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Lexicon Lookup] --> L2[Type Assignment]
    L2 --> L3[Lambda Abstraction]
    L3 --> L4[Function Application Engine]
    L4 --> L5[Quantifier Scope Resolver]
    L5 --> L6[FOL Formula Builder]
    L6 --> L7[Truth Condition Evaluator]
    L7 --> OUT[Semantic Representation]

    L1 --- W1[Words and Morphemes]
    L4 --- R1[Syntactic Tree Input]
    L6 --- R2[Type Theoretic Constraints]
    L7 --- R3[Model World Mapping]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Reading Note:** In all the above diagrams, arrows indicate *information flow direction*. Boxes with double borders (e.g., `CP`, `SA`) represent *theoretical frameworks*, while single-bordered boxes represent *computational components or sub-concepts*.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution as per KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:**
> - Part A: 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks
> - Part B (Module Internal Choice): 1 question × 14 marks (out of two options) = 14 marks
> - Total per question paper contribution from this topic in full module context varies; the pattern below mirrors KTU ESE valuation style.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**
**Differentiate between Semantics and Pragmatics with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (Expected Length: ~120 words):**

| Parameter | Semantics | Pragmatics |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Study of literal meaning | Study of intended meaning in context |
| Context Dependence | Minimal (literal) | Heavy (situational) |
| Output | Truth conditions / Propositions | Speech acts / Implicatures |
| Example | *"The cat is on the mat"* denotes a cat on a mat | *"The cat is on the mat"* (said to a guest) → hint to remove the cat |

**Semantics** deals with the literal, context-independent meaning of linguistic expressions. For example, the sentence *"It is raining"* semantically means that precipitation is occurring. **Pragmatics**, in contrast, deals with how contextual factors (speaker intent, situation, shared knowledge) influence interpretation. The same sentence, said to someone holding a picnic basket, pragmatically implies *"Let's not go for the picnic."* Thus, while semantics provides the *propositional content*, pragmatics interprets the *communicative intent* behind the utterance.

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**
**Explain the concept of *Presupposition* with two examples.**

**Model Answer:**

A **presupposition** is an implicit assumption that must be true for an utterance to be meaningful or appropriate.

> *Example 1:* *"Riya stopped smoking."*
> Presupposes: *Riya used to smoke.* (This remains true even if we negate: *"Riya did not stop smoking"* still presupposes she once smoked.)

> *Example 2:* *"The present King of France is wise."*
> Presupposes: *There is a present King of France.* (P. F. Strawson's classic example to highlight presupposition failure in non-existent referents.)

**Triggers of Presupposition:** factive verbs (*regret*), change-of-state verbs (*stop*), cleft sentences (*It was John who...*), temporal clauses (*before, after*).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Sub-parts)

> **Instructions (KTU Style):** *Answer one of the following (a) or (b). Each carries 14 marks split as (i) = 7 marks and (ii) = 7 marks.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]**

#### (a) (i) **Explain Frege's Principle of Compositionality. How does Montague Grammar implement it for the sentence *"Some boy loves every girl"*? Derive its logical form.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Compositionality Principle (Frege, 1892):**
The meaning of a complex expression is a *function of* the meanings of its parts and the syntactic rules used to combine them.

$$[\![\alpha \oplus \beta]\!] = f([\![\alpha]\!], [\![\beta]\!])$$

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Lexicon:**

| Word | Type | Denotation |
|---|---|---|
| some | Det | $\lambda P.\lambda Q.\exists x\,(P(x) \wedge Q(x))$ |
| every | Det | $\lambda P.\lambda Q.\forall x\,(P(x) \to Q(x))$ |
| boy | N | $\lambda x.\text{boy}(x)$ |
| girl | N | $\lambda x.\text{girl}(x)$ |
| loves | TV | $\lambda Y.\lambda x.\forall y\,(Y(y) \to \text{loves}(x, y))$ *(under "every" scope)* |

**Composition:**

- (every + girl) $\Rightarrow \lambda Q.\forall y\,(\text{girl}(y) \to Q(y))$
- (loves + (every+girl)) $\Rightarrow \lambda x.\forall y\,(\text{girl}(y) \to \text{loves}(x, y))$
