- (some + boy) $\Rightarrow \lambda Q.\exists x\,(\text{boy}(x) \wedge Q(x))$
- Final S: $\exists x\,(\text{boy}(x) \wedge \forall y\,(\text{girl}(y) \to \text{loves}(x, y)))$

**Final Logical Form:**
$$\boxed{\exists x\,(\text{boy}(x) \wedge \forall y\,(\text{girl}(y) \to \text{loves}(x, y)))}$$

**Reading:** *There exists some boy who loves every girl.*

---

#### (a) (ii) **Discuss the major lexical semantic relations with examples. How does WordNet encode them?** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The major lexical semantic relations in CL are:

1. **Synonymy** — Same/near meaning: *big ≈ large*
2. **Antonymy** — Opposite: *hot / cold*; *tall / short*
3. **Hyponymy / Hypernymy** — IS-A: *rose is-a flower*; *flower hypernym of rose*
4. **Meronymy / Holonymy** — Part-of: *wheel part-of car*
5. **Polysemy** — Multiple related senses: *head* (body / leader)
6. **Homonymy** — Same form, unrelated: *bank* (river / financial)
7. **Troponymy** — Manner relation in verbs: *stroll ⊂ walk*

**WordNet Encoding (George Miller, Princeton, 1995):**
WordNet is a large lexical database of English where:
- **Synsets** (synonym sets) are the basic units representing a concept.
- Relations are encoded as **pointers** between synsets: `~hypernym`, `~hyponym`, `~part_of`, `~antonym`.
- Example entry: `{car, auto, automobile}` → hypernym → `{motor vehicle}`; hyponym → `{sedan, hatchback, ...}`.

**Significance in CL:**
- Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD)
- Query expansion in IR
- Lexical entailment detection
- Knowledge graph construction (modern equivalent: ConceptNet)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### (b) (i) **Explain Grice's Cooperative Principle and his four Maxims of Conversation. Illustrate with two examples how flouting the maxims gives rise to conversational implicature.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**The Cooperative Principle (Grice, 1975):**
*"Make your conversational contribution such as is required, at the stage at which it occurs, by the accepted purpose or direction of the talk exchange."*

**The Four Maxims:**

| Maxim | Content |
|---|---|
| **Quantity** | (1) Give enough info. (2) Do not give too much. |
| **Quality** | (1) Do not lie. (2) Do not assert without evidence. |
| **Relation** | Be relevant. |
| **Manner** | (1) Be clear. (2) Avoid ambiguity. (3) Be brief. (4) Be orderly. |

**Examples of Flouting:**

> **Example 1 (Flouting Maxim of Quantity):**
> A: *"Where did John go?"* B: *"He went to the city."*
> (City is too broad → flouting Quantity → implicature: B doesn't know exactly where.)

> **Example 2 (Flouting Maxim of Relation):**
> A: *"Will you marry me?"* B: *"You are very kind."*
> (Irrelevant reply → flouting Relation → implicature: polite refusal.)

**Generalized vs. Particularized Implicature:**
- Generalized: arises by default (e.g., *"Some men came"* → *Not all men came*).
- Particularized: depends on specific context (as in Example 2 above).

---

#### (b) (ii) **Describe Searle's Taxonomy of Illocutionary Acts with one example for each type. Explain the distinction between direct and indirect speech acts.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Searle's Five Categories (1969):**

| # | Category | Function | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Assertives** | Commit speaker to truth of proposition | *"It is raining."* |
| 2 | **Directives** | Get hearer to do something | *"Close the window."* |
| 3 | **Commissives** | Commit speaker to future action | *"I will pay you tomorrow."* |
| 4 | **Expressives** | Express psychological state | *"I congratulate you."* |
| 5 | **Declarations** | Bring about a state of affairs | *"I now pronounce you married."* |

**Direct vs. Indirect Speech Acts:**

- **Direct Speech Act:** The illocutionary force matches the grammatical form (imperative → directive; declarative → assertive). Example: *"Pass the salt."* is literally a directive.

- **Indirect Speech Act:** The literal form does *not* match the intended force. Example: *"Can you pass the salt?"* is grammatically a question, but its illocutionary force is a **directive** (request). The hearer infers the request through:
  1. Literal question content (do you have the ability?).
  2. Contextual reasoning (we are at dinner; the request is most relevant).
  3. The Cooperative Principle (be relevant → assume the most relevant force).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. ❌ Confusing **Searle's** taxonomy with **Austin's** performative/constative distinction — Searle *refined and expanded* Austin.
> 2. ❌ Treating **Implicature** as equivalent to **Presupposition** — implicature is *cancellable*, presupposition is generally *not cancellable* without contradiction.
> 3. ❌ In **compositional semantics**, forgetting that quantifier scope can produce multiple logical forms for one sentence; KTU expects mention of *scope ambiguity*.
> 4. ❌ Writing *"speech act"* without specifying which of the **three sub-acts** (locutionary / illocutionary / perlocutionary) is being discussed.
> 5. ❌ In **lexical semantics**, ignoring **polysemy vs. homonymy** distinction — they look similar but homonyms are *unrelated senses* and polysemous words share a *core meaning*.
> 6. ❌ Providing examples without **formal logical translation** in derivation questions — KTU awards full 7 marks only when both formal + intuitive explanations are present.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST**

- **Semantics = Literal Meaning; Pragmatics = Intended Meaning** — This is the single most-tested distinction.
- **Compositionality** is THE foundational principle of formal semantics: complex meaning ← parts + rules.
- **Montague Grammar** uses **typed lambda calculus** to implement compositionality; memorize type signatures: N→$e\to t$, IV→$e\to t$, TV→$(e\to t)\to e\to t$.
- **Truth-Conditional Semantics**: sentence meaning = set of worlds where it is true. Use **$M, w \models S$** notation.
- **Lexical Semantic Relations**: Synonymy, Antonymy, Hyponymy/Hypernymy, Meronymy, Polysemy, Homonymy — be ready to *classify* any word pair.
- **Semantic Roles** (Agent, Patient, Instrument, Theme, Goal, Source, Beneficiary, Manner, Location) — frequently asked via *"Identify the thematic role of X in Y."*
- **Three Speech Acts** (Austin): Locutionary (what was said), Illocutionary (what was meant), Perlocutionary (what effect occurred).
- **Searle's 5 Illocutionary Categories**: Assertives, Directives, Commissives, Expressives, Declarations — memorize with examples.
- **Grice's Maxims**: Quantity, Quality, Relation, Manner — flouting produces **implicature**.
- **Generalized** implicature is context-free; **Particularized** implicature is context-dependent.
- **Deixis** requires context anchoring: person, place, time, discourse, social.
- **Presupposition** is *triggered* by factives, change-of-state verbs, clefts, *before/after* — and it *survives negation*.
- **WordNet** is the canonical computational resource encoding lexical relations via **synsets + pointers**.
- **Scope ambiguity** in quantifiers ($\forall \exists$ vs. $\exists \forall$) can yield multiple valid logical forms for one English sentence — always mention it when deriving FOL.
- **Model-theoretic semantics** uses the form **$[\![S]\!]_M$**; KTU prefers the symbol pair $[\![\cdot]\!]$ for interpretation brackets.
- For **exam speed**: derive FOL *bottom-up* and *show every composition step* — partial marks are awarded for intermediate results.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Corpus-Based Work
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Corpus-Based Work

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Corpus-Based Work** in computational linguistics refers to a research methodology and empirical approach where linguistic theories, hypotheses, lexical patterns, grammatical structures, and semantic regularities are investigated, validated, or refuted through the systematic analysis of large, machine-readable collections of authentic language data known as **corpora** (singular: *corpus*).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, corpus-based work is positioned as the empirical backbone of modern computational linguistics, sitting at the intersection of **linguistics, statistics, and computer science**. A *corpus* is not merely a collection of texts; it is a **principled, sampled, and often annotated** collection of texts assembled according to explicit design criteria to be representative of a particular language, genre, register, or domain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Spirit):** Corpus-based work involves the construction, annotation, querying, and statistical analysis of corpora to extract linguistic knowledge — including frequency distributions, collocational patterns, concordances, and n-gram statistics — for use in language modelling, lexicography, NLP, and grammar research.

### Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Corpus** | A structured, machine-readable collection of texts assembled for linguistic analysis. |
| **Corpus Linguistics** | The methodology and discipline of studying language through corpora using empirical, quantitative methods. |
| **Concordance** | A list of all occurrences of a search term in a corpus, typically displayed in **Key Word in Context (KWIC)** format. |
| **Collocation** | A habitual, statistically significant co-occurrence of two or more words within a defined span. |
| **Annotation** | The process of adding linguistic metadata (POS tags, lemmas, parse trees, semantic roles) to a raw corpus. |
| **Token** | An individual instance of a word or punctuation mark in a corpus. |
| **Type** | A unique word form in a corpus (vocabulary size). |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Linguist's Observatory** 🔭
> Imagine a traditional astronomer who, centuries ago, could only study the night sky with the naked eye. Then the telescope was invented — suddenly, the astronomer could observe millions of stars systematically. Before the telescope, observations were anecdotal; after it, they became empirical and verifiable.
>
> **A corpus is the telescope of the linguist.** Before corpora, linguists relied on **introspection** (asking native speakers what sounds "grammatical") and on **invented examples** (Chomsky's famous "Colorless green ideas sleep furiously"). These methods are prone to bias and cannot capture the statistical reality of how language is actually used.
>
> With a corpus, a linguist can look at **millions of real sentences** from newspapers, novels, emails, social media, or academic papers and ask: *How often does this word appear? What words does it typically appear near? In what grammatical patterns does it occur?* The answers come from evidence, not intuition.

A second useful analogy: a corpus is like a **database for a hospital**. Just as a hospital database stores thousands of patient records that doctors query to discover patterns (e.g., which drug works best for which condition), a corpus stores millions of language samples that computational linguists query to discover patterns about how language works.

## 1.3 Physical Standards and Standard Metrics

The discipline relies on several widely accepted metrics:

- **Type-Token Ratio (TTR)** — a measure of lexical diversity
- **Words Per Million (WPM / WPM)** — normalised frequency
- **Mutual Information (MI)** — strength of association between two words
- **T-score** — statistical confidence of a collocation
- **Log-Likelihood Ratio (LLR)** — significance testing for collocations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These are the **standard statistical measures** that KTU examiners expect students to know how to compute and interpret in a 14-mark question on corpus-based work.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Pipeline of Corpus-Based Work

Corpus-based work follows a **five-stage operational pipeline** that is examinable in KTU papers:

1. **Corpus Design & Sampling** — Define the population (e.g., "British English of the 21st century"), select texts, ensure representativeness and balance.
2. **Corpus Collection & Digitisation** — Acquire texts, convert to machine-readable format (typically UTF-8 plain text or XML).
3. **Preprocessing & Tokenisation** — Clean the data, split into sentences and tokens, handle case-folding, lemmatisation, POS-tagging.
4. **Annotation (Optional but Common)** — Add linguistic layers (POS, lemma, syntax, semantics, discourse).
5. **Querying & Statistical Analysis** — Generate frequency lists, concordances, collocation tables, n-grams. Apply statistical tests (MI, T-score, LLR).

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind the pipeline:** A linguist cannot trust the result *"word X appears 3 times more than word Y"* unless they know (a) the corpus is representative, (b) the texts are clean, and (c) the counting method is consistent. Each stage of the pipeline is a guarantee of scientific rigour.

## 2.2 Types of Corpora (Examinable Classification)

| Type | Description | Example |
|------|-------------|---------|
| **General / Reference Corpus** | Large, balanced, multi-genre corpus for general linguistic research. | **BNC** (British National Corpus, 100M words), **COCA** (Corpus of Contemporary American English, 1B+ words). |
| **Specialised / Domain Corpus** | Focused on a specific domain or register. | Medical corpus, legal corpus, academic writing corpus. |
| **Monolingual Corpus** | Texts in a single language. | BNC. |
| **Multilingual Corpus** | Texts in multiple languages, each treated separately. | Europarl (parallel corpus of EU proceedings). |
| **Parallel Corpus** | Source texts and their translations aligned sentence-by-sentence. | **Europarl**, **OPUS**. |
| **Comparable Corpus** | Two or more monolingual corpora in different languages on the same topic/domain. | EN-HI comparable news corpora. |
| **Annotated Corpus** | Enriched with linguistic labels (POS, lemma, syntax). | **Penn Treebank**, **Universal Dependencies**. |
| **Monitor Corpus** | Continuously updated to track language change. | **COHA** (Historical). |
| **Learner Corpus** | Texts produced by language learners. | **ICLE** (International Corpus of Learner English). |
| **Diachronic / Synchronic** | Diachronic spans time; synchronic captures a single time-slice. | COCA (synchronic) vs. COHA (diachronic). |

## 2.3 Core Methods of Corpus-Based Analysis

### A. Frequency Analysis
The simplest and most fundamental operation. Counts how many times each word/form occurs in the corpus. Output: a **frequency list**.

### B. Concordancing (KWIC)
Displaying every occurrence of a search term (*node word*) with a fixed window of context to the left and right. Example for the node word **"strong"**:

```
... a [STRONG] argument ...
... felt [STRONG] enough to ...
... economically [STRONG] region ...
```

This is the **bread-and-butter tool** of corpus linguistics, exposed by tools like **AntConc**, **WordSmith Tools**, **Sketch Engine**, and **Lancaster Corpus Toolbox**.

### C. Collocation Extraction
Identifying words that co-occur with the node word **more often than chance**. Statistical measures: MI, T-score, LLR.

### D. N-gram Analysis
Counting sequences of *N* consecutive tokens. Bigrams (2), trigrams (3), 4-grams, etc. Critical for language modelling and machine translation.

### E. Dispersion / Distribution Analysis
Tracking how a word's frequency is distributed across the corpus (e.g., is the word concentrated in one sub-corpus, or evenly spread?). Tool: **DPplot**, **WordSmith dispersion plot**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are the core computational toolkit of corpus-based work.** Memorise the notation, the formula, and the *interpretation* of each measure.

Let $f(w)$ denote the frequency of word $w$ in a corpus of $N$ total tokens.

| # | Measure | Formula | Interpretation |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | **Normalised Frequency (WPM)** | $\text{WPM}(w) = \dfrac{f(w)}{N} \times 1{,}000{,}000$ | Frequency per million words; allows comparison across corpora of different sizes. |
| 2 | **Type-Token Ratio (TTR)** | $\text{TTR} = \dfrac{\vert V \vert}{N}$ | Lexical diversity; ratio of unique types to total tokens. Range: 0 to 1. |
| 3 | **Mean Segment TTR (MSTTR)** | Compute TTR on fixed-size chunks (e.g., 1000 tokens), then average. | Solves TTR's length-sensitivity problem. |
| 4 | **Mutual Information (MI)** | $\text{MI}(w_1, w_2) = \log_2 \dfrac{P(w_1, w_2)}{P(w_1) \cdot P(w_2)}$ | Strength of association between $w_1$ and $w_2$. Higher MI = stronger collocation. |
| 5 | **T-score (Collocation)** | $T = \dfrac{\bar{x} - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}$ | Statistical confidence; \|T\| > 1.96 ⇒ significant at p < 0.05. |
| 6 | **Log-Likelihood Ratio (LLR)** | $\text{LLR} = 2 \sum O_i \ln\!\left(\dfrac{O_i}{E_i}\right)$ | Hypothesis test: observed vs. expected frequencies. LLR > 3.84 ⇒ p < 0.05. |
| 7 | **Bigram Probability (MLE)** | $P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \dfrac{f(w_{i-1}, w_i)}{f(w_{i-1})}$ | Maximum-likelihood estimate of a bigram. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility beyond linguistics:** These same statistical measures power modern NLP systems — language models (n-gram probabilities), speech recognition, machine translation evaluation, and even search-engine ranking (TF-IDF is a close cousin of MI).

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

Corpus-based work is the invisible engine behind:
- **Search engines** (Google's n-gram models, query expansion).
- **Machine translation** (training parallel corpora).
- **Speech recognition** (language model probabilities).
- **Lexicography** (every modern dictionary, including Oxford and Merriam-Webster, is now corpus-driven).
- **Grammar reference works** (e.g., the *Cambridge Grammar of the English Language* is corpus-based).
- **Plagiarism detection, sentiment analysis, and chatbots** — all rely on corpus statistics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1: Mutual Information (MI) for Collocation

**Setup.** Consider a small corpus of $N = 10{,}000$ tokens. We want to determine whether **"strong coffee"** is a genuine collocation or a random co-occurrence.

**Observed frequencies (counts in the corpus):**

- $f(\text{strong}) = 200$
- $f(\text{coffee}) = 100$
- $f(\text{strong coffee}) = 30$ (the joint bigram frequency)

**Step 1 — Compute individual probabilities.**

$$
P(\text{strong}) = \frac{f(\text{strong})}{N} = \frac{200}{10{,}000} = 0.02
$$

$$
P(\text{coffee}) = \frac{f(\text{coffee})}{N} = \frac{100}{10{,}000} = 0.01
$$

**Step 2 — Compute joint probability.**

$$
P(\text{strong coffee}) = \frac{f(\text{strong coffee})}{N} = \frac{30}{10{,}000} = 0.003
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the MI formula.**

$$
\text{MI}(\text{strong, coffee}) = \log_2 \frac{P(\text{strong}, \text{coffee})}{P(\text{strong}) \cdot P(\text{coffee})}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the values.**

$$
\text{MI} = \log_2 \frac{0.003}{0.02 \times 0.01} = \log_2 \frac{0.003}{0.0002}
$$

$$
\text{MI} = \log_2(15) \approx 3.91 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**

> [!NOTE]
> An MI score of **≥ 3 bits** is generally considered strong evidence of a real collocation. Here, MI ≈ 3.91 confirms that "strong coffee" is a meaningful, non-random collocation — i.e., knowing that "strong" appears makes "coffee" 15 times more likely than chance would predict.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2: T-Score for the Same Pair

**Step 1 — Compute the mean frequency of "coffee" per "strong" context.**

Mean co-occurrence per "strong" occurrence:

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{f(\text{strong coffee})}{f(\text{strong})} = \frac{30}{200} = 0.15
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the expected (chance) frequency.**

Expected co-occurrence under independence:

$$
E = f(\text{strong}) \times P(\text{coffee}) = 200 \times 0.01 = 2
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the standard deviation of observed co-occurrences.**

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{E \times \left(1 - P(\text{coffee})\right)} = \sqrt{2 \times (1 - 0.01)} = \sqrt{1.98} \approx 1.407
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the T-score.**

$$
T = \frac{\bar{x} - \mu}{\sigma \,/\, \sqrt{n}} = \frac{f(\text{strong coffee}) - E}{\sigma} = \frac{30 - 2}{1.407} \approx 19.9
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A \|T-score\| of **19.9** far exceeds the critical value of **1.96**, so the collocation "strong coffee" is **statistically significant at p < 0.05** (in fact, p < 0.001). The T-score confirms MI's verdict.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3: Type-Token Ratio (TTR) and the Length Problem

Suppose we have two corpora:

| Corpus | Tokens (N) | Types (\|V\|) | TTR |
|--------|-----------|-------------|------|
| Corpus A (short) | 100 | 80 | 0.80 |
| Corpus B (long)  | 1,000,000 | 50,000 | 0.05 |

**Step 1 — Apply the formula.**

$$
\text{TTR}_A = \frac{80}{100} = 0.80
$$

$$
\text{TTR}_B = \frac{50{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} = 0.05
$$

**Step 2 — The paradox.**

> [!NOTE]
> Corpus B has **vastly more vocabulary** (50,000 types vs. 80) yet a *lower* TTR. This is the **length-sensitivity problem** of TTR — longer texts always look less diverse. Solution: use **MSTTR** (Mean Segment TTR) with fixed window, e.g., 1,000 tokens.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Measure

| Research Question | Best Measure | Why |
|-------------------|--------------|-----|
| How often does a word occur? | Raw frequency / WPM | Direct count. |
| Is this word's frequency unusual? | WPM + comparison to reference corpus | Benchmarking. |
| Are two words a real collocation? | MI, T-score, LLR | Statistical association. |
| Is the collocation "fixed" or "free"? | MI (high) vs. T-score (low) | MI favours low-frequency strong pairs (e.g., "red herring"). |
| How diverse is this text? | MSTTR | Length-insensitive vocabulary richness. |
| How is a word distributed across the corpus? | Dispersion plot | Identifies "spike" terms. |
| What's the probability of the next word? | Bigram MLE | Language modelling. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Corpus-Based Work Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Corpus Design] --> B[Step 2: Collection and Digitisation]
    B --> C[Step 3: Preprocessing and Tokenisation]
    C --> D[Step 4: Linguistic Annotation]
    D --> E[Step 5: Statistical Analysis]
    E --> F[Output: Frequency Lists]
    E --> G[Output: Concordance KWIC Lines]
    E --> H[Output: Collocation Tables]
    E --> I[Output: N-gram Models]
    F --> J[Application: Lexicography, NLP, MT]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
```

## 4.2 Corpus Type Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    root[Corpus] --> mono[Monolingual]
    root --> multi[Multilingual]
    multi --> parallel[Parallel Aligned]
    multi --> comparable[Comparable]
    root --> gen[General or Reference]
    root --> spec[Specialised or Domain]
    root --> ann[Annotated]
    ann --> pos[POS Tagged]
    ann --> syn[Syntactically Parsed]
    ann --> sem[Semantically Tagged]
    root --> mon[Monitor or Diachronic]
    root --> learner[Learner Corpus]
```

## 4.3 Collocation Statistical-Decision Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Corpus Tokens] --> B[Compute Raw Co-occurrence Counts]
    B --> C[Choose Statistic]
    C --> D1[Mutual Information]
    C --> D2[T-Score]
    C --> D3[Log Likelihood Ratio]
    D1 --> E{Threshold Check}
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E -->|MI greater than 3| F[Strong Collocation]
    E -->|Absolute T greater than 1.96| F
    E -->|LLR greater than 3.84| F
    E -->|Below Threshold| G[Not a Significant Collocation]
    F --> H[Add to Collocation Dictionary]
    G --> I[Discard or Re test]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Corpus Query System

```mermaid
    user[User or Researcher] --> query[Query Interface]

    stats --> ts[T-Score Calculator]
    stats --> llr[Log Likelihood Module]

    llr --> display
    freq --> display
    display --> user
```
