<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🎯 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**Define a corpus. Differentiate between a *general corpus* and a *specialised corpus* with one example of each.** (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**
A corpus is a principled, machine-readable collection of authentic texts assembled for linguistic analysis. A **general corpus** is large, balanced, and multi-genre, designed to represent a language as a whole — e.g., the **British National Corpus (BNC)**. A **specialised corpus** is restricted to a particular domain, register, or genre — e.g., a **medical research article corpus**. The key difference is *scope*: general corpora aim for broad representativeness, while specialised corpora aim for depth in a particular subdomain.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
**What is a concordance? Explain KWIC with an example.** (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**
A **concordance** is a list of every occurrence of a search term in a corpus, displayed together with its surrounding context. **KWIC (Key Word In Context)** is the standard display format in which the search term (*node word*) is centred, with a fixed number of characters/words of left context (L1, L2, …) and right context (R1, R2, …). Example for node word **"bank"**:
```
…the river BANK was muddy…   L3 L2 L1 [BANK] R1 R2 R3
…withdrawal at the BANK…      L3 L2 L1 [BANK] R1 R2 R3
…blood BANK in the city…      L3 L2 L1 [BANK] R1 R2 R3
```
KWIC enables the linguist to study the **collocational and grammatical behaviour** of a word empirically.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**, Module-1 long-answer questions carry 14 marks and typically offer an internal choice. The two alternative questions below mirror that structure.

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Collocation Statistics**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Define *collocation*. Explain with formulas how **Mutual Information (MI)** and the **T-score** are used to identify statistically significant collocations in a corpus. (7 marks)

**(b)** Consider a corpus of **N = 50,000 tokens** with the following observed counts: $f(\text{make}) = 500$, $f(\text{decision}) = 150$, $f(\text{make decision}) = 45$. Compute (i) the MI score and (ii) the T-score for the pair *("make", "decision")*. Comment on whether it qualifies as a statistically significant collocation. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Definition.** A *collocation* is a habitual co-occurrence of two or more words within a defined span (typically ±3 to ±5 words) that occurs more often than would be expected by chance.

**Mutual Information (MI).** MI quantifies how much the actual co-occurrence of two words $w_1$ and $w_2$ deviates from what would be expected if they were statistically independent.

$$
\text{MI}(w_1, w_2) = \log_2 \frac{P(w_1, w_2)}{P(w_1) \cdot P(w_2)}
$$

A higher MI score indicates a stronger association. An MI ≥ 3 bits is conventionally taken as evidence of a real collocation. MI is particularly good at identifying *low-frequency, strong* pairs (e.g., *red herring*).

**T-score.** The T-score is a standard hypothesis-test statistic measuring whether the observed co-occurrence deviates significantly from the expected value under independence.

$$
T = \frac{f(w_1 w_2) - E(f(w_1 w_2))}{\sigma}
$$

where $E = f(w_1) \cdot P(w_2)$ and $\sigma = \sqrt{E \cdot (1 - P(w_2))}$. A \|T-score\| > 1.96 indicates significance at the 5% level. T-score is better for *high-frequency* collocations.

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute probabilities.**

$$
P(\text{make}) = \frac{500}{50{,}000} = 0.01
$$

$$
P(\text{decision}) = \frac{150}{50{,}000} = 0.003
$$

$$
P(\text{make decision}) = \frac{45}{50{,}000} = 0.0009
$$

**Step 2 — Compute MI.**

$$
\text{MI} = \log_2 \frac{0.0009}{0.01 \times 0.003} = \log_2 \frac{0.0009}{0.00003} = \log_2(30) \approx 4.91
$$

**Step 3 — Compute T-score.**

$$
E = f(\text{make}) \times P(\text{decision}) = 500 \times 0.003 = 1.5
$$

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{1.5 \times (1 - 0.003)} = \sqrt{1.4955} \approx 1.223
$$

$$
T = \frac{45 - 1.5}{1.223} \approx 35.5
$$

**Step 4 — Comment.**

> [!NOTE]
> **MI ≈ 4.91 bits** (well above the 3-bit threshold) **and** **T-score ≈ 35.5** (vastly above 1.96) both confirm that **"make decision"** is a **highly significant collocation** at p < 0.001. It should be entered into the collocation dictionary.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Corpus Types and Annotation**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Classify corpora along three dimensions: (i) language coverage, (ii) time coverage, (iii) annotation level. Give one named example for each category. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the role of **annotation** in corpus-based work. Explain with examples the difference between **POS-tagging**, **lemmatisation**, and **syntactic parsing** as three common annotation layers. Why is the **Penn Treebank** considered a landmark in corpus linguistics? (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**(i) Language coverage:**
- *Monolingual corpus* — e.g., **BNC** (British National Corpus, 100M words, English only).
- *Parallel corpus* — e.g., **Europarl** (European Parliament proceedings aligned across 21 languages).
- *Comparable corpus* — e.g., English-Hindi newspaper corpora on the same dates.

**(ii) Time coverage:**
- *Synchronic* — captures one time-slice; e.g., **COCA** (1990–present, updated).
- *Diachronic* — spans historical time; e.g., **COHA** (Corpus of Historical American English, 1810s–2000s).

**(iii) Annotation level:**
- *Raw / unannotated* — e.g., the original **Google Books N-grams** raw text.
- *POS-tagged* — e.g., **Brown Corpus** (first POS-tagged corpus, 1960s).
- *Fully parsed* — e.g., **Penn Treebank** (syntactic treebank).

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Role of annotation.** Annotation adds layers of linguistic information to raw text, transforming a corpus from a "bag of words" into a richly structured resource. It enables **higher-level queries** (e.g., "find all passive-voice constructions with a modal auxiliary") and supports the **training of statistical NLP models** (taggers, parsers, NER systems).

**Three annotation layers:**

| Layer | What it adds | Example sentence: *"The cats quickly ate the fish."* |
|-------|-------------|--------------------------------------------------------|
| **POS-tagging** | Part-of-speech label on each token. | The/DT cats/NNS quickly/RB ate/VBD the/DT fish/NN ./PUNCT |
| **Lemmatisation** | Base form (lemma) of each inflected word. | cats → *cat*; ate → *eat*; fish → *fish* |
| **Syntactic Parsing** | Phrase-structure tree or dependency graph. | (S (NP The cats) (ADVP quickly) (VP ate (NP the fish))) |

**Why Penn Treebank is a landmark.** The Penn Treebank (≈ 4.5M words of parsed American English, released 1992–) was the first **large-scale, hand-parsed, syntactically annotated corpus**. It (i) launched the field of **statistical parsing**, (ii) became the de-facto training/evaluation benchmark, and (iii) seeded follow-up projects like **OntoNotes**, **Universal Dependencies**, and modern neural parsers.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — How Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing tokens and types.** Examiners frequently test: *"A corpus of 50,000 tokens contains 5,000 types. What is TTR?"* — students who confuse the two lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply by $10^6$** in WPM (normalised frequency) calculations.
> 3. **Writing MI formula without the $\log_2$ base** — the base matters; KTU expects $\log_2$ (in bits).
> 4. **Threshold memory.** Memorise: MI ≥ 3 ⇒ collocation; \|T\| > 1.96 ⇒ significant; LLR > 3.84 ⇒ significant. Examiners often award a mark just for stating the threshold.
> 5. **Saying "corpus = dataset of texts"** without including the *principled* and *representative* criteria — a corpus is not just any collection; it is **sampled and balanced by design**.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **corpus** is a *principled, machine-readable* collection of authentic texts, **not** a random pile of files.
- ✅ The **five-stage pipeline** is: *Design → Collection → Preprocessing → Annotation → Analysis*.
- ✅ **KWIC** (Key Word In Context) is the canonical display format for concordances.
- ✅ **Tokens** = total running words; **Types** = unique distinct words. **TTR = Types / Tokens**.
- ✅ TTR has a **length-sensitivity problem** — use **MSTTR** to fix it.
- ✅ **WPM** (Words Per Million) is the standard normalised frequency.
- ✅ **MI** measures association strength: $\text{MI} = \log_2 \dfrac{P(w_1, w_2)}{P(w_1) P(w_2)}$; threshold **≥ 3 bits**.
- ✅ **T-score** is a hypothesis test; threshold **\|T\| > 1.96** ⇒ significant at p < 0.05.
- ✅ **LLR** is the most robust collocation test; threshold **> 3.84** ⇒ significant.
- ✅ **MI is best for low-frequency fixed phrases**; **T-score is best for high-frequency collocations**.
- ✅ Key corpus examples: **BNC, COCA, COHA, Europarl, Penn Treebank, ICLE, Google N-grams**.
- ✅ Major tools: **AntConc, WordSmith Tools, Sketch Engine, NLTK, spaCy**.
- ✅ Three annotation layers: **POS-tagging, Lemmatisation, Syntactic Parsing**.
- ✅ The **Penn Treebank** is the landmark annotated corpus that launched statistical NLP.
- ✅ Corpus types by language: **monolingual, multilingual, parallel, comparable**.
- ✅ Corpus types by time: **synchronic vs. diachronic**.
- ✅ Corpus types by audience: **native / learner corpus (e.g., ICLE)**.
- ✅ Always state **assumptions, thresholds, and interpretations** in your KTU answers — partial credit depends on them.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Ambiguity of Language (NLP Challenges)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The Ambiguity of Language (NLP Challenges)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Natural Language Processing (NLP)**, **Linguistic Ambiguity** is formally defined as a property of a linguistic expression (word, phrase, sentence, or discourse) whereby it can be interpreted in more than one distinct and well-formed way, generating multiple valid semantic, syntactic, or pragmatic readings. According to the foundational computational linguistics literature, ambiguity exists when a single surface form (the *signifiant*) maps onto two or more distinct underlying representations (the *signifié*).

For KTU 2024 Scheme assessments, ambiguity is categorized as a primary **core challenge of NLP**, sitting alongside the other classical "AI-complete" problems such as anaphora resolution, coreference, metaphor, and context-dependence. It is the central reason why rule-based and statistical parsers require probabilistic disambiguation frameworks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEADT632 – Module 1):**
> Ambiguity is a foundational problem that motivates almost every downstream NLP task — POS tagging, Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD), parsing, and Machine Translation. A 14-mark KTU question on this topic *will* expect you to classify ambiguity types with examples and explain a disambiguation method.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Signpost with Many Arrows"

Imagine you are standing at a single road signpost in Kerala, and the sign reads: **"Bank."** Your brain must instantly ask — *bank of a river?* or *bank where I deposit money?*

Linguistic ambiguity works identically: a single string of characters (a *word*, *phrase*, or *sentence*) acts like that signpost, and the listener/reader's job — which the NLP system must automate — is to disambiguate which interpretation is correct **given the surrounding context**.

In short:

- **Surface form** = the signpost (the literal string)
- **Multiple readings** = the arrows pointing in different directions
- **Context** = the road you are actually walking on (which arrow is the right one)

## 1.3 Why Ambiguity is the *Hard Problem* of NLP

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** Human languages have an **ambiguity rate of approximately 1 in every 4 sentences** in conversational speech. The average English sentence is estimated to have **3–5 plausible parses** and each content word can have **2–8 dictionary senses** (e.g., WordNet averages ~3 senses per polysemous word).

This is why NLP is hard: the *input* is a highly compressed, ambiguous symbolic stream, and the *output* is a precise, unambiguous logical form that a machine can act upon.

## 1.4 The Five Classical Layers of Ambiguity (KTU Classification)

For KTU 2024 evaluation, ambiguity is officially stratified into the following five layers:

1. **Phonological / Acoustic Ambiguity** — same sound, different meanings
2. **Lexical Ambiguity** — same word, multiple senses (polysemy, homonymy)
3. **Syntactic / Structural Ambiguity** — same sentence, multiple parse trees
4. **Semantic Ambiguity** — same structure, multiple logical meanings
5. **Pragmatic / Discourse Ambiguity** — same utterance, multiple intents based on context

We will treat each one with mathematical/structural rigor in SECTION_2 and SECTION_3.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layer 1 — Phonological (Acoustic) Ambiguity

Occurs when two or more distinct lexical items are pronounced identically but written differently. This is the **homophone** class.

- **Example:** *"Their / There / They're"* — three different words, one identical pronunciation `/ðɛər/`.
- **Engineering utility:** This is the central failure mode of **Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR)** systems. The acoustic model outputs a phoneme lattice; the language model must resolve the lexical choice.

## 2.2 Layer 2 — Lexical Ambiguity (The Most Tested in KTU)

A single **lemma** (dictionary form) carries multiple distinct senses. Subdivided into two KTU-required sub-classes:

### 2.2.1 Homonymy
- **Definition:** Two unrelated meanings accidentally share the same form. They have **different etymological origins**.
- **Example:** *Bank* (river bank) ← Old English *banki* (slope); *Bank* (financial institution) ← Italian *banca* (bench). The two senses are **unrelated**.
- **Mathematical test:** If replacing the word with a near-synonym preserves both senses, the senses are *not* related. Substitution fails.

### 2.2.2 Polysemy
- **Definition:** A single word has multiple *related* senses that evolved metaphorically or metonymically.
- **Example:** *Head* of a person, *head* of a department, *head* of a beer. All share the abstract sense of "topmost / leading part."
- **Mathematical test:** A *regular polysemy* relation exists if sense B can be derived from sense A by a systematic semantic transformation (e.g., CONTAINER-FOR-CONTENTS, ANIMAL-FOR-MEAT).

### 2.2.3 Formal Sub-Taxonomy (KTU Expectation)

| Sub-type | Relationship of Senses | Etymology | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Homonymy | Unrelated | Different | Bat (animal) / Bat (sports) |
| Polysemy | Related (metaphor/metonymy) | Same | Mouth (of face) / Mouth (of river) |
| Homograph | Same spelling, different meaning | Variable | Lead (metal) / Lead (guide) |
| Homophone | Same pronunciation, different spelling | Variable | Right / Write / Rite |

## 2.3 Layer 3 — Syntactic (Structural / Grammatical) Ambiguity

A sentence has **more than one valid constituency parse tree**. Three canonical subtypes are tested in KTU 14-mark questions:

### 2.3.1 Attachment Ambiguity (PP-attachment)
**Sentence:** *"I saw the man with the telescope."*
- **Reading A:** I used the telescope (to see the man) → *PP attaches to verb "saw"*
- **Reading B:** The man had the telescope (I saw him) → *PP attaches to noun "man"*

The Prepositional Phrase (PP) *"with the telescope"* can attach to either the **VP** or the **NP**.

### 2.3.2 Coordination Ambiguity
**Sentence:** *"old men and women."*
- **Reading A:** [old men] and [women] → only men are old
- **Reading B:** old [men and women] → both are old

### 2.3.3 Garden-Path Sentences
**Sentence:** *"The horse raced past the barn fell."*
The reader is tricked into interpreting *raced* as the main verb, only to be forced to re-parse it as a reduced relative clause.

## 2.4 Layer 4 — Semantic Ambiguity

A sentence has one syntactic structure but multiple possible **logical / model-theoretic interpretations**.

### 2.4.1 Quantifier Scope Ambiguity
**Sentence:** *"Every student read a book."*

Formally, this is the ambiguity between:

$$
\forall x \, \exists y : \text{Student}(x) \rightarrow \text{Read}(x, y) \land \text{Book}(y)
$$

(For every student, there exists a *possibly different* book) versus

$$
\exists y \, \forall x : \text{Student}(x) \rightarrow \text{Read}(x, y) \land \text{Book}(y)
$$

(There is *one specific* book that every student read).

### 2.4.2 Anaphoric / Referential Ambiguity
**Sentence:** *"The teacher told the student that he had failed."*
- Who does *he* refer to? The teacher or the student? Co-reference resolution must disambiguate.

## 2.5 Layer 5 — Pragmatic / Discourse Ambiguity

The same utterance conveys different **speech acts** or **intents** based on context.

- **Sentence:** *"Can you pass the salt?"*
- **Surface meaning:** Yes/No question about ability
- **Pragmatic meaning:** A polite imperative request
- This requires **speech act theory** (Austin & Searle) and **intent classification** to resolve.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the following table. It is the single highest-yield artefact for this topic in KTU ESE.

| Ambiguity Layer | Core Question Asked | Formal Locus | Classical Resolution Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| Phonological | Which word was *spoken*? | Acoustic signal | HMM acoustic model + LM |
| Lexical | Which *sense* of the word? | Word-level | Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD) |
| Syntactic | Which *parse tree*? | Sentence structure | Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar (PCFG) |
| Semantic | Which *logical form*? | Lambda-calculus / FOL | Model-theoretic semantics |
| Pragmatic | Which *speech act / intent*? | Discourse context | Intent classification, dialogue state |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Layer of Ambiguity That Breaks It |
|---|---|
| **Machine Translation** (Google Translate) | Lexical + Syntactic |
| **Voice Assistants** (Alexa, Google Assistant) | Phonological + Pragmatic |
| **Search Engines** (Google Search) | Lexical + Semantic |
| **Legal NLP** (contract review) | Pragmatic + Semantic |
| **Medical NLP** (clinical NER) | Lexical (homonymy of "discharge", "positive") |

## 2.8 Why Disambiguation is Non-Trivial — The Sub-Symbolic Argument

Even with $N$ words in a sentence and an average of $k$ parses per word, the total parse space is **exponential**:

$$
\text{Number of parses} \approx \mathcal{O}(c^{N})
$$

where $c$ is the **Chomsky-Schützenberger constant** for the grammar ($c \geq 1$ for an ambiguous grammar). For $N = 20$ words, even with $c = 2$, the number of parses can exceed $2^{20} \approx 10^6$, which is why exhaustive search is infeasible and **probabilistic / neural methods are mandatory**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: The Disambiguation Pipeline

For a 14-mark KTU question, examiners expect a *pipeline diagram* in words. Here is the canonical disambiguation algorithm in five steps:

1. **Tokenization & Morphological Analysis** — split the surface string into tokens and stems. *Removes morphological ambiguity.*
2. **Part-of-Speech (POS) Tagging** — assign a grammatical category to each token. *Partially resolves lexical ambiguity (e.g., "book" as NN vs VB).*
3. **Parsing** — assign a constituency/dependency structure. *Resolves syntactic ambiguity.*
4. **Semantic Role Labeling & WSD** — assign senses and predicate-argument structure. *Resolves lexical + semantic ambiguity.*
5. **Discourse / Pragmatic Resolution** — incorporate world knowledge, prior discourse, and intent. *Resolves pragmatic ambiguity.*

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 1 — Lexical Ambiguity (Polysemy vs Homonymy)

**Problem:** Given the word **"bat,"** classify it as polysemy or homonymy and design a disambiguation rule.

**Step 1: List candidate senses from a lexical resource (e.g., WordNet).**
S1 = flying mammal (noun)
S2 = sports equipment (noun)
S3 = to hit (verb)

**Step 2: Test etymological origin.**
- S1: from Old English *batt* (a club used for beating)
- S2: from Old French *batte* (a piece of wood)
- S3: same as S1/S2, derived from the noun

The animal sense (S1) and the sports sense (S2) share the noun origin; the verb (S3) is denominal. Therefore S1, S2, S3 are **related** via the "instrument-for-action" metonymy.

**Step 3: Apply the substitution test.**
Replace *bat* with a near-synonym in each frame:
- "A *bat* flew over the cave." → synonym *mammal* ✓
- "He swung the *bat*." → synonym *mammal* ✗ (substitution fails)

Because substitution fails, the senses are **distinct dictionary entries** but etymologically related. **Conclusion: Polysemy** (specifically, regular polysemy by metonymy).

**Step 4: Encode in a machine-readable form.**
For KTU, you may use the WordNet lexical matrix:

$$
\text{LEMMA}(\text{bat}) = \{ \text{synset}_1, \text{synset}_2, \text{synset}_3 \}
$$

Each synset has a unique **sense key** $s_i$ and a gloss (definition). The WSD task is to choose $s^*$ such that:

$$
s^{*} = \underset{s_i \in S}{\arg\max} \, P(s_i \mid \text{context})
$$

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 2 — Syntactic Ambiguity (PP-Attachment)

**Problem:** Disambiguate *"I saw the man with the telescope."*

**Step 1: Generate all valid parse trees.**
The prepositional phrase *with the telescope* can be attached to either the **verb phrase (VP)** or the **noun phrase (NP)**.

**Step 2: Formalize attachment as a binary decision.**
Let:
- $A_V$ = PP attaches to VP
- $A_N$ = PP attaches to NP

We need to choose $A^* = \arg\max \{ P(A_V \mid S), \, P(A_N \mid S) \}$.

**Step 3: Apply Bayes' theorem.**

$$
P(A \mid S) = \frac{P(S \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(S)}
$$

Since $P(S)$ is constant, we compare:

$$
P(A_V \mid S) \propto P(S \mid A_V) \cdot P(A_V)
$$

**Step 4: Lexicalized attachment heuristic (Collins / Ratnaparkhi).**
A simplified log-linear model gives:

$$
\log P(A_V \mid S) \approx \log P(\text{PP} \mid V, \text{head}=V) + \log P(\text{NP} \mid V, \text{head}=\text{man})
$$

**Step 5: Compute probabilities using a treebank (e.g., Penn Treebank statistics).**
Empirically, in the Penn Treebank, P(VP-attachment | verb = *saw*) $\approx 0.71$, and P(NP-attachment | noun = *man*, head = *telescope*) $\approx 0.29$ in this specific sentence. The disambiguator returns:

$$
A^* = A_V \quad \text{(PP attaches to the verb)}
$$

**Conclusion:** Reading A is preferred — "I used the telescope to see the man."

## 3.4 Worked Derivation 3 — Quantifier Scope Ambiguity (Model Theory)

**Problem:** Disambiguate *"Every student read a book."*

**Step 1: Convert to First-Order Logic (FOL) — Reading A.**

$$
\forall x \big[ \text{Student}(x) \rightarrow \exists y \big( \text{Book}(y) \land \text{Read}(x,y) \big) \big]
$$

Step 1.1: Universal quantifier ranges over students.
Step 1.2: Existential quantifier is *inside* the universal scope.
Step 1.3: For each student, a *new* $y$ (book) may be chosen. → **Distributive reading.**

**Step 2: Convert to FOL — Reading B.**

$$
\exists y \big[ \text{Book}(y) \land \forall x \big( \text{Student}(x) \rightarrow \text{Read}(x,y) \big) \big]
$$

Step 2.1: Existential quantifier is *outside*.
Step 2.2: A *single* book $y$ is fixed first.
Step 2.3: Every student read *that same* book. → **Collective / specific reading.**

**Step 3: Difference in truth conditions.**
Let the domain be $\{ s_1, s_2 \}$ (two students) and the books read be $\{ b_1, b_2 \}$.

- If $s_1$ read $b_1$ and $s_2$ read $b_2$ only, Reading A is **TRUE**, Reading B is **FALSE**.
- If both read the *same* book $b_1$, **both** readings are TRUE.

This formal proof is the strongest demonstration in KTU valuation that the two parses are **logically distinct**, not just stylistically different.

## 3.5 Worked Derivation 4 — Probabilistic Disambiguation (Naive Bayes for WSD)

For a content word $w$ with senses $\{s_1, \ldots, s_k\}$ in a context $\mathbf{c} = (c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_m)$:

$$
\hat{s} = \underset{s_i}{\arg\max} \, P(s_i \mid \mathbf{c}) = \underset{s_i}{\arg\max} \, P(s_i) \prod_{j=1}^{m} P(c_j \mid s_i)
$$

Taking the log:

$$
\log P(s_i \mid \mathbf{c}) = \log P(s_i) + \sum_{j=1}^{m} \log P(c_j \mid s_i)
$$

The sense with the **maximum a posteriori (MAP)** probability is selected. This is the foundational equation for classical supervised WSD systems such as the **朴素贝叶斯 (Naive Bayes)** and **Maximum Entropy** classifiers, and it is the same form that neural BERT-based systems approximate with a softmax over sense embeddings.

## 3.6 Worked Derivation 5 — Resolving Pronominal Anaphora (Hobbs' Algorithm — Simplified)

**Sentence:** *"The trophy did not fit in the suitcase because **it** was too small."*

Who is *it*? The trophy or the suitcase?

Hobbs' algorithm (1978) searches the parse tree from left to right and selects the most recent plausible antecedent that satisfies selectional restrictions.

- Antecedent 1: *trophy* (NP1)
- Antecedent 2: *suitcase* (NP2)

The verb *fit* selects for a CONTAINER (suitcase) as its theme, and a CONTAINED-OBJECT (trophy) as its location. Therefore *it* refers to the **suitcase**.

$$
\text{Antecedent}(\text{it}) = \text{suitcase} \quad \text{because} \quad \text{suitcase} \in \text{SEM-CLASS}(\text{Theme}(\text{fit}))
$$

This is the **selectional restriction** rule — a cornerstone of semantic disambiguation that KTU examiners often test.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Five Layers of Linguistic Ambiguity

This is the **canonical KTU exam block diagram** you should reproduce in your answer sheet for a 14-mark question.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Linguistic Ambiguity"] --> B["Phonological Layer"]
    A --> C["Lexical Layer"]
    A --> D["Syntactic Layer"]
    A --> E["Semantic Layer"]
    A --> F["Pragmatic Layer"]
    
    B --> B1["Homophones"]
    B --> B2["Example: their there theyre"]
    
    C --> C1["Homonymy"]
    C --> C2["Polysemy"]
    C1 --> C1a["Unrelated senses"]
    C2 --> C2a["Related senses"]
    
    D --> D1["PP Attachment"]
    D --> D2["Coordination Scope"]
    D --> D3["Garden Path"]
    
    E --> E1["Quantifier Scope"]
    E --> E2["Anaphora Reference"]
    
    F --> F1["Speech Act"]
    F --> F2["Deixis Context"]
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Disambiguation Pipeline (Engineering Architecture)

This is the **block-level functional architecture** that maps how a real NLP system processes ambiguous input and resolves each layer progressively.

```mermaid
graph LR
    RawText["Raw Text Input"] --> Tok["Tokenizer"]
    Tok --> Morph["Morphological Analyzer"]
    Morph --> PosT["POS Tagger"]
    PosT --> Parser["Constituency Parser"]
    Parser --> WSD["Word Sense Disambiguator"]
    WSD --> SRL["Semantic Role Labeler"]
    SRL --> Prag["Pragmatic Resolver"]
    Prag --> LF["Logical Form Output"]
    
    PosT -.->|Resolves Layer 3 partial| Parser
    Parser -.->|Resolves Layer 3 full| WSD
    WSD -.->|Resolves Layer 2| SRL
    SRL -.->|Resolves Layer 4| Prag
    Prag -.->|Resolves Layer 5| LF
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Decision Flow for WSD

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start["Input: word w in context c"] --> Dict["Lookup w in Lexical Resource"]
    Dict --> Senses["Get Sense Set S equals s1 s2 ... sk"]
    Senses --> Context["Extract Context Features from c"]
    Context --> Bayes["Compute P si given c for all senses"]
    Bayes --> Max["Select sense with maximum probability"]
    Max --> Output["Output: Disambiguated Sense s star"]
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Syntactic Disambiguation of PP-Attachment

```mermaid
graph TD
    S["Sentence: I saw the man with the telescope"] --> V1["VP-attachment"]
    S --> V2["NP-attachment"]
    V1 --> R1["I used the telescope to see the man"]
    V2 --> R2["I saw the man who had the telescope"]
    V1 --> P1["P(VP attach) approx 0.71"]
    V2 --> P2["P(NP attach) approx 0.29"]
    P1 --> Decision["Winner: VP-attachment"]
    P2 --> Decision
```

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Quantifier Scope Tree (Reading A vs Reading B)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q["Sentence: Every student read a book"] --> RA["Reading A: Distributive"]
    Q --> RB["Reading B: Collective"]
    RA --> RA1["FORALL x Student x implies EXISTS y Book y and Read x y"]
    RB --> RB1["EXISTS y Book y and FORALL x Student x implies Read x y"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> These five diagrams collectively map every layer of ambiguity to a concrete engineering module. KTU examiners reward students who include such structural diagrams in their 14-mark answers — they are explicit visual proof of understanding, and the mark allocation typically reserves **2–3 marks** for clarity of representation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 1]`
**Q: Define linguistic ambiguity. Differentiate between homonymy and polysemy with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Linguistic ambiguity is the property of a linguistic expression admitting more than one valid interpretation. **Homonymy** refers to words with the *same form* but *unrelated meanings* and *different etymologies* (e.g., *bank* — river bank vs financial bank). **Polysemy** refers to a single word with *multiple related senses* derived through metonymy or metaphor (e.g., *head* — of a person, of a department, of a beer). The substitution test fails for homonyms but succeeds for polysemous senses that share a common semantic core. (3 marks)

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: What is syntactic ambiguity? Explain PP-attachment with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Syntactic ambiguity arises when a sentence can be parsed into more than one valid grammatical structure. **PP-attachment** occurs when a prepositional phrase (PP) can syntactically attach to either a verb phrase or a noun phrase. Example: *"I saw the man with the telescope"* — the PP *with the telescope* can attach to the verb *saw* (I used the telescope) or to the noun *man* (the man possessed the telescope). Statistical parsers resolve this using lexicalized attachment probabilities. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2]`

**Q (a) [7 marks]:** Classify the different types of linguistic ambiguity with suitable examples. Why is ambiguity considered a fundamental challenge in NLP?

**Model Answer (7 marks):**
Linguistic ambiguity is classified into **five layers**:

1. **Phonological Ambiguity** — Same sound, different words. Example: *right / write / rite*.
2. **Lexical Ambiguity** — Same word, multiple senses. Subdivided into *homonymy* (e.g., *bat* — animal vs sports equipment) and *polysemy* (e.g., *mouth* — of face vs of river).
3. **Syntactic Ambiguity** — Same sentence, multiple parse trees. Example: *"old men and women"* (coordination scope ambiguity).
4. **Semantic Ambiguity** — Same structure, multiple logical meanings. Example: *"Every student read a book"* (quantifier scope).
5. **Pragmatic Ambiguity** — Same utterance, different speech acts. Example: *"Can you pass the salt?"* — question of ability vs polite imperative.

**Why it is fundamental to NLP:** A natural language sentence is a *compressed symbolic stream* that maps to an exponential space of interpretations. The number of parses grows as $\mathcal{O}(c^N)$ for an N-word sentence, making exhaustive enumeration infeasible. Without disambiguation, downstream tasks (translation, Q&A, summarization) inherit compounded errors. Hence, every NLP pipeline must incorporate probabilistic and contextual models to resolve ambiguity at each layer. (7 marks)

**Q (b) [7 marks]:** Explain Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD). Describe the Naive Bayes approach to WSD with its mathematical formulation.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**
**Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD)** is the task of determining the correct sense of a polysemous word in a given context. It is the canonical solution to *lexical ambiguity*.

**Naive Bayes formulation:**
Given a word $w$ with senses $\{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_k\}$ and a context $\mathbf{c} = (c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_m)$ (surrounding words/features), the optimal sense is:

$$
\hat{s} = \underset{s_i}{\arg\max} \, P(s_i \mid \mathbf{c})
$$

Applying Bayes' theorem and assuming conditional independence of context features:

$$
\hat{s} = \underset{s_i}{\arg\max} \, P(s_i) \prod_{j=1}^{m} P(c_j \mid s_i)
$$

Taking logarithms for numerical stability:

$$
\log \hat{s} = \underset{s_i}{\arg\max} \left[ \log P(s_i) + \sum_{j=1}^{m} \log P(c_j \mid s_i) \right]
$$

The prior $P(s_i)$ is estimated from sense-tagged corpora (e.g., SemCor). The likelihoods $P(c_j \mid s_i)$ are estimated as relative frequencies of feature $c_j$ co-occurring with sense $s_i$. The sense with maximum a posteriori (MAP) probability is selected. Limitations: the conditional independence assumption is unrealistic, motivating modern neural (BERT-based) WSD systems. (7 marks)

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 3]`

**Q (a) [7 marks]:** With a suitable example, explain quantifier scope ambiguity. How is it represented in First-Order Logic (FOL)?

**Model Answer (7 marks):**
**Quantifier scope ambiguity** arises when a sentence contains two or more quantified noun phrases whose relative scope (which quantifier binds which variable first) is syntactically underdetermined.

**Example:** *"Every student read a book."*
This sentence admits two distinct logical readings.

**Reading A (Distributive — ∀ > ∃):**

$$
\forall x \big[ \text{Student}(x) \rightarrow \exists y \big( \text{Book}(y) \land \text{Read}(x, y) \big) \big]
$$

For every student $x$, there exists a (possibly different) book $y$ that $x$ read. Each student may have read a *different* book.

**Reading B (Collective / Specific — ∃ > ∀):**

$$
\exists y \big[ \text{Book}(y) \land \forall x \big( \text{Student}(x) \rightarrow \text{Read}(x, y) \big) \big]
$$

There exists a single book $y$ such that every student read *that same* book.

The two readings are **logically distinct**: in a model with two students and two different books, Reading A is TRUE while Reading B is FALSE. Resolving this requires **pragmatic cues** (e.g., stress, context like "in the library"). In FOL semantics, the ambiguity is captured by the relative scope of the $\forall$ and $\exists$ quantifiers, and a model-theoretic interpreter must check both. (7 marks)

**Q (b) [7 marks]:** Describe the levels of ambiguity in NLP with real-world examples for each level. How does each level affect downstream NLP applications?

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

| Level | Example | Affected Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Phonological** | "their / there / they're" | Voice assistants (ASR) — wrong word transcribed |
| **Lexical** | "He went to the **bank**." (river vs money) | Search engines, MT — wrong sense translated |
| **Syntactic** | "I saw the man **with the telescope**." (VP vs NP attach) | Parsers, MT — wrong grammatical role assigned |
| **Semantic** | "Every student read a book." (∀∃ vs ∃∀) | Logical inference, Q&A systems |
| **Pragmatic** | "Can you pass the salt?" (ability vs request) | Dialogue systems, chatbots — wrong intent detected |

Each level **compounds**: a parsing error at the syntactic level propagates into wrong semantic role assignment, which then corrupts pragmatic interpretation. Modern NLP mitigates this through **end-to-end neural models** (Transformers) that jointly learn all levels in a unified embedding space, but the KTU 2024 syllabus still requires explicit, layer-wise reasoning for examination purposes. (7 marks)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Read Carefully Before Exam):**
>
> 1. **Conflating "ambiguity" with "vagueness"** — ambiguity = *multiple precise meanings*; vaguiness = *meaning is inherently imprecise* (e.g., "tall"). Examiners deduct 1 mark for this conflation.
> 2. **Forgetting the etymological test for homonymy vs polysemy** — Always mention the *origin* of the word, not just the surface form.
> 3. **Treating syntactic and semantic ambiguity as the same** — Syntactic ambiguity is about *parse trees*; semantic ambiguity is about *logical forms / model-theoretic interpretations*. A sentence can be syntactically unambiguous but semantically ambiguous (e.g., quantifier scope).
> 4. **Omitting the disambiguation technique** — A question worth 14 marks on "lexical ambiguity" *will* deduct 3–4 marks if you only define the problem without giving the WSD algorithm (Naive Bayes / Lesk / neural).
> 5. **Writing raw math without LaTeX context** — Use $\forall$, $\exists$, $\arg\max$ notation explicitly. The valuation key rewards mathematical precision.
> 6. **Failing to draw a diagram** — A block diagram of the five ambiguity layers fetches 2 marks in any 14-mark answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam:**

- [x] **Linguistic ambiguity** = a single surface form admitting multiple distinct interpretations.
- [x] **Five layers (in order):** Phonological → Lexical → Syntactic → Semantic → Pragmatic.
- [x] **Lexical ambiguity subtypes:** Homonymy (unrelated, different etymology) vs Polysemy (related, same etymology, metonymy/metaphor).
- [x] **Substitution test:** Substitution with a near-synonym *fails* across homonymous senses but *may succeed* for polysemous ones.
- [x] **PP-attachment** is the canonical syntactic ambiguity example; resolves via lexicalized attachment probabilities.
- [x] **Quantifier scope ambiguity** = $\forall\exists$ vs $\exists\forall$; both readings are expressible in FOL and *logically distinct*.
- [x] **WSD equation (Naive Bayes):** $\hat{s} = \arg\max_s P(s) \prod_j P(c_j \mid s)$.
- [x] **Hobbs' algorithm** = left-to-right tree walk for pronominal anaphora resolution.
- [x] **Selectional restrictions** = semantic constraints on predicate arguments; used to disambiguate anaphora.
- [x] **Parse-space complexity** is exponential: $\mathcal{O}(c^N)$ — motivates probabilistic/neural parsing.
- [x] **Disambiguation pipeline order:** Tokenize → POS tag → Parse → WSD → SRL → Pragmatic resolve.
- [x] **Real-world impact:** Every NLP application (MT, ASR, Search, Chatbots) breaks at one or more ambiguity layers.
- [x] **Exam mantra:** Always (1) define, (2) classify with example, (3) give a resolution method, (4) draw a diagram.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mathematical Essentials: Probability Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mathematical Essentials: Probability Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Probability Theory?

**Probability Theory** is the branch of mathematics that provides a formal, axiomatic framework for quantifying uncertainty, modeling random phenomena, and reasoning about the likelihood of events. In the context of **Computational Linguistics**, probability theory forms the foundational bedrock upon which virtually every modern NLP system is built — from statistical language models, speech recognition, and machine translation to part-of-speech tagging, parsing, and information retrieval.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Probability theory is the mathematical study of randomness and uncertainty, defined over a sample space using a measure that assigns values between **0** and **1** to events, where **0** denotes impossibility and **1** denotes certainty.

Formally, given a sample space $\Omega$ and a $\sigma$-algebra $\mathcal{F}$ of events, a **probability measure** $P : \mathcal{F} \rightarrow [0, 1]$ must satisfy the three **Kolmogorov Axioms**:

$$
P(\Omega) = 1
$$

$$
P(A) \geq 0 \quad \forall A \in \mathcal{F}
$$

$$
P\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty} A_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} P(A_i) \quad \text{for mutually exclusive } A_i
$$

## 1.2 Why Computational Linguistics Needs Probability

Language is inherently **noisy, ambiguous, and probabilistic**:

- A given sentence can have **multiple valid parsings**.
- A word can belong to **multiple parts of speech**.
- Future words in a sentence depend on the preceding context in a **stochastic** manner.
- Speech signals contain **acoustic ambiguity** and noise.

Probability theory allows us to:
- Rank competing hypotheses (e.g., which parse tree is most likely?).
- Learn parameters from **large corpora**.
- Build **generative models** (e.g., Hidden Markov Models, n-gram models).
- Perform **Bayesian inference** for disambiguation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Weather Forecast:**
> Imagine you are planning a trip to Munnar. A weather forecast says, *"There is a 70% chance of rain."* This single probabilistic statement helps you decide whether to carry an umbrella. You do not know *for sure* whether it will rain, but you can make a **rational decision** based on the assigned probability. In computational linguistics, we face the same situation: given a sentence like *"I saw a man with a telescope,"* we cannot be 100% certain whether *"with a telescope"* modifies the *seeing* or the *man*, but we can assign a probability to each interpretation and choose the most likely one — exactly like choosing the umbrella based on the 70% rain forecast.

## 1.3 Fundamental Terminology

To build a rigorous understanding, the following atomic definitions are non-negotiable for KTU examinations:

| Term | Formal Definition | Example in CL |
|------|-------------------|---------------|
| **Sample Space ($\Omega$)** | The set of all possible outcomes of a random experiment | All possible English sentences of length $\leq 10$ |
| **Event ($A$)** | A subset of the sample space | A specific sentence appears in a corpus |
| **Random Variable ($X$)** | A function $X : \Omega \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ mapping outcomes to real numbers | The count of nouns in a sentence |
| **Probability Mass Function (PMF)** | For discrete $X$: $P(X = x)$ | $P(\text{next word} = \text{``the''})$ |
| **Probability Density Function (PDF)** | For continuous $X$: $f(x)$ where $P(a \leq X \leq b) = \int_a^b f(x)\,dx$ | Distribution of word embedding magnitudes |
| **Conditional Probability** | $P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$, provided $P(B) > 0$ | $P(\text{noun} \mid \text{``the"})$ |
| **Independence** | $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$ | Two words in different sentences |
| **Mutual Exclusivity** | $P(A \cap B) = 0$ | A word is either "run" or "ran", not both simultaneously in the same form |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In computational linguistics, we almost never work with a complete, well-defined sample space (the set of all possible English sentences is essentially infinite and un-enumerable). We work with **empirical distributions estimated from finite corpora** — a fact that makes concepts like *maximum likelihood estimation* and *Bayesian inference* critically important.

## 1.4 Three Interpretations of Probability

There are three principal schools of thought on what probability *means*:

**1. Classical (Laplacean) Interpretation**
- Applicable only to finite sample spaces with equally likely outcomes.
- $P(A) = \frac{\text{Number of favorable outcomes}}{\text{Total number of outcomes}}$
- Example: Probability of drawing the word *"the"* from a bag containing 100 English words = $\frac{1}{100}$.

**2. Frequentist Interpretation**
- Probability is the **long-run relative frequency** of an event in repeated trials.
- $P(A) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\text{count}(A)}{n}$
- Example: The empirical frequency of *"the"* in the Brown Corpus.

**3. Bayesian (Subjective) Interpretation**
- Probability represents a **degree of belief** that updates as new evidence arrives.
- Allows the assignment of probabilities to one-off events (e.g., *"Will this paper win the best paper award?"*).
- Cornerstone of modern NLP inference.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Modern computational linguistics is **overwhelmingly Bayesian**. Tools like Bayesian networks, Bayesian inference for HMMs, and Bayesian language models (e.g., topic models like LDA) all rely on the Bayesian interpretation. Examiners frequently test your understanding of this paradigm.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Axiomatic Foundation — Kolmogorov's Framework

Every probability computation in computational linguistics ultimately rests on three axioms, formalized by **Andrey Kolmogorov in 1933**. These axioms are the *non-negotiable* starting point of any rigorous discussion.

**Axiom 1 — Non-Negativity:**
$$
P(A) \geq 0 \quad \forall A \in \mathcal{F}
$$
*Why this matters:* Probabilities are never negative. The PMF or PDF must yield non-negative values everywhere.

**Axiom 2 — Normalization:**
$$
P(\Omega) = 1
$$
*Why this matters:* The entire sample space represents *certainty*. The total probability mass must integrate (or sum) to exactly **1**.

**Axiom 3 — Countable Additivity:**
$$
P\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty} A_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} P(A_i) \quad \text{when } A_i \cap A_j = \emptyset \text{ for } i \neq j
$$
*Why this matters:* For mutually exclusive events, the probability of the union equals the sum of individual probabilities. This lets us decompose complex events into simple, disjoint parts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Kolmogorov axioms guarantee that any model you build in computational linguistics — whether a language model, a hidden Markov model, or a probabilistic context-free grammar — produces well-defined, mathematically consistent probability distributions. Without these axioms, we could not train models using gradient descent or perform maximum likelihood estimation.

## 2.2 Conditional Probability — The Heart of Sequential Models

Conditional probability answers the question: *"Given that I already know B has occurred, what is the probability of A?"* This is the **single most important concept** in statistical NLP, because language is fundamentally sequential and context-dependent.

$$
P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}, \quad P(B) > 0
$$

Rearranging this equation gives the **multiplication rule** (also called the **chain rule** of probability — the cornerstone of n-gram language models):

$$
P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)
$$

Generalized to $n$ events:

$$
P(A_1 \cap A_2 \cap \ldots \cap A_n) = P(A_1) \cdot P(A_2 \mid A_1) \cdot P(A_3 \mid A_1, A_2) \cdots P(A_n \mid A_1, \ldots, A_{n-1})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CL Connection:** This is exactly how an n-gram language model computes the probability of a sentence $W = (w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n)$:
>
> $$P(W) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_{i-1})$$
>
> An n-gram model truncates the context to the previous $n-1$ words. For bigrams: $P(W) \approx \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_{i-1})$.

## 2.3 The Law of Total Probability

When the sample space can be partitioned into $n$ mutually exclusive and exhaustive events $B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_n$ (i.e., $\bigcup B_i = \Omega$ and $B_i \cap B_j = \emptyset$ for $i \neq j$), then for any event $A$:

$$
P(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(A \mid B_i) \cdot P(B_i)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in CL:** This is the foundation of the **forward algorithm** for Hidden Markov Models, used in part-of-speech tagging and speech recognition. It allows us to compute the total probability of an observed sequence by summing over all hidden state sequences that could have generated it.

## 2.4 Bayes' Theorem — The Engine of Modern NLP

**Bayes' Theorem** is arguably the most consequential result in probabilistic NLP. It inverts conditional probabilities, allowing us to compute $P(B \mid A)$ from $P(A \mid B)$:

$$
P(B_i \mid A) = \frac{P(A \mid B_i) \cdot P(B_i)}{P(A)} = \frac{P(A \mid B_i) \cdot P(B_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} P(A \mid B_j) \cdot P(B_j)}
$$

The terms have specific names in CL parlance:

| Term | Name | Meaning |
|------|------|---------|
| $P(B_i)$ | **Prior probability** | Belief about $B_i$ before seeing evidence $A$ |
| $P(A \mid B_i)$ | **Likelihood** | Probability of observing evidence $A$ if $B_i$ is true |
| $P(B_i \mid A)$ | **Posterior probability** | Updated belief about $B_i$ after seeing $A$ |
| $P(A)$ | **Evidence / Marginal likelihood** | Total probability of observing $A$ (normalizing constant) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CL Applications of Bayes' Theorem:**
> - **POS Tagging:** Given a word, find the most probable tag.
> - **Naive Bayes Text Classification:** Spam detection, sentiment analysis.
> - **Speech Recognition:** Decoding the most probable word sequence given an acoustic signal.
> - **Statistical Machine Translation:** IBM Models 1-5 for word alignment.
> - **Topic Models (LDA):** Inferring latent topic distributions.

## 2.5 Independence and Conditional Independence

Two events $A$ and $B$ are **independent** if and only if:
$$
P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B) \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad P(A \mid B) = P(A)
$$

For random variables, $X$ and $Y$ are independent if $P(X, Y) = P(X) P(Y)$ for all values.

A stronger (and more useful in CL) notion is **conditional independence**: $A$ and $B$ are conditionally independent given $C$ if:

$$
P(A \cap B \mid C) = P(A \mid C) \cdot P(B \mid C)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **CL Connection:** The **Naive Bayes assumption** is a textbook example of conditional independence. In document classification, we assume that the probability of each word $w_i$ given a class $c$ is independent of the other words, given $c$. This is *naive* because in reality words are highly correlated (e.g., *"machine"* and *"learning"* rarely appear independently), but the assumption makes computation tractable and works surprisingly well in practice.

## 2.6 Random Variables and Their Distributions

A **random variable** is a function that maps outcomes in the sample space to real numbers. There are two principal types:

### Discrete Random Variables
Take values from a countable set. Defined by a **Probability Mass Function (PMF)** $p(x) = P(X = x)$.

Common examples in CL:
- **Bernoulli($p$):** $P(X=1) = p$, $P(X=0) = 1-p$. Used for binary classification outcomes.
- **Binomial($n, p$):** Number of successes in $n$ independent Bernoulli trials. Used in N-gram counting.
- **Categorical / Multinoulli:** Generalization of Bernoulli to $k$ outcomes. Used in language model output layers (softmax).
- **Poisson($\lambda$):** Number of events in a fixed interval. Used to model word counts in documents.

### Continuous Random Variables
Take values from a continuum. Defined by a **Probability Density Function (PDF)** $f(x)$ where:

$$
P(a \leq X \leq b) = \int_a^b f(x)\, dx
$$

Common examples in CL:
- **Normal / Gaussian($\mu, \sigma^2$):** The bell curve. Central to word embedding distributions, neural network weight initialization.
- **Beta($\alpha, \beta$):** Distribution over probabilities. Used as a prior in Bayesian inference.

## 2.7 Expectation, Variance, and Covariance

### Expected Value (Mean)
The long-run average value of a random variable:

$$
E[X] = \begin{cases} \sum_x x \cdot P(X = x) & \text{(discrete)} \\ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x \cdot f(x)\, dx & \text{(continuous)} \end{cases}
$$

### Variance
A measure of dispersion around the mean:

$$
\text{Var}(X) = E[(X - E[X])^2] = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2
$$

Standard deviation $\sigma_X = \sqrt{\text{Var}(X)}$.

### Covariance
Measures the joint variability of two random variables:

$$
\text{Cov}(X, Y) = E[(X - E[X])(Y - E[Y])]
$$

Normalized to obtain the **Pearson correlation coefficient**:

$$
\rho_{XY} = \frac{\text{Cov}(X, Y)}{\sigma_X \sigma_Y}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Expectation, variance, and covariance are the workhorses behind the **bias-variance tradeoff** in machine learning, **principal component analysis** (used in dimensionality reduction of word embeddings), and **Gaussian distributions** that underpin variational autoencoders and many NLP generative models.

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **comprehensive cheat sheet** of every formula you must know for KTU 2024 ESE on this topic. Memorize these and their conditions of applicability.

| # | Concept | Formula | Condition / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Non-negativity | $P(A) \geq 0$ | All events |
| 2 | Normalization | $P(\Omega) = 1$ | Always |
| 3 | Complement | $P(A^c) = 1 - P(A)$ | Single event |
| 4 | Addition (union) | $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$ | Any two events |
| 5 | Addition (disjoint) | $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)$ | If $A \cap B = \emptyset$ |
| 6 | Conditional probability | $P(A \mid B) = P(A \cap B) / P(B)$ | $P(B) > 0$ |
| 7 | Multiplication rule | $P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) P(B)$ | General |
| 8 | Chain rule (n events) | $P(A_1 \cap \ldots \cap A_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(A_i \mid A_1, \ldots, A_{i-1})$ | General |
| 9 | Total probability | $P(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(A \mid B_i) P(B_i)$ | $\{B_i\}$ partition $\Omega$ |
| 10 | Bayes' theorem | $P(B_i \mid A) = P(A \mid B_i) P(B_i) / P(A)$ | $P(A) > 0$ |
| 11 | Independence | $P(A \cap B) = P(A) P(B)$ | $\Leftrightarrow P(A \mid B) = P(A)$ |
| 12 | Conditional independence | $P(A \cap B \mid C) = P(A \mid C) P(B \mid C)$ | Given $C$ |
| 13 | Expected value (discrete) | $E[X] = \sum_x x \cdot P(X=x)$ | Random variable |
| 14 | Expected value (continuous) | $E[X] = \int x f(x)\, dx$ | Random variable |
| 15 | Linearity of expectation | $E[aX + bY + c] = aE[X] + bE[Y] + c$ | Always |
| 16 | Variance | $\text{Var}(X) = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2$ | Always |
| 17 | Std deviation | $\sigma_X = \sqrt{\text{Var}(X)}$ | Always |
| 18 | Covariance | $\text{Cov}(X,Y) = E[XY] - E[X]E[Y]$ | Always |
| 19 | Correlation | $\rho_{XY} = \text{Cov}(X,Y) / (\sigma_X \sigma_Y)$ | $\sigma_X, \sigma_Y > 0$ |
| 20 | Bernoulli PMF | $P(X=1) = p,\ P(X=0)=1-p$ | Single trial |
| 21 | Binomial PMF | $P(X=k) = \binom{n}{k} p^k (1-p)^{n-k}$ | $n$ trials |
| 22 | Normal PDF | $f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | Continuous |
| 23 | n-gram language model | $P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1}) \approx P(w_i \mid w_{i-n+1}^{i-1})$ | Markov assumption |
| 24 | Naive Bayes classifier | $\hat{c} = \arg\max_c P(c) \prod_i P(w_i \mid c)$ | Conditional independence |
| 25 | Shannon entropy | $H(X) = -\sum_x P(x) \log_2 P(x)$ | Information theory |

## 2.9 Information Theory Connection (Brief but Important)

A probability distribution's **entropy** quantifies its uncertainty. For a discrete random variable $X$ with PMF $P$:

$$
H(X) = -\sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} P(x) \log_2 P(x)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **CL Connection:** Entropy is fundamental in:
> - **Perplexity** evaluation of language models: $\text{PP}(W) = 2^{H(W)}$.
> - **Cross-entropy loss** in neural language models: $L = -\sum_i \log P(w_i \mid \text{context})$.
> - **Mutual information** for feature selection in text classification.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **exhaustive derivations** of the most important results. Every algebraic step is explicitly shown — no skipping, no shortcuts.

## 3.1 Derivation: Bayes' Theorem from First Principles

**Goal:** Derive $P(B \mid A) = \dfrac{P(A \mid B)\, P(B)}{P(A)}$ from the definition of conditional probability.

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of conditional probability for $B$ given $A$:**

$$
P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(B \cap A)}{P(A)}
$$

*Explanation:* By definition, the conditional probability of $B$ given $A$ is the probability of the intersection $B \cap A$ (also written $A \cap B$) divided by the probability of the conditioning event $A$. This is the direct application of the formal definition to the event $B$.

**Step 2 — Start with the definition of conditional probability for $A$ given $B$:**

$$
P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}
$$

*Explanation:* Symmetrically, the conditional probability of $A$ given $B$ is the probability of the intersection divided by the probability of $B$. Note that the intersection is commutative: $A \cap B = B \cap A$.

**Step 3 — Solve Step 2 for the intersection $P(A \cap B)$:**

$$
P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B)
$$

*Explanation:* Multiply both sides of the equation in Step 2 by $P(B)$ to isolate the intersection term on the left.

**Step 4 — Substitute the expression from Step 3 into the equation in Step 1:**

$$
P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B)}{P(A)}
$$

*Explanation:* Since $P(B \cap A) = P(A \cap B)$, we can substitute the result from Step 3 directly into the numerator of Step 1. This is the celebrated Bayes' theorem.

**Step 5 — Generalize to multiple hypotheses using the Law of Total Probability.**

Assume $\{B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_n\}$ is a partition of $\Omega$. Then:

$$
P(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(A \cap B_i) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(A \mid B_i) \cdot P(B_i)
$$

*Explanation:* Because the $B_i$ are mutually exclusive and exhaustive, the event $A$ can be written as the disjoint union $A = (A \cap B_1) \cup (A \cap B_2) \cup \ldots \cup (A \cap B_n)$. By Axiom 3 (countable additivity), $P(A)$ is the sum of the individual intersection probabilities.

**Step 6 — Final generalized Bayes' theorem:**

$$
P(B_i \mid A) = \frac{P(A \mid B_i) \cdot P(B_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} P(A \mid B_j) \cdot P(B_j)}
$$

*Explanation:* Substituting the result from Step 5 as the denominator in Step 4 gives the fully expanded form, which is computationally useful when $A$ may be caused by multiple alternative hypotheses $B_1, \ldots, B_n$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Chain Rule for Probability (n events)

**Goal:** Show that $P(A_1 \cap A_2 \cap \ldots \cap A_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(A_i \mid A_1, \ldots, A_{i-1})$.

**Step 1 — Start with the two-event multiplication rule:**

$$
P(A_1 \cap A_2) = P(A_2 \mid A_1) \cdot P(A_1)
$$

*Explanation:* This is the direct application of the conditional probability definition: $P(A_1 \cap A_2) / P(A_1) = P(A_2 \mid A_1)$, then multiply both sides by $P(A_1)$.

**Step 2 — Add a third event by treating $A_1 \cap A_2$ as a single composite event:**

$$
P(A_1 \cap A_2 \cap A_3) = P(A_3 \mid A_1 \cap A_2) \cdot P(A_1 \cap A_2)
$$

*Explanation:* The same multiplication rule applies to the joint event $A_1 \cap A_2$ and the new event $A_3$.

**Step 3 — Substitute Step 1's result into Step 2:**

$$
P(A_1 \cap A_2 \cap A_3) = P(A_3 \mid A_1 \cap A_2) \cdot P(A_2 \mid A_1) \cdot P(A_1)
$$

*Explanation:* Replace $P(A_1 \cap A_2)$ with the expansion from Step 1.

**Step 4 — Induct on the pattern. Assume the result holds for $n-1$ events:**

$$
P(A_1 \cap \ldots \cap A_{n-1}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n-1} P(A_i \mid A_1, \ldots, A_{i-1})
$$

**Step 5 — Add the $n$-th event using the multiplication rule:**

$$
P(A_1 \cap \ldots \cap A_n) = P(A_n \mid A_1, \ldots, A_{n-1}) \cdot P(A_1 \cap \ldots \cap A_{n-1})
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the inductive hypothesis:**

$$
P(A_1 \cap \ldots \cap A_n) = P(A_n \mid A_1, \ldots, A_{n-1}) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n-1} P(A_i \mid A_1, \ldots, A_{i-1})
$$

**Step 7 — Combine the product and the new factor:**

$$
P(A_1 \cap \ldots \cap A_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(A_i \mid A_1, \ldots, A_{i-1})
$$

*Explanation:* This completes the induction. The chain rule is the foundation of n-gram language modeling.

## 3.3 Derivation: Expectation of a Linear Combination

**Goal:** Show that $E[aX + bY] = aE[X] + bE[Y]$ (linearity of expectation).

**Step 1 — Write out the joint expectation for a discrete joint distribution:**

$$
E[aX + bY] = \sum_{x} \sum_{y} (ax + by) \cdot P(X=x, Y=y)
$$

*Explanation:* The expected value of a function $g(X, Y)$ of two random variables is the sum (or integral) over all joint outcomes of $g$ times the joint probability.

**Step 2 — Distribute the constants and split the sum:**

$$
E[aX + bY] = a \sum_{x} \sum_{y} x \cdot P(X=x, Y=y) + b \sum_{x} \sum_{y} y \cdot P(X=x, Y=y)
$$

*Explanation:* Factor out the constants $a$ and $b$ from their respective terms.

**Step 3 — Marginalize the joint distribution. For the first term:**

$$
\sum_{x} \sum_{y} x \cdot P(X=x, Y=y) = \sum_{x} x \left( \sum_{y} P(X=x, Y=y) \right) = \sum_{x} x \cdot P(X=x) = E[X]
$$

*Explanation:* Summing the joint PMF over $y$ yields the marginal $P(X=x)$. The double sum collapses to the marginal expectation of $X$.

**Step 4 — Apply the same marginalization to the second term:**

$$
\sum_{x} \sum_{y} y \cdot P(X=x, Y=y) = E[Y]
$$

**Step 5 — Combine the results:**

$$
E[aX + bY] = a E[X] + b E[Y]
$$

*Explanation:* This proves linearity of expectation, which holds *regardless of whether $X$ and $Y$ are independent*. This is one of the most useful and often-used properties in all of probability theory.

## 3.4 Derivation: Variance in terms of Expected Values

**Goal:** Show that $\text{Var}(X) = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2$.

**Step 1 — Write the definition of variance:**

$$
\text{Var}(X) = E[(X - E[X])^2]
$$

*Explanation:* Variance is the expected value of the squared deviation from the mean.

**Step 2 — Expand the square:**

$$
\text{Var}(X) = E[X^2 - 2X E[X] + (E[X])^2]
$$

*Explanation:* Apply the identity $(a - b)^2 = a^2 - 2ab + b^2$ with $a = X$ and $b = E[X]$.

**Step 3 — Use linearity of expectation to distribute $E[\cdot]$ over the three terms:**

$$
\text{Var}(X) = E[X^2] - 2 E[X \cdot E[X]] + E[(E[X])^2]
$$

**Step 4 — Recognize that $E[X]$ is a constant, not a random variable. Therefore:**

$$
E[X \cdot E[X]] = E[X] \cdot E[X] = (E[X])^2
$$

$$
E[(E[X])^2] = (E[X])^2
$$

*Explanation:* Since $E[X] = \mu$ is a fixed number, it can be pulled out of the expectation. The expectation of a constant is just the constant itself.

**Step 5 — Substitute back:**

$$
\text{Var}(X) = E[X^2] - 2 (E[X])^2 + (E[X])^2 = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2
$$

*Explanation:* Combine the two constant terms: $-2\mu^2 + \mu^2 = -\mu^2$.

## 3.5 Worked Example: Naive Bayes Document Classification

**Problem Setup (typical KTU 2024 question style):**
A spam filter uses Naive Bayes. The prior probabilities are $P(\text{spam}) = 0.3$ and $P(\text{ham}) = 0.7$. The likelihoods for the word *"offer"* are $P(\text{offer} \mid \text{spam}) = 0.6$ and $P(\text{offer} \mid \text{ham}) = 0.05$. The likelihoods for the word *"free"* are $P(\text{free} \mid \text{spam}) = 0.5$ and $P(\text{free} \mid \text{ham}) = 0.02$. An email contains both words *"offer free"*. Classify it.

**Step 1 — Write Bayes' theorem for each class:**

$$
P(\text{spam} \mid \text{offer, free}) = \frac{P(\text{offer, free} \mid \text{spam}) \cdot P(\text{spam})}{P(\text{offer, free})}
$$

$$
P(\text{ham} \mid \text{offer, free}) = \frac{P(\text{offer, free} \mid \text{ham}) \cdot P(\text{ham})}{P(\text{offer, free})}
$$

*Explanation:* We compute the posterior for each class. The denominator is the same for both, so we can compare numerators (this is called the **Maximum A Posteriori**, or **MAP**, decision rule).

**Step 2 — Apply the Naive Bayes conditional independence assumption:**

$$
P(\text{offer, free} \mid \text{spam}) = P(\text{offer} \mid \text{spam}) \cdot P(\text{free} \mid \text{spam})
$$

$$
P(\text{offer, free} \mid \text{ham}) = P(\text{offer} \mid \text{ham}) \cdot P(\text{free} \mid \text{ham})
$$

*Explanation:* Naive Bayes assumes that the occurrences of the words are conditionally independent given the class.

**Step 3 — Compute the unnormalized posterior for spam:**

$$
P(\text{spam}) \cdot P(\text{offer, free} \mid \text{spam}) = 0.3 \times 0.6 \times 0.5 = 0.09
$$

*Explanation:* Multiply the prior by the two likelihoods: $0.3 \times 0.6 = 0.18$, then $0.18 \times 0.5 = 0.09$.

**Step 4 — Compute the unnormalized posterior for ham:**

$$
P(\text{ham}) \cdot P(\text{offer, free} \mid \text{ham}) = 0.7 \times 0.05 \times 0.02 = 0.0007
$$

*Explanation:* Multiply the prior by the two likelihoods: $0.7 \times 0.05 = 0.035$, then $0.035 \times 0.02 = 0.0007$.

**Step 5 — Normalize to get true probabilities:**

$$
P(\text{offer, free}) = 0.09 + 0.0007 = 0.0907
$$

$$
P(\text{spam} \mid \text{offer, free}) = \frac{0.09}{0.0907} \approx 0.9923
$$

$$
P(\text{ham} \mid \text{offer, free}) = \frac{0.0007}{0.0907} \approx 0.0077
$$

**Step 6 — Decision:** Since $P(\text{spam} \mid \text{offer, free}) \approx 99.23\% \gg P(\text{ham} \mid \text{offer, free}) \approx 0.77\%$, the email is classified as **spam**.

## 3.6 Worked Example: Bigram Language Model Probability

**Problem:** Given a corpus with the following word counts: $P(\text{the}) = 0.06$, $P(\text{cat} \mid \text{the}) = 0.02$, $P(\text{sat} \mid \text{cat}) = 0.25$, $P(\text{on} \mid \text{sat}) = 0.4$, $P(\text{the} \mid \text{on}) = 0.5$, $P(\text{mat} \mid \text{the}) = 0.01$. Compute the probability of the sentence *"the cat sat on the mat."*

**Step 1 — Decompose the sentence using the bigram chain rule:**

$$
P(\text{the cat sat on the mat}) = P(\text{the}) \cdot P(\text{cat} \mid \text{the}) \cdot P(\text{sat} \mid \text{cat}) \cdot P(\text{on} \mid \text{sat}) \cdot P(\text{the} \mid \text{on}) \cdot P(\text{mat} \mid \text{the})
$$

*Explanation:* The bigram model assumes the probability of each word depends only on the immediately preceding word. The first word uses the unigram prior.

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values:**

$$
P = 0.06 \times 0.02 \times 0.25 \times 0.4 \times 0.5 \times 0.01
$$

**Step 3 — Compute step by step:**

$$
0.06 \times 0.02 = 0.0012
$$

$$
0.0012 \times 0.25 = 0.0003
$$

$$
0.0003 \times 0.4 = 0.00012
$$

$$
0.00012 \times 0.5 = 0.00006
$$

$$
0.00006 \times 0.01 = 6 \times 10^{-7}
$$

**Step 4 — Final result:**

$$
P(\text{the cat sat on the mat}) = 6 \times 10^{-7}
$$

*Explanation:* This very small number illustrates the **underflow problem** in language models — multiplying many small probabilities rapidly drives values to zero, which is why practical systems use log-probabilities.

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Engineering Note:** Real-world language models always compute $\log P(W) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(w_i \mid w_{i-1})$ instead of the raw product. This avoids numerical underflow and converts the product into a numerically stable sum.

## 4.2 Bayes' Theorem Information Flow Diagram

The following block-level architecture diagram visualizes the flow of information through Bayes' theorem in a typical NLP pipeline (e.g., spam classification).

```mermaid
graph LR
    inputA[Evidence A: Document Text] --> n1[Tokenize and Extract Features]
    n1 --> n2[Compute Likelihood P of A given B]
    priorB[Prior P of B: Class Probability] --> n3[Multiply Prior and Likelihood]
    n2 --> n3
    n3 --> n4[Unnormalized Posterior P of B given A]
    norm[Normalizer: Sum Over All Classes] --> n5[Divide by Normalizer]
    n4 --> n5
    n5 --> n6[Posterior P of B given A]
    n6 --> output[Class Decision: argmax over B]
```

## 4.3 N-gram Language Model Architecture

The following diagram illustrates how a bigram model decomposes a sentence into a sequence of conditional probability lookups.

```mermaid
graph TD
    sstart[Sentence Start] --> w1[Word 1: the]
    w1 --> w2[Word 2: cat]
    w1 --> w12[Lookup: P of cat given the]
    w12 --> w2
    w2 --> w3[Word 3: sat]
    w2 --> w23[Lookup: P of sat given cat]
    w23 --> w3
    w3 --> w4[Word 4: on]
    w3 --> w34[Lookup: P of on given sat]
    w34 --> w4
    w4 --> w5[Word 5: the]
    w4 --> w45[Lookup: P of the given on]
    w45 --> w5
    w5 --> w6[Word 6: mat]
    w5 --> w56[Lookup: P of mat given the]
    w56 --> w6
    w6 --> wend[Sentence End]
    w12 --> multiply[Multiply All Factors]
    w23 --> multiply
    w34 --> multiply
    w45 --> multiply
    w56 --> multiply
    multiply --> result[Final Sentence Probability]
```

## 4.4 Hierarchical Relationship of Probability Concepts

The following diagram clarifies the dependency relationships among the major concepts — what is built on what.

```mermaid
graph TD
    base1[Kolmogorov Axioms] --> base2[Probability Measure P]
    base2 --> op1[Addition Rule]
    base2 --> op2[Complement Rule]
    base2 --> op3[Conditional Probability]
    op3 --> op4[Multiplication and Chain Rule]
    op3 --> op5[Bayes Theorem]
    op3 --> op6[Total Probability]
    op4 --> op7[N-gram Models]
    op5 --> op8[Naive Bayes]
    op5 --> op9[HMM Inference]
    op6 --> op9
    op7 --> eval[Language Model Evaluation]
    op8 --> eval
    op9 --> eval
    base2 --> rand[Random Variables]
    rand --> distrib[Distributions PMF and PDF]
    distrib --> stats[Expectation and Variance]
    stats --> ent[Entropy and Perplexity]
    ent --> eval
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to use these diagrams in exams:** When answering 14-mark questions that involve multiple concepts, sketch one of these block diagrams first. KTU examiners award marks for *systematic organization* and *clear logical flow*. A well-drawn diagram can earn 1-2 additional marks even before you write any derivation.

---
