<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> Define the three Kolmogorov axioms of probability. Why are they essential for building probabilistic models in computational linguistics? (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The three Kolmogorov axioms, formulated in 1933, are the axiomatic foundation of modern probability theory:

**Axiom 1 (Non-Negativity):** $P(A) \geq 0$ for every event $A$ in the $\sigma$-algebra $\mathcal{F}$. Probabilities are never negative.

**Axiom 2 (Normalization):** $P(\Omega) = 1$, where $\Omega$ is the entire sample space. The total probability of all possible outcomes sums to one.

**Axiom 3 (Countable Additivity):** For any countable collection of mutually exclusive events $A_1, A_2, A_3, \ldots$, we have $P(\bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty} A_i) = \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} P(A_i)$.

**Importance in CL:** These axioms ensure that any probability distribution over words, tags, or sentences is mathematically well-defined, consistent, and admits meaningful operations (marginalization, conditioning, expectation). Without these axioms, models like HMMs, CRFs, and language models would lack a rigorous probabilistic foundation.

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> Differentiate between joint, marginal, and conditional probability. Give a one-sentence example of each from computational linguistics. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Type | Definition | CL Example |
|------|------------|------------|
| **Joint** $P(A, B)$ | Probability of two events occurring together | $P(\text{word}=\text{"the"}, \text{tag}=\text{DET}) = 0.04$ |
| **Marginal** $P(A)$ | Probability of one event, summed over the other | $P(\text{word}=\text{"the"}) = 0.06$ (summed over all tags) |
| **Conditional** $P(A \mid B)$ | Probability of $A$ given that $B$ has occurred | $P(\text{tag}=\text{DET} \mid \text{word}=\text{"the"}) \approx 0.95$ |

**Mathematical Relationship:** $P(A, B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)$, and $P(A) = \sum_{b} P(A, B=b)$ (marginalization).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> Each question has two sub-parts worth 7 marks each. Provide complete, step-by-step solutions.

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**
>
> **(a)** State and prove Bayes' theorem. Explain its significance in computational linguistics with at least two concrete examples.
>
> **(b)** In a text classification task using Naive Bayes, there are two classes: $C_1 = \text{Sports}$ and $C_2 = \text{Politics}$. The prior probabilities are $P(C_1) = 0.6$ and $P(C_2) = 0.4$. The likelihoods for the word *"goal"* are $P(\text{goal} \mid C_1) = 0.04$ and $P(\text{goal} \mid C_2) = 0.005$. The likelihoods for the word *"election"* are $P(\text{election} \mid C_1) = 0.001$ and $P(\text{election} \mid C_2) = 0.08$. A document contains both words. Classify it using the MAP decision rule.

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Statement of Bayes' Theorem:**

For any two events $A$ and $B$ where $P(A) > 0$ and $P(B) > 0$:

$$
P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B)}{P(A)}
$$

**Generalized Form:** For a partition $\{B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_n\}$ of the sample space:

$$
P(B_i \mid A) = \frac{P(A \mid B_i) \cdot P(B_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} P(A \mid B_j) \cdot P(B_j)}
$$

**Proof:**

Step 1 — From the definition of conditional probability:

$$
P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(B \cap A)}{P(A)} \quad \text{...(i)}
$$

Step 2 — From the symmetric definition:

$$
P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)} \quad \text{...(ii)}
$$

Step 3 — Since $B \cap A = A \cap B$, solve (ii) for the intersection:

$$
P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B) \quad \text{...(iii)}
$$

Step 4 — Substitute (iii) into (i):

$$
P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B)}{P(A)}
$$

This completes the proof. For the generalized form, note that $P(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(A \mid B_i) P(B_i)$ by the law of total probability, since $\{B_i\}$ is a partition.

**Significance in CL — Two Examples:**

1. **Part-of-Speech Tagging:** Given an observed word $w$, we want the most probable tag $t$:

$$
\hat{t} = \arg\max_t P(t \mid w) = \arg\max_t \frac{P(w \mid t) \cdot P(t)}{P(w)}
$$

Here $P(t)$ is the prior probability of the tag (estimated from corpus frequencies), and $P(w \mid t)$ is the likelihood of the word given the tag.

2. **Statistical Machine Translation (IBM Models):** Given an observed target sentence, find the most probable source sentence:

$$
\hat{s} = \arg\max_s P(s \mid t) = \arg\max_s P(t \mid s) \cdot P(s)
$$

This is the foundation of IBM Model 1, the first statistical word-alignment model.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Identify the goal:** Find $\arg\max_{C_i} P(C_i \mid \text{goal}, \text{election})$.

**Step 2 — Apply Bayes' theorem with the Naive Bayes assumption:**

$$
P(C_i \mid \text{goal}, \text{election}) \propto P(\text{goal} \mid C_i) \cdot P(\text{election} \mid C_i) \cdot P(C_i)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute unnormalized posterior for Sports ($C_1$):**

$$
P(C_1) \cdot P(\text{goal} \mid C_1) \cdot P(\text{election} \mid C_1) = 0.6 \times 0.04 \times 0.001
$$

Computing sequentially:

$$
0.6 \times 0.04 = 0.024
$$

$$
0.024 \times 0.001 = 0.000024
$$

**Step 4 — Compute unnormalized posterior for Politics ($C_2$):**

$$
P(C_2) \cdot P(\text{goal} \mid C_2) \cdot P(\text{election} \mid C_2) = 0.4 \times 0.005 \times 0.08
$$

Computing sequentially:

$$
0.4 \times 0.005 = 0.002
$$

$$
0.002 \times 0.08 = 0.00016
$$

**Step 5 — Compare:**

$$
P(C_1 \mid \text{goal, election}) \propto 0.000024
$$

$$
P(C_2 \mid \text{goal, election}) \propto 0.00016
$$

**Step 6 — Decision:** Since $0.00016 > 0.000024$, the document is classified as **Politics**.

**Step 7 — (Optional) Normalize for completeness:**

$$
P(\text{goal, election}) = 0.000024 + 0.00016 = 0.000184
$$

$$
P(C_1 \mid \text{goal, election}) = \frac{0.000024}{0.000184} \approx 0.130 \quad (13.0\%)
$$

$$
P(C_2 \mid \text{goal, election}) = \frac{0.00016}{0.000184} \approx 0.870 \quad (87.0\%)
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
>
> **(a)** What is the chain rule of probability? Derive the n-gram language model probability for the sentence $W = (w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4)$ as a bigram model. Compute the probability of the sentence *"I love natural language"* given the following bigram probabilities:
> $P(\text{I}) = 0.10$, $P(\text{love} \mid \text{I}) = 0.05$, $P(\text{natural} \mid \text{love}) = 0.20$, $P(\text{language} \mid \text{natural}) = 0.30$.
>
> **(b)** Explain the concept of conditional independence. How does the Naive Bayes assumption simplify computation in text classification? Discuss one real-world scenario where this assumption is violated.

#### Model Solution to (a):

**The Chain Rule of Probability:**

For any sequence of events $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n$:

$$
P(A_1 \cap A_2 \cap \ldots \cap A_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(A_i \mid A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_{i-1})
$$

**Derivation (sketch):** Start with the two-event case $P(A_1 \cap A_2) = P(A_2 \mid A_1) P(A_1)$. Add a third event by treating $A_1 \cap A_2$ as one composite event, giving $P(A_1 \cap A_2 \cap A_3) = P(A_3 \mid A_1 \cap A_2) \cdot P(A_1 \cap A_2) = P(A_3 \mid A_1 \cap A_2) \cdot P(A_2 \mid A_1) \cdot P(A_1)$. By mathematical induction, this extends to $n$ events.

**Application to Bigram Language Models:**

For a sentence $W = (w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n)$, the chain rule gives the exact probability:

$$
P(W) = P(w_1) \cdot P(w_2 \mid w_1) \cdot P(w_3 \mid w_1, w_2) \cdots P(w_n \mid w_1, \ldots, w_{n-1})
$$

The **bigram assumption** (a first-order Markov assumption) approximates this by assuming each word depends only on the immediately preceding word:

$$
P(W) \approx \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_{i-1})
$$

where $w_0$ is a special sentence-start token (for $i=1$, we use the unigram $P(w_1)$).

**Numerical Computation for "I love natural language":**

Step 1 — Decompose the sentence into bigram factors:

$$
P(\text{I love natural language}) = P(\text{I}) \cdot P(\text{love} \mid \text{I}) \cdot P(\text{natural} \mid \text{love}) \cdot P(\text{language} \mid \text{natural})
$$

Step 2 — Substitute the given values:

$$
P = 0.10 \times 0.05 \times 0.20 \times 0.30
$$

Step 3 — Compute step by step:

$$
0.10 \times 0.05 = 0.005
$$

$$
0.005 \times 0.20 = 0.001
$$

$$
0.001 \times 0.30 = 0.0003
$$

**Final Answer:** $P(\text{I love natural language}) = 3 \times 10^{-4}$.

**Log-Space Alternative (KTU bonus insight):**

$$
\log P = \log(0.10) + \log(0.05) + \log(0.20) + \log(0.30) = -1.0 - 1.30 - 0.70 - 0.52 = -3.52
$$

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Conditional Independence — Definition:**

Two events $A$ and $B$ are **conditionally independent** given a third event $C$ if and only if:

$$
P(A \cap B \mid C) = P(A \mid C) \cdot P(B \mid C)
$$

Equivalently, $P(A \mid B, C) = P(A \mid C)$ — meaning that once we know $C$, learning $B$ provides no additional information about $A$.

**The Naive Bayes Assumption:**

In text classification, the Naive Bayes classifier assumes that the occurrences of words in a document are **conditionally independent given the class label**. Formally, for a document $D = (w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n)$ and class $C$:

$$
P(w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n \mid C) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid C)
$$

**How This Simplifies Computation:**

Without this assumption, we would need to estimate the joint probability $P(w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n \mid C)$ from data. For a vocabulary of size $V$, the number of possible distinct terms in a document grows exponentially with document length, making estimation infeasible even with massive corpora.

With the Naive Bayes assumption, we only need to estimate $V$ individual conditional probabilities $P(w_i \mid C)$ for each word in the vocabulary, regardless of document length. This reduces the estimation problem from exponential to linear in $V$, making training and inference computationally tractable.

**Classification Rule under Naive Bayes:**

$$
\hat{C} = \arg\max_C P(C \mid D) = \arg\max_C P(C) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid C)
$$

**Violation of the Naive Bayes Assumption in the Real World:**

Consider **spam detection in emails**. The Naive Bayes assumption states that the presence of *"free"* in an email is independent of the presence of *"offer"*, given that the email is spam. In reality, this is false — words like *"free"* and *"offer"* are **strongly correlated** in spam emails (they frequently co-occur in phrases like *"free offer"*). When a classifier sees *"free"*, it should be more likely to also see *"offer"*, but Naive Bayes treats them as if they were statistically unrelated.

This violation is why Naive Bayes, despite its simplicity, sometimes makes systematic errors. However, in practice, the classification accuracy is often surprisingly high (typically 90%+ on text classification tasks), suggesting that the assumption — though technically wrong — is not catastrophic for many real-world applications. More sophisticated models like **Logistic Regression**, **Maximum Entropy (MaxEnt) classifiers**, and **BERT-based neural classifiers** explicitly model word correlations and generally achieve higher accuracy.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Probability Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to normalize** in Bayes' theorem: Students frequently compute $P(A \mid B_i) \cdot P(B_i)$ for each hypothesis and pick the largest, forgetting to (a) acknowledge the normalization is implicit (acceptable if the comparison is clear) or (b) explicitly divide by the sum when true posterior probabilities are required. Examiners may deduct 1-2 marks if the methodology is unclear.
>
> 2. **Confusing prior with likelihood**: $P(B \mid A)$ is the *posterior*; $P(A \mid B)$ is the *likelihood*. Mixing these up is one of the most frequent errors in Bayesian problems.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Naive Bayes conditional independence assumption**: When asked to compute a joint probability $P(w_1, w_2 \mid C)$, students sometimes multiply the likelihoods without explicitly stating the assumption. Always write: *"By the Naive Bayes conditional independence assumption, $P(w_1, w_2 \mid C) = P(w_1 \mid C) \cdot P(w_2 \mid C)$."*
>
> 4. **Arithmetic errors in long chains of multiplication**: Probability questions often involve many small numbers multiplied together. Use **log-probabilities** to avoid underflow and to make arithmetic cleaner: $\log(a \cdot b) = \log a + \log b$.
>
> 5. **Skipping the derivation steps**: In a 7-mark sub-question, you must show every algebraic step. A single line "By Bayes' theorem, the result is..." will earn you at most 2-3 marks. Always show all intermediate work.
>
> 6. **Missing the partition condition in Total Probability**: The Law of Total Probability requires that the conditioning events form a *partition* (mutually exclusive and exhaustive). State this condition explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

The following is a **high-density rapid-revision checklist** for the topic *"Mathematical Essentials: Probability Theory"*.

### Foundational Concepts
- **Probability** is a function $P: \mathcal{F} \to [0, 1]$ satisfying the three Kolmogorov axioms.
- **Sample space** $\Omega$ = set of all outcomes. **Event** $A \subseteq \Omega$.
- **Random variable** = a function from $\Omega$ to $\mathbb{R}$.

### Core Formulas (must know cold)
- Complement: $P(A^c) = 1 - P(A)$.
- Addition: $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$.
- Conditional: $P(A \mid B) = P(A \cap B) / P(B)$.
- Chain rule: $P(A_1, \ldots, A_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(A_i \mid A_1, \ldots, A_{i-1})$.
- Total probability: $P(A) = \sum_i P(A \mid B_i) P(B_i)$.
- Bayes' theorem: $P(B \mid A) = P(A \mid B) P(B) / P(A)$.
- Independence: $P(A \cap B) = P(A) P(B)$.
- Conditional independence: $P(A, B \mid C) = P(A \mid C) P(B \mid C)$.

### Distributions to Know
- **Bernoulli** (binary outcomes), **Binomial** (count of successes), **Categorical/Multinoulli** (k-class output of softmax), **Gaussian** (continuous, bell curve), **Poisson** (word counts).

### Expectation & Variance
- $E[X] = \sum_x x P(x)$ (discrete) or $\int x f(x) dx$ (continuous).
- $E[aX + bY + c] = aE[X] + bE[Y] + c$ (linearity, *always*).
- $\text{Var}(X) = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2$.
- $\text{Cov}(X, Y) = E[XY] - E[X]E[Y]$.

### CL-Specific Applications
- **n-gram language model:** $P(W) \approx \prod_i P(w_i \mid w_{i-n+1}^{i-1})$.
- **Naive Bayes classification:** $\hat{C} = \arg\max_C P(C) \prod_i P(w_i \mid C)$.
- **POS tagging (HMM):** $\hat{t}_1^n = \arg\max P(t_1^n) \prod_i P(w_i \mid t_i) P(t_i \mid t_{i-1})$.
- **Entropy & perplexity:** $H(X) = -\sum_x P(x) \log_2 P(x)$; Perplexity = $2^H$.

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- Never confuse $P(A \mid B)$ with $P(B \mid A)$.
- Always state the Naive Bayes assumption explicitly.
- Use log-probabilities to avoid numerical underflow in long products.
- Verify that conditioning events form a partition before applying total probability.

### Examiner-Approved Format Tips
- **Always** define your notation at the start: "Let $A$ be the event..., $B$ be the event...".
- **Always** state which axiom, rule, or theorem you are applying at each step.
- **Always** show intermediate arithmetic; do not skip steps even if they seem trivial.
- **Use diagrams** wherever the question involves multiple interacting components (e.g., Bayes' theorem, HMMs).
- **Cite the chain rule and Bayes' theorem by name** when applying them.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Probability Space
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Probability Space

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the rigorous mathematical framework formalized by **Andrey Kolmogorov (1933)**, a **Probability Space** is a mathematical triplet that completely defines a random experiment in measure-theoretic terms. It is formally denoted as:

$$\left(\Omega, \mathcal{F}, P\right)$$

where each component is precisely defined as:

- **Sample Space (Ω)**: The set of *all* possible elementary outcomes of a random experiment. Every individual outcome is represented by a single element $\omega \in \Omega$.
- **Sigma-Algebra (Event Space, $\mathcal{F}$)**: A non-empty collection of subsets of $\Omega$ that is closed under complementation and countable unions. Each element $A \in \mathcal{F}$ is called an **event**.
- **Probability Measure (P)**: A function $P : \mathcal{F} \rightarrow [0, 1]$ that assigns a real number in the interval $[0, 1]$ to every event, satisfying Kolmogorov's three axioms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PEADT632):** In Computational Linguistics, the sample space $\Omega$ is the set of all possible strings (sentences) over a given alphabet, and $\mathcal{F}$ is typically the Borel σ-algebra on this discrete space. Probability spaces form the *mathematical backbone* of statistical language models, HMMs, and n-gram models.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuitive Picture

Imagine a **fair, six-sided die** rolled onto a perfectly flat, smooth table.

| Component | Formal Symbol | Die Analogy |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|
| Sample Space | $\Omega$ | The set $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$ — every face that *could* land up |
| Event Space | $\mathcal{F}$ | Every "question" you might ask about the roll: "Did I get an even number?" $\rightarrow \{2, 4, 6\}$ |
| Probability Measure | $P$ | The actual numerical chance: $P(\{4\}) = \frac{1}{6}$, $P(\text{even}) = \frac{3}{6}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three components and not just one?** Because the **experiment** (Ω), the **questions we want to ask** ($\mathcal{F}$), and the **numerical likelihoods** (P) are logically distinct ideas. The σ-algebra guarantees that no "garbage" question is asked, and the measure gives concrete numbers. In linguistics, this separation lets us cleanly separate *what a language is* ($\Omega$), *what we want to measure* ($\mathcal{F}$), and *how likely it is* (P).

## 1.3 The Three Kolmogorov Axioms

For a function $P$ to qualify as a valid probability measure, it must satisfy:

1. **Non-negativity:** $\forall A \in \mathcal{F}, \quad P(A) \geq 0$
2. **Normalization:** $P(\Omega) = 1$
3. **Countable Additivity (σ-additivity):** For any *countable* collection of pairwise disjoint events $\{A_i\}_{i=1}^{\infty}$:

$$P\!\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty} A_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} P(A_i\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Discrete vs. Continuous Ω:** In Computational Linguistics, $\Omega$ is almost always **countable** (finite or countably infinite), since strings over a finite alphabet are enumerable. This is why we often skip measure-theoretic subtleties and use simple **finite probability distributions** in practice. The continuous case arises in Bayesian inference over continuous parameter spaces.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Sigma-Algebra (σ-Algebra) — Detailed Structure

A collection $\mathcal{F} \subseteq 2^{\Omega}$ is a **σ-algebra** on $\Omega$ if and only if the following three closure properties hold:

1. **Contains the whole space:** $\Omega \in \mathcal{F}$
2. **Closed under complementation:** If $A \in \mathcal{F}$, then $A^c = \Omega \setminus A \in \mathcal{F}$
3. **Closed under countable unions:** If $A_1, A_2, A_3, \ldots \in \mathcal{F}$, then $\bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty} A_i \in \mathcal{F}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why σ-algebras and not just "any subset"?** In real analysis, some "pathological" sets (e.g., non-measurable sets via the Axiom of Choice) cannot be assigned consistent probabilities. σ-algebras restrict attention to "well-behaved" collections of events for which probability is always well-defined.

### Special Cases of σ-Algebras

| Type | Description | Linguistic Use |
|------|-------------|----------------|
| Trivial σ-algebra | $\mathcal{F} = \{\emptyset, \Omega\}$ | Only "always true" / "always false" |
| Power set | $\mathcal{F} = 2^{\Omega}$ | All subsets (default for countable Ω) |
| Borel σ-algebra | Generated by open sets | Continuous probability (rare in NLP) |

## 2.2 Constructing Probability Spaces — A Procedural Recipe

For any KTU exam problem, follow this three-step construction:

- **Step 1 (Define Ω):** Enumerate all elementary outcomes.
- **Step 2 (Define $\mathcal{F}$):** For finite/discrete $\Omega$, set $\mathcal{F} = 2^{\Omega}$. For continuous, generate the smallest σ-algebra containing all events of interest.
- **Step 3 (Define P):** Assign non-negative numbers to singletons summing to 1, then extend to all events via additivity.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers ~80% of probability questions in KTU ESE for Computational Linguistics.**

| # | Formula / Property | LaTeX Form | Domain of Validity |
|---|--------------------|-----------|---------------------|
| 1 | Probability Space | $(\Omega, \mathcal{F}, P)$ | All probability theory |
| 2 | Complement Rule | $P(A^c) = 1 - P(A)$ | Any valid probability space |
| 3 | Addition Rule (General) | $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$ | Any two events |
| 4 | Mutually Exclusive | If $A \cap B = \emptyset$, then $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)$ | Disjoint events only |
| 5 | Conditional Probability | $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$, $P(B) > 0$ | Bayes' framework |
| 6 | Chain Rule (Product) | $P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B)$ | Sequential events |
| 7 | Total Probability | $P(A) = \displaystyle\sum_{i} P(A \mid B_i) P(B_i)$ | Partition $\{B_i\}$ of $\Omega$ |
| 8 | Bayes' Theorem | $P(B_j \mid A) = \dfrac{P(A \mid B_j) P(B_j)}{\sum_i P(A \mid B_i) P(B_i)}$ | Prior → Posterior update |
| 9 | Independence | $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$ | Independent events |
| 10 | Inclusion-Exclusion | $P\!\left(\bigcup_i A_i\right) = \sum P(A_i) - \sum P(A_i \cap A_j) + \ldots$ | Finite collection |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **Computational Linguistics (PEADT632)**, probability spaces directly enable:

- **Language Models (n-grams):** $P(w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}, \ldots, w_{i-n+1})$
- **Hidden Markov Models (HMMs):** Joint distribution over hidden states and observations.
- **Speech Recognition:** Acoustic model $P(\text{phoneme} \mid \text{audio})$, language model $P(\text{word sequence})$.
- **Machine Translation:** Statistical MT scores translations via $P(\text{English} \mid \text{French})$.
- **Naive Bayes Text Classifiers:** Spam detection, sentiment analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Every "probability" you ever see in NLP — perplexity, entropy, likelihood — is implicitly a computation on a *probability space* $(\Omega, \mathcal{F}, P)$. Understanding this triplet is therefore non-negotiable for serious work in CL.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Proof of the Complement Rule

**Statement:** For any event $A \in \mathcal{F}$, $P(A^c) = 1 - P(A)$.

**Proof.** We use the fact that $A$ and $A^c$ are mutually exclusive (disjoint) and that $A \cup A^c = \Omega$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Identify the partition.} \quad & A \cap A^c = \emptyset, \quad A \cup A^c = \Omega \\
\text{Step 2: Apply Axiom 3 (countable additivity).} \quad & P(A \cup A^c) = P(A) + P(A^c) \\
\text{Step 3: Substitute from the partition identity.} \quad & P(\Omega) = P(A) + P(A^c) \\
\text{Step 4: Apply Axiom 2 (normalization, } P(\Omega) = 1\text{).} \quad & 1 = P(A) + P(A^c) \\
\text{Step 5: Rearrange to isolate } P(A^c). \quad & P(A^c) = 1 - P(A) \qquad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Proof of the Addition Rule

**Statement:** For any two events $A, B \in \mathcal{F}$:

$$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$$

**Proof.** The events $A$ and $B$ may overlap, so a naive sum $P(A) + P(B)$ *double-counts* the intersection. We must subtract it.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Partition } A \cup B. \quad & A \cup B = (A \setminus B) \cup (A \cap B) \cup (B \setminus A) \\
& \text{where the three pieces are pairwise disjoint.} \\
\text{Step 2: Apply additivity.} \quad & P(A \cup B) = P(A \setminus B) + P(A \cap B) + P(B \setminus A) \\
\text{Step 3: Note that } A = (A \setminus B) \cup (A \cap B). \quad & P(A) = P(A \setminus B) + P(A \cap B) \\
& \Rightarrow P(A \setminus B) = P(A) - P(A \cap B) \\
\text{Step 4: Similarly for } B. \quad & P(B \setminus A) = P(B) - P(A \cap B) \\
\text{Step 5: Substitute back.} \quad & P(A \cup B) = [P(A) - P(A \cap B)] + P(A \cap B) + [P(B) - P(A \cap B)] \\
\text{Step 6: Simplify.} \quad & P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B) \qquad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bayes' Theorem from Conditional Probability

**Statement:** $P(B_j \mid A) = \dfrac{P(A \mid B_j)\, P(B_j)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} P(A \mid B_i)\, P(B_i)}$, where $\{B_i\}_{i=1}^{n}$ partitions $\Omega$.

**Proof.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Start with definition of conditional probability.} \quad & P(B_j \mid A) = \frac{P(B_j \cap A)}{P(A)} \\
\text{Step 2: Apply chain rule to numerator.} \quad & P(B_j \cap A) = P(A \mid B_j)\, P(B_j) \\
\text{Step 3: Express } P(A) \text{ via law of total probability.} \quad & P(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(A \cap B_i) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(A \mid B_i)\, P(B_i) \\
\text{Step 4: Combine.} \quad & P(B_j \mid A) = \frac{P(A \mid B_j)\, P(B_j)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} P(A \mid B_i)\, P(B_i)} \qquad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** Consider a corpus where the vocabulary has only three words: $\Omega = \{\text{the, cat, sat}\}$. Define events $A = \{\text{the}\}$, $B = \{\text{cat, sat}\}$, $C = \{\text{the, cat}\}$. Probabilities are: $P(\text{the}) = 0.5$, $P(\text{cat}) = 0.3$, $P(\text{sat}) = 0.2$. Compute (i) $P(B)$, (ii) $P(A \cup C)$, (iii) $P(C \mid A)$, (iv) verify $P(A) + P(A^c) = 1$.

**Solution.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(i) } P(B) &= P(\{\text{cat, sat}\}) = P(\text{cat}) + P(\text{sat}) = 0.3 + 0.2 = 0.5 \\
\text{(ii) } P(A \cup C) &= P(A) + P(C) - P(A \cap C) \\
&= 0.5 + [0.5 + 0.3] - P(\{\text{the, cat}\}) \\
&= 0.5 + 0.8 - 0.8 = 0.5 \\
\text{(iii) } P(C \mid A) &= \frac{P(C \cap A)}{P(A)} = \frac{P(\{\text{the, cat}\})}{P(\{\text{the}\})} = \frac{0.8}{0.5} = 1.6 \\
\text{(iv) } A^c &= \{\text{cat, sat}\} \Rightarrow P(A^c) = 0.5, \quad P(A) + P(A^c) = 0.5 + 0.5 = 1 \;\checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Trap:** In part (iii), $P(C \mid A) = 1.6$ is technically invalid (must be $\leq 1$). This signals that the *events are not well-chosen*: $A \subseteq C$, so $A \cap C = A$, and conditional on $A$ we are *certain* to be in $C$. The correct ratio is $\frac{0.5}{0.5} = 1$, not $\frac{0.8}{0.5}$. The **arithmetic error** here costs full marks. Always check: $P(C \cap A) \leq P(A)$.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Architecture of a Probability Space

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["PROBABILITY SPACE (Ω, F, P)"] --> B["Sample Space Ω"]
    A --> C["Sigma-Algebra F"]
    A --> D["Probability Measure P"]
    B --> B1["Set of all elementary outcomes ω"]
    B --> B2["Example: all English sentences"]
    B --> B3["Example: all 6-sided die faces"]
    C --> C1["Collection of events A ⊆ Ω"]
    C --> C2["Closed under complement"]
    C --> C3["Closed under countable union"]
    D --> D1["Function P : F → [0, 1]"]
    D --> D2["Satisfies Kolmogorov Axioms"]
    D --> D3["A1: Non-negativity"]
    D --> D4["A2: Normalization P(Ω) = 1"]
    D --> D5["A3: Countable Additivity"]
    D2 --> D3
    D2 --> D4
    D2 --> D5
    C1 --> E["EVENT A"]
    E --> F["Numerical Probability P(A) ∈ [0,1]"]
    D1 --> F
```

## 4.2 Probability Computation Flow in Computational Linguistics

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Raw Text Corpus"] --> B["Tokenization"]
    B --> C["Vocabulary V = {w1, w2, ...}"]
    C --> D["Sample Space Ω = V* (all strings)"]
    D --> E["Define F = 2^Ω (discrete case)"]
    E --> F["Estimate P from frequencies"]
    F --> G["MLE: P(w) = count(w) / total"]
    G --> H["Smoothed Model (Laplace, Kneser-Ney)"]
    H --> I["Applications: LM, MT, ASR, Tagging"]
    F --> J["Verify Axioms Hold"]
    J --> K1["A1: All P(w) ≥ 0"]
    J --> K2["A2: Σ P(w) = 1"]
    J --> K3["A3: Additivity for disjoint events"]
```

## 4.3 Axiomatic Dependency Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1["Axiom 1: Non-negativity P(A) ≥ 0"] --> OUT["Valid Probability Space"]
    A2["Axiom 2: Normalization P(Ω) = 1"] --> OUT
    A3["Axiom 3: σ-additivity"] --> OUT
    A1 --> R1["Complement Rule: P(A^c) = 1 - P(A)"]
    A2 --> R1
    A1 --> R2["Addition Rule: P(A∪B) = P(A)+P(B)-P(A∩B)"]
    A3 --> R2
    A1 --> R3["Conditional Probability: P(A|B) = P(A∩B)/P(B)"]
    A2 --> R3
    A2 --> R3
    R3 --> R4["Bayes' Theorem"]
    A2 --> R4
    A1 --> R4
```

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a probability space. List Kolmogorov's three axioms with their mathematical statements.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **probability space** is a mathematical triplet $(\Omega, \mathcal{F}, P)$ consisting of:

- **$\Omega$ (Sample Space):** The set of all possible elementary outcomes of a random experiment.
- **$\mathcal{F}$ (σ-algebra):** A collection of subsets of $\Omega$ that is closed under complementation and countable unions. Each member is an event.
- **$P$ (Probability Measure):** A function $P : \mathcal{F} \to [0, 1]$ satisfying Kolmogorov's axioms:

**Axiom 1 (Non-negativity):** $\forall A \in \mathcal{F},\ P(A) \geq 0$

**Axiom 2 (Normalization):** $P(\Omega) = 1$

**Axiom 3 (Countable Additivity):** For pairwise disjoint $\{A_i\} \subseteq \mathcal{F}$:

$$P\!\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty} A_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} P(A_i)$$

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the role of the σ-algebra $\mathcal{F}$ in a probability space. Why is it needed instead of using all subsets of $\Omega$?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The σ-algebra $\mathcal{F}$ defines the set of *measurable* events — those subsets of $\Omega$ to which the probability measure $P$ can be consistently assigned. It serves three purposes:

1. **Restricts the domain of $P$** to well-behaved sets, avoiding pathological non-measurable sets (e.g., Vitali sets from the Axiom of Choice).
2. **Guarantees closure properties**: complements and countable unions of events remain events, ensuring operations are well-defined.
3. **Enables measure-theoretic consistency**: In discrete spaces, $\mathcal{F} = 2^{\Omega}$ is fine, but in continuous spaces (e.g., Borel σ-algebra on $\mathbb{R}$), not all subsets can be assigned a probability without contradictions like the Banach–Tarski paradox.

**Why not all subsets?** In continuous sample spaces, attempting to assign a probability to *every* subset leads to contradictions and paradoxes. The σ-algebra elegantly avoids these by restricting to a structured, closed family of sets.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`**

**(a)** Define a probability space formally. State and prove the **Addition Rule** $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$ for any two events $A, B \in \mathcal{F}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a text corpus, the probability of a word being a *noun* is $0.4$, *verb* is $0.3$, and *adjective* is $0.2$. Events $A$, $B$, $C$ denote "word is a noun", "word is a verb", "word is an adjective" respectively. Given $P(A \cup B) = 0.6$ and $P(B \cap C) = 0.05$, compute (i) $P(A \cap B)$, (ii) $P(A^c \cap C)$, (iii) $P(B \mid C)$. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Definition and Proof (7 marks):**

A **probability space** is a triplet $(\Omega, \mathcal{F}, P)$, where $\Omega$ is the sample space, $\mathcal{F}$ is a σ-algebra of events, and $P: \mathcal{F} \to [0, 1]$ is a probability measure satisfying the three Kolmogorov axioms.

**Proof of the Addition Rule:**

Let $A, B \in \mathcal{F}$. Decompose $A \cup B$ into three disjoint parts:

$$A \cup B = (A \cap B^c) \cup (A \cap B) \cup (A^c \cap B)$$

These three pieces are pairwise disjoint. By Axiom 3:

$$P(A \cup B) = P(A \cap B^c) + P(A \cap B) + P(A^c \cap B)$$

Now, $A = (A \cap B^c) \cup (A \cap B)$ (disjoint union), so:

$$P(A) = P(A \cap B^c) + P(A \cap B) \;\Rightarrow\; P(A \cap B^c) = P(A) - P(A \cap B)$$

Similarly, $P(A^c \cap B) = P(B) - P(A \cap B)$. Substituting:

$$P(A \cup B) = [P(A) - P(A \cap B)] + P(A \cap B) + [P(B) - P(A \cap B)]$$

$$\therefore\ P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B) \qquad \blacksquare$$

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

Given: $P(A) = 0.4$, $P(B) = 0.3$, $P(C) = 0.2$, $P(A \cup B) = 0.6$, $P(B \cap C) = 0.05$.

**(i) Find $P(A \cap B)$:** From the Addition Rule:

$$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$$

$$0.6 = 0.4 + 0.3 - P(A \cap B)$$

$$P(A \cap B) = 0.7 - 0.6 = 0.1$$

**(ii) Find $P(A^c \cap C)$:** Note that $C = (A \cap C) \cup (A^c \cap C)$ (disjoint), so:

$$P(A^c \cap C) = P(C) - P(A \cap C)$$

We need $P(A \cap C)$. Since we have no direct data, but from total probability: $P(A \cup B \cup C) \leq 1$. However, assuming $A, B, C$ cover all word classes in this problem (with $P(A) + P(B) + P(C) = 0.9$ leaving $0.1$ for "other" — prepositions, adverbs):

Using $P(A \cup C) = P(A) + P(C) - P(A \cap C)$. Without $P(A \cup C)$, use the complement approach:

$$P(A^c) = 1 - 0.4 = 0.6$$

Since $B \subseteq A^c \cup (A^c \cap C)$ and $B \cap C$ overlaps with $A^c \cap C$:

$$P(B \cap C) = P(B \cap A \cap C) + P(B \cap A^c \cap C)$$

Taking the simpler route: $P(A^c \cap C) = P(C) - P(A \cap C)$. Assuming $A$ and $C$ are disjoint (typical in basic POS problems): $P(A \cap C) \approx 0$, so $P(A^c \cap C) \approx 0.2$. 

In a strict setting where $A, B, C$ are mutually exclusive (word-class partition): $P(A \cap C) = 0$ and $P(B \cap C) = 0$ — but the problem states $P(B \cap C) = 0.05$, so they are *not* mutually exclusive.

A cleaner interpretation: $\Omega = A \cup B \cup C \cup D$ where $D$ is "other." Using the inclusion-exclusion principle:

$$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B) = 0.4 + 0.3 - P(A \cap B) = 0.6 \Rightarrow P(A \cap B) = 0.1$$

For $P(A^c \cap C)$: $A^c$ contains $B, C, D$. So $P(A^c \cap C) = P(C) - P(A \cap C)$. Without $P(A \cap C)$, we use $P(A \cup B \cup C) = P(A) + P(B) + P(C) - P(A \cap B) - P(A \cap C) - P(B \cap C) + P(A \cap B \cap C)$. Assuming triple intersection is negligible:

$$0.6 + P(C) - P(A \cap C) = 0.4 + 0.3 + 0.2 - 0.1 - P(A \cap C) - 0.05$$
$$0.6 + 0.2 - P(A \cap C) = 0.75 - P(A \cap C) \Rightarrow 0.8 = 0.75 \;\text{(contradiction if } P(A \cap C) \text{ cancels)}$$

Taking the standard KTU assumption: $P(A^c \cap C) = P(C) - P(A \cap C) = 0.2 - 0 = \mathbf{0.2}$ (assuming noun–adjective disjointness).

**(iii) Find $P(B \mid C)$:**

$$P(B \mid C) = \frac{P(B \cap C)}{P(C)} = \frac{0.05}{0.2} = 0.25$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`**

**(a)** State Bayes' Theorem. In a **Naive Bayes spam classifier**, the prior probability of spam is $P(S) = 0.3$ and the prior of ham is $P(H) = 0.7$. The probability that the word "FREE" appears given spam is $P(F \mid S) = 0.8$, and given ham is $P(F \mid H) = 0.1$. Compute the posterior probability $P(S \mid F)$ that an email is spam given it contains the word "FREE". **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Prove that if $A$ and $B$ are **independent events**, then $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$. Also show that independence of $A$ and $B$ implies independence of $A$ and $B^c$, with full proof. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Bayes' Theorem and Spam Classification (7 marks):**

**Statement of Bayes' Theorem:** For a partition $\{B_i\}$ of $\Omega$ and any event $A$ with $P(A) > 0$:

$$P(B_j \mid A) = \frac{P(A \mid B_j)\, P(B_j)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} P(A \mid B_i)\, P(B_i)}$$

**Computation:**

Given: $P(S) = 0.3$, $P(H) = 0.7$, $P(F \mid S) = 0.8$, $P(F \mid H) = 0.1$.

**Step 1:** Compute total probability of "FREE":

$$P(F) = P(F \mid S) P(S) + P(F \mid H) P(H)$$
$$P(F) = (0.8)(0.3) + (0.1)(0.7) = 0.24 + 0.07 = 0.31$$

**Step 2:** Apply Bayes' Theorem:

$$P(S \mid F) = \frac{P(F \mid S)\, P(S)}{P(F)} = \frac{(0.8)(0.3)}{0.31} = \frac{0.24}{0.31} \approx 0.7742$$

**Conclusion:** $\boxed{P(S \mid F) \approx 0.7742}$ — the email is classified as **spam** (since $P(S \mid F) > 0.5$).

**(b) Proof of Independence Properties (7 marks):**

**Claim 1:** If $A \perp B$, then $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$.

**Proof.** By definition of conditional probability: $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$ for $P(B) > 0$.

Independence is defined as $P(A \mid B) = P(A)$. Therefore:

$$P(A) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)} \;\Rightarrow\; P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B) \qquad \blacksquare$$

**Claim 2:** If $A \perp B$, then $A \perp B^c$.

**Proof.** We need to show $P(A \cap B^c) = P(A) \cdot P(B^c)$.

Using the partition $A = (A \cap B) \cup (A \cap B^c)$ (disjoint union), by additivity:

$$P(A) = P(A \cap B) + P(A \cap B^c)$$

By Claim 1 (independence): $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$. Substituting:

$$P(A) = P(A) \cdot P(B) + P(A \cap B^c)$$

By the complement rule: $P(B^c) = 1 - P(B)$. Rearranging:

$$P(A \cap B^c) = P(A) - P(A) \cdot P(B) = P(A)[1 - P(B)] = P(A) \cdot P(B^c)$$

This is exactly the definition of $A \perp B^c$. Hence $A$ and $B^c$ are independent. $\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to state all three components** of $(\Omega, \mathcal{F}, P)$ when defining a probability space (loses ~1 mark).
> 2. **Confusing $\Omega$ (outcomes) with $\mathcal{F}$ (events)** — a single outcome $\omega$ is *not* an event in general; events are *subsets* of $\Omega$.
> 3. **Skipping the closure properties** of σ-algebras (complement, countable union) — full credit requires all three properties.
> 4. **Arithmetic error in Bayes' Theorem** by not computing $P(A)$ in the denominator first (a common slip; full marks only for showing all intermediate steps).
> 5. **Assuming mutual exclusivity in word-class problems** — KTU often deliberately gives data where words can belong to multiple classes, so check carefully before applying $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Probability Space**

### 🎯 Core Definition
- Probability space = $(\Omega, \mathcal{F}, P)$ — a triplet of sample space, σ-algebra, and probability measure.
- $\Omega$: set of *all* elementary outcomes.
- $\mathcal{F}$: σ-algebra — collection of events, closed under complement and countable union.
- $P: \mathcal{F} \to [0, 1]$ — measure satisfying Kolmogorov's three axioms.

### 🎯 Kolmogorov's Three Axioms
- A1: Non-negativity — $P(A) \geq 0$
- A2: Normalization — $P(\Omega) = 1$
- A3: σ-additivity — for disjoint $\{A_i\}$: $P(\cup A_i) = \sum P(A_i)$

### 🎯 Essential Formulas (Most-Tested)
- Complement: $P(A^c) = 1 - P(A)$
- Addition: $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$
- Conditional: $P(A \mid B) = P(A \cap B) / P(B)$
- Chain: $P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B)$
- Bayes: $P(B \mid A) = P(A \mid B) P(B) / P(A)$
- Total Probability: $P(A) = \sum_i P(A \mid B_i) P(B_i)$
- Independence: $P(A \cap B) = P(A) P(B)$

### 🎯 σ-Algebra Properties (Closure)
- Contains $\Omega$.
- Closed under complement.
- Closed under countable unions.
- Equivalently closed under countable intersections (derived property).

### 🎯 CL-Specific Applications
- **Language models:** $(\Omega, \mathcal{F}, P)$ with $\Omega = \Sigma^*$ (all strings), $\mathcal{F} = 2^{\Omega}$, $P$ estimated by MLE.
- **Naive Bayes:** Uses conditional independence $P(w_1, \ldots, w_n \mid c) = \prod P(w_i \mid c)$.
- **HMMs, n-grams, MT, ASR** all live on probability spaces.

### 🎯 Common Exam Traps
- Don't confuse $\omega$ (outcome) with $A$ (event).
- $P(A \mid B)$ undefined when $P(B) = 0$.
- Independence $\neq$ Mutually exclusive (in fact, $P(A)=0$ or $P(B)=0$ are the only cases where both can hold).
- Continuous sample spaces need *Borel* σ-algebras, not the power set.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Study Tip:** Practice *constructing* probability spaces from linguistic scenarios (e.g., "given a corpus of 10,000 sentences, define the probability space for bigram model"). This is the most-asked KTU Part B question format. Master the triplet notation, the axioms, and Bayes' Theorem — and you have ~90% of the marks on this topic locked in.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conditional Probability and Independence
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Conditional Probability and Independence # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Conditional Probability In probability theory, **Conditional Probability** is the measure of the probability of an event occurring, given that another event has already occurred. If $A$ and $B$ are two events defined on a sample space $S$, with $P(B) > 0$, then the conditional probability of $A$ given $B$ is formally defined as: $$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$$ This is the foundational definition that bridges classical probability with probabilistic reasoning under partial information. The notation $P(A \mid B)$ is read as "the probability of $A$ given $B$." > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Conditional probability is the mathematical cornerstone of **statistical language models** in computational linguistics, including N-gram models, Hidden Markov Models (HMMs), and Bayesian classifiers. The famous Bayes' Theorem — used in Naive Bayes text classifiers and POS taggers — is a direct corollary of this concept. ## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — Real World Analogy Imagine you walk into a college classroom and randomly pick a student. The probability that the student is female is approximately 50% (assuming equal gender distribution). However, **if you are told beforehand that the student is wearing a bindi or a saree (a typical Indian traditional female attire), your belief about the student's gender instantly changes** — it now becomes much higher (say 95%). This is conditional probability in action. In simpler terms: - **Without extra information:** $P(\text{Female}) \approx 0.5$ - **With extra information (given the attire):** $P(\text{Female} \mid \text{Wears Saree}) \approx 0.95$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Intuition:** Conditional probability answers the question — *"How does my belief about event $A$ change when I learn that event $B$ has happened?"* It restricts our universe of possibilities from the entire sample space $S$ to just the outcomes where $B$ is true. ## 1.3 Formal Definition of Independence Two events $A$ and $B$ are said to be **statistically independent** if the occurrence of one does not affect the probability of the occurrence of the other. Mathematically: $$P(A \mid B) = P(A) \quad \text{and} \quad P(B \mid A) = P(B)$$ Equivalently, this leads to the multiplicative rule: $$P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mutually Exclusive $\neq$ Independent:** A common KTU examiner trap. If $A \cap B = \emptyset$ (mutually exclusive), the events are *highly dependent*, not independent. Knowing $A$ occurred guarantees $B$ did NOT occur. ## 1.4 Application Context in Computational Linguistics In computational linguistics, conditional probability forms the foundation of: - **N-gram Language Models:** $P(w_n \mid w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_{n-1})$ — probability of the next word given the previous words. - **Naive Bayes Text Classification:** $P(\text{class} \mid \text{document}) \propto P(\text{document} \mid \text{class}) \cdot P(\text{class})$ - **Speech Recognition (HMMs):** Transition and emission probabilities. - **Machine Translation:** Alignment models use conditional probabilities to model word-to-word correspondences. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Properties of Conditional Probability Let $A$ and $B$ be events in a sample space $S$ with $P(B) > 0$. The function $P(\cdot \mid B)$ defined on the events of $S$ satisfies three Kolmogorov axioms: 1. **Non-negativity:** $P(A \mid B) \geq 0$ for every event $A$. 2. **Normalization:** $P(S \mid B) = 1$ — the entire restricted sample space $B$ has probability 1 relative to itself. 3. **Countable Additivity:** If $A_1, A_2, A_3, \ldots$ are pairwise disjoint events, then: $$P\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty} A_i \,\Big|\, B\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} P(A_i \mid B)$$ ## 2.2 The Multiplication Rule (Chain Rule) Starting from the definition, we can rearrange: $$P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B)$$ This generalizes to $n$ events: $$P(A_1 \cap A_2 \cap \ldots \cap A_n) = P(A_1) \cdot P(A_2 \mid A_1) \cdot P(A_3 \mid A_1 \cap A_2) \cdots P(A_n \mid A_1 \cap \ldots \cap A_{n-1})$$ This chain rule is **the most important formula** for language model derivations in computational linguistics. ## 2.3 The Law of Total Probability If $B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_n$ form a **partition** of the sample space $S$ (i.e., they are pairwise disjoint and their union is $S$), then for any event $A$: $$P(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(A \mid B_i) \cdot P(B_i)$$ ## 2.4 Bayes' Theorem Derived directly from the symmetry of joint probability, this is the cornerstone of modern NLP: $$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$$ When combined with the law of total probability, the denominator expands: $$P(A_i \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A_i) \cdot P(A_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} P(B \mid A_j) \cdot P(A_j)}$$ ## 2.5 Independence — Extended Definitions - **Pairwise Independence:** $A$ and $B$ are independent if $P(A \cap B) = P(A) P(B)$. - **Mutual Independence:** Events $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n$ are mutually independent if for every subset: $$P\left(\bigcap_{i \in S} A_i\right) = \prod_{i \in S} P(A_i)$$ ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Formula** | **Mathematical Form** | **Use Case** | |-------------|----------------------|--------------| | Conditional Probability | $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$ | Direct computation | | Multiplication Rule | $P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B)$ | Joint from conditional | | Chain Rule (n events) | $P(A_1 \cap \ldots \cap A_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(A_i \mid A_1 \cap \ldots \cap A_{i-1})$ | N-gram models | | Total Probability | $P(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(A \mid B_i) \cdot P(B_i)$ | Marginalization | | Bayes' Theorem | $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$ | Posterior computation | | Independence (2 events) | $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$ | Independence test | | Mutual Independence (n) | $P\left(\bigcap A_i\right) = \prod P(A_i)$ | NLP assumption | | Complement Rule | $P(A' \mid B) = 1 - P(A \mid B)$ | Simplification | | Symmetry of Joint | $P(A \cap B) = P(B \cap A)$ | Bayes derivation | ## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Engineering - **Spam Filtering:** Naive Bayes classifiers assume word occurrences are conditionally independent given the spam/ham class. - **Autocorrect/Predictive Text:** Uses bigram/trigram conditional probabilities: $P(\text{“good”} \mid \text{“very”})$. - **Medical Diagnosis Systems:** Bayesian networks compute posterior probabilities of diseases given symptoms. - **Speech Recognition:** Acoustic models use $P(\text{phoneme} \mid \text{audio signal})$ to decode the most likely word sequence. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Conditional Probability from Sample Space Reduction **Setup:** Consider a finite sample space $S = \{e_1, e_2, \ldots, e_n\}$ with uniform probability $P(e_i) = \frac{1}{n}$. Let $A$ and $B$ be two events with $B \neq \emptyset$. **Step 1: Classical probability of A given the new information B.** We restrict our attention to outcomes in $B$ only. Under uniform distribution restricted to $B$: $$P(A \mid B) = \frac{\text{Number of outcomes in } A \cap B}{\text{Number of outcomes in } B}$$ **Step 2: Express in terms of probabilities.** Let $\vert S \vert = N$, $\vert A \cap B \vert = k$, $\vert B \vert = m$. Then: $$P(A \mid B) = \frac{k}{m} = \frac{k/N}{m/N} = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$$ This completes the derivation. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Multiplication Rule Starting from the definition: $$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$$ Multiplying both sides by $P(B)$: $$P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B)$$ This is the **multiplication rule**. It allows us to compute joint probability from conditional and marginal probabilities. ## 3.3 Derivation of the Chain Rule (General Case) For three events $A$, $B$, $C$: **Step 1:** Define $D = A \cap B$. Then $P(A \cap B \cap C) = P(D \cap C)$. **Step 2:** Apply the multiplication rule to $D$ and $C$: $$P(D \cap C) = P(C \mid D) \cdot P(D) = P(C \mid A \cap B) \cdot P(A \cap B)$$ **Step 3:** Apply the multiplication rule to $A$ and $B$: $$P(A \cap B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)$$ **Step 4:** Combine: $$P(A \cap B \cap C) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A) \cdot P(C \mid A \cap B)$$ ## 3.4 Derivation of Bayes' Theorem **Step 1:** From the definition of conditional probability: $$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$$ **Step 2:** From the symmetric definition for $P(B \mid A)$: $$P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(A)}$$ **Step 3:** Solve each for $P(A \cap B)$: $$P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)$$ **Step 4:** Equate and solve for $P(A \mid B)$: $$P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)$$ $$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$$ ## 3.5 Derivation of the Law of Total Probability Let $B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_n$ be a partition of $S$. Then for any event $A$: **Step 1:** Event $A$ can be expressed as the union of disjoint pieces: $$A = (A \cap B_1) \cup (A \cap B_2) \cup \ldots \cup (A \cap B_n)$$ **Step 2:** By the additivity axiom (since the pieces are disjoint): $$P(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(A \cap B_i)$$ **Step 3:** Apply the multiplication rule $P(A \cap B_i) = P(A \mid B_i) \cdot P(B_i)$: $$P(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(A \mid B_i) \cdot P(B_i)$$ ## 3.6 Detailed Numerical Example — Bayes' Theorem in NLP Context **Problem:** In a text corpus, 40% of documents are about technology (Tech), and 60% are about sports (Sports). The word "algorithm" appears in 30% of Tech documents and only 2% of Sports documents. If a randomly chosen document contains the word "algorithm", what is the probability that it is a Tech document? **Solution:** **Step 1:** Define events. - $T$: document is about Tech, $P(T) = 0.4$ - $S$: document is about Sports, $P(S) = 0.6$ - $A$: document contains "algorithm" **Step 2:** Identify the given conditionals. - $P(A \mid T) = 0.30$ (likelihood) - $P(A \mid S) = 0.02$ (likelihood) **Step 3:** Apply Bayes' theorem. $$P(T \mid A) = \frac{P(A \mid T) \cdot P(T)}{P(A)}$$ **Step 4:** Compute $P(A)$ using the law of total probability. $$P(A) = P(A \mid T) \cdot P(T) + P(A \mid S) \cdot P(S)$$ $$P(A) = (0.30)(0.4) + (0.02)(0.6)$$ $$P(A) = 0.12 + 0.012 = 0.132$$ **Step 5:** Substitute back. $$P(T \mid A) = \frac{(0.30)(0.4)}{0.132} = \frac{0.12}{0.132} \approx 0.909$$ **Conclusion:** Given that a document contains "algorithm", there is approximately a **90.9%** probability that it belongs to the Tech category. This is the operational core of the **Naive Bayes Classifier**. ## 3.7 Worked Example — Chain Rule for Bigram Model **Problem:** Compute $P(\text{“I love NLP”})$ using a bigram language model with the following probabilities: - $P(\text{“I”}) = 0.10$ - $P(\text{“love”} \mid \text{“I”}) = 0.40$ - $P(\text{“NLP”} \mid \text{“love”}) = 0.05$ **Step 1:** Apply the chain rule. $$P(\text{“I love NLP”}) = P(\text{“I”}) \cdot P(\text{“love”} \mid \text{“I”}) \cdot P(\text{“NLP”} \mid \text{“love”})$$ **Step 2:** Substitute numerical values. $$P(\text{“I love NLP”}) = (0.10) \cdot (0.40) \cdot (0.05)$$ **Step 3:** Multiply step by step. $$P(\text{“I love NLP”}) = 0.04 \cdot 0.05 = 0.002$$ **Result:** The probability of the sentence "I love NLP" under this bigram model is $0.002$ or $0.2\%$. ## 3.8 Independence Verification Example **Problem:** A fair die is rolled twice. Let $A$ = "first roll is 4" and $B$ = "sum of rolls is 7". Check if $A$ and $B$ are independent. **Step 1:** Compute $P(A) = \frac{1}{6}$. **Step 2:** Compute $P(B) = \frac{6}{36} = \frac{1}{6}$ (outcomes: (1,6), (2,5), (3,4), (4,3), (5,2), (6,1)). **Step 3:** Compute $P(A \cap B)$ — first roll is 4 AND sum is 7. This requires second roll to be 3: only outcome (4,3). $$P(A \cap B) = \frac{1}{36}$$ **Step 4:** Check the independence condition. $$P(A) \cdot P(B) = \frac{1}{6} \cdot \frac{1}{6} = \frac{1}{36} = P(A \cap B)$$ **Conclusion:** Since $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$, the events $A$ and $B$ are **independent**. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conditional Probability — Sample Space Restriction Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD S["Sample Space S
All Outcomes"] B["Event B
P B = 0.6"] A["Event A
P A = 0.4"] AB["Intersection A intersect B
P A intersect B = 0.25"] S --> B S --> A A --> AB B --> AB note1["When we learn B occurred,
we restrict our universe
from S to B only"] note2["P A given B = P A intersect B / P B
= 0.25 / 0.6 = 0.4167"] B -.- note1 AB -.- note2 ``` ## 4.2 Bayes' Theorem — Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR P_A["Prior P A
Likelihood P B given A"] P_BA["P B given A"] P_B["Evidence P B"] P_AGivenB["Posterior P A given B"] P_A --> P_AGivenB P_BA --> P_AGivenB P_B --> P_AGivenB P_BA --> P_B P_A --> P_B noteFlow["Posterior is proportional to
Likelihood times Prior
normalized by Evidence"] P_AGivenB -.- noteFlow style P_AGivenB fill:#FFD700 style P_B fill:#90EE90 ``` ## 4.3 Chain Rule — Sequential Decomposition Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD Joint["Joint Probability
P A1 intersect A2 intersect A3"] Step1["Step 1: P A1
Marginal of first event"] Step2["Step 2: P A2 given A1
Conditional on first"] Step3["Step 3: P A3 given A1 A2
Conditional on previous two"] Product["Product: P A1 times P A2 given A1 times P A3 given A1 A2"] Joint --> Product Step1 --> Product Step2 --> Product Step3 --> Product nlp_note["This is exactly how N-gram language models
decompose sentence probabilities"] Joint -.- nlp_note ``` ## 4.4 Independence vs Mutual Exclusivity — Conceptual Map ```mermaid flowchart TD Concept["Relationship between Two Events A and B"] Independent["Independent
P A intersect B = P A times P B
Knowing one does not affect the other"] MutuallyExclusive["Mutually Exclusive
A intersect B = empty set
P A intersect B = 0
Knowing one guarantees the other did NOT occur"] Dependent["Dependent
P A given B differs from P A
Knowing one changes probability of other"] Concept --> Independent Concept --> MutuallyExclusive Concept --> Dependent warning["KTU Trap: Independent and Mutually Exclusive
are NOT the same thing.
If P A and P B are both positive and
events are mutually exclusive,
they are NEVER independent."] Independent -.- warning MutuallyExclusive -.- warning ``` ## 4.5 Law of Total Probability — Partition Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD Space["Sample Space S"] B1["B1"] B2["B2"] B3["B3"] Bn["Bn (others)"] Space --> B1 Space --> B2 Space --> B3 Space --> Bn AInB1["A intersect B1"] AInB2["A intersect B2"] AInB3["A intersect B3"] AInBn["A intersect Bn"] B1 --> AInB1 B2 --> AInB2 B3 --> AInB3 Bn --> AInBn formula["P A = Sum of P A given Bi times P Bi
for all i from 1 to n"] Space -.- formula ``` ## 4.6 Naive Bayes Text Classification — Architecture Block ```mermaid flowchart TD Input["Input Document
Words w1, w2, ..., wn"] Prior["Class Priors
P Tech and P Sports"] Like1["Likelihood P w1 given class"] Like2["Likelihood P w2 given class"] Liken["Likelihood P wn given class"] Multiply["Multiply all likelihoods
with prior"] Denominator["Normalization
Sum over all classes"] Output["Posterior
P class given document"] Input --> Like1 Input --> Like2 Input --> Liken Prior --> Multiply Like1 --> Multiply Like2 --> Multiply Liken --> Multiply Multiply --> Denominator Denominator --> Output indNote["Assumption: Words are
conditionally independent
given the class"] Multiply -.- indNote ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEADT632] **Define conditional probability. State and prove the multiplication rule.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** If $A$ and $B$ are two events defined on a sample space $S$ with $P(B) > 0$, then the conditional probability of $A$ given $B$ is defined as: $$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$$ **Multiplication Rule:** $P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B)$ **Proof:** Starting from the definition of conditional probability: $$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$$ Multiplying both sides by $P(B)$: $$P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B) = P(A \cap B)$$ Therefore, $P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B)$. (QED) ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024, PEADT632] **Distinguish between mutually exclusive and independent events with a suitable example.** **Model Answer:** | **Aspect** | **Mutually Exclusive** | **Independent** | |-----------|----------------------|-----------------| | Definition | $A \cap B = \emptyset$ | $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$ | | Joint Probability | $P(A \cap B) = 0$ | $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$ | | Effect of one event | Occurrence of $A$ prevents $B$ | Occurrence of $A$ has no effect on $B$ | | Coexistence | Cannot both occur | Can occur simultaneously | **Example:** Tossing a fair coin. Let $A$ = "heads appears" and $B$ = "tails appears". - $A$ and $B$ are **mutually exclusive** (both cannot occur in a single toss). - They are **NOT independent** because $P(A \mid B) = 0 \neq P(A) = 0.5$. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** A very common mistake is to assume that mutually exclusive events are independent. If $A$ and $B$ are mutually exclusive and both have positive probability, they are **always dependent**, not independent. Examiners specifically test this misconception. --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A: [KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper, PEADT632] **(a) [7 Marks]** A bag contains 5 red balls and 3 blue balls. Two balls are drawn **without replacement**. Find the probability that: 1. Both balls are red. 2. The first is red and the second is blue. 3. At least one ball is blue. **(b) [7 Marks]** State and prove Bayes' theorem. Hence solve: In a factory, Machine A produces 40% of items with 5% defect rate, Machine B produces 35% with 3% defect rate, and Machine C produces 25% with 2% defect rate. If a randomly selected item is defective, find the probability it came from Machine A. ### Model Solution to Question A(a): **Given:** 5 red + 3 blue = 8 balls total. Two balls drawn without replacement. **Part (i): Probability both are red.** **Step 1:** $P(\text{1st red}) = \frac{5}{8}$ **Step 2:** After removing one red, 4 red and 3 blue remain (7 total). $$P(\text{2nd red} \mid \text{1st red}) = \frac{4}{7}$$ **Step 3:** Apply the multiplication rule. $$P(\text{both red}) = \frac{5}{8} \cdot \frac{4}{7} = \frac{20}{56} = \frac{5}{14}$$ **Part (ii): Probability first red, second blue.** **Step 1:** $P(\text{1st red}) = \frac{5}{8}$ **Step 2:** After removing one red, 4 red and 3 blue remain. $$P(\text{2nd blue} \mid \text{1st red}) = \frac{3}{7}$$ **Step 3:** Multiply. $$P(\text{1st red and 2nd blue}) = \frac{5}{8} \cdot \frac{3}{7} = \frac{15}{56}$$ **Part (iii): Probability at least one is blue.** Using the complement rule: $P(\text{at least one blue}) = 1 - P(\text{none blue}) = 1 - P(\text{both red})$. $$P(\text{at least one blue}) = 1 - \frac{5}{14} = \frac{14 - 5}{14} = \frac{9}{14}$$ ### Model Solution to Question A(b): **Bayes' Theorem Statement:** If $B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_n$ are mutually exclusive and exhaustive events and $A$ is any event with $P(A) > 0$, then for each $i$: $$P(B_i \mid A) = \frac{P(A \mid B_i) \cdot P(B_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} P(A \mid B_j) \cdot P(B_j)}$$ **Proof:** By the definition of conditional probability: $$P(B_i \mid A) = \frac{P(B_i \cap A)}{P(A)}$$ By the multiplication rule: $P(B_i \cap A) = P(A \mid B_i) \cdot P(B_i)$. By the law of total probability: $P(A) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P(A \mid B_j) \cdot P(B_j)$. Substituting: $$P(B_i \mid A) = \frac{P(A \mid B_i) \cdot P(B_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} P(A \mid B_j) \cdot P(B_j)} \quad \text{(QED)}$$ **Numerical Solution:** **Step 1:** Define events. - $B_1$: item from Machine A, $P(B_1) = 0.40$ - $B_2$: item from Machine B, $P(B_2) = 0.35$ - $B_3$: item from Machine C, $P(B_3) = 0.25$ - $D$: item is defective **Step 2:** Likelihoods. - $P(D \mid B_1) = 0.05$ - $P(D \mid B_2) = 0.03$ - $P(D \mid B_3) = 0.02$ **Step 3:** Compute $P(D)$ using total probability. $$P(D) = (0.05)(0.40) + (0.03)(0.35) + (0.02)(0.25)$$ $$P(D) = 0.020 + 0.0105 + 0.005 = 0.0355$$ **Step 4:** Apply Bayes' theorem. $$P(B_1 \mid D) = \frac{P(D \mid B_1) \cdot P(B_1)}{P(D)} = \frac{(0.05)(0.40)}{0.0355}$$ $$P(B_1 \mid D) = \frac{0.020}{0.0355} \approx 0.5634$$ **Conclusion:** The probability that the defective item came from Machine A is approximately **0.5634** or **56.34%**. --- ### Question B: [KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper, PEADT632 — Alternative Choice] **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of statistical independence between two events. Prove that if $A$ and $B$ are independent events with $P(A) > 0$ and $P(B) > 0$, then they cannot be mutually exclusive. **(b) [7 Marks]** A language model computes bigram probabilities from a corpus. The following counts are observed: total word tokens = 10,000. The word "data" occurs 200 times, "science" occurs 150 times, and the bigram "data science" occurs 40 times. Compute $P(\text{“science”} \mid \text{“data”})$ and $P(\text{“data science”})$ using the chain rule. Discuss the independence assumption in this context. ### Model Solution to Question B(a): **Definition of Independence:** Two events $A$ and $B$ are said to be statistically independent if the occurrence of one does not affect the probability of the occurrence of the other. Formally, $A$ and $B$ are independent if: $$P(A \mid B) = P(A) \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$$ **Proof that Independent Events with Positive Probability Cannot Be Mutually Exclusive:** **Step 1:** Assume for contradiction that $A$ and $B$ are both independent and mutually exclusive. **Step 2:** Since they are mutually exclusive, $A \cap B = \emptyset$, which means: $$P(A \cap B) = 0$$ **Step 3:** Since they are independent, by the multiplication rule: $$P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$$ **Step 4:** Equating both expressions: $$P(A) \cdot P(B) = 0$$ **Step 5:** Since we are given that $P(A) > 0$ and $P(B) > 0$, their product must be positive. This is a contradiction. **Conclusion:** Therefore, $A$ and $B$ cannot be simultaneously independent (with positive probabilities) and mutually exclusive. (QED) ### Model Solution to Question B(b): **Step 1:** Compute the marginal probability of "data". $$P(\text{“data”}) = \frac{200}{10{,}000} = 0.02$$ **Step 2:** Compute the conditional probability $P(\text{“science”} \mid \text{“data”})$. $$P(\text{“science”} \mid \text{“data”}) = \frac{P(\text{“data science”})}{P(\text{“data”})} = \frac{\text{Count of bigram}}{\text{Count of “data”}}$$ $$P(\text{“science”} \mid \text{“data”}) = \frac{40}{200} = 0.20$$ **Step 3:** Apply the chain rule to compute $P(\text{“data science”})$. $$P(\text{“data science”}) = P(\text{“data”}) \cdot P(\text{“science”} \mid \text{“data”})$$ $$P(\text{“data science”}) = (0.02)(0.20) = 0.004$$ **Step 4:** Discussion of Independence Assumption. In language modeling, words are generally **not independent**. For instance, observing "data" strongly increases the probability of seeing "science" afterwards, since they form a common collocation. If we (incorrectly) assumed independence, we would estimate: $$P(\text{“data science”}) \stackrel{?}{=} P(\text{“data”}) \cdot P(\text{“science”}) = (0.02)(0.015) = 0.0003$$ The true probability (0.004) is **more than 13 times higher** than the independence estimate (0.0003), confirming that words in natural language are highly dependent on their context. The **Naive Bayes assumption** of conditional independence is therefore a strong simplifying assumption that is "wrong but useful" in many practical NLP applications. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to apply the conditional probability formula** when computing bigram probabilities. Always use Count(bigram)/Count(first word), not Count(bigram)/Total words. > 2. **Confusing $P(A \cap B)$ with $P(A) \cdot P(B)$** as the same thing — they are only equal when $A$ and $B$ are independent. > 3. **Skipping the proof step** in "state and prove" questions. Examiners award separate marks for the statement, the setup, and the algebraic manipulation. > 4. **Not simplifying fractions** in the final answer. Always reduce $\frac{20}{56}$ to $\frac{5}{14}$. > 5. **Using the wrong base event** in Bayes' theorem. The denominator is $P(A)$, not $P(B_i)$. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Conditional Probability** measures the probability of an event $A$ given that another event $B$ has occurred: $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$. - The **denominator $P(B)$** must always be strictly positive for the formula to be valid. - The **Multiplication Rule** $P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B)$ follows directly from the conditional probability definition. - The **Chain Rule** generalizes the multiplication rule to $n$ events and is the foundation of N-gram language models in computational linguistics. - **Bayes' Theorem** $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$ converts a conditional probability into its reverse form, with applications in Naive Bayes classifiers and HMMs. - The **Law of Total Probability** marginalizes over a partition of the sample space: $P(A) = \sum_{i} P(A \mid B_i) \cdot P(B_i)$. - **Independent events** satisfy $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$. The occurrence of one does not affect the other. - **Independent $\neq$ Mutually Exclusive.** Mutually exclusive events with positive probabilities are always dependent. - The **Naive Bayes assumption** in NLP assumes words are conditionally independent given the class — a strong but useful approximation. - For KTU exams, always: (i) state definitions precisely, (ii) show all algebraic steps, (iii) simplify final answers, and (iv) verify the validity of the conditional ($P(B) > 0$).

topics.title: Bayes Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bayes Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bayes' Theorem (Mathematical Statement)**
> Bayes' Theorem is a fundamental rule in probability theory that describes the probability of an event $A$ occurring, given that another event $B$ has already occurred (the conditional probability of $A$ given $B$), in terms of the reverse conditional probability and the individual marginal probabilities of $A$ and $B$.

Formally, for two events $A$ and $B$ where $P(B) > 0$:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$$

Where:
- $P(A \mid B)$ = **Posterior probability** — the updated probability of hypothesis $A$ after observing evidence $B$.
- $P(A)$ = **Prior probability** — the initial belief about $A$ before observing evidence.
- $P(B \mid A)$ = **Likelihood** — the probability of observing evidence $B$ assuming $A$ is true.
- $P(B)$ = **Evidence** (or *marginal likelihood*) — the total probability of observing $B$ under all possible hypotheses.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Doctor-Test Analogy**
> Imagine a disease that affects **1%** of the population. A test for the disease is **99% accurate** (it correctly identifies the disease 99% of the time, and correctly identifies its absence 99% of the time). If a random person tests **positive**, what is the probability they actually have the disease?
>
> Most people intuitively answer *"99%"*. They are wrong.
>
> - Out of 10,000 people: 100 actually have the disease, 9,900 do not.
> - Of the 100 sick: ~99 will test positive (**True Positives**).
> - Of the 9,900 healthy: ~99 will test positive (**False Positives**).
> - Total positives: $99 + 99 = 198$.
> - Probability of being sick given a positive test = $99 / 198 \approx \mathbf{50\%}$.
>
> This counter-intuitive result is precisely what Bayes' Theorem quantifies — the **"base rate"** of the prior dramatically shifts the answer.

### 1.3 Why Bayes' Theorem is Central to Computational Linguistics

In language technology, we are almost always **inferring hidden structure from observed text**. Some canonical NLP problems that reduce to Bayes' Theorem:

| Hidden Variable ($A$) | Observed Evidence ($B$) | NLP Task |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Correct word sense | Context words | Word Sense Disambiguation |
| Part-of-Speech tag | Word + context | POS Tagging (HMM) |
| Author / Spam class | Email content | Text Classification |
| Next word | Previous words | Language Modelling |
| Phoneme sequence | Audio signal | Speech Recognition |
| Translation | Source sentence | Statistical Machine Translation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In every NLP task above, the engineer's goal is to compute $P(\text{Hypothesis} \mid \text{Evidence})$ — the **posterior** — directly using Bayes' Theorem, because the prior $P(A)$ and likelihood $P(B \mid A)$ are far easier to estimate from data than the posterior itself.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Axiomatic Foundation

Bayes' Theorem is not an axiom; it is a **derived consequence** of the Kolmogorov axioms of probability. It rests on the following building blocks:

**2.1.1 Conditional Probability**
The probability of event $A$ given that $B$ has occurred:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}, \quad P(B) > 0$$

**2.1.2 Multiplication Rule (Chain Rule for two events)**
From the definition above:

$$P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)$$

**2.1.3 Law of Total Probability**
If $\{B_1, B_2, \dots, B_n\}$ is a **partition** of the sample space (i.e., the $B_i$ are mutually exclusive and $\bigcup_i B_i = \Omega$):

$$P(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(A \mid B_i) \cdot P(B_i)$$

**2.1.4 Bayes' Theorem (Expanded Form)**
Substituting the law of total probability into the denominator:

$$P(A_j \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A_j) \cdot P(A_j)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B \mid A_i) \cdot P(A_i)}$$

### 2.2 Why It Works — The "How" and "Why" Step-by-Step

- **Why reverse conditioning?** Often, causal direction in language data is hard to model. We see the *effect* (an observed sentence) and want to know the *cause* (the underlying intent or tag). Bayes flips the conditioning direction so we can model what is empirically easy to count.
- **How priors are obtained:** From large unlabeled corpora via simple counting: $P(A) \approx \frac{\text{count}(A)}{N}$.
- **How likelihoods are obtained:** From annotated data: $P(B \mid A) \approx \frac{\text{count}(A, B)}{\text{count}(A)}$.
- **How the evidence normalizes:** $P(B)$ acts as a *normalizing constant* ensuring the posteriors over all hypotheses sum to 1, producing a valid probability distribution.

### 2.3 Specialised Forms Used in Computational Linguistics

**2.3.1 Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) Decision**
For classification, we do not need the exact posterior — only the most probable class:

$$\hat{A} = \arg\max_{A} P(A \mid B) = \arg\max_{A} \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$$

Since $P(B)$ does not depend on $A$, it is dropped:

$$\hat{A} = \arg\max_{A} P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **decision rule** used by every Naive Bayes spam filter, sentiment classifier, and HMM-based tagger.

**2.3.2 Log-Odds Form (numerically stable)**
Taking $\log$ converts the product to a sum, preventing underflow with long documents:

$$\log P(A \mid B) = \log P(B \mid A) + \log P(A) - \log P(B)$$

**2.3.3 Naive Bayes Assumption (Conditional Independence)**
For a document $B$ composed of features $(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_k)$ (e.g., words), and class $A$:

$$P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_k \mid A) = \prod_{i=1}^{k} P(w_i \mid A)$$

This allows tractable estimation of $P(B \mid A)$ for high-dimensional text data.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Bayes' Theorem (basic) | $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(B \mid A)\,P(A)}{P(B)}$ | Inverting any conditional |
| 2 | Bayes' Theorem (multi-hypothesis) | $P(A_j \mid B) = \dfrac{P(B \mid A_j)\,P(A_j)}{\sum_i P(B \mid A_i)\,P(A_i)}$ | Classification with $>2$ classes |
| 3 | Law of Total Probability | $P(A) = \sum_i P(A \mid B_i)\,P(B_i)$ | Computing marginals $P(B)$ |
| 4 | Chain Rule (general) | $P(A_1,\dots,A_n) = \prod_i P(A_i \mid A_{<i})$ | Language model $P(w_1,\dots,w_n)$ |
| 5 | MAP Decision Rule | $\hat{A} = \arg\max_A P(B \mid A)\,P(A)$ | Spam / sentiment / POS tag |
| 6 | Log-form (numerical stability) | $\log P(A \mid B) \propto \log P(B \mid A) + \log P(A)$ | Long documents, small probs |
| 7 | Naive Bayes Decomposition | $P(B \mid A) = \prod_i P(w_i \mid A)$ | Text classification |
| 8 | Odds Ratio (for binary tasks) | $\dfrac{P(A \mid B)}{P(\neg A \mid B)} = \dfrac{P(B \mid A)}{P(B \mid \neg A)} \cdot \dfrac{P(A)}{P(\neg A)}$ | Feature weighting |
| 9 | Posterior $\propto$ Likelihood $\times$ Prior | $P(A \mid B) \propto P(B \mid A)\,P(A)$ | Quick mental computation |
| 10 | Independence criterion | $P(A \cap B) = P(A)\,P(B)$ | Verifying Naive Bayes assumption |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The proportional form $P(A \mid B) \propto P(B \mid A)\,P(A)$ is the most frequently tested line in KTU exams and is the foundation of nearly every probabilistic NLP model.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Computational Linguistics

- **Statistical Language Models:** Estimating $P(\text{next word} \mid \text{history})$ from text corpora — a direct conditional probability, with the marginal $P(\text{history})$ as the normalizer.
- **Speech Recognition:** Acoustic Model $P(\text{phoneme} \mid \text{audio})$ combined with Language Model $P(\text{word sequence})$ via Bayes' rule to recover the most likely word sequence.
- **Information Retrieval & Reranking:** Combining query likelihood with document priors.
- **Word Sense Disambiguation:** Choosing the sense $s$ maximising $P(s \mid \text{context}) \propto P(\text{context} \mid s)\,P(s)$.
- **Bayesian Neural Networks / Topic Models (LDA):** Generative probabilistic models rely on repeated application of Bayes' rule with priors (Dirichlet, Gaussian).
