> 4. **Kleene star is infinite** — never try to "list" all elements; describe the pattern.
> 5. **Sets vs. multisets** — duplicate elements in concatenation collapse in sets; show this clearly in computations.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Notation":**

- ☐ **Alphabet** $\Sigma$ is a **finite, non-empty** set of symbols.
- ☐ **String** $w$ is a **finite** sequence of symbols; **$\vert w \vert$** = its length.
- ☐ **Empty string** $\varepsilon$ has length **0** and is the concatenation identity.
- ☐ **Concatenation** is **associative** but **not commutative** in general.
- ☐ **String power**: $w^0 = \varepsilon$, $w^n = w \cdot w^{n-1}$, $\vert w^n \vert = n \cdot \vert w \vert$.
- ☐ **Reverse**: $w^R$ reverses symbol order; $(xy)^R = y^R x^R$ (anti-commutative).
- ☐ **Kleene star** $\Sigma^*$ = all strings over $\Sigma$ (including $\varepsilon$).
- ☐ **Kleene plus** $\Sigma^+$ = all non-empty strings = $\Sigma^* - \{\varepsilon\}$.
- ☐ **Language** $L \subseteq \Sigma^*$ is a set of strings.
- ☐ **Language operations**: $L \cup M$, $LM$, $L^n$, $L^*$, $L^R$, $\overline{L}$.
- ☐ **Formal grammar** $G = (V, T, P, S)$ — 4-tuple of non-terminals, terminals, productions, and start symbol.
- ☐ **Derivation**: $\Rightarrow$ (one step), $\Rightarrow^*$ (zero or more steps).
- ☐ **Set operations** to remember: $\cup, \cap, -, \subseteq, \mathcal{P}(A), \vert A \vert$.
- ☐ **Function** $f: A \to B$ has domain, codomain, range — distinguish carefully.
- ☐ **Graph** $G = (V, E)$, **Tree** = connected acyclic graph, **rooted tree** has a designated root.
- ☐ Always write **$\vert w \vert$** with the cardinality bars, never with vertical pipes inside markdown tables (use `\vert`).
- ☐ The order of $L_1 L_2$ matters: $L_1 L_2 \neq L_2 L_1$ in general.
- ☐ $\emptyset$ (empty set) and $\{\varepsilon\}$ (language with empty string) are **different languages** — a classic KTU trick question.

> **End of Module 1 — Notation Notes**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Joint and Conditional Distributions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Joint and Conditional Distributions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

In **Computational Linguistics**, every task — from speech recognition to machine translation, part-of-speech tagging, and language modeling — is fundamentally built upon the mathematics of **probability distributions over words, sentences, and linguistic events**. Two of the most foundational constructs in this probability framework are the **Joint Probability Distribution** and the **Conditional Probability Distribution**.

---

## 1.1 Joint Probability Distribution

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Joint Probability Distribution** — Let $X$ and $Y$ be two discrete random variables defined on a sample space $\Omega$. The **joint probability mass function (PMF)** is the function
> $$P(X = x, Y = y)$$
> that assigns to every ordered pair $(x, y)$ a non-negative real number such that the total probability over all possible pairs sums to **1**:
> $$\sum_{x \in X}\sum_{y \in Y} P(X = x, Y = y) = 1$$

For continuous random variables, this is replaced by the **joint probability density function (PDF)** $f_{X,Y}(x, y)$ with the property
$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X,Y}(x, y)\, dx\, dy = 1$$

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a classroom where each student is described by **two attributes**:
- $X$ = the student's **height category** {Short, Medium, Tall}
- $Y$ = the student's **favorite cuisine** {Dosa, Biriyani, Pizza}

A *joint distribution* answers the question: **"What is the probability that a randomly chosen student is BOTH Tall AND loves Pizza?"** It treats the two attributes as a *single combined event*. So $P(X = \text{Tall}, Y = \text{Pizza})$ is the chance of a specific pair occurring together.

In **NLP terms**: $P(\text{word}_1 = \text{"The"}, \text{word}_2 = \text{"cat"})$ is the joint probability of seeing the bigram *"The cat"*. This is the foundational quantity behind **n-gram language models**.

---

## 1.2 Conditional Probability Distribution

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conditional Probability Distribution** — Given two random variables $X$ and $Y$, the **conditional probability** of $X = x$ **given** that $Y = y$ has occurred (and $P(Y = y) > 0$) is defined as
> $$P(X = x \mid Y = y) = \frac{P(X = x, Y = y)}{P(Y = y)}$$
> The function $P(X \mid Y = y)$ for fixed $y$ is called the conditional distribution of $X$ given $Y = y$.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Continuing the classroom example: a *conditional distribution* restricts our universe to a *sub-population* and asks a refined question.
> "**GIVEN** that a student loves Pizza, what is the probability they are Tall?"

We are not asking about the whole class anymore — we have zoomed into only the Pizza-loving students. In NLP, this is exactly how **language models** work:
- $P(\text{next word} \mid \text{previous words})$ — probability of the next word **given** the context.
- $P(\text{tag} \mid \text{word})$ — probability of a part-of-speech tag **given** a particular word.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU** — Conditional distributions are the *workhorse* of Computational Linguistics. Almost every model (HMMs, CRFs, N-gram models, Neural LMs) is a conditional distribution in disguise.

---

## 1.3 Marginal Probability (Companion Concept)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marginal Probability** — The probability of one variable *alone*, obtained by summing/integrating out the other variable from the joint distribution:
> $$P(X = x) = \sum_{y} P(X = x, Y = y) \quad \text{(discrete)}$$
> $$f_X(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X,Y}(x, y)\, dy \quad \text{(continuous)}$$

This operation is called **marginalization** — it "collapses" a multi-dimensional table into a one-dimensional one.

---

## 1.4 Independence

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Two random variables $X$ and $Y$ are **independent** if and only if
> $$P(X = x, Y = y) = P(X = x) \cdot P(Y = y) \quad \forall\, x, y$$
> Equivalently, $P(X = x \mid Y = y) = P(X = x)$. Knowing $Y$ provides **no information** about $X$.

**Real-world metric reference:** A useful **standard metric** is the **chain rule depth** — the number of conditional dependencies a model captures. A bigram model has depth **2**, while a trigram model has depth **3**.


<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Big Picture: How the Three Distributions Interact

The joint, marginal, and conditional distributions form a **triangle of relationships**. Any one of them can be recovered from the other two. The core relationships are:

### 2.1.1 Product Rule (Multiplication Rule)
$$P(X, Y) = P(X \mid Y) \cdot P(Y) = P(Y \mid X) \cdot P(X)$$

**Why this matters:** This is the fundamental identity that allows us to *decompose* a complex joint distribution into simpler conditional + marginal pieces. Neural language models and Bayes' rule both rely on this.

### 2.1.2 Sum Rule (Marginalization)
$$P(X) = \sum_{y} P(X, Y = y)$$

**Why this matters:** It tells us how to *aggregate* information across an entire dimension. In language modeling, summing over all possible next-words yields the probability of a context.

### 2.1.3 Chain Rule of Probability
For $n$ random variables $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$:
$$P(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(X_i \mid X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_{i-1})$$

**Why this matters in NLP:** This is the **mathematical foundation of language modeling**. A sentence $W = w_1 w_2 \ldots w_n$ has probability
$$P(w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n) = P(w_1) \cdot P(w_2 \mid w_1) \cdot P(w_3 \mid w_1, w_2) \cdots P(w_n \mid w_1, \ldots, w_{n-1})$$

### 2.1.4 Bayes' Theorem
$$P(Y \mid X) = \frac{P(X \mid Y) \cdot P(Y)}{P(X)}$$

**Why this matters in NLP:** This is the engine behind **Naive Bayes classifiers**, **statistical machine translation**, **speech recognition decoders**, and **Bayesian inference for language models**. It lets us *invert* a conditional distribution when the natural direction of the data is reversed.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Statement | When to Use (NLP Context) |
|---|--------------|------------------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | Joint PMF | $P(X=x, Y=y) \geq 0,\ \sum_{x,y} P = 1$ | Defining probability of a word pair or (word, tag) pair |
| 2 | Conditional PMF | $P(X \mid Y=y) = \frac{P(X, Y=y)}{P(Y=y)}$ | Predicting next word given context |
| 3 | Marginal (discrete) | $P(X=x) = \sum_{y} P(X=x, Y=y)$ | Summing out hidden variables (e.g., tags) |
| 4 | Marginal (continuous) | $f_X(x) = \int f_{X,Y}(x,y)\, dy$ | Continuous-feature models (e.g., word embeddings) |
| 5 | Product Rule | $P(X, Y) = P(X \mid Y) P(Y)$ | Decomposing joint into simpler terms |
| 6 | Chain Rule ($n$ vars) | $P(X_1, \ldots, X_n) = \prod_{i} P(X_i \mid X_{<i})$ | Sentence probability in language models |
| 7 | Bayes' Theorem | $P(Y \mid X) = \frac{P(X \mid Y) P(Y)}{P(X)}$ | Naive Bayes, sentiment classification, POS tagging |
| 8 | Independence | $P(X, Y) = P(X) P(Y)$ | Naive Bayes assumption (features independent given class) |
| 9 | Conditional Independence | $P(X \mid Y, Z) = P(X \mid Z)$ | HMM assumption (tag depends only on previous tag) |
| 10 | Expectation (discrete) | $\mathbb{E}[X] = \sum_{x} x \cdot P(X=x)$ | Expected word count, expected loss |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility** — In real-world NLP pipelines (Google Translate, Apple Siri, OpenAI GPT), these formulas are implemented as **log-probabilities** to prevent numerical underflow. The chain rule becomes $\log P = \sum \log P(X_i \mid X_{<i})$, and Bayes' rule becomes $\log P(Y \mid X) = \log P(X \mid Y) + \log P(Y) - \log P(X)$.

---

## 2.3 Worked Example Setup (For Section 3)

Consider a small **tagged corpus** (used in Part-of-Speech tagging):

| Sentence | Word | Tag (Y) |
|----------|------|---------|
| 1 | The | DET |
| 2 | cat | NOUN |
| 3 | sat | VERB |
| 4 | The | DET |
| 5 | dog | NOUN |
| 6 | ran | VERB |
| 7 | The | DET |
| 8 | cat | NOUN |

Let $X$ = Word, $Y$ = Tag, with vocabulary $\{$The, cat, sat, dog, ran$\}$ and tagset $\{$DET, NOUN, VERB$\}$.


<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Conditional Probability from Joint Probability

We start from the **definition of probability of an intersection** for two events $A$ and $B$:
$$P(A \cap B) = \frac{\text{Number of outcomes in both } A \text{ and } B}{\text{Total number of outcomes}}$$

**Step 1 — Restrict the sample space.**
Given that event $B$ has occurred, the new sample space is $B$ itself. The conditional probability of $A$ given $B$ is:
$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{\text{Number of outcomes in } A \cap B}{\text{Number of outcomes in } B}$$

**Step 2 — Divide numerator and denominator by the total number of outcomes $\vert \Omega \vert$.**
$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{\frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert \Omega \vert}}{\frac{\vert B \vert}{\vert \Omega \vert}} = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$$

**Step 3 — Rewrite $P(A \cap B)$ as $P(A, B)$ (joint probability).**
$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A, B)}{P(B)}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute random variables $A \to X=x$ and $B \to Y=y$.**
$$P(X = x \mid Y = y) = \frac{P(X = x, Y = y)}{P(Y = y)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is the **foundation of all conditional reasoning in NLP**. The KTU board often asks students to *re-derive* this identity — memorize the logical steps, not just the final formula.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Chain Rule of Probability

We prove the chain rule by repeated application of the product rule $P(A, B) = P(A \mid B) P(B)$.

**Step 1 — Start with the joint probability of $n$ variables.**
$$P(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the product rule to the first two variables.**
$$P(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n) = P(X_1) \cdot P(X_2, \ldots, X_n \mid X_1)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the product rule recursively to the conditional term.**
$$P(X_2, \ldots, X_n \mid X_1) = P(X_2 \mid X_1) \cdot P(X_3, \ldots, X_n \mid X_1, X_2)$$

**Step 4 — Continue recursion until only one variable remains.**
After $n - 1$ recursive applications:
$$P(X_1, \ldots, X_n) = P(X_1) \cdot P(X_2 \mid X_1) \cdot P(X_3 \mid X_1, X_2) \cdots P(X_n \mid X_1, \ldots, X_{n-1})$$

**Step 5 — Write compactly using product notation.**
$$P(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(X_i \mid X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_{i-1})$$

where $X_1, \ldots, X_{i-1}$ is conventionally denoted as $X_{<i}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Bayes' Theorem

We begin from the symmetric product rule:

**Step 1 — Write the product rule in two ways.**
From the definition, we have:
$$P(X, Y) = P(X \mid Y) \cdot P(Y) \quad \text{(1)}$$
$$P(X, Y) = P(Y \mid X) \cdot P(X) \quad \text{(2)}$$

**Step 2 — Equate the right-hand sides of (1) and (2).**
$$P(X \mid Y) \cdot P(Y) = P(Y \mid X) \cdot P(X)$$

**Step 3 — Divide both sides by $P(Y)$ (assuming $P(Y) > 0$).**
$$P(X \mid Y) = \frac{P(Y \mid X) \cdot P(X)}{P(Y)}$$

**Step 4 — Rename variables to match standard convention ($X$ = evidence, $Y$ = hypothesis).**
$$P(\text{Hypothesis} \mid \text{Evidence}) = \frac{P(\text{Evidence} \mid \text{Hypothesis}) \cdot P(\text{Hypothesis})}{P(\text{Evidence})}$$

In **NLP terminology**:
$$P(\text{tag} \mid \text{word}) = \frac{P(\text{word} \mid \text{tag}) \cdot P(\text{tag})}{P(\text{word})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip** — The terms in Bayes' theorem have specific names: $P(\text{Hypothesis})$ is the **prior**, $P(\text{Evidence} \mid \text{Hypothesis})$ is the **likelihood**, $P(\text{Hypothesis} \mid \text{Evidence})$ is the **posterior**, and $P(\text{Evidence})$ is the **evidence** (or normalizing constant). Using these terms in your answer earns you extra marks.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example — Joint and Conditional Computation

Using the corpus from **Section 2.3**, compute all relevant joint, marginal, and conditional probabilities.

### Step 1 — Build the Joint Frequency Table
Counting co-occurrences of (Word, Tag) pairs from the 8-token corpus:

| Word \ Tag | DET | NOUN | VERB | **Row Total** |
|------------|-----|------|------|---------------|
| The        | 3   | 0    | 0    | **3**         |
| cat        | 0   | 2    | 0    | **2**         |
| sat        | 0   | 0    | 1    | **1**         |
| dog        | 0   | 1    | 0    | **1**         |
| ran        | 0   | 0    | 1    | **1**         |
| **Col Total** | **3** | **3** | **2** | **8**     |

### Step 2 — Convert Frequencies to Joint Probabilities
Divide each cell by the grand total $N = 8$:
$$P(X = \text{``The''}, Y = \text{DET}) = \frac{3}{8} = 0.375$$
$$P(X = \text{``cat''}, Y = \text{NOUN}) = \frac{2}{8} = 0.250$$
$$P(X = \text{``sat''}, Y = \text{VERB}) = \frac{1}{8} = 0.125$$
$$P(X = \text{``dog''}, Y = \text{NOUN}) = \frac{1}{8} = 0.125$$
$$P(X = \text{``ran''}, Y = \text{VERB}) = \frac{1}{8} = 0.125$$

**Verification of normalization:**
$$0.375 + 0.250 + 0.125 + 0.125 + 0.125 = 1.000 \;\checkmark$$

### Step 3 — Compute Marginal Probabilities (Sum Rule)
$$P(Y = \text{DET}) = \sum_{x} P(X = x, Y = \text{DET}) = 0.375 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 = 0.375$$
$$P(Y = \text{NOUN}) = 0 + 0.250 + 0 + 0.125 + 0 = 0.375$$
$$P(Y = \text{VERB}) = 0 + 0 + 0.125 + 0 + 0.125 = 0.250$$
$$P(X = \text{``The''}) = 0.375, \quad P(X = \text{``cat''}) = 0.250$$

### Step 4 — Compute Conditional Probabilities
Using the formula $P(X \mid Y) = \frac{P(X, Y)}{P(Y)}$:

**Compute $P(X = \text{``cat''} \mid Y = \text{NOUN})$:**
$$P(X = \text{``cat''} \mid Y = \text{NOUN}) = \frac{P(\text{``cat''}, \text{NOUN})}{P(Y = \text{NOUN})} = \frac{0.250}{0.375} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.6667$$

**Interpretation:** Given a randomly chosen noun, there is a **66.67%** chance the word is *"cat"*.

**Compute $P(Y = \text{VERB} \mid X = \text{``sat''})$:**
$$P(Y = \text{VERB} \mid X = \text{``sat''}) = \frac{P(\text{``sat''}, \text{VERB})}{P(X = \text{``sat''})} = \frac{0.125}{0.125} = 1.000$$

**Interpretation:** The word *"sat"* is **always** a verb in this corpus — a deterministic tag.

### Step 5 — Apply Bayes' Theorem (Inverting the Direction)
**Problem:** Compute $P(Y = \text{NOUN} \mid X = \text{``cat''})$ using Bayes' theorem, given:
- $P(X = \text{``cat''} \mid Y = \text{NOUN}) = \frac{2}{3}$ (likelihood)
- $P(Y = \text{NOUN}) = 0.375$ (prior)
- $P(X = \text{``cat''}) = 0.250$ (evidence)

**Step 5a — Substitute into Bayes' formula.**
$$P(Y = \text{NOUN} \mid X = \text{``cat''}) = \frac{P(X = \text{``cat''} \mid Y = \text{NOUN}) \cdot P(Y = \text{NOUN})}{P(X = \text{``cat''})}$$

**Step 5b — Plug in numerical values.**
$$P(Y = \text{NOUN} \mid X = \text{``cat''}) = \frac{(2/3) \cdot 0.375}{0.250} = \frac{0.250}{0.250} = 1.000$$

**Verification via direct formula:** $P(Y = \text{NOUN} \mid X = \text{``cat''}) = \frac{0.250}{0.250} = 1.000 \;\checkmark$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check passes** — the joint, marginal, and conditional computations are mutually consistent. In KTU exams, always perform this kind of cross-check to earn full marks.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Operation Summary

| Operation | Inputs Needed | Output | Formula Used |
|-----------|---------------|--------|--------------|
| Joint → Marginal | Joint $P(X,Y)$ | $P(X)$ or $P(Y)$ | Sum rule |
| Joint → Conditional | Joint $P(X,Y)$, $P(Y)>0$ | $P(X \mid Y)$ | Division rule |
| Marginal + Conditional → Joint | $P(X)$, $P(Y \mid X)$ | $P(X, Y)$ | Product rule |
| Conditional → Inverse Conditional | $P(X \mid Y)$, $P(Y)$, $P(X)$ | $P(Y \mid X)$ | Bayes' theorem |


<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Map — The Joint/Marginal/Conditional Triangle

The following diagram shows the three fundamental probability objects and the operations that connect them.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["JOINT DISTRIBUTION P of X and Y"]:::jointNode
    B["MARGINAL DISTRIBUTION P of X"]:::marginalNode
    C["MARGINAL DISTRIBUTION P of Y"]:::marginalNode
    D["CONDITIONAL DISTRIBUTION P of X given Y"]:::condNode
    E["CONDITIONAL DISTRIBUTION P of Y given X"]:::condNode
    F["CHAIN RULE"]:::ruleNode
    G["BAYES THEOREM"]:::ruleNode

    A -- "Sum over Y" --> B
    A -- "Sum over X" --> C
    A -- "Divide by P of Y" --> D
    A -- "Divide by P of X" --> E
    B -- "Multiply by P of Y given X" --> A
    C -- "Multiply by P of X given Y" --> A
    D -- "Multiply by P of Y" --> A
    E -- "Multiply by P of X" --> A
    D -. "Bayes inversion" .-> E
    E -. "Bayes inversion" .-> D
    A -. "Chain rule decomposition" .-> F
    A -. "Symmetric product rule" .-> G

    classDef jointNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef marginalNode fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef condNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ruleNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Language Model Pipeline

This diagram maps how joint and conditional distributions are *operationally deployed* in a typical **n-gram language model** used in NLP.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawText["RAW CORPUS TEXT"]:::inputNode
    tokenizer["TOKENIZER"]:::processNode
    vocab["VOCABULARY BUILDER"]:::processNode
    counter["N-GRAM COUNTER"]:::processNode
    jointTable["JOINT FREQUENCY TABLE P of w_i and w_{i+1}"]:::dataNode
    marginalTable["MARGINAL TABLE P of w_i"]:::dataNode
    condTable["CONDITIONAL TABLE P of w_{i+1} given w_i"]:::dataNode
    model["N-GRAM LANGUAGE MODEL"]:::modelNode
    scorer["SENTENCE PROBABILITY SCORER"]:::outputNode

    rawText --> tokenizer
    tokenizer --> vocab
    tokenizer --> counter
    counter --> jointTable
    counter --> marginalTable
    jointTable -- "Division operation" --> condTable
    marginalTable --> condTable
    condTable --> model
    model --> scorer
    jointTable --> scorer

    classDef inputNode fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef processNode fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#9370DB,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dataNode fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#5F9EA0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef modelNode fill:#98FB98,stroke:#2E8B57,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outputNode fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#FF4500,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Joint Distribution as a 2D Probability Surface (Conceptual Block Diagram)

The following block diagram illustrates the **structural relationship** between joint, marginal, and conditional distributions for a discrete case with $\vert X \vert = 3$ and $\vert Y \vert = 2$.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph JT["JOINT DISTRIBUTION 2D TABLE P of X equal x, Y equal y"]
        JT1["P of x1, y1 = 0.10"]
        JT2["P of x1, y2 = 0.20"]
        JT3["P of x2, y1 = 0.30"]
        JT4["P of x2, y2 = 0.10"]
        JT5["P of x3, y1 = 0.15"]
        JT6["P of x3, y2 = 0.15"]
    end

    subgraph MT["MARGINAL DISTRIBUTIONS 1D SUMMED ALONG AXES"]
        MX["P of X = 0.45, 0.40, 0.30"]
        MY["P of Y = 0.55, 0.45"]
    end

    subgraph CT["CONDITIONAL DISTRIBUTIONS NORMALIZED ROWS AND COLUMNS"]
        CXY["P of X given Y = y1: 0.182, 0.545, 0.273"]
        CYX["P of Y given X = x1: 0.333, 0.667"]
    end

    JT -- "Sum along columns" --> MX
    JT -- "Sum along rows" --> MY
    JT -- "Normalize each column by P of Y" --> CXY
    JT -- "Normalize each row by P of X" --> CYX
    MX --> CXY
    MY --> CYX

    classDef jtStyle fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#BDB76B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mtStyle fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ctStyle fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class JT1,jt2,jt3,jt4,jt5,jt6 jtStyle
    class MX,MY mtStyle
    class CXY,CYX ctStyle
```


<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions are aligned with **KTU 2024 Scheme** evaluation patterns. Part A carries **3 marks each**; Part B carries **14 marks each** with **internal choice** as per KTU ESE regulations.

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**Define joint probability distribution and conditional probability distribution. State the relationship between them.**

**Model Answer:**

A **joint probability distribution** of two discrete random variables $X$ and $Y$ is a function $P(X = x, Y = y)$ that assigns a non-negative probability to every ordered pair $(x, y)$, summing to 1 over the entire sample space:

$$\sum_{x \in X}\sum_{y \in Y} P(X = x, Y = y) = 1$$

A **conditional probability distribution** of $X$ given $Y = y$ is the distribution of $X$ *after restricting* the sample space to outcomes where $Y = y$ has occurred:

$$P(X = x \mid Y = y) = \frac{P(X = x, Y = y)}{P(Y = y)}, \quad P(Y = y) > 0$$

**Relationship:** The conditional distribution is obtained by **normalizing the joint distribution** along the $Y$-axis. Equivalently, $P(X, Y) = P(X \mid Y) \cdot P(Y)$ — the joint is the *product* of the conditional and the marginal. **(3 marks: 1 for joint def, 1 for conditional def, 1 for relationship)**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Explain the concept of independence between two random variables. Give one NLP example.**

**Model Answer:**

Two random variables $X$ and $Y$ are **independent** if and only if the occurrence of one does not affect the probability of the other. Mathematically:

$$P(X = x, Y = y) = P(X = x) \cdot P(Y = y) \quad \forall\, x, y$$

Equivalently, $P(X = x \mid Y = y) = P(X = x)$ — the conditional distribution of $X$ given $Y$ equals the marginal of $X$.

**NLP Example:** In a **bag-of-words (unigram) model**, the words in a document are assumed to be *conditionally independent given the class label*. That is, $P(w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n \mid C) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid C)$. This is the **Naive Bayes assumption** widely used in spam detection and sentiment classification. **(3 marks: 2 for mathematical definition, 1 for NLP example)**

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

#### (a) State and prove the chain rule of probability. How is it used to compute the probability of a sentence in a language model? (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Statement:** For $n$ random variables $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$:

$$P(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(X_i \mid X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_{i-1})$$

**Proof:**

We proceed by induction. **Base case** ($n = 1$): Trivially $P(X_1) = P(X_1)$. **Inductive step:** Assume the rule holds for $n - 1$ variables. Consider the joint distribution of $n$ variables. Apply the product rule to the first variable and the rest:

$$P(X_1, \ldots, X_n) = P(X_1) \cdot P(X_2, \ldots, X_n \mid X_1)$$

Now apply the inductive hypothesis to the conditional joint over $X_2, \ldots, X_n$ given $X_1$:

$$P(X_2, \ldots, X_n \mid X_1) = \prod_{i=2}^{n} P(X_i \mid X_1, \ldots, X_{i-1})$$

Substituting back:

$$P(X_1, \ldots, X_n) = P(X_1) \cdot \prod_{i=2}^{n} P(X_i \mid X_1, \ldots, X_{i-1}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(X_i \mid X_1, \ldots, X_{i-1})$$

This completes the induction. $\blacksquare$

**Application to Language Models:**
A sentence $W = w_1 w_2 \ldots w_n$ has probability:

$$P(w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n) = P(w_1) \cdot P(w_2 \mid w_1) \cdot P(w_3 \mid w_1, w_2) \cdots P(w_n \mid w_1, \ldots, w_{n-1})$$

In practice, an **n-gram model** approximates this by assuming each word depends only on the previous $(n-1)$ words. For a **bigram model** ($n = 2$):

$$P(w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n) \approx P(w_1) \cdot \prod_{i=2}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_{i-1})$$

This is computationally tractable and forms the basis of classical statistical language modeling.

---

#### (b) Consider the following mini-corpus:

> *"The cat sat. The dog ran. The cat ran."*

**Tokens:** The(×3), cat(×2), sat(×1), dog(×1), ran(×2). **Total: 9 tokens.**

**Compute:**
1. The joint probability $P(\text{``The''}, \text{``cat''})$ as a bigram.
2. The conditional probability $P(\text{``cat''} \mid \text{``The''})$.
3. The probability of the sentence *"The cat ran"* using the bigram model. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Extract bigrams and count.**

| Bigram | Count |
|--------|-------|
| (The, cat) | 2 |
| (cat, sat) | 1 |
| (The, dog) | 1 |
| (dog, ran) | 1 |
| (cat, ran) | 1 |
| (The, ran) | 0 |

**Step 2 — Compute bigram joint probabilities** (divide by total bigrams = 6, but for conditional we use prefix counts).

**Step 2a — Joint $P(\text{``The''}, \text{``cat''})$:** The bigram *(The, cat)* appears **2** times. If we treat this as joint event probability over the 6 bigrams:
$$P(\text{``The''}, \text{``cat''}) = \frac{2}{6} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$$

**Step 3 — Compute conditional $P(\text{``cat''} \mid \text{``The''})$:**

We need the count of bigrams starting with *"The"*:
- (The, cat): 2
- (The, dog): 1
- (The, ran): 0
- **Total bigrams starting with "The": 3**

$$P(\text{``cat''} \mid \text{``The''}) = \frac{C(\text{The, cat})}{C(\text{The})} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.6667$$

**Step 4 — Compute other bigram conditionals needed:**

$$P(\text{``ran''} \mid \text{``cat''}) = \frac{C(\text{cat, ran})}{C(\text{cat})} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$$
$$P(\text{``The''}) = \frac{3}{9} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333 \quad \text{(unigram prior)}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the chain rule for the sentence *"The cat ran"*:**

$$P(\text{``The cat ran''}) = P(\text{The}) \cdot P(\text{cat} \mid \text{The}) \cdot P(\text{ran} \mid \text{cat})$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the values:**
$$P(\text{``The cat ran''}) = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{2}{18} = \frac{1}{9} \approx 0.1111$$

**Final Answer:**
1. $P(\text{The}, \text{cat}) = 1/3$
2. $P(\text{cat} \mid \text{The}) = 2/3$
3. $P(\text{``The cat ran''}) = 1/9$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]`

#### (a) State and derive Bayes' theorem. Explain its application in Naive Bayes text classification. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Statement:** For two events $A$ and $B$ with $P(B) > 0$:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$$

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Write the product rule in two ways.** The joint probability $P(A, B)$ can be factored as:
$$P(A, B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B) \quad \text{(i)}$$
$$P(A, B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A) \quad \text{(ii)}$$

**Step 2 — Equate the right-hand sides of (i) and (ii).**
$$P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)$$

**Step 3 — Divide both sides by $P(B)$ (which is non-zero by assumption).**
$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$$

This is **Bayes' theorem**. $\blacksquare$

**Application in Naive Bayes Text Classification:**

Given a document $D$ with words $w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n$, we want to find the most probable class $C^*$ from a set of classes $\mathcal{C} = \{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_k\}$.

By Bayes' theorem:

$$P(C \mid D) = \frac{P(D \mid C) \cdot P(C)}{P(D)}$$

Since $P(D)$ is the same for all classes, we maximize the numerator:
$$C^* = \arg\max_{C \in \mathcal{C}} P(C) \cdot P(D \mid C)$$

Using the **Naive Bayes (conditional independence) assumption** that words are independent given the class:

$$P(D \mid C) = P(w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n \mid C) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid C)$$

Thus the classifier becomes:

$$C^* = \arg\max_{C} P(C) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid C)$$

In practice, log-probabilities are used:
$$C^* = \arg\max_{C} \left[\log P(C) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(w_i \mid C)\right]$$

**Real-world use:** Spam filtering (Gmail), sentiment analysis (positive/negative reviews), news categorization.

---

#### (b) A language identification system uses Bayes' theorem. For a given text fragment, $P(\text{English}) = 0.7$ and $P(\text{French}) = 0.3$. The likelihoods are $P(\text{fragment} \mid \text{English}) = 0.01$ and $P(\text{fragment} \mid \text{French}) = 0.05$. Compute the posterior probabilities $P(\text{English} \mid \text{fragment})$ and $P(\text{French} \mid \text{fragment})$. Which language is more likely? (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write Bayes' theorem for the English class.**

$$P(\text{Eng} \mid \text{Frag}) = \frac{P(\text{Frag} \mid \text{Eng}) \cdot P(\text{Eng})}{P(\text{Frag})}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the total evidence $P(\text{Frag})$ using the law of total probability.**

$$P(\text{Frag}) = P(\text{Frag} \mid \text{Eng}) \cdot P(\text{Eng}) + P(\text{Frag} \mid \text{Fr}) \cdot P(\text{Fr})$$
$$P(\text{Frag}) = (0.01)(0.7) + (0.05)(0.3)$$
$$P(\text{Frag}) = 0.007 + 0.015 = 0.022$$

**Step 3 — Compute the posterior for English.**

$$P(\text{Eng} \mid \text{Frag}) = \frac{(0.01)(0.7)}{0.022} = \frac{0.007}{0.022} = \frac{7}{22} \approx 0.3182$$

**Step 4 — Compute the posterior for French.**

$$P(\text{Fr} \mid \text{Frag}) = \frac{P(\text{Frag} \mid \text{Fr}) \cdot P(\text{Fr})}{P(\text{Frag})}$$
$$P(\text{Fr} \mid \text{Frag}) = \frac{(0.05)(0.3)}{0.022} = \frac{0.015}{0.022} = \frac{15}{22} \approx 0.6818$$

**Step 5 — Sanity check.**
$$P(\text{Eng} \mid \text{Frag}) + P(\text{Fr} \mid \text{Frag}) = \frac{7}{22} + \frac{15}{22} = \frac{22}{22} = 1.000 \;\checkmark$$

**Step 6 — Decision.**
Since $P(\text{French} \mid \text{fragment}) \approx 0.6818 > P(\text{English} \mid \text{fragment}) \approx 0.3182$, the system classifies the fragment as **French**, despite English being the more common prior language. This is because the **likelihood ratio** $\frac{P(\text{Frag} \mid \text{Fr})}{P(\text{Frag} \mid \text{Eng})} = 5$ strongly favors French.

**Final Answer:** $P(\text{Eng} \mid \text{Frag}) \approx 0.3182$, $P(\text{Fr} \mid \text{Frag}) \approx 0.6818$, **language is French**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to normalize**: In Bayes' theorem problems, students often compute $P(X \mid Y) \propto P(Y \mid X) P(X)$ and forget to divide by $P(X)$. Always compute the *total evidence* $P(X) = \sum_i P(X \mid Y_i) P(Y_i)$ when there are multiple hypotheses.
> 2. **Confusing $P(A \mid B)$ with $P(B \mid A)$**: The most common error. Always identify which is the **hypothesis** and which is the **evidence** before applying the formula.
> 3. **Misapplying independence**: Naive Bayes assumes conditional independence *given the class*, not absolute independence between features.
> 4. **Sign error in joint computations**: When computing joint from marginal and conditional, students sometimes invert the multiplication: $P(A, B) \neq P(A) + P(B)$ — it is always a **product**, not a sum.
> 5. **Not verifying normalization**: A correctly computed joint distribution must sum to 1; conditional distributions over a fixed conditioning event must also sum to 1. Always perform the sanity check.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔹 **Joint Distribution** $P(X, Y)$: probability of two events occurring together; sums to **1** over the full grid.
- 🔹 **Marginal Distribution** $P(X)$: obtained by summing out the other variable from the joint — *sum rule*.
- 🔹 **Conditional Distribution** $P(X \mid Y)$: obtained by normalizing the joint by $P(Y)$ — *product rule inverted*.
- 🔹 **Chain Rule**: $P(X_1, \ldots, X_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(X_i \mid X_{<i})$ — the foundation of sentence probability in language models.
- 🔹 **Bayes' Theorem**: $P(H \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H) P(H)}{P(E)}$ — enables inversion of conditional distributions; used in Naive Bayes, HMMs, MT.
- 🔹 **Independence**: $P(X, Y) = P(X) P(Y)$ — the Naive Bayes assumption for text classification.
- 🔹 **Conditional Independence**: $P(X \mid Y, Z) = P(X \mid Z)$ — the HMM assumption that a tag depends only on the previous tag.
- 🔹 **Always check normalization**: every valid joint and conditional distribution must sum to **1** in the discrete case.
- 🔹 **NLP applications**: bigram/trigram language models, Naive Bayes classifiers, HMM-based POS taggers, statistical machine translation, speech recognition decoders.
- 🔹 **Engineering tip**: use **log-probabilities** in code to prevent numerical underflow when multiplying many small probabilities.


<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Standard Distributions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Standard Distributions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are "Standard Distributions" in Computational Linguistics?

In **Computational Linguistics (CL)** and **Natural Language Processing (NLP)**, almost every phenomenon — word occurrences, sentence lengths, tag sequences, sound durations, and rare-event counts — is modeled as a **random variable** whose behavior we describe with a **probability distribution**. A *standard distribution* is a well-known, mathematically tractable probability function that we use to approximate, model, or smooth the statistical behavior of linguistic data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A **Standard Probability Distribution** is a closed-form mathematical function $P(x \mid \theta)$ that assigns a probability (or probability density) to every possible outcome $x$ of a random variable, governed by a small set of fixed parameters $\theta$ (such as mean $\mu$, variance $\sigma^2$, rate $\lambda$, or success probability $p$). In CL, these distributions act as the **statistical backbone** of language models, classifiers, and probabilistic parsers.

In simpler words, a distribution is a *recipe* that tells us **how likely** each outcome is. A "standard" distribution is a recipe that has a famous name, a known shape, and a known formula — so that we can use it across many problems without reinventing mathematics each time.

## 1.2 The Linguistic Intuition — A Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are sitting in a college canteen in Kerala and you start counting how many people order **"masala dosa"** versus **"chicken biryani"** versus **"tea"** in one hour. After many observations, you will notice:

- The count of *tea* orders is large, *masala dosa* is moderate, *chicken biryani* is rarer.
- The number of times any specific word (say, "the") appears in a corpus follows a similar pattern.
- A coin-flip style event (does this tweet contain a positive or negative word?) is binary.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> Think of a **distribution as a "language's fingerprint."** Some words are the "tea" of language — appearing almost everywhere ("the", "of", "a"). Other words are the "chicken biryani" — appearing occasionally ("serendipity", "photosynthesis"). The standard distribution is the *mathematical fingerprint scanner* that captures this pattern precisely. **Bernoulli** is the coin-flip, **Binomial/Multinomial** is the multiple-trial version, **Poisson** counts rare events, **Normal** captures the bell-curve spread, and **Zipf's Law** is the *special celebrity rule* that governs word frequencies in *every* natural language.

## 1.3 Why Are Standard Distributions Central to KTU Module 1?

In **KTU 2024 Scheme – PEADT632 (Computational Linguistics)**, Module 1 builds the probabilistic foundation for the entire syllabus. The university examiner expects students to:

1. Connect each distribution to a **linguistic application** (not just abstract probability).
2. Recognize which distribution fits which NLP task (e.g., Naive Bayes uses Multinomial; HMM emission uses Categorical).
3. Compute **Maximum Likelihood Estimates (MLE)** of parameters from corpus data.
4. Apply **Laplace (Add-1) Smoothing** — itself a distribution-manipulation technique.

### The Seven Standard Distributions KTU Tests On

| # | Distribution | Symbol | Linguistic Use |
|---|--------------|--------|----------------|
| 1 | Bernoulli | $Ber(p)$ | Binary sentiment, presence/absence of a feature |
| 2 | Categorical | $Cat(\mathbf{p})$ | One of $K$ word classes, POS tags |
| 3 | Binomial | $Bin(n, p)$ | $n$ trials, count of successes (rarely direct) |
| 4 | Multinomial | $Mult(n, \mathbf{p})$ | Bag-of-Words, n-gram counts, Naive Bayes |
| 5 | Poisson | $Poi(\lambda)$ | Sentence length, word co-occurrence counts |
| 6 | Normal (Gaussian) | $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ | Embedding noise, continuous word vectors |
| 7 | Zipf / Power Law | $P(r) \propto r^{-s}$ | Word frequency vs. rank (CL signature) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** *Standard Distributions* in Module 1 of Computational Linguistics is not about memorizing PDFs — it is about **mapping each distribution to a specific CL/NLP use case** and being able to **derive its likelihood and MLE parameters** from a small linguistic dataset.

### 1.4 Key Notation Used Throughout

- $P(X = x)$ — probability that discrete random variable $X$ takes value $x$.
- $f(x \mid \theta)$ — probability density for continuous $X$ with parameters $\theta$.
- $\mathbb{E}[X]$ — **expectation** (mean).
- $Var(X)$ — **variance** (spread).
- $MLE$ — **Maximum Likelihood Estimate** of a parameter.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Distributions — Step-by-Step Operational Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Bernoulli Distribution $Ber(p)$

- **Step 1 (Setup):** A single trial with exactly two outcomes — *success* (1) or *failure* (0).
- **Step 2 (Parameter):** Single parameter $p \in [0, 1]$ — probability of success.
- **Step 3 (PMF):** $P(X = x) = p^{x}(1-p)^{1-x}$ for $x \in \{0, 1\}$.
- **Step 4 (Linguistic Use):** Does this document contain the word "excellent"? Is this email spam? Is the next character a vowel?

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters in CL:** Bernoulli Naive Bayes is the textbook baseline for **sentiment classification** and **spam detection**. It is the *atomic* distribution that Binomial extends.

### 2.1.2 Categorical Distribution $Cat(\mathbf{p})$

- **Step 1 (Setup):** A single trial with $K$ mutually exclusive outcomes (generalization of Bernoulli).
- **Step 2 (Parameter):** Probability vector $\mathbf{p} = (p_1, p_2, \dots, p_K)$ with $\sum_k p_k = 1$.
- **Step 3 (PMF):** $P(X = k) = p_k$ for $k \in \{1, 2, \dots, K\}$.
- **Step 4 (Linguistic Use):** Assigning a single **POS tag** to a word (Noun / Verb / Adj / …); predicting the next **character** in a language model; topic assignment in LDA.

### 2.1.3 Binomial Distribution $Bin(n, p)$

- **Step 1 (Setup):** Count the number of successes in $n$ **independent** Bernoulli trials.
- **Step 2 (Parameters):** $n$ (number of trials) and $p$ (per-trial success probability).
- **Step 3 (PMF):** $P(X = k) = \binom{n}{k} p^{k} (1-p)^{n-k}$ for $k = 0, 1, \dots, n$.
- **Step 4 (Linguistic Use):** Out of $n=100$ sampled tweets, how many are positive? — Used in evaluation statistics and significance testing.

### 2.1.4 Multinomial Distribution $Mult(n, \mathbf{p})$

- **Step 1 (Setup):** Count how many times each of $K$ outcomes occurred across $n$ **independent** categorical trials.
- **Step 2 (Parameter):** $\mathbf{p} = (p_1, \dots, p_K)$, $\sum_k p_k = 1$, plus trial count $n$.
- **Step 3 (PMF):**
  $$P(X_1 = n_1, \dots, X_K = n_K) = \frac{n!}{n_1! n_2! \cdots n_K!} \prod_{k=1}^{K} p_k^{n_k}$$
  subject to $\sum_k n_k = n$.
- **Step 4 (Linguistic Use):** The **Bag-of-Words (BoW)** document model. **Multinomial Naive Bayes** is the workhorse classifier for text categorization. N-gram language models use it implicitly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU Module 1, the *Multinomial distribution* is the single most-tested distribution because it directly models **word-count vectors** — the input format of nearly every classical NLP pipeline.

### 2.1.5 Poisson Distribution $Poi(\lambda)$

- **Step 1 (Setup):** Count the number of **rare, independent events** occurring in a fixed interval of time/space.
- **Step 2 (Parameter):** $\lambda > 0$ — the average rate of occurrence.
- **Step 3 (PMF):** $P(X = k) = \frac{\lambda^{k} e^{-\lambda}}{k!}$ for $k = 0, 1, 2, \dots$
- **Step 4 (Linguistic Use):** Modeling **sentence length** in a corpus, **word co-occurrence counts** in a window, and **bursty term frequencies** in Information Retrieval (the classic *Burstiness* phenomenon).

### 2.1.6 Normal / Gaussian Distribution $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$

- **Step 1 (Setup):** A continuous random variable whose values cluster symmetrically around a mean.
- **Step 2 (Parameters):** Mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$.
- **Step 3 (PDF):**
  $$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$
- **Step 4 (Linguistic Use):** Modeling **additive Gaussian noise** in word embeddings (e.g., robust Word2Vec, differential privacy); **log-probabilities** of rare words (Central Limit Theorem on log-counts); **continuous linguistic features** like pitch, duration, reaction times.

### 2.1.7 Zipf / Power-Law Distribution

- **Step 1 (Setup):** A discrete distribution where the **frequency of an item is inversely proportional to its rank** in the frequency table.
- **Step 2 (Parameter):** Exponent $s$ (typically $s \approx 1$ for natural language, famously observed by G. K. Zipf in 1935).
- **Step 3 (PMF):** $P(r) = \frac{r^{-s}}{\zeta(s)}$ for rank $r = 1, 2, 3, \dots$, where $\zeta(s) = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k^{-s}$ is the **Riemann zeta function** (normalization constant).
- **Step 4 (Linguistic Use):** The **signature law of natural language** — a few words ("the", "of", "a") dominate, and a long tail of rare words fills the vocabulary. Critical for **stop-word filtering**, **vocabulary pruning**, and **sub-word tokenization (Byte-Pair Encoding)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Zipf matters most in CL:** Unlike the other six distributions, Zipf's Law is *empirically discovered* in language data. It is the bridge between abstract probability and the messy reality of corpora. **KTU examiners frequently test the formula $P(r) \propto r^{-s}$ and its log-log linear form.**

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> **Critical LaTeX Note:** The vertical pipe `\mid` is used below (not `\vert` or `\|`) to keep markdown tables unbroken. Every formula below is **mathematically verifiable**.

| Distribution | PMF / PDF (Compact Form) | Mean $\mathbb{E}[X]$ | Variance $Var(X)$ | MLE Parameter | CL Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bernoulli $Ber(p)$ | $p^{x}(1-p)^{1-x}$ | $p$ | $p(1-p)$ | $\hat{p} = \frac{\#\text{successes}}{N}$ | Binary sentiment, spam |
| Categorical $Cat(\mathbf{p})$ | $p_{k}$ for outcome $k$ | — | — | $\hat{p}_k = \frac{n_k}{N}$ | POS tagging, next-char LM |
| Binomial $Bin(n, p)$ | $\binom{n}{k} p^{k}(1-p)^{n-k}$ | $np$ | $np(1-p)$ | $\hat{p} = \frac{k}{n}$ | Tweet positivity count |
| Multinomial $Mult(n, \mathbf{p})$ | $\frac{n!}{\prod_k n_k!}\prod_k p_k^{n_k}$ | $n p_k$ for each $k$ | $n p_k(1-p_k)$ | $\hat{p}_k = \frac{n_k}{n}$ | Bag-of-Words, NB text classification |
| Poisson $Poi(\lambda)$ | $\frac{\lambda^{k} e^{-\lambda}}{k!}$ | $\lambda$ | $\lambda$ | $\hat{\lambda} = \bar{x} = \frac{1}{N}\sum x_i$ | Sentence length, term burstiness |
| Normal $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ | $\frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | $\mu$ | $\sigma^2$ | $\hat{\mu}=\bar{x}$, $\hat{\sigma}^{2}=\frac{1}{N}\sum(x_i-\bar{x})^{2}$ | Embedding noise, log-probs |
| Zipf / Power Law | $P(r) = r^{-s}/\zeta(s)$ | $\zeta(s-1)/\zeta(s)$ | finite for $s>3$ | $\hat{s}$ via log-log regression | Word frequency, vocabulary |

### 2.2.1 Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) — Universal Recipe

For any distribution $P(X \mid \theta)$ and observed data $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, \dots, x_N\}$ (i.i.d.):

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(x_i \mid \theta), \quad \ell(\theta) = \log \mathcal{L}(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(x_i \mid \theta)$$

$$\hat{\theta}_{MLE} = \arg\max_{\theta} \ell(\theta) \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \theta} = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> In an industrial NLP pipeline at a company like Google or Microsoft, the **Multinomial MLE** computes per-word probabilities from a corpus in $O(V)$ time, where $V$ is the vocabulary size. The **Poisson rate $\lambda$** is the engine behind Twitter's *trending topic* detection — when observed tweet-rate $\gg \lambda$, the topic is "bursty." **Zipf's Law** drives **cache design** in search engines: the top 100 words are kept in RAM, the long tail hits disk.

### 2.2.2 Laplace (Add-1) Smoothing — A Distribution Hack

To handle **zero-probability** words (a fatal flaw of pure MLE), we modify Multinomial MLE as:

$$\hat{p}_k^{Lap} = \frac{n_k + 1}{n + K}$$

where $K$ is vocabulary size. This is equivalent to assuming a **uniform Dirichlet prior** $\text{Dir}(\alpha = \mathbf{1})$ on the multinomial parameters — a Bayesian move the KTU examiner loves to test.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: MLE of Bernoulli Parameter $p$

**Setup:** We observe a corpus of $N$ independent binary outcomes $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N \in \{0, 1\}$, where $x_i = 1$ means "the $i$-th review is positive" and $x_i = 0$ means "negative." We want the MLE of $p = P(X = 1)$.

### Step 1 — Write the likelihood

Because each $x_i$ is independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) under $Ber(p)$:

$$\mathcal{L}(p) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} p^{x_i} (1-p)^{1-x_i}$$

### Step 2 — Take the log-likelihood

The log is a strictly increasing function, so maximizing $\log \mathcal{L}$ is equivalent to maximizing $\mathcal{L}$:

$$\ell(p) = \log \mathcal{L}(p) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ x_i \log p + (1 - x_i)\log(1-p) \right]$$

### Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to $p$

Using $\frac{d}{dp}\log p = \frac{1}{p}$ and $\frac{d}{dp}\log(1-p) = \frac{-1}{1-p}$:

$$\frac{d\ell}{dp} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ \frac{x_i}{p} - \frac{1 - x_i}{1-p} \right] = 0$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $p$

Pull out the constants and combine over a common denominator:

$$\frac{1}{p}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \;=\; \frac{1}{1-p}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(1 - x_i)$$

Let $S = \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$ (number of positive reviews). Then $\sum_i(1-x_i) = N - S$. Substituting:

$$\frac{S}{p} = \frac{N - S}{1-p} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad S(1-p) = p(N - S) \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad S = pN$$

$$\boxed{\hat{p}_{MLE} = \frac{S}{N} = \frac{\text{number of successes}}{\text{total trials}}}$$

### Step 5 — Second-derivative check (maximum, not minimum)

$$\frac{d^{2}\ell}{dp^{2}} = -\frac{S}{p^{2}} - \frac{N-S}{(1-p)^{2}} < 0$$

This is strictly negative for $p \in (0,1)$, confirming a **maximum**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: MLE of Multinomial Parameters $\mathbf{p}$

**Setup:** We have a document of $n$ total words, with $n_k$ occurrences of word $w_k$ for $k = 1, \dots, K$. Find $\hat{p}_k$.

### Step 1 — Likelihood under Multinomial

$$P(\mathbf{n} \mid \mathbf{p}) = \frac{n!}{n_1! n_2! \cdots n_K!} \prod_{k=1}^{K} p_k^{n_k}, \quad \text{subject to } \sum_{k=1}^{K} p_k = 1$$

### Step 2 — Log-likelihood (drop the multinomial coefficient — it does not depend on $\mathbf{p}$)

$$\ell(\mathbf{p}) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} n_k \log p_k + \text{const}$$

### Step 3 — Constrained optimization via Lagrange multiplier $\lambda$

$$\mathcal{J}(\mathbf{p}, \lambda) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} n_k \log p_k - \lambda \left( \sum_{k=1}^{K} p_k - 1 \right)$$

### Step 4 — Partial derivatives

For each $k$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{J}}{\partial p_k} = \frac{n_k}{p_k} - \lambda = 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad p_k = \frac{n_k}{\lambda}$$

### Step 5 — Use the constraint

$$\sum_{k=1}^{K} p_k = 1 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{n_k}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{\lambda}\sum_{k=1}^{K} n_k = \frac{n}{\lambda} = 1 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \lambda = n$$

### Step 6 — Final MLE

$$\boxed{\hat{p}_k^{MLE} = \frac{n_k}{n}}$$

This is the famous **relative-frequency estimate** — the *empirical word probability* in the corpus. It is exactly the KTU Module 1 result students must reproduce under exam conditions.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: MLE of Poisson Rate $\lambda$

**Setup:** Observed sentence lengths in a corpus: $x_1, \dots, x_N \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots\}$. Find $\hat{\lambda}$.

### Step 1 — Likelihood

$$\mathcal{L}(\lambda) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} \frac{\lambda^{x_i} e^{-\lambda}}{x_i!} = \frac{\lambda^{\sum x_i} e^{-N\lambda}}{\prod_i x_i!}$$

### Step 2 — Log-likelihood

$$\ell(\lambda) = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i\right) \log \lambda - N\lambda - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log(x_i!)$$

### Step 3 — Differentiate

$$\frac{d\ell}{d\lambda} = \frac{\sum_i x_i}{\lambda} - N = 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \lambda = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i = \bar{x}$$

### Step 4 — Verify maximum

$$\frac{d^{2}\ell}{d\lambda^{2}} = -\frac{\sum x_i}{\lambda^{2}} < 0 \quad \checkmark$$

$$\boxed{\hat{\lambda}_{MLE} = \bar{x} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i}$$

**Engineering Insight:** Poisson is the *only* standard distribution where the MLE of the parameter equals the **sample mean** — a property the examiner loves.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: MLE of Normal Parameters $(\mu, \sigma^2)$

**Setup:** Observations $x_1, \dots, x_N$ i.i.d. from $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$.

### Step 1 — Log-likelihood

$$\ell(\mu, \sigma^2) = -\frac{N}{2}\log(2\pi) - \frac{N}{2}\log(\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2$$

### Step 2 — Partial w.r.t. $\mu$

$$\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \mu} = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu) = 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \mu = \frac{1}{N}\sum x_i = \bar{x}$$

### Step 3 — Partial w.r.t. $\sigma^2$

$$\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \sigma^2} = -\frac{N}{2\sigma^2} + \frac{1}{2\sigma^{4}}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^{2} = 0$$

$$\boxed{\hat{\mu} = \bar{x}, \qquad \hat{\sigma}^{2} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^{2}}$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Zipf's Law from Log-Log Linear Regression

**Setup:** Observed word frequencies $f(r)$ at rank $r$ in a vocabulary-sorted corpus.

### Step 1 — Zipf's empirical statement

$$f(r) = C \cdot r^{-s}, \quad C, s > 0$$

### Step 2 — Take $\log$ of both sides

$$\log f(r) = \log C - s \log r$$

### Step 3 — Identify it as a line

Let $Y = \log f(r)$, $X = \log r$, $A = \log C$, $B = -s$. Then:

$$Y = A + B X$$

This is a **simple linear regression**. The slope $B$ (negative) gives $-\hat{s}$, and the intercept gives $\hat{C}$.

### Step 4 — Estimate by least squares

$$\hat{B} = \frac{\sum_i (X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})}{\sum_i (X_i - \bar{X})^{2}}, \qquad \hat{s} = -\hat{B}, \qquad \hat{C} = e^{\hat{A}}$$

### Step 5 — Final MLE-style closed form

$$\boxed{\hat{s} = -\frac{N \sum \log r_i \log f(r_i) - \sum \log r_i \sum \log f(r_i)}{N \sum (\log r_i)^2 - \left(\sum \log r_i\right)^2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **CL Insight:** For *Brown Corpus* and *Google Web Trillion Word Corpus*, $\hat{s} \approx 1.0$. For *Chinese*, $\hat{s} \approx 0.95$. For *child-directed speech*, $\hat{s} \approx 1.1$ (more peaked). This single exponent $\hat{s}$ is a **typological fingerprint** of a language.

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 7-Mark Sub-Question)

**Problem:** A small corpus of 5 documents has the following word counts for three words: $w_1$ ("data"), $w_2$ ("language"), $w_3$ ("model"). The counts are $n_1 = 8, n_2 = 5, n_3 = 7$ and total $n = 20$.

**(a) Find the MLE of the Multinomial distribution $P(w_k)$.**

Using the formula derived in 3.2:

$$\hat{p}_1 = \frac{8}{20} = 0.40, \quad \hat{p}_2 = \frac{5}{20} = 0.25, \quad \hat{p}_3 = \frac{7}{20} = 0.35$$

Check: $0.40 + 0.25 + 0.35 = 1.00 \;\checkmark$

**(b) Apply Laplace (Add-1) smoothing with vocabulary $K = 3$.**

$$\hat{p}_1^{Lap} = \frac{8+1}{20+3} = \frac{9}{23} \approx 0.391$$

$$\hat{p}_2^{Lap} = \frac{5+1}{20+3} = \frac{6}{23} \approx 0.261$$

$$\hat{p}_3^{Lap} = \frac{7+1}{20+3} = \frac{8}{23} \approx 0.348$$

Check: $\frac{9+6+8}{23} = \frac{23}{23} = 1.00 \;\checkmark$

**(c) Compute the log-probability of a new test document with counts $(1, 2, 0)$.**

Raw MLE: $\log P = 1 \log 0.40 + 2 \log 0.25 + 0 \log 0.35 = -0.916 - 2.773 = -3.689$

Laplace: $\log P = 1 \log 0.391 + 2 \log 0.261 + 0 \log 0.348 = -0.939 - 2.683 = -3.622$

> The Laplace estimate assigns a finite (non-zero) probability even to $(0,0,1)$ configurations that raw MLE would zero out — solving the *zero-frequency problem* that breaks Naive Bayes classifiers.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any three standard probability distributions used in Computational Linguistics and state one NLP application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Multinomial Distribution** — Models the *Bag-of-Words* representation of a document; the likelihood of a document is computed as the product of word-probabilities raised to their counts. Used in **Multinomial Naive Bayes text classification** and in n-gram language models.
2. **Poisson Distribution** — Models the *count of rare linguistic events* in a fixed interval, e.g., **sentence length** in a corpus or **word co-occurrence** in a sliding window. Used in **bursty term detection** for Information Retrieval and trending-topic identification.
3. **Zipf / Power Law Distribution** — Models the empirical relationship $P(r) \propto r^{-s}$ between the *rank* of a word and its *frequency*. Used in **stop-word filtering, vocabulary pruning, and Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE)** subword tokenization.

> [!NOTE]
> Bernoulli, Categorical, Binomial, and Normal are also valid alternatives; examiners award full marks for any three correctly paired distribution–application pairs.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE). Show that the MLE of the parameter $p$ of a Bernoulli distribution is $\hat{p} = \frac{S}{N}$ where $S$ is the number of successes in $N$ trials.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** MLE is the parameter value that maximizes the probability (or likelihood) of having observed the given data, assuming the data is i.i.d. For a distribution $P(x \mid \theta)$ and dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, \dots, x_N\}$:

$$\hat{\theta}_{MLE} = \arg\max_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta) = \arg\max_{\theta} \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(x_i \mid \theta) = \arg\max_{\theta} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(x_i \mid \theta)$$

**Derivation:** Likelihood $\mathcal{L}(p) = p^{S}(1-p)^{N-S}$. Log-likelihood $\ell(p) = S\log p + (N-S)\log(1-p)$. Differentiating: $\frac{d\ell}{dp} = \frac{S}{p} - \frac{N-S}{1-p} = 0 \Rightarrow S(1-p) = p(N-S) \Rightarrow \hat{p} = \frac{S}{N}$. Second derivative is negative, confirming a maximum.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) Derive the MLE of the parameters $\mathbf{p} = (p_1, p_2, p_3)$ of a Multinomial distribution from a corpus of documents with word-counts $(n_1, n_2, n_3)$ and total $n = n_1 + n_2 + n_3$. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Explain Laplace (Add-1) smoothing with the same setup and show how it overcomes the zero-probability problem in CL text classification. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1:** The PMF of the Multinomial distribution is:

$$P(n_1, n_2, n_3 \mid p_1, p_2, p_3) = \frac{n!}{n_1!\, n_2!\, n_3!}\, p_1^{n_1}\, p_2^{n_2}\, p_3^{n_3}$$

subject to the simplex constraint $p_1 + p_2 + p_3 = 1$.

**Step 2:** Log-likelihood (the multinomial coefficient $\frac{n!}{n_1! n_2! n_3!}$ does not depend on $\mathbf{p}$):

$$\ell(\mathbf{p}) = n_1 \log p_1 + n_2 \log p_2 + n_3 \log p_3 + \text{const}$$

**Step 3:** Constrained optimization using a Lagrange multiplier $\lambda$:

$$\mathcal{J} = n_1 \log p_1 + n_2 \log p_2 + n_3 \log p_3 - \lambda(p_1 + p_2 + p_3 - 1)$$

**Step 4:** Partial derivatives set to zero:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{J}}{\partial p_k} = \frac{n_k}{p_k} - \lambda = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; p_k = \frac{n_k}{\lambda}, \quad k \in \{1, 2, 3\}$$

**Step 5:** Apply constraint:

$$\frac{n_1 + n_2 + n_3}{\lambda} = \frac{n}{\lambda} = 1 \;\Longrightarrow\; \lambda = n$$

**Step 6:** Final MLE result:

$$\boxed{\hat{p}_k = \frac{n_k}{n}, \quad k = 1, 2, 3}$$

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Zero-Probability Problem:** If a word $w_k$ did not occur in the training corpus ($n_k = 0$), then $\hat{p}_k = 0$, and *any* test document containing $w_k$ will receive log-probability $-\infty$ — completely breaking the Naive Bayes classifier.

**Laplace Smoothing Fix:** Add a pseudo-count of 1 to every word's count:

$$\hat{p}_k^{Lap} = \frac{n_k + 1}{n + K}, \quad K = \text{vocabulary size}$$

**Effect:** Now $\hat{p}_k^{Lap} > 0$ for every $k$, even unseen words. The probability mass is "stolen" from observed words and redistributed uniformly to unseen ones. This is exactly the **maximum a posteriori (MAP) estimate under a uniform Dirichlet prior** $\text{Dir}(\alpha_1 = \alpha_2 = \dots = \alpha_K = 1)$.

**Numerical Example:** Suppose $n_1 = 8, n_2 = 5, n_3 = 7, n = 20, K = 3$.

$$\hat{p}_1^{Lap} = \frac{9}{23} \approx 0.391, \quad \hat{p}_2^{Lap} = \frac{6}{23} \approx 0.261, \quad \hat{p}_3^{Lap} = \frac{8}{23} \approx 0.348$$

**CL Significance:** Laplace smoothing is the *first line of defense* against data sparsity in n-gram language models and is foundational for more advanced techniques like **Kneser-Ney smoothing** used in modern speech recognition.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) State and explain Zipf's Law. Show how its parameters are estimated from a corpus using log-log linear regression. (7 Marks)**

**(b) A small corpus has the following word-rank-frequency data: rank 1 → 1000, rank 2 → 500, rank 3 → 333, rank 4 → 250. Estimate the Zipf exponent $\hat{s}$ and constant $\hat{C}$. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Statement of Zipf's Law:** In any sufficiently large natural-language corpus, the frequency $f(r)$ of the $r$-th most frequent word is inversely proportional to its rank $r$ raised to a positive exponent $s$:

$$f(r) = C \cdot r^{-s}, \quad r = 1, 2, 3, \dots$$

For most natural languages, $s \approx 1.0$ (Zipf's original finding), though exact values vary by genre and language.

**Estimation via Log-Log Linear Regression:** Taking $\log$ on both sides:

$$\log f(r) = \log C - s \log r$$

Let $Y = \log f(r)$, $X = \log r$, $A = \log C$, $B = -s$. We get the linear model $Y = A + BX$. The least-squares estimates are:

$$\hat{B} = \frac{\sum_i (X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})}{\sum_i (X_i - \bar{X})^2}, \quad \hat{A} = \bar{Y} - \hat{B}\bar{X}$$

The recovered parameters are:

$$\hat{s} = -\hat{B}, \quad \hat{C} = e^{\hat{A}}$$

**Why it matters in CL:** Zipf's Law guides *vocabulary size estimation* (Heaps' Law), *stop-word identification*, and *subword tokenization cutoffs* in BPE/SentencePiece.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1:** Compute $X_i = \log r_i$ and $Y_i = \log f(r_i)$ (using natural log):

| $r$ | $f(r)$ | $X = \log r$ | $Y = \log f(r)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1000 | 0.0000 | 6.9078 |
| 2 | 500  | 0.6931 | 6.2146 |
| 3 | 333  | 1.0986 | 5.8081 |
| 4 | 250  | 1.3863 | 5.5215 |

**Step 2:** Compute means:

$$\bar{X} = \frac{0.0000 + 0.6931 + 1.0986 + 1.3863}{4} = \frac{3.1780}{4} = 0.7945$$

$$\bar{Y} = \frac{6.9078 + 6.2146 + 5.8081 + 5.5215}{4} = \frac{24.4520}{4} = 6.1130$$

**Step 3:** Compute slope $\hat{B}$:

$$\sum (X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y}) = (-0.7945)(0.7948) + (-0.1014)(0.1016) + (0.3041)(-0.3049) + (0.5918)(-0.5915)$$

$$= -0.6314 - 0.0103 - 0.0927 - 0.3501 = -1.0845$$

$$\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2 = (0.7945)^2 + (0.1014)^2 + (0.3041)^2 + (0.5918)^2 = 0.6312 + 0.0103 + 0.0925 + 0.3502 = 1.0842$$

$$\hat{B} = \frac{-1.0845}{1.0842} \approx -1.0003$$

**Step 4:** Recover Zipf exponent:

$$\boxed{\hat{s} = -\hat{B} \approx 1.00}$$

**Step 5:** Intercept and constant:

$$\hat{A} = \bar{Y} - \hat{B}\bar{X} = 6.1130 - (-1.0003)(0.7945) = 6.1130 + 0.7947 = 6.9077$$

$$\hat{C} = e^{\hat{A}} = e^{6.9077} \approx 1000$$

**Step 6:** Final fitted model:

$$\boxed{f(r) \approx 1000 \cdot r^{-1.00}}$$

**Verification:** At $r = 1$: $f(1) \approx 1000$ ✓. At $r = 2$: $f(2) \approx 500$ ✓. At $r = 4$: $f(4) \approx 250$ ✓. The estimate is **excellent** — confirming the corpus exhibits classical Zipfian behavior with $s \approx 1$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the Lagrange multiplier** in the Multinomial MLE derivation. The constraint $\sum p_k = 1$ MUST be enforced, or you will lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing MLE with MAP.** MLE has no prior; Laplace smoothing is *Bayesian* (Dirichlet prior). Examiners explicitly deduct 1 mark for conflating them.
> 3. **Ignoring the constraint that $\hat{p}_k$ must sum to 1** in numerical problems — always do a *sanity check* in the last line.
> 4. **Using decimal points without units** in Zipf problems. Always state $s$ as a *dimensionless exponent*.
> 5. **Writing $\log$ without specifying base.** KTU examiners expect **natural log ($\ln$)** in derivations and **$\log_{10}$** in plots; be explicit.
> 6. **Skipping the second-derivative test** in MLE derivations. The KTU 2024 marking scheme allocates **0.5 to 1 mark** for verifying that the stationary point is a maximum, not a minimum.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Module 1 — Standard Distributions Revision Sheet**

- ✅ **Bernoulli $Ber(p)$** is the coin-flip; MLE $\hat{p} = S/N$. Used for **binary sentiment**.
- ✅ **Categorical $Cat(\mathbf{p})$** is the multi-outcome single-trial distribution; MLE $\hat{p}_k = n_k/N$. Used for **POS tagging** and **next-token prediction**.
- ✅ **Binomial $Bin(n, p)$** counts successes in $n$ trials; MLE $\hat{p} = k/n$. Used in **evaluation statistics**.
- ✅ **Multinomial $Mult(n, \mathbf{p})$** is the **Bag-of-Words** model; MLE $\hat{p}_k = n_k/n$. Used in **Naive Bayes text classification** — *most-tested distribution in KTU exams*.
- ✅ **Poisson $Poi(\lambda)$** models rare-event counts; MLE $\hat{\lambda} = \bar{x}$. Used for **sentence length and burstiness**.
- ✅ **Normal $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$** is the bell-curve; MLE $\hat{\mu}=\bar{x}$, $\hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum(x_i-\bar{x})^2$. Used for **continuous embeddings with noise**.
- ✅ **Zipf's Law** $f(r) = C r^{-s}$ with $s \approx 1$; estimated via **log-log linear regression**. The *empirical signature* of natural language. Used for **stop-word removal and BPE**.
- ✅ **Laplace (Add-1) Smoothing** $\hat{p}_k^{Lap} = (n_k+1)/(n+K)$ solves **zero-frequency**; equivalent to **MAP with Dirichlet(1,1,...,1) prior**.
- ✅ **Dirichlet–Multinomial conjugacy** is the engine of Bayesian smoothing; remember: *posterior $\alpha_k^{post} = \alpha_k^{prior} + n_k$*.
- ✅ **Sanity check:** All probability estimates must sum to 1; all variances must be $\geq 0$; all Poisson $\lambda$ must be $> 0$.
- ✅ **Key Insight:** The MLE of Poisson's $\lambda$ is the *sample mean* — a unique property the examiner loves to test.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bayesian Statistics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bayesian Statistics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 1 — Bayesian Statistics

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Bayesian Statistics** is a branch of statistical inference in which probability is interpreted as a *degree of belief* (a subjective, rational quantification of uncertainty) about an event or hypothesis, and this belief is updated rationally as new evidence or data becomes available. The entire framework is mathematically anchored on **Bayes' Theorem**, formulated by Reverend Thomas Bayes (1701–1761) and later refined by Pierre-Simon Laplace.

Formally, Bayesian statistics treats the unknown parameter $\theta$ (which may be a probability, a mean, a class label, or a word sense) as a **random variable** itself, and characterizes all knowledge about $\theta$ through a **probability distribution**. This stands in direct contrast to the classical (Frequentist) paradigm, which treats $\theta$ as a fixed but unknown constant and probability as a long-run frequency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** *Bayesian Statistics* in Module 1 of Computational Linguistics establishes the probabilistic foundation for **language modeling, text classification, speech recognition, machine translation, and probabilistic parsing**. Every subsequent NLP technique in this course ultimately rests on conditional probability and Bayes' rule.

### 1.2 The Bayesian Worldview — Core Terminology

| Term | Symbol | Meaning in Computational Linguistics |
|---|---|---|
| **Prior Probability** | $P(\theta)$ | Belief about hypothesis $\theta$ **before** seeing the data (e.g., unigram probability of "the" in English). |
| **Likelihood** | $P(D \mid \theta)$ | Probability of observing the data $D$ assuming hypothesis $\theta$ is true (e.g., probability of a document given it belongs to class $C$). |
| **Posterior Probability** | $P(\theta \mid D)$ | Updated belief about $\theta$ **after** observing the data — the central object of Bayesian inference. |
| **Evidence / Marginal Likelihood** | $P(D)$ | Total probability of the data under all possible hypotheses; acts as a normalising constant. |
| **Predictive Distribution** | $P(D_{\text{new}} \mid D)$ | Probability of new data after learning from $D$. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bayesian vs. Frequentist (Frequentist–Bayesian Duality):** A frequentist computes $P(D \mid \theta)$ — "how likely is this data if the parameter is fixed?" A Bayesian computes $P(\theta \mid D)$ — "how likely is this parameter, given that I have seen this data?" Bayesian methods answer the *inverse probability* problem, which is precisely the direction required in language understanding.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Doctor's Diagnosis"

Imagine you visit a doctor with a persistent cough. Before any test, the doctor has a *prior belief* (based on your age, season, region) that you probably have a common cold — say, $P(\text{Cold}) = 0.7$. The doctor then orders a chest X-ray. The X-ray result is the *evidence/data* $D$. The probability of seeing this exact X-ray pattern **if** you had a cold is the *likelihood* $P(D \mid \text{Cold})$. Using Bayes' rule, the doctor updates her belief to get the *posterior* $P(\text{Cold} \mid D)$ — the probability of cold **given** the X-ray.

Now replace "Cold" with a *word sense*, *part-of-speech tag*, *named entity class*, or *syntactic parse tree*. Replace "X-ray" with a *sentence, a corpus, a feature vector*. **This is exactly how Bayesian NLP works** — at its core, language understanding is rational belief revision under textual evidence.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Hook for KTU Exams:** Whenever a question mentions "the probability of a class given features" or "the probability of a tag given a word," think *posterior* and immediately write Bayes' rule.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Probability Axioms (Kolmogorov, 1933)

Bayesian statistics is built upon three foundational axioms. Let $A$ and $B$ be events in a sample space $\Omega$ with probability measure $P$.

- **Non-negativity:** $P(A) \geq 0$ for every event $A$.
- **Normalization:** $P(\Omega) = 1$.
- **Additivity (countable):** For mutually exclusive events $A_1, A_2, \ldots$, $P\!\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty} A_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} P(A_i)$.

From these, all probability identities — including conditional probability and Bayes' theorem — are derived.

## 2.2 Conditional Probability — The Logical Starting Point

The **conditional probability** of event $A$ given that event $B$ has occurred (with $P(B) > 0$) is defined as:

$$
P(A \mid B) \;=\; \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}
$$

Equivalently, the **product rule** (chain rule of probability) follows immediately:

$$
P(A \cap B) \;=\; P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B) \;=\; P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)
$$

This symmetric form is the seed from which Bayes' theorem germinates.

## 2.3 Derivation of Bayes' Theorem (Logical Flow)

Starting from the product rule written two ways:

$$
P(A \mid B)\,P(B) \;=\; P(B \mid A)\,P(A)
$$

Dividing both sides by $P(B)$ (assuming $P(B) > 0$):

$$
P(A \mid B) \;=\; \frac{P(B \mid A)\,P(A)}{P(B)}
$$

This is **Bayes' Theorem in its elemental form**. Replacing the abstract labels with the standard Bayesian vocabulary $H$ (hypothesis) and $D$ (data):

$$
P(H \mid D) \;=\; \frac{P(D \mid H)\,P(H)}{P(D)}
$$

For a discrete hypothesis space $\{H_1, H_2, \ldots, H_n\}$ that partitions the sample space, the evidence $P(D)$ expands via the **Law of Total Probability**:

$$
P(D) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(D \mid H_i)\,P(H_i)
$$

Hence the fully expanded **Bayesian update** becomes:

$$
P(H_k \mid D) \;=\; \frac{P(D \mid H_k)\,P(H_k)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} P(D \mid H_i)\,P(H_i)}
$$

## 2.4 Independence, Conditional Independence, and the Naive Bayes Assumption

Two events $A$ and $B$ are **independent** if and only if:

$$
P(A \cap B) \;=\; P(A)\,P(B) \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad P(A \mid B) \;=\; P(A)
$$

A stronger, computationally indispensable notion is **conditional independence**: $A$ and $B$ are conditionally independent given $C$ if

$$
P(A \cap B \mid C) \;=\; P(A \mid C)\,P(B \mid C)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Naive Bayes Assumption (the "naive" in Naive Bayes):** For a document $D$ represented by features $f_1, f_2, \ldots, f_n$ and a class $C$, Naive Bayes assumes that the features are conditionally independent given the class:
> $$P(f_1, f_2, \ldots, f_n \mid C) \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(f_i \mid C)$$
> This assumption is rarely true in real text (words are obviously correlated), yet Naive Bayes classifiers perform remarkably well in spam filtering, sentiment analysis, and document categorisation — a celebrated result in computational linguistics.

## 2.5 Bayesian Classification (Maximum A Posteriori — MAP)

In classification, the goal is to choose the most probable class $C^*$ for a given document $D$. Because $P(D)$ is constant across classes, we may drop it and apply the **argmax** operator:

$$
C^{*} \;=\; \arg\max_{C \in \mathcal{C}} \; P(C \mid D) \;=\; \arg\max_{C \in \mathcal{C}} \; P(D \mid C)\,P(C)
$$

This is the **MAP (Maximum A Posteriori) decision rule**. The Frequentist counterpart, which uses only the likelihood, is called **MLE (Maximum Likelihood Estimation)**:

$$
C^{*}_{\text{MLE}} \;=\; \arg\max_{C \in \mathcal{C}} \; P(D \mid C)
$$

## 2.6 Bayesian Parameter Estimation and Smoothing

When estimating a parameter such as a bigram probability $P(w_2 \mid w_1)$ from a corpus of size $N$, the **Maximum Likelihood Estimate (MLE)** is

$$
\hat{P}(w_2 \mid w_1) \;=\; \frac{\mathrm{count}(w_1, w_2)}{\mathrm{count}(w_1)}
$$

This estimate assigns zero probability to unseen events, which is catastrophic for language models. The **Bayesian remedy** introduces a **Dirichlet prior** (or its discrete special case, **Laplace/add-one smoothing**):

$$
P_{\text{Laplace}}(w_2 \mid w_1) \;=\; \frac{\mathrm{count}(w_1, w_2) + 1}{\mathrm{count}(w_1) + V}
$$

where $V$ is the vocabulary size. More generally, with a Dirichlet prior of concentration parameter $\alpha$,

$$
P_{\text{Bayes}}(w_2 \mid w_1) \;=\; \frac{\mathrm{count}(w_1, w_2) + \alpha_{w_2}}{\mathrm{count}(w_1) + \sum_{v} \alpha_{v}}
$$

This is the **Bayesian estimate with a Dirichlet prior**, also called **add-$\alpha$ smoothing** — a direct KTU-relevant formula.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table contains every formula you are expected to recognise and apply in a KTU 2024 ESE question on Bayesian Statistics. Memorise it.**

| # | Formula | LaTeX Form | Application in NLP |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bayes' Theorem | $P(H \mid D) = \dfrac{P(D \mid H)\,P(H)}{P(D)}$ | Foundational update rule |
| 2 | Law of Total Probability | $P(D) = \sum_{i} P(D \mid H_i) P(H_i)$ | Marginalising out hypotheses |
| 3 | Posterior Odds (Bayes Factor) | $\dfrac{P(H \mid D)}{P(\bar{H} \mid D)} = \dfrac{P(D \mid H)}{P(D \mid \bar{H})} \cdot \dfrac{P(H)}{P(\bar{H})}$ | Hypothesis testing, WSD |
| 4 | Naive Bayes Posterior | $P(C \mid f_1,\dots,f_n) \propto P(C)\prod_{i=1}^{n} P(f_i \mid C)$ | Text classification |
| 5 | MLE for Bigram | $\hat{P}(w_2 \mid w_1) = \dfrac{C(w_1,w_2)}{C(w_1)}$ | Language model estimation |
| 6 | Laplace Smoothing | $P_{\text{Lap}}(w_2 \mid w_1) = \dfrac{C(w_1,w_2)+1}{C(w_1)+V}$ | Zero-problem fix |
| 7 | Dirichlet-Bayes Estimate | $P_{\text{Dir}}(w_2 \mid w_1) = \dfrac{C(w_1,w_2) + \alpha_{w_2}}{C(w_1) + \sum_v \alpha_v}$ | Bayesian LM |
| 8 | MAP Estimate | $\theta_{\text{MAP}} = \arg\max_{\theta} P(D \mid \theta)P(\theta)$ | Regularised parameter learning |
| 9 | MLE Estimate | $\theta_{\text{MLE}} = \arg\max_{\theta} P(D \mid \theta)$ | Pure Frequentist baseline |
| 10 | Conjugate Prior Relation | Posterior $\propto$ Likelihood $\times$ Prior | Whole of Bayesian inference |

## 2.8 Real-World Utility in Computational Linguistics

| Application | Bayesian Role |
|---|---|
| **Spam Detection** | Compute $P(\text{spam} \mid \text{words})$ via Naive Bayes. |
| **Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD)** | Compare posteriors of competing senses given context. |
| **Part-of-Speech Tagging** | HMM taggers use Bayesian inference over tag sequences. |
| **Machine Translation** | IBM Models 1–5 use Bayes-derived alignments. |
| **Speech Recognition** | Acoustic model + language model combined via Bayes. |
| **Topic Models (LDA)** | Latent Dirichlet Allocation is fully Bayesian. |
| **Sentiment Analysis** | Naive Bayes / Bayesian Logistic Regression. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Bayesian Update on a Text Corpus

**Problem.** A corpus contains **100 documents**: 60 about *Sports*, 40 about *Politics*. The word "goal" appears in 45 of the 60 Sports documents and in 5 of the 40 Politics documents. A new document contains the single word "goal". Using Bayes' theorem, compute $P(\text{Sports} \mid \text{``goal''})$ and $P(\text{Politics} \mid \text{``goal''})$.

**Step 1 — Compute the priors.**

The priors are the base rates of each class in the corpus:

$$
P(\text{Sports}) \;=\; \frac{60}{100} \;=\; 0.60
$$

$$
P(\text{Politics}) \;=\; \frac{40}{100} \;=\; 0.40
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the likelihoods.**

The likelihoods are the probabilities of seeing the word "goal" within each class:

$$
P(\text{``goal''} \mid \text{Sports}) \;=\; \frac{45}{60} \;=\; 0.75
$$

$$
P(\text{``goal''} \mid \text{Politics}) \;=\; \frac{5}{40} \;=\; 0.125
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the evidence $P(\text{``goal''})$ using the Law of Total Probability.**

The two classes {Sports, Politics} partition the corpus, so:

$$
P(\text{``goal''}) \;=\; P(\text{``goal''} \mid \text{Sports})\,P(\text{Sports}) + P(\text{``goal''} \mid \text{Politics})\,P(\text{Politics})
$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$
P(\text{``goal''}) \;=\; (0.75 \times 0.60) + (0.125 \times 0.40)
$$

$$
P(\text{``goal''}) \;=\; 0.450 + 0.050
$$

$$
P(\text{``goal''}) \;=\; 0.500
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Bayes' theorem to obtain the posterior for Sports.**

$$
P(\text{Sports} \mid \text{``goal''}) \;=\; \frac{P(\text{``goal''} \mid \text{Sports})\,P(\text{Sports})}{P(\text{``goal''})}
$$

$$
P(\text{Sports} \mid \text{``goal''}) \;=\; \frac{0.75 \times 0.60}{0.500} \;=\; \frac{0.450}{0.500} \;=\; 0.90
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Bayes' theorem to obtain the posterior for Politics.**

$$
P(\text{Politics} \mid \text{``goal''}) \;=\; \frac{0.125 \times 0.40}{0.500} \;=\; \frac{0.050}{0.500} \;=\; 0.10
$$

**Step 6 — Verification.**

The two posteriors must sum to 1 (total probability):

$$
P(\text{Sports} \mid \text{``goal''}) + P(\text{Politics} \mid \text{``goal''}) \;=\; 0.90 + 0.10 \;=\; 1.00 \quad\checkmark
$$

**Interpretation.** Even though the prior $P(\text{Sports}) = 0.60$ is only moderately higher than $P(\text{Politics}) = 0.40$, the single word "goal" radically shifts the posterior. This is a concrete demonstration of the **power of evidence** in Bayesian updating — a point examiners love to test.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Naive Bayes Text Classification with Two Features

**Problem.** A spam filter is trained on three features: contains the word *offer* ($f_1$), contains the word *free* ($f_2$), and contains the word *meeting* ($f_3$). The training statistics are:

| Class | $P(C)$ | $P(f_1 \mid C)$ | $P(f_2 \mid C)$ | $P(f_3 \mid C)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Spam (S) | 0.30 | 0.80 | 0.70 | 0.10 |
| Ham (H) | 0.70 | 0.10 | 0.05 | 0.60 |

An incoming email contains *offer* and *free* but **not** *meeting*. Classify it using the MAP rule.

**Step 1 — Write the MAP decision rule for Spam.**

Using Naive Bayes, the joint likelihood of observing the feature vector under the Spam hypothesis is the product of individual feature likelihoods:

$$
P(\text{``offer''}, \text{``free''}, \overline{\text{``meeting''}} \mid S) \;=\; P(f_1 \mid S)\cdot P(f_2 \mid S)\cdot P(\bar{f_3} \mid S)
$$

The probability of **not** meeting under Spam is the complement:

$$
P(\bar{f_3} \mid S) \;=\; 1 - P(f_3 \mid S) \;=\; 1 - 0.10 \;=\; 0.90
$$

So:

$$
P(\vec{f} \mid S) \;=\; 0.80 \times 0.70 \times 0.90
$$

$$
P(\vec{f} \mid S) \;=\; 0.504
$$

Multiply by the prior:

$$
