P(\vec{f} \mid S)\,P(S) \;=\; 0.504 \times 0.30 \;=\; 0.1512
$$

**Step 2 — Repeat for Ham.**

$$
P(\bar{f_3} \mid H) \;=\; 1 - 0.60 \;=\; 0.40
$$

$$
P(\vec{f} \mid H) \;=\; 0.10 \times 0.05 \times 0.40 \;=\; 0.002
$$

Multiply by the prior:

$$
P(\vec{f} \mid H)\,P(H) \;=\; 0.002 \times 0.70 \;=\; 0.0014
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the argmax MAP rule.**

Because $P(D)$ is the same in both, we compare the unnormalised numerators:

$$
0.1512 \;>\; 0.0014
$$

Therefore:

$$
C^{*} \;=\; \arg\max_{C \in \{S, H\}} P(C \mid \vec{f}) \;=\; \text{Spam}
$$

**Step 4 — (Optional) Compute the exact posteriors using $P(D)$.**

Total evidence:

$$
P(\vec{f}) \;=\; 0.1512 + 0.0014 \;=\; 0.1526
$$

Posterior for Spam:

$$
P(S \mid \vec{f}) \;=\; \frac{0.1512}{0.1526} \;\approx\; 0.9908
$$

Posterior for Ham:

$$
P(H \mid \vec{f}) \;=\; \frac{0.0014}{0.1526} \;\approx\; 0.0092
$$

**Interpretation.** The Bayesian spam filter is **99.08% confident** that the email is spam — a near-certain classification derived from just three binary features.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Bayesian Smoothing on Bigram Counts

**Problem.** A small corpus has the following bigram counts: $C(\text{``of the''}) = 8$, $C(\text{``of''}) = 10$, vocabulary size $V = 1000$. Compute (a) the MLE, (b) the Laplace-smoothed estimate, and (c) the add-0.1 Dirichlet-smoothed estimate for $P(\text{``the''} \mid \text{``of''})$.

**Part (a) — MLE.**

$$
\hat{P}_{\text{MLE}}(\text{the} \mid \text{of}) \;=\; \frac{C(\text{of, the})}{C(\text{of})} \;=\; \frac{8}{10} \;=\; 0.80
$$

**Part (b) — Laplace (add-one) smoothing.**

$$
P_{\text{Lap}}(\text{the} \mid \text{of}) \;=\; \frac{C(\text{of, the}) + 1}{C(\text{of}) + V} \;=\; \frac{8 + 1}{10 + 1000} \;=\; \frac{9}{1010} \;\approx\; 0.00891
$$

Note the dramatic reduction: Laplace smoothing distributes probability mass over the entire vocabulary, so individual probabilities shrink — this is the expected behaviour.

**Part (c) — Dirichlet smoothing with $\alpha = 0.1$.**

With $\alpha$ uniform across the vocabulary, $\alpha_w = 0.1$ for all $w$, so $\sum_v \alpha_v = 0.1 \times V = 100$:

$$
P_{\text{Dir}}(\text{the} \mid \text{of}) \;=\; \frac{C(\text{of, the}) + 0.1}{C(\text{of}) + 100} \;=\; \frac{8.1}{110} \;\approx\; 0.0736
$$

**Analytical Insight.** MLE (0.80) is unrealistically high because the corpus is tiny. Laplace (0.0089) is far too low because add-one is too aggressive for large $V$. Dirichlet with $\alpha = 0.1$ (0.0736) hits a reasonable middle ground — illustrating why Bayesian smoothing with tuned priors dominates MLE in real language models.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Bayesian Reasoning Patterns in KTU

| Scenario | Prior $P(H)$ | Likelihood $P(D \mid H)$ | Posterior $P(H \mid D)$ | Take-Away |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Medical test for rare disease | Low (0.01) | High if sick, low if healthy | Still low for rare disease (base-rate fallacy) | Priors matter enormously |
| Frequent word in English | High (0.07 for "the") | High if document is English | Slightly higher | Evidence confirms the prior |
| Bigram never seen in training | MLE = 0 | N/A | $\approx 0$ breaks the LM | Smoothing rescues us |
| Strong contextual cue for word sense | Equal priors | One sense has much higher $P(D \mid H)$ | Posterior concentrates on one sense | Evidence disambiguates |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bayesian Inference Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram captures the complete **Bayesian update pipeline** as applied in computational linguistics — from raw data input to posterior-based decision.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Corpus / Document D] --> B[Extract Features]
    B --> C[Compute Likelihood P(D given H)]
    D[Background Knowledge] --> E[Define Prior P of H]
    C --> F[Apply Bayes Theorem]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Compute Evidence P of D]
    F --> H[Compute Posterior P of H given D]
    H --> I{Decision Stage}
    I --> J[Classification via argmax]
    I --> K[Probabilistic Prediction]
    I --> L[Parameter Estimation]
    J --> M[Output Class Label]
    K --> N[Output Distribution]
    L --> O[Refined Model]
```

**Reading the diagram.** Start at node **A** with the raw textual input. The pipeline extracts features (**B**), evaluates how likely those features are under each competing hypothesis (**C**), and merges this with background knowledge encoded as the prior (**D → E**). Bayes' theorem (**F**) combines both ingredients; the evidence (**G**) normalises the result into a true posterior (**H**). The downstream consumer (**I**) may be classification, prediction, or parameter refinement.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Naive Bayes Classifier

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[Training Corpus] --> P1[Prior Estimation P of C]
    D1 --> P2[Conditional Feature Counts P of f given C]
    P1 --> M1[Multiply Priors and Likelihoods]
    P2 --> M1
    M1 --> M2[Argmax over Classes]
    T1[Test Document] --> F1[Feature Extraction]
    F1 --> M1
    M2 --> OUT[Predicted Class Label]
```

**Reading the diagram.** The left branch builds the model from training data: class priors and conditional feature probabilities. The right branch transforms a test document into features. The two streams converge in the MAP decision module, which outputs the predicted class label.

## 4.3 Hierarchical Bayesian Reasoning (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[Bayesian Statistics] --> R1[Probability as Belief]
    R0 --> R2[Bayes Theorem]
    R0 --> R3[Inference]
    R1 --> R11[Prior P of H]
    R1 --> R12[Posterior P of H given D]
    R2 --> R21[Conditional Probability]
    R2 --> R22[Law of Total Probability]
    R2 --> R23[Naive Bayes Assumption]
    R3 --> R31[MAP Estimation]
    R3 --> R32[MLE Baseline]
    R3 --> R33[Bayesian Smoothing]
    R33 --> R331[Laplace Smoothing]
    R33 --> R332[Dirichlet Prior Smoothing]
    R11 --> NLP1[Text Classification]
    R12 --> NLP1
    R23 --> NLP1
    R31 --> NLP1
    R33 --> NLP2[Language Modelling]
    NLP1 --> APP1[Spam Detection]
    NLP1 --> APP2[Sentiment Analysis]
    NLP2 --> APP3[Speech Recognition]
    NLP2 --> APP4[Machine Translation]
```

**Reading the diagram.** Bayesian statistics decomposes into three pillars: *belief interpretation*, *the theorem itself*, and *inference algorithms*. Each pillar generates NLP applications. This is the cognitive map you should hold in your head while writing the exam.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read This First!**
> Examiners report that students **lose 2 to 4 marks per question** on Bayesian problems for the following recurring mistakes:
> 1. **Confusing prior and likelihood** — writing $P(H \mid D)$ when $P(D \mid H)$ is asked. Always read the conditioning bar.
> 2. **Forgetting the normalising constant $P(D)$** — in MAP classification it is fine to drop $P(D)$, but if the question explicitly asks for a **posterior probability**, you **must** compute it.
> 3. **Treating MLE and MAP as identical** — MLE maximises $P(D \mid \theta)$; MAP maximises $P(D \mid \theta)P(\theta)$. The prior is the difference.
> 4. **Skipping the verification step** — always check $\sum P(C \mid D) = 1$ to catch arithmetic slips.
> 5. **Ignoring the "naive" assumption** — in Naive Bayes derivations, you must **state** the conditional independence assumption before writing the product formula.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State and explain Bayes' Theorem in probability. Discuss its significance in computational linguistics with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Bayes' theorem expresses the posterior probability of a hypothesis $H$ given observed data $D$ in terms of the prior and the likelihood:

$$
P(H \mid D) \;=\; \frac{P(D \mid H)\,P(H)}{P(D)}
$$

where $P(H)$ is the **prior** belief about the hypothesis, $P(D \mid H)$ is the **likelihood** of observing $D$ under $H$, and $P(D)$ is the **evidence** acting as a normalising constant.

**Significance in Computational Linguistics:** Bayes' theorem allows us to compute the probability of a *linguistic category* (such as a part-of-speech tag, word sense, or document class) given *observed text* — exactly the inverse-probability problem that NLP faces. For example, in spam detection we compute $P(\text{spam} \mid \text{email content})$ by combining the prior $P(\text{spam})$ with the likelihood of the email's words under the spam hypothesis. The posterior is the basis for the MAP classification rule used in Naive Bayes text classifiers. *(3 marks — 1 for formula, 1 for terminology, 1 for NLP example.)*

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Distinguish between Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) and Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) estimation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | MLE | MAP |
|---|---|---|
| Objective | $\theta_{\text{MLE}} = \arg\max_{\theta} P(D \mid \theta)$ | $\theta_{\text{MAP}} = \arg\max_{\theta} P(D \mid \theta)\,P(\theta)$ |
| Uses prior? | No | Yes |
| Paradigm | Frequentist | Bayesian |
| Behaviour with small data | Overfits, may give zero probabilities | Regularised by the prior |
| NLP example | Bigram MLE: $\hat{P}(w_2 \mid w_1) = \frac{C(w_1,w_2)}{C(w_1)}$ | Laplace-smoothed bigram: $\frac{C(w_1,w_2)+1}{C(w_1)+V}$ |

MLE selects the parameter value that makes the observed data most probable. MAP additionally incorporates a prior distribution $P(\theta)$, which regularises the estimate and prevents degenerate values such as zero probabilities. In language modelling, MLE assigns zero probability to unseen n-grams, whereas MAP (with a uniform or Dirichlet prior) assigns a small non-zero probability — making MAP strictly more robust in practice. *(3 marks.)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Answer the following:**
**(a)** Derive Bayes' theorem from the axioms of conditional probability. Explain each term with reference to a text classification task. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Consider a binary text classification problem with classes *Sports* and *Politics*. The corpus contains 200 Sports and 300 Politics documents. The word "match" appears in 120 Sports and 30 Politics documents. Compute $P(\text{Sports} \mid \text{``match''})$ using Bayes' theorem. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation of Bayes' Theorem (7 marks)**

We begin with the product rule of probability. For any two events $A$ and $B$ with $P(B) > 0$:

$$
P(A \cap B) \;=\; P(A \mid B)\,P(B)
$$

By the commutativity of intersection, the same joint probability can be written as:

$$
P(B \cap A) \;=\; P(B \mid A)\,P(A)
$$

Since $P(A \cap B) = P(B \cap A)$, we equate:

$$
P(A \mid B)\,P(B) \;=\; P(B \mid A)\,P(A)
$$

Dividing both sides by $P(B)$:

$$
P(A \mid B) \;=\; \frac{P(B \mid A)\,P(A)}{P(B)}
$$

Replacing $A \rightarrow H$ (hypothesis) and $B \rightarrow D$ (data):

$$
P(H \mid D) \;=\; \frac{P(D \mid H)\,P(H)}{P(D)}
$$

**Term interpretation in text classification (e.g., spam detection):**
- $P(H \mid D) = P(\text{spam} \mid \text{email})$ — **posterior**: probability that the email is spam given its content.
- $P(D \mid H) = P(\text{email} \mid \text{spam})$ — **likelihood**: probability of seeing this email if it were spam.
- $P(H) = P(\text{spam})$ — **prior**: base rate of spam in incoming mail.
- $P(D)$ — **evidence**: total probability of this email under all classes. *(7 marks: 4 for derivation, 3 for term-by-term NLP interpretation.)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Compute priors.**

$$
P(\text{Sports}) = \frac{200}{200 + 300} = \frac{200}{500} = 0.40
$$

$$
P(\text{Politics}) = \frac{300}{500} = 0.60
$$

**Step 2 — Compute likelihoods.**

$$
P(\text{``match''} \mid \text{Sports}) = \frac{120}{200} = 0.60
$$

$$
P(\text{``match''} \mid \text{Politics}) = \frac{30}{300} = 0.10
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the evidence via the Law of Total Probability.**

$$
P(\text{``match''}) = (0.60 \times 0.40) + (0.10 \times 0.60) = 0.240 + 0.060 = 0.300
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Bayes' theorem.**

$$
P(\text{Sports} \mid \text{``match''}) = \frac{0.60 \times 0.40}{0.300} = \frac{0.240}{0.300} = 0.80
$$

**Step 5 — Verification.**

$$
P(\text{Politics} \mid \text{``match''}) = 1 - 0.80 = 0.20
$$

Direct check: $\frac{0.10 \times 0.60}{0.300} = \frac{0.060}{0.300} = 0.20 \checkmark$

**Answer:** $P(\text{Sports} \mid \text{``match''}) = \mathbf{0.80}$. *(7 marks: 1 each for priors, likelihoods, evidence, Bayes step, final answer, plus 2 for verification and interpretation.)*

---

### Question B (Internal Choice). `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Answer the following:**
**(a)** Explain the Naive Bayes classifier for text classification. State the "naive" assumption explicitly and derive the decision rule. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A Naive Bayes spam classifier has the following statistics: $P(\text{spam}) = 0.25$, $P(\text{ham}) = 0.75$, $P(\text{``offer''} \mid \text{spam}) = 0.70$, $P(\text{``offer''} \mid \text{ham}) = 0.05$, $P(\text{``free''} \mid \text{spam}) = 0.60$, $P(\text{``free''} \mid \text{ham}) = 0.02$. Classify an email containing *both* "offer" and "free" using the MAP rule. Show all steps. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Naive Bayes Classifier Theory (7 marks)**

The **Naive Bayes classifier** is a probabilistic model that assigns a document $D$ (represented as a feature vector $\vec{f} = (f_1, f_2, \ldots, f_n)$) to the class $C$ that maximises the posterior $P(C \mid \vec{f})$.

**The Naive Assumption (explicitly stated):**
The features $f_1, f_2, \ldots, f_n$ are **conditionally independent of each other given the class** $C$. Formally:

$$
P(f_1, f_2, \ldots, f_n \mid C) \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(f_i \mid C)
$$

This is "naive" because in real text the words are highly correlated (e.g., "New" and "York"), yet the simplification often yields excellent classification accuracy.

**Derivation of the Decision Rule.**

By Bayes' theorem, the posterior is

$$
P(C \mid \vec{f}) \;=\; \frac{P(\vec{f} \mid C)\,P(C)}{P(\vec{f})}
$$

Since $P(\vec{f})$ does not depend on the class, it is a constant during classification and can be dropped. Applying the naive assumption:

$$
P(\vec{f} \mid C) \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(f_i \mid C)
$$

Therefore, the **Naive Bayes MAP decision rule** is

$$
C^{*} \;=\; \arg\max_{C \in \mathcal{C}} \; P(C) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(f_i \mid C)
$$

*(7 marks: 2 for stating the assumption, 3 for derivation, 2 for the final decision rule.)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical MAP Classification (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Write the unnormalised posteriors for both classes.**

For the email $D$ containing *offer* and *free*:

$$
P(D \mid \text{spam})\,P(\text{spam}) \;=\; P(\text{``offer''} \mid \text{spam})\cdot P(\text{``free''} \mid \text{spam}) \cdot P(\text{spam})
$$

$$
P(D \mid \text{ham})\,P(\text{ham}) \;=\; P(\text{``offer''} \mid \text{ham})\cdot P(\text{``free''} \mid \text{ham}) \cdot P(\text{ham})
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Spam unnormalised posterior.**

$$
P(D \mid \text{spam})\,P(\text{spam}) = 0.70 \times 0.60 \times 0.25 = 0.105
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Ham unnormalised posterior.**

$$
P(D \mid \text{ham})\,P(\text{ham}) = 0.05 \times 0.02 \times 0.75 = 0.00075
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the argmax MAP rule.**

Because $0.105 > 0.00075$:

$$
C^{*} = \arg\max \{\,0.105,\; 0.00075\,\} = \text{Spam}
$$

**Step 5 — (Optional but credit-worthy) Compute exact posteriors.**

Total evidence:

$$
P(D) = 0.105 + 0.00075 = 0.10575
$$

Posterior probabilities:

$$
P(\text{spam} \mid D) = \frac{0.105}{0.10575} \approx 0.9929
$$

$$
P(\text{ham} \mid D) = \frac{0.00075}{0.10575} \approx 0.0071
$$

**Final Answer:** The email is classified as **Spam** with posterior probability $\approx 0.9929$. *(7 marks.)*

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the KTU 2024 ESE — Module 1: Bayesian Statistics**

- **Bayes' Theorem** $P(H \mid D) = \dfrac{P(D \mid H)\,P(H)}{P(D)}$ — write it from memory in under 10 seconds.
- **Four pillars**: prior $P(H)$, likelihood $P(D \mid H)$, evidence $P(D)$, posterior $P(H \mid D)$.
- **MAP rule** for classification: $C^{*} = \arg\max_C P(C) P(D \mid C)$.
- **MLE** uses only the likelihood; **MAP** multiplies by the prior.
- **Naive Bayes assumption**: features are conditionally independent given the class — **state it explicitly** in every answer.
- **Zero-probability problem** in language models → solved by **Laplace (add-one)** or **Dirichlet** smoothing.
- **Laplace formula**: $P(w_2 \mid w_1) = \dfrac{C(w_1,w_2) + 1}{C(w_1) + V}$.
- **Dirichlet formula**: $P(w_2 \mid w_1) = \dfrac{C(w_1,w_2) + \alpha_{w_2}}{C(w_1) + \sum_v \alpha_v}$.
- **Posterior probabilities must sum to 1** — always verify at the end of a numerical problem.
- **Bayes' factor** = $\dfrac{P(D \mid H)}{P(D \mid \bar{H})}$ — useful in word-sense disambiguation questions.
- **NLP applications** to mention in any 14-mark answer: spam filtering, POS tagging, WSD, MT alignment, topic modelling (LDA), sentiment analysis.
- **Frequentist vs Bayesian**: the former treats parameters as fixed constants; the latter treats them as random variables with distributions.
- **Examiner's hot-button**: forgetting the normalising constant $P(D)$ in posterior calculation — costs 1 to 2 marks per slip.
- **Vocabulary size $V$** appears in every smoothing formula — do not omit it.
- **Use $\arg\max$ notation** (not just "pick the biggest") to score the methodology mark.
- **Conjugate prior** concept: when prior and posterior belong to the same distribution family, the prior is conjugate to the likelihood. Dirichlet–Multinomial is the canonical pair for language models.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forming Equivalence Classes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Forming Equivalence Classes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the mathematical foundations of **Computational Linguistics** and **Formal Language Theory**, an **equivalence relation** ~ on a set S is a binary relation that partitions S into disjoint subsets called **equivalence classes**. Formally, an equivalence class of an element $x \in S$ is defined as:

$$[x] = \{ y \in S \mid x \sim y \}$$

In the context of formal languages over an alphabet $\Sigma$, we define the **Nerode right congruence** (or right-invariant equivalence) on $\Sigma^*$ with respect to a language $L \subseteq \Sigma^*$ as:

$$x \sim_L y \iff (\forall z \in \Sigma^*) : (xz \in L \iff yz \in L)$$

Two strings $x$ and $y$ are **equivalent** if and only if, when followed by *any* suffix $z$, they both yield the same membership status in the language $L$ (i.e., both in $L$ or both not in $L$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme — PEADT632 Module 2):**
> Forming equivalence classes is the *theoretical bridge* between regular expressions, finite automata, and the minimal deterministic recognizer of a language. It is the central tool in the **Myhill–Nerode Theorem** and the algorithmic basis of **DFA Minimization**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Library Catalog" Metaphor**
>
> Imagine a vast library where every book string is uniquely identified by its call number. Two books are placed in the **same equivalence class** if a librarian, scanning *any* future borrower slip, cannot distinguish which book was originally there based on the borrowing history alone. They are *behaviourally indistinguishable* with respect to the library's lending language.
>
> In NLP, the same idea governs **tokenization equivalence**: two character streams belong to the same class if every downstream language model assigns them identical continuation probabilities.

### 1.3 Three Defining Properties

For a relation ~ to qualify as an **equivalence relation**, it must satisfy exactly three axioms:

| Property | Symbolic Form | Plain English Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Reflexivity** | $x \sim x$ | Every element is equivalent to itself. |
| **Symmetry** | $x \sim y \Rightarrow y \sim x$ | If $a$ relates to $b$, then $b$ relates to $a$. |
| **Transitivity** | $x \sim y \text{ and } y \sim z \Rightarrow x \sim z$ | Equivalence is chainable. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A relation that satisfies only the first two is called a **tolerance relation**. A relation that is reflexive and transitive (but not necessarily symmetric) is a **preorder** or **quasi-order**. Only the full triple earns the label "equivalence."

### 1.4 Right Congruence — The Linguistic Twist

A relation ~ on $\Sigma^*$ is a **right congruence** if it is an equivalence relation *and* it is preserved under right-concatenation:

$$x \sim y \iff (\forall z \in \Sigma^*) : (xz \sim yz)$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is precisely why it is the *right* tool for language recognition: an automaton reads input left-to-right, so the **future suffix** is what matters — never the past prefix.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How Equivalence Classes Are Formed

The construction proceeds in discrete logical stages:

1. **Seed the Relation**: Begin with the *Myhill–Nerode indistinguishability* definition — two prefixes are equivalent iff they route the machine to the same "fate class" (accept / reject) for every continuation.
2. **Reflexive Closure**: Declare $x \sim x$ trivially for every string $x \in \Sigma^*$. This grounds the partition.
3. **Pairwise Marking (Distinguishability Test)**: For each pair $(x, y)$, search for a *witness suffix* $z$ such that exactly one of $xz, yz$ is in $L$. If found, mark $(x, y)$ as **distinguishable** and split them into different classes.
4. **Transitive Closure**: Once $x \sim y$ and $y \sim z$ are established, force $x \sim z$ into the same class.
5. **Partition Refinement**: Iterate the marking process. Two states that are *not* marked distinguishable at the end belong to the **same equivalence class**.
6. **Quotient Construction**: The set of equivalence classes $\Sigma^* / \sim_L$ becomes the state set of the **minimal DFA** $M_L$.

### 2.2 The Myhill–Nerode Theorem (Core Theorem of Module 2)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Myhill–Nerode, 1958):**
> A language $L \subseteq \Sigma^*$ is **regular** if and only if the equivalence relation $\sim_L$ has a **finite number of equivalence classes**. Moreover, the number of equivalence classes equals the **minimum number of states** in any DFA accepting $L$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| $[x]_{\sim_L}$ | Equivalence class of $x$ under $\sim_L$ | Class representative |
| $\Sigma^* / \sim_L$ | Quotient set (set of all classes) | State set of minimal DFA |
| $\text{index}(\sim_L)$ | Number of distinct equivalence classes | Equals $\#$states of min. DFA |
| $x \sim_L y$ | $x$ and $y$ indistinguishable by $L$ | DFA state merging condition |
| $\delta([q], a) = [\delta(q,a)]$ | Well-defined transition on classes | Quotient automaton property |
| $L = \bigcup_{x \in R} [x]$ where $R$ picks one rep per class | $L$ is union of accepting classes | Accepting set construction |
| $z \in \Sigma^* \mid (xz \in L) \oplus (yz \in L)$ | Witness (distinguishing suffix) | Table-filling algorithm |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Equivalence-class minimization is the engine inside every modern **lexical analyzer** (e.g., `lex`, `flex`), **regex engine** (RE2, PCRE), and **compiler front-end**. It guarantees the smallest possible automaton, which directly translates to lower memory footprint and faster matching.

### 2.4 The Three Pillars of Distinguishability

1. **Language Membership Distinguishability**: $x, y$ differ on the empty suffix $\varepsilon$ iff exactly one is in $L$. This separates *accepting* from *non-accepting* states in the first pass.
2. **Single-Step (1-step) Distinguishability**: $x, y$ are 1-step distinguishable if for some symbol $a \in \Sigma$, $xa$ and $ya$ are already distinguished.
3. **Iterative Refinement**: Repeat 1-step distinguishability until no new pairs are marked — the **fixed point** is the final partition.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Equivalence Definition to Quotient Automaton

**Step 1 — Begin with the language $L$ and alphabet $\Sigma$.**

Let $L \subseteq \Sigma^*$. Define the indistinguishability relation:

$$x \sim_L y \iff (\forall z \in \Sigma^*)(xz \in L \leftrightarrow yz \in L)$$

**Step 2 — Verify it is an equivalence relation.**

- *Reflexive*: For any $x \in \Sigma^*$, the statement "$xz \in L \leftrightarrow xz \in L$" is a tautology, so $x \sim_L x$.
- *Symmetric*: The biconditional $\leftrightarrow$ is symmetric, so $x \sim_L y \Rightarrow y \sim_L x$.
- *Transitive*: Biconditional is transitive; chaining yields $x \sim_L z$.

**Step 3 — Verify it is a right congruence.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x \sim_L y &\iff (\forall z \in \Sigma^*)(xz \in L \leftrightarrow yz \in L) \\
&\iff (\forall w \in \Sigma^*)(\forall z \in \Sigma^*)\bigl((x w)z \in L \leftrightarrow (y w)z \in L\bigr) \\
&\iff (\forall w \in \Sigma^*)(xw \sim_L yw)
\end{aligned}
$$

The right side reads: *"for every appended string $w$, $xw$ and $yw$ remain indistinguishable."* Hence ~$_L$ is preserved under right-multiplication — it is a **right congruence**.

**Step 4 — Construct the quotient DFA $M_L / \sim_L$.**

$$
M_L \;=\; \bigl(\Sigma^*/{\sim_L},\; \Sigma,\; \delta_L,\; [\varepsilon],\; F_L \bigr)
$$

where:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_L([x], a) &= [xa] \\
F_L &= \{[x] \mid x \in L\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Show $\delta_L$ is well-defined.**

If $[x] = [y]$ (i.e., $x \sim_L y$), then for any $a \in \Sigma$:

$$(xa)z = x(az) \in L \iff y(az) = (ya)z \in L$$

Hence $xa \sim_L ya$, meaning $\delta_L([x], a) = [xa] = [ya] = \delta_L([y], a)$. The transition does not depend on the chosen representative. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Worked Example: Table-Filling Minimization

**Given DFA** $M$ over $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$ with states $\{A, B, C, D, E\}$, accepting state $=\{E\}$.

**Transition Table:**

| State | on $a$ | on $b$ | Accept? |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | B | C | No |
| B | B | D | No |
| C | B | C | No |
| D | C | E | No |
| E | B | C | Yes |

**Step 1 — Initialize: Mark all (accept, non-accept) pairs.**

Distinct pairs (lower-triangular): $(A,B), (A,C), (A,D), (A,E), (B,C), (B,D), (B,E), (C,D), (C,E), (D,E)$.

Mark all pairs where one member is accepting (E) and the other is not:

$$
\text{Marked} = \{(A,E),\, (B,E),\, (C,E),\, (D,E)\}
$$

**Step 2 — Iterative refinement. For each unmarked pair $(p,q)$, check all symbols.**

**Iteration 1 — Check $(A,B)$:**
- On $a$: pair $(B, B)$ — same state, **not distinguishable**. ✓
- On $b$: pair $(C, D)$ — currently **unmarked**. So no witness yet.

**Check $(A,C)$:**
- On $a$: $(B, B)$ — same. ✓
- On $b$: $(C, C)$ — same. ✓
- No distinguishing symbol found → $(A,C)$ **stays unmarked** so far.

**Check $(A,D)$:**
- On $a$: $(B, C)$ — unmarked. ✓
- On $b$: $(C, E)$ — **MARKED**! Witness suffix = $b$.
- ⇒ Mark $(A, D)$.

**Check $(B, C)$:**
- On $a$: $(B, B)$ — same. ✓
- On $b$: $(D, C)$ — unmarked. ✓
- No witness yet.

**Check $(B, D)$:**
- On $a$: $(B, C)$ — unmarked. ✓
- On $b$: $(D, E)$ — **MARKED**! Witness = $b$.
- ⇒ Mark $(B, D)$.

**Check $(C, D)$:**
- On $a$: $(B, C)$ — unmarked. ✓
- On $b$: $(C, E)$ — **MARKED**! Witness = $b$.
- ⇒ Mark $(C, D)$.

**Iteration 2 — Recheck $(A, B)$:**
- On $a$: $(B, B)$ — same. ✓
- On $b$: $(C, D)$ — now **MARKED** (from Iter 1). Witness = $b$.
- ⇒ Mark $(A, B)$.

**Recheck $(A, C)$:**
- On $a$: $(B, B)$ — same. ✓
- On $b$: $(C, C)$ — same. ✓
- No witness even after iteration 2. **Unmarked.**

**Recheck $(B, C)$:**
- On $a$: $(B, B)$ — same. ✓
- On $b$: $(D, C)$ — **MARKED** (same as $(C,D)$). Witness = $b$.
- ⇒ Mark $(B, C)$.

**Step 3 — Fixed-point reached. Final unmarked pairs:**

$$(A, C) \text{ only.}$$

**Step 4 — Form equivalence classes:**

$$[A] = \{A, C\}, \quad [B] = \{B\}, \quad [D] = \{D\}, \quad [E] = \{E\}$$

**Step 5 — Build minimal DFA** with 4 states (down from 5).

| State | on $a$ | on $b$ | Accept? |
|---|---|---|---|
| $[A] = \{A,C\}$ | $[B]$ | $[A]$ | No |
| $[B] = \{B\}$ | $[B]$ | $[D]$ | No |
| $[D] = \{D\}$ | $[A]$ | $[E]$ | No |
| $[E] = \{E\}$ | $[B]$ | $[A]$ | Yes |

> [!NOTE]
> The 5-state DFA was reduced to a **4-state minimal DFA** by merging states $A$ and $C$ into a single equivalence class — saving **20% memory** in the recognition engine.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — State-Pair Distinguishability Table

| Pair | Iter 0 | Iter 1 | Iter 2 | Final |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| (A,B) | — | — | ✗ (via b→CD) | ✗ |
| (A,C) | — | — | — | ✓ Equivalent |
| (A,D) | — | ✗ (via b→CE) | — | ✗ |
| (A,E) | ✗ | — | — | ✗ |
| (B,C) | — | — | ✗ (via b→DC) | ✗ |
| (B,D) | — | ✗ (via b→DE) | — | ✗ |
| (B,E) | ✗ | — | — | ✗ |
| (C,D) | — | ✗ (via b→CE) | — | ✗ |
| (C,E) | ✗ | — | — | ✗ |
| (D,E) | ✗ | — | — | ✗ |

(✗ = distinguishable / different classes; ✓ = indistinguishable / same class)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Equivalence Class Formation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Language L over Sigma] --> B[Define Nerode Relation x ~L y]
    B --> C{Verify Three Properties}
    C --> C1[Reflexive: x ~L x]
    C --> C2[Symmetric: x ~L y implies y ~L x]
    C --> C3[Transitive: chain closure]
    C1 --> D[Confirmed Equivalence Relation]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E[Check Right Congruence]
    E --> F[Enumerate String Pairs from Sigma-star]
    F --> G[Apply Table-Filling Algorithm]
    G --> G1[Init: mark accept-vs-nonaccept pairs]
    G1 --> G2[Iterate: for each unmarked pair, hunt for witness suffix z]
    G2 --> G3{Fixed point reached?}
    G3 -- No --> G2
    G3 -- Yes --> H[Partition Sigma-star into Classes]
    H --> I[Quotient Construction: M mod ~L]
    I --> J[Minimal DFA with N states]
    J --> K{N finite?}
    K -- Yes --> L[L is Regular]
    K -- No --> M[L is Non-Regular]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Partition Refinement State Graph

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Initial["Initial Partition P0"]
        direction TB
        NA["Non-Accepting Class = {A, B, C, D}"]
        AC["Accepting Class = {E}"]
    end

    subgraph Iter1["After Iteration 1"]
        direction TB
        P1A["{A, B, C}"]
        P1B["{D}"]
        P1C["{E}"]
    end

    subgraph Iter2["After Iteration 2 (Final)"]
        direction TB
        F1["{A, C}"]
        F2["{B}"]
        F3["{D}"]
        F4["{E}"]
    end

    Initial --> Iter1
    Iter1 --> Iter2
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Minimization Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    User([User Regex or DFA]) --> Front[Front-End Lexer]
    Front --> Parse[Parser: Build Raw NFA]
    Parse --> Det[Determinization: Subset Construction]
    Det --> RawDFA[(Raw DFA: Possibly Redundant)]
    RawDFA --> MinEngine{Minimization Engine}
    MinEngine --> T1[Step 1: Partition Accept vs Non-Accept]
    T1 --> T2[Step 2: Refine by Transition Signatures]
    T2 --> T3[Step 3: Iterate to Fixed Point]
    T3 --> T4[Step 4: Merge Equivalent States]
    T4 --> MinDFA[(Minimal DFA)]
    MinDFA --> Deploy([Deploy to Scanner / Tokenizer])
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Define an equivalence relation. State and briefly justify the three properties that a binary relation must satisfy to be called an equivalence relation.*

**Model Answer:**

An **equivalence relation** ~ on a set $S$ is a binary relation that partitions $S$ into disjoint subsets called equivalence classes. The three required properties are:

1. **Reflexivity**: $x \sim x$ for every $x \in S$.
2. **Symmetry**: If $x \sim y$, then $y \sim x$.
3. **Transitivity**: If $x \sim y$ and $y \sim z$, then $x \sim z$.

These three together guarantee that ~ induces a unique, well-defined partition of $S$ into non-overlapping equivalence classes whose union is $S$ itself.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 each for the three properties with brief justification)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is a right congruence? Why is right congruence (and not left congruence) the natural choice for defining language equivalence in finite automata?*

**Model Answer:**

A **right congruence** on $\Sigma^*$ is an equivalence relation ~ such that for all $x, y \in \Sigma^*$ and all $w \in \Sigma^*$:

$$x \sim y \implies xw \sim yw$$

Right congruence is natural for finite automata because **DFAs read input left-to-right**. The future behaviour of the machine depends only on what has been consumed (the prefix) and what is yet to come (the suffix). Two prefixes are "equivalent" iff every possible future suffix produces the same accept/reject outcome — this is exactly a right-congruence condition. Left congruence would require matching past history, which DFAs do not retain.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for the linguistic/automata justification)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Theory + Theorem Application*

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** State and prove the **Myhill–Nerode Theorem**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Consider the language $L = \{w \in \{a, b\}^* \mid w \text{ contains } bb \text{ as a substring}\}$. Find the number of equivalence classes of $\sim_L$. Construct the minimal DFA. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (a) — Myhill–Nerode Theorem:**

**Statement.** A language $L \subseteq \Sigma^*$ is regular **iff** the equivalence relation $\sim_L$ has a **finite** number of equivalence classes. Moreover, this number equals the **minimum number of states** in any DFA accepting $L$.

**Proof (⇒ direction — Regular ⇒ Finite Index):**
Let $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ be a DFA accepting $L$ with $|Q| = n$. For any $x, y \in \Sigma^*$, if $\hat{\delta}(q_0, x) = \hat{\delta}(q_0, y)$, then for any suffix $z$:

$$\hat{\delta}(q_0, xz) = \delta(\hat{\delta}(q_0, x), z) = \delta(\hat{\delta}(q_0, y), z) = \hat{\delta}(q_0, yz)$$

Hence $xz \in L \iff yz \in L$, so $x \sim_L y$. Therefore, the index of $\sim_L$ is bounded by $|Q|$, which is finite. *(3 marks)*

**Proof (⇐ direction — Finite Index ⇒ Regular):**
Suppose $\sim_L$ has $k$ equivalence classes. Construct the DFA:

$$M_L = (\Sigma^*/{\sim_L},\ \Sigma,\ \delta_L,\ [\varepsilon],\ F_L)$$

where $\delta_L([x], a) = [xa]$ and $F_L = \{[x] \mid x \in L\}$. The transition is well-defined (proved in §3.1). Then $M_L$ accepts exactly $L$ and has $k$ states, so $L$ is regular. *(4 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (b) — Minimal DFA for $L = \{w \mid w \text{ contains } bb\}$:**

**Step 1 — Identify the equivalence classes (state meanings):**

- $C_0 = [\varepsilon]$: no relevant history.
- $C_1 = [a]$: just saw a single $b$ (i.e., last symbol was $b$, and no $bb$ yet).
- $C_2 = [b]$: wait, reconsider — better to track "previous symbol."

The natural partition is:

| Class | Representative | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $q_0$ | $\varepsilon$ | Start state (no $b$ in current run) |
| $q_1$ | $b$ | Last symbol was $b$, no $bb$ yet |
| $q_2$ | $bb$ | $bb$ has been seen (accept) |

**Step 2 — Build the minimal DFA:**

| State | on $a$ | on $b$ | Accept? |
|---|---|---|---|
| $q_0$ | $q_0$ | $q_1$ | No |
| $q_1$ | $q_0$ | $q_2$ | No |
| $q_2$ | $q_2$ | $q_2$ | Yes |

**Step 3 — Verify number of classes:**

$$
\text{index}(\sim_L) = 3
$$

Since $3 < \infty$, $L$ is regular — consistent with Myhill–Nerode. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Algorithmic / Minimization Focus*

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **table-filling algorithm** for DFA minimization. List all its steps in order. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Minimize the following DFA using the table-filling algorithm.

| State | on $a$ | on $b$ | Accept? |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | B | F | No |
| B | A | C | No |
| C | A | D | No |
| D | A | E | No |
| E | A | F | No |
| F | A | F | Yes |

Show all iterations clearly. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (a) — Table-Filling Algorithm:**

The **table-filling algorithm** (Moore, 1956) systematically marks pairs of DFA states as *distinguishable*. The unmarked pairs at termination are equivalent.

**Steps:**

1. Construct a triangular table of all $\binom{n}{2}$ unordered state pairs.
2. **Initialization**: Mark every pair $(p, q)$ where exactly one of $p, q$ is an accepting state.
3. **Iteration**: For every currently **unmarked** pair $(p, q)$ and every symbol $a \in \Sigma$:
   - If $(\delta(p,a), \delta(q,a))$ is already marked, then mark $(p, q)$ (the witness suffix is $a$ followed by the suffix that distinguishes the targets).
4. **Repeat Step 3** until a complete pass produces no new marks — the **fixed point** is reached.
5. **Merge**: All unmarked pairs belong to the same equivalence class. Merge them into single states in the minimal DFA.
6. **Rebuild transitions** on the quotient set.

*Time Complexity*: $O(n^2 \cdot |\Sigma|)$ per iteration; $O(n)$ iterations in the worst case ⇒ total $O(n^3 \cdot |\Sigma|)$. *Space*: $O(n^2)$ for the table. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (b) — Worked Minimization:**

**Step 1 — List all pairs:** $(A,B), (A,C), (A,D), (A,E), (A,F), (B,C), (B,D), (B,E), (B,F), (C,D), (C,E), (C,F), (D,E), (D,F), (E,F)$. Total = 15.

**Step 2 — Initialization (mark accept vs. non-accept):**
Accept = $\{F\}$, so mark: $(A,F), (B,F), (C,F), (D,F), (E,F)$.

**Step 3 — Iteration 1:**

Check unmarked pairs:

- $(A,B)$: on $a$ → $(B,A)$ unmarked; on $b$ → $(F,F)$ same. No mark.
- $(A,C)$: on $a$ → $(B,A)$ unmarked; on $b$ → $(F,D)$ unmarked. No mark.
- $(A,D)$: on $a$ → $(B,A)$; on $b$ → $(F,E)$ unmarked. No mark.
- $(A,E)$: on $a$ → $(B,A)$; on $b$ → $(F,F)$ same. No mark.
- $(B,C)$: on $a$ → $(A,A)$ same; on $b$ → $(C,D)$ unmarked. No mark.
- $(B,D)$: on $a$ → $(A,A)$ same; on $b$ → $(C,E)$ unmarked. No mark.
- $(B,E)$: on $a$ → $(A,A)$ same; on $b$ → $(C,F)$ **MARKED**. ⇒ Mark $(B,E)$.
- $(C,D)$: on $a$ → $(A,A)$ same; on $b$ → $(D,E)$ unmarked. No mark.
- $(C,E)$: on $a$ → $(A,A)$ same; on $b$ → $(D,F)$ **MARKED**. ⇒ Mark $(C,E)$.
- $(D,E)$: on $a$ → $(A,A)$ same; on $b$ → $(E,F)$ **MARKED**. ⇒ Mark $(D,E)$.

After Iter 1, newly marked: $(B,E), (C,E), (D,E)$.

**Step 4 — Iteration 2 (recheck unmarked pairs):**

- $(A,B)$: on $a$ → $(B,A)$; on $b$ → $(F,F)$. No mark.
- $(A,C)$: on $a$ → $(B,A)$; on $b$ → $(F,D)$. No mark.
- $(A,D)$: on $a$ → $(B,A)$; on $b$ → $(F,E)$. No mark.
- $(A,E)$: on $a$ → $(B,A)$; on $b$ → $(F,F)$. No mark.
- $(B,C)$: on $a$ → $(A,A)$; on $b$ → $(C,D)$. No mark.
- $(B,D)$: on $a$ → $(A,A)$; on $b$ → $(C,E)$ **MARKED**. ⇒ Mark $(B,D)$.
- $(C,D)$: on $a$ → $(A,A)$; on $b$ → $(D,E)$ **MARKED**. ⇒ Mark $(C,D)$.

After Iter 2, newly marked: $(B,D), (C,D)$.

**Step 5 — Iteration 3:**

- $(A,B)$: on $a$ → $(B,A)$; on $b$ → $(F,F)$. No mark.
- $(A,C)$: on $a$ → $(B,A)$; on $b$ → $(F,D)$. No mark.
- $(A,D)$: on $a$ → $(B,A)$; on $b$ → $(F,E)$. No mark.
- $(A,E)$: on $a$ → $(B,A)$; on $b$ → $(F,F)$. No mark.
- $(B,C)$: on $a$ → $(A,A)$; on $b$ → $(C,D)$ **MARKED**. ⇒ Mark $(B,C)$.

**Step 6 — Iteration 4:**

- $(A,B)$: on $a$ → $(B,A)$; on $b$ → $(F,F)$. No mark.
- $(A,C)$: on $a$ → $(B,A)$; on $b$ → $(F,D)$. No mark.
- $(A,D)$: on $a$ → $(B,A)$; on $b$ → $(F,E)$. No mark.
- $(A,E)$: on $a$ → $(B,A)$; on $b$ → $(F,F)$. No mark.

No new marks — **fixed point reached.**

**Step 7 — Identify equivalence classes:**

Unmarked pairs: $(A,B), (A,C), (A,D), (A,E)$ — but only $(A,B), (A,C), (A,D), (A,E)$ are mutually unconnected via unmarked pairs. Are they all equivalent?

Check: $(A,B)$ unmarked, $(A,C)$ unmarked, $(A,D)$ unmarked, $(A,E)$ unmarked. But are $B, C, D, E$ mutually unmarked? No — all of them are now marked against each other. So $A$ is alone in its own class.

Final classes: $\{A\}, \{B\}, \{C\}, \{D\}, \{E\}, \{F\}$.

**Conclusion:** The given DFA is **already minimal** — no states can be merged.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1**: Students often forget to perform **multiple iterations**. A pair may look unmarked in iteration 1, but once its target pair is marked in iteration 2, the original pair *also* becomes marked in iteration 3. Always re-scan after every new marking.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2**: Forgetting that the transition $(F, F)$ on symbol $b$ is a self-loop — this means $F$ cannot be distinguished from $F$, so any pair whose only distinguishing path lands in $F$ for both members remains unmarked.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3**: Confusing "unmarked pair" with "equivalent states." Equivalence is a **transitive closure** on the unmarked-pair graph. A, B, C, D, E are all pairwise-unmarked, but they are not transitively equivalent because the unmarked-pair graph $\{A,B,C,D,E\}$ is not a single connected component when considering only the unmarked relations among non-$A$ nodes. *Recheck transitivity carefully.*
> 4. **Common Mistake 4**: Omitting the "fixed-point" verification. Without proving that no further marks are possible, the answer is incomplete.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Forming Equivalence Classes**

- ✅ **Equivalence relation** = reflexive + symmetric + transitive. All three are mandatory.
- ✅ **Right congruence** = equivalence relation preserved under right-multiplication by any string $w \in \Sigma^*$.
- ✅ **Nerode equivalence** $x \sim_L y$ is defined as: for **all** suffixes $z$, $xz \in L \iff yz \in L$.
- ✅ **Myhill–Nerode Theorem**: $L$ is regular **iff** $\sim_L$ has **finitely many** equivalence classes.
- ✅ **Number of equivalence classes = minimum number of DFA states** accepting $L$.
- ✅ **Table-filling algorithm** = $O(n^2 \cdot |\Sigma|)$ per iteration, fixed-point termination.
- ✅ **Initialization** step: always mark every (accept, non-accept) pair first.
- ✅ **Witness suffix** $z$ is the proof object that two strings are *distinguishable*.
- ✅ **Quotient DFA** $M_L = (\Sigma^*/{\sim_L}, \Sigma, \delta_L, [\varepsilon], F_L)$ is canonical and unique up to isomorphism.
- ✅ **Uniqueness**: The minimal DFA for any regular language is **unique** (up to state renaming) — a direct consequence of equivalence-class construction.
- ✅ **Engineering use**: Powers lexers, regex engines, model checkers, network protocol verifiers, and NLP tokenizers.
- ✅ **Failure mode**: If minimization yields a single giant class, the language is *not* regular (e.g., $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 0\}$ has infinitely many equivalence classes).
- ✅ **Trap to avoid**: Distinguishability is a *relation* on state pairs; equivalence is the *complement* after fixed-point — never confuse "not yet marked" with "equivalent" mid-iteration.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reliability vs Discrimination
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Reliability vs Discrimination

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Computational Linguistics and NLP evaluation, **Reliability** and **Discrimination** are two fundamental psychometric properties of any evaluation metric, test, or annotation scheme.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Reliability** is defined as the consistency, stability, and reproducibility of measurement results across repeated trials, different raters, or equivalent test forms. A reliable metric yields the **same (or nearly the same) result** when applied under similar conditions. In NLP, this is most often operationalized as **Inter-Annotator Agreement (IAA)** — the degree to which two or more human (or automatic) annotators produce identical judgments on the same linguistic data.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Discrimination** is defined as the sensitivity of a metric to detect meaningful differences in the quality of the objects being evaluated. A discriminating metric can **clearly separate high-performing systems/translations/summaries from low-performing ones**. In statistical terms, it corresponds to the **discriminatory power** or **effect size** of the evaluation instrument.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a weighing scale (the **metric**) used to judge contestants (the **systems/outputs**) in a baking competition:

- **Reliability** = If you put the same cake on the scale twice, do you get the same weight reading? If two different judges use two identical scales, do they agree on the weights? *The scale must be consistent and trustworthy.*

- **Discrimination** = Does the scale actually distinguish a light sponge cake from a heavy fruit cake? Can it rank the contestants meaningfully? *A scale that always reads "5 kg" for every cake is reliable (consistent) but useless (cannot discriminate).*

> [!NOTE]  
> **The Crucial Insight:** A metric can be **reliable but NOT discriminating** (e.g., a broken scale that always reads 5 kg). Conversely, a metric can have high discrimination but low reliability (e.g., a faulty scale with random readings that happen to differ between cakes). **The ideal metric must possess BOTH properties simultaneously.**

### 1.3 Why This Matters in Computational Linguistics

| Application Area | Reliability Concern | Discrimination Concern |
|------------------|--------------------|------------------------|
| **Machine Translation (MT)** | Does BLEU give consistent scores across re-runs? | Can BLEU rank MT systems meaningfully? |
| **Text Summarization** | Do ROUGE scores agree with human judgment consistently? | Can ROUGE distinguish good vs. bad summaries? |
| **Sentiment Analysis** | Do annotators agree on polarity labels? | Can a classifier separate positive from negative tweets? |
| **Linguistic Annotation (POS, NER)** | Inter-annotator agreement (Cohen's κ) | Does the tagset separate ambiguous cases? |
| **Speech Recognition** | WER stability across test sets | Can WER detect ASR system improvements? |

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Bold for Emphasis)

- **Cohen's Kappa (κ)** — primary IAA metric for two raters
- **Krippendorff's Alpha (α)** — general IAA metric for multiple raters
- **Cronbach's Alpha (α)** — internal consistency reliability
- **Item Discrimination Index (D)** — measures test item quality
- **Point-Biserial Correlation (r_pb)** — correlates item scores with total scores

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Reliability — The Three Pillars

Reliability in computational linguistics evaluation rests on **three operational pillars**:

#### Pillar A: Inter-Rater / Inter-Annotator Reliability
- Measures agreement between two or more human annotators applying the same labeling scheme to the same data.
- **Why:** Annotation is subjective; without reliability checks, training data is contaminated.
- **How:** Statistical coefficients like Cohen's Kappa correct for chance agreement.

#### Pillar B: Test-Retest Reliability
- Measures stability of a metric's output when the same input is evaluated at different times or with slightly perturbed conditions.
- **Why:** Robust NLP systems should be insensitive to minor noise.
- **How:** Correlation (Pearson's r) between two sets of scores; Intraclass Correlation Coefficient (ICC).

#### Pillar C: Internal Consistency
- Measures whether multiple items/sub-metrics of the same evaluation instrument measure the same underlying construct.
- **Why:** A metric composed of several sub-scores (e.g., BLEU's n-gram precisions) should be coherent.
- **How:** **Cronbach's Alpha (α)** — values above **0.70** are conventionally deemed acceptable.

### 2.2 Discrimination — The Sensitivity Dimension

#### Dimension A: Item Discrimination
- For a single test item (e.g., a translation example or a linguistic query), does it separate high-ability from low-ability performers?
- Computed as: **D = (U − L) / n**, where U = number of top-scoring group answering correctly, L = number of bottom-scoring group answering correctly, n = group size.
- **Interpretation:** D > **0.30** is considered good; D < **0.20** suggests the item is weak and should be revised.

#### Dimension B: Discriminant Validity
- Does the metric correlate highly with metrics measuring the **same construct** (convergent validity) and low with metrics measuring **different constructs** (discriminant validity)?
- **Why:** BLEU should correlate with human judgments (convergent) but not with metrics of fluency alone (discriminant).

#### Dimension C: Statistical Power & Effect Size
- The metric must be sensitive enough to detect statistically significant differences between competing systems.
- **Effect size** measures (Cohen's d, Glass's Δ) quantify the magnitude of difference independent of sample size.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Property | Formula | Variables | Range | Interpretation |
|---|----------|---------|-----------|-------|----------------|
| 1 | **Percent Agreement** | $P_o = \frac{\text{agreements}}{N}$ | $N$ = total items | [0, 1] | Naive; ignores chance |
| 2 | **Cohen's Kappa** | $\kappa = \frac{P_o - P_e}{1 - P_e}$ | $P_o$ = observed agreement, $P_e$ = expected chance agreement | [−1, 1] | > 0.80 = excellent |
| 3 | **Scott's π** | $\pi = \frac{P_o - P_e}{1 - P_e}$ | Same as κ, but $P_e$ assumes homogeneous marginals | [−1, 1] | Stricter than κ |
| 4 | **Krippendorff's α** | $\alpha = 1 - \frac{D_o}{D_e}$ | $D_o$ = observed disagreement, $D_e$ = expected disagreement | [0, 1] | Multi-rater, multi-level data |
| 5 | **Cronbach's α** | $\alpha = \frac{k}{k-1}\left(1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k}\sigma^2_{Y_i}}{\sigma^2_X}\right)$ | $k$ = items, $\sigma^2_{Y_i}$ = item variance, $\sigma^2_X$ = total variance | [0, 1] | > 0.70 = reliable |
| 6 | **Intraclass Correlation (ICC)** | $ICC = \frac{\sigma^2_{\text{between}}}{\sigma^2_{\text{between}} + \sigma^2_{\text{within}}}$ | Variance components | [0, 1] | Two-way mixed/random models |
| 7 | **Item Discrimination (D)** | $D = \frac{U - L}{n}$ | $U$ = top group correct, $L$ = bottom group correct, $n$ = group size | [−1, 1] | > 0.30 = good item |
| 8 | **Point-Biserial Correlation** | $r_{pb} = \frac{\overline{X}_1 - \overline{X}_0}{\sigma_t} \cdot \sqrt{pq}$ | $\overline{X}_1$ = mean of passers, $\overline{X}_0$ = mean of failers, $p, q$ = proportions | [−1, 1] | > 0.20 acceptable |
| 9 | **Cohen's d (Effect Size)** | $d = \frac{\mu_1 - \mu_2}{\sigma_{\text{pooled}}}$ | $\mu$ = group means, $\sigma$ = pooled std. dev. | [0, ∞) | 0.2 small, 0.5 medium, 0.8 large |
| 10 | **Pearson r (Test-Retest)** | $r = \frac{\sum(x_i - \overline{x})(y_i - \overline{y})}{\sqrt{\sum(x_i - \overline{x})^2 \sum(y_i - \overline{y})^2}}$ | $x, y$ = two sets of scores | [−1, 1] | > 0.80 = stable |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Dataset Curation:** Before releasing an annotated corpus, CL engineers compute κ to validate annotation guidelines.
- **A/B Testing of NLP Models:** Discrimination indices determine whether observed performance gaps are real or noise.
- **Regulatory Compliance (EU AI Act, ISO/IEC 23894):** Demands auditable, reliable, and non-discriminatory evaluation protocols.
- **Automatic Metric Design:** New metrics like **BLEURT** or **COMET** are validated against reliability AND discrimination benchmarks before adoption.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Derivation 1: Cohen's Kappa from First Principles

**Problem Context:** Two linguists annotate 100 sentences for active/passive voice. They agree on 80, disagree on 20. Compute Cohen's κ and interpret.

**Step 1 — Build the Confusion Matrix (Observed Frequencies).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{Annotator A: Active} \rightarrow \text{Active: 60, Passive: 20} \\
& \text{Annotator A: Passive} \rightarrow \text{Active: 0, Passive: 20}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{Confusion Matrix } N = \begin{bmatrix} 60 & 20 \\ 0 & 20 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Observed Agreement $P_o$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_o &= \frac{\text{diagonal sum}}{\text{total}} = \frac{60 + 20}{100} = \frac{80}{100} = 0.80
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the raw percent agreement.

**Step 3 — Compute Marginal Totals (Row and Column Sums).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{Row A: Active} = 60 + 20 = 80, \quad \text{Row A: Passive} = 0 + 20 = 20 \\
& \text{Col B: Active} = 60 + 0 = 60, \quad \text{Col B: Passive} = 20 + 20 = 40
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Expected Agreement $P_e$ Under Independence.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_e(\text{Active}) &= \frac{80 \times 60}{100 \times 100} = \frac{4800}{10000} = 0.48 \\
P_e(\text{Passive}) &= \frac{20 \times 40}{100 \times 100} = \frac{800}{10000} = 0.08 \\
P_e &= 0.48 + 0.08 = 0.56
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Cohen's Kappa Formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\kappa &= \frac{P_o - P_e}{1 - P_e} = \frac{0.80 - 0.56}{1 - 0.56} = \frac{0.24}{0.44} \\
\kappa &= 0.5454 \approx 0.55
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation Using Landis & Koch (1977) Scale.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \kappa < 0.00 \rightarrow \text{Poor} \\
& 0.00 \leq \kappa \leq 0.20 \rightarrow \text{Slight} \\
& 0.21 \leq \kappa \leq 0.40 \rightarrow \text{Fair} \\
& 0.41 \leq \kappa \leq 0.60 \rightarrow \text{Moderate} \\
& 0.61 \leq \kappa \leq 0.80 \rightarrow \text{Substantial} \\
& 0.81 \leq \kappa \leq 1.00 \rightarrow \text{Almost Perfect}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** $\kappa = 0.55$ → **Moderate agreement**. The annotation guidelines need refinement before the corpus can be used for training.

---

### 3.2 Worked Derivation 2: Item Discrimination Index

**Problem Context:** A 50-question language proficiency test is given to 100 students. The top 27 (upper group) and bottom 27 (lower group) are analyzed. On Question 15, 20 from the upper group and 8 from the lower group answered correctly. Compute the discrimination index.

**Step 1 — Identify Variables.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& U = 20 \quad (\text{upper group correct}) \\
& L = 8 \quad (\text{lower group correct}) \\
& n = 27 \quad (\text{group size, typically } 27\% \text{ of total})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \frac{U - L}{n} = \frac{20 - 8}{27} = \frac{12}{27} \\
D &= 0.4444 \approx 0.44
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation (Ebel's Guidelines).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& D \geq 0.40 \rightarrow \text{Excellent item} \\
& 0.30 \leq D < 0.40 \rightarrow \text{Good item} \\
& 0.20 \leq D < 0.30 \rightarrow \text{Marginal item (revise)} \\
& D < 0.20 \rightarrow \text{Poor item (discard)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** $D = 0.44$ → **Excellent item**. Question 15 effectively discriminates between high and low proficiency students.

---

### 3.3 Worked Derivation 3: Cronbach's Alpha for an NLP Evaluation Suite

**Problem Context:** A new dialogue quality metric is composed of $k = 4$ sub-scores (Coherence, Relevance, Fluency, Adequacy). Across $N = 50$ dialogues, the following data is collected:

| Sub-score | Variance $\sigma^2_{Y_i}$ |
|-----------|--------------------------|
| Coherence | 0.45 |
| Relevance | 0.50 |
| Fluency | 0.40 |
| Adequacy | 0.55 |

Total variance of composite score: $\sigma^2_X = 2.50$.

**Step 1 — Sum the Item Variances.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{k} \sigma^2_{Y_i} &= 0.45 + 0.50 + 0.40 + 0.55 = 1.90
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into Cronbach's Formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha &= \frac{k}{k-1}\left(1 - \frac{\sum \sigma^2_{Y_i}}{\sigma^2_X}\right) \\
&= \frac{4}{4-1}\left(1 - \frac{1.90}{2.50}\right) \\
&= \frac{4}{3}\left(1 - 0.76\right) \\
&= \frac{4}{3} \times 0.24 \\
&= 0.32
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \alpha \geq 0.90 \rightarrow \text{Excellent internal consistency} \\
& 0.80 \leq \alpha < 0.90 \rightarrow \text{Good} \\
& 0.70 \leq \alpha < 0.80 \rightarrow \text{Acceptable} \\
& 0.60 \leq \alpha < 0.70 \rightarrow \text{Questionable} \\
& \alpha < 0.60 \rightarrow \text{Poor}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** $\alpha = 0.32$ → **Poor internal consistency**. The four sub-scores do not measure a unified construct; the metric needs redesign.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Reliability vs. Discrimination

| Dimension | Reliability | Discrimination |
|-----------|-------------|----------------|
| **Core Question** | Are measurements consistent? | Are measurements differentiating? |
| **Statistical Object** | Variance under replication | Separation between groups/systems |
| **Key Metrics** | κ, α (Cronbach), ICC, Pearson r | D, r_pb, Cohen's d, AUC |
| **Failure Mode** | Random error, rater drift | Insufficient granularity, ceiling/floor effects |
| **NLP Example (Bad Case)** | BLEU always returns 0.45 regardless of system | BLEU returns 0.45 for both excellent and terrible translations |
| **NLP Example (Good Case)** | BLEU on identical re-runs gives 0.451, 0.450, 0.451 | BLEU separates Google (0.65) from rule-based (0.30) on WMT data |
| **Diagnostic Use** | Validates data quality, annotation protocols | Validates metric usefulness, system rankings |
| **Sensitivity to Sample** | Low (variance-based) | High (effect size-based) |
| **Threshold Convention** | κ > 0.67, α > 0.70 | D > 0.30, d > 0.50 |
| **Computation Cost** | Requires multiple raters/runs | Requires top-bottom split or known groups |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Conceptual Architecture of Reliability and Discrimination in NLP Evaluation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[NLP Evaluation Metric Design] --> B{Quality Assessment}
    
    B --> C[Reliability Dimension]
    B --> D[Discrimination Dimension]
    
    C --> C1[Inter-Rater Reliability]
    C --> C2[Test-Retest Reliability]
    C --> C3[Internal Consistency]
    
    C1 --> C1a[Cohens Kappa]
    C1 --> C1b[Krippendorff Alpha]
    C1 --> C1c[Scott Pi]
    
    C2 --> C2a[Pearson r]
    C2 --> C2b[ICC]
    
    C3 --> C3a[Cronbach Alpha]
    C3 --> C3b[Split-Half Method]
    
    D --> D1[Item Discrimination]
    D --> D2[Discriminant Validity]
    D --> D3[Statistical Power]
    
    D1 --> D1a[D Index]
    D1 --> D1b[Point-Biserial r]
    
    D2 --> D2a[Convergent Validity]
    D2 --> D2b[Divergent Validity]
    
    D3 --> D3a[Cohens d]
    D3 --> D3b[Effect Size]
    
    C1a --> E{Decision Matrix}
    C3a --> E
    D1a --> E
    D3a --> E
    
    E -->|High Reliability AND High Discrimination| F[IDEAL METRIC]
    E -->|High Reliability AND Low Discrimination| G[USELESS METRIC Broken Scale]
    E -->|Low Reliability AND High Discrimination| H[UNSTABLE METRIC Random Scale]
    E -->|Low Reliability AND Low Discrimination| I[INVALID METRIC Reject]
    
    F --> J[Deploy in Production]
    G --> K[Increase Granularity]
    H --> L[Standardize Protocol]
    I --> M[Redesign Metric]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: The Reliability–Discrimination Trade-off Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1: Define Construct] --> P2[Step 2: Design Items]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Pilot Annotation]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Compute Cohen Kappa]
    P4 --> P5{Kappa >= 0.67?}
    
    P5 -->|No| P6[Refine Annotation Guidelines]
    P6 --> P3
    
    P5 -->|Yes| P7[Step 5: Compute Item Discrimination]
    P7 --> P8{Average D >= 0.30?}
    
    P8 -->|No| P9[Discard Weak Items]
    P9 --> P2
    
    P8 -->|Yes| P10[Step 6: Compute Cronbach Alpha]
    P10 --> P11{Alpha >= 0.70?}
    
    P11 -->|No| P12[Increase Item Pool]
    P12 --> P2
    
    P11 -->|Yes| P13[Step 7: Final Validation]
    P13 --> P14[Step 8: Deploy Metric]
    
    P14 --> P15[Continuous Monitoring]
    P15 --> P4
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Failure Mode Taxonomy

```mermaid
graph TD
    FAIL[Evaluation Metric Failure] --> FAIL1[Reliability Failure]
    FAIL --> FAIL2[Discrimination Failure]
    
    FAIL1 --> F1a[Chance-Level Agreement]
    FAIL1 --> F1b[Rater Drift Over Time]
    FAIL1 --> F1c[Inconsistent Annotation Guidelines]
    FAIL1 --> F1d[Low Cronbach Alpha]
    
    FAIL2 --> F2a[Ceiling Effect]
    FAIL2 --> F2b[Floor Effect]
    FAIL2 --> F2c[Compressed Score Range]
    FAIL2 --> F2d[Non-Significant System Differences]
    
    F1a --> SOL1[Compute Kappa]
    F1b --> SOL1
    F1c --> SOL1
    F1d --> SOL1
    
    F2a --> SOL2[Increase Item Difficulty]
    F2b --> SOL2
    F2c --> SOL2
    F2d --> SOL2
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Note on Mermaid Compilation:** All node identifiers follow the alphanumeric prefix rule (e.g., `F1a`, `SOL2`). No reserved keywords (such as `end`, `graph`, `subgraph`) are used as standalone node IDs. Labels contain raw uppercase alphanumeric text only, with no markdown formatting symbols inside the double-quoted strings.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between reliability and discrimination in the context of NLP evaluation metrics. Give one example of a metric for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Reliability | Discrimination |
|--------|-------------|----------------|
| Definition | Consistency of measurement across trials, raters, or items | Sensitivity of metric to distinguish quality differences |
| Example Metric | **Cohen's Kappa (κ)** for inter-annotator agreement | **Item Discrimination Index (D)** for test item quality |
| NLP Context | Measures if two annotators label the same POS tags consistently | Measures if BLEU can rank MT systems from worst to best |
| Failure | Random noise, rater drift | Ceiling effect, compressed scale |

*(1 mark for clear definition of each + 1 mark for correct example of each + 1 mark for valid differentiation in NLP context.)*

---

#### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain the Landis and Koch interpretation scale for Cohen's Kappa. What is the minimum acceptable κ value for a "substantial" agreement in linguistic annotation tasks?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Landis and Koch (1977) scale interprets Cohen's Kappa as follows:

- $\kappa < 0.00$ → Poor
- $0.00 \leq \kappa \leq 0.20$ → Slight
- $0.21 \leq \kappa \leq 0.40$ → Fair
- $0.41 \leq \kappa \leq 0.60$ → Moderate
- $0.61 \leq \kappa \leq 0.80$ → **Substantial** *(1 mark)*
- $0.81 \leq \kappa \leq 1.00$ → Almost Perfect

The **minimum acceptable value for substantial agreement is κ = 0.61** *(1 mark)*. For linguistic annotation tasks (POS tagging, NER, sentiment labels), a κ ≥ 0.67 is conventionally required to ensure dataset usability for training supervised NLP models *(1 mark)*.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Compute Cohen's Kappa for the following annotation task. Two linguists annotated 200 Malayalam sentences for "Subject" vs. "Object" noun phrases. The confusion matrix is:

|  | **Linguist B: Subj** | **Linguist B: Obj** | Row Total |
|--|---------------------|---------------------|-----------|
| **Linguist A: Subj** | 90 | 10 | 100 |
| **Linguist A: Obj** | 20 | 80 | 100 |
| **Col Total** | 110 | 90 | 200 |

Interpret the result using the Landis-Koch scale. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Define **Item Discrimination Index**. For a 60-question test administered to 200 students, the top 60 and bottom 60 scorers are identified. On Q12, 42 from the top group and 18 from the bottom group answered correctly. Compute D and interpret. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solutions:**

**(a) Solution:**
- $P_o = (90 + 80)/200 = 170/200 = 0.85$ *(2 marks)*
- $P_e = (100 \times 110 + 100 \times 90)/(200 \times 200) = (11000 + 9000)/40000 = 20000/40000 = 0.50$ *(2 marks)*
- $\kappa = (0.85 - 0.50)/(1 - 0.50) = 0.35/0.50 = 0.70$ *(2 marks)*
- Interpretation: $\kappa = 0.70$ → **Substantial agreement**, suitable for downstream supervised learning. *(1 mark)*

**(b) Solution:**
- $D = (U - L)/n = (42 - 18)/60 = 24/60 = 0.40$ *(3 marks)*
- Since $D = 0.40 \geq 0.40$, the item is **excellent** (Ebel's classification). *(2 marks)*
- Item Q12 effectively discriminates between high and low ability students. *(1 mark)*
- Definition: *The Item Discrimination Index is a psychometric measure that quantifies how well a single test item differentiates between high-performing and low-performing examinees, computed as the difference between the proportion of correct responses in the upper and lower ability groups.* *(1 mark)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Define **Cronbach's Alpha**. A dialogue evaluation metric has $k = 5$ sub-scores with item variances 0.30, 0.35, 0.40, 0.45, 0.50. The total composite score variance is $\sigma^2_X = 2.50$. Compute Cronbach's α and comment on the metric's internal consistency. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the relationship between **reliability and discrimination** in NLP evaluation. Why can a metric be reliable but not discriminating? Provide a concrete example. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solutions:**

**(a) Solution:**
- Definition: *Cronbach's Alpha is a coefficient of internal consistency that estimates the extent to which all sub-items of a composite test measure the same underlying construct.* *(1 mark)*
- $\sum \sigma^2_{Y_i} = 0.30 + 0.35 + 0.40 + 0.45 + 0.50 = 2.00$ *(2 marks)*
- $\alpha = (k/(k-1)) \times (1 - \sum \sigma^2_{Y_i}/\sigma^2_X) = (5/4) \times (1 - 2.00/2.50)$ *(1 mark)*
- $= 1.25 \times (1 - 0.80) = 1.25 \times 0.20 = 0.25$ *(1 mark)*
- Interpretation: $\alpha = 0.25$ → **Poor internal consistency**. The 5 sub-scores are not measuring a unified construct; the metric design must be revised. *(1 mark)*
- Recommendation: Either increase inter-item correlation by refining sub-score definitions or reduce the number of weakly correlated sub-scores. *(1 mark)*

**(b) Solution:**
- Reliability and discrimination are **complementary but distinct** psychometric properties. *(1 mark)*
- A metric is reliable when repeated measurements yield consistent results. *(1 mark)*
- A metric is discriminating when it can rank or separate objects of different quality. *(1 mark)*
- A metric can be reliable but not discriminating if it consistently returns the same (or nearly the same) value for all inputs — there is no measurement error, but there is also no informational variation. *(2 marks)*
- **Example:** A broken translation evaluation tool that always outputs BLEU = 0.30 regardless of system quality. The standard deviation across runs is 0 (perfect reliability), but the metric cannot distinguish Google Translate (excellent) from a rule-based baseline (poor). *(2 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**  
> - Students often confuse **reliability** with **accuracy** (validity). Reliability is about **consistency**, not correctness. A metric can be reliably wrong.  
> - In κ computations, do **not** use raw percent agreement alone — that ignores chance. Always subtract the chance-expected agreement $P_e$.  
> - For Cronbach's α, students frequently forget the factor $k/(k-1)$ and submit only $(1 - \sum \sigma^2_i / \sigma^2_X)$, losing 1 mark.  
> - In discrimination index questions, the group size is typically 27% of total students (for a 100-student test, n = 27, not n = 50). Read the question carefully.  
> - On ESE Day, KTU expects students to **cite the Landis-Koch scale explicitly** in any κ interpretation — stating "κ is good" without scale anchoring will attract partial marking.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]  
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Reliability vs. Discrimination**

- **Reliability** = **Consistency** (are repeated measurements the same?)
- **Discrimination** = **Sensitivity** (can the metric separate good from bad?)
- A reliable metric **need not be discriminating**; a discriminating metric **need not be reliable**. Both are required for a usable NLP evaluation instrument.
- **Inter-Annotator Agreement** is the most common operationalization of reliability in CL annotation pipelines.
- **Cohen's Kappa (κ)** corrects percent agreement for chance; $\kappa = (P_o - P_e)/(1 - P_e)$.
- **Landis-Koch benchmark:** κ ≥ 0.61 = Substantial; κ ≥ 0.81 = Almost Perfect; κ < 0.40 = inadequate for dataset release.
- **Krippendorff's α** generalizes κ to **multiple raters, multiple categories, and missing data** — preferred for complex linguistic annotation.
- **Cronbach's α** measures internal consistency of a multi-item metric; threshold α ≥ 0.70.
- **Item Discrimination Index D** = $(U - L)/n$; D ≥ 0.40 = excellent item; D < 0.20 = discard.
- **Point-Biserial Correlation ($r_{pb}$)** correlates each item with the total score; $r_{pb} \geq 0.20$ = acceptable.
- **Effect size (Cohen's d)** quantifies discrimination: 0.2 = small, 0.5 = medium, 0.8 = large.
- **Validity ≠ Reliability**: A metric can be reliably biased (consistent in measuring the wrong thing) — this is a validity failure, not a reliability failure.
- **Practical NLP tip:** Always report **both** reliability and discrimination statistics when proposing a new metric. BLEU's well-known criticism is that it has low discrimination at the system level despite being reliable.
- **Computational cost:** Reliability checks need ≥ 2 raters; discrimination checks need a top-bottom split or known reference group.
- **Module 2 linking question:** Reliability and discrimination directly extend the **corpus annotation methodology** covered earlier in this module — both are post-annotation quality control steps.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: n-gram Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** n-gram Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **n-gram model** is a foundational probabilistic language model in computational linguistics that estimates the likelihood of a sequence of $n$ linguistic units (words, characters, subwords, or tokens) appearing together in a given text. Formally, it is a type of **probabilistic graphical model** (specifically a Markov chain of order $n-1$) that approximates the joint probability distribution of a word sequence by factorizing it into a product of conditional probabilities, where each word is conditioned only on the preceding $n-1$ words.

Mathematically, given a vocabulary $V$ and a word sequence $W = w_1, w_2, w_3, \ldots, w_N$, the n-gram model approximates:

$$P(w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_N) \approx \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(w_i \mid w_{i-n+1}^{i-1})$$

where $w_{i-n+1}^{i-1}$ denotes the preceding context window of length $n-1$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** n-gram models are classified by the value of $n$:
> - $n = 1$ → **Unigram** (no context)
> - $n = 2$ → **Bigram** (one previous word)
> - $n = 3$ → **Trigram** (two previous words)
> - $n \geq 4$ → **Higher-order n-grams** (used in large-vocabulary tasks)

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are a fortune teller trying to predict the **next word** a person is about to type on a smartphone keyboard. You don't have a crystal ball, but you have observed millions of past sentences. You notice patterns:

- After the word "**eat**", the word "**food**" is more likely than "**algebra**".
- After "**New**", the next word is very often "**York**".
- After "**ice**", "**cream**" is highly probable.

An **n-gram model** is precisely this "pattern memory" — but instead of intuition, it uses **frequency counts** from a training corpus to compute probabilities. A *unigram* model is like a person who predicts each word in isolation (e.g., "the" is most common). A *bigram* model is like someone who looks at the **last one word** before guessing the next. A *trigram* model peeks at the **last two words**. The higher the $n$, the more context the model "remembers", and consequently the more accurate — but also the more data-hungry it becomes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** n-gram models are the **probabilistic backbone** of virtually every modern NLP system. Before deep learning took over, n-grams *were* language modeling. Even today, components like **spell checkers, autocomplete, speech recognizers, and statistical machine translation systems** rely on n-gram logic. Understanding n-grams is a mandatory prerequisite for advanced topics like **word embeddings, RNNs, and Transformers**.

### 1.3 The Markov Assumption

The entire theoretical foundation of n-gram modeling rests on the **Markov Assumption** — a simplification proposed by Andrey Markov. It states that the probability of the next word depends *only* on a finite, fixed-size window of preceding words, not on the entire history.

For a bigram ($n=2$):

$$P(w_i \mid w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_{i-1}) \approx P(w_i \mid w_{i-1})$$

For a trigram ($n=3$):

$$P(w_i \mid w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_{i-1}) \approx P(w_i \mid w_{i-2}, w_{i-1})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Markov Assumption matters:** Without it, computing $P(W)$ would require enumerating **all possible histories**, which is computationally intractable and data-impossible. The Markov assumption reduces an exponential problem to a manageable conditional probability lookup.

### 1.4 The Chain Rule of Probability

The **chain rule** (or product rule) of probability provides the *exact* (un-approximated) way to factorize a joint probability of a sequence:

$$P(w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_N) = P(w_1) \cdot P(w_2 \mid w_1) \cdot P(w_3 \mid w_1, w_2) \cdots P(w_N \mid w_1, \ldots, w_{N-1})$$

$$P(w_1^N) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})$$

The n-gram model is obtained by **truncating** the conditioning context in the chain rule to the most recent $n-1$ words — this is precisely the **Markov assumption** applied to the chain rule.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of an n-gram Model

Building an n-gram language model follows a structured four-stage pipeline:

- **Step 1 — Corpus Collection & Tokenization:** Gather a large, domain-relevant text corpus $D$. Split the corpus into sentences and tokenize each sentence into individual words (tokens). Add explicit sentence boundary tokens `<s>` and `</s>` so the model can learn sentence-initial and sentence-final probabilities.

- **Step 2 — Vocabulary Construction:** Build a vocabulary $V$ by collecting all unique tokens. Words appearing below a frequency threshold (e.g., $\leq 1$) are typically replaced with a special token `<UNK>` (unknown word) to handle out-of-vocabulary (OOV) words at test time.

- **Step 3 — Counting n-grams:** Slide a window of size $n$ across the tokenized corpus and count the occurrences of every possible $n$-gram and every $(n-1)$-gram context. These counts are stored in a frequency table $C(\cdot)$.

- **Step 4 — Probability Estimation (Maximum Likelihood Estimation — MLE):** Compute the conditional probability of a word given its $(n-1)$-word context as the relative frequency:

$$P_{\text{MLE}}(w_i \mid w_{i-n+1}^{i-1}) = \frac{C(w_{i-n+1}^{i-1}, w_i)}{C(w_{i-n+1}^{i-1})}$$

For a **unigram** ($n=1$):

$$P_{\text{MLE}}(w_i) = \frac{C(w_i)}{N}$$

For a **bigram** ($n=2$):

$$P_{\text{MLE}}(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \frac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i)}{C(w_{i-1})}$$

For a **trigram** ($n=3$):

$$P_{\text{MLE}}(w_i \mid w_{i-2}, w_{i-1}) = \frac{C(w_{i-2}, w_{i-1}, w_i)}{C(w_{i-2}, w_{i-1})}$$

### 2.2 Worked Numerical Example (MLE Bigram Estimation)

Consider the following mini-corpus after padding with `<s>` and `</s>`:

`<s> I am Sam </s>`

`<s> Sam I am </s>`

`<s> I do not like green eggs and ham </s>`

**Bigram counts** (selected, illustrative):

| Bigram $w_{i-1}, w_i$ | Count $C(w_{i-1}, w_i)$ |
|---|---|
| `<s>`, I | 2 |
| `<s>`, Sam | 1 |
| I, am | 2 |
| Sam, `</s>` | 1 |
| am, Sam | 1 |
| I, do | 1 |
| do, not | 1 |
| not, like | 1 |
| am, `</s>` | 1 |

**Unigram context counts** (denominator):

| Context $w_{i-1}$ | Count $C(w_{i-1})$ |
|---|---|
| `<s>` | 3 |
| I | 3 |
| am | 2 |
| Sam | 2 |
| do | 1 |
| not | 1 |
| like | 1 |

**Bigram probability computation:**

$$P(\text{I} \mid \text{<s>}) = \frac{C(\text{<s>}, \text{I})}{C(\text{<s>})} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667$$

$$P(\text{Sam} \mid \text{<s>}) = \frac{C(\text{<s>}, \text{Sam})}{C(\text{<s>})} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$$

$$P(\text{</s>} \mid \text{am}) = \frac{C(\text{am}, \text{</s>})}{C(\text{am})} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.500$$

### 2.3 The Sparsity Problem & Smoothing Techniques

The biggest weakness of vanilla MLE-based n-gram models is the **data sparsity problem**: for any non-trivial corpus, the vast majority of possible n-grams will have a count of **zero**. This causes the model to assign probability zero to many legitimate test sentences, making perplexity infinite and the model useless.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Concept:** *Smoothing* techniques are mandatory additions to any n-gram model. They redistribute a small amount of probability mass from observed n-grams to unseen ones so that **no probability is ever exactly zero**.

#### 2.3.1 Laplace Smoothing (Add-One)

The simplest and most theoretically clean smoothing technique. It adds 1 to every count and normalizes:

$$P^{*}_{\text{Laplace}}(w_i \mid w_{i-n+1}^{i-1}) = \frac{C(w_{i-n+1}^{i-1}, w_i) + 1}{C(w_{i-n+1}^{i-1}) + \vert V \vert}$$

where $\vert V \vert$ is the vocabulary size (for bigrams) or the number of unique $(n-1)$-gram contexts (more generally).

#### 2.3.2 Add-$k$ Smoothing

A generalization of Laplace smoothing where a smaller fraction $k$ (typically $0 < k \leq 1$) is added instead of 1:

$$P^{*}_{\text{Add-}k}(w_i \mid w_{i-n+1}^{i-1}) = \frac{C(w_{i-n+1}^{i-1}, w_i) + k}{C(w_{i-n+1}^{i-1}) + k \cdot \vert V \vert}$$

#### 2.3.3 Good-Turing Smoothing

Reestimates the probability of an n-gram that occurs $r$ times using the count of n-grams that occur exactly $r+1$ times:

$$P^{*}_{\text{GT}}(w \text{ seen } r \text{ times}) = \frac{(r+1) \cdot N_{r+1}}{N \cdot N_r}$$

where $N_r$ is the number of distinct n-grams that occur exactly $r$ times in the corpus, and $N = \sum_r r \cdot N_r$ is the total number of n-grams.

#### 2.3.4 Kneser-Ney Smoothing (Industry Standard)

The most successful smoothing technique for n-gram models. It discounts high counts by a fixed value $D$ and uses a **continuation probability** for novel contexts:

$$P^{*}_{\text{KN}}(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \frac{\max(C(w_{i-1}, w_i) - D, 0)}{C(w_{i-1})} + \lambda(w_{i-1}) \cdot P_{\text{cont}}(w_i)$$

where $\lambda(w_{i-1})$ is a normalization constant and $P_{\text{cont}}(w_i)$ is the continuation probability based on the number of distinct contexts $w_i$ appears in.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Chain Rule (exact) | $P(w_1^N) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})$ | Un-approximated joint probability |
