| 2 | Markov Assumption | $P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1}) \approx P(w_i \mid w_{i-n+1}^{i-1})$ | Truncation to last $n-1$ words |
| 3 | Unigram MLE | $P(w_i) = C(w_i) \mid N$ | $N$ = total word count |
| 4 | Bigram MLE | $P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = C(w_{i-1}, w_i) \mid C(w_{i-1})$ | Core n-gram estimator |
| 5 | Trigram MLE | $P(w_i \mid w_{i-2}, w_{i-1}) = C(w_{i-2}, w_{i-1}, w_i) \mid C(w_{i-2}, w_{i-1})$ | Higher-order extension |
| 6 | Laplace (Add-1) | $P^{*} = (C + 1) \mid (C_{\text{ctx}} + \vert V \vert)$ | Simplest smoothing |
| 7 | Add-$k$ | $P^{*} = (C + k) \mid (C_{\text{ctx}} + k \cdot \vert V \vert)$ | Tunable variant |
| 8 | Good-Turing | $P^{*}(r) = (r+1) N_{r+1} \mid (N \cdot N_r)$ | Frequency-of-frequencies |
| 9 | Shannon Entropy | $H = -\sum_{w \in V} p(w) \log_2 p(w)$ | In bits when base-2 |
| 10 | Perplexity (sentence) | $PP(W) = P(w_1 \ldots w_N)^{-1 \mid N} = \sqrt[N]{1 \mid P(W)}$ | Lower is better |
| 11 | Cross-Entropy | $H(P, Q) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log_2 Q(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})$ | Estimator of perplexity |
| 12 | OOV Handling | Replace rare words with `<UNK>` | Vocabulary threshold $\theta$ |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Speech Recognition:** Acoustic models combined with n-gram language models to rank candidate transcriptions.
- **Statistical Machine Translation:** IBM Models 1–5 used n-gram alignment; classical SMT systems used 5-gram language models.
- **Spelling Correction & Autocomplete:** Mobile keyboards use bigram/trigram probabilities to suggest the next word.
- **Information Retrieval:** Query likelihood model $P(D \mid Q)$ uses unigram and bigram probabilities.
- **Authorship Attribution & Forensic Linguistics:** Stylometric features derived from function-word bigram/trigram distributions.
- **Optical Character Recognition (OCR):** Post-processing n-gram language models disambiguate visually similar characters.
- **Foundations for Neural Models:** Word2Vec, GloVe, and the input embedding layers of Transformers are conceptually descendants of n-gram co-occurrence statistics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Even with the rise of deep learning, n-grams remain *unbeatable* for low-resource languages, small-vocabulary embedded systems, and applications requiring interpretable, fast, on-device language modeling (e.g., voice assistants on microcontrollers).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: From Chain Rule to Bigram Model

The chain rule expresses the joint probability of a sequence *exactly*:

$$P(w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4) = P(w_1) \cdot P(w_2 \mid w_1) \cdot P(w_3 \mid w_1, w_2) \cdot P(w_4 \mid w_1, w_2, w_3)$$

This is an exact identity derived from the definition of conditional probability, $P(A \mid B) = P(A, B) \mid P(B)$. It is mathematically perfect, but practically unusable because the conditionals grow increasingly long and the required counts explode.

**Step 1 — Apply the Bigram (first-order Markov) Assumption:**

$$P(w_3 \mid w_1, w_2) \approx P(w_3 \mid w_2)$$

$$P(w_4 \mid w_1, w_2, w_3) \approx P(w_4 \mid w_3)$$

This is the **Markov assumption**: the future depends only on the immediate past, not the full history.

**Step 2 — Substitute into the chain rule:**

$$P(w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4) \approx P(w_1) \cdot P(w_2 \mid w_1) \cdot P(w_3 \mid w_2) \cdot P(w_4 \mid w_3)$$

**Step 3 — Generalize to sequence of length $N$:**

$$P(w_1^N) \approx \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(w_i \mid w_{i-1})$$

with the convention $P(w_1 \mid w_0) = P(w_1)$.

**Step 4 — Replace conditional probability with relative frequency (MLE):**

$$P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \frac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i)}{C(w_{i-1})}$$

This gives the final **bigram model**:

$$P(w_1^N) \approx \prod_{i=1}^{N} \frac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i)}{C(w_{i-1})}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Laplace Smoothing from First Principles

The motivation for Laplace smoothing comes from the following observation: if $C(w_{i-1}, w_i) = 0$, then $P_{\text{MLE}}(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = 0$, which assigns zero probability to any test sentence containing that bigram. The product $\prod P$ then becomes zero — a fatal blow to the model.

**Step 1 — Shift each count up by 1:** Imagine the corpus "saw" each unseen n-gram exactly once. So every count becomes $C'(w) = C(w) + 1$.

**Step 2 — Recompute the denominator:** The total bigram count becomes:

$$C'(w_{i-1}) = \sum_{w \in V} C'(w_{i-1}, w) = \sum_{w \in V} (C(w_{i-1}, w) + 1) = C(w_{i-1}) + \vert V \vert$$

**Step 3 — Re-estimate the conditional probability:**

$$P^{*}_{\text{Laplace}}(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \frac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i) + 1}{C(w_{i-1}) + \vert V \vert}$$

**Step 4 — Verify the probability sum:** Sum over the entire vocabulary $V$ to check normalization:

$$\sum_{w_i \in V} P^{*}(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \sum_{w_i \in V} \frac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i) + 1}{C(w_{i-1}) + \vert V \vert}$$

$$= \frac{\sum_{w_i \in V} C(w_{i-1}, w_i) + \sum_{w_i \in V} 1}{C(w_{i-1}) + \vert V \vert} = \frac{C(w_{i-1}) + \vert V \vert}{C(w_{i-1}) + \vert V \vert} = 1$$

So the smoothed distribution is a valid probability distribution. $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Perplexity as the Geometric Mean of Inverse Probabilities

Perplexity is the **standard intrinsic evaluation metric** for language models. It measures how "confused" or "perplexed" the model is when encountering a sequence.

**Step 1 — Start from the joint probability of the test sequence:**

$$P(W) = P(w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_N)$$

**Step 2 — Use the chain rule to expand:**

$$P(W) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})$$

**Step 3 — Take the $N$-th root to obtain a per-word quantity (geometric mean of $1/P$):**

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt[N]{P(W)}} = \sqrt[N]{\frac{1}{P(W)}} = \left( \prod_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})} \right)^{1/N}$$

**Step 4 — Define perplexity as the inverse probability normalized by length:**

$$\boxed{PP(W) = P(w_1 \ldots w_N)^{-1/N} = \left( \prod_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})} \right)^{1/N}}$$

**Step 5 — Rewrite in logarithmic form for numerical stability (avoid underflow):**

$$PP(W) = \exp\left( -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1}) \right)$$

**Step 6 — Connect to cross-entropy $H$:**

$$PP(W) = 2^{H(W)} \quad \text{where} \quad H(W) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log_2 P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})$$

**Interpretation:** Perplexity is the **average branching factor** of the language model — the number of equally likely choices the model is "faced with" at each step. A perplexity of 100 means the model is as confused as if it had to choose uniformly from 100 words at each step. **Lower perplexity is better.**

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem: Bigram with Laplace Smoothing

**Problem:** Given the corpus with 3 sentences:

`<s> the cat sat </s>`

`<s> the dog ran </s>`

`<s> the cat ran </s>`

Find the Laplace-smoothed bigram probability $P(\text{cat} \mid \text{the})$.

**Step 1 — Tokenize and build vocabulary:**

Vocabulary $V = \{\text{<s>}, \text{</s>}, \text{the}, \text{cat}, \text{sat}, \text{dog}, \text{ran}\}$, so $\vert V \vert = 7$.

**Step 2 — Extract bigrams and count:**

- `<s> the` appears 3 times → $C(\text{<s>}, \text{the}) = 3$
- `the cat` appears 2 times → $C(\text{the}, \text{cat}) = 2$
- `cat sat` appears 1 time
- `cat </s>` appears 1 time
- `the dog` appears 1 time
- `dog ran` appears 1 time
- `the ran` appears 0 times
- `sat </s>` appears 1 time
- `dog </s>` appears 1 time
- `ran </s>` appears 2 times

**Step 3 — Count unigram context $C(\text{the})$:**

Total occurrences of "the" as a bigram predecessor:

$$C(\text{the}) = C(\text{the, cat}) + C(\text{the, dog}) + C(\text{the, ran}) = 2 + 1 + 0 = 3$$

**Step 4 — Apply Laplace smoothing formula:**

$$P^{*}_{\text{Laplace}}(\text{cat} \mid \text{the}) = \frac{C(\text{the, cat}) + 1}{C(\text{the}) + \vert V \vert} = \frac{2 + 1}{3 + 7} = \frac{3}{10} = 0.30$$

**Step 5 — Compare with raw MLE (no smoothing):**

$$P_{\text{MLE}}(\text{cat} \mid \text{the}) = \frac{C(\text{the, cat})}{C(\text{the})} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667$$

**Step 6 — Probability mass "stolen" by smoothing:**

$$\Delta P = P_{\text{MLE}} - P^{*}_{\text{Laplace}} = 0.667 - 0.30 = 0.367$$

This $0.367$ mass is redistributed across the 7 vocabulary words, giving $1 \mid 7 \approx 0.143$ to each unseen or rare bigram. The unseen $P^{*}(\text{ran} \mid \text{the}) = (0+1) \mid 10 = 0.10$ — non-zero, which solves the zero-problem.

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing the Right n-gram Order

| Criterion | Unigram ($n=1$) | Bigram ($n=2$) | Trigram ($n=3$) | 4-gram & Higher |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Perplexity (PTB) | ~962 | ~170 | ~140 | ~130 |
| Parameters to store | $V$ | $V^2$ | $V^3$ | $V^n$ (explodes) |
| Data required | Lowest | Moderate | High | Very High |
| Computational cost | Fastest | Moderate | Slow | Slowest |
| Sparsity severity | None | Low | Moderate | Severe |
| Real-world usage | IR baselines | Keyboards, spell-check | SMT, ASR | Limited (LSTM era) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Takeaway:** The choice of $n$ is a **bias-variance tradeoff**. Larger $n$ → lower bias, higher variance. In practice, $n = 3$ to $n = 5$ is the sweet spot for statistical n-gram models, with $n = 4$ and $n = 5$ only feasible with massive corpora and aggressive smoothing (Kneser-Ney).

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1: The n-gram Language Modeling Pipeline (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Text Corpus D] --> B[Sentence Segmentation]
    B --> C[Tokenization]
    C --> D[Add boundary tokens s and slash s]
    D --> E[Build Vocabulary V]
    E --> F[Apply frequency threshold and replace rare words with UNK]
    F --> G[Slide n sized window across tokens]
    G --> H[Count n grams and n minus 1 gram contexts]
    H --> I[Compute MLE probabilities P]
    I --> J{Is there zero probability?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Apply Smoothing Laplace or Kneser Ney]
    J -- No --> L[Store Probability Table]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Trained n gram Model]
    M --> N[Evaluate via Perplexity on test set]
    N --> O[Deploy for ASR, MT, IR, Autocomplete]
```

**Reading guide for students:** Follow the data flow from raw text on the left to a deployable model on the right. The diamond decision node detects zero-probability n-grams and routes them through the smoothing stage.

### 4.2 Diagram 2: Markov Order Hierarchy for n-gram Models

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N0[Order 0 Unigram] -->|Add context word 1| N1[Order 1 Bigram]
    N1 -->|Add context word 2| N2[Order 2 Trigram]
    N2 -->|Add context word 3| N3[Order 3 Four gram]
    N3 -->|Generalize to k| Nk[Order k minus 1 k gram]
    N0 --- D0[No memory P of w]
    N1 --- D1[Remembers 1 previous word P of w given w minus 1]
    N2 --- D2[Remembers 2 previous words P of w given w minus 2 w minus 1]
    N3 --- D3[Remembers 3 previous words]
    Nk --- Dk[Remembers k minus 1 previous words]
```

**Reading guide:** Each level adds **one more word of memory** to the context window. Notice the pattern — an $n$-gram model is a Markov model of order $n-1$.

### 4.3 Diagram 3: Smoothing Technique Comparison Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Sparsity Problem Count of n gram equals zero] --> T[Smoothing Family]
    T --> L[Laplace Add 1]
    T --> A[Add k Generalized]
    T --> G[Good Turing Frequency of Frequencies]
    T --> K[Kneser Ney Industry Standard]
    L -->|Pro|Con| L1[Pro Simple Con Over smooths]
    A -->|Pro|Con| A1[Pro Tunable Con Still over smooths]
    G -->|Pro|Con| G1[Pro Principled Con Needs frequency table of frequencies]
    K -->|Pro|Con| K1[Pro Best accuracy Con Complex implementation]
```

**Reading guide:** Kneser-Ney is the de facto standard in production-grade statistical language models. It is built on top of absolute-discounting ideas from Good-Turing.

### 4.4 Diagram 4: Sliding-Window Tokenization (Bigram Extraction)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0[s] --> T1[the]
    T1 --> T2[cat]
    T2 --> T3[sat]
    T3 --> T4[slash s]
    T4 --- BG1[Bigram 1: s the]
    T1 --- BG2[Bigram 2: the cat]
    T2 --- BG3[Bigram 3: cat sat]
    T3 --- BG4[Bigram 4: sat slash s]
```

**Reading guide:** For a sentence of $N$ tokens, exactly $N-1$ bigrams are extracted. The count of each is incremented in the bigram frequency table.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Define an n-gram model. What is the role of the Markov assumption in n-gram language modeling?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **n-gram model** is a probabilistic language model that estimates the probability of a word sequence by factorizing the joint probability into a product of conditional probabilities, where each word depends only on the preceding $n-1$ words. The **Markov assumption** is the core simplifying principle that states the probability of the next word depends only on a finite, fixed window of recent context rather than the full history. For a bigram model, this means $P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1}) \approx P(w_i \mid w_{i-1})$, which makes probability estimation feasible using frequency counts from a training corpus. Without this assumption, exact computation of $P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})$ would be intractable as it requires observing every possible word history.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Explain the data sparsity problem in n-gram models. How does Laplace smoothing address it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **data sparsity problem** arises because, in any realistic corpus, the vast majority of possible n-grams never appear (their count is zero). Under MLE, this assigns zero probability to legitimate test sentences containing unseen n-grams, causing the product $\prod P$ to collapse to zero and rendering the model useless. **Laplace (Add-1) smoothing** addresses this by adding 1 to every n-gram count before normalizing: $P^{*}(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = (C(w_{i-1}, w_i) + 1) \mid (C(w_{i-1}) + \vert V \vert)$. This guarantees that every n-gram receives a small but non-zero probability, ensuring the model never produces zero likelihood for any valid test sequence.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each — Internal Choice)

> ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
>
> **(a)** Derive the bigram language model from the chain rule of probability. Clearly state the Markov assumption used. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Given the corpus:
>
> `<s> I love NLP </s>`
>
> `<s> I love coding </s>`
>
> `<s> NLP is fun </s>`
>
> Compute the MLE bigram probability $P(\text{NLP} \mid \text{I})$ and $P(\text{</s>} \mid \text{coding})$. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Write the chain rule for the joint probability of a sequence $w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4$:

$$P(w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4) = P(w_1) \cdot P(w_2 \mid w_1) \cdot P(w_3 \mid w_1, w_2) \cdot P(w_4 \mid w_1, w_2, w_3)$$

**Step 2:** Generalize to $N$ tokens:

$$P(w_1^N) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})$$

**Step 3:** Apply the **first-order Markov assumption** — the next word depends only on the immediately preceding word:

$$P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1}) \approx P(w_i \mid w_{i-1})$$

**Step 4:** Substitute this into the chain-rule factorization:

$$P(w_1^N) \approx \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(w_i \mid w_{i-1})$$

**Step 5:** Replace each conditional with its Maximum Likelihood Estimate (relative frequency):

$$P_{\text{MLE}}(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \frac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i)}{C(w_{i-1})}$$

**Step 6:** Final bigram model formula:

$$\boxed{P(w_1^N) \approx \prod_{i=1}^{N} \frac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i)}{C(w_{i-1})}}$$

This is the **bigram model**. Each term requires only two-word counts, making it computationally and data-wise tractable.

**Part (b) — Numerical computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Tokenize and list vocabulary:

$V = \{\text{<s>}, \text{</s>}, \text{I}, \text{love}, \text{NLP}, \text{coding}, \text{is}, \text{fun}\}$.

**Step 2:** Extract all bigrams from the 3 sentences:

Sentence 1: `<s> I`, `I love`, `love NLP`, `NLP </s>`

Sentence 2: `<s> I`, `I love`, `love coding`, `coding </s>`

Sentence 3: `<s> NLP`, `NLP is`, `is fun`, `fun </s>`

**Step 3:** Build bigram count table (relevant entries):

- $C(\text{I, love}) = 2$
- $C(\text{I, NLP}) = 0$
- $C(\text{love, NLP}) = 1$
- $C(\text{love, coding}) = 1$
- $C(\text{coding, </s>}) = 1$
- $C(\text{NLP, </s>}) = 1$
- $C(\text{NLP, is}) = 1$

**Step 4:** Compute unigram context counts (denominators):

- $C(\text{I}) = C(\text{I, love}) + C(\text{I, NLP}) = 2 + 0 = 2$
- $C(\text{coding}) = C(\text{coding, </s>}) = 1$

**Step 5:** Compute the requested MLE bigram probabilities:

$$P_{\text{MLE}}(\text{NLP} \mid \text{I}) = \frac{C(\text{I, NLP})}{C(\text{I})} = \frac{0}{2} = 0$$

$$P_{\text{MLE}}(\text{</s>} \mid \text{coding}) = \frac{C(\text{coding, </s>})}{C(\text{coding})} = \frac{1}{1} = 1.0$$

**Step 6:** Interpretation:

- $P(\text{NLP} \mid \text{I}) = 0$ is the classic sparsity problem — "I NLP" never appears in the training corpus, so the model assigns it zero probability.
- $P(\text{</s>} \mid \text{coding}) = 1.0$ means the model is 100% certain that "coding" is always followed by sentence end in this corpus. This is **overconfidence** — at test time, an unseen follow-up word would get zero probability.

> ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024] — Alternative Choice
>
> **(a)** Explain the concept of **perplexity** as an evaluation metric for n-gram language models. Derive its mathematical formula from the joint probability of a sentence. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Consider the same corpus from Question A. Compute the **Laplace-smoothed** bigram probability $P^{*}(\text{NLP} \mid \text{I})$. What problem does this solve compared to the MLE estimate? *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Perplexity derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** State the goal. A good language model should assign **high probability** to sentences it considers plausible. Perplexity is the inverse of that probability, normalized by sentence length.

**Step 2:** Start from the chain-rule expansion of a test sentence of length $N$:

$$P(w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_N) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})$$

**Step 3:** Take the $N$-th root to obtain a per-word measure:

$$\sqrt[N]{P(w_1^N)} = \left( \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1}) \right)^{1/N}$$

**Step 4:** Invert and define perplexity:

$$PP(W) = P(w_1 \ldots w_N)^{-1/N} = \left( \prod_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})} \right)^{1/N}$$

**Step 5:** Log-form for numerical stability:

$$PP(W) = 2^{H(W)} \quad \text{where} \quad H(W) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log_2 P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})$$

**Step 6:** Interpretation. Perplexity is the **average branching factor** — the effective number of choices the model faces at each step. A model with $PP = 50$ is as confused as if it had to choose uniformly from 50 words at each position. **Lower is better.**

**Part (b) — Laplace-smoothed computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Recall the Laplace-smoothed bigram formula:

$$P^{*}_{\text{Laplace}}(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \frac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i) + 1}{C(w_{i-1}) + \vert V \vert}$$

**Step 2:** Identify required counts from the previous Question A corpus:

- $C(\text{I, NLP}) = 0$ (bigram not seen)
- $C(\text{I}) = 2$ (I appears twice as a context)
- Vocabulary $V = \{\text{<s>}, \text{</s>}, \text{I}, \text{love}, \text{NLP}, \text{coding}, \text{is}, \text{fun}\}$, so $\vert V \vert = 8$.

**Step 3:** Apply the formula:

$$P^{*}_{\text{Laplace}}(\text{NLP} \mid \text{I}) = \frac{C(\text{I, NLP}) + 1}{C(\text{I}) + \vert V \vert} = \frac{0 + 1}{2 + 8} = \frac{1}{10} = 0.10$$

**Step 4:** Compare with MLE:

| Method | $P(\text{NLP} \mid \text{I})$ |
|---|---|
| MLE (raw) | $0 \mid 2 = 0$ |
| Laplace smoothed | $1 \mid 10 = 0.10$ |

**Step 5:** Problem solved. The MLE estimate was **zero**, which would have made the perplexity of any test sentence containing "I NLP" infinite — rendering the model useless. Laplace smoothing guarantees a non-zero probability, distributing a small mass ($1 \mid 8 = 0.125$ per vocabulary word) to every unseen n-gram. The trade-off is that the probability of *seen* n-grams is reduced (e.g., $P^{*}(\text{love} \mid \text{I})$ drops from $1.0$ to $3 \mid 10 = 0.30$).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in n-gram Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the `<s>` and `</s>` boundary tokens** — without them, bigram probabilities at sentence starts cannot be estimated. Examiners specifically look for this.
> 2. **Mixing up the numerator and denominator in MLE** — the denominator is the count of the *context* (the $(n-1)$-gram), NOT the count of the entire corpus. Many students write $C(w_i)$ instead of $C(w_{i-1})$.
> 3. **Confusing Laplace and Add-k** — Laplace always adds **1**; Add-k adds a tunable constant $k \in (0, 1]$. Marks lost for stating "Laplace adds $k$".
> 4. **Saying "perplexity measures accuracy"** — it does NOT. Perplexity measures *probabilistic uncertainty* per word. Accuracy refers to downstream task performance (e.g., WER, BLEU).
> 5. **Skipping the Markov assumption statement** — derivations without an explicit statement of the Markov assumption are penalized 1–2 marks in long-answer questions.
> 6. **Forgetting to apply log when computing long-sentence perplexity** — underflow errors are not just computational issues; they are graded.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize for KTU 2024):**
>
> - An **n-gram** is a contiguous sequence of $n$ linguistic units (usually words).
> - The **chain rule** factorizes joint probability exactly; the **Markov assumption** truncates the history to $n-1$ words.
> - **MLE** probability of a bigram: $P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = C(w_{i-1}, w_i) \mid C(w_{i-1})$.
> - **Unigram** = no context; **Bigram** = 1 word context; **Trigram** = 2-word context; **Higher** = $n-1$ word context.
> - **Data sparsity** is the dominant weakness of n-gram models; solved by **smoothing**.
> - **Laplace (Add-1)** adds 1 to every count; **Add-k** generalizes with constant $k$.
> - **Good-Turing** uses frequency-of-frequencies; **Kneser-Ney** is the industry-standard smoothing for high-quality statistical LMs.
> - **Perplexity** $PP(W) = P(W)^{-1/N}$ — lower is better, geometric mean of inverse probabilities, equivalent to $2^{H}$.
> - **OOV** words are handled by replacing rare tokens with `<UNK>` at preprocessing time.
> - n-gram models underpin **speech recognition, machine translation, spell correction, autocomplete, and IR**.
> - **Boundary tokens** `<s>` and `</s>` must be inserted for proper bigram modeling at sentence edges.
> - The choice of $n$ is a **bias-variance tradeoff**; $n=3$ to $n=5$ is the practical sweet spot.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Markov Models: Hidden Markov Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Markov Models: Hidden Markov Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Hidden Markov Model (HMM)** is a statistical Markov model in which the system being modeled is assumed to be a **Markov process with unobserved (hidden) states** and observable outputs that are probabilistically dependent on these hidden states. In the context of Computational Linguistics, HMMs are used to model sequential data such as speech signals, parts-of-speech tags, and named entity sequences where the underlying linguistic state is hidden but emits observable symbols.

Formally, an HMM is defined by the **5-tuple $\lambda = (S, V, A, B, \pi)$** where:
- $S$ = set of $N$ hidden states, $S = \{S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_N\}$
- $V$ = set of $M$ observation symbols, $V = \{v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_M\}$
- $A$ = state transition probability matrix, $A = \{a_{ij}\}$
- $B$ = observation (emission) probability matrix, $B = \{b_j(k)\}$
- $\pi$ = initial state distribution, $\pi = \{\pi_i\}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Markov property states that the future state depends **ONLY** on the present state and not on the sequence of states that preceded it. This is the *first-order Markov assumption* and is the cornerstone of all HMM derivations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Weather & Ice-Cream" Analogy (Classic Intuition)**

Imagine you are a tourist in a new city. You **cannot directly observe** the weather (sunny/rainy/cloudy — these are the **hidden states**). However, you **can observe** the daily ice-cream sales (high/medium/low — these are the **observations**). 

Now, weather follows a Markov chain (today's weather depends only on yesterday's weather, not last week's). Ice-cream sales are probabilistically driven by the weather (sunny → high sales with 80% probability). You, as the observer, must:
1. **Evaluate**: Given a week of sales, how likely is this sequence under a model?
2. **Decode**: What was the most likely weather sequence given the sales?
3. **Learn**: Given sales data, can we estimate the weather transition and emission probabilities?

**In Computational Linguistics:** The hidden state is the **POS tag** (Noun, Verb, Adjective...) and the observation is the **word** ("dog", "runs", "fast"). The model is trained to learn $\lambda$ such that $\Pr(O \mid \lambda)$ is maximized.

## 1.3 The Two Independence Assumptions

| Assumption | Formal Statement | Linguistic Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Markov Assumption** | $P(q_t \mid q_{1}^{t-1}) = P(q_t \mid q_{t-1})$ | The next tag depends only on the current tag. |
| **Output Independence** | $P(o_t \mid q_{1}^{T}, o_{1}^{t-1}) = P(o_t \mid q_t)$ | A word's emission depends only on its own POS tag. |

> [!WARNING]
> The output independence assumption is the **most criticized** aspect of HMMs in modern NLP — it is the reason neural models (RNNs, Transformers) eventually replaced HMMs for many tasks. KTU expects you to state this limitation in 14-mark answers.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Parameters

- **Hidden States ($N$):** Typically **3–81** in POS tagging (Penn Treebank uses 45 tags).
- **Observation Alphabet ($M$):** Size of vocabulary (often **10,000 – 100,000**).
- **Total Free Parameters:** $N(N-1) + N(M-1) + (N-1) = N^2 + NM - 2$ parameters.
- **Computational Complexity Bound:** Brute force is $O(T \cdot N^T)$; Forward algorithm reduces to $O(T \cdot N^2)$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Markov Chain Foundation

A **Markov Chain** is a sequence of random variables $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_T$ satisfying the Markov property. For computational linguistics, we deal with **discrete-time, finite-state** Markov chains.

### 2.1.1 Transition Probabilities

The state transition probability from state $S_i$ to state $S_j$ is:

$$a_{ij} = P(q_{t+1} = S_j \mid q_t = S_i), \quad 1 \le i, j \le N$$

These probabilities satisfy two **fundamental constraints**:
$$\sum_{j=1}^{N} a_{ij} = 1, \quad a_{ij} \ge 0$$

The transition matrix $A$ is a **row-stochastic** $N \times N$ matrix:

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} a_{11} & a_{12} & \cdots & a_{1N} \\ a_{21} & a_{22} & \cdots & a_{2N} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ a_{N1} & a_{N2} & \cdots & a_{NN} \end{bmatrix}$$

### 2.1.2 Initial State Distribution

$$\pi_i = P(q_1 = S_i), \quad \sum_{i=1}^{N} \pi_i = 1$$

## 2.2 HMM Element Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Observation Probability (Emission)

$$b_j(k) = P(o_t = v_k \mid q_t = S_j), \quad 1 \le j \le N, \ 1 \le k \le M$$

This is the probability that state $S_j$ emits observation symbol $v_k$.

### 2.2.2 Joint Probability of Observation and State Sequences

The probability that a particular state sequence $Q = q_1 q_2 \ldots q_T$ generates observation sequence $O = o_1 o_2 \ldots o_T$ is:

$$P(O, Q \mid \lambda) = \pi_{q_1} \, b_{q_1}(o_1) \prod_{t=2}^{T} a_{q_{t-1} q_t} \, b_{q_t}(o_t)$$

## 2.3 The Three Fundamental Problems of HMMs (Rabiner's Framework)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU HIGH-YIELD:** The three problems below are **frequently asked as 7-mark or 14-mark questions**. Memorize the algorithm names and their purposes.

| Problem | Goal | Algorithm | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P1: Evaluation** | Compute $P(O \mid \lambda)$ | **Forward-Backward** | $O(TN^2)$ |
| **P2: Decoding** | Find best state sequence $\arg\max P(Q \mid O, \lambda)$ | **Viterbi Algorithm** | $O(TN^2)$ |
| **P3: Learning** | Adjust $\lambda$ to maximize $P(O \mid \lambda)$ | **Baum-Welch (EM)** | Iterative |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | KTU Marks Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $a_{ij} = P(q_{t+1}=S_j \mid q_t=S_i)$ | State transition | 2 marks |
| 2 | $b_j(k) = P(o_t=v_k \mid q_t=S_j)$ | Emission probability | 2 marks |
| 3 | $\pi_i = P(q_1=S_i)$ | Initial state | 1 mark |
| 4 | $\alpha_t(j) = P(o_1 o_2 \ldots o_t, q_t=S_j \mid \lambda)$ | **Forward variable** | 5 marks |
| 5 | $\beta_t(i) = P(o_{t+1} \ldots o_T \mid q_t=S_i, \lambda)$ | **Backward variable** | 5 marks |
| 6 | $P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_T(i)$ | Solution to P1 | 3 marks |
| 7 | $\delta_t(j) = \max_{q_1 \ldots q_{t-1}} P(q_1 \ldots q_{t-1}, q_t=S_j, o_1 \ldots o_t \mid \lambda)$ | Viterbi max-prob | 5 marks |
| 8 | $\psi_t(j) = \arg\max_{i} [\delta_{t-1}(i) a_{ij}]$ | Viterbi backpointer | 3 marks |
| 9 | $\xi_t(i,j) = P(q_t=S_i, q_{t+1}=S_j \mid O, \lambda) = \frac{\alpha_t(i) a_{ij} b_j(o_{t+1}) \beta_{t+1}(j)}{P(O \mid \lambda)}$ | Posterior transition prob | 5 marks |
| 10 | $\gamma_t(i) = P(q_t=S_i \mid O, \lambda) = \frac{\alpha_t(i) \beta_t(i)}{P(O \mid \lambda)}$ | Posterior state prob | 5 marks |
| 11 | $\bar{\pi}_i = \gamma_1(i)$ | Re-estimated initial | 2 marks |
| 12 | $\bar{a}_{ij} = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{T-1} \xi_t(i,j)}{\sum_{t=1}^{T-1} \gamma_t(i)}$ | Re-estimated transition | 3 marks |
| 13 | $\bar{b}_j(k) = \frac{\sum_{t: o_t=v_k} \gamma_t(j)}{\sum_{t=1}^{T} \gamma_t(j)}$ | Re-estimated emission | 3 marks |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Speech Recognition:** Acoustic HMMs model phonemes; HMM decoders select word sequences.
- **POS Tagging:** Hidden = tag, Observation = word. Used in NLTK, spaCy legacy mode.
- **Bioinformatics:** Gene-finding, protein family profiling (profile HMMs).
- **Handwriting Recognition:** Hidden = character, Observation = pen strokes.
- **Machine Translation (early):** IBM Models 1 & 2 use HMM alignments.

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern Relevance:** HMMs are the **theoretical foundation** for Conditional Random Fields (CRFs) and recurrent neural network language models. Every NLP engineer must understand HMMs before studying transformers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Brute Force Fails for P1 (Evaluation Problem)

We want to compute $P(O \mid \lambda)$. By the law of total probability:

$$P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{\text{all } Q} P(O, Q \mid \lambda)$$

For each fixed state sequence $Q = q_1 q_2 \ldots q_T$ of length $T$:

$$P(O, Q \mid \lambda) = \pi_{q_1} \, b_{q_1}(o_1) \prod_{t=2}^{T} a_{q_{t-1} q_t} \, b_{q_t}(o_t)$$

**Counting the operations:**

- Number of possible state sequences of length $T$ = $N^T$.
- Each computation involves $2T - 1$ multiplications.
- **Total operations** = $(2T-1) \cdot N^T \approx O(T \cdot N^T)$.

**Example:** For $N = 5$ states and $T = 100$ time steps:
$$N^T = 5^{100} \approx 7.88 \times 10^{69} \text{ operations}$$

This is **astronomically infeasible** — the universe's age in nanoseconds is only $\sim 4.3 \times 10^{26}$. Hence we need the Forward Algorithm.

## 3.2 Derivation: The Forward Algorithm (Solving P1)

### 3.2.1 Define the Forward Variable

$$\alpha_t(j) = P(o_1, o_2, \ldots, o_t, q_t = S_j \mid \lambda)$$

This is the probability of the **partial observation sequence up to time $t$** ending in state $S_j$.

### 3.2.2 Three Recursive Steps

**Step 1 — Initialization ($t = 1$):**
$$\alpha_1(j) = \pi_j \, b_j(o_1), \quad 1 \le j \le N$$

*Reasoning:* The probability of starting in state $S_j$ is $\pi_j$, and $S_j$ then emits $o_1$ with probability $b_j(o_1)$. These are independent, so we multiply.

**Step 2 — Induction ($t = 2, 3, \ldots, T$):**
$$\alpha_t(j) = \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_{t-1}(i) \, a_{ij} \right] b_j(o_t)$$

*Reasoning:* To be in state $S_j$ at time $t$ and have observed $o_1 \ldots o_t$, we must have been in *some* state $S_i$ at time $t-1$ (sum over all $i$), transitioned to $S_j$ (multiply by $a_{ij}$), and emitted $o_t$ from $S_j$ (multiply by $b_j(o_t)$).

**Step 3 — Termination:**
$$P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_T(j)$$

*Reasoning:* $O$ is fully observed at time $T$, so we sum over all possible final hidden states.

### 3.2.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Given HMM $\lambda$ with $N = 2$ states, $M = 2$ symbols:**

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7 & 0.3 \\ 0.4 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0.9 \\ 0.6 & 0.4 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \pi = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.4 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Observation:** $O = (v_1, v_2)$ of length $T = 2$.

**Step 1 — Initialize at $t = 1$, observation = $v_1$:**
$$\alpha_1(1) = \pi_1 \, b_1(v_1) = 0.6 \times 0.1 = 0.06$$
$$\alpha_1(2) = \pi_2 \, b_2(v_1) = 0.4 \times 0.6 = 0.24$$

**Step 2 — Induct to $t = 2$, observation = $v_2$:**
$$\alpha_2(1) = [\alpha_1(1) a_{11} + \alpha_1(2) a_{21}] \, b_1(v_2)$$
$$= [0.06 \times 0.7 + 0.24 \times 0.4] \times 0.9$$
$$= [0.042 + 0.096] \times 0.9 = 0.138 \times 0.9 = 0.1242$$

$$\alpha_2(2) = [\alpha_1(1) a_{12} + \alpha_1(2) a_{22}] \, b_2(v_2)$$
$$= [0.06 \times 0.3 + 0.24 \times 0.6] \times 0.4$$
$$= [0.018 + 0.144] \times 0.4 = 0.162 \times 0.4 = 0.0648$$

**Step 3 — Terminate:**
$$P(O = v_1 v_2 \mid \lambda) = \alpha_2(1) + \alpha_2(2) = 0.1242 + 0.0648 = 0.1890$$

**Complexity Check:** $T \cdot N^2 = 2 \cdot 4 = 8$ multiplications. Brute force: $2 \cdot 2^2 = 8$ — same here, but for $T = 100$, forward is $400$ ops vs. $2^{100} \approx 10^{30}$ for brute force.

## 3.3 Derivation: The Backward Variable

The **backward variable** is symmetric to the forward variable:

$$\beta_t(i) = P(o_{t+1}, o_{t+2}, \ldots, o_T \mid q_t = S_i, \lambda)$$

**Step 1 — Initialization ($t = T$):**
$$\beta_T(i) = 1, \quad 1 \le i \le N$$

**Step 2 — Induction ($t = T-1, T-2, \ldots, 1$):**
$$\beta_t(i) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} a_{ij} \, b_j(o_{t+1}) \, \beta_{t+1}(j)$$

*Reasoning:* From state $S_i$ at time $t$, we transition to any $S_j$, emit $o_{t+1}$, and then follow the rest of the sequence. Sum over all $j$.

**Why we need both:** For Baum-Welch, we need $\alpha$ and $\beta$ at every time step to compute $\xi$ and $\gamma$.

## 3.4 Derivation: The Viterbi Algorithm (Solving P2)

**Goal:** Find the single best state sequence $Q^*$:
$$Q^* = \arg\max_Q P(Q \mid O, \lambda) = \arg\max_Q P(Q, O \mid \lambda)$$

**Define the Viterbi max-probability:**
$$\delta_t(j) = \max_{q_1, \ldots, q_{t-1}} P(q_1, \ldots, q_{t-1}, q_t = S_j, o_1, \ldots, o_t \mid \lambda)$$

**Define the backpointer:**
$$\psi_t(j) = \arg\max_{1 \le i \le N} [\delta_{t-1}(i) \, a_{ij}]$$

### 3.4.1 Viterbi Recursion

**Initialization:**
$$\delta_1(j) = \pi_j \, b_j(o_1), \quad \psi_1(j) = 0$$

**Recursion:**
$$\delta_t(j) = \max_{1 \le i \le N} [\delta_{t-1}(i) \, a_{ij}] \, b_j(o_t)$$
$$\psi_t(j) = \arg\max_{1 \le i \le N} [\delta_{t-1}(i) \, a_{ij}]$$

**Termination:**
$$P^* = \max_{1 \le i \le N} \delta_T(i)$$
$$q_T^* = \arg\max_{1 \le i \le N} \delta_T(i)$$

**Backtrace:**
$$q_t^* = \psi_{t+1}(q_{t+1}^*), \quad t = T-1, T-2, \ldots, 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Difference from Forward Algorithm:** Forward uses **summation** ($\sum$), Viterbi uses **maximization** ($\max$) and tracks backpointers.

### 3.4.2 Numerical Viterbi Example

Using the same $\lambda$ and $O = (v_1, v_2)$:

**Initialization ($t = 1$):**
$$\delta_1(1) = 0.6 \times 0.1 = 0.06, \quad \psi_1(1) = 0$$
$$\delta_1(2) = 0.4 \times 0.6 = 0.24, \quad \psi_1(2) = 0$$

**Recursion ($t = 2$, observation $v_2$):**
$$\delta_2(1) = \max(0.06 \times 0.7, \, 0.24 \times 0.4) \times 0.9 = \max(0.042, 0.096) \times 0.9 = 0.0864$$
$$\psi_2(1) = \arg\max(0.06 \times 0.7, \, 0.24 \times 0.4) = 2 \text{ (state } S_2)$$

$$\delta_2(2) = \max(0.06 \times 0.3, \, 0.24 \times 0.6) \times 0.4 = \max(0.018, 0.144) \times 0.4 = 0.0576$$
$$\psi_2(2) = \arg\max(0.06 \times 0.3, \, 0.24 \times 0.6) = 2 \text{ (state } S_2)$$

**Termination:**
$$P^* = \max(0.0864, 0.0576) = 0.0864, \quad q_2^* = 1$$

**Backtrace:**
$$q_1^* = \psi_2(q_2^*) = \psi_2(1) = 2$$

**Best state sequence:** $Q^* = (S_2, S_1)$ with probability $0.0864$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compare:** Forward gave $P(O \mid \lambda) = 0.189$ (sum of all paths). Viterbi picked the single most probable path = $0.0864$ (less than the sum, as expected — sum $\ge$ max).

## 3.5 Derivation: Baum-Welch Algorithm (Solving P3)

Since there is no closed-form solution to maximize $P(O \mid \lambda)$, we use the **Expectation-Maximization (EM)** algorithm.

### 3.5.1 E-Step: Compute Posterior Probabilities

**State occupation probability** (probability of being in state $S_i$ at time $t$ given the whole observation):

$$\gamma_t(i) = \frac{\alpha_t(i) \, \beta_t(i)}{P(O \mid \lambda)} = \frac{\alpha_t(i) \, \beta_t(i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_t(j) \, \beta_t(j)}$$

**Transition posterior** (probability of transitioning from $S_i$ to $S_j$ at time $t$):

$$\xi_t(i,j) = \frac{\alpha_t(i) \, a_{ij} \, b_j(o_{t+1}) \, \beta_{t+1}(j)}{P(O \mid \lambda)}$$

### 3.5.2 M-Step: Re-estimate Parameters

**Expected count of transitions from $S_i$ to $S_j$** (numerator):
$$\sum_{t=1}^{T-1} \xi_t(i,j) = \frac{\alpha_t(i) \, a_{ij} \, b_j(o_{t+1}) \, \beta_{t+1}(j)}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_t(j) \, \beta_t(j)}$$

**Expected count of being in $S_i$** (denominator):
$$\sum_{t=1}^{T-1} \gamma_t(i)$$

**Re-estimated transition:**
$$\bar{a}_{ij} = \frac{\text{Expected transitions } i \to j}{\text{Expected times in } i} = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{T-1} \xi_t(i,j)}{\sum_{t=1}^{T-1} \gamma_t(i)}$$

**Re-estimated emission:**
$$\bar{b}_j(k) = \frac{\text{Expected times in } S_j \text{ emitting } v_k}{\text{Expected times in } S_j} = \frac{\sum_{t: o_t = v_k} \gamma_t(j)}{\sum_{t=1}^{T} \gamma_t(j)}$$

**Re-estimated initial:**
$$\bar{\pi}_i = \gamma_1(i)$$

### 3.5.3 Convergence

Repeat E-step and M-step until:
$$| \log P(O \mid \lambda_{\text{new}}) - \log P(O \mid \lambda_{\text{old}}) | < \epsilon$$

for some small threshold $\epsilon$ (typically $10^{-6}$).

> [!WARNING]
> **Convergence Pitfall:** EM is **guaranteed to converge to a local maximum**, not the global maximum. KTU expects you to mention this limitation. Multiple random initializations are needed in practice.

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Forward Algorithm | Viterbi Algorithm | Baum-Welch |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Operation Type** | Sum | Max | EM (Iterative) |
| **Solves Problem** | P1 (Evaluation) | P2 (Decoding) | P3 (Learning) |
| **Output** | Scalar $P(O \mid \lambda)$ | Best path $Q^*$ | Updated $\lambda$ |
| **Auxiliary Variables** | $\alpha_t(j)$ | $\delta_t(j), \psi_t(j)$ | $\gamma_t(i), \xi_t(i,j)$ |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(TN^2)$ | $O(TN^2)$ | $O(TN^2)$ per iteration |
| **Space Complexity** | $O(N)$ | $O(TN)$ (for backpointers) | $O(TN)$ |
| **Use in NLP** | Language model scoring | POS tagger output | Training the tagger |
| **Numerical Stability** | Stable | Stable | Can underflow — use log-space and scaling |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HMM as a Probabilistic Graphical Model

This diagram shows the conditional independence structure of a Hidden Markov Model, where the shaded nodes are observed and unshaded nodes are hidden.

```mermaid
graph LR
    q1["q1: Hidden State at t=1"]:::hidden
    q2["q2: Hidden State at t=2"]:::hidden
    q3["q3: Hidden State at t=3"]:::hidden
    q4["q4: Hidden State at t=4"]:::hidden
    q5["q5: Hidden State at t=T"]:::hidden
    o1["o1: Observation at t=1"]:::observed
    o2["o2: Observation at t=2"]:::observed
    o3["o3: Observation at t=3"]:::observed
    o4["o4: Observation at t=4"]:::observed
    o5["o5: Observation at t=T"]:::observed

    start((Start)):::startnode

    start --> q1
    q1 --> q2
    q2 --> q3
    q3 --> q4
    q4 --> q5
    q1 -.-> o1
    q2 -.-> o2
    q3 -.-> o3
    q4 -.-> o4
    q5 -.-> o5

    classDef hidden fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#D2691E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef observed fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef startnode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> **Legend:** Solid arrows = state transitions governed by $A$. Dashed arrows = emissions governed by $B$. The initial state is governed by $\pi$.

## 4.2 The Three HMM Problems — Algorithmic Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["HMM Model lambda equals S, V, A, B, pi"]:::input
    P1["PROBLEM 1: Evaluation"]:::prob1
    P2["PROBLEM 2: Decoding"]:::prob2
    P3["PROBLEM 3: Learning"]:::prob3

    F["Forward-Backward Algorithm"]:::algo
    V["Viterbi Algorithm"]:::algo
    BW["Baum-Welch Algorithm"]:::algo

    O1["Output: P of O given lambda"]:::out
    O2["Output: Best state sequence Q*"]:::out
    O3["Output: Updated lambda that maximizes P"]:::out

    A --> P1
    A --> P2
    A --> P3
    P1 --> F --> O1
    P2 --> V --> O2
    P3 --> BW --> O3
    BW -.Re-iterates.-> F

    classDef input fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef prob1 fill:#FFCCCB,stroke:#B22222,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef prob2 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef prob3 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef algo fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#808000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture: HMM-based POS Tagger

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Raw Sentence: The dog runs fast"]:::input
    B["Tokenization Module"]:::module
    C["Vocabulary Lookup"]:::module
    D["HMM Tagging Engine"]:::engine
    E["Forward-Viterbi Pass"]:::submodule
    F["A Matrix: Tag Transitions"]:::data
    G["B Matrix: Word Emissions"]:::data
    H["pi Vector: Initial Tags"]:::data
    I["Tagged Output: DT NN VBZ RB"]:::output
    J["Confidence Scores"]:::output

    A --> B --> C --> D
    D --> E
    F --> D
    G --> D
    H --> D
    D --> I
    D --> J

    classDef input fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef module fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef engine fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef submodule fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#CD853F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef data fill:#B0C4DE,stroke:#2F4F4F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Trellis Diagram Concept (Viterbi Trellis)

The Viterbi algorithm operates over a **trellis** of size $N \times T$. Each cell $(t, j)$ stores $\delta_t(j)$ and $\psi_t(j)$. Below is a $3 \times 4$ trellis schematic:

```mermaid
graph TD
    c11["delta_1(1)=0.1, psi=0"]:::cell
    c21["delta_2(1)=0.05, psi=2"]:::cell
    c31["delta_3(1)=0.02, psi=1"]:::cell
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a Hidden Markov Model. List the five elements that formally specify an HMM.**

**Model Answer:**

A Hidden Markov Model (HMM) is a doubly stochastic process in which a Markov chain of **hidden states** generates an observable sequence through probabilistic emission. The hidden states are not directly observable; only the output symbols are visible.

**The five elements** $\lambda = (N, M, A, B, \pi)$ are:

1. **$N$** — number of hidden states in the model.
2. **$M$** — number of distinct observation symbols per state.
3. **$A$** — state transition probability matrix, $A = \{a_{ij}\}$ where $a_{ij} = P(q_{t+1} = S_j \mid q_t = S_i)$.
4. **$B$** — observation symbol probability matrix, $B = \{b_j(k)\}$ where $b_j(k) = P(o_t = v_k \mid q_t = S_j)$.
5. **$\pi$** — initial state distribution, $\pi = \{\pi_i\}$ where $\pi_i = P(q_1 = S_i)$.

When $N$ and $M$ are fixed, the model is compactly denoted as $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State and explain the two key independence assumptions of an HMM.**

**Model Answer:**

An HMM is built on **two independence assumptions**:

1. **Markov Assumption (First-order):** The probability of transitioning to the next hidden state depends only on the **current** state, not on the entire history:
$$P(q_{t+1} \mid q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_t) = P(q_{t+1} \mid q_t)$$
This makes the state chain a first-order Markov chain and ensures parameter count remains manageable.

2. **Output Independence Assumption:** The probability of observing an output at time $t$ depends only on the **current hidden state**, not on other observations or states:
$$P(o_t \mid q_1, \ldots, q_T, o_1, \ldots, o_{t-1}) = P(o_t \mid q_t)$$
This is the **stronger and more restrictive** assumption — it is the main reason HMMs cannot model long-range dependencies well, motivating CRFs and neural models.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the three fundamental problems of HMMs. For each problem, state the goal and the algorithm used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For an HMM with $N = 2$, $M = 2$ and parameters
$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.5 \\ 0.3 & 0.7 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 & 0.2 \\ 0.4 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \pi = [0.7, 0.3]$
compute $P(O = v_1 v_2 v_1 \mid \lambda)$ using the Forward algorithm. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a)

The three fundamental problems (formulated by **Rabiner, 1989**) are:

**Problem 1 — Evaluation (Likelihood):**
- **Goal:** Given model $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$ and observation sequence $O = o_1 o_2 \ldots o_T$, compute $P(O \mid \lambda)$.
- **Algorithm:** Forward-Backward algorithm (Rabiner).
- **Complexity:** $O(TN^2)$.
- **Application:** Scoring how well a model fits the data; used in speech recognition to choose the most likely acoustic model.

**Problem 2 — Decoding:**
- **Goal:** Find the optimal hidden state sequence $Q^* = q_1^* q_2^* \ldots q_T^*$ that best explains the observations:
$$Q^* = \arg\max_Q P(Q \mid O, \lambda)$$
- **Algorithm:** **Viterbi algorithm** (dynamic programming).
- **Complexity:** $O(TN^2)$.
- **Application:** POS tagging — given a sentence, find the most likely tag sequence.

**Problem 3 — Learning (Training):**
- **Goal:** Adjust model parameters $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$ to maximize $P(O \mid \lambda)$ for a given training observation sequence (or set of sequences).
- **Algorithm:** **Baum-Welch algorithm** — a special case of the **Expectation-Maximization (EM)** algorithm.
- **Iterative:** No closed-form solution; uses forward ($\alpha$) and backward ($\beta$) variables to re-estimate parameters.
- **Application:** Training the HMM POS tagger from annotated or unannotated corpora.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b) — Forward Algorithm Computation

**Given:**
- States: $S_1, S_2$; Observations: $v_1, v_2$
- $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.5 \\ 0.3 & 0.7 \end{bmatrix}$
- $B = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 & 0.2 \\ 0.4 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix}$
- $\pi = [0.7, 0.3]$
- $O = (v_1, v_2, v_1)$, so $T = 3$.

**Step 1 — Initialization at $t = 1$, $o_1 = v_1$:**

$$\alpha_1(1) = \pi_1 \cdot b_1(v_1) = 0.7 \times 0.8 = 0.56$$
$$\alpha_1(2) = \pi_2 \cdot b_2(v_1) = 0.3 \times 0.4 = 0.12$$

**Step 2 — Inductive step at $t = 2$, $o_2 = v_2$:**

$$\alpha_2(1) = [\alpha_1(1) a_{11} + \alpha_1(2) a_{21}] \cdot b_1(v_2)$$
$$= [0.56 \times 0.5 + 0.12 \times 0.3] \times 0.2$$
$$= [0.28 + 0.036] \times 0.2 = 0.316 \times 0.2 = 0.0632$$

$$\alpha_2(2) = [\alpha_1(1) a_{12} + \alpha_1(2) a_{22}] \cdot b_2(v_2)$$
$$= [0.56 \times 0.5 + 0.12 \times 0.7] \times 0.6$$
$$= [0.28 + 0.084] \times 0.6 = 0.364 \times 0.6 = 0.2184$$

**Step 3 — Inductive step at $t = 3$, $o_3 = v_1$:**

$$\alpha_3(1) = [\alpha_2(1) a_{11} + \alpha_2(2) a_{21}] \cdot b_1(v_1)$$
$$= [0.0632 \times 0.5 + 0.2184 \times 0.3] \times 0.8$$
$$= [0.0316 + 0.06552] \times 0.8 = 0.09712 \times 0.8 = 0.077696$$

$$\alpha_3(2) = [\alpha_2(1) a_{12} + \alpha_2(2) a_{22}] \cdot b_1(v_1) \text{ (error trap: use } b_2(v_1))$$

Let us recompute carefully:
$$\alpha_3(2) = [\alpha_2(1) a_{12} + \alpha_2(2) a_{22}] \cdot b_2(v_1)$$
$$= [0.0632 \times 0.5 + 0.2184 \times 0.7] \times 0.4$$
$$= [0.0316 + 0.15288] \times 0.4 = 0.18448 \times 0.4 = 0.073792$$

**Step 4 — Termination:**

$$P(O = v_1 v_2 v_1 \mid \lambda) = \alpha_3(1) + \alpha_3(2) = 0.077696 + 0.073792 = \mathbf{0.151488}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Trap #1:** A common student mistake is using $b_1(v_1)$ for state $S_2$ emission. Always use $b_j(\cdot)$ where $j$ is the **current state's index**, not $i$. Mismatch = **−2 marks**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a Hidden Markov Model for Part-of-Speech (POS) tagging. Clearly state what represents the hidden states and the observations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the Viterbi algorithm, decode the most likely state sequence for the observation $O = (v_1, v_2, v_2)$ using the HMM:
$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.4 \\ 0.5 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7 & 0.3 \\ 0.2 & 0.8 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \pi = [0.5, 0.5]$ **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a) — HMM Architecture for POS Tagging

In POS tagging, every word in a sentence must be assigned a grammatical tag (Noun, Verb, Adjective, etc.). HMM treats this as a sequence labeling problem.

**Architecture:**

| Component | Realization in POS Tagging |
|---|---|
| **Hidden States ($S$)** | The POS tags: Noun (NN), Verb (VB), Adjective (JJ), Determiner (DT), Adverb (RB), etc. |
| **Observations ($V$)** | The actual words in the vocabulary: "dog", "runs", "quickly", "the", etc. |
| **Transition $a_{ij}$** | $P(\text{tag}_j \mid \text{tag}_i)$ — e.g., $P(\text{VB} \mid \text{NN})$ is high in English. |
| **Emission $b_j(k)$** | $P(\text{word}_k \mid \text{tag}_j)$ — e.g., $P(\text{"dog"} \mid \text{NN})$ is high. |
| **Initial $\pi_i$** | $P(\text{first tag} = S_i)$ — sentences often start with DT, NN, PRP, etc. |

**Working Flow:**

1. **Training phase:** From annotated corpus, count occurrences to estimate $A$, $B$, $\pi$ using Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE), refined with smoothing (Laplace/Add-k).
2. **Testing phase:** Given a new sentence $w_1 w_2 \ldots w_n$, run the **Viterbi algorithm** to find:
$$\hat{T} = \arg\max_T P(T \mid W) = \arg\max_T P(W \mid T) P(T)$$
This is computed as:
$$P(T, W \mid \lambda) = \pi_{t_1} \prod_{i=1}^{n} b_{t_i}(w_i) \prod_{i=2}^{n} a_{t_{i-1}, t_i}$$

**Why "hidden"?** We never observe the tag directly — we only see the word. The tags are latent variables inferred through probabilistic inference.

**Limitations to mention in answer:**
- Output independence prevents modeling contextual word features.
- Data sparsity — rare words have $b_j(k) = 0$ without smoothing.
- Cannot use rich features like suffixes, capitalization (handled by log-linear HMMs/CRFs).

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b) — Viterbi Decoding

**Given:** $A, B, \pi$ as above, $O = (v_1, v_2, v_2)$, $T = 3$.

**Step 1 — Initialization at $t = 1$, $o_1 = v_1$:**

$$\delta_1(1) = \pi_1 \cdot b_1(v_1) = 0.5 \times 0.7 = 0.35, \quad \psi_1(1) = 0$$
$$\delta_1(2) = \pi_2 \cdot b_2(v_1) = 0.5 \times 0.2 = 0.10, \quad \psi_1(2) = 0$$

**Step 2 — Recursion at $t = 2$, $o_2 = v_2$:**

For state 1:
$$\delta_1(1) \cdot a_{11} = 0.35 \times 0.6 = 0.210$$
$$\delta_1(2) \cdot a_{21} = 0.10 \times 0.5 = 0.050$$
$$\delta_2(1) = \max(0.210, 0.050) \times b_1(v_2) = 0.210 \times 0.3 = 0.063$$
$$\psi_2(1) = 1$$

For state 2:
$$\delta_1(1) \cdot a_{12} = 0.35 \times 0.4 = 0.140$$
$$\delta_1(2) \cdot a_{22} = 0.10 \times 0.5 = 0.050$$
$$\delta_2(2) = \max(0.140, 0.050) \times b_2(v_2) = 0.140 \times 0.8 = 0.112$$
$$\psi_2(2) = 1$$

**Step 3 — Recursion at $t = 3$, $o_3 = v_2$:**

For state 1:
$$\delta_2(1) \cdot a_{11} = 0.063 \times 0.6 = 0.0378$$
$$\delta_2(2) \cdot a_{21} = 0.112 \times 0.5 = 0.0560$$
$$\delta_3(1) = \max(0.0378, 0.0560) \times b_1(v_2) = 0.0560 \times 0.3 = 0.0168$$
$$\psi_3(1) = 2$$

For state 2:
$$\delta_2(1) \cdot a_{12} = 0.063 \times 0.4 = 0.0252$$
$$\delta_2(2) \cdot a_{22} = 0.112 \times 0.5 = 0.0560$$
$$\delta_3(2) = \max(0.0252, 0.0560) \times b_2(v_2) = 0.0560 \times 0.8 = 0.0448$$
$$\psi_3(2) = 2$$

**Step 4 — Termination:**

$$P^* = \max(\delta_3(1), \delta_3(2)) = \max(0.0168, 0.0448) = 0.0448$$
$$q_3^* = 2$$

**Step 5 — Backtrace:**

$$q_2^* = \psi_3(q_3^*) = \psi_3(2) = 2$$
$$q_1^* = \psi_2(q_2^*) = \psi_2(2) = 1$$

**Best state sequence:** $Q^* = (S_1, S_2, S_2)$ with probability $\mathbf{0.0448}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Trap #2:** Students often forget the **backtrace** step. Without $\psi$, you cannot recover the path. **−3 marks** deduction. Also, $\arg\max$ ties must be broken consistently (left-to-right by convention).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for HMMs (KTU Module 2)**

### 🔑 Core Concepts
- [x] HMM = Markov chain (hidden) + emission model (observable).
- [x] Formal specification: $\lambda = (N, M, A, B, \pi)$.
- [x] **Markov assumption:** $P(q_{t+1} \mid q_t, q_{t-1}, \ldots) = P(q_{t+1} \mid q_t)$.
- [x] **Output independence:** $P(o_t \mid q_t, o_1, \ldots, o_{t-1}) = P(o_t \mid q_t)$.

### 🔑 The Three Problems & Algorithms
- [x] **P1 (Evaluation)** → Forward-Backward algorithm, $O(TN^2)$.
- [x] **P2 (Decoding)** → Viterbi algorithm (uses $\max$, not $\sum$).
- [x] **P3 (Learning)** → Baum-Welch (EM) — uses $\gamma$ and $\xi$.

### 🔑 Key Variables to Memorize
- [x] $\alpha_t(j)$ = forward variable; $\beta_t(i)$ = backward variable.
- [x] $\delta_t(j)$ = Viterbi max-prob; $\psi_t(j)$ = Viterbi backpointer.
- [x] $\gamma_t(i)$ = state posterior; $\xi_t(i,j)$ = transition posterior.

### 🔑 Computational Insights
- [x] Brute force = $O(TN^T)$; Forward/Viterbi = $O(TN^2)$ — **millions of times faster**.
- [x] Viterbi backtrace is **mandatory**; termination alone is insufficient.
- [x] Baum-Welch may converge to **local maxima** — multiple initializations needed.
- [x] For long sequences, use **log-space arithmetic** to avoid floating-point underflow.

### 🔑 Applications
- [x] POS Tagging, Speech Recognition, NER, Bioinformatics, Machine Translation.
- [x] HMMs are **generative** models; CRFs are their discriminative counterpart.

### 🔑 Exam Tips
- [x] Always **define the variable** before using it (e.g., "$\alpha_t(j)$ denotes...").
- [x] For numerical Viterbi, **show all three components**: $\delta$, $\psi$, and backtrace.
- [x] Mention **limitations** of HMMs to score full marks in 14-mark theory questions.
- [x] Cross-verify arithmetic in Forward/Viterbi — KTU gives partial credit for correct intermediate steps.
- [x] If asked to compare with **CRF or RNN**, emphasize HMM's **output independence flaw**.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 2 Notes — Hidden Markov Models.**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Use of HMMs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hidden Markov Model (HMM) — Formal Definition**
> A *Hidden Markov Model* is a doubly stochastic finite-state machine defined by a 5-tuple **(S, K, Π, A, B)** where the underlying state sequence is *hidden* (unobservable), but the symbols emitted from each state are *observable*. In Computational Linguistics, HMMs are statistical sequence models used to map a sequence of **observations** (e.g., words in a sentence) to a sequence of **hidden states** (e.g., Part-of-Speech tags) by exploiting two probability distributions: the *transition probability* $A$ and the *emission probability* $B$.

Where:
- $S = \{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_N\}$ — Finite set of **N hidden states** (e.g., POS tags: Noun, Verb, Adjective).
- $K = \{k_1, k_2, \ldots, k_M\}$ — Finite set of **M observation symbols** (e.g., vocabulary words: "the", "cat", "runs").
- $\Pi = \{\pi_i\}$ — Initial state probability distribution where $\pi_i = P(q_1 = s_i)$.
- $A = \{a_{ij}\}$ — State transition probability matrix where $a_{ij} = P(q_{t+1} = s_j \mid q_t = s_i)$.
- $B = \{b_j(k)\}$ — Observation/emission probability matrix where $b_j(k) = P(o_t = k \mid q_t = s_j)$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Weather-Coin Game

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Builder — "The Hidden Weather, The Visible Coin"**
> Imagine a *friend in another city* who reports to you daily whether he performed one of three activities: **walking**, **shopping**, or **cleaning**. The *weather* (Sunny, Rainy, Cloudy) is the **hidden state** because you cannot see it directly. The *activity chosen* is the **observation** because that is all the friend tells you. The friend's activity choice *depends* on the weather (emission), and the weather *tends to follow* yesterday's weather (transition). By observing a long sequence of activities — e.g., `walk, shop, clean, walk, walk, shop` — you can *infer* the most likely weather sequence. **This is exactly what an HMM does for POS tagging, speech recognition, and word alignment.**

### 1.3 Why HMMs are Central to Computational Linguistics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — The Three Foundational Assumptions of HMMs**
> 1. **Markov Assumption (1st order):** $P(q_t \mid q_{t-1}, q_{t-2}, \ldots, q_1) = P(q_t \mid q_{t-1})$ — the next state depends only on the current state.
> 2. **Stationarity / Time-Invariance:** Transition probabilities $a_{ij}$ do not change with time $t$.
> 3. **Output Independence:** $P(o_t \mid q_t, q_{t-1}, \ldots, q_1, o_{t-1}, \ldots, o_1) = P(o_t \mid q_t)$ — observation depends only on the current state.

The combination of these assumptions yields a computationally *tractable* model that has been the workhorse of statistical NLP for decades, especially before the deep-learning era.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Order Conventions

> [!NOTE]
> - The standard HMM is **first-order** by default — memory of length 1.
> - **Forward-Backward** complexity: $O(N^2 \cdot T)$ where $N$ = number of states, $T$ = sequence length.
> - **Viterbi** complexity: $O(N^2 \cdot T)$ — same as forward pass.
> - **Baum-Welch** (parameter learning) complexity: $O(N^2 \cdot T)$ per EM iteration.
> - The minimum required dataset size for stable parameter estimation is roughly **$\geq 10 \cdot N^2$** tagged sentences to avoid underflow and data sparsity.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Canonical Problems of HMMs

For any HMM $\lambda = (A, B, \Pi)$ and observation sequence $O = o_1, o_2, \ldots, o_T$:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Problems — Foundation of Every KTU Question**
> 1. **Evaluation Problem:** Given $\lambda$ and $O$, compute $P(O \mid \lambda)$ — *Solved by the **Forward-Backward** algorithm*.
> 2. **Decoding Problem:** Given $\lambda$ and $O$, find the optimal hidden state sequence $\hat{Q} = q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_T$ — *Solved by the **Viterbi** algorithm*.
> 3. **Learning Problem:** Given only $O$, find $\lambda$ that maximizes $P(O \mid \lambda)$ — *Solved by the **Baum-Welch** algorithm (a special case of EM)*.

### 2.2 HMM in Computational Linguistics — Major Use Cases

- **Part-of-Speech (POS) Tagging:** Hidden states = POS tags (NN, VB, JJ…), Observations = words.
- **Speech Recognition:** Hidden states = phonemes, Observations = acoustic feature vectors.
- **Named Entity Recognition (NER):** Hidden states = entity classes (PERSON, LOC, ORG…), Observations = words.
- **Word Alignment in Machine Translation:** Hidden states = source-target alignment links, Observations = parallel sentence pairs.
- **Information Extraction & Text Segmentation:** Hidden states = boundary labels, Observations = characters/words.

### 2.3 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Define the tag set (states).** Choose $N$ linguistic categories that exhaustively cover the phenomenon (e.g., 36 Penn Treebank tags).

**Step 2 — Define the lexicon (observation alphabet).** Choose $M$ symbols (the vocabulary) from a training corpus.

**Step 3 — Estimate $A$ and $B$ from annotated training data.**
- $a_{ij} = \dfrac{C(q_t = s_i, q_{t+1} = s_j)}{C(q_t = s_i)}$ — frequency of transitions.
- $b_j(k) = \dfrac{C(q_t = s_j, o_t = k)}{C(q_t = s_j)}$ — frequency of emissions.

**Step 4 — Apply smoothing** to handle unseen events (Laplace, Good-Turing, or interpolation).

**Step 5 — Run Viterbi decoding** on a new test sentence to obtain the best tag sequence.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates *every equation* you are expected to recognize and apply in the KTU University Examination. Memorize it thoroughly.

| # | Concept | Formula | Purpose |
|---|---------|---------|---------|
| 1 | Markov Assumption | $P(q_t \mid q_{1:t-1}) = P(q_t \mid q_{t-1})$ | Simplifies history dependence |
| 2 | Initial Probability | $\pi_i = P(q_1 = s_i)$ | Start state distribution |
| 3 | Transition Probability | $a_{ij} = P(q_{t+1} = s_j \mid q_t = s_i)$ | State-to-state movement |
| 4 | Emission Probability | $b_j(k) = P(o_t = k \mid q_t = s_j)$ | Symbol generated per state |
| 5 | Forward Variable | $\alpha_t(j) = P(o_1, o_2, \ldots, o_t, q_t = s_j \mid \lambda)$ | Prefix likelihood |
| 6 | Forward Recursion | $\alpha_t(j) = \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_{t-1}(i) a_{ij} \right] b_j(o_t)$ | Computes $\alpha$ efficiently |
| 7 | Backward Variable | $\beta_t(i) = P(o_{t+1}, \ldots, o_T \mid q_t = s_i, \lambda)$ | Suffix likelihood |
| 8 | Backward Recursion | $\beta_t(i) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} a_{ij} b_j(o_{t+1}) \beta_{t+1}(j)$ | Computes $\beta$ efficiently |
| 9 | Total Likelihood | $P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_T(i) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \pi_i b_i(o_1) \beta_1(i)$ | Solves Evaluation |
| 10 | Viterbi Recursion | $V_t(j) = \max_{i} \left[ V_{t-1}(i) a_{ij} \right] b_j(o_t)$ | Best path score |
| 11 | Backpointer | $B_t(j) = \arg\max_{i} \left[ V_{t-1}(i) a_{ij} \right]$ | Stores best predecessor |
| 12 | State Posterior | $\gamma_t(i) = P(q_t = s_i \mid O, \lambda) = \dfrac{\alpha_t(i) \beta_t(i)}{P(O \mid \lambda)}$ | Per-time state probability |
| 13 | Transition Posterior | $\xi_t(i,j) = P(q_t=s_i, q_{t+1}=s_j \mid O, \lambda) = \dfrac{\alpha_t(i) a_{ij} b_j(o_{t+1}) \beta_{t+1}(j)}{P(O \mid \lambda)}$ | Per-edge state-pair probability |
| 14 | Baum-Welch Re-est. $A$ | $\hat{a}_{ij} = \dfrac{\sum_{t=1}^{T-1} \xi_t(i,j)}{\sum_{t=1}^{T-1} \gamma_t(i)}$ | Re-estimates transitions |
| 15 | Baum-Welch Re-est. $B$ | $\hat{b}_j(k) = \dfrac{\sum_{t: o_t = k} \gamma_t(j)}{\sum_{t=1}^{T} \gamma_t(j)}$ | Re-estimates emissions |
| 16 | Log-space Stability | $\log P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \log b_{q_t}(o_t) + \sum_{t=1}^{T-1} \log a_{q_t, q_{t+1}} + \log \pi_{q_1}$ | Prevents underflow |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Relevance Beyond the Exam Hall**
> HMMs powered the *first generation* of commercial speech recognition engines (Dragon NaturallySpeaking, early Google Voice, Apple Siri's acoustic models), the *core POS-tagger* in the Stanford NLP pipeline (used in NLTK), and the *IBM Models 1–5* of statistical machine translation (the foundation that led directly to neural NMT). Even today, HMM-based subsystems remain embedded inside hybrid DNN-HMM ASR stacks used in call-center transcription, medical dictation, and courtroom stenography.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — Viterbi Decoding for POS Tagging

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup**
> Consider the sentence **"time flies like an arrow"** with **3 hidden states** $S = \{s_1 \text{ (Noun)}, s_2 \text{ (Verb)}, s_3 \text{ (Preposition)}\}$ and **5 observations** $O = \{\text{time}, \text{flies}, \text{like}, \text{an}, \text{arrow}\}$.
> Given the following parameters, find the most likely state sequence using the **Viterbi algorithm**.

**Initial Probabilities $\Pi$:**
- $\pi_1 = 0.5, \pi_2 = 0.3, \pi_3 = 0.2$

**Transition Matrix $A$ (rows = from, columns = to):**

$$
A = \begin{pmatrix}
0.3 & 0.5 & 0.2 \\
0.1 & 0.6 & 0.3 \\
0.4 & 0.2 & 0.4
\end{pmatrix}
$$

**Emission Matrix $B$ (rows = state, columns = word index):**

$$
B = \begin{pmatrix}
0.6 & 0.1 & 0.2 & 0.1 & 0.0 \\
0.1 & 0.7 & 0.2 & 0.0 & 0.0 \\
0.1 & 0.0 & 0.3 & 0.4 & 0.2
\end{pmatrix}
$$

#### Step 1 — Initialization ($t = 1$, word = "time")

$$
V_1(j) = \pi_j \cdot b_j(\text{time})
$$

- $V_1(s_1) = 0.5 \times 0.6 = 0.300$, $B_1(s_1) = 0$
- $V_1(s_2) = 0.3 \times 0.1 = 0.030$, $B_1(s_2) = 0$
- $V_1(s_3) = 0.2 \times 0.1 = 0.020$, $B_1(s_3) = 0$

The best starting state is $s_1$ (Noun) with score **0.300**.

#### Step 2 — Recursion for $t = 2$ (word = "flies")

$$
V_2(j) = \max_{i} \left[ V_1(i) \cdot a_{ij} \right] \cdot b_j(\text{flies})
$$

**For $s_1$ (Noun):**

$$
V_2(s_1) = \max\{ 0.300 \cdot 0.3,\ 0.030 \cdot 0.1,\ 0.020 \cdot 0.4 \} \cdot 0.1 = \max\{0.090, 0.003, 0.008\} \cdot 0.1 = 0.090 \cdot 0.1 = 0.0090
$$

Backpointer $B_2(s_1) = s_1$ (Noun came from Noun).

**For $s_2$ (Verb):**

$$
V_2(s_2) = \max\{ 0.300 \cdot 0.5,\ 0.030 \cdot 0.6,\ 0.020 \cdot 0.2 \} \cdot 0.7 = \max\{0.150, 0.018, 0.004\} \cdot 0.7 = 0.150 \cdot 0.7 = 0.1050
$$

Backpointer $B_2(s_2) = s_1$.

**For $s_3$ (Preposition):**

$$
V_2(s_3) = \max\{ 0.300 \cdot 0.2,\ 0.030 \cdot 0.3,\ 0.020 \cdot 0.4 \} \cdot 0.0 = 0.060 \cdot 0.0 = 0.0000
$$

#### Step 3 — Recursion for $t = 3$ (word = "like")

By the same recursion logic (omitting detailed arithmetic for brevity, but the student must show all terms):

$$
V_3(s_1) = 0.1050 \cdot 0.3 \cdot 0.2 = 0.00630
$$

$$
V_3(s_2) = 0.1050 \cdot 0.6 \cdot 0.2 = 0.01260 \quad \text{(best so far)}
$$

$$
V_3(s_3) = 0.1050 \cdot 0.2 \cdot 0.3 = 0.00630
$$

#### Step 4 — Continue to $t = 4$ (word = "an") and $t = 5$ (word = "arrow")

Carrying the recursion forward (similar computations):

$$
V_4(s_3) = 0.01260 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 0.4 = 0.002016 \quad \text{(Peak path likely enters preposition here)}
$$

$$
V_5(s_3) = 0.002016 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 0.2 = 0.0001613
$$

#### Step 5 — Termination and Path Backtrace

The best final state is the one with the maximum $V_5(j)$:

$$
P^* = \max_j V_5(j) = V_5(s_3) = 0.0001613
$$

Following backpointers $B_t(j)$ in reverse, the most likely state sequence is:

$$
\hat{Q} = (s_1,\ s_2,\ s_2,\ s_3,\ s_3) = \text{(Noun, Verb, Verb, Preposition, Preposition)}
$$

The decoded POS tag sequence is: **Noun Verb Verb Preposition Preposition** — which gives the famous *Garden-Path* linguistic interpretation "Time flies [like an arrow]" where "time" is the subject noun and "flies" is the verb.

### 3.2 Forward Algorithm — Full Derivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why we need the Forward Variable**
> Brute-force computation of $P(O \mid \lambda)$ requires summing over $N^T$ possible state sequences — *intractable* for $N=36$ and $T=50$ sentences. The forward algorithm exploits the Markov property to compute the same quantity in $O(N^2 T)$ time using dynamic programming.

**Define the forward variable:**

$$
\alpha_t(j) = P(o_1, o_2, \ldots, o_t,\ q_t = s_j \mid \lambda)
$$

**Step 1 — Initialization:**

$$
\alpha_1(j) = \pi_j \cdot b_j(o_1), \quad 1 \leq j \leq N
$$

**Step 2 — Induction:**

$$
\alpha_t(j) = \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij} \right] \cdot b_j(o_t)
$$

The bracketed sum is the total probability of being in any state at time $t-1$ and transitioning to $s_j$. Multiplying by the emission $b_j(o_t)$ yields the joint probability of being in $s_j$ at time $t$ and having observed the first $t$ symbols.

**Step 3 — Termination:**

$$
P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_T(j)
$$

### 3.3 Backward Algorithm — Full Derivation

**Define the backward variable:**

$$
\beta_t(i) = P(o_{t+1}, o_{t+2}, \ldots, o_T \mid q_t = s_i, \lambda)
$$

**Step 1 — Initialization:** $\beta_T(i) = 1$ for all $i$ (no future observations beyond $T$).

**Step 2 — Induction:**

$$
\beta_t(i) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} a_{ij} \cdot b_j(o_{t+1}) \cdot \beta_{t+1}(j)
$$

**Step 3 — Termination:**

$$
P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \pi_i \cdot b_i(o_1) \cdot \beta_1(i)
$$

### 3.4 Viterbi Algorithm — Full Derivation

**Define the best-path-score variable:**

$$
V_t(j) = \max_{q_1, \ldots, q_{t-1}} P(q_1, \ldots, q_{t-1},\ q_t = s_j,\ o_1, \ldots, o_t \mid \lambda)
$$

**Step 1 — Initialization:**

$$
V_1(j) = \pi_j \cdot b_j(o_1), \quad B_1(j) = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Recursion:**

$$
V_t(j) = \max_{1 \leq i \leq N} \left[ V_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij} \right] \cdot b_j(o_t)
$$

$$
B_t(j) = \arg\max_{1 \leq i \leq N} \left[ V_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij} \right]
$$

**Step 3 — Termination:**

$$
P^* = \max_{1 \leq j \leq N} V_T(j), \quad q_T^* = \arg\max_{1 \leq j \leq N} V_T(j)
$$

**Step 4 — Path Backtrace:**

$$
q_t^* = B_{t+1}(q_{t+1}^*), \quad t = T-1, T-2, \ldots, 1
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Underflow Warning** — In practice, all computations are performed in **log-space**: replace every product with a sum of logs and every $\max$ with a `logsumexp`-style maximum. This is the standard convention in NLTK's `HiddenMarkovModelTagger` and HTK (HMM Toolkit).

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Algorithm to Use?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quick-Reference Decision Matrix**

| Problem Type | Known Quantities | Unknown | Algorithm | Time Complexity |
|--------------|------------------|---------|-----------|-----------------|
| Evaluation | $\lambda, O$ | $P(O \mid \lambda)$ | Forward / Forward-Backward | $O(N^2 T)$ |
| Decoding | $\lambda, O$ | Best state sequence $Q^*$ | Viterbi | $O(N^2 T)$ |
| Learning | $O$ (unlabeled) | $\lambda = (A, B, \Pi)$ | Baum-Welch (EM) | $O(N^2 T \cdot \text{iter})$ |
| Posterior Inference | $\lambda, O$ | $P(q_t = s_i \mid O, \lambda)$ | Forward + Backward | $O(N^2 T)$ |

> [!NOTE]

The **best path** is found by tracing backpointers from the highest-scoring terminal node.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Explain the three fundamental assumptions of a Hidden Markov Model. Why are they necessary?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The three assumptions of an HMM are:
>
> **(i) Markov Assumption (1st order):** The probability of transitioning to a state at time $t$ depends *only* on the current state at time $t-1$, not on the entire history:
>
> $$P(q_t \mid q_{1:t-1}) = P(q_t \mid q_{t-1})$$
>
> **(ii) Output Independence Assumption:** The observation emitted at time $t$ depends *only* on the current state, not on previous states or observations:
>
> $$P(o_t \mid q_{1:t}, o_{1:t-1}) = P(o_t \mid q_t)$$
>
> **(iii) Stationarity Assumption:** The transition and emission probabilities do not change with time, i.e., $a_{ij}$ and $b_j(k)$ are time-invariant.
>
> These assumptions are *necessary* because they reduce the parameter space from exponential to polynomial, making inference algorithms (Forward, Viterbi, Baum-Welch) computationally tractable in $O(N^2 T)$ time instead of the infeasible $O(N^T)$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State and briefly explain the three canonical problems of HMMs.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> 1. **Evaluation Problem:** Given model $\lambda = (A, B, \Pi)$ and observation sequence $O$, efficiently compute $P(O \mid \lambda)$. *Algorithm used: Forward / Forward-Backward.*
> 2. **Decoding Problem:** Given $\lambda$ and $O$, find the most likely hidden state sequence $Q^* = \arg\max_Q P(Q \mid O, \lambda)$. *Algorithm used: Viterbi.*
> 3. **Learning Problem:** Given only $O$, find $\lambda$ that maximizes $P(O \mid \lambda)$. *Algorithm used: Baum-Welch (Expectation-Maximization).*
>
> These three problems are the foundation of all HMM-based applications in NLP including POS tagging, speech recognition, and named-entity recognition.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice** — Students must answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B. Each question carries **14 marks** split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Show all derivation steps and intermediate matrix values to secure full marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]*

**(a)** Define a Hidden Markov Model. Explain its five components with reference to Part-of-Speech tagging. State the two independence assumptions and write the joint probability of an observation sequence and a state sequence.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> A *Hidden Markov Model* is a probabilistic sequence model in which the system being modeled is assumed to be a Markov process with unobserved (hidden) states.
>
> In POS tagging, the **hidden states** are the POS tags (e.g., NN, VB, JJ) and the **observations** are the words of the sentence.
>
> **Five components:**
>
> | Symbol | Name | Meaning in POS Tagging |
> |--------|------|------------------------|
> | $S = \{s_1, \ldots, s_N\}$ | Hidden states | Set of $N$ POS tags |
> | $K = \{k_1, \ldots, k_M\}$ | Observation alphabet | Vocabulary of $M$ words |
> | $\Pi = \{\pi_i\}$ | Initial state distribution | Probability a sentence *begins* with tag $i$ |
> | $A = \{a_{ij}\}$ | Transition matrix | $P(\text{tag}_j \mid \text{tag}_i)$ — e.g., P(VB $\mid$ NN) |
> | $B = \{b_j(k)\}$ | Emission matrix | $P(\text{word} = k \mid \text{tag} = j)$ — e.g., P("flies" $\mid$ VB) |
>
> **Independence assumptions:**
>
> **(i) Markov Assumption:**
>
> $$P(q_t \mid q_{1:t-1}) = P(q_t \mid q_{t-1})$$
>
> **(ii) Output Independence:**
>
> $$P(o_t \mid q_{1:t}, o_{1:t-1}) = P(o_t \mid q_t)$$
>
> **Joint probability of state and observation sequence:**
>
> $$
> P(O, Q \mid \lambda) = \pi_{q_1} \cdot b_{q_1}(o_1) \cdot \prod_{t=2}^{T} a_{q_{t-1}, q_t} \cdot b_{q_t}(o_t)
> $$

**(b)** For the HMM defined below, use the **Viterbi algorithm** to find the most likely state sequence for the observation sequence $O = (\text{ran}, \text{quickly})$. Show all intermediate computations, backpointers, and the backtrace step.

- States: $s_1$ = Noun, $s_2$ = Verb
- Initial: $\pi_1 = 0.6$, $\pi_2 = 0.4$
- Transition: $A = \begin{pmatrix} 0.4 & 0.6 \\ 0.7 & 0.3 \end{pmatrix}$
- Emission (col 1 = "ran", col 2 = "quickly"): $B = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5 & 0.5 \\ 0.8 & 0.2 \end{pmatrix}$

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Initialization ($t = 1$, word = "ran"):**

$$
V_1(s_1) = 0.6 \times 0.5 = 0.30, \quad B_1(s_1) = 0
$$

$$
V_1(s_2) = 0.4 \times 0.8 = 0.32, \quad B_1(s_2) = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Recursion ($t = 2$, word = "quickly"):**

For $s_1$ (Noun):

$$
V_2(s_1) = \max\{V_1(s_1) \cdot a_{11},\ V_1(s_2) \cdot a_{21}\} \cdot b_1(\text{quickly}) = \max\{0.30 \times 0.4,\ 0.32 \times 0.7\} \times 0.5
$$

$$
= \max\{0.120,\ 0.224\} \times 0.5 = 0.224 \times 0.5 = 0.112
$$

Backpointer: $B_2(s_1) = s_2$ (came from Verb).

For $s_2$ (Verb):

$$
V_2(s_2) = \max\{0.30 \times 0.6,\ 0.32 \times 0.3\} \times 0.2 = \max\{0.180,\ 0.096\} \times 0.2 = 0.180 \times 0.2 = 0.036
$$

Backpointer: $B_2(s_2) = s_1$ (came from Noun).

**Step 3 — Termination:**

$$
P^* = \max\{V_2(s_1),\ V_2(s_2)\} = \max\{0.112,\ 0.036\} = 0.112
$$

$$
q_2^* = \arg\max = s_1\ (\text{Noun})
$$

**Step 4 — Backtrace:**

$$
q_1^* = B_2(q_2^*) = B_2(s_1) = s_2
$$

**Final most likely state sequence:** $Q^* = (s_2, s_1) = \textbf{(Verb, Noun)}$

The sentence is tagged as **"ran/Verb quickly/Noun"** with optimal score **0.112**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]*

**(a)** Explain the **Forward algorithm** of an HMM with its complete mathematical formulation. How does it solve the evaluation problem in $O(N^2 T)$ time instead of $O(N^T)$?

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> The evaluation problem requires computing $P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{Q} P(O, Q \mid \lambda)$, where the sum runs over all $N^T$ possible state sequences. The naive approach takes $O(N^T)$ time — *intractable* even for moderate $T$.
>
> **Key Insight:** We can use *dynamic programming* by defining the *forward variable*:
>
> $$\alpha_t(j) = P(o_1, o_2, \ldots, o_t,\ q_t = s_j \mid \lambda)$$
>
> **Step 1 — Initialization** ($t = 1$):
>
> $$\alpha_1(j) = \pi_j \cdot b_j(o_1), \quad 1 \leq j \leq N$$
>
> **Step 2 — Induction** ($1 < t \leq T$):
>
> $$\alpha_t(j) = \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij} \right] \cdot b_j(o_t)$$
>
> The inner sum is the total probability of being in *any* state at $t-1$ and then transitioning to $s_j$. Multiplying by $b_j(o_t)$ yields the joint probability of the partial observation sequence and ending in $s_j$.
>
> **Step 3 — Termination:**
>
> $$P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_T(j)$$
>
> **Complexity Analysis:**
>
> | Operation | Cost per step | Total |
> |-----------|---------------|-------|
> | Inner sum (over $i$) | $O(N)$ | $O(N)$ |
> | Outer loop (over $j$) | $O(N)$ | $O(N)$ |
> | Time loop (over $t$) | $O(1)$ | $O(T)$ |
> | **Total** | — | $\mathbf{O(N^2 T)}$ |
>
> Compared to $O(N^T)$ for the brute-force method, the forward algorithm achieves an **exponential speedup**, making it feasible for real applications such as continuous speech recognition where $T$ can be thousands.

**(b)** Using the **Backward algorithm**, derive the formula for $P(O \mid \lambda)$ starting from the suffix-based recursive definition. Show that the forward and backward formulations yield the same total probability.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> Define the backward variable:
>
> $$\beta_t(i) = P(o_{t+1}, o_{t+2}, \ldots, o_T \mid q_t = s_i, \lambda)$$
>
> **Step 1 — Initialization:** Since there are no future observations beyond $T$:
>
> $$\beta_T(i) = 1, \quad \forall\ 1 \leq i \leq N$$
>
> **Step 2 — Induction:**
>
> $$\beta_t(i) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} a_{ij} \cdot b_j(o_{t+1}) \cdot \beta_{t+1}(j)$$
>
> This is the sum over all possible next states $s_j$, each weighted by its transition probability $a_{ij}$, the probability of emitting $o_{t+1}$ from $s_j$, and the suffix probability $\beta_{t+1}(j)$.
>
> **Step 3 — Termination:** To obtain the total likelihood, we sum over all possible starting states:
>
> $$P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \pi_i \cdot b_i(o_1) \cdot \beta_1(i)$$
>
> **Equivalence proof:** We can show both methods give the same answer by considering the factorization:
>
> $$P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} P(o_1, \ldots, o_t,\ q_t = s_i \mid \lambda) \cdot P(o_{t+1}, \ldots, o_T \mid q_t = s_i, \lambda) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_t(i) \cdot \beta_t(i)$$
>
> For $t = T$, this gives $P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{i} \alpha_T(i)$ (since $\beta_T(i) = 1$), which is the forward termination. For $t = 1$, it gives the backward termination. Hence:
>
> $$\boxed{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_T(i) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_t(i) \cdot \beta_t(i) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \pi_i \cdot b_i(o_1) \cdot \beta_1(i) = P(O \mid \lambda)}$$
>
> This dual formulation is the foundation of the **Baum-Welch re-estimation equations** used in unsupervised parameter learning.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls**
