> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by the emission probability $b_j(o_t)$ in the Viterbi recursion.** The max-product form is $\max_i[V_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij}] \cdot b_j(o_t)$. Students frequently omit the trailing $b_j$ and lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing $\arg\max$ with $\max$ in the backpointer step.** The backpointer $B_t(j)$ stores the index $i$ that *produced* the maximum — not the maximum value itself.
> 3. **Writing $P(O \mid \lambda)$ without showing the recurrence derivation.** KTU examiners require both the *recursion* and the *termination* step. Just stating the final formula costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Using brute-force summation** ($N^T$ terms) instead of the dynamic-programming forward variable — this shows conceptual misunderstanding and loses 3–4 marks.
> 5. **Omitting the backtrace step in Viterbi.** The decoded sequence is incomplete without the back-pointer traversal.
> 6. **Failing to mention underflow prevention** via log-space computation — penalised in 14-mark questions for missing practical detail.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Use of HMMs in Computational Linguistics**

- ✅ An HMM is defined by the 5-tuple $\lambda = (S, K, \Pi, A, B)$.
- ✅ The two foundational assumptions are **Markov-1** and **Output Independence**.
- ✅ The **three canonical problems** are Evaluation, Decoding, and Learning.
- ✅ **Evaluation → Forward Algorithm** → computes $P(O \mid \lambda)$ in $O(N^2 T)$.
- ✅ **Decoding → Viterbi Algorithm** → finds the *single best* state sequence in $O(N^2 T)$.
- ✅ **Learning → Baum-Welch (EM)** → iteratively re-estimates $A$, $B$, and $\Pi$.
- ✅ The **Viterbi recursion** uses *max-product* (not sum-product): $V_t(j) = \max_i [V_{t-1}(i) a_{ij}] b_j(o_t)$.
- ✅ The **Forward recursion** uses *sum-product*: $\alpha_t(j) = \sum_i \alpha_{t-1}(i) a_{ij} \cdot b_j(o_t)$.
- ✅ The **backpointer** $B_t(j)$ stores the predecessor state that maximises the path score.
- ✅ The **termination** step of Viterbi is $q_T^* = \arg\max_j V_T(j)$.
- ✅ The **total likelihood** equals both $\sum_j \alpha_T(j)$ and $\sum_i \pi_i b_i(o_1) \beta_1(i)$.
- ✅ Major CL applications: **POS tagging, Speech Recognition, NER, Word Alignment, Text Segmentation**.
- ✅ The **forward-backward** combination enables computation of *posterior* probabilities $\gamma_t(i)$ and $\xi_t(i,j)$.
- ✅ The **Baum-Welch re-estimation** of $A$ is $\hat{a}_{ij} = \sum_t \xi_t(i,j) / \sum_t \gamma_t(i)$.
- ✅ The **Baum-Welch re-estimation** of $B$ is $\hat{b}_j(k) = \sum_{t: o_t = k} \gamma_t(j) / \sum_t \gamma_t(j)$.
- ✅ Always perform computations in **log-space** to avoid numerical underflow.
- ✅ HMMs are **generative** models — they model $P(O, Q)$, not $P(Q \mid O)$ directly.
- ✅ The HMM tagger is a *generative* counterpart to the *discriminative* **MEMM** and **CRF** taggers that succeeded it.
- ✅ Standard HMM complexity for POS tagging: $N = 36$ tags, $T \approx 20$ words per sentence → $\sim 25{,}920$ operations per sentence.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Form of HMM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** General Form of HMM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

A **Hidden Markov Model (HMM)** is a statistical Markov model in which the system being modeled is assumed to be a Markov process with unobserved (i.e., hidden) states. In the general form, an HMM is formally defined as a 5-tuple (or equivalently, a 3-tuple of parameter sets):

$$\lambda = (S, V, A, B, \pi)$$

where:
- $S = \{S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_N\}$ is the set of **N hidden states**.
- $V = \{v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_M\}$ is the set of **M possible observation symbols** (the alphabet of the observed output).
- $A = \{a_{ij}\}$ is the **state transition probability matrix**, of dimension $N \times N$.
- $B = \{b_j(k)\}$ is the **observation emission probability matrix**, of dimension $N \times M$.
- $\pi = \{\pi_i\}$ is the **initial state distribution** vector of length $N$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compact Representation:** For most practical and exam purposes, an HMM is succinctly represented as a single parameter set:
> $$\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$$
> This compact form is what is referred to as the **"General Form of HMM"** in the KTU 2024 Computational Linguistics syllabus.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **spy standing outside a sealed, windowless room** (the "hidden world"). You cannot see the person inside, but you can hear them *speak* words. You also cannot see them *walk* between rooms, but you hear the door open and close. The spy has to guess the *invisible* path of the person (hidden states) using only the *visible* words (observations).

- The **hidden states** = which room the person is in (e.g., Kitchen, Library, Bedroom) — *you cannot see this*.
- The **observations** = the words you hear (e.g., "cold," "book," "sleepy") — *you hear this*.
- The **transition probabilities ($a_{ij}$)** = the likelihood of moving from one room to another.
- The **emission probabilities ($b_j(k)$)** = the likelihood of hearing a particular word *while* the person is in a particular room.
- The **initial distribution ($\pi$)** = where you believe the person started.

The "Markov" property means: the next room depends **only on the current room** (memoryless), not on the full history of past rooms. The "Hidden" part means: you only hear words, never see the room.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** HMMs excel at modeling **sequential data** where the underlying generative process is invisible but produces observable signals. In Computational Linguistics, this is foundational to **Part-of-Speech (POS) Tagging, Speech Recognition, Named Entity Recognition, and Word Alignment** in Machine Translation.

### Standard Metrics in HMMs

- **Number of hidden states ($N$):** A primary hyperparameter of the model.
- **Number of observation symbols ($M$):** The size of the output vocabulary.
- **Sequence length ($T$):** The number of time steps in the observation sequence.
- **Joint probability scaling convention:** All probabilities are normalized such that $\sum_{j=1}^{N} a_{ij} = 1$ for each row of $A$, and $\sum_{k=1}^{M} b_j(k) = 1$ for each state $j$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Core Markov Assumptions

The General Form of HMM rests on **two simplifying assumptions** that make computation tractable. KTU examiners frequently test these:

**Assumption 1 — Markov (Memoryless) Assumption:**
The probability of transitioning to a new hidden state depends *only* on the *current* hidden state, not on the entire history.

$$P(q_{t+1} \mid q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_t, o_1, \ldots, o_t) = P(q_{t+1} \mid q_t)$$

**Assumption 2 — Output Independence Assumption:**
The observation at time $t$ depends *only* on the hidden state at time $t$, not on earlier states or observations.

$$P(o_t \mid q_1, \ldots, q_t, o_1, \ldots, o_{t-1}, o_{t+1}, \ldots, o_T) = P(o_t \mid q_t)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These assumptions are **simplifications** (often violated in real language data), but they make the math tractable and form the philosophical foundation of HMMs. KTU questions sometimes ask students to **critically evaluate** these assumptions.

### 2.2 The Three Parameter Sets (A, B, π) — Deep Dive

#### (a) State Transition Probability Matrix — $A$
- **Dimension:** $N \times N$
- **Element:** $a_{ij} = P(q_{t+1} = S_j \mid q_t = S_i)$ — the probability of moving from state $S_i$ to state $S_j$.
- **Row-stochastic constraint:**
$$\sum_{j=1}^{N} a_{ij} = 1, \quad \forall i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, N\}$$
- **Interpretation:** Each row of $A$ is a valid probability distribution over the next state.

#### (b) Observation Emission Probability Matrix — $B$
- **Dimension:** $N \times M$
- **Element:** $b_j(k) = P(o_t = v_k \mid q_t = S_j)$ — the probability of *emitting* observation symbol $v_k$ while in state $S_j$.
- **Row-stochastic constraint:**
$$\sum_{k=1}^{M} b_j(k) = 1, \quad \forall j \in \{1, 2, \ldots, N\}$$

#### (c) Initial State Distribution — $\pi$
- **Dimension:** Vector of length $N$
- **Element:** $\pi_i = P(q_1 = S_i)$ — the probability that the system *starts* in state $S_i$.
- **Constraint:**
$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} \pi_i = 1$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Name | Dimension | Formal Definition | Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\lambda$ | HMM parameter set | — | $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$ | — |
| $N$ | Number of hidden states | Scalar | $\vert S \vert$ | $N \ge 1$ |
| $M$ | Number of observation symbols | Scalar | $\vert V \vert$ | $M \ge 1$ |
| $T$ | Length of observation sequence | Scalar | $T = \vert O \vert$ | $T \ge 1$ |
| $S$ | Set of hidden states | Set of size $N$ | $\{S_1, \ldots, S_N\}$ | — |
| $V$ | Set of observation symbols | Set of size $M$ | $\{v_1, \ldots, v_M\}$ | — |
| $A$ | Transition matrix | $N \times N$ | $a_{ij} = P(q_{t+1}=S_j \mid q_t=S_i)$ | $\sum_j a_{ij} = 1$ |
| $B$ | Emission matrix | $N \times M$ | $b_j(k) = P(o_t=v_k \mid q_t=S_j)$ | $\sum_k b_j(k) = 1$ |
| $\pi$ | Initial distribution | Vector of length $N$ | $\pi_i = P(q_1=S_i)$ | $\sum_i \pi_i = 1$ |
| $O$ | Observation sequence | Vector of length $T$ | $O = (o_1, o_2, \ldots, o_T)$ | — |
| $Q$ | Hidden state sequence | Vector of length $T$ | $Q = (q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_T)$ | — |
| $O \vert \lambda$ | Sequence given model | — | $P(O \vert \lambda)$ | computed via Forward |
| $Q^* \vert O, \lambda$ | Best state sequence | — | $\arg\max_Q P(Q \vert O, \lambda)$ | computed via Viterbi |

### 2.4 The Three Fundamental Problems of HMMs (KTU High-Yield)

For any given KTU exam question, you will be asked to **describe, formulate, or solve** one of these three canonical problems. Memorize the structure:

**Problem 1 — Evaluation (Likelihood):**
Given $O = (o_1, \ldots, o_T)$ and $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$, compute $P(O \mid \lambda)$.
- **Algorithm:** Forward-Backward Procedure.
- **Why it matters:** Lets us score how well a model fits a sequence (used in speech recognition to pick the best model).

**Problem 2 — Decoding:**
Given $O$ and $\lambda$, find the optimal hidden state sequence $Q^* = (q_1^*, \ldots, q_T^*)$.
- **Algorithm:** Viterbi Algorithm (dynamic programming).
- **Why it matters:** POS tagging — given words (observations), find the tag sequence (hidden states).

**Problem 3 — Learning (Training):**
Given only $O$, find $\lambda^* = \arg\max_{\lambda} P(O \mid \lambda)$ — the model parameters that best explain the observations.
- **Algorithm:** Baum-Welch Algorithm (a special case of Expectation-Maximization).
- **Why it matters:** Training an HMM from unlabeled data (unsupervised learning).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **Computational Linguistics** and adjacent engineering fields, HMMs power:

- **Speech Recognition Systems** (e.g., early versions of Google Voice, Dragon NaturallySpeaking).
- **POS Tagging** in NLP pipelines (e.g., the HMM-based TnT tagger).
- **Bioinformatics** — gene prediction, protein sequence alignment, profile HMMs (used in Pfam, BLAST).
- **Handwriting Recognition** (used in check-processing systems).
- **Cryptanalysis and Time-Series Analysis.**

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Joint Probability of an Observation-State Pair

The most fundamental derivation in HMMs is computing the joint probability of an observation sequence $O = (o_1, o_2, \ldots, o_T)$ **given** a specific hidden state sequence $Q = (q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_T)$.

**Starting Point:** The joint probability of the state sequence AND the observation sequence is:

$$P(O, Q \mid \lambda) = P(O \mid Q, \lambda) \cdot P(Q \mid \lambda)$$

We expand this into a product of per-time-step terms using the two Markov assumptions.

**Step 1 — Expand the state-sequence probability $P(Q \mid \lambda)$:**

By the chain rule of probability and the Markov assumption:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(Q \mid \lambda) &= P(q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_T \mid \lambda) \\
&= P(q_1) \cdot P(q_2 \mid q_1) \cdot P(q_3 \mid q_2) \cdots P(q_T \mid q_{T-1}) \\
&= \pi_{q_1} \cdot \prod_{t=1}^{T-1} a_{q_t, q_{t+1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The first state is drawn from $\pi$, and each subsequent transition uses row $a_{q_t}$ of matrix $A$.

**Step 2 — Expand the observation probability $P(O \mid Q, \lambda)$:**

By the output-independence assumption, each observation depends only on the *current* state:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(O \mid Q, \lambda) &= P(o_1, o_2, \ldots, o_T \mid q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_T, \lambda) \\
&= P(o_1 \mid q_1) \cdot P(o_2 \mid q_2) \cdots P(o_T \mid q_T) \\
&= \prod_{t=1}^{T} b_{q_t}(o_t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Combine the two products:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(O, Q \mid \lambda) &= P(Q \mid \lambda) \cdot P(O \mid Q, \lambda) \\
&= \pi_{q_1} \cdot \prod_{t=1}^{T-1} a_{q_t, q_{t+1}} \cdot \prod_{t=1}^{T} b_{q_t}(o_t)
\end{aligned}
$$

> This is the **most-tested equation** in KTU HMM questions. Memorize the structure: one initial term, a chain of transitions, and a chain of emissions.

### 3.2 Derivation: Likelihood of an Observation Sequence (Marginalization)

To compute the **likelihood** $P(O \mid \lambda)$ (Problem 1), we marginalize out the unknown state sequence by summing over **all possible** $Q \in \mathcal{Q}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(O \mid \lambda) &= \sum_{Q \in \mathcal{Q}} P(O, Q \mid \lambda) \\
&= \sum_{Q \in \mathcal{Q}} \pi_{q_1} b_{q_1}(o_1) \prod_{t=1}^{T-1} a_{q_t, q_{t+1}} b_{q_{t+1}}(o_{t+1})
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Why this is computationally infeasible:** The number of possible state sequences is $N^T$. For $N = 5$ and $T = 100$, this is $5^{100} \approx 7.8 \times 10^{69}$ sequences — astronomically large. This motivates the **Forward Algorithm**, which exploits dynamic programming.

### 3.3 Derivation: The Forward Variable (Motivation for Forward Algorithm)

Define the **forward variable** $\alpha_t(j)$ as the probability of observing the partial sequence $o_1, o_2, \ldots, o_t$ **and** being in state $S_j$ at time $t$:

$$\alpha_t(j) = P(o_1, o_2, \ldots, o_t, q_t = S_j \mid \lambda)$$

**Step 1 — Initialization (Base Case) at $t = 1$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_1(j) &= P(o_1, q_1 = S_j \mid \lambda) \\
&= P(o_1 \mid q_1 = S_j) \cdot P(q_1 = S_j) \\
&= b_j(o_1) \cdot \pi_j
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Recursive Induction (the heart of the algorithm):**

To compute $\alpha_{t+1}(j)$, we consider *all* possible previous states $S_i$ that could have led to $S_j$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_{t+1}(j) &= P(o_1, \ldots, o_{t+1}, q_{t+1} = S_j \mid \lambda) \\
&= \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_t(i) \cdot a_{ij} \right] \cdot b_j(o_{t+1})
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** First, we are in some state $S_i$ at time $t$ (prob $\alpha_t(i)$). We transition to $S_j$ (prob $a_{ij}$). We sum over all $i$ (marginalization). Then we emit $o_{t+1}$ from $S_j$ (prob $b_j(o_{t+1})$).

**Step 3 — Termination:** The full likelihood is obtained by summing over all possible final states:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(O \mid \lambda) &= \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_T(j) \\
&= \sum_{j=1}^{N} P(o_1, \ldots, o_T, q_T = S_j \mid \lambda)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Complexity Note:** The forward algorithm reduces complexity from $O(N^T \cdot T)$ to $O(N^2 \cdot T)$ — a massive computational savings and the central engineering insight of HMMs.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Three HMM Problems Summary

| Problem | Formal Statement | Algorithm | Time Complexity | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Evaluation** | Compute $P(O \mid \lambda)$ | Forward-Backward | $O(N^2 T)$ | A scalar probability |
| **Decoding** | Find $\arg\max_Q P(Q \mid O, \lambda)$ | Viterbi | $O(N^2 T)$ | Optimal state sequence $Q^*$ |
| **Learning** | Find $\arg\max_\lambda P(O \mid \lambda)$ | Baum-Welch (EM) | $O(N^2 T)$ per iteration | New parameters $\lambda^*$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Unrolled (Time-Expanded) HMM as a Trellis Diagram

The most common KTU-asked diagram is the **trellis** that unfolds the HMM across time steps $t = 1, 2, 3, \ldots, T$. Each column is a time step; each node is a state; each edge is a transition.

```mermaid
graph LR
    T1_S1["t1 S1"] --> T2_S1["t2 S1"]
    T1_S1 --> T2_S2["t2 S2"]
    T1_S1 --> T2_S3["t2 S3"]
    T1_S2["t1 S2"] --> T2_S1["t2 S1"]
    T1_S2 --> T2_S2["t2 S2"]
    T1_S2 --> T2_S3["t2 S3"]
    T1_S3["t1 S3"] --> T2_S1["t2 S1"]
    T1_S3 --> T2_S2["t2 S2"]
    T1_S3 --> T2_S3["t2 S3"]
    T2_S1 --> T3_S1["t3 S1"]
    T2_S1 --> T3_S2["t3 S2"]
    T2_S1 --> T3_S3["t3 S3"]
    T2_S2 --> T3_S1["t3 S1"]
    T2_S2 --> T3_S2["t3 S2"]
    T2_S2 --> T3_S3["t3 S3"]
    T2_S3 --> T3_S1["t3 S1"]
    T2_S3 --> T3_S2["t3 S2"]
    T2_S3 --> T3_S3["t3 S3"]
    OBS1(("o1")) -.-> T1_S1
    OBS1 -.-> T1_S2
    OBS1 -.-> T1_S3
    OBS2(("o2")) -.-> T2_S1
    OBS2 -.-> T2_S2
    OBS2 -.-> T2_S3
    OBS3(("o3")) -.-> T3_S1
    OBS3 -.-> T3_S2
    OBS3 -.-> T3_S3
    style OBS1 fill:#ffd,stroke:#333
    style OBS2 fill:#ffd,stroke:#333
    style OBS3 fill:#ffd,stroke:#333
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows are **transitions** $a_{ij}$ (state-to-state). Dotted arrows are **emissions** $b_j(k)$ (state-to-observation). The yellow circles labeled $o_1, o_2, o_3$ are the *observed* symbols; the boxes are the *hidden* states.

### 4.2 Compact / Block-Diagram View of the General Form

When asked to draw the **"General Form of HMM"** in 2 marks, KTU expects the compact three-box representation:

```mermaid
graph LR
    PI["pi = Initial State Distribution"] --> A["A = Transition Matrix NxN"]
    A --> B["B = Emission Matrix NxM"]
    B --> OUT["Output Sequence O = o1 o2 ... oT"]
    PI --> H1["Hidden States Q = q1 q2 ... qT"]
    A --> H1
    B --> H1
    H1 --> OUT
    style PI fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#333
    style A fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333
    style H1 fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#333
    style OUT fill:#d9d2e9,stroke:#333
```

> **Color Legend:** Blue = Initial distribution. Green = Transition. Yellow = Emission. Red = Hidden states. Purple = Observed output.

### 4.3 Functional Block Architecture: HMM Inference Pipeline

```mermaid
graph TD
    INPUT["INPUT: Observation Sequence O and Model lambda"] --> FORWARD["Forward Algorithm: Compute alpha_t"]
    FORWARD --> DECISION{"What is the goal?"}
    DECISION -->|"Likelihood P(O|lambda)"| EVAL["Evaluation Output: Scalar Probability"]
    DECISION -->|"Best State Sequence"| VITERBI["Viterbi Algorithm: Find Q*"]
    DECISION -->|"Update Parameters"| BAUMWELCH["Baum-Welch EM: New lambda*"]
    VITERBI --> DECODE["Decoding Output: State Path"]
    BAUMWELCH --> TRAIN["Learning Output: Trained Model"]
    EVAL --> DONE["Done"]
    DECODE --> DONE
    TRAIN --> DONE
    style INPUT fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#333
    style FORWARD fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333
    style VITERBI fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#333
    style BAUMWELCH fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#333
    style DONE fill:#d9d2e9,stroke:#333
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the general form of a Hidden Markov Model. List and briefly explain its five components.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A Hidden Markov Model (HMM) is a doubly stochastic process used to model sequential data. Its **general form** is the 5-tuple:

$$\lambda = (S, V, A, B, \pi)$$

**(1) $S = \{S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_N\}$:** The set of **$N$ hidden states**. These are unobservable but influence the outputs.

**(2) $V = \{v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_M\}$:** The set of **$M$ observation symbols** (alphabet of observed output).

**(3) $A = [a_{ij}]$:** The **state transition probability matrix** of size $N \times N$, where $a_{ij} = P(q_{t+1} = S_j \mid q_t = S_i)$.

**(4) $B = [b_j(k)]$:** The **observation emission probability matrix** of size $N \times M$, where $b_j(k) = P(o_t = v_k \mid q_t = S_j)$.

**(5) $\pi = [\pi_i]$:** The **initial state distribution**, where $\pi_i = P(q_1 = S_i)$.

Compactly, this is written as $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$.

---

#### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State and explain the two key assumptions of a Hidden Markov Model.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

HMMs rest on **two simplifying assumptions** that make inference tractable:

**(1) Markov (Memoryless) Assumption:** The future state depends only on the *current* state, not on the history:

$$P(q_{t+1} \mid q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_t) = P(q_{t+1} \mid q_t)$$

**(2) Output Independence Assumption:** The observation at time $t$ depends only on the hidden state at time $t$, and is conditionally independent of all other observations and past states:

$$P(o_t \mid q_1, \ldots, q_T, o_1, \ldots, o_{t-1}) = P(o_t \mid q_t)$$

These assumptions reduce the joint probability to a tractable product of per-step transition and emission terms.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the general form of an HMM. Define all its parameters clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the joint probability $P(O, Q \mid \lambda)$ for an observation sequence $O = (o_1, o_2, o_3)$ and a state sequence $Q = (q_1, q_2, q_3)$. Discuss why direct marginalization is computationally infeasible. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — General Form of HMM (7 Marks):**

A Hidden Markov Model is a 5-tuple:

$$\lambda = (S, V, A, B, \pi)$$

**Parameter definitions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \{S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_N\} &\text{(set of N hidden states)} \\
V &= \{v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_M\} &\text{(set of M observation symbols)} \\
A &= [a_{ij}]_{N \times N}, \quad a_{ij} = P(q_{t+1} = S_j \mid q_t = S_i) &\text{(transition matrix)} \\
B &= [b_j(k)]_{N \times M}, \quad b_j(k) = P(o_t = v_k \mid q_t = S_j) &\text{(emission matrix)} \\
\pi &= [\pi_i]_{1 \times N}, \quad \pi_i = P(q_1 = S_i) &\text{(initial distribution)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    PI["pi: Initial Prob"] --> A["A: Transition NxN"]
    A --> B["B: Emission NxM"]
    PI --> Q["Q: Hidden States q1 q2 ... qT"]
    A --> Q
    B --> Q
    B --> O["O: Observations o1 o2 ... oT"]
    Q --> O
    style PI fill:#cfe2f3
    style A fill:#d9ead3
    style B fill:#fff2cc
    style Q fill:#f4cccc
    style O fill:#d9d2e9
```

**Part (b) — Joint Probability Derivation (7 Marks):**

For $O = (o_1, o_2, o_3)$ and $Q = (q_1, q_2, q_3)$, the joint probability is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(O, Q \mid \lambda) &= P(q_1) \cdot P(q_2 \mid q_1) \cdot P(q_3 \mid q_2) \cdot P(o_1 \mid q_1) \cdot P(o_2 \mid q_2) \cdot P(o_3 \mid q_3) \\
&= \pi_{q_1} \cdot a_{q_1 q_2} \cdot a_{q_2 q_3} \cdot b_{q_1}(o_1) \cdot b_{q_2}(o_2) \cdot b_{q_3}(o_3) \\
&= \pi_{q_1} \, b_{q_1}(o_1) \cdot a_{q_1 q_2} \, b_{q_2}(o_2) \cdot a_{q_2 q_3} \, b_{q_3}(o_3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why direct marginalization is infeasible:**

To compute $P(O \mid \lambda)$, we must sum over **all** possible state sequences $Q$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(O \mid \lambda) &= \sum_{Q \in \mathcal{Q}} P(O, Q \mid \lambda)
\end{aligned}
$$

The number of possible state sequences is $N^T$. For typical values $N = 50$ (POS tags), $T = 100$ (sentence length), this gives $50^{100} \approx 10^{170}$ sequences — far beyond any computer's capacity. This motivates the **Forward Algorithm** with complexity $O(N^2 T)$.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define the forward variable $\alpha_t(j)$. Derive its initialization and recursive formula. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** State and explain the three fundamental problems of HMMs. For each problem, name the algorithm used to solve it. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Forward Variable Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Definition:**

$$\alpha_t(j) = P(o_1, o_2, \ldots, o_t, q_t = S_j \mid \lambda)$$

This is the probability of observing the partial sequence $o_1 \ldots o_t$ **and** being in state $S_j$ at time $t$.

**Initialization ($t = 1$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_1(j) &= P(o_1, q_1 = S_j \mid \lambda) \\
&= P(o_1 \mid q_1 = S_j) \cdot P(q_1 = S_j) \\
&= b_j(o_1) \cdot \pi_j, \quad \text{for } j = 1, 2, \ldots, N
\end{aligned}
$$

**Recursive Step ($1 \le t \le T-1$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_{t+1}(j) &= \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_t(i) \cdot a_{ij} \right] \cdot b_j(o_{t+1})
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Sum over all previous states $S_i$, weighted by the transition probability $a_{ij}$, then multiply by the emission probability of $o_{t+1}$ from $S_j$.

**Termination:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(O \mid \lambda) &= \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_T(j)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Three Fundamental Problems (7 Marks):**

**Problem 1 — Evaluation (Likelihood):**
- **Goal:** Compute $P(O \mid \lambda)$ — the probability of the observation sequence given the model.
- **Algorithm:** **Forward-Backward Algorithm**.
- **Application:** Model selection (e.g., choosing the best language model in speech recognition).

**Problem 2 — Decoding:**
- **Goal:** Find the optimal state sequence $Q^* = \arg\max_Q P(Q \mid O, \lambda)$.
- **Algorithm:** **Viterbi Algorithm** (dynamic programming).
- **Application:** POS tagging (find best tag sequence for a sentence).

**Problem 3 — Learning (Training):**
- **Goal:** Adjust parameters $\lambda^* = \arg\max_\lambda P(O \mid \lambda)$ from observed data.
- **Algorithm:** **Baum-Welch Algorithm** (Expectation-Maximization).
- **Application:** Training an HMM from unannotated corpora.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing dimensions of $A$ and $B$:** $A$ is $N \times N$, $B$ is $N \times M$. Writing $A$ as $N \times M$ is a common 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Forgetting the row-stochastic constraint:** $A$ and $B$ are row-stochastic (rows sum to 1), NOT column-stochastic. Examiners check this explicitly.
> 3. **Mixing up the two Markov assumptions:** The Markov assumption is about *states* (next state depends on current state). The output independence assumption is about *observations* (observation depends on current state). They are **distinct** assumptions.
> 4. **Skipping the marginalization justification:** When asked *why* the forward algorithm is needed, do not just write the formula — you must explicitly state that direct summation has complexity $O(N^T)$, which is infeasible.
> 5. **Notation error in $\pi_{q_1}$ vs $\pi_1$:** Use $\pi_{q_1}$ (the initial probability of *the actual state* $q_1$) to be precise. Bare $\pi_1$ is ambiguous.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **General form:** $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$ is the compact KTU-required notation.
- **Five components:** $S$ (states), $V$ (observations), $A$ (transitions), $B$ (emissions), $\pi$ (initial distribution).
- **Markov assumption** governs state transitions; **output-independence assumption** governs emissions.
- **Joint probability** $P(O, Q \mid \lambda) = \pi_{q_1} b_{q_1}(o_1) \prod_{t=1}^{T-1} a_{q_t q_{t+1}} b_{q_{t+1}}(o_{t+1})$ — memorize this.
- **Likelihood requires marginalization** over all state paths — directly $O(N^T)$, hence the **Forward Algorithm** with $O(N^2 T)$.
- **Forward variable** $\alpha_t(j)$ is the workhorse of dynamic programming; initialization uses $\pi_j b_j(o_1)$; recursion multiplies sum of $\alpha_t(i) a_{ij}$ by $b_j(o_{t+1})$.
- **Three canonical problems:** Evaluation (Forward), Decoding (Viterbi), Learning (Baum-Welch).
- **Trellis diagram** is the visual representation KTU loves: time on horizontal axis, states as nodes, transitions as solid arrows, emissions as dotted arrows.
- **Real-world uses** in CL: POS tagging, speech recognition, NER, machine translation word alignment.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Probability of an Observation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Probability of an Observation # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the context of **Computational Linguistics** and **Statistical Language Modeling**, the **Probability of an Observation** refers to the likelihood of observing a specific sequence of observable events (such as words, phonemes, characters, or part-of-speech tags) given an underlying probabilistic model. The concept is most rigorously formulated in the framework of **Hidden Markov Models (HMMs)**, where we seek to compute: $$P(O \mid \lambda)$$ Here: - $O = (O_1, O_2, \ldots, O_T)$ is the **observation sequence** of length $T$ - $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$ is the **HMM parameter set**, where: - $A = \{a_{ij}\}$ is the **state transition probability matrix** - $B = \{b_j(k)\}$ is the **emission (observation) probability matrix** - $\pi = \{\pi_i\}$ is the **initial state distribution** > [!NOTE] > **Key Definition (Rabiner, 1989 — Canonical HMM Formulation)** > The probability of an observation sequence $O$ given the model $\lambda$ is the sum of the probabilities of all possible hidden state sequences $Q = (q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_T)$ that could have generated $O$, marginalized over the hidden variable: > $$P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{Q} P(O, Q \mid \lambda)$$ ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Underground Cave" Intuition Imagine you are a **detective** standing outside an underground cave system. You can **only hear sounds** (observations) coming from inside — echoes, dripping water, footsteps — but you **cannot see** the hidden people (states) inside. - The **observations** $O$ = sounds you hear (e.g., "$O_1$ = splash, $O_2$ = echo, $O_3$ = footstep") - The **hidden states** $Q$ = the actual person walking through Cave A, B, or C - The **model** $\lambda$ = your prior knowledge of how people typically move between caves ($A$) and how often they make each sound in each cave ($B$) Your task: **"How likely is it that I would have heard exactly THIS sequence of sounds?"** That likelihood is $P(O \mid \lambda)$. The naive way: enumerate every possible path of people through the caves. The smart way: use the **Forward Algorithm** — a dynamic programming trick that propagates probability forward through time, reusing intermediate results. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why is this problem non-trivial?** > The hidden states are **unobserved (latent)**. We must therefore *marginalize* (sum out) over **all** possible hidden state sequences. A naive enumeration requires $N^T$ computations (exponential in sequence length). The **Forward Algorithm** reduces this to $O(N^2 T)$ — a polynomial-time tractable solution. ## 1.3 Key Metrics & Standard Notations | Symbol | Meaning | Standard Range | |---|---|---| | $N$ | Number of hidden states | $N \geq 2$ | | $M$ | Number of distinct observation symbols | $M \geq 2$ | | $T$ | Length of observation sequence | $T \geq 1$ | | $a_{ij}$ | Transition prob: state $S_i \to S_j$ | $[0,1]$, $\sum_j a_{ij} = 1$ | | $b_j(k)$ | Emission prob of symbol $v_k$ in state $S_j$ | $[0,1]$, $\sum_k b_j(k) = 1$ | | $\pi_i$ | Initial prob of state $S_i$ | $[0,1]$, $\sum_i \pi_i = 1$ | --- > [!TIP] > **KTU 2024 Board Exam Quick-Fact:** Whenever a question asks "compute $P(O \mid \lambda)$", the examiner expects you to either (a) write the **naive summation** for small $T$ (max $T=3$), or (b) write and apply the **Forward Algorithm** recursion. Never confuse $P(O \mid \lambda)$ with the **decoding** problem $P(Q \mid O, \lambda)$ — they are mathematically distinct (one uses sum, the other uses max). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Canonical HMM Problems (Foundational Context) To firmly situate the "Probability of an Observation" problem, recall that any HMM is governed by three classical problems (Rabiner's taxonomy): | Problem | Goal | Algorithm | Output | |---|---|---|---| | **Problem 1 (Evaluation)** | Compute $P(O \mid \lambda)$ | **Forward / Backward Algorithm** | A scalar probability | | **Problem 2 (Decoding)** | Find best state sequence $\arg\max_Q P(Q \mid O, \lambda)$ | **Viterbi Algorithm** | A state sequence | | **Problem 3 (Learning)** | Adjust $\lambda$ to maximize $P(O \mid \lambda)$ | **Baum–Welch (EM)** | New $\lambda$ parameters | > Our focus in this topic is **Problem 1 — the Evaluation Problem**. ## 2.2 The Naive (Brute-Force) Approach For any fixed state sequence $Q = (q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_T)$ of length $T$, the joint probability of $O$ and $Q$ is: $$P(O, Q \mid \lambda) = \pi_{q_1} \cdot b_{q_1}(O_1) \cdot a_{q_1 q_2} \cdot b_{q_2}(O_2) \cdot a_{q_2 q_3} \cdots a_{q_{T-1} q_T} \cdot b_{q_T}(O_T)$$ Which can be compactly written as: $$P(O, Q \mid \lambda) = \pi_{q_1} \, b_{q_1}(O_1) \prod_{t=2}^{T} a_{q_{t-1} q_t} \, b_{q_t}(O_t)$$ To obtain $P(O \mid \lambda)$, we sum over **all** $N^T$ possible state sequences: $$\boxed{P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_T} \pi_{q_1} \, b_{q_1}(O_1) \prod_{t=2}^{T} a_{q_{t-1} q_t} \, b_{q_t}(O_t)}$$ - **Computational cost:** $O(2T \cdot N^T)$ — exponential. - **Practical limit:** Only feasible for $T \leq 10$ and $N \leq 3$ in exam settings. ## 2.3 The Forward Algorithm — Efficient Dynamic Programming The key insight: at time $t$, the probability of being in state $S_j$ **and** having produced observations $O_1, \ldots, O_t$ depends only on the previous time step. We define the **forward variable**: $$\alpha_t(i) = P(O_1, O_2, \ldots, O_t, \, q_t = S_i \mid \lambda)$$ ### Step-by-Step Logic 1. **Initialization ($t = 1$):** Probability of starting in state $S_i$ AND emitting $O_1$ from $S_i$. 2. **Induction ($t = 1 \to T-1$):** For each state $S_j$ at time $t+1$, sum the contributions from ALL predecessor states $S_i$ at time $t$, multiply by transition $a_{ij}$ and emission $b_j(O_{t+1})$. 3. **Termination ($t = T$):** Sum the forward variables over all $N$ possible final states. ### Recursion Formulas $$\alpha_1(i) = \pi_i \, b_i(O_1) \quad \text{for } 1 \leq i \leq N$$ $$\alpha_{t+1}(j) = \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_t(i) \, a_{ij} \right] b_j(O_{t+1}) \quad \text{for } 1 \leq j \leq N, \, 1 \leq t \leq T-1$$ $$P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_T(i)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why this works:** By the chain rule of probability and the Markov property, all paths to $S_j$ at time $t+1$ share a common substructure (the observation prefix $O_1 \ldots O_t$). Caching $\alpha_t(i)$ turns an exponential search into a polynomial one. ## 2.4 The Backward Algorithm (Companion Procedure) The **backward variable** $\beta_t(i)$ gives the probability of observing the **suffix** $O_{t+1}, \ldots, O_T$ given that we are in state $S_i$ at time $t$: $$\beta_t(i) = P(O_{t+1}, O_{t+2}, \ldots, O_T \mid q_t = S_i, \lambda)$$ **Initialization:** $\beta_T(i) = 1$ for all $i$. **Induction:** $\beta_t(i) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} a_{ij} \, b_j(O_{t+1}) \, \beta_{t+1}(j)$. **Equivalently:** $P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \pi_i \, b_i(O_1) \, \beta_1(i)$. ## 2.5 The KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD: Memorize this table — it appears verbatim in nearly every KTU HMM question.** | # | Formula | Name | Used For | |---|---|---|---| | F1 | $P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_Q P(O, Q \mid \lambda)$ | Marginal definition | Conceptual proof | | F2 | $P(O, Q \mid \lambda) = \pi_{q_1} b_{q_1}(O_1) \prod_{t=2}^{T} a_{q_{t-1}q_t} b_{q_t}(O_t)$ | Joint sequence probability | Naive computation | | F3 | $\alpha_1(i) = \pi_i b_i(O_1)$ | Forward init | Forward algorithm | | F4 | $\alpha_{t+1}(j) = \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_t(i) a_{ij} \right] b_j(O_{t+1})$ | Forward induction | Forward algorithm | | F5 | $P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_T(i)$ | Forward termination | Final answer | | F6 | $\beta_T(i) = 1$ | Backward init | Backward algorithm | | F7 | $\beta_t(i) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} a_{ij} b_j(O_{t+1}) \beta_{t+1}(j)$ | Backward induction | Backward algorithm | | F8 | $P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_t(i) \beta_t(i)$ | Mixed forward–backward | Smoothing (any $t$) | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Speech Recognition:** Computing $P(O \mid \lambda_{\text{word}})$ for acoustic word models. - **POS Tagging:** $P(\text{word sequence} \mid \text{Tag-HMM})$ — likelihood of a corpus given a tagger. - **Bioinformatics (Gene Finding):** $P(\text{DNA sequence} \mid \text{Gene-HMM})$. - **Handwriting Recognition, Machine Translation alignment, Anomaly Detection** in time-series. --- > [!TIP] > **Engineering Tip:** In production-grade HMM toolkits (HTK, Kaldi, HMMlearn), the Forward Algorithm is implemented in **log-space** (i.e., compute $\log \alpha_t(i)$) to avoid **numerical underflow** — probabilities drop to $\sim 10^{-300}$ for $T \geq 100$. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked-Out Derivation 1: From Joint to Marginal (Naive Method) **Given:** A 2-state HMM with $N = 2$ states $\{S_1, S_2\}$, $M = 2$ observations $\{a, b\}$, and an observation sequence $O = (a, b)$ of length $T = 2$. **Hypothetical parameters:** $$\pi = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 \\ 0.4 \end{bmatrix}, \quad A = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7 & 0.3 \\ 0.4 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} b_1(a)=0.5 & b_1(b)=0.5 \\ b_2(a)=0.3 & b_2(b)=0.7 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 1:** Enumerate all $N^T = 2^2 = 4$ possible state sequences: | Path # | State Sequence $Q$ | |---|---| | 1 | $(S_1, S_1)$ | | 2 | $(S_1, S_2)$ | | 3 | $(S_2, S_1)$ | | 4 | $(S_2, S_2)$ | **Step 2:** Compute $P(O, Q \mid \lambda)$ for each path using F2. **Path 1 — $Q = (S_1, S_1)$, $O = (a, b)$:** $$P(O, Q_1 \mid \lambda) = \pi_1 \cdot b_1(a) \cdot a_{11} \cdot b_1(b) = (0.6)(0.5)(0.7)(0.5) = 0.105$$ **Path 2 — $Q = (S_1, S_2)$:** $$P(O, Q_2 \mid \lambda) = \pi_1 \cdot b_1(a) \cdot a_{12} \cdot b_2(b) = (0.6)(0.5)(0.3)(0.7) = 0.063$$ **Path 3 — $Q = (S_2, S_1)$:** $$P(O, Q_3 \mid \lambda) = \pi_2 \cdot b_2(a) \cdot a_{21} \cdot b_1(b) = (0.4)(0.3)(0.4)(0.5) = 0.024$$ **Path 4 — $Q = (S_2, S_2)$:** $$P(O, Q_4 \mid \lambda) = \pi_2 \cdot b_2(a) \cdot a_{22} \cdot b_2(b) = (0.4)(0.3)(0.6)(0.7) = 0.0504$$ **Step 3:** Sum the joint probabilities to obtain the marginal: $$P(O \mid \lambda) = 0.105 + 0.063 + 0.024 + 0.0504 = 0.2424$$ ## 3.2 Worked-Out Derivation 2: The Forward Algorithm (Same Problem) We now recompute $P(O \mid \lambda)$ using the Forward Algorithm and verify the answer is **identical**. **Step 1 — Initialization ($t = 1$, observation $O_1 = a$):** $$\alpha_1(1) = \pi_1 \cdot b_1(a) = (0.6)(0.5) = 0.30$$ $$\alpha_1(2) = \pi_2 \cdot b_2(a) = (0.4)(0.3) = 0.12$$ **Step 2 — Induction ($t = 2$, observation $O_2 = b$):** For state $S_1$ at $t = 2$: $$\alpha_2(1) = \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{2} \alpha_1(i) \, a_{i1} \right] \cdot b_1(b)$$ $$= \left[ (0.30)(0.7) + (0.12)(0.4) \right] \cdot (0.5) = [0.21 + 0.048] \cdot 0.5 = (0.258)(0.5) = 0.129$$ For state $S_2$ at $t = 2$: $$\alpha_2(2) = \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{2} \alpha_1(i) \, a_{i2} \right] \cdot b_2(b)$$ $$= \left[ (0.30)(0.3) + (0.12)(0.6) \right] \cdot (0.7) = [0.09 + 0.072] \cdot 0.7 = (0.162)(0.7) = 0.1134$$ **Step 3 — Termination:** $$P(O \mid \lambda) = \alpha_2(1) + \alpha_2(2) = 0.129 + 0.1134 = 0.2424$$ $$\boxed{P(O \mid \lambda) = 0.2424}$$ **Verification:** The result **matches exactly** the naive brute-force sum in §3.1. ✅ ## 3.3 Worked-Out Derivation 3: Scaling for Numerical Stability For longer sequences ($T > 50$), direct computation causes **floating-point underflow**. The standard fix is the **scaling coefficients** $c_t$ (Rabiner, 1989): $$c_t = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_t(i)}$$ $$\hat{\alpha}_t(i) = c_t \cdot \alpha_t(i)$$ **Step 1:** Compute raw $\alpha_1(i)$ as before. **Step 2:** Compute $c_1 = 1 / (\alpha_1(1) + \alpha_1(2))$. **Step 3:** Rescale: $\hat{\alpha}_1(i) = c_1 \alpha_1(i)$. **Step 4:** Recurse using $\hat{\alpha}_{t+1}(j) = c_{t+1} \left[ \sum_i \hat{\alpha}_t(i) a_{ij} \right] b_j(O_{t+1})$. **Step 5:** Recover the true log-probability: $$\log P(O \mid \lambda) = -\sum_{t=1}^{T} \log c_t$$ ## 3.4 Complexity Comparison (Analytical Matrix) | Method | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Numerical Stability | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Naive Enumeration** | $O(2T \cdot N^T)$ | $O(T)$ | Stable for $T \leq 5$ | Teaching, small $T$ | | **Forward Algorithm** | $O(N^2 T)$ | $O(N)$ | Stable with scaling | Production systems | | **Backward Algorithm** | $O(N^2 T)$ | $O(N)$ | Stable with scaling | Smoothing, learning | | **Forward–Backward (Baum–Welch)** | $O(N^2 T)$ per iteration | $O(NT)$ | Iterative convergence | Training | | **Log-Forward Algorithm** | $O(N^2 T)$ | $O(N)$ | Inherently stable | Long sequences | --- > [!TIP] > **For a KTU exam with $N = 2, T = 2$:** Both naive and Forward methods yield the same answer. Examiners usually want the **Forward Algorithm** because it scales. Always show all three steps — Initialization, Induction, Termination — to claim full marks. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Trellis Diagram — The Forward Algorithm Path The **trellis** is the canonical visual representation of how the Forward Algorithm propagates probabilities. Each column corresponds to a time step; each row corresponds to a hidden state. Edges are weighted by transition probabilities $a_{ij}$, and each node is weighted by the emission probability $b_i(O_t)$. ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef initNode fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef midNode fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#ef6c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef termNode fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef obsNode fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 t1s1["S1 — t=1, O1=a
alpha1(1) = pi1 b1 a"] t1s2["S2 — t=1, O1=a
alpha1(2) = pi2 b2 a"] t2s1["S1 — t=2, O2=b
alpha2(1)"] t2s2["S2 — t=2, O2=b
alpha2(2)"] t3s1["S1 — t=3, O3=a
alpha3(1)"] t3s2["S2 — t=3, O3=a
alpha3(2)"] outP["P O lambda = sum alpha3 i"] t1s1 -->|a11 b1| t2s1 t1s1 -->|a12 b2| t2s2 t1s2 -->|a21 b1| t2s1 t1s2 -->|a22 b2| t2s2 t2s1 -->|a11 b1| t3s1 t2s1 -->|a12 b2| t3s2 t2s2 -->|a21 b1| t3s1 t2s2 -->|a22 b2| t3s2 t3s1 --> outP t3s2 --> outP t1s1:::initNode t1s2:::initNode t2s1:::midNode t2s2:::midNode t3s1:::termNode t3s2:::termNode outP:::obsNode ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Forward Algorithm Pipeline The following flowchart maps the **algorithmic pipeline** of computing $P(O \mid \lambda)$ in a production NLP/CL system. ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef inputCls fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px classDef procCls fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px classDef outCls fill:#f1f8e9,stroke:#33691e,stroke-width:2px classDef failCls fill:#ffebee,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px A["Input Observation Sequence O = O1 OT"]:::inputCls B["Load Model Parameters lambda = A B pi"]:::inputCls C{"T greater than threshold"}:::procCls D["Use Naive Enumeration N to power T"]:::procCls E["Initialize Forward Variable alpha 1 i"]:::procCls F["For t = 1 to T minus 1 Recurse Induction Step"]:::procCls G["Apply Scaling Factor c t for Numerical Stability"]:::procCls H["Termination Sum alpha T i"]:::procCls I["Output P O lambda as scalar"]:::outCls J["Apply Log Space Transformation"]:::procCls K["Numerical Underflow Detected"]:::failCls A --> C B --> C C -->|Yes small T| D C -->|No large T| E D --> I E --> F F --> G G --> H H --> I G -->|Underflow Risk| K K --> J J --> I ``` ## 4.3 Probability Propagation Map — Forward ↔ Backward Symmetry ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef fwd fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px classDef bwd fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px classDef mid fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px alpha1["alpha 1 i
= pi i b i O1"]:::fwd alphaT["alpha t i"]:::fwd alphaT1["alpha t plus 1 j
sum over i alpha t i aij times bj O t plus 1"]:::fwd alphaEnd["alpha T i"]:::fwd betaT["beta T i = 1"]:::bwd betaT1["beta t i
sum over j aij bj O t plus 1 beta t plus 1 j"]:::bwd beta1["beta 1 i"]:::bwd combined["P O lambda = sum i alpha t i beta t i"]:::mid alpha1 --> alphaT alphaT --> alphaT1 alphaT1 --> alphaEnd betaT --> betaT1 betaT1 --> beta1 alphaEnd --> combined beta1 --> combined ``` --- > [!TIP] > **Diagram Reading Tip:** In the trellis, every **node** stores one $\alpha_t(i)$ value. Every **edge** represents a transition weighted by $a_{ij}$. The "thickness" of paths in textbooks often encodes probability mass — paths with higher cumulative $\alpha$ are "fatter". For exam answers, always **label the node with the $\alpha$ value** you computed. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. Define the "Probability of an Observation" in the context of Hidden Markov Models.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **probability of an observation** in an HMM is the likelihood $P(O \mid \lambda)$ of observing a given sequence of symbols $O = (O_1, O_2, \ldots, O_T)$ under a specific model $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$. Mathematically, it is computed by marginalizing over all possible hidden state sequences $Q$: $$P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{Q} P(O, Q \mid \lambda)$$ where $P(O, Q \mid \lambda) = \pi_{q_1} b_{q_1}(O_1) \prod_{t=2}^{T} a_{q_{t-1} q_t} b_{q_t}(O_t)$. This is known as the **Evaluation Problem** (HMM Problem 1) and is efficiently solved using the **Forward Algorithm** in $O(N^2 T)$ time, as opposed to the naive $O(N^T)$ enumeration. **(3 marks)** --- ### **Q2. What is the Forward Variable? Write its initialization formula.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **forward variable** $\alpha_t(i)$ is defined as the joint probability of observing the partial sequence $O_1, O_2, \ldots, O_t$ AND being in state $S_i$ at time $t$, given the model $\lambda$: $$\alpha_t(i) = P(O_1, O_2, \ldots, O_t, \, q_t = S_i \mid \lambda)$$ **Initialization (at $t = 1$):** $$\alpha_1(i) = \pi_i \cdot b_i(O_1) \quad \text{for } 1 \leq i \leq N$$ This represents the probability of starting in state $S_i$ and emitting the first observation $O_1$. The forward variable is the core of the dynamic programming trick that makes $P(O \mid \lambda)$ tractable. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]` #### **Part (a) — [7 Marks]** **Explain the three fundamental problems of HMMs. With a suitable block diagram, describe how the Forward Algorithm solves Problem 1 (the Evaluation Problem).** **Model Solution:** The three canonical HMM problems (Rabiner, 1989) are: 1. **Evaluation Problem:** Given $O$ and $\lambda$, compute $P(O \mid \lambda)$. Solved by the Forward–Backward Algorithm. 2. **Decoding Problem:** Given $O$ and $\lambda$, find the optimal state sequence $Q^*$ that best "explains" the observations. Solved by the **Viterbi Algorithm**. 3. **Learning Problem:** Given $O$, find $\lambda^*$ that maximizes $P(O \mid \lambda^*)$. Solved by the **Baum–Welch Algorithm** (a special case of EM). For the **Evaluation Problem**, the Forward Algorithm defines $\alpha_t(i)$ and proceeds in three steps: - **Step 1 — Initialization:** $\alpha_1(i) = \pi_i b_i(O_1)$ for $1 \leq i \leq N$. - **Step 2 — Induction:** $\alpha_{t+1}(j) = \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_t(i) a_{ij} \right] b_j(O_{t+1})$ for $1 \leq t \leq T-1$, $1 \leq j \leq N$. - **Step 3 — Termination:** $P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_T(i)$. The block diagram (refer §4.2) shows the pipeline: Input → Initialization → Induction → (Optional Scaling) → Termination → Output. **(7 marks — full marks require: 2 marks for three problems, 3 marks for the three forward steps, 2 marks for diagram/pipeline explanation)** #### **Part (b) — [7 Marks]** **Consider a 2-state HMM with states $\{S_1, S_2\}$, observation symbols $\{A, B\}$, and parameters:** $$\pi = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix}, \quad A = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.4 \\ 0.3 & 0.7 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} b_1(A)=0.8 & b_1(B)=0.2 \\ b_2(A)=0.4 & b_2(B)=0.6 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Compute $P(O = A, A \mid \lambda)$ using the Forward Algorithm.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Initialization ($t = 1$, $O_1 = A$):** $$\alpha_1(1) = \pi_1 b_1(A) = (0.5)(0.8) = 0.40$$ $$\alpha_1(2) = \pi_2 b_2(A) = (0.5)(0.4) = 0.20$$ **Step 2 — Induction ($t = 2$, $O_2 = A$):** For state $S_1$ at $t = 2$: $$\alpha_2(1) = [\alpha_1(1) a_{11} + \alpha_1(2) a_{21}] \cdot b_1(A)$$ $$= [(0.40)(0.6) + (0.20)(0.3)] \cdot (0.8)$$ $$= [0.24 + 0.06] \cdot 0.8 = (0.30)(0.8) = 0.24$$ For state $S_2$ at $t = 2$: $$\alpha_2(2) = [\alpha_1(1) a_{12} + \alpha_1(2) a_{22}] \cdot b_2(A)$$ $$= [(0.40)(0.4) + (0.20)(0.7)] \cdot (0.4)$$ $$= [0.16 + 0.14] \cdot 0.4 = (0.30)(0.4) = 0.12$$ **Step 3 — Termination:** $$P(O = A, A \mid \lambda) = \alpha_2(1) + \alpha_2(2) = 0.24 + 0.12 = \mathbf{0.36}$$ **(7 marks — 2 marks for initialization, 3 marks for induction with full expansion, 2 marks for termination and final answer)** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]` #### **Part (a) — [7 Marks]** **Derive the probability of an observation sequence using the naive (enumeration) method. Clearly state the joint probability formula and show how marginalization over all state sequences yields $P(O \mid \lambda)$. Why is this method computationally infeasible for long sequences?** **Model Solution:** For a fixed hidden state sequence $Q = (q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_T)$, the joint probability of the observation sequence $O$ AND the state sequence $Q$ is given by applying the chain rule and exploiting the Markov property: $$P(O, Q \mid \lambda) = P(q_1) \cdot P(O_1 \mid q_1) \cdot P(q_2 \mid q_1) \cdot P(O_2 \mid q_2) \cdots P(q_T \mid q_{T-1}) \cdot P(O_T \mid q_T)$$ Substituting the HMM parameters: $$P(O, Q \mid \lambda) = \pi_{q_1} \cdot b_{q_1}(O_1) \prod_{t=2}^{T} a_{q_{t-1} q_t} \cdot b_{q_t}(O_t)$$ Since the hidden state sequence is **not observed**, we marginalize (sum) over all $N^T$ possible state sequences: $$P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{q_1=1}^{N} \sum_{q_2=1}^{N} \cdots \sum_{q_T=1}^{N} \pi_{q_1} \, b_{q_1}(O_1) \prod_{t=2}^{T} a_{q_{t-1} q_t} \, b_{q_t}(O_t)$$ **Computational Infeasibility:** - For each state sequence, we perform approximately $2T$ multiplications. - The number of state sequences is $N^T$. - Total operations: $O(2T \cdot N^T)$ — **exponential** in $T$. For $N = 5$ and $T = 100$, this requires $\sim 5^{100} \approx 7.8 \times 10^{69}$ operations — completely infeasible. This motivates the **Forward Algorithm** ($O(N^2 T)$). **(7 marks — 3 marks for joint formula, 2 marks for marginalization, 2 marks for complexity analysis)** #### **Part (b) — [7 Marks]** **Define the backward variable $\beta_t(i)$. Write its initialization and induction formulas, and show how $P(O \mid \lambda)$ can be expressed using $\beta_1(i)$.** **Model Solution:** The **backward variable** $\beta_t(i)$ is defined as the probability of observing the **suffix** $O_{t+1}, O_{t+2}, \ldots, O_T$ given that the system is in state $S_i$ at time $t$: $$\beta_t(i) = P(O_{t+1}, O_{t+2}, \ldots, O_T \mid q_t = S_i, \lambda)$$ **Initialization (at $t = T$):** $$\beta_T(i) = 1 \quad \text{for } 1 \leq i \leq N$$ (Intuition: if we are at the end of the sequence, the probability of observing the empty suffix is 1.) **Induction (recursion from $t = T-1$ down to $1$):** $$\beta_t(i) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} a_{ij} \cdot b_j(O_{t+1}) \cdot \beta_{t+1}(j) \quad \text{for } t = T-1, T-2, \ldots, 1$$ **Computing $P(O \mid \lambda)$ using $\beta_1(i)$:** We sum over all possible starting states $S_i$, weighted by $\pi_i$, the emission $b_i(O_1)$, and the backward variable $\beta_1(i)$: $$P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \pi_i \cdot b_i(O_1) \cdot \beta_1(i)$$ **Significance:** The backward variable is essential for the **Baum–Welch re-estimation formulas** used in HMM training, where we need $P(q_t = S_i \mid O, \lambda)$ — this requires $\alpha_t(i) \beta_t(i)$. **(7 marks — 2 marks for definition, 2 marks for initialization, 2 marks for induction, 1 mark for termination formula)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing Evaluation with Decoding:** Students often write the **Viterbi** (max) algorithm when the question asks for $P(O \mid \lambda)$ (sum). Read the question carefully — "probability" = **sum**, "best path" = **max**. > 2. **Forgetting the Emission Term $b_j(O_{t+1})$:** A classic error is to compute $\alpha_{t+1}(j) = \sum_i \alpha_t(i) a_{ij}$ WITHOUT multiplying by $b_j(O_{t+1})$. This is worth **at least 1 mark lost** in KTU valuations. > 3. **Off-by-one Indexing:** $\alpha_1(i)$ uses $O_1$ (not $O_0$); the induction step takes you from $t$ to $t+1$. Many students confuse $t$ and $t+1$ in their notation. > 4. **Not Showing All Three Steps:** The Forward Algorithm has **three** named steps (Initialization, Induction, Termination). Examiners award marks for each. Skipping "Termination" loses 1–2 marks. > 5. **Treating $\pi$ as a probability mass over states but forgetting to validate $\sum \pi_i = 1$.** If the question's $\pi$ doesn't sum to 1, check it first. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [x] **$P(O \mid \lambda)$** is the **Evaluation Problem** (HMM Problem 1). - [x] The **Forward Variable** $\alpha_t(i)$ = prob. of partial obs. sequence $O_1 \ldots O_t$ AND being in state $S_i$ at time $t$. - [x] **Three Steps of Forward Algorithm:** Initialization, Induction, Termination. - [x] **Naive method complexity:** $O(N^T)$ — exponential; **Forward method:** $O(N^2 T)$ — polynomial. - [x] The forward algorithm uses **dynamic programming** to avoid recomputation of overlapping subproblems. - [x] **Backward variable** $\beta_t(i)$ = prob. of future observation suffix given current state. - [x] $P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_i \alpha_T(i) = \sum_i \alpha_t(i) \beta_t(i)$ (for any $t$). - [x] **Numerical underflow** is a real concern for $T > 50$; use **scaling** or **log-space**. - [x] The forward algorithm appears in **speech recognition, POS tagging, bioinformatics, OCR**. - [x] **Rabiner's 1989 tutorial** is the canonical reference for HMMs in CL/NLP — examiners love questions that mirror its notation. - [x] **Always** include the emission probability $b_j(O_{t+1})$ in the induction step — non-negotiable. - [x] Marginalization (sum) ≠ Maximization (Viterbi) — know the difference cold. - [x] The **joint** $P(O, Q \mid \lambda)$ is a **product** of $\pi$, $b$, and $a$ terms; the **marginal** $P(O \mid \lambda)$ is a **sum** over $Q$ of that product. --- > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Exam Mantra for this topic:** > *"Sum over all paths, cache the prefix, terminate by aggregating the final column — that's the Forward Algorithm."*

topics.title: Best State Sequence
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Best State Sequence

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the framework of a **Hidden Markov Model (HMM)**, the **Best State Sequence** problem (also called the **Viterbi Decoding Problem** or **Maximum A-Posteriori (MAP) Decoding**) is formally defined as follows:

> **Definition (Best State Sequence Problem):**
> Given an HMM $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$ and an observation sequence $O = O_1 O_2 \ldots O_T$, find the single best state sequence $Q^* = q_1^* q_2^* \ldots q_T^*$ that maximizes the joint probability $P(Q, O \vert \lambda)$ over all possible state sequences of length $T$.

Mathematically:
$$Q^* = \arg\max_{Q} P(Q, O \mid \lambda)$$

The standard solution is the **Viterbi Algorithm**, a dynamic programming procedure that computes this maximum in $O(N^2 T)$ time, where $N$ is the number of hidden states and $T$ is the length of the observation sequence.

### The HMM Tuple $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$

| Symbol | Name | Dimension | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| $N$ | Number of states | scalar | Size of hidden state space $S = \{S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_N\}$ |
| $M$ | Number of observation symbols | scalar | Size of vocabulary $V = \{v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_M\}$ |
| $A$ | State transition matrix | $N \times N$ | $A_{ij} = P(q_{t+1} = S_j \mid q_t = S_i)$ |
| $B$ | Emission / Observation matrix | $N \times M$ | $B_{ik} = P(O_t = v_k \mid q_t = S_i)$ |
| $\pi$ | Initial state distribution | $N \times 1$ | $\pi_i = P(q_1 = S_i)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Best State Sequence is one of the **three canonical problems of HMMs**. You must distinguish it clearly from (1) **Likelihood** (Forward Algorithm) and (2) **Parameter Estimation** (Baum-Welch / Forward-Backward). Best State Sequence = **Decoding**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **detective** in a coastal town.

* You **cannot see** the weather (Hidden States: ☀️ Sunny, 🌧️ Rainy).
* You **can observe** your friend carrying an **umbrella, hat, or nothing** (Observations).
* You maintain a probability table of: *"If it was Sunny yesterday, what's the chance it is Rainy today?"* (Transition matrix $A$) and *"If it's Sunny, what's the chance my friend carries an umbrella?"* (Emission matrix $B$).
* At the end of 3 days, your friend carried: **Umbrella, Hat, Nothing**.

**Question:** What was the *most likely* weather sequence (Hidden States) over those 3 days that *best explains* your friend's accessories?

This is exactly the **Best State Sequence** problem. For each day, you do not just want the single most likely state in isolation (that would be a **pointwise maximum**), but the **jointly optimal path** through time — because the constraint of yesterday's state restricts today's state through $A$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Student Misconception:** "Best state sequence = run $\arg\max$ of $\gamma_t(i)$ at each $t$." This is **wrong**. The pointwise best states may form an **infeasible sequence** (e.g., transition probability $A_{ij} = 0$). The Viterbi algorithm guarantees a globally consistent, feasible path.

## 1.3 Why "Best" is Ambiguous — Two Interpretations

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Distinction (frequently tested in KTU):**
> 1. **Best state sequence** (what this topic is about): a single sequence $Q^*$ maximizing $P(Q, O \vert \lambda)$. **Solved by Viterbi.**
> 2. **Best individual state at time $t$**: $\gamma_t(i) = P(q_t = S_i \mid O, \lambda)$, found by **Forward-Backward**. This can produce locally optimal but globally inconsistent sequences.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Naive (Brute-Force) Approach

In principle, one could evaluate $P(Q, O \vert \lambda)$ for **every** possible state sequence of length $T$ and pick the best. There are $N^T$ such sequences. For $N = 5, T = 10$, that is nearly **10 million** sequences. **Exponentially infeasible.**

## 2.2 Key Idea: Dynamic Programming (Bellman-Ford / DP Recursion)

The Viterbi algorithm exploits a beautiful recursive structure:

> The best path ending at state $S_j$ at time $t$ depends **only** on the best paths ending at each state at time $t-1$ — **not** on the entire history before that.

This is the **Markov property** + **optimal substructure** of DP.

## 2.3 The Three Viterbi Quantities

### (a) Viterbi Probability (Best Path Probability So Far)

$$\delta_t(j) = \max_{q_1, \ldots, q_{t-1}} P(q_1 \ldots q_{t-1}, q_t = S_j, O_1 \ldots O_t \mid \lambda)$$

It is the highest probability of any partial path that ends in state $S_j$ at time $t$ and produces the observations seen so far.

### (b) Backpointer (Argument Tracker)

$$\psi_t(j) = \arg\max_{i} \left[ \delta_{t-1}(i) \cdot A_{ij} \right]$$

It records **which previous state** led to the best score for state $S_j$ at time $t$. This is what enables **backtracking** at the end.

### (c) Final Best Score

$$P^* = \max_{1 \le j \le N} \delta_T(j)$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Purpose | Unit / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Joint probability of $Q, O$ | $P(Q, O \vert \lambda) = \pi_{q_1} B_{q_1, O_1} \prod_{t=2}^{T} A_{q_{t-1}, q_t} B_{q_t, O_t}$ | Full evaluation of a candidate path | Probability $\in [0, 1]$ |
| 2 | **Viterbi recursion** | $\delta_t(j) = \max\limits_{1 \le i \le N} \left[ \delta_{t-1}(i) A_{ij} \right] \cdot B_{j, O_t}$ | Step-by-step DP core | Probability |
| 3 | Backpointer | $\psi_t(j) = \arg\max\limits_{i} \left[ \delta_{t-1}(i) A_{ij} \right]$ | Track optimal predecessor | State index |
| 4 | Initialization | $\delta_1(j) = \pi_j \cdot B_{j, O_1}$ | Seed step $t=1$ | Probability |
| 5 | Termination | $P^* = \max\limits_{j} \delta_T(j)$ | Best overall score | Probability |
| 6 | Backtrack | $q_t^* = \psi_{t+1}(q_{t+1}^*)$ | Recover path, $t = T-1, \ldots, 1$ | State index |
| 7 | Log-form (numerical stability) | $\log \delta_t(j) = \max\limits_i \left[ \log \delta_{t-1}(i) + \log A_{ij} \right] + \log B_{j, O_t}$ | Avoid underflow | Log-probability |
| 8 | Posterior (contrast) | $\gamma_t(i) = \dfrac{\alpha_t(i)\beta_t(i)}{\sum_j \alpha_t(j)\beta_t(j)}$ | NOT for best path; for individual best state | Probability |
| 9 | Path count | $N^T$ | Brute-force complexity | Exponential |
| 10 | Viterbi complexity | $O(N^2 T)$ | Dynamic programming cost | Polynomial |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use of `\max` vs `\sum`:** In Viterbi, you use $\max$ (maximization). In the Forward algorithm, you use $\sum$ (summation). This single symbol difference is the **most common KTU exam trap**.

## 2.5 Engineering & Linguistic Applications

* **Part-of-Speech (POS) Tagging** — words = observations, tags = hidden states. Viterbi finds the globally optimal tag sequence for a sentence.
* **Speech Recognition** — acoustic frames = observations, phonemes/words = hidden states.
* **Bioinformatics** — DNA sequence alignment, gene finding (CpG islands).
* **Handwriting & Gesture Recognition.**
* **Optical Character Recognition (OCR)**, **Channel decoding** in digital communications.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Viterbi Recursion

We want to show that the best partial path probability $\delta_t(j)$ can be computed recursively from $\delta_{t-1}(\cdot)$.

### Starting point — Definition:

$$\delta_t(j) = \max_{q_1, \ldots, q_{t-1}} P(q_1 \ldots q_{t-1}, q_t = S_j, O_1 \ldots O_t \mid \lambda)$$

### Step 1: Factor using conditional independence (Markov + Output independence)

The joint probability of a path up to time $t$ can be split as:

$$P(q_1 \ldots q_t, O_1 \ldots O_t \mid \lambda) = P(q_1 \ldots q_{t-1}, O_1 \ldots O_{t-1} \mid \lambda) \cdot P(q_t = S_j \mid q_{t-1}) \cdot P(O_t \mid q_t = S_j)$$

### Step 2: Separate maximization over the prefix and the last transition

Let the optimal prefix ending at $S_i$ at time $t-1$ have probability $\delta_{t-1}(i)$. Then:

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta_t(j) &= \max_{i} \left[ \max_{q_1,\ldots,q_{t-2}} P(q_1 \ldots q_{t-2}, q_{t-1} = S_i, O_1 \ldots O_{t-1} \mid \lambda) \right] \cdot A_{ij} \cdot B_{j, O_t} \\
&= \max_{i} \left[ \delta_{t-1}(i) \cdot A_{ij} \right] \cdot B_{j, O_t}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the Viterbi recursion. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Classic Weather–Umbrella HMM)

> [!NOTE]
> **Setup (Jurafsky & Martin–style canonical example):**
> * Hidden states: $S_1 =$ Rainy, $S_2 =$ Sunny
> * Observations: $v_1 =$ Umbrella (U), $v_2 =$ No-umbrella (N)
> * Transition matrix $A$:
> $$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7 & 0.3 \\ 0.4 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{(rows: from, cols: to)}$$
> * Emission matrix $B$:
> $$B = \begin{bmatrix} 0.9 & 0.1 \\ 0.2 & 0.8 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{(Rainy, Sunny; U, N)}$$
> * Initial distribution: $\pi = [0.6, \; 0.4]$
> * Observation sequence: $O = \text{U, N, U}$  $\Rightarrow T = 3$

**Goal:** Compute the best state sequence $Q^*$ for $O$.

### Step 1: Initialization ($t = 1$, observation U)

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta_1(\text{Rainy}) &= \pi_{\text{Rainy}} \cdot B_{\text{Rainy},U} = 0.6 \times 0.9 = 0.54 \\
\delta_1(\text{Sunny}) &= \pi_{\text{Sunny}} \cdot B_{\text{Sunny},U} = 0.4 \times 0.2 = 0.08
\end{aligned}$$

Backpointers: $\psi_1(\cdot)$ undefined (no predecessor).

| State | $\delta_1$ | $\psi_1$ |
|---|---|---|
| Rainy | **0.54** | — |
| Sunny | **0.08** | — |

### Step 2: Recursion at $t = 2$, observation N

For each state, evaluate the best incoming path:

**Path ending in Rainy at $t=2$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{from Rainy: } & \delta_1(\text{Rainy}) \cdot A_{\text{RR}} \cdot B_{\text{R},N} = 0.54 \times 0.7 \times 0.1 = 0.0378 \\
\text{from Sunny: } & \delta_1(\text{Sunny}) \cdot A_{\text{SR}} \cdot B_{\text{R},N} = 0.08 \times 0.4 \times 0.1 = 0.0032
\end{aligned}$$

$$\delta_2(\text{Rainy}) = \max(0.0378,\; 0.0032) = 0.0378, \quad \psi_2(\text{Rainy}) = \text{Rainy}$$

**Path ending in Sunny at $t=2$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{from Rainy: } & 0.54 \times 0.3 \times 0.8 = 0.1296 \\
\text{from Sunny: } & 0.08 \times 0.6 \times 0.8 = 0.0384
\end{aligned}$$

$$\delta_2(\text{Sunny}) = \max(0.1296,\; 0.0384) = 0.1296, \quad \psi_2(\text{Sunny}) = \text{Rainy}$$

| State | $\delta_2$ | $\psi_2$ |
|---|---|---|
| Rainy | **0.0378** | Rainy |
| Sunny | **0.1296** | Rainy |

### Step 3: Recursion at $t = 3$, observation U

**Path ending in Rainy at $t=3$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{from Rainy: } & 0.0378 \times 0.7 \times 0.9 = 0.023814 \\
\text{from Sunny: } & 0.1296 \times 0.4 \times 0.9 = 0.046656
\end{aligned}$$

$$\delta_3(\text{Rainy}) = 0.046656, \quad \psi_3(\text{Rainy}) = \text{Sunny}$$

**Path ending in Sunny at $t=3$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{from Rainy: } & 0.0378 \times 0.3 \times 0.2 = 0.002268 \\
\text{from Sunny: } & 0.1296 \times 0.6 \times 0.2 = 0.015552
\end{aligned}$$

$$\delta_3(\text{Sunny}) = 0.015552, \quad \psi_3(\text{Sunny}) = \text{Runny} \quad \text{(typo: should be Rainy)}$$

$$\delta_3(\text{Sunny}) = 0.015552, \quad \psi_3(\text{Sunny}) = \text{Rainy}$$

| State | $\delta_3$ | $\psi_3$ |
|---|---|---|
| Rainy | **0.046656** | Sunny |
| Sunny | **0.015552** | Rainy |

### Step 4: Termination

$$P^* = \max(\delta_3(\text{Rainy}),\; \delta_3(\text{Sunny})) = \max(0.046656,\; 0.015552) = 0.046656$$

$$q_3^* = \text{Rainy}$$

### Step 5: Backtrack

$$q_2^* = \psi_3(q_3^*) = \psi_3(\text{Rainy}) = \text{Sunny}$$

$$q_1^* = \psi_2(q_2^*) = \psi_2(\text{Sunny}) = \text{Rainy}$$

### ✅ Final Answer

$$\boxed{Q^* = \text{Rainy, Sunny, Rainy} \quad \text{with } P^* = 0.046656}$$

## 3.3 Comparison Matrix: Viterbi vs Forward vs Forward-Backward

| Feature | Viterbi | Forward | Forward-Backward |
|---|---|---|---|
| Operator | $\max$ | $\sum$ | $\sum$ (twice) |
| Output | Single best path $Q^*$ | $P(O \vert \lambda)$ | $P(q_t = S_i \vert O, \lambda)$ |
| Backpointers | Yes (to recover path) | No | No |
| Complexity | $O(N^2 T)$ | $O(N^2 T)$ | $O(N^2 T)$ |
| Use case | **Decoding** | **Evaluation** | **Training (Baum-Welch) + per-state posterior** |
| Numerical risk | Underflow (use log) | Underflow (use scaling) | Underflow (use scaling) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HMM as a Probabilistic Graphical Model

```mermaid
graph LR
  q1(("q1")) --> q2(("q2"))
  q2 --> q3(("q3"))
  q3 --> q4(("q4"))
  o1(["O1"]) -.- q1
  o2(["O2"]) -.- q2
  o3(["O3"]) -.- q3
  o4(["O4"]) -.- q4
```

**Interpretation:** Solid horizontal arrows = hidden state transitions governed by matrix $A$. Dashed vertical arrows = emissions governed by matrix $B$. The shaded / bracketed nodes $O_t$ are the **only** observables; $q_t$ must be inferred.

## 4.2 Viterbi Trellis for the Worked Example

```mermaid
graph TD
  S1R(("Rainy, 0.54")) --> S2R(("Rainy, 0.0378"))
  S1R --> S2S(("Sunny, 0.1296"))
  S1S(("Sunny, 0.08")) --> S2R
  S1S --> S2S
  S2R --> S3R(("Rainy, 0.046656"))
  S2R --> S3S(("Sunny, 0.015552"))
  S2S --> S3R
  S2S --> S3S
  S3R -.BACKTRACK.-> S2S
  S2S -.BACKTRACK.-> S1R
```

* Each node = a state at a time step, labeled with $\delta_t(\text{state})$.
* Edges = candidate transitions.
* **Dashed backtrack edges** = the recovered best path $\text{Rainy} \to \text{Sunny} \to \text{Rainy}$, traced using backpointers $\psi$.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Viterbi Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
  A["INPUT BLOCK\nHMM lambda = A B pi\nObservation sequence O"] --> B["INIT BLOCK\ndelta1, psi1"]
  B --> C["RECURSION BLOCK\nFor t = 2 to T\nCompute delta t and psi t"]
  C --> D{"TERMINATION BLOCK\nPick qT star = argmax delta T"}
  D --> E["BACKTRACK BLOCK\nRecover q star from psi"]
  E --> F["OUTPUT BLOCK\nBest state sequence Q star and P star"]
```

**Block Roles:**

1. **INPUT** — accepts the model and the observation string.
2. **INIT** — seeds the DP table at $t=1$.
3. **RECURSION** — the main $O(N^2 T)$ loop applying the Viterbi formula.
4. **TERMINATION** — selects the rightmost column winner.
5. **BACKTRACK** — reads the backpointer table in reverse to reconstruct the path.
6. **OUTPUT** — emits both the sequence $Q^*$ and its probability $P^*$.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
*State any two differences between the Forward algorithm and the Viterbi algorithm in the context of HMMs.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Forward Algorithm | Viterbi Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Uses **summation** $\sum_i$ over previous states to compute total probability of observations. | Uses **maximization** $\max_i$ over previous states to find the best single path. |
| 2 | Computes the **likelihood** $P(O \mid \lambda)$ only; does not give a state sequence. | Computes the **best state sequence** $Q^*$ along with its probability $P^*$. |
| 3 | Does not maintain backpointers. | Maintains a backpointer $\psi_t(j)$ to recover the path by backtracking. |
| 4 | Used in evaluation and Baum-Welch training. | Used in decoding (e.g., POS tagging, speech recognition). |

*(Any two well-explained differences ⇒ full 3 marks.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the Viterbi probability $\delta_t(j)$ and the backpointer $\psi_t(j)$ in an HMM.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

* $\delta_t(j)$ is defined as the **maximum probability** of being in state $S_j$ at time $t$, having generated the observations $O_1 \ldots O_t$, computed over all possible state sequences of length $t-1$ that end at $S_j$.
$$\delta_t(j) = \max_{q_1,\ldots,q_{t-1}} P(q_1\ldots q_{t-1}, q_t=S_j, O_1\ldots O_t \mid \lambda)$$

* $\psi_t(j)$ is the **backpointer** — it stores the state index $i$ at time $t-1$ that achieved the maximum in the Viterbi recursion, so that the best path can be reconstructed by backtracking.
$$\psi_t(j) = \arg\max_{i}\left[\delta_{t-1}(i)\,A_{ij}\right]$$

* Together, $\delta$ and $\psi$ form the dynamic-programming table that the Viterbi algorithm fills in $O(N^2 T)$.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — read carefully:**
> * **Do NOT confuse the three HMM problems.** Marks are deducted heavily when students compute $P(O \mid \lambda)$ (Forward) when the question asks for the **best state sequence** (Viterbi), or vice versa.
> * Always **show the backpointer table explicitly** and perform the backtracking step. A correct $P^*$ alone, without $Q^*$, fetches only ~7 of 14 marks.
> * When asked for $Q^*$, the answer **must list the states in order** with their probabilities; examiners check the sequence's feasibility against $A$.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Adapted]**

Consider the HMM with two hidden states $S_1$ (H) and $S_2$ (C) representing **H**ot and **C**old weather. Observation symbols are **C** (chocolate ice-cream sold) and **V** (vanilla ice-cream sold). Parameters:

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.4 \\ 0.5 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 & 0.2 \\ 0.3 & 0.7 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \pi = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7 \\ 0.3 \end{bmatrix}$$

The observation sequence for 3 days is $O = \text{C, V, C}$.

**(a)** Set up the Viterbi algorithm, including the initialization and the recursion formula. Compute the Viterbi table $\delta_t(j)$ and the backpointer table $\psi_t(j)$ for all $t = 1, 2, 3$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the best state sequence $Q^*$ and its probability $P^*$. Show the backtracking step clearly. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Viterbi formulas used:**

$$\delta_1(j) = \pi_j \cdot B_{j, O_1}, \quad \delta_t(j) = \max_{i}\left[\delta_{t-1}(i) A_{ij}\right] \cdot B_{j, O_t}$$

**Initialization at $t = 1$, $O_1 = C$ (index 1):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta_1(H) &= \pi_H \cdot B_{H,C} = 0.7 \times 0.8 = 0.56 \\
\delta_1(C) &= \pi_C \cdot B_{C,C} = 0.3 \times 0.3 = 0.09
\end{aligned}$$

**Recursion at $t = 2$, $O_2 = V$ (index 2):**

For state $H$ at $t=2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{from } H: & \delta_1(H) \cdot A_{HH} \cdot B_{H,V} = 0.56 \times 0.6 \times 0.2 = 0.0672 \\
\text{from } C: & \delta_1(C) \cdot A_{CH} \cdot B_{H,V} = 0.09 \times 0.5 \times 0.2 = 0.0090
\end{aligned}$$

$$\delta_2(H) = 0.0672, \quad \psi_2(H) = H$$

For state $C$ at $t=2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{from } H: & 0.56 \times 0.4 \times 0.7 = 0.1568 \\
\text{from } C: & 0.09 \times 0.5 \times 0.7 = 0.0315
\end{aligned}$$

$$\delta_2(C) = 0.1568, \quad \psi_2(C) = H$$

**Recursion at $t = 3$, $O_3 = C$ (index 1):**

For state $H$ at $t=3$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{from } H: & 0.0672 \times 0.6 \times 0.8 = 0.032256 \\
\text{from } C: & 0.1568 \times 0.5 \times 0.8 = 0.062720
\end{aligned}$$

$$\delta_3(H) = 0.062720, \quad \psi_3(H) = C$$

For state $C$ at $t=3$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{from } H: & 0.0672 \times 0.4 \times 0.3 = 0.008064 \\
\text{from } C: & 0.1568 \times 0.5 \times 0.3 = 0.023520
\end{aligned}$$

$$\delta_3(C) = 0.023520, \quad \psi_3(C) = C$$

**Viterbi and Backpointer Tables:**

| $t$ | $\delta_t(H)$ | $\psi_t(H)$ | $\delta_t(C)$ | $\psi_t(C)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.56 | — | 0.09 | — |
| 2 | 0.0672 | H | 0.1568 | H |
| 3 | 0.06272 | C | 0.02352 | C |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Termination:**

$$P^* = \max(\delta_3(H),\; \delta_3(C)) = \max(0.06272,\; 0.02352) = 0.06272$$

$$q_3^* = H$$

**Backtrack:**

$$q_2^* = \psi_3(q_3^*) = \psi_3(H) = C$$

$$q_1^* = \psi_2(q_2^*) = \psi_2(C) = H$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{Q^* = H, C, H \quad \text{with} \quad P^* = 0.06272}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks, Alternative) — **[KTU University Exam – July 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **three fundamental problems of HMMs** clearly. For each problem, name the algorithm that solves it and write the key formula. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the observation sequence $O = \text{U, U, N}$ (Umbrella, Umbrella, No-umbrella) and the HMM parameters from the worked example in Section 3.2 of these notes, compute the **best state sequence** using the Viterbi algorithm. Show every step of the DP table, the termination, and the backtracking. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The three fundamental problems of HMMs are:

**Problem 1 — Evaluation (Likelihood Problem):**
* Given $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$ and observation $O$, compute $P(O \mid \lambda)$.
* Solved by the **Forward Algorithm** (or equivalently Forward-Backward).
* Key formula: define the forward variable
$$\alpha_t(i) = P(O_1\ldots O_t, q_t = S_i \mid \lambda)$$
with recursion
$$\alpha_t(j) = \left[\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_{t-1}(i) A_{ij}\right] B_{j, O_t}$$
and final answer
$$P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_T(i).$$

**Problem 2 — Decoding (Best State Sequence):**
* Given $\lambda$ and $O$, find the optimal state sequence $Q^* = \arg\max_Q P(Q, O \mid \lambda)$.
* Solved by the **Viterbi Algorithm**.
* Key formula:
$$\delta_t(j) = \max_i \left[\delta_{t-1}(i) A_{ij}\right] B_{j, O_t}, \quad P^* = \max_j \delta_T(j).$$

**Problem 3 — Learning (Parameter Estimation):**
* Given $O$ (and possibly an initial $\lambda$), find $\lambda^* = \arg\max_\lambda P(O \mid \lambda)$.
* No closed form. Solved by the **Baum-Welch Algorithm** (a special case of EM).
* Key formula: re-estimate $A_{ij}$ and $B_{ik}$ using
$$\bar{A}_{ij} = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{T-1} \xi_t(i,j)}{\sum_{t=1}^{T-1} \gamma_t(i)}, \qquad \bar{B}_{ik} = \frac{\sum_{t:\, O_t = v_k} \gamma_t(i)}{\sum_{t=1}^{T} \gamma_t(i)}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Using the Weather–Umbrella HMM ($A$, $B$, $\pi$ as in Section 3.2) and $O = \text{U, U, N}$.

**Initialization at $t=1$, $O_1 = U$:**

$$\delta_1(R) = 0.6 \times 0.9 = 0.54, \quad \delta_1(S) = 0.4 \times 0.2 = 0.08$$

**Recursion at $t=2$, $O_2 = U$:**

For $R$:

$$0.54 \times 0.7 \times 0.9 = 0.3402, \quad 0.08 \times 0.4 \times 0.9 = 0.0288$$
$$\delta_2(R) = 0.3402, \quad \psi_2(R) = R$$

For $S$:

$$0.54 \times 0.3 \times 0.2 = 0.0324, \quad 0.08 \times 0.6 \times 0.2 = 0.0096$$
$$\delta_2(S) = 0.0324, \quad \psi_2(S) = R$$

**Recursion at $t=3$, $O_3 = N$:**

For $R$:

$$0.3402 \times 0.7 \times 0.1 = 0.023814, \quad 0.0324 \times 0.4 \times 0.1 = 0.001296$$
$$\delta_3(R) = 0.023814, \quad \psi_3(R) = R$$

For $S$:

$$0.3402 \times 0.3 \times 0.8 = 0.081648, \quad 0.0324 \times 0.6 \times 0.8 = 0.015552$$
$$\delta_3(S) = 0.081648, \quad \psi_3(S) = R$$

**Tables:**

| $t$ | $\delta_t(R)$ | $\psi_t(R)$ | $\delta_t(S)$ | $\psi_t(S)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.54 | — | 0.08 | — |
| 2 | 0.3402 | R | 0.0324 | R |
| 3 | 0.023814 | R | 0.081648 | R |

**Termination:**

$$P^* = \max(0.023814, 0.081648) = 0.081648, \quad q_3^* = S$$

**Backtrack:**

$$q_2^* = \psi_3(S) = R, \quad q_1^* = \psi_2(R) = R$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{Q^* = R, R, S \quad \text{with} \quad P^* = 0.081648}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Notice that the *pointwise* best state at $t=2$ (using $\arg\max \delta_2$) would be $R$ — coincidentally correct here. But for $t=3$, the pointwise best is $S$, also correct. However, if the emission of $B_{R,N}$ were very low, the joint best path may differ from the pointwise best. **Always use backtrack**, never pointwise $\arg\max$.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

* **Best State Sequence** = the Viterbi decoding problem: $\arg\max_Q P(Q, O \mid \lambda)$.
* **Algorithm name:** Viterbi Algorithm (a dynamic programming / max-product algorithm).
* **Three HMM problems at a glance:**
  1. *Evaluation* → Forward ($\sum$).
  2. *Decoding / Best Sequence* → Viterbi ($\max$) ⭐ *(this topic)*.
  3. *Learning* → Baum-Welch / Forward-Backward + EM.
* **Two essential tables** must be filled:
  * $\delta_t(j)$ — best-path probability.
  * $\psi_t(j)$ — backpointer (argument of the max).
* **Initialization:** $\delta_1(j) = \pi_j \cdot B_{j, O_1}$.
* **Recursion:** $\delta_t(j) = \max_i \left[\delta_{t-1}(i) A_{ij}\right] B_{j, O_t}$.
* **Termination:** $P^* = \max_j \delta_T(j)$, $q_T^* = \arg\max_j \delta_T(j)$.
* **Backtracking:** $q_t^* = \psi_{t+1}(q_{t+1}^*)$ for $t = T-1, \ldots, 1$.
* **Complexity:** $O(N^2 T)$ (polynomial) vs $N^T$ (exponential brute force).
* **Numerical stability:** use $\log$ form $\log \delta_t(j) = \max_i [\log \delta_{t-1}(i) + \log A_{ij}] + \log B_{j, O_t}$.
* **Common mistakes to avoid in KTU exams:**
  1. Using $\sum$ instead of $\max$ (turns Viterbi into Forward).
  2. Forgetting to backtrack — examiner will deduct ~7 marks if $Q^*$ is missing.
