  3. Reporting a path that violates $A$ (infeasible).
  4. Confusing $\delta$ (best path) with $\gamma$ (posterior).
  5. Mixing up the dimensions of $A$ and $B$ (rows vs columns of emissions).
* **Real-world applications to remember:** POS tagging, speech recognition, bioinformatics, OCR, channel decoding.
* **Mnemonic:** "**M**ax for **M**ost likely **P**ath" (Viterbi); "**S**um for **S**core of all paths" (Forward).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Word Sense Disambiguation: Methodological Preliminaries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Word Sense Disambiguation: Methodological Preliminaries**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD)

**Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD)** is the computational task of determining which sense of a polysemous or homonymous word is intended in a given context, by selecting the correct meaning from a predefined **sense inventory** (such as WordNet, Oxford Dictionary, or a domain-specific thesaurus).

Formally, given:
- A word $w$ with senses $\{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_k\}$
- A surrounding context $C = (w_{-n}, \ldots, w_{-1}, w, w_{+1}, \ldots, w_{+n})$

WSD aims to compute:

$$
\hat{s} = \arg\max_{s_i \in S(w)} P(s_i \mid C)
$$

where $\hat{s}$ is the most probable sense of $w$ given its context $C$, and $S(w)$ is the set of candidate senses for word $w$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** WSD is treated as the **central pivot problem** in computational semantics. It is formally classified as an **AI-hard problem** and acts as an **intermediate task** that powers downstream NLP applications like Machine Translation, Information Retrieval, Sentiment Analysis, and Question Answering.

### 1.2 Types of Lexical Ambiguity

Lexical ambiguity arises when a single word form maps to multiple distinct meanings. KTU examiners expect students to clearly distinguish between these forms:

| Ambiguity Type | Definition | Classic Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Homonymy** | Identical spelling/sound, *unrelated* meanings | **Bank** → financial institution / river side |
| **Polysemy** | One word, *related* senses via metaphorical extension | **Head** → body part / leader / top of object |
| **Heteronymy** | Same spelling, *different pronunciation* | **Lead** (/liːd/) guide vs. (/lɛd/) metal |
| **Metonymy** | Name of an attribute used for the thing itself | **The Crown** → monarchy / royal family |
| **Categorial Ambiguity** | One word belongs to multiple POS classes | **Run** → verb (I run) / noun (a long run) |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The Multilingual Traffic Sign

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Smart Translator Glasses"**
> Imagine you are walking in Kerala and you see the word "**Bank**" on a board. Your brain instantly decides whether it refers to a place to withdraw money (the financial *bank*) or the edge of a river (the *bank* of Pamba). You do this by **scanning the surrounding context** — seeing words like "ATM, money, account" pushes you toward the financial meaning, while "river, water, boat" pushes you toward the geographical meaning.
>
> Computational Linguistics attempts to teach a machine to make this **same contextual decision** automatically. That decision-making pipeline is **Word Sense Disambiguation**.

### 1.4 The WSD Task Formulations

WSD is canonically split into two evaluation paradigms in KTU pedagogy:

1. **Lexical Sample Task:** Disambiguate a *predefined list* of target words within a small, manually annotated corpus. Example: from the Senseval-2/SemEval competitions, only ~100 words are evaluated.
2. **All-Words Task:** Disambiguate *every open-class word* (nouns, verbs, adjectives, adverbs) in an entire document. This is computationally harder and requires automated sense taggers.

### 1.5 Why WSD is "AI-Complete"

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Many students treat WSD as a simple lookup. It is **not**. To resolve sense perfectly, a system would need *complete world knowledge* and *deep pragmatic reasoning* — equivalent to passing the Turing Test. This is why WSD is sometimes referred to as an **AI-complete problem**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Methodological Pipeline of WSD

Every WSD system, irrespective of its underlying algorithm, follows a **standard 4-stage methodological pipeline**:

1. **Sense Inventory Selection** — Choose the dictionary/thesaurus (WordNet, BabelNet, etc.) whose sense IDs will be the output labels.
2. **Contextual Feature Extraction** — Convert the surrounding words into machine-readable signals (bag-of-words, POS tags, collocations, syntactic dependencies, embeddings).
3. **Disambiguation Algorithm Application** — Apply a rule-based, statistical, or neural model to score each candidate sense.
4. **Evaluation** — Compare predicted senses against a gold-standard annotated corpus (e.g., SemCor, OMSTI) using Precision, Recall, and F-score.

### 2.2 Mathematical Foundation: Bayes' Optimal Decision

The most theoretically important formula in WSD is the **Bayes' Optimal Decision Rule**:

$$
\hat{s} = \arg\max_{s \in S(w)} P(s \mid C)
$$

Applying Bayes' Theorem:

$$
\hat{s} = \arg\max_{s} \frac{P(C \mid s) \cdot P(s)}{P(C)}
$$

Since $P(C)$ is constant for a given context, it is dropped:

$$
\hat{s} = \arg\max_{s} P(C \mid s) \cdot P(s)
$$

Where:
- $P(s)$ = **Prior probability** of sense $s$ (e.g., the most frequent sense of a noun is usually 60–80% likely).
- $P(C \mid s)$ = **Likelihood** — the probability of seeing this exact context if sense $s$ were true.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — questions worth 3–7 marks are routinely framed directly from it.**

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Used In |
|---|---|---|
| $\hat{s} = \arg\max_{s} P(s \mid C)$ | Bayes' Decision Rule | All probabilistic WSD |
| $P(s \mid C) \propto P(C \mid s) \cdot P(s)$ | Bayes' Theorem (post. ∝ likelihood × prior) | Naive Bayes, HMMs |
| $\text{Acc} = \frac{\text{Correct}}{\text{Total}}$ | Accuracy | Evaluation |
| $P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Precision | Evaluation |
| $R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Recall | Evaluation |
| $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | F1-Score (harmonic mean) | Evaluation |
| $\text{CM}(w, C) = \frac{2 \cdot \vert O_v(w) \cap O_v(C) \vert}{\vert O_v(w) \vert + \vert O_v(C) \vert}$ | Concept Mass (Adapted Lesk) | Knowledge-based |
| $\text{CosSim}(\vec{v}_w, \vec{v}_c) = \frac{\vec{v}_w \cdot \vec{v}_c}{\vert \vec{v}_w \vert \cdot \vert \vec{v}_c \vert}$ | Cosine Similarity (vector WSD) | Distributional / Neural |
| $H(S \mid C) = -\sum_i P(s_i \mid C) \log P(s_i \mid C)$ | Conditional Entropy (sense uncertainty) | Information-theoretic WSD |
| $MFS(w) = \arg\max_{s} P(s)$ | Most Frequent Sense baseline | Upper bound for unsupervised |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does a B.Tech engineer care about WSD?**
> - **Machine Translation:** Translating "**bat**" as a wooden stick vs. a flying mammal depends on sense.
> - **Information Retrieval:** A search engine for "**Jaguar cars**" must not return pages about the animal.
> - **Medical NLP:** "**Cold**" the symptom (common cold) vs. "**cold**" the temperature must be distinguished in clinical text mining.
> - **Chatbots & LLMs:** Modern Transformer-based sense representations (BERT, RoBERTa) implicitly perform WSD as a sub-routine of contextualized embeddings.

### 2.5 Supervised WSD as a Classification Problem

In supervised WSD, we treat each sense $s_i$ as a **class label** and the context $C$ as a **feature vector** $\vec{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$. The problem reduces to standard pattern recognition:

$$
f : \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{S}, \quad f(\vec{x}) = \hat{s}
$$

The training set is $\{(C_j, s_j)\}_{j=1}^{m}$ drawn from a sense-tagged corpus like **SemCor** (≈ 234,000 tagged words).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Full Derivation: From Bayes' Decision Rule to Naive Bayes for WSD

We derive the **Naive Bayes WSD classifier**, which is the most commonly asked derivation in KTU ESE papers.

**Step 1 — Problem Statement.**

We want to find the sense $s$ of a target word $w$ in context $C$, where $C$ contains $n$ context features $\{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_n\}$.

$$
\hat{s} = \arg\max_{s \in S(w)} P(s \mid c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_n)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Bayes' Theorem.**

$$
P(s \mid c_1, \ldots, c_n) = \frac{P(c_1, \ldots, c_n \mid s) \cdot P(s)}{P(c_1, \ldots, c_n)}
$$

**Step 3 — Drop the denominator** since it does not depend on $s$:

$$
\hat{s} = \arg\max_{s} P(c_1, \ldots, c_n \mid s) \cdot P(s)
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Naive (Conditional Independence) Assumption.**

We assume that the presence of each context feature is statistically independent *given* the sense. This is "naive" because in real language, "ATM" and "money" co-occur — they are not independent. But the assumption makes computation tractable.

$$
P(c_1, \ldots, c_n \mid s) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(c_i \mid s)
$$

**Step 5 — Final Naive Bayes WSD Rule.**

$$
\hat{s} = \arg\max_{s \in S(w)} P(s) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(c_i \mid s)
$$

**Step 6 — Use log-space for numerical stability** (otherwise, multiplying many small probabilities causes underflow):

$$
\hat{s} = \arg\max_{s} \left[ \log P(s) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(c_i \mid s) \right]
$$

**Step 7 — Estimate the probabilities** with Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) and Laplace smoothing to handle unseen features:

$$
P(s) = \frac{\text{count}(s)}{\text{count}(\text{total senses})}
$$

$$
P(c_i \mid s) = \frac{\text{count}(c_i, s) + 1}{\text{count}(s) + \vert V \vert}
$$

where $\vert V \vert$ is the vocabulary size. The `+1` is **Laplace (additive) smoothing** to prevent zero probabilities for unseen context words.

### 3.2 Derivation: Precision, Recall, F1 in WSD Evaluation

Let the system predict sense $s_i$ for word $w$ in $N$ test instances, of which $TP$ are true positives, $FP$ false positives, and $FN$ false negatives.

**Step 1 — Accuracy:**

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}
$$

**Step 2 — Per-sense Precision** (of all instances tagged $s_i$, how many were correct?):

$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}
$$

**Step 3 — Per-sense Recall** (of all instances that *should* have been $s_i$, how many were found?):

$$
R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

**Step 4 — Macro-averaged F1** (averaged uniformly across all senses, which is the KTU-standard):

$$
F_1^{\text{macro}} = \frac{1}{\vert S \vert} \sum_{j=1}^{\vert S \vert} \frac{2 \cdot P_j \cdot R_j}{P_j + R_j}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of WSD Methodological Families

This comparison matrix is a **high-yield KTU exam table** — memorize the columns.

| Methodology Family | Knowledge Source | Data Requirement | Strength | Weakness | Best Accuracy Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Dictionary-Based (Lesk)** | Machine-readable dictionary glosses | None (unsupervised) | Simple, transparent | Gloss overlap is brittle | 50–65% |
| **Knowledge-Based (Graph)** | WordNet graph structure | None | Uses semantic relations | Sparse connections | 60–70% |
| **Supervised (Naive Bayes, SVM, MaxEnt)** | Sense-tagged corpus (SemCor) | Large labeled corpus | High accuracy | Annotation bottleneck | 70–80% |
| **Semi-Supervised (Yarowsky, Bootstrapping)** | Small seed + unlabeled corpus | Small seeds | Avoids full annotation | Error propagation | 65–75% |
| **Unsupervised (Context Clustering)** | Raw corpus only | None | No labels needed | Clusters ≠ senses | 60–68% |
| **Neural / Transformer (BERT, ELMo)** | Pretrained language model | Optional fine-tuning | State-of-the-art | Black-box, expensive | 80–85% (fine-tuned) |
| **MFS Baseline** | Sense frequency counts | None | Surprisingly strong upper-bound | Cannot beat ceiling | 65–78% |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Lesk Algorithm

**Context:** *"He deposited cash in the **bank**."*

**WordNet Glosses (simplified):**
- **bank#1** (financial): *"a financial institution that accepts deposits and channels those deposits into lending activities"*
- **bank#2** (river): *"sloping land beside a body of water"*

**Overlap computation:**

$$
\text{Overlap}_1 = \vert \{\text{deposited, cash, bank#1\_gloss}\} \cap \{\text{financial, institution, deposits, lending}\} \vert = 1 \text{ (deposits)}
$$

$$
\text{Overlap}_2 = \vert \{\text{deposited, cash, bank#2\_gloss}\} \cap \{\text{sloping, land, water}\} \vert = 0
$$

**Decision:** Since $\text{Overlap}_1 > \text{Overlap}_2$, the system selects **bank#1 (financial sense)**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: WSD Methodological Pipeline

    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    D6 --> E
    E --> F[Argmax over Sense Probabilities]

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

**Q1. Define Word Sense Disambiguation. Why is it considered an AI-hard problem?**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
WSD is the computational process of selecting the correct sense of a polysemous word from a sense inventory, given its context. Formally, $\hat{s} = \arg\max_{s} P(s \mid C)$.
It is considered AI-hard (1 mark) because complete disambiguation requires full world knowledge, deep pragmatics, and commonsense reasoning (1 mark) — equivalent to solving the general AI problem. For example, distinguishing "**bank**" in "He went to the bank" requires knowing the *transactional* vs. *geographical* context (1 mark).

---

**Q2. Differentiate between Homonymy and Polysemy with examples.**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary Exam]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Homonymy:** Two words have identical form but *unrelated* meanings and *separate etymological origins* (1 mark). Example: **bat** (cricket equipment) vs. **bat** (flying mammal) — derived from different Old English roots (1 mark).
- **Polysemy:** A single word has *related* multiple senses arising from metaphorical/figurative extension (1 mark). Example: **head** of a body, **head** of an organization, **head** of a queue — all linked by the metaphor of "top" or "leading position" (1 mark).

---

### **PART B — Essay / Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `Choice 1 — Knowledge-Based & Supervised Track`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the methodological pipeline of Word Sense Disambiguation. List the major sense inventories used. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. **Sense Inventory Selection (1 mark):** WordNet (Princeton), BabelNet (multilingual), Wikipedia, Roget's Thesaurus. WordNet is the most common — it organizes senses as **synsets** (sets of synonymous words) with glosses and semantic relations like hypernymy, hyponymy, and meronymy.

2. **Contextual Feature Extraction (2 marks):**
   - Local features: bag-of-words in a ±5 to ±10 word window.
   - Positional features: distance from target word.
   - Syntactic features: grammatical relations (subject, object) of the target word.
   - Semantic features: domain tags, topic distributions.

3. **Disambiguation Algorithm (2 marks):** Apply a rule-based, statistical, or neural algorithm. For example, the **Adapted Lesk algorithm** computes overlap between context words and the glosses/examples of each candidate sense, then picks the sense with the highest weighted overlap.

4. **Evaluation (2 marks):** Use a gold-standard corpus (e.g., SemCor for training, OMSTI for testing). Compute Precision, Recall, F1. Compare to the **Most Frequent Sense (MFS) baseline** — a strong unsupervised ceiling around 65%.

**Pipeline Diagram (already shown in Section 4.1) — see the 4-stage flow.**

---

**(b)** Explain the **Lesk Algorithm** and the **Adapted Lesk Algorithm** with an example. Compare their effectiveness. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Original Lesk (1986):** Choose the sense whose dictionary gloss shares the maximum number of words with the context of the target word. **(1 mark)**

$$
\hat{s} = \arg\max_{s} \vert \text{Gloss}(s) \cap \text{Context}(w) \vert
$$

**Example:** For "**pine cone**" in *"The pine cone fell from the tree"*:
- pine#1 gloss: *"evergreen tree with needles"* → overlap = 2 (evergreen, tree)
- pine#2 gloss: *"to yearn or suffer"* → overlap = 0
- **Result:** pine#1 is selected (1 mark).

**Weakness:** Glosses are often short, leading to sparse overlaps (1 mark).

**Adapted Lesk (Banerjee & Pedersen, 2002):** Expands the comparison to include glosses of related synsets (hypernyms, hyponyms, holonyms) and uses weighted word overlap. **(1 mark)**

$$
\hat{s} = \arg\max_{s} \sum_{r \in \text{relations}(s)} w_r \cdot \vert \text{Gloss}(r(s)) \cap \text{Context}(w) \vert
$$

**Effectiveness:** Adapted Lesk typically improves accuracy from ~50% (original) to ~65% on standard test sets because of richer semantic neighborhoods. **(1 mark)**

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Original Lesk | Adapted Lesk |
|---|---|---|
| Gloss source | Sense only | Sense + related synsets |
| Overlap weighting | Uniform | Weighted by relation type |
| Accuracy on Senseval-2 | ~50% | ~65% |
| Computational cost | Low | Moderate |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `Choice 2 — Statistical & Neural Track`

**(a)** Derive the **Naive Bayes decision rule for WSD**. Show all steps and explain the role of Laplace smoothing. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

The derivation is given in full in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The examiner expects the student to reproduce:

**Step 1:** Start from $\hat{s} = \arg\max_{s} P(s \mid C)$ **(0.5 mark)**

**Step 2:** Apply Bayes' Theorem: $P(s \mid C) = \frac{P(C \mid s) P(s)}{P(C)}$ **(0.5 mark)**

**Step 3:** Drop $P(C)$ since it is constant w.r.t. $s$: $\hat{s} = \arg\max_{s} P(C \mid s) P(s)$ **(0.5 mark)**

**Step 4:** Naive Conditional Independence: $P(C \mid s) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(c_i \mid s)$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 5:** Final rule: $\hat{s} = \arg\max_{s} P(s) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(c_i \mid s)$ **(0.5 mark)**

**Step 6:** Use log-space: $\hat{s} = \arg\max_{s} \left[ \log P(s) + \sum_i \log P(c_i \mid s) \right]$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 7:** MLE estimates: $P(s) = \frac{N_s}{N}$, $P(c_i \mid s) = \frac{N_{c_i, s}}{N_s}$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 8 (Laplace Smoothing — 2 marks):** If a context word $c_i$ was never seen with sense $s$ in training, the MLE gives $P(c_i \mid s) = 0$, which makes the entire product zero and breaks the model. Laplace (add-1) smoothing fixes this:

$$
P(c_i \mid s) = \frac{N_{c_i, s} + 1}{N_s + \vert V \vert}
$$

The `+1` in the numerator and `$\vert V \vert$` (vocabulary size) in the denominator ensure *no* probability is ever exactly zero, while still allowing rare events to have small but non-zero probabilities.

---

**(b)** Explain the **Most Frequent Sense (MFS) baseline**. Why is it considered a strong baseline in WSD evaluation? Discuss briefly the **Unsupervised and Semi-Supervised approaches** to WSD. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**MFS Baseline (2 marks):** For each target word, the MFS approach simply picks the most common sense as found in a reference corpus (e.g., SemCor). Formally, $\hat{s} = \arg\max_{s} P(s)$.

**Why is MFS so strong? (2 marks):**
- Sense distributions are highly **skewed** — for many common English nouns and verbs, one sense accounts for 70–90% of occurrences (Zipfian distribution).
- A system that always picks this dominant sense automatically achieves 65–78% accuracy *without using any context at all*.
- This sets a *lower-bound target* — any new WSD algorithm must significantly outperform MFS to be considered useful.

**Unsupervised WSD (1.5 marks):**
- **Word Sense Discrimination/Clustering:** Groups instances of a word into clusters based on contextual similarity. The clusters themselves become the senses — *no predefined inventory is needed*. Classic algorithm: **Lin (1998)**, using distributional similarity.
- **Limitation:** Clusters may not align with dictionary senses, making evaluation difficult.

**Semi-Supervised WSD (1.5 marks):**
- **Yarowsky's Algorithm (1995):** Uses a small set of seed collocations (e.g., "**bank**" + "money" → financial sense; "**bank**" + "river" → geographical sense) and iteratively bootstraps labels to new contexts using two principles: (i) *One Sense Per Discourse* and (ii) *One Sense Per Collocation*.
- **Co-training:** Trains two independent classifiers on different feature views (e.g., local vs. global context) and lets them label each other's data.
- **Advantage:** Avoids the expensive full-corpus annotation of supervised learning.

---

### **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks on WSD Questions:**
> 1. **Conflating Homonymy and Polysemy** — Examiners deduct 1 full mark if you mix these up. Always cite the etymological origin as the discriminator.
> 2. **Forgetting to drop $P(C)$** in Bayes' derivation — the denominator *must* be explicitly mentioned as constant-w.r.t.-$s$ for full marks.
> 3. **Missing Laplace Smoothing** — In a Naive Bayes derivation, omitting the `+1` numerator and `$\vert V \vert$` denominator loses 2 marks. This is a "must-include" step.
> 4. **Confusing WSD evaluation** with generic classification evaluation. Always state the test corpus (SemCor / OMSTI / SemEval) and the metric (Macro-F1, not just accuracy).
> 5. **Omitting the MFS baseline discussion** — Any WSD algorithm presented without comparing to the MFS ceiling is considered incomplete by KTU examiners.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read this on the morning of the exam):**

- **WSD =** $\arg\max_{s} P(s \mid C)$; the foundational equation of the entire topic.
- **Two task formulations:** *Lexical Sample* (fixed target list) vs. *All-Words* (every word).
- **Five methodological families:** Knowledge-based, Supervised, Semi-supervised, Unsupervised, Neural.
- **Ambiguity types:** Homonymy (unrelated origins) **≠** Polysemy (related senses). Metonymy is a *pragmatic* ambiguity.
- **Bayes' Decision Rule:** Drop the denominator; maximize $P(C \mid s) P(s)$.
- **Naive Bayes = Bayes + Conditional Independence Assumption.** Use **log-space** to avoid underflow.
- **Laplace Smoothing:** $P(c_i \mid s) = \frac{N_{c_i, s} + 1}{N_s + \vert V \vert}$ — always non-zero.
- **Lesk Algorithm:** Gloss overlap; **Adapted Lesk** adds related-synset overlap.
- **MFS Baseline:** $\hat{s} = \arg\max_{s} P(s)$ — accuracy 65–78%; the universal ceiling.
- **Yarowsky's two principles:** *One Sense Per Discourse* + *One Sense Per Collocation*.
- **Evaluation metrics:** Precision, Recall, Macro-F1; gold corpora = SemCor, OMSTI.
- **Vector / Neural WSD:** Cosine similarity of contextualized embeddings (BERT, ELMo).
- **Sense Inventory = WordNet** (synsets + hypernymy + glosses). Memorize its structure.
- **WSD is AI-hard** because perfect disambiguation requires complete world knowledge.
- **Concept Mass** (Adapted Lesk) and **Cosine Similarity** (Neural WSD) are the two key scoring formulas.
- **Pipeline:** Inventory → Feature Extraction → Algorithm → Evaluation.
- **Downstream applications:** MT, IR, QA, Sentiment Analysis, Medical NLP.

---

**End of Module 3 Notes — Word Sense Disambiguation: Methodological Preliminaries**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Supervised and Unsupervised Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Supervised and Unsupervised Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

In the context of **Computational Linguistics (PEADT632, KTU 2024 Scheme)**, machine learning paradigms are divided based on the nature of the training signal available to a learner. **Module 3** specifically deals with two foundational paradigms that drive almost every NLP system — from spam filters to topic miners.

### 1.1 Supervised Learning — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Supervised Learning** is a machine learning paradigm in which a model is trained on a labeled dataset, where each training instance is a pair $(x_i, y_i)$ consisting of an input feature vector $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ and a known target label $y_i$. The objective is to learn a mapping function $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ such that the predicted output $\hat{y} = f(x)$ minimizes a chosen loss function $\mathcal{L}(y, \hat{y})$ over unseen data.

In Computational Linguistics, the feature vector $x$ typically encodes linguistic properties such as bag-of-words counts, TF-IDF weights, word embeddings, or part-of-speech tags, while the label $y$ may be a syntactic tag, sentiment class, named-entity type, or language identifier.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Sub-Tasks in Supervised NLP**
> - **Classification** → discrete labels (e.g., sentiment polarity: positive / neutral / negative).
> - **Regression** → continuous output (e.g., predicting readability score, translation confidence).
> - **Sequence Labeling** → label per token (e.g., POS tagging, Named Entity Recognition).

### 1.2 Unsupervised Learning — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unsupervised Learning** is a machine learning paradigm in which a model is trained on an unlabeled dataset containing only input instances $\{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$ without any corresponding target labels. The objective is to discover hidden structure, latent representations, clusters, or generative distributions $p(x)$ inherent in the data.

In NLP, unsupervised methods are widely used for **topic modeling** (LDA), **word embeddings** (Word2Vec, GloVe), **document clustering**, and **language modeling**.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Classroom Model**
> Imagine a linguistics professor teaching a child grammar:
> - **Supervised Learning** = The professor shows the child **1000 sentences already tagged** with their parts of speech ("The/DT cat/NN sat/VBD"). The child observes inputs mapped to correct answers, and learns the rule. *(Labeled teaching)*
> - **Unsupervised Learning** = The professor dumps **a million raw English newspaper articles** on the child's desk with no labels. The child, on her own, notices that certain words frequently appear together, that "cats" and "dogs" behave similarly, and that articles cluster into topics like *sports* and *politics*. *(Self-discovery)*

### 1.4 The Hybrid Paradigm — A Quick Mention

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Semi-Supervised Learning** combines a small amount of labeled data with a large pool of unlabeled data. It is highly relevant to NLP because labeled linguistic corpora (e.g., annotated treebanks) are expensive, while raw text is virtually free. Methods include self-training, co-training, and graph-based label propagation.

### 1.5 Comparison Snapshot

| Dimension | Supervised Learning | Unsupervised Learning |
|---|---|---|
| **Training Data** | Labeled $(x_i, y_i)$ | Unlabeled $\{x_i\}$ |
| **Goal** | Learn mapping $f(x) \rightarrow y$ | Learn structure $p(x)$ or clusters |
| **Feedback Signal** | Direct (loss from true label) | Indirect (reconstruction, similarity) |
| **NLP Examples** | POS tagging, sentiment, NER | Topic modeling, word embeddings, clustering |
| **Evaluation** | Accuracy, F1, Precision, Recall | Coherence, Silhouette, Perplexity, intrinsic word similarity |
| **Data Cost** | High (annotation effort) | Low (raw text) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Supervised Learning — Operational Logic

The supervised pipeline in NLP can be broken into **six logical steps**:

1. **Corpus Acquisition & Annotation** — Collect a domain corpus and annotate it with linguistic labels (e.g., IOB tags for NER).
2. **Feature Engineering** — Convert raw text into numerical vectors. Common choices: bag-of-words (BoW), TF-IDF, n-grams, word embeddings (Word2Vec, BERT).
3. **Model Selection** — Choose a hypothesis class: Naive Bayes, Logistic Regression, SVM, CRF, or neural sequence models (BiLSTM, Transformers).
4. **Loss Definition** — Quantify the cost of wrong predictions, e.g., cross-entropy for classification.
5. **Parameter Optimization** — Use gradient descent, SGD, or L-BFGS to minimize empirical risk $\hat{R}(f)$.
6. **Evaluation on Held-Out Set** — Measure generalization on a development/test set using KTU-standard metrics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM) Principle**
> The supervised learner minimizes the **expected loss** over the unknown true distribution $p(x,y)$ by minimizing the **empirical loss** computed on the training set. This is the foundational justification for why supervised training generalizes.

### 2.2 Unsupervised Learning — Operational Logic

1. **Corpus Acquisition** — Gather a large raw text corpus (no labels needed).
2. **Representation Learning** — Map tokens/documents to a vector space (e.g., distributional semantics hypothesis: "a word is characterized by the company it keeps").
3. **Structure Discovery** — Apply clustering (k-means, hierarchical), dimensionality reduction (SVD, t-SNE), or generative modeling (LDA, VAE).
4. **Intrinsic/Extrinsic Evaluation** — Measure cluster coherence, nearest-neighbor word similarity, or downstream task performance.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet** for Module 3 problems. Memorize the symbols and units carefully.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Empirical Risk | $\hat{R}(f) = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}\!\left(y_i, f(x_i)\right)$ | $N$ = number of training samples; $\mathcal{L}$ = loss function |
| 2 | Bayes Decision Rule | $\hat{y} = \arg\max_{y \in \mathcal{Y}} \; P(y \mid x)$ | Choose class with max posterior |
| 3 | Naive Bayes Posterior | $P(c \mid d) \propto P(c) \prod_{w \in d} P(w \mid c)$ | $c$ = class; $d$ = document; $w$ = word |
| 4 | Multinomial NB Likelihood (with Laplace smoothing) | $P(w_i \mid c) = \dfrac{\text{count}(w_i, c) + \alpha}{\sum_{w \in V} \text{count}(w, c) + \alpha \vert V \vert}$ | $\alpha$ = smoothing parameter (typically **1**); $\vert V \vert$ = vocabulary size |
| 5 | k-Means Objective (SSE) | $J = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_j} \Vert x - \mu_j \Vert_2^{\,2}$ | $k$ = number of clusters; $\mu_j$ = centroid of cluster $j$; $C_j$ = set of points in cluster $j$ |
| 6 | k-Means Centroid Update | $\mu_j = \dfrac{1}{\vert C_j \vert} \sum_{x \in C_j} x$ | Mean of all points in cluster $j$ |
| 7 | TF-IDF Weight | $\text{tf-idf}(w, d) = \text{tf}(w, d) \cdot \log\!\left(\dfrac{N}{\text{df}(w)}\right)$ | $\text{tf}$ = term frequency; $\text{df}$ = document frequency; $N$ = total docs |
| 8 | Entropy (Uncertainty) | $H(Y) = -\sum_{y} P(y) \log_2 P(y)$ | Measured in **bits**; $H \geq 0$ |
| 9 | Cross-Entropy Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} = -\sum_{i=1}^{C} y_i \log \hat{y}_i$ | $C$ = number of classes; $\hat{y}_i$ = predicted probability |
| 10 | Silhouette Score | $s(i) = \dfrac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$ | $a(i)$ = mean intra-cluster distance; $b(i)$ = nearest-cluster mean distance |
| 11 | Singular Value Decomposition (used in LSA) | $X = U \Sigma V^{T}$ | $X$ = term-document matrix; $\Sigma$ = singular values |
| 12 | Cosine Similarity (used everywhere in NLP) | $\cos(\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}) = \dfrac{\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v}}{\Vert \mathbf{u} \Vert_2 \, \Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert_2}$ | Used to compare document / embedding vectors |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Computational Linguistics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does KTU emphasize this module?**
> Every modern NLP pipeline — from **Google Translate** to **chatbots** to **search engines** — is built on these two paradigms. Supervised learning powers production **sentiment classifiers** and **named-entity taggers**, while unsupervised learning underpins **pretrained embeddings** (Word2Vec, GloVe, fastText) and **topic discovery** in massive corpora. Mastering both is non-negotiable for an NLP engineer.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Naive Bayes Decision Rule (Supervised)

The Naive Bayes classifier is a direct application of **Bayes' Theorem** combined with a **conditional independence assumption** over features. This derivation is a KTU-favorite.

**Step 1 — Start with Bayes' Theorem:**

$$P(c \mid d) = \dfrac{P(d \mid c) \, P(c)}{P(d)}$$

Here, $P(d)$ is a normalizing constant independent of the class $c$, so for the purpose of choosing the best class we can drop it.

**Step 2 — Apply the argmax simplification:**

$$\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c} \; P(c \mid d) = \arg\max_{c} \; P(d \mid c) \, P(c)$$

This is the **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP)** decision rule.

**Step 3 — Expand the document likelihood using the bag-of-words representation:**

Let the document $d$ be a sequence of word tokens $(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n)$. The likelihood becomes a joint probability over words.

$$P(d \mid c) = P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n \mid c)$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Naive (conditional independence) assumption:**

The Naive Bayes assumption states that the occurrence of each word $w_i$ is conditionally independent of every other word given the class $c$. Therefore, the joint probability factorizes as a product of marginals.

$$P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n \mid c) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid c)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back to obtain the working Naive Bayes formula:**

$$\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c} \; P(c) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid c)$$

**Step 6 — Estimate the parameters from training data using maximum likelihood estimation with Laplace smoothing:**

$$P(w_i \mid c) = \dfrac{\text{count}(w_i, c) + \alpha}{\sum_{w \in V} \text{count}(w, c) + \alpha \vert V \vert}$$

Where $\alpha = 1$ for Laplace (add-one) smoothing, preventing zero probabilities for unseen words.

**Step 7 — Numerical example (KTU-style toy problem):**

> [!NOTE]
> Suppose we want to classify the document $d = \{ \text{“love great movie”} \}$ as *positive* (P) or *negative* (N) using the following training statistics:
> - $P(P) = 0.6$, $P(N) = 0.4$
> - $P(\text{love} \mid P) = 0.4$, $P(\text{love} \mid N) = 0.1$
> - $P(\text{great} \mid P) = 0.3$, $P(\text{great} \mid N) = 0.05$
> - $P(\text{movie} \mid P) = 0.2$, $P(\text{movie} \mid N) = 0.3$

We compute the unnormalized posteriors:

$$P(P) \cdot P(\text{love} \mid P) \cdot P(\text{great} \mid P) \cdot P(\text{movie} \mid P) = 0.6 \times 0.4 \times 0.3 \times 0.2 = 0.0144$$

$$P(N) \cdot P(\text{love} \mid N) \cdot P(\text{great} \mid N) \cdot P(\text{movie} \mid N) = 0.4 \times 0.1 \times 0.05 \times 0.3 = 0.0006$$

Since $0.0144 > 0.0006$, the document is classified as **positive** with a posterior ratio of $24 : 1$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: k-Means Clustering Objective (Unsupervised)

The k-Means algorithm is the canonical unsupervised method. Its derivation begins by formalizing what "good clustering" means.

**Step 1 — Define the objective function (Sum of Squared Errors):**

We want to partition the $N$ data points into $k$ disjoint clusters $\{C_1, C_2, \dots, C_k\}$ so that the within-cluster variance is minimized.

$$J(\{C_j\}, \{\mu_j\}) = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_j} \Vert x - \mu_j \Vert_2^{\,2}$$

Here, $\mu_j$ is the centroid (mean vector) of cluster $C_j$, and $\Vert \cdot \Vert_2$ is the Euclidean $\mathcal{L}_2$ norm.

**Step 2 — Show that the optimal centroid is the cluster mean:**

We minimize $J$ with respect to $\mu_j$ by setting the partial derivative to zero.

$$\dfrac{\partial J}{\partial \mu_j} = -2 \sum_{x \in C_j} (x - \mu_j) = 0$$

Solving this algebraic equation for $\mu_j$:

$$\sum_{x \in C_j} x = \sum_{x \in C_j} \mu_j = \vert C_j \vert \cdot \mu_j$$

$$\therefore \mu_j = \dfrac{1}{\vert C_j \vert} \sum_{x \in C_j} x$$

This is the cluster mean vector — the optimal centroid for a fixed assignment.

**Step 3 — Show that the optimal assignment is the nearest-centroid rule:**

For a fixed set of centroids, each point $x$ should be assigned to the cluster $j^*$ whose centroid is closest in Euclidean distance.

$$j^* = \arg\min_{j} \; \Vert x - \mu_j \Vert_2^{\,2}$$

**Step 4 — The Lloyd's Algorithm (Iterative Procedure):**

The k-Means algorithm alternates between these two optimality conditions until convergence.

1. **Initialize** centroids $\{\mu_1, \dots, \mu_k\}$ (randomly or via k-means++).
2. **Assignment step:** Assign each $x$ to the nearest centroid.
3. **Update step:** Recompute each $\mu_j$ as the mean of its assigned points.
4. **Repeat** steps 2–3 until assignments stop changing or $J$ falls below a threshold.

**Step 5 — Convergence guarantee:**

Because there are finitely many possible assignments ($k^N$) and $J$ is monotonically non-increasing at each step, k-Means **always converges** to a local minimum. (Global optimality is NP-hard, which is why multiple restarts are recommended.)

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Choose Which Paradigm

| Scenario | Best Paradigm | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| You have 50k labeled tweets for sentiment | Supervised | Direct labels available; high accuracy possible |
| You have 10M raw multilingual web pages | Unsupervised | Manual labeling is infeasible; discover latent topics/languages |
| You have 500 labeled + 50k unlabeled reviews | Semi-Supervised | Combine the two to leverage both signals |
| You need to find synonyms in a vocabulary | Unsupervised | Distributional hypothesis: similar contexts → similar meaning |
| You need to predict the next word | Supervised (LM) | Language modeling is supervised over context-target pairs |
| You need to group news articles by topic | Unsupervised | No topic labels available a priori |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Supervised Learning Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following diagram illustrates the end-to-end supervised NLP pipeline as taught under KTU 2024 Scheme PEADT632.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Text Corpus] --> B[Annotation and Labeling]
    B --> C[Tokenization and Preprocessing]
    C --> D[Feature Extraction: BoW, TF-IDF, Embeddings]
    D --> E[Train-Test Split]
    E --> F[Model Training: NB, SVM, Neural Net]
    F --> G[Loss Computation: Cross-Entropy]
    G --> H{Converged?}
    H -- No --> F
    H -- Yes --> I[Evaluation: Accuracy, F1, Precision, Recall]
    I --> J[Deployed NLP Model]

    subgraph TrainingLoop[Iterative Optimization]
        F
        G
        H
    end
```

### 4.2 Unsupervised Learning Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Unlabeled Text Corpus] --> B[Tokenization and Normalization]
    B --> C[Vectorization: Embeddings, TF-IDF, Co-occurrence Matrix]
    C --> D[Algorithm Selection]
    D --> E1[Clustering: k-Means, Hierarchical, DBSCAN]
    D --> E2[Dimensionality Reduction: SVD, t-SNE, UMAP]
    D --> E3[Generative Modeling: LDA, Word2Vec, GloVe]
    E1 --> F[Intrinsic Evaluation: Silhouette, Coherence]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Discovered Structure: Topics, Clusters, Embeddings]
    G --> H[Optional: Downstream Supervised Use]
```

### 4.3 Side-by-Side Paradigm Comparison Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SUP[Supervised Learning]
        S1[Labeled Data: x, y] --> S2[Learn f: X to Y]
        S2 --> S3[Predict y for new x]
    end

    subgraph UNS[Unsupervised Learning]
        U1[Unlabeled Data: x only] --> U2[Discover Structure]
        U2 --> U3[Clusters or Latent Reps]
    end

    S3 --> OUT1[Classification, Tagging, Sentiment]
    U3 --> OUT2[Topics, Embeddings, Groupings]
```

### 4.4 k-Means Convergence Loop (Algorithmic Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K1[Initialize k Centroids] --> K2[Assign Each Point to Nearest Centroid]
    K2 --> K3[Recompute Centroids as Cluster Means]
    K3 --> K4{Assignments Changed?}
    K4 -- Yes --> K2
    K4 -- No --> K5[Converged: Output Final Clusters]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]**
>
> **Q1. Differentiate between supervised and unsupervised learning with one NLP example for each.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

- **Supervised learning** uses a labeled training set $\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ where both the input $x_i$ and the desired output $y_i$ are known. The model learns a mapping $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ by minimizing a loss function. *NLP Example:* **Part-of-Speech (POS) tagging**, where each word is labeled with its grammatical tag (NN, VB, JJ, etc.) and a classifier like CRF or BiLSTM is trained.
- **Unsupervised learning** uses only inputs $\{x_i\}$ without labels and aims to discover hidden structure, clusters, or low-dimensional representations. *NLP Example:* **Topic modeling with LDA** on a news corpus to discover latent themes such as "politics", "sports", and "technology" without any pre-assigned topic labels.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]**
>
> **Q2. State Bayes' Theorem and explain the Naive Bayes assumption used in text classification.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Bayes' Theorem:**

$$P(c \mid d) = \dfrac{P(d \mid c) \, P(c)}{P(d)}$$

where $P(c \mid d)$ is the posterior probability of class $c$ given document $d$, $P(d \mid c)$ is the likelihood, $P(c)$ is the class prior, and $P(d)$ is the evidence.

**Naive Bayes Assumption:** The occurrence of each word in the document is **conditionally independent** of every other word, given the class label. Hence:

$$P(d \mid c) = P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n \mid c) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid c)$$

This simplifies computation dramatically and, despite being a "naive" assumption, often yields competitive accuracy in tasks like spam detection and sentiment analysis.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Pattern, Module 3]**
>
> **Question A (14 Marks)**
>
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **Naive Bayes classifier** for text classification. Derive the Naive Bayes decision rule from Bayes' Theorem and clearly state the conditional independence assumption. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A movie-review classifier is trained on the following statistics for classes *Positive* (P) and *Negative* (N). Classify the review $d = \{ \text{“amazing plot”} \}$ using Naive Bayes with Laplace smoothing ($\alpha = 1$, vocabulary size $\vert V \vert = 6$). *(7 marks)*
>
> | Word | count(w, P) | count(w, N) |
> |---|---|---|
> | amazing | 4 | 1 |
> | plot | 3 | 2 |
> | boring | 1 | 5 |
> | good | 5 | 2 |
> | bad | 0 | 4 |
> | acting | 2 | 3 |
>
> Class priors: $P(P) = 0.5$, $P(N) = 0.5$.

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) [7 marks]** The full derivation is presented in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The student should:

1. State Bayes' Theorem: $P(c \mid d) = \dfrac{P(d \mid c) P(c)}{P(d)}$ — *(1 mark)*
2. Apply the MAP decision rule: $\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c} P(c) P(d \mid c)$ — *(1 mark)*
3. State the **Naive Bayes conditional independence assumption** that words are independent given the class — *(2 marks)*
4. Show the factorized form: $P(d \mid c) = \prod_{i} P(w_i \mid c)$ — *(1 mark)*
5. Give the Laplace-smoothed parameter estimate — *(1 mark)*
6. State at least one real-world NLP application (spam detection, sentiment) — *(1 mark)*

**(b) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Compute the total word counts per class (denominators):**

$$\sum_{w \in V} \text{count}(w, P) = 4+3+1+5+0+2 = 15$$

$$\sum_{w \in V} \text{count}(w, N) = 1+2+5+2+4+3 = 17$$

**Step 2 — Apply Laplace smoothing to obtain the word likelihoods:**

For the words *amazing* and *plot* (which appear in the test document):

$$P(\text{amazing} \mid P) = \dfrac{4 + 1}{15 + 1 \cdot 6} = \dfrac{5}{21}$$

$$P(\text{amazing} \mid N) = \dfrac{1 + 1}{17 + 1 \cdot 6} = \dfrac{2}{23}$$

$$P(\text{plot} \mid P) = \dfrac{3 + 1}{15 + 6} = \dfrac{4}{21}$$

$$P(\text{plot} \mid N) = \dfrac{2 + 1}{17 + 6} = \dfrac{3}{23}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the unnormalized posteriors for each class:**

$$\text{Score}(P) = P(P) \cdot P(\text{amazing} \mid P) \cdot P(\text{plot} \mid P) = 0.5 \times \dfrac{5}{21} \times \dfrac{4}{21}$$

$$\text{Score}(P) = \dfrac{10}{441} \approx 0.02268$$

$$\text{Score}(N) = P(N) \cdot P(\text{amazing} \mid N) \cdot P(\text{plot} \mid N) = 0.5 \times \dfrac{2}{23} \times \dfrac{3}{23}$$

$$\text{Score}(N) = \dfrac{3}{529} \approx 0.00567$$

**Step 4 — Compare and classify:**

Since $\text{Score}(P) = 0.02268 > \text{Score}(N) = 0.00567$, the document is classified as **Positive**. The posterior ratio is approximately **4 : 1** in favor of the positive class. — *(Classification statement carries the final 1 mark.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Naive Bayes Problems**
> 1. **Smoothing denominator:** A common mistake is to forget the $\alpha \vert V \vert$ term in the Laplace denominator. Many students write $\dfrac{4}{15}$ instead of $\dfrac{5}{21}$ and lose 2 marks immediately.
> 2. **Underflow risk:** For long documents, the product $\prod P(w_i \mid c)$ can underflow. In practice, take the **log-sum** $\sum \log P(w_i \mid c)$, but the final argmax is unchanged. Examiners appreciate seeing this remark.
> 3. **Forgetting the prior:** Some students compute only the likelihood and ignore $P(c)$. Always include the class prior in the final score.
> 4. **Vocab size = 6:** Make sure you use $\vert V \vert = 6$ as given in the question, not the number of words in the test document.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Pattern, Module 3]**
>
> **Question B (14 Marks)**
>
> **(a)** What is **k-Means clustering**? Explain its objective function and the iterative procedure (Lloyd's algorithm) used to minimize it. Mention the convergence properties. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Apply k-Means clustering (with $k=2$, Euclidean distance) to the following 5 two-dimensional document-embedding points: $A=(1,1)$, $B=(1,2)$, $C=(2,1)$, $D=(8,8)$, $E=(9,9)$. Use initial centroids $C_1 = (1,1)$ and $C_2 = (8,8)$. Show **two iterations** explicitly and report the final cluster assignments. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) [7 marks]**

1. **Definition [1 mark]:** k-Means is an **unsupervised clustering** algorithm that partitions $N$ data points into $k$ disjoint clusters by minimizing within-cluster variance.

2. **Objective function [2 marks]:**
$$J = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_j} \Vert x - \mu_j \Vert_2^{\,2}$$
where $\mu_j$ is the centroid of cluster $j$.

3. **Lloyd's Algorithm (two alternating steps) [2 marks]:**
   - **Assignment step:** $j^* = \arg\min_{j} \Vert x - \mu_j \Vert_2^{\,2}$
   - **Update step:** $\mu_j = \dfrac{1}{\vert C_j \vert} \sum_{x \in C_j} x$

4. **Convergence [1 mark]:** k-Means always converges to a **local minimum** in finite steps because (i) the number of possible assignments $k^N$ is finite, and (ii) $J$ is monotonically non-increasing.

5. **Practical note [1 mark]:** Global optimality is NP-hard; use **multiple restarts** or **k-means++ initialization** to avoid poor local minima.

**(b) [7 marks]**

**Iteration 1 — Assignment step:**

Compute squared Euclidean distance from each point to both centroids $C_1=(1,1)$ and $C_2=(8,8)$:

- $A=(1,1)$: $d^2(A,C_1) = 0$, $d^2(A,C_2) = 98$ → assign to **Cluster 1**
- $B=(1,2)$: $d^2(B,C_1) = 1$, $d^2(B,C_2) = 85$ → assign to **Cluster 1**
- $C=(2,1)$: $d^2(C,C_1) = 1$, $d^2(C,C_2) = 74$ → assign to **Cluster 1**
- $D=(8,8)$: $d^2(D,C_1) = 98$, $d^2(D,C_2) = 0$ → assign to **Cluster 2**
- $E=(9,9)$: $d^2(E,C_1) = 128$, $d^2(E,C_2) = 2$ → assign to **Cluster 2**

So after Iteration 1: Cluster 1 = $\{A, B, C\}$, Cluster 2 = $\{D, E\}$.

**Iteration 1 — Update step:**

$$\mu_1 = \dfrac{(1,1)+(1,2)+(2,1)}{3} = \left(\dfrac{4}{3}, \dfrac{4}{3}\right)$$

$$\mu_2 = \dfrac{(8,8)+(9,9)}{2} = (8.5, 8.5)$$

**Iteration 2 — Assignment step with new centroids:**

- $A=(1,1)$: $d^2(A, \mu_1) = (1-\tfrac{4}{3})^2 + (1-\tfrac{4}{3})^2 = \tfrac{2}{9} \approx 0.22$, $d^2(A, \mu_2) = 112.5$ → **Cluster 1**
- $B=(1,2)$: $d^2(B, \mu_1) = (1-\tfrac{4}{3})^2 + (2-\tfrac{4}{3})^2 = \tfrac{1}{9} + \tfrac{4}{9} = \tfrac{5}{9} \approx 0.56$, $d^2(B, \mu_2) \approx 85.25$ → **Cluster 1**
- $C=(2,1)$: $d^2(C, \mu_1) = (2-\tfrac{4}{3})^2 + (1-\tfrac{4}{3})^2 = \tfrac{4}{9} + \tfrac{2}{9} = \tfrac{6}{9} \approx 0.67$, $d^2(C, \mu_2) \approx 75.25$ → **Cluster 1**
- $D=(8,8)$: $d^2(D, \mu_1) \approx 74.22$, $d^2(D, \mu_2) = 0.5$ → **Cluster 2**
- $E=(9,9)$: $d^2(E, \mu_1) \approx 96.22$, $d^2(E, \mu_2) = 0.5$ → **Cluster 2**

**Iteration 2 — Update step:**

Cluster 1 = $\{A, B, C\}$ — assignments unchanged, so the algorithm has **converged**. Final centroids are $\mu_1 = (4/3, 4/3)$ and $\mu_2 = (8.5, 8.5)$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — k-Means Problems**
> 1. **Squared vs. Unsquared distance:** Either form gives the same argmax assignment, but be consistent. Mixing them mid-solution costs marks.
> 2. **Stopping criterion:** A common omission is failing to mention **when to stop**. State that iterations stop when assignments no longer change OR when $J$ decreases below a threshold.
> 3. **Local vs. global minimum:** Do not claim k-Means finds the global optimum. It only finds a local minimum.
> 4. **Initial centroid sensitivity:** Different initializations can produce different final clusters. Mention **k-means++** as the standard remedy.
> 5. **Curse of dimensionality:** k-Means struggles in high-dimensional sparse NLP spaces. Mention cosine distance on TF-IDF as a common alternative.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Supervised and Unsupervised Learning (Module 3)**
>
> - ✅ **Supervised Learning** requires **labeled** data $(x_i, y_i)$; goal is to learn $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$.
> - ✅ **Unsupervised Learning** works on **unlabeled** data $\{x_i\}$; goal is structure discovery.
> - ✅ **Bayes' Theorem** is the foundation of Naive Bayes: $P(c \mid d) \propto P(c) P(d \mid c)$.
> - ✅ **Naive Bayes Assumption:** words are conditionally independent given the class — $P(d \mid c) = \prod_i P(w_i \mid c)$.
> - ✅ **Laplace smoothing** formula: $P(w \mid c) = \dfrac{\text{count}(w,c) + \alpha}{\sum_w \text{count}(w,c) + \alpha \vert V \vert}$, with $\alpha = 1$ by default.
> - ✅ **k-Means objective** is **SSE** (sum of squared errors): $J = \sum_j \sum_{x \in C_j} \Vert x - \mu_j \Vert_2^{\,2}$.
> - ✅ **Lloyd's algorithm** alternates between **assignment** (nearest centroid) and **update** (recompute mean).
> - ✅ k-Means **converges** to a **local minimum**; global minimum is NP-hard.
> - ✅ **Cosine similarity** is the standard similarity measure for sparse TF-IDF vectors; **Euclidean distance** for dense embeddings.
> - ✅ **NLP applications of Supervised Learning:** POS tagging, NER, sentiment analysis, machine translation, text classification.
> - ✅ **NLP applications of Unsupervised Learning:** word embeddings (Word2Vec, GloVe), topic modeling (LDA, NMF), document clustering, language modeling.
> - ✅ **Semi-supervised** learning is the practical middle-ground for NLP, where labels are scarce but raw text is abundant.
> - ✅ **Evaluation metrics** differ: supervised uses **Accuracy / F1 / Precision / Recall**, unsupervised uses **Silhouette / Coherence / Perplexity**.
> - ✅ **TF-IDF** is a foundational feature representation: $\text{tf}(w,d) \cdot \log(N / \text{df}(w))$.
> - ✅ Always remember to **mention the Naive Bayes prior** $P(c)$ and to **use $\vert V \vert$ in the Laplace denominator** — these are the two most common KTU mistakes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pseudowords
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pseudowords

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pseudoword (Computational Linguistics Definition):**
> A **pseudoword** (also called a *nonword*, *nonce word*, or *nonsense word*) is a letter string that is pronounceable in a given natural language and conforms to the **phonotactic** and **orthographic** constraints of that language, but which is **not a legitimate lexical item** in the mental lexicon or in any standard dictionary of that language.

In formal terms, given a language $\mathcal{L}$ with lexicon $\mathcal{W} = \{w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n\}$, a pseudoword $p$ satisfies the following properties:

$$p \notin \mathcal{W} \quad \wedge \quad p \models \phi(\mathcal{L})$$

where $\phi(\mathcal{L})$ denotes the set of well-formedness constraints (phonotactic, orthographic, morphological) of language $\mathcal{L}$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy — "The Stranger at the Party":**
> Imagine a person at a party who is **dressed exactly like the guests** (same style of clothes, speaks the same language, behaves politely), but whose **name does not appear on the guest list**. Nobody has met this person before, yet everything about them *looks familiar* and *sounds natural*.
>
> - A **real word** is a *registered guest* whose identity is known.
> - A **pseudoword** is a *well-dressed stranger* — it looks and sounds like a word, but it is not in the dictionary.

When your brain encounters a pseudoword like **"blicket"**, it tries very hard to "log in" the string into your mental lexicon. Because the form follows English rules (consonant cluster + vowel + consonant + vowel + consonant), your brain processes it almost as fast as a real word, but it ultimately fails the lookup. This conflict is precisely what makes pseudowords so scientifically valuable.

## 1.3 Key Properties of a Well-Formed Pseudoword

| # | Property | Description |
|---|----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Phonotactic Plausibility** | Must obey the legal sound/letter patterns of the target language |
| 2 | **Pronounceability** | A native speaker should be able to articulate it without hesitation |
| 3 | **Lexical Novelty** | Must not appear in any standard lexicon or corpus |
| 4 | **Morphological Plausibility** | Should not accidentally form a real word when inflected (or, if it does, the inflection is documented) |
| 5 | **Sublexical Familiarity** | Its sub-word parts (bigrams, trigrams) should match language statistics |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> Pseudowords are foundational in the **Wug Test** (Berko & Brown, 1958), in **lexical decision experiments**, and in modern **language model evaluation** (e.g., BLiMP, SyntaxGym benchmarks).

## 1.4 Distinction from Related Terms

| Term | Meaning | Example |
|------|---------|---------|
| **Pseudoword / Nonword** | Letter string obeying language rules, not a real word | *blicket*, *wug* |
| **Nonce Word** | Newly coined, *contextually used* as if real (may enter language) | *quark* (originally) |
| **Jargon** | Specialized vocabulary of a profession | *parse tree* |
| **Neologism** | A genuinely new word entering common use | *selfie* |
| **Gibberish / Non-sense** | Random, violates phonotactic rules | *xqz*k, *fghjkl* |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Anatomy of a Pseudoword

A pseudoword is constructed by manipulating real linguistic material while preserving the **statistical fingerprint** of the language. The construction pipeline operates at multiple levels:

### 2.1.1 Orthographic Level (Letter Sequence)
The pseudoword's letter sequence is sampled such that the **bigram** and **trigram** frequencies match those of real text in language $\mathcal{L}$.

### 2.1.2 Phonological Level (Sound Sequence)
The CV (consonant–vowel) skeleton must follow the **syllable structure templates** of the language. For English, the legal templates include:

$$CV, CVC, CCVC, CVCC, CCVCC, VC, VCC$$

where $C$ = consonant onset/coda, $V$ = vowel nucleus.

### 2.1.3 Morphological Level
A pseudoword should not accidentally be morphologically decomposable into real affixes + stem, *unless* this is the experimental goal (e.g., the Wug test deliberately uses $-s$ pluralization).

### 2.1.4 Frequency-Neutral Position
Ideally, the **sub-lexical bigram frequency** of a pseudoword should be statistically comparable to the average of real words, preventing a "low frequency" confound in lexical decision tasks.

## 2.2 The Famous "Wug Test" — Berko & Brown (1958)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Wug Test (1958):**
> Jean Berko and Roger Brown introduced the pseudoword **"wug"** to children and asked them to pluralize it. The famous image showed a small bird-like creature labeled *"wug"*, and a second picture with two of them labeled *"These are ___"*. Most children correctly produced *"wugs"* (with the regular /z/ plural ending), proving that morphological rules are **productive** even for unseen forms.

This experiment established that:
1. The human mental grammar contains **abstract productive rules**, not memorized forms.
2. Pseudowords can probe **implicit linguistic competence** that real-word tests cannot.
3. Children as young as 4 years demonstrate rule-governed productivity.

## 2.3 Types of Pseudowords

| # | Type | Construction Method | Example | Use Case |
|---|------|--------------------|---------|----------|
| 1 | **Phonotactic Pseudoword** | Random letter strings obeying phonotactics | *blicket*, *fep* | Lexical decision tasks |
| 2 | **Transposed-Letter Pseudoword** | Swap two letters of a real word | *jugde* ← *judge* | Reading research, dyslexia studies |
| 3 | **Substitution Pseudoword** | Replace one letter with another | *blass* ← *class* | Sub-lexical processing |
| 4 | **Pseudohomophone** | Sounds like a real word but spelled differently | *wun* ← *one* | Phonological vs orthographic processing |
| 5 | **Pseudomorphological Form** | Real stem + real affix, but no attested lexical entry | *unfriend* (now real) | Morphological productivity tests |
| 6 | **Semantic Pseudoword** | Plausible but not real (e.g., brand-like) | *Trilon* | Brand name generation |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Domain | Notes |
|---|---------|----------------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | Well-formed pseudoword condition | $p \notin \mathcal{W} \;\wedge\; p \models \phi(\mathcal{L})$ | Set theory | Definition |
| 2 | Legal English syllable templates | $CV,\;CVC,\;CCVC,\;CVCC,\;VC,\;VCC$ | Structural | Phonotactic |
| 3 | Bigram frequency match | $\displaystyle P(b_i \mid b_{i-1}) \approx \bar{P}_{\text{real}}$ | Probability $\in [0,1]$ | Statistical validity |
| 4 | Orthographic Neighborhood | $\displaystyle N = \sum_{w \in \mathcal{W}} \mathbb{1}[d_H(p,w) \le 1]$ | Integer count | $d_H$ = Hamming distance |
| 5 | Pseudoword Pronounceability | $\displaystyle \text{Pron}(p) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(\text{phon}_i \mid \text{phon}_{i-1})$ | Probability | Higher = more natural |
| 6 | Levenshtein-based similarity to nearest real word | $\displaystyle s(p) = 1 - \frac{d_L(p,\, \arg\min_{w} d_L(p,w))}{\max(\vert p \vert, \vert w \vert)}$ | $[0,1]$ | Quality metric |
| 7 | Wug test pluralization | $/\text{w}\text{ʌ}\text{g}/ \rightarrow /(\text{w}\text{ʌ}\text{gz})/$ | Phonological | Regular $-s$ allomorph |
| 8 | Mean reaction time prediction | $\displaystyle \text{RT} \approx \alpha + \beta \cdot N - \gamma \cdot \log f(p_{\text{sub}})$ | Milliseconds | Lexical decision model |

> **Symbol Legend:**
> $\mathcal{W}$ = lexicon, $\phi$ = well-formedness constraint, $d_H$ = Hamming distance, $d_L$ = Levenshtein distance, $f(\cdot)$ = sub-lexical frequency, $N$ = neighborhood size.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Pseudowords |
|--------|---------------------------|
| **Cognitive Neuroscience** | fMRI/EEG studies isolating lexical vs sub-lexical processing |
| **Reading Disability Diagnosis** | Detecting dyslexia via slow/non-responses to pseudowords |
| **Vocabulary Testing** | Peabody Picture Vocabulary Test uses pseudoword controls |
| **Aphasia Assessment** | Token Test, Psycholinguistic Assessments of Language Processing (PALPA) |
| **NLP Language Model Evaluation** | BLiMP, GLUE diagnostics, SyntaxGym |
| **Spelling & Typo-Correction** | Building training data for noisy-channel models |
| **Brand Name Generation** | Marketers use phonotactic generators to invent unique names |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Orthographic Neighborhood Metric for Pseudowords

> **Goal:** Quantify how "word-like" a candidate pseudoword $p$ is by counting how many real words lie within a small edit distance.

### Step 1 — Define the Lexicon
Let the real-word lexicon be

$$\mathcal{W} = \{w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n\}$$

where each $w_i$ is a tokenized word form in language $\mathcal{L}$.

### Step 2 — Define the Hamming Distance
The **Hamming distance** $d_H$ between two equal-length strings counts the number of positions at which the corresponding symbols differ:

$$d_H(x, y) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \mathbb{1}[x_i \neq y_i], \quad \text{where } m = \vert x \vert = \vert y \vert$$

For unequal lengths, we extend to **Levenshtein (edit) distance** $d_L$, which counts insertions, deletions, and substitutions:

$$d_L(x, y) = \min_{(op)} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \text{cost}(op_k)$$

### Step 3 — Define the Neighborhood
The **orthographic neighborhood** of pseudoword $p$ is the set of real words within edit distance $\le 1$:

$$\mathcal{N}(p) = \{w \in \mathcal{W} : d_L(p, w) \le 1\}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Neighborhood Size
The cardinality gives the **neighborhood count** $N$:

$$N = \vert \mathcal{N}(p) \vert = \sum_{w \in \mathcal{W}} \mathbb{1}\!\left[\,d_L(p, w) \le 1\,\right]$$

> **Interpretation:** A *low* $N$ means the pseudoword is isolated (good for clean experiments); a *high* $N$ means many real-word "look-alikes" (good for stress-testing the mental lexicon).

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example
Let $\mathcal{W} = \{\text{book}, \text{look}, \text{hook}, \text{cook}, \text{rook}, \text{took}, \text{back}\}$ and $p = \text{``blook''}$.

Evaluate edit distances to each $w \in \mathcal{W}$:

$$d_L(\text{blook}, \text{book}) = 1 \quad (\text{insert } l)$$
$$d_L(\text{blook}, \text{look}) = 1 \quad (\text{substitute } b \rightarrow l)$$

All other words have $d_L \ge 2$ (at least two substitutions/insertions needed).

Therefore:

$$N = \vert \mathcal{N}(\text{blook}) \vert = \vert\{\text{book}, \text{look}\}\vert = 2$$

The pseudoword *blook* has an orthographic neighborhood of size **2**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Pronounceability Score from Bigram Probabilities

> **Goal:** Compute a probabilistic pronounceability score for a candidate pseudoword.

### Step 1 — Build Bigram Model
Estimate letter-bigram probabilities $P(c_j \mid c_i)$ from a corpus $\mathcal{C}$:

$$P(c_j \mid c_i) = \frac{\text{count}(c_i c_j \in \mathcal{C})}{\sum_{k} \text{count}(c_i c_k \in \mathcal{C})}$$

### Step 2 — Decompose the Pseudoword
For pseudoword $p = c_1 c_2 \ldots c_n$, extract the bigram sequence:

$$B(p) = \{(c_1, c_2), (c_2, c_3), \ldots, (c_{n-1}, c_n)\}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Joint Probability
The pronounceability score is the joint probability of the bigram sequence under the language model:

$$\text{Pron}(p) = \prod_{i=1}^{n-1} P(c_{i+1} \mid c_i)$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Log-Space
For numerical stability and averaging, use the **log-probability**:

$$\log \text{Pron}(p) = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \log P(c_{i+1} \mid c_i)$$

> **Interpretation:** Higher (less negative) log-probabilities indicate more natural, language-like pseudowords.

### Step 5 — Worked Example
Consider $p = \text{``blicket''}$. Let us assume (illustrative) bigram probabilities:

$$P(\text{l} \mid \text{b}) = 0.04, \quad P(\text{i} \mid \text{l}) = 0.18, \quad P(\text{c} \mid \text{i}) = 0.05$$
$$P(\text{k} \mid \text{c}) = 0.02, \quad P(\text{e} \mid \text{k}) = 0.09, \quad P(\text{t} \mid \text{e}) = 0.13$$

Then:

$$\text{Pron}(\text{blicket}) = 0.04 \times 0.18 \times 0.05 \times 0.02 \times 0.09 \times 0.13$$
$$= 0.04 \cdot 0.18 = 0.0072$$
$$= 0.0072 \cdot 0.05 = 0.00036$$
$$= 0.00036 \cdot 0.02 = 0.0000072$$
$$= 0.0000072 \cdot 0.09 = 6.48 \times 10^{-7}$$
$$= 6.48 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 0.13 = 8.424 \times 10^{-8}$$

In log-space:

$$\log_{10}\!\big(\text{Pron}(\text{blicket})\big) \approx -7.07$$

> **Comment:** A typical English 7-letter real word has $\log_{10}\text{Pron}$ in the range $-6$ to $-4$. Our value of $-7.07$ indicates *blicket* is slightly less natural than average — a deliberate property for experimental stimuli.

## 3.3 Derivation: Pseudoword Generation via Letter Substitution

> **Goal:** Systematically generate a pseudoword from a real word by controlled substitution while preserving pronounceability.

### Step 1 — Choose a Source Real Word
Let $w = \text{``blank''}$ with phoneme structure /blæŋk/.

### Step 2 — Identify Substitution Candidates
Select a non-initial, non-final letter $c_i$ (to avoid altering the word's skeleton too drastically). Let $c_i = \text{``a''}$ (position 3).

### Step 3 — Choose a Replacement Letter
Replace $c_i$ with $c_i'$ such that:
- $c_i'$ shares the same **voicing** and **place of articulation** features.
- The resulting string is still pronounceable.

Let $c_i' = \text{``u''}$.

### Step 4 — Form the Pseudoword
$$p = \text{``blunk''}$$

### Step 5 — Validate
- $p \notin \mathcal{W}$? ✓ (no dictionary entry)
- Pronounceable? ✓ (/blʌŋk/ — valid English CVC structure)
- $d_L(p, w) = 1$ ✓ (good neighborhood candidate)
- Bigram score within acceptable range? ✓ (assumed from corpus)

> **Result:** *blunk* is a valid pseudoword with $N \ge 1$ (it neighbors *blank*, *clunk*, *flunk*, *plunk*).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Pseudoword Generation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Corpus Input] --> B[Letter N-gram Statistics]
    B --> C[Phonotactic Rule Extractor]
    C --> D[Constraint Filter]
    B --> D
    D --> E[Candidate Generator]
    E --> F{Real Word?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Reject Candidate]
    F -- No --> H[Pronounceability Scorer]
    H --> I{Score Above Threshold?}
    I -- No --> G
    I -- Yes --> J[Validate Phonotactic Well-Formedness]
    J --> K[Output Validated Pseudoword]
    K --> L[Store in Stimulus Pool]
```

## 4.2 Taxonomy of Pseudoword Types

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pseudoword] --> B[Phonotactic]
    A --> C[Transposed Letter]
    A --> D[Substitution]
    A --> E[Pseudohomophone]
    A --> F[Pseudomorphological]
    B --> B1[Example: blicket]
    C --> C1[Example: jugde]
    D --> D1[Example: blass from class]
    E --> E1[Example: wun from one]
    F --> F1[Example: unfriend]
```

## 4.3 Experimental Use-Case Map for Pseudowords

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pseudoword Stimulus] --> B[Lexical Decision Task]
    A --> C[Reading Time Studies]
    A --> D[Morphological Productivity Tests]
    A --> E[Neuroimaging Experiments]
    A --> F[NLP Model Evaluation]
    B --> B1[Measure RT and Accuracy]
    C --> C1[Eye Tracking and Self Paced Reading]
    D --> D1[Wug Test and Inflection Tasks]
    E --> E1[fMRI and ERP Signatures]
    F --> F1[BLiMP SyntaxGym GLUE Diagnostics]
```

## 4.4 Lexical Decision Task Workflow (Block-Level View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Trial Onset] --> B[Display String]
    B --> C{Is it a Real Word?}
    C -- Press YES --> D{Word?}
    C -- Press NO --> E{Pseudoword?}
    D -- Correct --> F[Log Hit]
    D -- Incorrect --> G[Log Miss]
    E -- Correct --> H[Log Correct Rejection]
    E -- Incorrect --> I[Log False Alarm]
    F --> J[Compute Sensitivity d-prime]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Update Lexical Processing Model]
```
