<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: Define the term "pseudoword" in computational linguistics. State TWO essential properties that a valid pseudoword must satisfy.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **pseudoword** is a letter string that conforms to the orthographic and phonotactic rules of a natural language but does **not** correspond to any legitimate lexical entry in that language's dictionary or mental lexicon.

**Two essential properties:**
1. **Phonotactic well-formedness** — the string must be pronounceable by a native speaker of the language (e.g., English allows *blicket* but not *xqzk*).
2. **Lexical novelty** — the string must not occur in the standard lexicon or in any reasonable corpus of the language.

*(For 3 marks, the definition carries 1 mark, each property 1 mark.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: What is the "Wug Test"? Who introduced it and what does it demonstrate about human language processing?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Wug Test** is a psycholinguistic experiment introduced by **Jean Berko and Roger Brown (1958)** that uses a cartoon pseudoword *wug* (depicted as a small bird-like creature). Children are shown the picture of a *wug* and then a picture of two of them, asked *"These are ___"*. Most children correctly produce *wugs*, applying the regular English pluralization rule $/z/$.

It demonstrates that:
1. Human linguistic competence includes **abstract, productive morphological rules** that can be applied to never-before-heard forms.
2. Children possess **implicit grammatical knowledge** that goes beyond memorized vocabulary.
3. Pseudowords are powerful tools for probing **competence vs. performance** distinctions in language.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the different **types of pseudowords** used in computational linguistics and psycholinguistics. For each type, provide the construction method and one illustrative example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **lexical decision task** in detail. Explain how pseudowords are used as experimental controls in such tasks, and discuss how the **orthographic neighborhood size** affects reaction times. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Types of Pseudowords (7 marks):**

A pseudoword is a non-lexical string that obeys a language's phonotactic constraints. Six major types are used in CL and psycholinguistics:

1. **Phonotactic Pseudowords** — random letter strings that obey language-specific phonotactic rules. Example: *blicket*, *fep*.
2. **Transposed-Letter Pseudowords** — created by swapping two adjacent letters of a real word. Example: *jugde* ← *judge*, *recieve* ← *receive*.
3. **Substitution Pseudowords** — created by replacing one letter of a real word with a different letter. Example: *blass* ← *class*, *houp* ← *hope*.
4. **Pseudohomophones** — strings that sound like real words but are spelled differently. Example: *wun* (sounds like *one*), *rite* (could be *right* or *write*).
5. **Pseudomorphological Forms** — combinations of a real stem with a real affix that have no attested lexical entry. Example: *unfriend* (now adopted), *happiness* (real — contrast).
6. **Semantic/Brand Pseudowords** — plausible-looking but non-existent words. Example: *Trilon*, *Zoltrax*.

**Construction principles** for each type include matching letter-bigram statistics, preserving syllable templates (e.g., CVC, CCVC), and ensuring non-occurrence in target corpora.

---

**(b) Lexical Decision Task and Orthographic Neighborhood (7 marks):**

The **lexical decision task (LDT)** is a foundational psycholinguistic paradigm in which a participant is shown a letter string on a screen and must press **YES** if it is a real word in the language and **NO** if it is not (i.e., if it is a pseudoword). Reaction time (RT) and accuracy are recorded.

**Role of pseudowords in LDT:**
- They serve as **non-lexical baselines** against which real-word processing can be compared.
- They control for **perceptual and motor** confounds (eye fixation, button-press).
- They enable calculation of **d-prime (d′)**, a sensitivity index distinguishing hits from false alarms.

**Orthographic neighborhood effect:**

The orthographic neighborhood size $N$ (number of real words within edit distance 1) modulates processing:

- **Low N** (isolated pseudowords) → longer RTs and higher accuracy on rejection (clear "not a word" signal).
- **High N** (many neighbors) → faster RTs but more **false alarms** because the pseudoword competes with multiple lexical entries.

Empirically:

$$\text{RT}_{\text{reject}}(p) \approx \alpha + \beta \cdot N(p) - \gamma \cdot \log f(p)$$

where $f(p)$ is sub-lexical frequency. This equation shows RT *decreases* as neighborhood size $N$ increases (because the string is harder to reject).

**Practical implications:**
- LDT with pseudoword controls is the basis of **vocabulary tests** (e.g., Peabody).
- It informs **dyslexia screening** (poor readers show abnormal LDT patterns).
- It is now used in **NLP** to evaluate whether language models make human-like lexical decisions.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** Discuss the role of **pseudowords in NLP language model evaluation**. Mention at least two modern benchmarks that use pseudoword-based stimuli and explain their design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With suitable derivations, explain how the **pronounceability score** of a pseudoword is computed using **letter-bigram probabilities**. Show all mathematical steps clearly. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Pseudowords in NLP Language Model Evaluation (7 marks):**

Modern large language models (LLMs) like GPT, BERT, and T5 are evaluated not only on real-world tasks but also on whether they obey **minimal grammaticality** constraints. Pseudowords are crucial because they isolate **syntactic and morphological competence** from world knowledge.

**Two key benchmarks:**

1. **BLiMP (Benchmark of Linguistic Minimal Pairs of English)** — Presents pairs of sentences differing in one grammatical feature (e.g., subject–verb agreement, anaphor binding). Pseudoword-like nonce words (e.g., *the blicket is sleeping* vs. *the blickets is sleeping*) are used to **eliminate semantic confounds** and test pure grammatical knowledge. The model must assign higher probability to the grammatical sentence.

2. **SyntaxGym / BLiMP multilingual extensions** — Test cross-linguistic generalization using pseudoword stimuli in languages like Hindi, Russian, Basque. Pseudowords in agglutinative languages (e.g., *unblicketing*) probe morphological productivity at scale.

3. **GLUE Diagnostics** — Although primarily real-word, certain sub-tasks use controlled nonce stimuli to probe specific linguistic phenomena.

**Why pseudowords help:**
- They **eliminate lexical memorization** — the model cannot rely on having seen the word.
- They **control for word frequency** — pseudowords have known, neutral frequency.
- They **isolate grammatical rules** from lexical associations.

**Design considerations:**
- Pseudowords must be **phonotactically legal** in the target language.
- They must be **morphologically decomposable** (e.g., allow pluralization probes).
- They must **not collide** with real words in the training corpus (verified via lookup).

---

**(b) Pronounceability Score Derivation (7 marks):**

We derive the bigram-based pronounceability score step-by-step.

**Step 1 — Estimate Bigram Probabilities**

For each letter pair $(c_i, c_j)$ in a corpus $\mathcal{C}$:

$$P(c_j \mid c_i) = \frac{\text{count}(c_i c_j \in \mathcal{C})}{\sum_{k} \text{count}(c_i c_k \in \mathcal{C})}$$

These probabilities are pre-computed once from a large corpus.

**Step 2 — Tokenize the Pseudoword into Bigrams**

For $p = c_1 c_2 \cdots c_n$, extract the bigram sequence:

$$B(p) = \big\{(c_1, c_2),\; (c_2, c_3),\; \ldots,\; (c_{n-1}, c_n)\big\}$$

**Step 3 — Form the Joint Probability**

By the chain rule of probability:

$$\text{Pron}(p) = P(c_1) \cdot P(c_2 \mid c_1) \cdot P(c_3 \mid c_2) \cdots P(c_n \mid c_{n-1})$$

Assuming a uniform letter prior $P(c_1) = \frac{1}{26}$ (constant for all pseudowords), we drop this term for relative comparison and write:

$$\text{Pron}(p) = \prod_{i=1}^{n-1} P(c_{i+1} \mid c_i)$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Log-Space for Numerical Stability**

Underflow is a problem for long strings. Use the log-probability:

$$\log \text{Pron}(p) = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \log P(c_{i+1} \mid c_i)$$

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example**

Let $p = \text{``flen''}$. Assume bigram probabilities:

$$P(\text{l} \mid \text{f}) = 0.02, \quad P(\text{e} \mid \text{l}) = 0.20, \quad P(\text{n} \mid \text{e}) = 0.10$$

Compute:

$$\text{Pron}(\text{flen}) = 0.02 \times 0.20 \times 0.10 = 0.0004$$

In log-space:

$$\log_{10}(0.0004) = \log_{10}(4) - 4 = 0.602 - 4 = -3.398$$

This score can be compared against a reference distribution of real-word scores to flag the pseudoword as **acceptable**, **marginally natural**, or **unnatural**.

**Step 6 — Interpretation and Thresholding**

In practice, a threshold $\tau$ (e.g., $\tau = -5.0$ in $\log_{10}$ for English 4-letter strings) is applied. Pseudowords with $\log \text{Pron}(p) < \tau$ are rejected. This ensures that experimental stimuli are consistently natural-sounding.

> **Examiner's Note:** The key insight in the derivation is the *chain-rule decomposition* of joint probability into a product of conditional bigram probabilities, with optional log-space conversion.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Defining pseudoword as "any random letter string" — *incorrect*; it must be **phonotactically legal**.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (–2 marks):** Confusing pseudoword with **nonce word** or **neologism** — they are not synonyms.
> - **Common Mistake 3 (–1 mark):** Forgetting to **derive** the pronounceability formula in part-(b) questions; merely stating the formula without showing the chain-rule step loses a mark.
> - **Common Mistake 4 (–1 mark):** In lexical decision answers, omitting the **d-prime / accuracy metrics** discussion.
> - **Common Mistake 5 (–1 mark):** Misattributing the Wug test to Chomsky or Skinner — it is **Berko & Brown, 1958**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- ⭐ **Definition:** Pseudoword = phonotactically legal + lexically non-existent string.
- ⭐ **Wug Test (Berko & Brown, 1958):** Establishes **morphological productivity** using *wug* → *wugs*.
- ⭐ **Six Types:** Phonotactic, Transposed-Letter, Substitution, Pseudohomophone, Pseudomorphological, Semantic.
- ⭐ **Lexical Decision Task (LDT):** YES/NO judgment; pseudowords act as **non-lexical controls**.
- ⭐ **Orthographic Neighborhood $N$:** count of real words within edit distance 1; high $N$ → faster RT but more false alarms.
- ⭐ **Pronounceability formula:** $\text{Pron}(p) = \prod_{i=1}^{n-1} P(c_{i+1} \mid c_i)$, computed in **log-space** to avoid underflow.
- ⭐ **Syllable templates** (English): $CV, CVC, CCVC, CVCC, VC, VCC$.
- ⭐ **NLP Applications:** BLiMP, SyntaxGym, GLUE diagnostics — pseudowords isolate **syntactic/morphological competence** from world knowledge.
- ⭐ **Clinical Applications:** Dyslexia screening, aphasia assessment (PALPA, Token Test).
- ⭐ **Key Distinction:** Pseudoword ≠ Nonce word ≠ Neologism ≠ Gibberish.
- ⭐ **Math must-haves:** Hamming distance $d_H$, Levenshtein distance $d_L$, bigram chain rule, log-probability thresholding.
- ⭐ **Examiner's trap:** Always state **both** the phonotactic-plausibility condition AND the lexical-novelty condition in any definition.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Upper and Lower Bounds on Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Upper and Lower Bounds on Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 · Upper and Lower Bounds on Performance

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the evaluation framework of Computational Linguistics (CL), the **performance of an NLP system** is never interpreted as an absolute number. Instead, it is always situated between two theoretical reference poles — the **lower bound (floor)** and the **upper bound (ceiling)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Upper Bound (Ceiling Performance):** The theoretical *maximum* performance a system can achieve on a given linguistic task, typically estimated using *human performance* (inter-annotator agreement), *oracle systems* (perfect-information baselines), or *theoretical information-theoretic limits*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lower Bound (Floor / Baseline Performance):** The *minimum* acceptable or naive performance against which any intelligent system must be compared, typically established by *majority-class prediction*, *random guessing*, or *trivial rule-based systems*.

Mathematically, for any metric $\mathcal{M}$ (e.g., accuracy, F1, BLEU):

$$\mathcal{M}_{\text{lower}} \;\le\; \mathcal{M}_{\text{system}} \;\le\; \mathcal{M}_{\text{upper}}$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Room Analogy"** — Imagine evaluating how well a person can play chess.
> - The **lower bound (floor)** is: a monkey randomly moving pieces (~0% win rate against a grandmaster).
> - The **upper bound (ceiling)** is: Magnus Carlsen playing his best possible game.
> - Any new chess engine we build must show its Elo rating *relative to both extremes*; reporting "Elo 1500" means nothing unless we know the floor (0) and ceiling (~2880).
>
> In CL, when we say "our NER model achieves 88% F1", the examiner wants to know: 88% relative to *what*? If the lower bound (majority-class baseline) is 84% and the upper bound (human IAA) is 96%, then our system is performing **poorly** despite the seemingly high number. **This is the entire purpose of bounds.**

### 1.3 Why Bounds Matter in CL — Core Motivation

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Contextualization** | Raw metrics are meaningless without reference points |
| **Headroom estimation** | Tells us how much *room for improvement* exists |
| **Task difficulty assessment** | Tight upper–lower gap → hard task; wide gap → room for improvement |
| **Research direction** | Wide gap + low system score → model needs work; narrow gap → task is near-saturated |
| **Realistic deployment** | Sets stakeholder expectations for production NLP systems |

### 1.4 Key Metrics Used as Bounds

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard CL evaluation uses these well-established metrics as bound references:
> - **Lower bound metrics**: Accuracy (majority class), F1 (random tagging), WER (noisy channel floor), Perplexity (uniform distribution).
> - **Upper bound metrics**: **Inter-Annotator Agreement (IAA)** — Cohen's Kappa ($\kappa$), Fleiss' Kappa, Krippendorff's Alpha; Human ceiling; Oracle F1.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the Bounds Framework

The evaluation of any CL system follows a strict hierarchical logic:

### A. Establishing the Lower Bound (Floor)

**Step 1 — Identify the task type.**
- Classification (POS tagging, NER, sentiment) → use *class distribution*.
- Sequence labeling → use *unigram tagger*.
- Generation (MT, summarization) → use *copy-source baseline*.

**Step 2 — Compute the trivial baseline.**
- For a $k$-class classification with class frequencies $\{f_1, f_2, \ldots, f_k\}$ and $N = \sum f_i$ total instances, the *majority-class baseline* is:
$$L_{\text{majority}} = \frac{\max(f_1, f_2, \ldots, f_k)}{N}$$

**Step 3 — Verify with a random baseline** (sanity check):
$$L_{\text{random}} = \frac{1}{k}$$

**Why this works:** Any *intelligent* system must beat both. If it does not, the system is worse than a one-line dictionary lookup.

### B. Establishing the Upper Bound (Ceiling)

**Step 1 — Collect multiple human annotations** (typically 2 or more).
**Step 2 — Compute pairwise agreement** using a chance-corrected statistic.
**Step 3 — Interpret against benchmark thresholds.**

For 2 annotators → **Cohen's Kappa** ($\kappa$).
For $\ge 3$ annotators → **Fleiss' Kappa** or **Krippendorff's Alpha**.

**Why this works:** If two trained linguists disagree on 10% of tokens, then no machine can do better than ~90% on that data — human variability is the *information-theoretic ceiling* imposed by the annotation guidelines.

### C. The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why chance-correction?** Because if both annotators and the system always say "NOUN", they "agree" trivially. Kappa removes this artifact.
- **Why multiple annotators?** A single human's accuracy is a *biased* upper bound (that human may be the easiest on themselves). Agreement captures task *inherent ambiguity*.
- **Why oracle?** Sometimes features are sufficient to predict perfectly (e.g., in deterministic morphological tasks), and the oracle tells us the *Bayes-optimal* ceiling.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbols | Typical Value (English CL) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Random baseline (k-class) | $L_{\text{rand}} = 1/k$ | $k$ = number of classes | POS: $1/45 \approx 0.022$ |
| 2 | Majority-class baseline | $L_{\text{maj}} = \max(f_i)/\sum f_i$ | $f_i$ = class frequency | POS: $\approx 0.13$–$0.16$ |
| 3 | Unigram tagger baseline | $L_{\text{uni}} = \sum_{w} P(t^* \mid w) / \mid W \mid$ | $t^* = \arg\max_t P(t \mid w)$ | English POS: $\approx 0.90$ |
| 4 | Observed agreement | $p_o = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i} n_{ii}$ | $n_{ii}$ = diagonal of confusion matrix | — |
| 5 | Expected agreement | $p_e = \sum_{c} \left(\frac{n_{c\cdot}}{N}\right)\left(\frac{n_{\cdot c}}{N}\right)$ | row/column marginals | — |
| 6 | **Cohen's Kappa** | $\kappa = \dfrac{p_o - p_e}{1 - p_e}$ | $p_o, p_e$ as above | $\kappa \in [-1, 1]$ |
| 7 | Per-subject agreement (Fleiss) | $P_i = \frac{1}{n(n-1)}\left(\sum_j n_{ij}^2 - n\right)$ | $n$ = raters per item | — |
| 8 | Mean $P$ (Fleiss) | $\bar{P} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_i P_i$ | $N$ = items | — |
| 9 | Category prob. (Fleiss) | $p_j = \sum_i n_{ij} / (N \cdot n)$ | $j$ = category index | — |
| 10 | **Fleiss' Kappa** | $\kappa_F = \dfrac{\bar{P} - \bar{P}_e}{1 - \bar{P}_e}, \quad \bar{P}_e = \sum_j p_j^2$ | — | $\kappa_F \in [-1, 1]$ |
| 11 | Krippendorff's Alpha (nominal) | $\alpha = \dfrac{D_o - D_e}{D_e}$ | $D_o$ = observed disagreement | $\alpha \in [-1, 1]$ |
| 12 | WER (lower bound for ASR) | $\text{WER} = (S + D + I)/N$ | $S,D,I$ = sub, del, ins | Human: $\approx 0.04$–$0.11$ |
| 13 | Oracle upper bound (F1) | $F_1^{\text{oracle}} = \text{best F1 over rule-set}$ | — | Task-specific |
| 14 | Headroom | $H = \mathcal{M}_{\text{upper}} - \mathcal{M}_{\text{system}}$ | — | Larger $H$ = more room |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Landis & Koch (1977) Kappa Interpretation Scale** (memorize for KTU):
> - $\kappa < 0$ → Poor (worse than chance)
> - $0 \le \kappa \le 0.20$ → Slight
> - $0.21 \le \kappa \le 0.40$ → Fair
> - $0.41 \le \kappa \le 0.60$ → Moderate
> - $0.61 \le \kappa \le 0.80$ → Substantial  ← **typical "good" CL annotation**
> - $0.81 \le \kappa \le 1.00$ → Almost perfect

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

| CL Application | Lower Bound | Upper Bound | Engineering Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| **POS Tagging (English)** | Unigram tagger (~90%) | IAA (~96–97%) | Neural taggers at 97.3% leave 0% headroom → near saturation |
| **Machine Translation** | Copy-source / word-for-word | Human parity (WMT) | Determines when NMT is "good enough" for deployment |
| **Speech Recognition** | Phone-loop WER | Human WER | Sets the floor for industrial dictation systems |
| **Sentiment Analysis** | Always-positive baseline | Crowd-worker IAA | Flags when models exceed human reliability |
| **Information Extraction** | Surface-form baseline | Joint-model oracle | Identifies architectural bottlenecks |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cohen's Kappa ($\kappa$)

Cohen's Kappa is the *chance-corrected* agreement between two annotators. Let us derive it from first principles.

### Setup

Suppose two annotators $A$ and $B$ label $N$ items into $k$ categories. Construct a $k \times k$ **confusion matrix** $M$ where:
- $n_{ij}$ = number of items labeled category $i$ by $A$ and category $j$ by $B$.
- Row marginal: $n_{i\cdot} = \sum_j n_{ij}$ (total items $A$ put in category $i$).
- Column marginal: $n_{\cdot j} = \sum_i n_{ij}$ (total items $B$ put in category $j$).
- Total: $N = \sum_{i,j} n_{ij}$.

### Step 1 — Observed Agreement ($p_o$)

The total items where both annotators agreed is the sum of the diagonal entries:

$$p_o = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{k} n_{ii}$$

This is the **raw accuracy of one annotator measured against the other**.

### Step 2 — Expected Agreement by Chance ($p_e$)

If the annotators labeled independently (using their marginal distributions), the probability that they both label an item as category $i$ is:

$$P(\text{both choose } i \text{ by chance}) = \frac{n_{i\cdot}}{N} \cdot \frac{n_{\cdot i}}{N}$$

Summing over all categories:

$$p_e = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_{i\cdot} \cdot n_{\cdot i}}{N^2}$$

### Step 3 — Chance-Corrected Agreement

The **excess agreement over chance** is $p_o - p_e$. The **maximum possible excess** is $1 - p_e$ (when $p_o = 1$). Normalizing:

$$\kappa = \frac{p_o - p_e}{1 - p_e}$$

### Worked Numerical Example

Suppose 2 annotators, 3 categories (N=V, V, Adj), 100 tokens:

| | B: N | B: V | B: Adj | Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A: N** | 40 | 5 | 5 | 50 |
| **A: V** | 3 | 30 | 2 | 35 |
| **A: Adj** | 2 | 3 | 10 | 15 |
| **Total** | 45 | 38 | 17 | 100 |

**Compute $p_o$:**

$$p_o = \frac{40 + 30 + 10}{100} = 0.80$$

**Compute $p_e$:**

$$p_e = \frac{50 \cdot 45}{100^2} + \frac{35 \cdot 38}{100^2} + \frac{15 \cdot 17}{100^2}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
p_e &= \frac{2250}{10000} + \frac{1330}{10000} + \frac{255}{10000} \\
&= 0.2250 + 0.1330 + 0.0255 \\
&= 0.3835
\end{aligned}$$

**Compute $\kappa$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\kappa &= \frac{0.80 - 0.3835}{1 - 0.3835} \\
&= \frac{0.4165}{0.6165} \\
&\approx 0.6756
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation (Landis & Koch):** $\kappa \approx 0.68$ → **Substantial agreement**. So a POS tagger's ceiling is roughly 0.80 raw, but the *chance-corrected* ceiling is 0.68. A model reaching 0.85 accuracy on this task is performing *super-human* only if measured against the chance-corrected ceiling — a classic KTU-style reasoning trap.

## 3.2 Derivation: Majority-Class Baseline

For a multi-class dataset with class frequencies $f_1, f_2, \ldots, f_k$:

$$L_{\text{maj}} = \frac{\max(f_1, f_2, \ldots, f_k)}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} f_i}$$

### Example: NER on CoNLL-2003 (English)

Class distribution: PER=11.4%, LOC=8.7%, ORG=9.1%, MISC=5.4%, O=65.4%

$$L_{\text{maj}} = \frac{0.654}{1.000} = 0.654$$

So a "stupid" baseline that always predicts "O" (non-entity) gets **65.4% F1-equivalent** without any intelligence. Any system must beat this *and* account for class imbalance. This is why the *macro-F1* (not micro-F1) is used as the upper-bound metric in NER.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Bound per CL Task

| CL Task | Recommended Lower Bound | Recommended Upper Bound | Why This Pair? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **POS Tagging** | Unigram tagger | IAA ($\kappa$ / Accuracy) | Trivial statistical regularity is high; human disagreement sets true ceiling |
| **NER** | Majority-"O" baseline | Inter-annotator F1 | Class imbalance makes "O" a strong floor |
| **Sentiment (binary)** | Always-positive / always-negative | Human IAA | Naive baseline captures class skew |
| **Parsing (constituency)** | Left-branching baseline | Tree-bank IAA | Structural regularity varies |
| **Machine Translation** | Copy-source / word-for-word | Human parity (BLEU) | Fluency floor is set by copy; ceiling by reference translations |
| **Speech Recognition** | Phone-loop / LM-only | Human WER on same audio | LM gives a strong "no acoustic model" floor |
| **Coreference Resolution** | Closest-mention baseline | MUC / B-CUBED IAA | Mention-pair distance sets a non-trivial floor |
| **Word Sense Disambiguation** | Most-frequent-sense | Human IAA ($\kappa$) | MFS is famously a hard-to-beat baseline |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: CL Evaluation Bounds Framework

The following diagram maps the *interactions* between datasets, baselines, models, humans, and bound metrics — the entire evaluation lifecycle in CL.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Linguistic Data] --> B[Annotation Guidelines]
    B --> C[Annotator Pool]
    C --> D[Multiple Human Labels per Item]
    D --> E[Pairwise Confusion Matrix]
    E --> F[Cohens Kappa or Fleiss Kappa]
    F --> G[Upper Bound Ceiling]
    A --> H[Class Frequency Analysis]
    H --> I[Majority Class Predictor]
    I --> J[Random Predictor]
    I --> K[Lower Bound Floor]
    J --> K
    A --> L[CL System Model]
    L --> M[Predictions on Test Set]
    M --> N[Compute Metric M system]
    G --> O[Contextualize M system]
    K --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P{Headroom Analysis}
    P --> Q[M system near ceiling]
    P --> R[M system far from ceiling]
    P --> S[M system below floor]
    R --> T[Model needs improvement]
    Q --> U[Task near saturation]
    S --> V[Model worse than trivial baseline]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[POS Tagging] --> B1[UB: IAA 97 percent]
    T1 --> B2[LB: Unigram 90 percent]
    T2[Named Entity Recognition] --> B3[UB: Inter annotator F1]
    T2 --> B4[LB: Always O baseline]
    T3[Machine Translation] --> B5[UB: Human BLEU]
    T3 --> B6[LB: Copy source BLEU]
    T4[Sentiment Analysis] --> B7[UB: Crowd IAA]
    T4 --> B8[LB: Majority class]
    T5[Speech Recognition] --> B9[UB: Human WER]
    T5 --> B10[LB: Phone loop WER]
    B1 --> X[Bounded Performance Space]

    B3 --> X
    B4 --> X
    B5 --> X
    B6 --> X
    B7 --> X
    B8 --> X
    B9 --> X
    B10 --> X

```

## 4.3 Decision Tree: When to Use Which Bound

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New CL Task to Evaluate] --> Q1{Is human annotation available?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Number of annotators per item?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3[Use literature IAA as proxy]
    Q2 -->|2 annotators| R1[Use Cohens Kappa as UB]
    Q2 -->|3 or more| R2[Use Fleiss Kappa or Krippendorff Alpha]
    Q3 --> R3[Cite domain IAA from related tasks]
    R1 --> S1[Establish UB]
    R2 --> S1
    R3 --> S1
    Start --> Q4{Class balanced?}
    Q4 -->|No| R4[Use macro F1 with majority class LB]
    Q4 -->|Yes| R5[Use accuracy with random LB 1 divided by k]
    R4 --> S2[Establish LB]
    R5 --> S2
    S1 --> Z[Compare M system to UB and LB]
    S2 --> Z
    Z --> Out[Report bounded performance with headroom]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]
**Define upper bound and lower bound in the context of computational linguistics evaluation. Why are both necessary for meaningful system evaluation?**

**Model Answer:**

The **upper bound (ceiling)** represents the *best achievable* performance on a linguistic task, typically approximated by **inter-annotator agreement (IAA)** among trained linguists or by an **oracle system** with perfect access to relevant features.

The **lower bound (floor)** represents the *trivial baseline* performance that any intelligent system must beat, typically the **majority-class predictor** or a **random guesser**.

Both are necessary because raw metrics (e.g., "90% accuracy") are *context-free*. A 90% accuracy is excellent for NER (where majority-class is 65%) but poor for POS tagging (where unigram baseline is 90%). Without bounds, performance is **incomparable across tasks** and **insensitive to task difficulty**. The interval $[\text{LB}, \text{UB}]$ provides the *normalized coordinate system* in which any CL system must be evaluated.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition of UB, 1 for definition of LB, 1 for necessity justification)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]
**Explain Cohen's Kappa with its formula and interpret the value $\kappa = 0.72$ using the Landis and Koch scale.**

**Model Answer:**

Cohen's Kappa is a **chance-corrected agreement statistic** for two annotators labeling $N$ items into $k$ categories, given by:

$$\kappa = \frac{p_o - p_e}{1 - p_e}$$

where $p_o = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i} n_{ii}$ is the observed agreement (proportion of diagonal entries) and $p_e = \sum_i \frac{n_{i\cdot} \cdot n_{\cdot i}}{N^2}$ is the agreement expected by chance from the marginal distributions.

**Interpretation of $\kappa = 0.72$:** According to the **Landis and Koch (1977) scale**, this value falls in the range $[0.61, 0.80]$, which corresponds to **Substantial Agreement**. In CL, this is a typical "good" annotation quality, indicating the linguistic task has been operationalized consistently and the dataset is reliable for training and evaluation.

*(3 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for explanation of $p_o$ and $p_e$, 1 for Landis-Koch interpretation)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full ESE-Style Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> *As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 3 carries internal choice. Both Question A and Question B are below — the student answers ONE.*

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choose OR**

#### (a) [7 Marks]
**Explain in detail the role of Inter-Annotator Agreement (IAA) as an upper bound in computational linguistics tasks. Use POS tagging and Named Entity Recognition as illustrative examples.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition and Significance:**
Inter-Annotator Agreement (IAA) quantifies the degree of consensus among two or more human annotators applying the same labeling scheme. In CL, IAA serves as a **task-intrinsic upper bound** because no machine learning model can systematically exceed the agreement level achievable by qualified humans working from the same guidelines. If humans disagree on 4% of tokens, the data itself carries irreducible ambiguity, and the achievable ceiling is ~96%.

**Why IAA is the *correct* upper bound (not single-human accuracy):**
- Single-human accuracy is *self-biased* (humans agree with themselves trivially).
- IAA captures the **inherent ambiguity** of the linguistic phenomenon, the **granularity of the scheme**, and the **difficulty of edge cases**.

**Example 1: POS Tagging (English, Penn Treebank):**
- Two trained linguists typically achieve **~96–97% agreement** on token-level POS labels.
- Chance-corrected Cohen's Kappa: $\kappa \approx 0.95$–$0.97$ → "Almost perfect" agreement.
- **Implication:** A neural POS tagger reaching 97.3% accuracy is at the ceiling; further gains are statistical noise.
- **Headroom:** A 94% tagger still has 3% room to grow.

**Example 2: Named Entity Recognition (CoNLL-2003 English):**
- IAA on entity boundaries: **F1 $\approx$ 95–96%**.
- Chance-corrected Fleiss' Kappa: $\kappa_F \approx 0.85$–$0.90$.
- **Implication:** State-of-the-art models at F1 $\approx 0.94$ are within 1–2% of the ceiling.
- **Caveat:** IAA is *lower* for fine-grained entity typing (e.g., OntoNotes with 18 entity types) due to taxonomic overlap — a common KTU-style trap.

**Statistical Choices (KTU high-yield):**
- 2 annotators → **Cohen's Kappa**.
- 3+ annotators → **Fleiss' Kappa**.
- Multiple items with partial overlap → **Krippendorff's Alpha** (handles missing data).

**Summary Table (write in exam):**

| Task | Typical IAA | Metric | Ceiling Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| POS Tagging | 96–97% | Cohen's $\kappa$ | Near-saturated |
| NER (coarse) | 95–96% F1 | Fleiss' $\kappa_F$ | Saturated |
| NER (fine-grained) | 85–90% F1 | Fleiss' $\kappa_F$ | Non-saturated |
| Word Sense | 80–85% | Cohen's $\kappa$ | Hard task |
| Coreference | 85–90% | MUC / B-CUBED | Moderate |

*(7 marks: 2 for IAA concept, 2 for POS example, 2 for NER example, 1 for metric choices)*

---

#### (b) [7 Marks]
**Discuss various baseline methods used to establish lower bounds in CL. Why is the choice of baseline critical for valid evaluation?**

**Model Answer:**

**Why baselines are not trivial:**
A common KTU pitfall is treating *any* system comparison as fair. Without a *carefully chosen* baseline, a "good" model may simply be exploiting class skew or surface-form regularity. Baselines reveal whether a model is **learning the task** or **exploiting artifacts**.

**Tier 1 — Trivial Baselines (Universal Floor):**
1. **Random Guesser:** Predict each class with probability $1/k$. Accuracy = $1/k$.
2. **Majority-Class Predictor:** Always predict the most frequent class. For NER: $\sim 65.4\%$; for sentiment: $\sim 80\%$ (on imbalanced data).
3. **Zero Rule (ZeroR):** Predicts only the majority class; useful as a *sanity floor* in any classification experiment.

**Tier 2 — Task-Specific Weak Baselines (Stronger Floor):**
4. **Unigram Tagger (POS):** For each word $w$, predict $t^* = \arg\max_t P(t \mid w)$. On English PTB, this gives $\sim 90\%$ accuracy — a *very high* floor that naive neural models must beat.
5. **Most-Frequent-Sense (Word Sense Disambiguation):** Always predict the dominant sense; famously a hard-to-beat baseline.
6. **Copy-Source / Word-for-Word (MT):** Lower bound for translation BLEU.
7. **Closest-Mention (Coreference):** Link each pronoun to the nearest preceding noun.
8. **Phone-Loop / LM-Only (ASR):** Acoustic-free transcription floor.
9. **Surface-Form Gazetteette (NER):** Lookup-based entity tagger; strong because most entities are capitalized.

**Tier 3 — Strong Baselines (Reference Points):**
10. **Linear/Logistic models** (e.g., logistic regression with hand features) on small data.
11. **Previous SOTA** (when framed as a "must beat" target rather than a floor).

**Why baseline choice is critical:**
- **Statistical validity:** A 2% improvement over a weak baseline is *insignificant*; over a strong baseline, it is *substantial*.
- **Reproducibility:** Without a published baseline number, the field cannot accumulate knowledge.
- **Task calibration:** POS tagging's strong unigram baseline means new models must use *neural* methods; sentiment's weak majority baseline makes the task look "easy" artificially.

**Concluding remark:** A KTU-grade answer must *always* mention the specific baseline, its numerical value, and the *delta* over which the system improves.

*(7 marks: 1 for trivial, 2 for task-specific with examples, 2 for why-choice-matters, 2 for tier structure and KTU trap)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### (a) [7 Marks]
**Derive Cohen's Kappa from first principles and explain why it is preferred over raw percentage agreement in CL evaluation.**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation (reproduced from §3.1 with full steps):**

Let two annotators $A, B$ label $N$ items into $k$ categories. Define the $k \times k$ confusion matrix $M$ with entries $n_{ij}$, marginals $n_{i\cdot}$, $n_{\cdot j}$.

**Step 1: Raw agreement.** Items on the diagonal of $M$ are agreed-upon. Total agreed = $\sum_i n_{ii}$. Normalized:

$$p_o = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{k} n_{ii}$$

**Step 2: Chance agreement.** If $A$ labels item $i$ with category $c$ with probability $P_A(c) = n_{c\cdot}/N$ (their empirical distribution), and $B$ does the same with $P_B(c) = n_{\cdot c}/N$, then the probability both pick category $c$ by chance is $P_A(c) \cdot P_B(c)$. Summing:

$$p_e = \sum_{c=1}^{k} P_A(c) \cdot P_B(c) = \sum_{c=1}^{k} \frac{n_{c\cdot} \cdot n_{\cdot c}}{N^2}$$

**Step 3: Chance correction.** The *excess* agreement beyond chance is $p_o - p_e$. The maximum *possible* excess is $1 - p_e$ (when $p_o = 1$). The ratio:

$$\kappa = \frac{p_o - p_e}{1 - p_e} = \frac{\text{Excess agreement}}{\text{Maximum possible excess}}$$

**Boundary behaviour:**
- $p_o = 1 \Rightarrow \kappa = 1$ (perfect agreement).
- $p_o = p_e \Rightarrow \kappa = 0$ (chance-level agreement).
- $p_o < p_e \Rightarrow \kappa < 0$ (systematic disagreement).

**Why $\kappa$ is preferred over raw percentage:**

1. **Controls for class skew.** In NER, "always O" gives 65% raw agreement — *not* a useful measure. Cohen's Kappa correctly assigns near-zero credit to such trivial agreement.
2. **Enables cross-task comparison.** Two POS datasets with different class distributions can be compared on the *same* $\kappa$ scale.
3. **Theoretical grounding.** $\kappa$ is mathematically equivalent to a one-sided proportion test, providing a sound basis for hypothesis testing.
4. **Standardized interpretation.** Landis-Koch scale provides a *universal* qualitative anchor ($\kappa = 0.6$ means "Substantial" regardless of task).

**Example highlighting the difference (KTU high-yield):**
If both annotators always label "NOUN" on a 5-tag scheme with skewed data, raw agreement = 50%, but $p_e = 0.5^2 + 0.125 \times 4 = 0.5$, giving $\kappa = 0$. The raw number is misleading; $\kappa$ exposes the chance artifact.

*(7 marks: 3 for derivation, 2 for boundary behaviour, 2 for advantages over raw agreement)*

---

#### (b) [7 Marks]
**Compare and contrast upper and lower bounds in POS tagging evaluation. What is the typical headroom available in modern neural POS taggers?**

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table (write this in exam):**

| Aspect | Lower Bound (Floor) | Upper Bound (Ceiling) |
|---|---|---|
| **Source** | Trivial statistical regularity | Human annotator agreement |
| **Specific value** | Unigram tagger: ~90.0% acc. | Inter-annotator: ~96–97% acc. |
| **Metric** | Token accuracy (no correction) | Token accuracy + Cohen's $\kappa$ |
| **Interpretation** | "What is the trivial ceiling I must beat?" | "What is the irreducible ambiguity of the task?" |
| **Trap** | Reporting it as "low" makes the task look harder than it is | Reporting it as "high" hides the true headroom |
| **Headroom signal** | High floor → task is easy; low floor → task is hard | High ceiling → data is clean; low ceiling → scheme is ambiguous |

**Concrete Numerical Landscape (English PTB):**
- **Zero baseline (random):** $1/45 \approx 2.2\%$.
- **Majority-class (NN):** $\approx 13.5\%$.
- **Unigram tagger:** $\approx 90.0\%$.
- **Trigram HMM:** $\approx 95.5\%$.
- **Neural BiLSTM:** $\approx 97.3\%$.
- **IAA ceiling (Cohen's $\kappa$):** $\approx 96$–$97\%$ accuracy, $\kappa \approx 0.95$.

**Contrast: why POS is "near-saturated":**
- The **gap between the unigram floor (90%) and the IAA ceiling (97%)** is only **~7%**.
- Modern neural taggers sit at **~97.3%**, which is *slightly above* the IAA ceiling — this is explained by the fact that **neural models exploit distributional regularities not visible to a single human pass**, effectively acting as a *super-human aggregator* of prior knowledge.
- **Headroom for new models:** Effectively **0% to 0.5%**. The task is *saturated* in English.

**Contrast: low-resource languages differ dramatically:**
- For **Telugu, Malayalam, or low-resource African languages** in Universal Dependencies, the IAA may be only **85–90%** because the annotation guidelines are still being refined, and the unigram baseline may be **70–80%**.
- Headroom is **10–20%**, meaning *significant* model improvements are still possible.

**Key KTU Takeaway:**
> The headroom $H = \mathcal{M}_{\text{UB}} - \mathcal{M}_{\text{system}}$ is the **single most informative number** in CL evaluation. A high absolute accuracy with low headroom (POS English) is less publishable than a lower absolute accuracy with high headroom (low-resource NER). *The system does not exist in isolation; it exists relative to the bounds.*

*(7 marks: 2 for comparison table, 2 for numerical landscape, 2 for saturation argument, 1 for low-resource contrast)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on This Topic — Examiner's Verdict:**
>
> 1. **Reporting raw accuracy without bounds** — The #1 mistake. "Our model gets 92%" with no floor/ceiling earns partial credit at best. Always report a triplet: $(\text{LB}, \text{system}, \text{UB})$.
> 2. **Confusing upper bound with SOTA** — SOTA is the *best published number*; upper bound is the *theoretical limit*. They are different concepts. SOTA may *exceed* apparent IAA ceiling for noisy data — discuss this *carefully*.
> 3. **Using micro-F1 instead of macro-F1 for upper bound in NER** — On imbalanced data, micro-F1 hides class-level information. Macro-F1 is the *correct* upper-bound metric.
> 4. **Forgetting to chance-correct** — Reporting 90% agreement on a 2-class task with 50% prior is *meaningless*. Always use $\kappa$ when the prior is non-uniform.
> 5. **No numerical example in the answer** — KTU examiners award marks for *worked numbers*. A formula without a substitution is worth 1 mark; with substitution and interpretation, it is worth 3.
> 6. **Stating $\kappa$ range without Landis-Koch interpretation** — Always pair the number with the qualitative label.
> 7. **Mixing up "headroom" with "room to improve" intuitively** — Headroom is a *quantitative metric*, not a feeling. Compute it as a percentage gap.
> 8. **Citing kappa for >2 annotators** — Cohen's $\kappa$ is **pairwise only**. For 3+ raters, use Fleiss' $\kappa$ or Krippendorff's $\alpha$. This is a *favorite* KTU trick question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Upper and Lower Bounds on Performance**

- [ ] **Lower Bound (Floor)** = the *trivial* baseline a system must beat. Methods: random guesser ($1/k$), majority-class predictor, unigram tagger, copy-source, MFS.
- [ ] **Upper Bound (Ceiling)** = the *theoretical maximum* a system can reach. Methods: **inter-annotator agreement (IAA)**, oracle systems, human performance.
- [ ] **Two-annotator IAA** → **Cohen's Kappa** ($\kappa$).
- [ ] **Three-or-more annotator IAA** → **Fleiss' Kappa** ($\kappa_F$) or **Krippendorff's Alpha** ($\alpha$).
- [ ] **Cohen's Kappa formula:** $\kappa = \dfrac{p_o - p_e}{1 - p_e}$.
- [ ] **Landis & Koch scale** (memorize thresholds): Slight < 0.20 < Fair < 0.40 < Moderate < 0.60 < Substantial < 0.80 < Almost Perfect.
- [ ] **Majority-class baseline** = $\max(f_i) / N$ — a high value signals class imbalance.
- [ ] **Unigram tagger baseline** for POS ~ 90% in English — much higher than majority class.
- [ ] **Headroom** $H = \mathcal{M}_{\text{UB}} - \mathcal{M}_{\text{system}}$ is the key diagnostic number.
- [ ] **POS Tagging (English)** is **near-saturated**: headroom ≈ 0% for neural models.
- [ ] **NER** has high IAA on coarse types, lower on fine-grained types.
- [ ] **MT** uses BLEU: copy-source is the floor, human parity is the ceiling.
- [ ] **ASR** uses WER: phone-loop is the floor, human WER is the ceiling.
- [ ] Always report evaluation as a **triplet**: $(\text{LB}, \text{system}, \text{UB})$.
- [ ] **Chance correction** is *mandatory* when class prior is non-uniform.
- [ ] **Macro-F1** is preferred over micro-F1 for upper-bound estimation on imbalanced data.
- [ ] **Low-resource languages** have *wider headroom* — research opportunities.
- [ ] **Bounds are task-intrinsic**, not model-intrinsic — they depend on the *data* and *annotation scheme*, not the model.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Supervised Disambiguation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Supervised Disambiguation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3 — SUPERVISED DISAMBIGUATION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Supervised Disambiguation** (commonly known as **Supervised Word Sense Disambiguation / Supervised WSD**) is a machine learning paradigm in Computational Linguistics where a classifier is trained on a **manually sense-annotated corpus** (e.g., SemCor, OMSTI) to predict the correct sense of a polysemous word given its surrounding context. Each occurrence of a target word in the training set is represented as a **feature vector** $X = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ paired with a gold-standard sense label $y \in \{s_1, s_2, \dots, s_k\}$ drawn from a sense inventory such as **WordNet**, **Princeton WordNet**, or **BabelNet**.

Formally, the model learns a hypothesis function:

$$f : X \rightarrow Y, \quad f(X) = \hat{y}$$

such that the expected loss $\mathbb{E}[\mathcal{L}(y, \hat{y})]$ is minimized over the joint distribution $\mathcal{D}(X, Y)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Supervised Disambiguation is a **high-weightage topic** in Module 3 of PEADT632. Examiners frequently test the **Naive Bayes formulation**, **decision list method**, and **feature engineering** for WSD. Memorize the Bayes' rule derivation — it appears almost every semester.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Expert Tutor" Analogy

Imagine you are learning to disambiguate the English word *"bank"* (river bank vs. financial bank). A **supervised** system is like being coached by an expert tutor:

1. The tutor hands you **10,000 sentences** containing the word *bank*, each carefully labeled with the correct sense: `BANK#1 (financial)` or `BANK#2 (river)`. This is the **labeled training corpus**.
2. The tutor points out **clues**: *money, loan, account* usually signal finance; *river, water, fishing* signal geography. These are the **contextual features**.
3. After studying, when shown a *new* sentence — *"I deposited cash at the bank"* — the system calculates the most probable sense based on the learned clues.

This is **supervised learning**: every example comes with a teacher-provided answer. The opposite (no labels) is *unsupervised* disambiguation, where the system clusters senses on its own.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "supervised"?** The model is "watched over" (Latin: *super + videre* = to see over) by a human-curated gold standard. Without labeled data, no supervised system can exist.

### 1.3 Core Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Sense Inventory** | The dictionary of possible senses (e.g., WordNet) |
| **Target Word** | The polysemous word being disambiguated (e.g., *bank*) |
| **Context Window** | The $k$ tokens to the left + $k$ tokens to the right of the target |
| **Feature Vector** | A numerical representation of the context |
| **Gold Standard** | Manually verified correct senses in the training data |
| **Classifier** | The ML model (Naive Bayes, SVM, Neural Net, etc.) |
| **Lesk Baseline** | A simple dictionary-overlap baseline often used for comparison |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** When asked *"What is supervised disambiguation?"* in a 3-mark question, always mention **(a) sense-annotated corpus, (b) feature extraction, (c) classifier training, (d) prediction on unseen data** — these four pillars guarantee full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Pipeline of a Supervised WSD System

The supervised disambiguation pipeline consists of **five sequential stages**:

1. **Corpus Selection & Sense Annotation** — Choose a balanced, representative corpus (e.g., SemCor ≈ 234,000 annotated tokens) and assign each ambiguous occurrence a sense ID from a predefined inventory.
2. **Feature Engineering** — Convert each context into a structured vector. Common feature types:
   - **Bag-of-Words (BoW)** features in a context window
   - **Local Collocations** — bigrams/trigrams around the target
   - **Part-of-Speech (POS) tags** of surrounding words
   - **Syntactic Dependencies** — subject/object relations
   - **Semantic Features** — hypernyms, domain indicators
3. **Model Training** — Feed feature vectors + gold labels into a statistical classifier.
4. **Prediction / Inference** — Apply the trained model to disambiguate unseen instances.
5. **Evaluation** — Measure **Precision (P)**, **Recall (R)**, **Accuracy (A)**, and **F1-Score**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **"All-Words"** vs **"Targeted WSD"** distinction is essential for KTU exams. *All-Words* disambiguates every word in a text; *Targeted WSD* disambiguates only a pre-selected set of polysemous words.

## 2.2 The Bayes Optimal Classifier — Theoretical Foundation

The mathematical backbone of most supervised WSD is **Bayes' Decision Theory**. For a target word $w$ with possible senses $\{s_1, s_2, \dots, s_k\}$ and a context $C$:

$$\hat{s} = \underset{s_i \in S}{\arg\max}\ P(s_i \mid C)$$

Applying Bayes' Theorem:

$$P(s_i \mid C) = \frac{P(C \mid s_i) \cdot P(s_i)}{P(C)}$$

Since $P(C)$ is constant across senses:

$$\hat{s} = \underset{s_i}{\arg\max}\ P(C \mid s_i) \cdot P(s_i)$$

### 2.2.1 Naive Bayes Assumption

To make the computation tractable, the **conditional independence assumption** is applied — every feature $f_j$ in the context is assumed independent of every other feature given the sense:

$$P(C \mid s_i) = \prod_{j=1}^{n} P(f_j \mid s_i)$$

Therefore the final decision rule becomes:

$$\hat{s} = \underset{s_i}{\arg\max}\ P(s_i) \cdot \prod_{j=1}^{n} P(f_j \mid s_i)$$

To prevent **numerical underflow** (a common KTU exam pitfall), this product is converted to a log-space sum:

$$\hat{s} = \underset{s_i}{\arg\max}\ \left[ \log P(s_i) + \sum_{j=1}^{n} \log P(f_j \mid s_i) \right]$$

## 2.3 Decision List Method (Yarowsky, 1994)

A **Decision List (DL)** is an ordered list of rules of the form:

$$\text{Rule}_i : \text{IF (feature condition)} \text{ THEN predict sense } s^*$$

Each rule carries a **log-likelihood score**:

$$\text{Score}(f, s_1) = \log \left( \frac{P(f \mid s_1)}{P(f \mid s_2)} \right)$$

Rules are sorted in descending order of absolute score, and the **first matching rule** is applied at inference time.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bayes Decision Rule | $\hat{s} = \arg\max_{s} P(s \mid C)$ | Core WSD decision |
| 2 | Bayes Theorem | $P(s \mid C) = \dfrac{P(C \mid s) \cdot P(s)}{P(C)}$ | Probability transformation |
| 3 | Naive Bayes Product | $P(C \mid s) = \prod_{j=1}^{n} P(f_j \mid s)$ | Independence assumption |
| 4 | Log-Space Decision Rule | $\hat{s} = \arg\max_{s} \left[ \log P(s) + \sum_{j} \log P(f_j \mid s) \right]$ | Avoids underflow |
| 5 | Prior Probability | $P(s_i) = \dfrac{\text{count}(s_i)}{N}$ | Sense frequency in corpus |
| 6 | Likelihood (MLE) | $P(f_j \mid s_i) = \dfrac{\text{count}(f_j, s_i)}{\text{count}(s_i)}$ | Feature-sense co-occurrence |
| 7 | Laplace Smoothing | $P(f_j \mid s_i) = \dfrac{\text{count}(f_j, s_i) + 1}{\text{count}(s_i) + V}$ | Prevents zero probabilities |
| 8 | Decision List Score | $\text{Score} = \log \dfrac{P(f \mid s_1)}{P(f \mid s_2)}$ | Yarowsky decision lists |
| 9 | Precision | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Sense-specific accuracy |
| 10 | Recall | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Coverage of true sense |
| 11 | F1-Score | $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of P and R |
| 12 | Overall Accuracy | $A = \dfrac{\text{correctly disambiguated}}{\text{total test instances}}$ | System-level metric |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $V$ in Laplace smoothing?** $V$ is the **vocabulary size** (total distinct features in the training set). Forgetting to add $V$ to the denominator is the **#1 most common KTU valuation error** in smoothing questions.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

Supervised disambiguation is foundational to:

- **Machine Translation** (MT): choosing correct sense for "bass" — fish vs. music
- **Information Retrieval (IR)**: indexing by sense, not just by string
- **Sentiment Analysis**: "small" in "small loan" vs. "small problem" carry different sentiment weights
- **Question Answering (QA)**: matching the sense of question terms to document terms
- **Text Summarization** & **Chatbots** — preserving semantic fidelity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Real-world systems (e.g., Google Translate pre-Transformer era) used **Maximum Entropy** and **SVM-based** supervised WSD with hand-crafted features. Modern systems prefer **contextual embeddings (BERT, RoBERTa)** which perform *implicit* WSD via attention.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Bayes Optimal Decision Rule for WSD

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Let the target word be $w = $ *"bank"* with sense inventory $S = \{s_1 = \text{financial}, s_2 = \text{geographic}\}$. Given a context $C$, we must choose $\hat{s} \in S$.

We seek the sense that **minimizes the probability of error** (0-1 loss):

$$\hat{s} = \underset{s_i \in S}{\arg\min}\ \sum_{j=1}^{k} P(s_j \mid C) \cdot \mathcal{L}(s_i, s_j)$$

Under **0-1 loss**, this reduces to:

$$\hat{s} = \underset{s_i \in S}{\arg\max}\ P(s_i \mid C)$$

**Reasoning:** The minimum-error classifier always picks the **most probable sense** in the context — the **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP)** estimate.

---

### 3.1.2 Application of Bayes' Theorem

$$P(s_i \mid C) = \frac{P(C \mid s_i) \cdot P(s_i)}{P(C)}$$

Since $P(C)$ does not depend on $s_i$, it can be dropped from the maximization:

$$\hat{s} = \underset{s_i}{\arg\max}\ P(C \mid s_i) \cdot P(s_i)$$

This is the **fundamental WSD decision rule** that students must memorize verbatim for KTU exams.

---

### 3.1.3 Naive Bayes Independence Assumption

We model the context $C$ as a multiset of features $F = \{f_1, f_2, \dots, f_n\}$ extracted from a window of $\pm k$ tokens around the target. The naive assumption states:

$$P(C \mid s_i) = P(f_1, f_2, \dots, f_n \mid s_i) = \prod_{j=1}^{n} P(f_j \mid s_i)$$

Substituting back:

$$\hat{s} = \underset{s_i}{\arg\max}\ P(s_i) \cdot \prod_{j=1}^{n} P(f_j \mid s_i)$$

---

### 3.1.4 Log-Space Transformation

Computing the product $\prod_{j=1}^{n} P(f_j \mid s_i)$ for large $n$ (e.g., $n = 50$ features) causes **floating-point underflow** when probabilities are small. The standard fix is to take the **monotonic logarithm**:

$$\log\left(P(s_i) \cdot \prod_{j=1}^{n} P(f_j \mid s_i)\right) = \log P(s_i) + \sum_{j=1}^{n} \log P(f_j \mid s_i)$$

Because $\log(\cdot)$ is monotonically increasing, the argmax is preserved:

$$\hat{s} = \underset{s_i}{\arg\max}\ \left[ \log P(s_i) + \sum_{j=1}^{n} \log P(f_j \mid s_i) \right]$$

---

### 3.1.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Exam Style)

**Training Data (small):**

| Sentence | Sense |
|---|---|
| "money bank account" | $s_1$ (financial) |
| "river bank fishing" | $s_2$ (geographic) |
| "bank loan money" | $s_1$ (financial) |
| "bank water river" | $s_2$ (geographic) |

**Vocabulary** $V = \{money, account, loan, river, fishing, water\}$, $|V| = 6$.

**Test sentence:** *"bank river money"*

**Step 1 — Compute Priors:**
$P(s_1) = \dfrac{2}{4} = 0.5, \quad P(s_2) = \dfrac{2}{4} = 0.5$

**Step 2 — Compute Likelihoods (raw MLE):**

For $s_1$ (financial, 6 feature tokens across 2 sentences):
- $\text{count}(money, s_1) = 2, \quad \text{count}(account, s_1) = 1, \quad \text{count}(loan, s_1) = 1$
- $\text{count}(river, s_1) = 0, \quad \text{count}(fishing, s_1) = 0, \quad \text{count}(water, s_1) = 0$

For $s_2$ (geographic, 6 feature tokens):
- $\text{count}(river, s_2) = 2, \quad \text{count}(water, s_2) = 1, \quad \text{count}(fishing, s_2) = 1$
- All other counts = 0

**Step 3 — Apply Laplace Smoothing:**

$$P(f_j \mid s_i) = \frac{\text{count}(f_j, s_i) + 1}{\text{count}(s_i) + V} = \frac{\text{count}(f_j, s_i) + 1}{6 + 6} = \frac{\text{count}(f_j, s_i) + 1}{12}$$

**Step 4 — Likelihood Table:**

| Feature | $P(f \mid s_1)$ | $P(f \mid s_2)$ |
|---|---|---|
| money | $3/12 = 0.250$ | $1/12 \approx 0.083$ |
| river | $1/12 \approx 0.083$ | $3/12 = 0.250$ |
| account | $2/12 \approx 0.167$ | $1/12 \approx 0.083$ |
| loan | $2/12 \approx 0.167$ | $1/12 \approx 0.083$ |
| fishing | $1/12 \approx 0.083$ | $2/12 \approx 0.167$ |
| water | $1/12 \approx 0.083$ | $2/12 \approx 0.167$ |

**Step 5 — Compute Scores for Test Sentence** *"bank river money"* (features: $river, money$):

$$\text{Score}(s_1) = \log(0.5) + \log(0.083) + \log(0.250)$$
$$\text{Score}(s_1) = -0.693 + (-2.489) + (-1.386) = -4.568$$

$$\text{Score}(s_2) = \log(0.5) + \log(0.250) + \log(0.083)$$
$$\text{Score}(s_2) = -0.693 + (-1.386) + (-2.489) = -4.568$$

**Step 6 — Tie Resolution:**

In case of a tie, Naive Bayes defaults to the **most frequent sense (MFS)** baseline — which often acts as a strong fallback in real systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Marker Expectation:** Even in case of a tie, students must **show all 6 steps explicitly** as above. Skipping the Laplace smoothing step (Step 3) costs 2 marks. Forgetting to convert to log-space costs 1 mark.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Supervised vs Unsupervised vs Knowledge-Based WSD

| Dimension | Supervised WSD | Unsupervised WSD | Knowledge-Based WSD |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Required** | Sense-annotated corpus | Raw unannotated text | Dictionary / Ontology (WordNet) |
| **Sense Inventory** | External (e.g., WordNet) | Induced from clustering | External (e.g., WordNet) |
| **Algorithm** | Naive Bayes, SVM, DL, Neural | k-means, EM, LSA | Lesk, Walker's algorithm |
| **Accuracy (typical)** | **Highest (60–70% F1)** | Moderate (50–60%) | Moderate (50–65%) |
| **Major Bottleneck** | Knowledge Acquisition Bottleneck | Sense granularity issues | Coverage of knowledge base |
| **Corpus Example** | SemCor, OMSTI | Wikipedia dumps | WordNet glosses |
| **Scalability** | Low (per-word model) | High | Medium |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Knowledge Acquisition Bottleneck** — the manual cost of annotating large sense corpora — is the single biggest weakness of supervised WSD and a **favourite 3-mark KTU question**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Supervised WSD Pipeline (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Annotated Corpus e.g. SemCor] --> B[Preprocessing Tokenization and POS Tagging]
    B --> C[Context Window Extraction plus k equals 5]
    C --> D[Feature Engineering BoW, Collocations, POS, Dependencies]
    D --> E[Feature Vector Representation]
    E --> F[Train Test Split 80 by 20]
    F --> G[Supervised Classifier Training Naive Bayes, SVM, Decision List, Neural Net]
    G --> H[Trained Model with Learned Parameters theta]
    H --> I[New Unseen Sentence Input]
    I --> J[Same Feature Pipeline applied to test sentence]
    J --> K[Inference argmax over sense posterior]
    K --> L[Predicted Sense Label]
    L --> M[Evaluation Module Precision, Recall, F1, Accuracy]
    M --> N[Output Results and Confusion Matrix]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** The left vertical branch (A → G) is the **offline training phase**. The right vertical branch (I → K) is the **online inference phase**. The **trained model $\theta$** is the bridge between them. KTU examiners often ask students to label which stages belong to training vs. testing.

## 4.2 Decision List Inference Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Test Instance arrives with context] --> B[Extract all candidate features]
    B --> C[Iterate through sorted Decision List]
    C --> D{Rule matched}
    D -- Yes --> E[Predict sense from that rule]
    D -- No --> F[Move to next rule in list]
    F --> C
    C --> G[End of list reached]
    G --> H{Default rule exists}
    H -- Yes --> I[Apply Most Frequent Sense MFS baseline]
    H -- No --> J[Output default MFS prediction]
    E --> K[Final Predicted Sense]
    I --> K
    J --> K
```

## 4.3 Confusion Matrix Structure (WSD Evaluation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Predicted Sense s1] -->|TP s1 s1| B[True Positive s1]
    A -->|FN s1 s2| C[False Negative s2 as s1]
    A -->|FP s1 s3| D[False Positive s3 as s1]
    E[Predicted Sense s2] -->|TN s2 s2| F[True Negative s2]
    E -->|FN s2 s1| G[False Negative s1 as s2]
    H[Predicted Sense s3] -->|TN s3 s3| I[True Negative s3]
    H -->|FP s3 s1| J[False Positive s1 as s3]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** In multi-class WSD (k senses), "True Negative" is **not a single cell** but the sum of all off-diagonal correct rejections. Many students mistakenly report $TN$ from the diagonal — this loses marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** Define Supervised Disambiguation. Mention any two features used in supervised WSD.
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Supervised Disambiguation is a corpus-based approach to Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD) in which a statistical or neural classifier is trained on a **manually sense-annotated corpus** to predict the correct sense of a polysemous word from its surrounding context.

Two commonly used features are:
1. **Bag-of-Words (BoW) features** — the presence or frequency of words in a context window of $\pm k$ tokens around the target.
2. **Local Collocations** — ordered bigrams or trigrams (e.g., *target_word +1*, *target_word -1*) that capture local syntactic patterns.

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark per feature = 3 marks)*

---

### **Q2.** What is the Naive Bayes assumption in the context of WSD? Why is it "naive"?
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **Naive Bayes assumption** states that, given a target word's sense $s_i$, all features in the surrounding context are **conditionally independent** of each other:

$$P(f_1, f_2, \dots, f_n \mid s_i) = \prod_{j=1}^{n} P(f_j \mid s_i)$$

It is called **"naive"** because in real natural language, contextual features are *highly correlated* (e.g., the word *money* often co-occurs with *bank* and *account* — these are not independent). The assumption simplifies computation drastically, allowing a tractable product of likelihoods, even though it is a strong and often linguistically false simplification.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a Supervised Word Sense Disambiguation system with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of each module in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Apply the Naive Bayes classifier to disambiguate the word *plant* in the sentence *"The plant near the factory grows fast"* given the following mini-training corpus. Use Laplace smoothing with $V = 5$. Show all steps. **(7 Marks)**

| Training Sentence | Sense |
|---|---|
| "factory plant production" | $s_1$ (industrial) |
| "green plant leaf garden" | $s_2$ (living) |
| "plant factory chemical" | $s_1$ (industrial) |
| "plant grow garden" | $s_2$ (living) |

#### Model Solution

**(a) Architecture of Supervised WSD System (7 Marks):**

A supervised WSD system consists of the following modules (refer to the diagram in Section 4.1):

1. **Corpus Acquisition & Annotation Module** — Collects and manually annotates a training corpus with sense labels from a predefined inventory such as WordNet. *(1 mark)*
2. **Preprocessing Module** — Performs tokenization, sentence splitting, and POS tagging. *(1 mark)*
3. **Context Window Module** — Extracts a fixed window of $\pm k$ tokens around each target word occurrence. *(1 mark)*
4. **Feature Engineering Module** — Converts the context into a structured feature vector using BoW, collocations, POS tags, and syntactic dependencies. *(1 mark)*
5. **Classifier Training Module** — Feeds the feature vectors and gold labels to a learning algorithm (Naive Bayes, SVM, Decision List, Neural Net). The model learns parameters $\theta$ that maximize the posterior $P(s \mid C)$. *(1.5 marks)*
6. **Inference & Evaluation Module** — Predicts senses for unseen test data and computes Precision, Recall, F1, and Accuracy. *(1.5 marks)*

---

**(b) Naive Bayes Worked Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Feature Extraction for Test Sentence:**

Test sentence: *"The **plant** near the factory grows fast"*

Context window (excluding function words): $\{near, factory, grows, fast\}$. However, taking the **two most informative features** for clarity: $\{factory, grows\}$.

**Step 2 — Compute Priors:**

$$P(s_1) = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5, \quad P(s_2) = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5$$

**Step 3 — Count Feature Occurrences per Sense:**

For $s_1$ (industrial): features observed = {factory, plant, production, plant, factory, chemical}
- $\text{count}(factory, s_1) = 2$, $\text{count}(plant, s_1) = 2$, $\text{count}(production, s_1) = 1$, $\text{count}(chemical, s_1) = 1$
- $\text{count}(grows, s_1) = 0$, $\text{count}(green, s_1) = 0$, $\text{count}(leaf, s_1) = 0$, $\text{count}(garden, s_1) = 0$, $\text{count}(grow, s_1) = 0$
- Total tokens for $s_1$: $N_1 = 6$

For $s_2$ (living): features = {green, plant, leaf, garden, plant, grow, garden}
- $\text{count}(green, s_2) = 1$, $\text{count}(plant, s_2) = 2$, $\text{count}(leaf, s_2) = 1$, $\text{count}(garden, s_2) = 2$, $\text{count}(grow, s_2) = 1$
- $\text{count}(factory, s_2) = 0$, $\text{count}(production, s_2) = 0$, $\text{count}(chemical, s_2) = 0$, $\text{count}(grows, s_2) = 0$
- Total tokens for $s_2$: $N_2 = 7$

**Step 4 — Apply Laplace Smoothing with $V = 5$:**

$$P(f \mid s_i) = \frac{\text{count}(f, s_i) + 1}{N_i + V}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Required Likelihoods:**

$$P(factory \mid s_1) = \frac{2 + 1}{6 + 5} = \frac{3}{11} \approx 0.273$$

$$P(factory \mid s_2) = \frac{0 + 1}{7 + 5} = \frac{1}{12} \approx 0.083$$

$$P(grows \mid s_1) = \frac{0 + 1}{6 + 5} = \frac{1}{11} \approx 0.091$$

$$P(grows \mid s_2) = \frac{0 + 1}{7 + 5} = \frac{1}{12} \approx 0.083$$

**Step 6 — Compute Posterior Scores (Log-Space):**

$$\text{Score}(s_1) = \log(0.5) + \log(0.273) + \log(0.091)$$
$$\text{Score}(s_1) = -0.693 + (-1.299) + (-2.398) = -4.390$$

$$\text{Score}(s_2) = \log(0.5) + \log(0.083) + \log(0.083)$$
$$\text{Score}(s_2) = -0.693 + (-2.489) + (-2.489) = -5.671$$

**Step 7 — Decision:**

Since $\text{Score}(s_1) = -4.390 > \text{Score}(s_2) = -5.671$, the classifier predicts **$s_1$ (industrial sense)** — which is the correct sense for *"plant near the factory"*. ✅

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the Decision List method of Yarowsky for supervised WSD. How are decision rules ranked? Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the major challenges and limitations of Supervised Disambiguation. How do these motivate the use of Semi-Supervised and Unsupervised approaches? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution Outline

**(a) Decision List Method (7 Marks):**
- Definition: A **Decision List** is an ordered, exhaustive list of `IF-THEN` rules used for WSD. *(1 mark)*
- Each rule contains a feature and the sense it predicts. *(1 mark)*
- **Ranking via log-likelihood score:** $\text{Score} = \log \dfrac{P(f \mid s_1)}{P(f \mid s_2)}$ — absolute value determines rank. *(2 marks)*
- At inference, the **first matching rule** is applied; if no rule matches, the **Most Frequent Sense (MFS)** baseline is used. *(1 mark)*
- **Advantages:** Interpretable, easy to implement, no need for full probabilistic model. **Limitations:** Binary sense assumption in the original formulation, sensitive to noisy data. *(2 marks)*

**(b) Challenges & Motivation (7 Marks):**
- **Knowledge Acquisition Bottleneck** — manual annotation is expensive. *(2 marks)*
- **Sparsity** — many sense-feature combinations never occur in training. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Domain Dependence** — models trained on news fail on biomedical text. *(1.5 marks)*
- These motivate **Semi-Supervised** (e.g., bootstrapping with a small seed) and **Unsupervised** (clustering) methods that reduce annotation cost. *(2 marks)*

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Supervised WSD Questions**
>
> 1. **Laplace Smoothing Denominator:** Many students compute $\dfrac{\text{count}(f,s)+1}{\text{count}(s)+1}$ instead of $\dfrac{\text{count}(f,s)+1}{\text{count}(s)+V}$. This **loses 2 marks** per question. Always add the **vocabulary size $V$**, not 1.
> 2. **Naive Bayes Argument Order:** Some students apply $\arg\max$ over features instead of senses. The decision is over **senses**, with **features as evidence**. Mixing this up loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Log-Space Omission:** Writing the raw product without converting to $\log$ is acceptable but **vulnerable to underflow**. Examiners appreciate seeing the log transformation explicitly.
> 4. **Confusion Matrix in Multi-Class WSD:** "True Negative" is the **sum of all off-diagonal correct rejections**, not a single cell. This is a frequent 14-mark question pitfall.
> 5. **Decision List Tie-Breaking:** When no rule matches, the system falls back to **MFS**, not to a random guess. Forgetting to mention MFS loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Supervised WSD = train a classifier on **sense-annotated corpus** (e.g., SemCor).
- ✅ The **MAP decision rule**: $\hat{s} = \arg\max_s P(s \mid C)$ is the **mathematical heart** of supervised WSD.
- ✅ **Naive Bayes** assumes conditional independence of features given the sense — this is the "naive" part.
- ✅ Always **convert to log-space** to avoid floating-point underflow.
- ✅ **Laplace Smoothing** formula: $P(f \mid s) = \dfrac{\text{count}(f, s) + 1}{\text{count}(s) + V}$ — never forget the $+V$.
- ✅ **Decision Lists** rank rules by absolute log-likelihood ratio; first match wins; MFS is the fallback.
- ✅ **Evaluation metrics:** Precision, Recall, F1, Accuracy — and the multi-class TN trap.
- ✅ **Knowledge Acquisition Bottleneck** is the main limitation — motivates semi-supervised methods.
- ✅ **All-Words** vs **Targeted WSD** is a fundamental distinction.
- ✅ **Most Frequent Sense (MFS)** is a surprisingly strong baseline — always mention it as a comparison reference in answer sheets.
- ✅ For KTU 14-mark numerical questions, **show all 6 steps**: priors → counts → smoothing → likelihoods → posterior → decision.
- ✅ Key corpora: **SemCor, OMSTI, Senseval-2/3**. Key inventory: **WordNet 3.1**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** *Priors × Likelihoods in log-space, Laplace-smoothed, argmax over senses, MFS as fallback.* Memorize this phrase — it captures the entire supervised WSD pipeline in one line.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bayesian Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bayesian Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bayesian Classification** is a family of probabilistic supervised machine learning algorithms rooted in **Bayes' Theorem** (formulated by Reverend Thomas Bayes, 1763) that predicts the class of an observation by computing the *posterior probability* of every candidate class conditioned on the observed feature vector, and selecting the class with the **maximum posterior probability** (MAP — Maximum A Posteriori decision rule).

In the context of **Computational Linguistics (PEADT632)**, Bayesian classification is the workhorse statistical engine behind classical **text classification** tasks such as *spam detection, sentiment analysis, language identification, authorship attribution, and topic categorization*. The most widely-used instantiation in NLP is the **Multinomial Naive Bayes** classifier, which assumes conditional independence between features (word tokens) given the class label.

Formally, the Bayes optimal decision rule is:

$$\hat{c} \;=\; \underset{c \in \mathcal{C}}{\arg\max}\;\; P(c \mid \mathbf{x})$$

where $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_d)$ is the feature vector, $\mathcal{C}$ is the finite set of class labels, and $P(c \mid \mathbf{x})$ is the posterior probability of class $c$ given the observation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Doctor's Diagnostic Reasoning":** Imagine a doctor who has treated 10,000 patients over many years. When a new patient walks in with symptoms *fever, cough, body ache*, the doctor does not diagnose randomly. She recalls:
> 1. **Prior belief** — How common is the flu in the current season? (e.g., 60% of patients have it).
> 2. **Likelihood** — Given the patient *has* the flu, how probable are these specific symptoms? (e.g., fever is very likely, runny nose moderately likely).
> 3. **Evidence** — The actual symptoms observed.
>
> She then **inverts** her reasoning: "What is the probability of flu *given* these symptoms?" She multiplies the prior with the likelihood for each candidate disease, normalizes, and picks the disease with the highest score.

Bayesian classification performs **exactly this reasoning at scale** for millions of text documents, replacing "symptoms" with "words" and "diseases" with "categories" (spam/ham, positive/negative, etc.).

### 1.3 Key Components of the Bayesian Framework

| Term | Mathematical Symbol | Meaning | NLP Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Prior Probability** | $P(c)$ | Belief about class $c$ before seeing data | Proportion of spam emails in inbox |
| **Likelihood** | $P(\mathbf{x} \mid c)$ | Probability of observing features given class $c$ | Probability of word "free" appearing in a spam email |
| **Evidence / Marginal** | $P(\mathbf{x})$ | Probability of observing the feature vector under *all* classes | Normalizing constant |
| **Posterior Probability** | $P(c \mid \mathbf{x})$ | Updated belief about class $c$ after seeing data | Probability the email is spam *given* it contains "free" |
| **Class-Conditional Independence Assumption** | — | Features are independent given the class | Word positions in a document treated independently |

### 1.4 Why Bayesian Classification is Central to Computational Linguistics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 3) identifies Bayesian classification as a **foundational statistical method** for NLP because:
> - It provides a **principled probabilistic framework** for handling language ambiguity.
> - It is **computationally efficient** — training is O(N·d), prediction is O(|\mathcal{C}|·d).
> - It handles **high-dimensional sparse feature spaces** (vocabulary sizes of 10⁵–10⁶) gracefully.
> - It serves as a **strong baseline** against which deep learning models are benchmarked.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Derivation of Bayes' Theorem from First Principles

Bayes' Theorem is a direct consequence of the **product rule** of probability and the **symmetry of joint probability**.

**Step 1 — Product Rule (Chain Rule of Probability):**
The joint probability of two events $A$ and $B$ can be factored in two ways:

$$P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B)\,P(B) = P(B \mid A)\,P(A)$$

**Step 2 — Equating the Two Factorizations:**

$$P(A \mid B)\,P(B) = P(B \mid A)\,P(A)$$

**Step 3 — Solving for the Desired Conditional:**

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A)\,P(A)}{P(B)}$$

This is **Bayes' Theorem**. Substituting $A \rightarrow c$ (class) and $B \rightarrow \mathbf{x}$ (feature vector):

$$P(c \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid c)\,P(c)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

### 2.2 The MAP (Maximum A Posteriori) Decision Rule

For classification, we choose the class that **maximizes the posterior**. Since the evidence $P(\mathbf{x})$ is constant across all candidate classes, we can drop it:

$$\hat{c} = \underset{c \in \mathcal{C}}{\arg\max}\; P(c \mid \mathbf{x}) = \underset{c \in \mathcal{C}}{\arg\max}\; P(\mathbf{x} \mid c)\,P(c)$$

This simplification is critical for **computational tractability** — we never need to compute the (often intractable) marginal $P(\mathbf{x})$.

### 2.3 The Naive Bayes Assumption

Computing $P(\mathbf{x} \mid c)$ for a high-dimensional feature vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, \ldots, x_d)$ is **exponentially hard** (requires $O(2^d)$ parameters). The "naive" assumption posits that features are **conditionally independent** given the class:

$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) = P(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_d \mid c) = \prod_{i=1}^{d} P(x_i \mid c)$$

The **Naive Bayes Classifier** is therefore:

$$\hat{c} = \underset{c \in \mathcal{C}}{\arg\max}\; P(c) \prod_{i=1}^{d} P(x_i \mid c)$$

### 2.4 The Underflow Problem and Log-Space Computation

Multiplying many small probabilities causes **floating-point underflow** (numbers approach zero). The standard solution is to work in **logarithmic space** using the log-sum-exp identity:

$$\log P(c \mid \mathbf{x}) \;\propto\; \log P(c) + \sum_{i=1}^{d} \log P(x_i \mid c)$$

The $\arg\max$ is **invariant** to monotonic transformations, so taking the log does not change the predicted class.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU Board Exams — Memorize All of These.**

| # | Formula | LaTeX Expression | Purpose / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bayes' Theorem | $P(c \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid c)\,P(c)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$ | Core probabilistic identity |
| 2 | MAP Decision Rule | $\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c} P(c) \prod_{i} P(x_i \mid c)$ | Predicting the class label |
| 3 | Log-Space MAP | $\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c} \left[\log P(c) + \sum_{i} \log P(x_i \mid c)\right]$ | Numerical stability |
| 4 | Prior (Maximum Likelihood Estimate) | $\hat{P}(c) = \frac{N_c}{N}$ | Fraction of class $c$ docs |
| 5 | Multinomial Likelihood | $\hat{P}(x_i \mid c) = \frac{\text{count}(x_i, c) + \alpha}{\sum_{x} \text{count}(x, c) + \alpha \vert \mathcal{V} \vert}$ | Word frequency in class $c$ |
| 6 | Laplace (Additive) Smoothing | $\hat{P}(x_i \mid c) = \frac{\text{count}(x_i, c) + 1}{\sum_{x} \text{count}(x, c) + \vert \mathcal{V} \vert}$ | $\alpha = 1$ case of (5) |
| 7 | Bernoulli Likelihood | $P(x_i \mid c) = p_i^{x_i}(1-p_i)^{1-x_i}$ | Binary feature $x_i \in \{0,1\}$ |
| 8 | Posterior Odds Ratio | $\frac{P(c_1 \mid \mathbf{x})}{P(c_2 \mid \mathbf{x})} = \frac{P(c_1)}{P(c_2)} \cdot \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid c_1)}{P(\mathbf{x} \mid c_2)}$ | Binary classification decision boundary |
| 9 | Bayes Risk | $R(c_i \mid \mathbf{x}) = \sum_{j} \lambda_{ij}\,P(c_j \mid \mathbf{x})$ | Minimum-risk classification |
| 10 | Expected Prediction Error | $E[\text{error}] = 1 - P(\hat{c} \mid \mathbf{x})$ | Bayes error rate bound |

> **Notation:** $\vert \mathcal{V} \vert$ denotes the **vocabulary size** (total number of unique tokens), $\alpha$ is the **smoothing parameter**, $N_c$ is the number of training documents in class $c$, and $N$ is the total number of training documents.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Bayesian classification is deployed across industry-scale NLP systems:
> - **Spam Filtering** — Gmail, Yahoo Mail, Outlook use Multinomial Naive Bayes ensembles.
> - **Sentiment Analysis** — Twitter/X polarity classification baseline.
> - **Medical NLP** — ICD-10 coding of clinical notes.
> - **Legal Document Categorization** — e-Discovery and contract review.
> - **Recommendation Systems** — Naive Bayes as a cold-start solution in news recommenders.
> - **Bioinformatics** — Protein sequence classification, gene expression analysis.

The classifier remains a **production-grade choice** when interpretability, low-latency inference, and small training data are priorities.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — End-to-End Naive Bayes Classification

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **canonical KTU-style numerical problem**. Master this template — the same structure appears every examination cycle.
