**Banerjee & Pedersen (2002)** generalized the Lesk algorithm by exploiting the full relational structure of WordNet. Instead of relying solely on gloss-gloss overlap, the extended variant compares glosses against:

1. The gloss itself.
2. The glosses of all **hypernyms** of the sense.
3. The glosses of all **hyponyms** of the sense.
4. The glosses of all **meronyms** of the sense.
5. The glosses of all **holonyms** of the sense.
6. The glosses of all **coordinate terms** (sister synsets).
7. The **example sentences** attached to the synset.
8. The **lemmas** (synonym words) of related synsets.

### 2.2.1 Extended Lesk Formal Definition

Let $R(s_i) = \{g(r) : r \in \text{Relations}(s_i)\}$ be the bag-of-words from all related glosses and example sentences reachable from $s_i$ within one relational hop. Then:

$$
\text{ExtendedOverlap}(s_i) = \vert R(s_i) \cap C \vert
$$

$$
s^*_{\text{ext}} = \arg\max_{s_i \in S(w_t)} \text{ExtendedOverlap}(s_i)
$$

A further **length-normalized** variant is sometimes used:

$$
\text{WeightedOverlap}(s_i) = \frac{\vert R(s_i) \cap C \vert}{\vert R(s_i) \cup C \vert}
$$

This **Jaccard-style normalization** prevents bias toward senses that have larger relational subgraphs.

---

## 2.3 Thesaurus-Based Semantic Similarity Measures

Beyond raw overlap, thesauri (especially WordNet) enable **path-based** and **information-content-based** similarity measures between word senses.

### 2.3.1 Wu–Palmer Similarity (Wu & Palmer, 1994)

$$
\text{sim}_{\text{WP}}(s_1, s_2) = \frac{2 \cdot \text{depth}(\text{LCS}(s_1, s_2))}{\text{depth}(s_1) + \text{depth}(s_2)}
$$

Where $\text{LCS}$ is the **Lowest Common Subsumer** (deepest shared hypernym) and $\text{depth}(x)$ is the number of hypernym edges from $x$ to the root.

### 2.3.2 Resnik Similarity (Resnik, 1995)

$$
\text{sim}_{\text{Resnik}}(s_1, s_2) = -\log P(\text{LCS}(s_1, s_2))
$$

Where $P(c)$ is the probability of concept $c$ occurring in a reference corpus, computed as the relative frequency of $c$ and all its hyponyms.

### 2.3.3 Leacock–Chodorow Similarity

$$
\text{sim}_{\text{LC}}(s_1, s_2) = -\log \frac{\text{len}(s_1, s_2)}{2 \cdot D}
$$

Where $\text{len}(s_1, s_2)$ is the shortest path length between senses and $D$ is the maximum depth of the taxonomy.

### 2.3.4 Adapted/Enhanced Lesk (Banerjee & Pedersen, 2003)

A weighted extension using TF-IDF on gloss tokens:

$$
\text{Score}_{\text{Enhanced}}(s_i) = \sum_{t \in g(s_i) \cap C} \text{tfidf}(t, g(s_i))
$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Authoritative KTU Formula Reference Table — memorize all entries.**

| $\#$ | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Domain | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Original Lesk Overlap | $\sum_{c_j \in C} \vert g(s_i) \cap g(c_j) \vert$ | $g$ = gloss, $C$ = context | Classic Lesk (1986) |
| 2 | Simplified Lesk | $\vert g(s_i) \cap C \vert$ | $g$ = gloss, $C$ = context | Vasilescu et al. (2004) |
| 3 | Extended Lesk | $\vert R(s_i) \cap C \vert$ | $R$ = relational bag-of-words | Banerjee & Pedersen (2002) |
| 4 | Length-Normalized Overlap | $\frac{\vert R(s_i) \cap C \vert}{\vert R(s_i) \cup C \vert}$ | Jaccard-style | Bias reduction |
| 5 | Wu–Palmer Similarity | $\frac{2 \cdot \text{depth}(\text{LCS})}{\text{depth}(s_1) + \text{depth}(s_2)}$ | Path-based | Range $\left[0, 1\right]$ |
| 6 | Resnik Similarity | $-\log P(\text{LCS}(s_1, s_2))$ | IC-based | Range $\left[0, \infty\right)$ |
| 7 | Leacock–Chodorow | $-\log \frac{\text{len}(s_1, s_2)}{2 \cdot D}$ | Path + depth | Range $\left[0, \log(2D)\right]$ |
| 8 | Enhanced Lesk (TF-IDF) | $\sum_{t \in g(s_i) \cap C} \text{tfidf}(t)$ | TF-IDF weighted | Modern variant |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL TABLE-FORMATTING NOTE:** Throughout these notes, the absolute value and set-cardinality notation uses $\vert$ (rendered as `\vert`) rather than the markdown-reserved vertical bar character to preserve KTU-rendering integrity of tables.

---

## 2.5 Why and How — The Engineering Rationale

The "Why" behind each step of the Lesk paradigm is rooted in the **distributional hypothesis** (Harris, 1954): *words that occur in similar contexts tend to have similar meanings*. By converting senses into glosses (mini-contexts) and comparing them, the algorithm effectively answers: *"Which sense's typical usage environment best matches the observed usage environment?"*

The "How" of engineering deployment:
- **Real-world utility:** Lesk-family algorithms power **machine translation disambiguation** (e.g., distinguishing *"bass"* the fish from *"bass"* the instrument), **information retrieval query expansion**, and **semantic search engines**.
- **Resource requirement:** Zero annotated training data — only a thesaurus — making it **low-resource friendly**, a major advantage for low-resource Indian languages.
- **Failure mode:** *Gloss sparsity* — when the target word's gloss uses different vocabulary than the surrounding sentence, overlap is zero or near-zero, leading to random tie-breaking.
</br>
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Original Lesk on a Polysemous Word

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Sentence:** *"He sat on the **bank** of the river and watched the flowing water."*
> **Target word:** `bank`
> **Thesaurus:** WordNet (simplified glosses for pedagogical clarity)

### Step 1 — Enumerate Candidate Senses

From WordNet, `bank` has (among others) the following relevant senses:

- **Sense $s_1$ (financial institution):**
  Gloss $g(s_1)$: *"a financial institution that accepts deposits and channels those deposits into lending activities"*

- **Sense $s_2$ (sloping land beside water):**
  Gloss $g(s_2)$: *"sloping land (especially the slope beside a body of water)"*

- **Sense $s_3$ (arrangement in rows):**
  Gloss $g(s_3)$: *"a long pile or heap"*

- **Sense $s_4$ (flight maneuver):**
  Gloss $g(s_4)$: *"a flight maneuver; aircraft tips laterally about its longitudinal axis"*

### Step 2 — Identify the Context Window

Using a symmetric context window of size $\pm 4$ around `bank`:

$$
C = \{\text{sat, on, the, of, river, and, watched, flowing, water}\}
$$

After **stop-word removal** ($\{\text{on, the, of, and}\}$ removed):

$$
C = \{\text{sat, river, watched, flowing, water}\}
$$

### Step 3 — Tokenize Each Gloss (Lowercase, Stop-word Removal, Stemming)

- $g(s_1) \to \{\text{financial, institution, accepts, deposits, channels, deposits, lending, activities}\}$
- $g(s_2) \to \{\text{sloping, land, especially, slope, beside, body, water}\}$
- $g(s_3) \to \{\text{long, pile, heap}\}$
- $g(s_4) \to \{\text{flight, maneuver, aircraft, tips, laterally, longitudinal, axis}\}$

### Step 4 — Compute the Simplified Lesk Overlap for Each Sense

$$
\text{Overlap}_{\text{simple}}(s_i) = \vert g(s_i) \cap C \vert
$$

For $s_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
g(s_1) \cap C &= \{\text{financial, institution, accepts, deposits, channels, deposits, lending, activities}\} \\
&\cap \{\text{sat, river, watched, flowing, water}\} \\
&= \emptyset
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore $\text{Overlap}_{\text{simple}}(s_1) = 0$.

For $s_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
g(s_2) \cap C &= \{\text{sloping, land, especially, slope, beside, body, water}\} \\
&\cap \{\text{sat, river, watched, flowing, water}\} \\
&= \{\text{water}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore $\text{Overlap}_{\text{simple}}(s_2) = 1$.

For $s_3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
g(s_3) \cap C &= \{\text{long, pile, heap}\} \cap \{\text{sat, river, watched, flowing, water}\} \\
&= \emptyset
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore $\text{Overlap}_{\text{simple}}(s_3) = 0$.

For $s_4$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
g(s_4) \cap C &= \{\text{flight, maneuver, aircraft, tips, laterally, longitudinal, axis}\} \\
&\cap \{\text{sat, river, watched, flowing, water}\} \\
&= \emptyset
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore $\text{Overlap}_{\text{simple}}(s_4) = 0$.

### Step 5 — Apply the Argmax Selection Rule

$$
\begin{aligned}
s^* &= \arg\max_{s_i \in S(\text{bank})} \text{Overlap}_{\text{simple}}(s_i) \\
&= \arg\max \{0, 1, 0, 0\} \\
&= s_2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Interpret the Result

The algorithm correctly selects **$s_2$ — "sloping land beside water"** because the word *water* in the context matches the word *water* in the gloss. Despite the simplicity of the match (only 1 overlapping word), this is the canonical demonstration of the Lesk algorithm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Observation for KTU Answer Sheets:** The Simplified Lesk often yields **zero overlap** in realistic scenarios due to gloss sparsity. This is precisely the motivation for the Extended Lesk algorithm.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — Extended Lesk on the Same Sentence

The Extended Lesk enriches the comparison set $R(s_i)$ by including glosses from hypernyms, hyponyms, meronyms, examples, etc. Using the simplified WordNet subgraph for `bank`:

### Relational Bag Construction

For $s_2$ (sloping land beside water):
- Gloss of $s_2$: *sloping land beside body water*
- Hypernym gloss: *geological formation, geological feature* → $\{\text{geological, formation, feature}\}$
- Example sentence: *"they pulled the canoe up on the bank"* → $\{\text{pulled, canoe, up, bank\}}$

Aggregated $R(s_2) = \{\text{sloping, land, beside, body, water, geological, formation, feature, pulled, canoe}\}$

For $s_1$ (financial institution):
- Gloss of $s_1$: *financial institution accepts deposits channels lending*
- Hypernym gloss: *institution, establishment, creation* → $\{\text{institution, establishment, creation}\}$
- Meronym gloss: *commercial bank, savings bank* → $\{\text{commercial, savings}\}$
- Example: *"he deposited money at the bank"* → $\{\text{deposited, money}\}$

Aggregated $R(s_1) = \{\text{financial, institution, accepts, deposits, channels, lending, establishment, creation, commercial, savings, deposited, money\}$

### Compute Extended Overlap

Context (unchanged): $C = \{\text{sat, river, watched, flowing, water}\}$.

For $s_2$:

$$
R(s_2) \cap C = \{\text{water}\}
$$

Therefore $\text{ExtendedOverlap}(s_2) = 1$.

For $s_1$:

$$
R(s_1) \cap C = \emptyset
$$

Therefore $\text{ExtendedOverlap}(s_1) = 0$.

The Extended Lesk confirms $s_2$ as the winner. In harder cases with denser relational subgraphs, the extended overlap typically outperforms simplified Lesk by **15–25% in F-score** (Banerjee & Pedersen, 2002).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Lesk Family

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Comparison Table for Examination Use:**

| Variant | Coverage of Context | Coverage of Sense | Key Strength | Key Weakness | KTU Exam Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Original Lesk (1986)** | Glosses of all context words | Gloss only | Conceptual simplicity | O($n^2$) gloss lookups | Foundational |
| **Simplified Lesk (2004)** | Context words directly | Gloss only | Fast, $O(n)$ lookup | Gloss sparsity | Frequently asked |
| **Extended Lesk (2002)** | Context words directly | Gloss + hypernyms + hyponyms + meronyms + examples + holonyms | Higher coverage, better F1 | Increased precomputation | **Mandatory** |
| **Enhanced Lesk (2003)** | Context with TF-IDF weighting | Gloss + relations weighted | Length-bias correction | TF-IDF tuning required | Advanced |
| **Adapted Lesk (Kilgarriff)** | Disambiguated context iteratively | Gloss + relations | Reduces cascading errors | Multi-pass overhead | Bonus point |

---

## 3.4 Tie-Breaking and Tie-Handling Protocols

When two or more senses share the maximum overlap, the following tie-breaking heuristics are documented:

1. **First-sense heuristic** — Choose the sense listed first in the thesaurus (default for WordNet, justified by Zipfian sense-frequency distribution).
2. **Sense-frequency ranking** — Choose the sense with highest corpus frequency (e.g., SemCor counts).
3. **Depth-preference heuristic** — Choose the sense deeper in the hypernym hierarchy (more specific).
4. **Random selection** — Last-resort fallback (poor; never used in production).

For the KTU answer, state: *"When ties occur, the first-sense heuristic is applied by default, following the empirical observation that sense frequencies follow a power-law distribution."*
</br>
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Operational Flowchart — Original Lesk Algorithm

The following Mermaid block diagrams the end-to-end execution pipeline of the Original Lesk algorithm. Node identifiers are alphanumeric with letter prefixes to comply with the KTU compilation safeguards.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Input Sentence with Ambiguous Target Word] --> nodeB[Identify Target Word wt]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Extract Symmetric Context Window of Size n]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Retrieve All Candidate Senses S from Thesaurus]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Initialize Empty Overlap Score List]
    nodeE --> nodeF{Begin Loop Over Each Sense si}
    nodeF --> nodeG[Tokenize Gloss g of si]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Tokenize Gloss of Each Context Word cj]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Compute Pairwise Overlap between g and each g of cj]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Sum Pairwise Overlaps into Score si]
    nodeJ --> nodeK{More Senses to Process?}
    nodeK -->|Yes| nodeF
    nodeK -->|No| nodeL[Identify Sense with Maximum Score]
    nodeL --> nodeM{Is there a Tie?}
    nodeM -->|Yes| nodeN[Apply First-Sense Heuristic]
    nodeM -->|No| nodeP[Output Selected Sense as Disambiguated Meaning]
    nodeN --> nodeP
    nodeP --> nodeQ[Return Tagged Sense]
```

## 4.2 Extended Lesk Pipeline — Relational Enrichment Architecture

This block diagram illustrates how the Extended Lesk augments the comparison set by traversing the WordNet relational graph.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeR[Target Sense si] --> nodeS[Direct Gloss g of si]
    nodeR --> nodeT[Hypernym Subgraph Traversal]
    nodeR --> nodeU[Hyponym Subgraph Traversal]
    nodeR --> nodeV[Meronym and Holonym Traversal]
    nodeR --> nodeW[Example Sentence Extraction]
    nodeS --> nodeX[Relational Bag Aggregator R si]
    nodeT --> nodeX
    nodeU --> nodeX
    nodeV --> nodeX
    nodeW --> nodeX
    nodeX --> nodeY[Context Window C of Target Word]
    nodeY --> nodeZ[Set Intersection R si intersect C]
    nodeZ --> nodeAA[Compute Final Score]
    nodeAA --> nodeAB[Argmax Across All Candidate Senses]
    nodeAB --> nodeAC[Output Disambiguated Sense Label]
```

## 4.3 WordNet Hypernym Hierarchy — Lexical Taxonomy for `bank`

The following graph shows a simplified portion of the WordNet hypernym hierarchy relevant to `bank`, illustrating how Wu–Palmer and Leacock–Chodorow similarities exploit depth information.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeBA[Entity] --> nodeBB[Physical Entity]
    nodeBB --> nodeBC[Object]
    nodeBC --> nodeBD[Structure]
    nodeBD --> nodeBE[Artifact]
    nodeBE --> nodeBF[Establishment]
    nodeBF --> nodeBG[Financial Institution]
    nodeBG --> nodeBH[Bank Sense 1 Money]
    nodeBB --> nodeBI[Location]
    nodeBI --> nodeBJ[Region]
    nodeBJ --> nodeBK[Geographical Feature]
    nodeBK --> nodeBL[Geological Formation]
    nodeBL --> nodeBM[Sloping Land]
    nodeBM --> nodeBN[Bank Sense 2 River]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Algorithm Complexity Profile

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeCA[Original Lesk] -->|O k times n times G| nodeCB[Quadratic in Context Size]
    nodeCC[Simplified Lesk] -->|O k times G| nodeCD[Linear Lookup]
    nodeCE[Extended Lesk] -->|O k times R times n| nodeCF[Linear with Precomputed Relational Bag]
    nodeCG[Enhanced Lesk TFIDF] -->|O k times R times n times log V| nodeCH[Linear with Vocabulary Indexed TFIDF]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide for KTU Students:**
> - $k$ = number of candidate senses of the target word.
> - $n$ = number of context words.
> - $G$ = average gloss length in tokens.
> - $R$ = average relational-bag size (Extended Lesk).
> - $V$ = vocabulary size of the thesaurus (for TF-IDF indexing).
> For typical WordNet inputs ($k \approx 5$, $n \approx 10$, $G \approx 15$), Original Lesk performs **750 token comparisons** while Simplified Lesk performs **only 75** — a **10× speedup** with negligible accuracy loss for short contexts.
</br>
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Q: Define Word Sense Disambiguation. Explain the role of a thesaurus as a knowledge source in thesaurus-based disambiguation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD)** is the NLP task of computationally identifying the correct sense of a polysemous word from a predefined sense inventory, based on its surrounding context. (1 mark)

In thesaurus-based disambiguation, the thesaurus (e.g., WordNet) serves as the **sense inventory** and the **semantic knowledge source**. It provides: (1) enumerated senses for each word, (2) glosses (dictionary definitions) for overlap comparison, and (3) relational structure (hypernymy, hyponymy, meronymy) used by Extended Lesk variants to enrich the comparison context. (2 marks)

The thesaurus replaces the need for annotated training corpora, making this approach ideal for **low-resource languages and zero-shot scenarios**.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Q: What is the simplified Lesk algorithm? State its key formula.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Simplified Lesk Algorithm** (Vasilescu et al., 2004) is a computationally efficient variant of the original Lesk algorithm that removes the need to look up glosses of context words. (1 mark)

**Formula:**

$$
s^* = \arg\max_{s_i \in S(w_t)} \vert g(s_i) \cap C \vert
$$

where $g(s_i)$ is the gloss of candidate sense $s_i$ and $C$ is the bag of context words after stop-word removal. (2 marks)

This reduces algorithmic complexity from $O(k \cdot n \cdot G)$ to $O(k \cdot G)$ while retaining most of the disambiguation accuracy for short contexts.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

> **(a)** Explain in detail the **Original Lesk Algorithm** for Word Sense Disambiguation. State the underlying hypothesis, list all computational steps, and discuss its limitations. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Describe the **Extended Lesk Algorithm**. Show how it overcomes the limitations of the original Lesk and provide a comparative analysis. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Original Lesk Algorithm (7 marks):**

1. **Underlying Hypothesis (1 mark):** Lesk (1986) proposed that *the correct sense of a polysemous word is the one whose definition shares the greatest number of words with the definitions of the surrounding context words*. This is rooted in the distributional hypothesis — words used in similar contexts have related meanings.

2. **Algorithm Steps (3 marks):**
   - Identify the ambiguous target word $w_t$.
   - Retrieve all $k$ senses $\{s_1, \dots, s_k\}$ from the thesaurus.
   - Extract context words $C = \{c_1, \dots, c_m\}$ within a window of size $n$.
   - For each sense $s_i$, compute pairwise overlap between $g(s_i)$ and each $g(c_j)$.
   - Sum pairwise overlaps to obtain $\text{Overlap}(s_i)$.
   - Select $s^* = \arg\max_{s_i} \text{Overlap}(s_i)$.

3. **Formal Expression (1 mark):**

$$
\text{Overlap}(s_i) = \sum_{c_j \in C} \vert g(s_i) \cap g(c_j) \vert
$$

4. **Limitations (2 marks):**
   - **Gloss sparsity:** Overlap is frequently zero because gloss vocabulary rarely matches context vocabulary.
   - **Computational cost:** $O(k \cdot n \cdot G)$ gloss lookups make it expensive.
   - **No tie-breaking strategy** in the original formulation.
   - **Ignores relational structure** of the thesaurus.

**Part (b) — Extended Lesk Algorithm (7 marks):**

1. **Motivation (1 mark):** Banerjee & Pedersen (2002) recognized that WordNet senses are not isolated — they are embedded in a rich graph of semantic relations. The Extended Lesk exploits this structure.

2. **Relational Bag Construction (2 marks):** For each candidate sense $s_i$, aggregate glosses from:
   - The sense's own gloss.
   - Hypernym glosses (generalization).
   - Hyponym glosses (specialization).
   - Meronym and holonym glosses (part-whole).
   - Example sentences in the synset.
   - Coordinate-term glosses (sister synsets).

   Formally: $R(s_i) = \{g(r) : r \in \text{Relations}(s_i)\}$.

3. **Overlap Computation (1 mark):**

$$
\text{ExtendedOverlap}(s_i) = \vert R(s_i) \cap C \vert
$$

4. **Advantages over Original Lesk (2 marks):**
   - **Coverage:** Larger comparison set mitigates gloss sparsity.
   - **Accuracy:** Reported improvement of **15–25% in F-score** on standard WSD benchmarks (SemCor, Senseval-2).
   - **Robustness:** Better disambiguation of low-frequency senses via inherited hypernym clues.

5. **Comparative Summary Table (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Original Lesk | Extended Lesk |
|---|---|---|
| Comparison Set | Gloss only | Gloss + 6 relation types |
| F-Score (Senseval-2) | $\approx 42\%$ | $\approx 56\%$ |
| Complexity | $O(k \cdot n \cdot G)$ | $O(k \cdot R \cdot n)$ |
| Precomputation | None | Relational bag indexing |

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

> **(a)** Discuss the various **thesaurus-based semantic similarity measures** used in WSD. Derive and explain the Wu–Palmer and Resnik similarity formulas. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a suitable example, demonstrate the **Simplified Lesk algorithm** step-by-step on a polysemous English word. Show all overlap computations explicitly. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Thesaurus-Based Similarity Measures (7 marks):**

1. **Introduction (1 mark):** Thesaurus-based similarity measures quantify semantic relatedness between two word senses using the structural properties of the thesaurus graph. They are crucial for WSD because they allow ranking candidate senses by their similarity to context senses.

2. **Wu–Palmer Similarity (3 marks):**

   **Derivation:**
   Let $\text{LCS}(s_1, s_2)$ denote the *Lowest Common Subsumer* — the deepest synset that is a hypernym of both $s_1$ and $s_2$. Let $\text{depth}(x)$ denote the number of hypernym edges from $x$ to the root.

   The intuition: two senses are more similar if their LCS is deep (specific) and they are themselves deep.

   **Formula:**

$$
\text{sim}_{\text{WP}}(s_1, s_2) = \frac{2 \cdot \text{depth}(\text{LCS}(s_1, s_2))}{\text{depth}(s_1) + \text{depth}(s_2)}
$$

   **Range:** $[0, 1]$ where $1$ indicates identical senses and $0$ indicates only the root is shared.

3. **Resnik Similarity (2 marks):**

   Let $P(c)$ be the probability that a randomly drawn noun instance in a reference corpus belongs to concept $c$ (computed as the relative frequency of $c$ and all its hyponyms). The information content is $\text{IC}(c) = -\log P(c)$.

   **Formula:**

$$
\text{sim}_{\text{Resnik}}(s_1, s_2) = \text{IC}(\text{LCS}(s_1, s_2)) = -\log P(\text{LCS}(s_1, s_2))
$$

   **Range:** $[0, \infty)$. Higher values mean greater semantic relatedness.

4. **Other Notable Measures (1 mark):**
   - **Leacock–Chodorow:** Path-length based, normalized by taxonomy depth.
   - **Jiang–Conrath:** Combines IC and path length.
   - **Lin:** IC-based ratio symmetric to Resnik.

**Part (b) — Simplified Lesk Worked Example (7 marks):**

**Sentence:** *"The chef ground fresh **pepper** to season the soup."*

**Target word:** `pepper`

**Candidate Senses (WordNet simplified):**

- $s_1$ (vegetable): *"a sweet and mild bell pepper or capsicum used in cooking"*
- $s_2$ (spice): *"a pungent condiment derived from dried ground peppercorns"*
- $s_3$ (sprayer): *"a device that sprays a liquid under pressure"*

**Step 1 — Context Extraction (1 mark):**
Window $\pm 5$ around `pepper`: $C = \{\text{chef, ground, fresh, to, season, the, soup}\}$.
After stop-word removal: $C = \{\text{chef, ground, fresh, season, soup}\}$.

**Step 2 — Tokenize Glosses (1 mark):**
- $g(s_1) = \{\text{sweet, mild, bell, pepper, capsicum, cooking}\}$
- $g(s_2) = \{\text{pungent, condiment, dried, ground, peppercorns}\}$
- $g(s_3) = \{\text{device, sprays, liquid, pressure}\}$

**Step 3 — Compute Overlaps (2 marks):**
- $g(s_1) \cap C = \emptyset \Rightarrow \text{Overlap}(s_1) = 0$
- $g(s_2) \cap C = \{\text{ground}\} \Rightarrow \text{Overlap}(s_2) = 1$
- $g(s_3) \cap C = \emptyset \Rightarrow \text{Overlap}(s_3) = 0$

**Step 4 — Argmax Selection (1 mark):**

$$
s^* = \arg\max\{0, 1, 0\} = s_2
$$

**Step 5 — Result Interpretation (2 marks):**
The algorithm selects **$s_2$** (the spice sense) because the word *ground* appears in both the gloss and the context — semantically consistent with the act of grinding a spice for seasoning soup. This demonstrates the algorithm's dependence on direct lexical coincidence and motivates the need for Extended Lesk to handle sparser contexts.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Formula substitution errors** — Students frequently write $\vert g(s_i) \cap C \vert$ but forget to mention the **stop-word removal** and **stemming** preprocessing steps. Without these, the formula is incomplete and may lose **1–2 marks**.
>
> 2. **Confusing Original vs Simplified Lesk** — A common error is stating that Simplified Lesk uses glosses of context words. It does **not** — it directly intersects the sense gloss with the raw context word list. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
>
> 3. **Missing the relational bag** in Extended Lesk answers — Students often describe Extended Lesk as "Lesk with more words" without enumerating the **six relation types** (hypernym, hyponym, meronym, holonym, examples, coordinate terms). This loses 2 marks.
>
> 4. **Forgetting tie-breaking** — When two senses have equal overlap, the answer must specify the **first-sense heuristic**. Marks are deducted if this is omitted.
>
> 5. **No worked numerical example** — For 7-mark sub-parts, KTU 2024 scheme mandates **worked computations**. A purely descriptive answer without overlap arithmetic loses at least 3 marks.
>
> 6. **Mis-stating Wu–Palmer range** — The range is $[0, 1]$, **not** $[0, \infty)$. Confusing this with Resnik's range is a frequent slip-up.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Thesaurus-Based Disambiguation:**

- **Core Paradigm:** Knowledge-based WSD using a thesaurus (WordNet, Roget's) as sense inventory and semantic resource.
- **Canonical Algorithm:** Original Lesk (1986) — gloss-gloss overlap maximization.
- **Simplified Lesk (2004):** Direct gloss-to-context overlap; $O(k \cdot G)$ complexity.
- **Extended Lesk (2002):** Includes hypernym, hyponym, meronym, holonym, example, and coordinate glosses.
- **Enhanced Lesk (2003):** TF-IDF weighted overlap to reduce length bias.
- **Wu–Palmer Similarity:** $\frac{2 \cdot \text{depth}(\text{LCS})}{\text{depth}(s_1) + \text{depth}(s_2)}$ — range $[0, 1]$.
- **Resnik Similarity:** $-\log P(\text{LCS})$ — range $[0, \infty)$; IC-based.
- **Leacock–Chodorow:** $-\log \frac{\text{len}(s_1, s_2)}{2 \cdot D}$ — path-based.
- **Lowest Common Subsumer (LCS):** Deepest shared hypernym — central to all path-based similarity measures.
- **First-Sense Heuristic:** Default tie-breaker; justified by Zipfian sense-frequency distribution.
- **Gloss Sparsity:** Principal failure mode — addressed by Extended Lesk's relational enrichment.
- **Knowledge Sources:** WordNet (English), EuroWordNet, Hindi WordNet (IndoWordNet), Roget's Thesaurus.
- **Evaluation Benchmarks:** SemCor, Senseval-2, Senseval-3, SemEval-2007 Task 17.
- **Zero-Shot Advantage:** No annotated corpus required — suitable for low-resource Indian languages.
- **Algorithm Complexity Summary:** Original $O(k \cdot n \cdot G)$, Simplified $O(k \cdot G)$, Extended $O(k \cdot R \cdot n)$.
- **Engineering Applications:** Machine translation disambiguation, query expansion in IR, semantic web, sentiment analysis sense selection.
- **Comparative F-Score:** Simplified Lesk $\approx 42\%$, Extended Lesk $\approx 56\%$ on Senseval-2 (Banerjee & Pedersen, 2002).
- **KTU 2024 Mandate:** Memorize all four similarity formulas (Wu–Palmer, Resnik, Leacock–Chodorow, Lin) and the two algorithm variants (Simplified + Extended Lesk).
- **Never confuse** *polysemy* (related senses) with *homonymy* (unrelated senses) — Lesk assumes polysemous glosses share vocabulary.
</br>
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lexical Acquisition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Lexical Acquisition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Lexical Acquisition** is the process by which a learner (human or computational system) constructs, expands, and refines its internal lexicon — the structured repository of word forms, their morphological properties, syntactic categories, semantic representations, and pragmatic associations. In Computational Linguistics (CL), it refers specifically to the automated induction of lexical knowledge from raw or annotated text corpora, enabling NLP systems to recognize novel words, infer word senses, learn sub-word units, and ground language in statistical regularities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Lexical acquisition encompasses the *machine learning* and *statistical inference* of word-level linguistic knowledge, including morphology, orthography, phonology (in spoken systems), syntax, and semantics, from structured or unstructured language data.

The lexicon in computational terms is formally represented as:
$$
L = \{ \langle w, c, m, s \rangle \mid w \in W,\ c \in C,\ m \in M,\ s \in S \}
$$
where:
- $w$ = word form (orthographic / phonological surface)
- $c$ = syntactic category (POS tag)
- $m$ = morphological features (root, affixes, inflection)
- $W, C, M, S$ = vocabularies of forms, categories, morphemes, senses

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Child and the Corpus

Imagine a **toddler** sitting in a kitchen. Every day, the mother points to a red round object and says *"apple"*. After enough repetitions in enough contexts (*"I ate an apple," "Cut the apple," "Apple is red"*), the child forms an internal concept. Notice what happened:
- The child did **not** receive a dictionary definition.
- The child **counted** co-occurrences ("apple" appears near "red", "fruit", "eat").
- The child **generalized** — applying the word to similar red round objects.
- The child **distinguished** senses — "apple" (fruit) vs. "Apple" (the company).

A **computational lexical acquisition system** is exactly this child, but with mathematics:
- The **corpus** is the kitchen (raw sentences).
- The **context window** is the mother's pointing finger.
- The **statistical model** is the child's brain.
- The **lexicon** is the memory built up over time.

## 1.3 Scope of Lexical Acquisition

Lexical acquisition is **not a single task** — it is a family of related sub-problems:

| Sub-task | Example | Output |
|----------|---------|--------|
| **Word Segmentation** | Splitting *"rocketscience"* → *"rocket science"* | Token boundaries |
| **Morphological Acquisition** | Learning that *"unhappiness"* = *un-* + *happy* + *-ness* | Morpheme rules |
| **Part-of-Speech Induction** | Tagging *"book"* as noun vs. verb | Syntactic class |
| **Word Sense Disambiguation** | *"bank"* → financial vs. river | Sense ID |
| **Distributional Semantics** | Vector for *"king"* ≈ vector for *"queen"* − *"woman"* + *"man"* | Embeddings |
| **Sub-word Acquisition** | Learning BPE / WordPiece tokens | Subword vocabulary |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Lexical acquisition is the **bridge** between raw text and structured linguistic knowledge. Without it, NLP systems cannot generalize beyond their training vocabulary.

## 1.4 Why Lexical Acquisition is Hard — The Lexical Gap

Natural language contains:
- **Neologisms** — new words appearing yearly (*"selfie," "cryptocurrency," "doomscroll"*)
- **Polysemy** — one form, many meanings (*"head" of a person, of a department, of a beer*)
- **Synonymy** — many forms, one meaning (*"buy" ≈ "purchase"*)
- **Morphological productivity** — indefinite number of derived forms
- **Domain shift** — *"cell"* in biology vs. telephony vs. prison

This forces acquisition systems to be **data-driven**, **probabilistic**, and **open-vocabulary**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Computational Architecture of Lexical Acquisition

Modern lexical acquisition pipelines operate in **four logical layers**:

1. **Corpus Layer** — Raw text input $D = \{d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_N\}$
2. **Tokenization Layer** — Segmentation into token stream $T = (t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_M)$
3. **Feature Extraction Layer** — Context windows, n-gram counts, co-occurrence matrices
4. **Inference Layer** — Probabilistic / neural model producing lexicon entries

The acquisition is **supervised**, **semi-supervised**, or **unsupervised** depending on availability of labeled data.

## 2.2 Theoretical Frameworks

### 2.2.1 Probabilistic / Statistical Framework
- Treats each word as a random variable.
- Estimates $P(w)$, $P(w \mid c)$, $P(c \mid w)$ from corpus frequencies.
- **Bayesian inference** updates belief as more data arrives.

### 2.2.2 Distributional Hypothesis (Firth, 1957)
> *"You shall know a word by the company it keeps."*

Words appearing in similar **contexts** have similar **meanings**. This is the foundation of all modern embedding methods.

Formally, two words $w_1, w_2$ are semantically similar if:
$$
P(\text{context} \mid w_1) \approx P(\text{context} \mid w_2)
$$

### 2.2.3 Connectionist / Neural Framework
- Words represented as dense vectors $\vec{w} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ in a continuous semantic space.
- Acquired via gradient descent on large corpora (Word2Vec, GloVe, BERT).

### 2.2.4 Symbolic / Rule-Based Framework
- Hand-crafted morphological rules.
- Finite-state morphology (e.g., Koskenniemi's two-level model).

## 2.3 Acquisition from Context — Operational Steps

For an **unknown word** $w^*$ encountered in context $\mathbf{c} = (c_{-k}, \ldots, c_{+k})$:

1. **Detect novelty** — Is $w^* \in L$? If not, trigger acquisition.
2. **Extract features** — POS of context words, surrounding morphology, capitalization.
3. **Hypothesize category** — Use statistical classifier (HMM, CRF, Transformer).
4. **Hypothesize meaning** — Cluster $w^*$ with known words by context similarity.
5. **Update lexicon** — Insert $\langle w^*, \hat{c}, \hat{m}, \hat{s} \rangle$ into $L$.
6. **Confidence scoring** — Assign a probability / entropy score to the new entry.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Range / Unit** |
|-------------|-------------|-------------|------------------|
| Word Frequency | $f(w) = \dfrac{\text{count}(w)}{N}$ | Relative frequency of word $w$ | $[0, 1]$, dimensionless |
| MLE Probability | $P(w) = \dfrac{f(w)}{\sum_{w'} f(w')}$ | Maximum likelihood estimate | $[0, 1]$ |
| Conditional Probability | $P(w_2 \mid w_1) = \dfrac{\text{count}(w_1, w_2)}{\text{count}(w_1)}$ | Bigram probability | $[0, 1]$ |
| Pointwise Mutual Information | $\text{PMI}(w_1, w_2) = \log_2 \dfrac{P(w_1, w_2)}{P(w_1) \cdot P(w_2)}$ | Association strength | $\mathbb{R}$, bits |
| TF-IDF Weight | $w_{td} = \text{tf}(t,d) \cdot \log_2 \dfrac{N}{\text{df}(t)}$ | Term importance | $\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ |
| Cosine Similarity | $\cos(\vec{u}, \vec{v}) = \dfrac{\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v}}{\Vert\vec{u}\Vert \, \Vert\vec{v}\Vert}$ | Vector similarity | $[-1, 1]$ |
| Shannon Entropy | $H(W) = -\sum_{i} P(w_i) \log_2 P(w_i)$ | Lexical uncertainty | bits |
| Bayes' Acquisition | $P(h \mid d) = \dfrac{P(d \mid h) \cdot P(h)}{P(d)}$ | Posterior hypothesis given data | $[0, 1]$ |
| Smoothing (Laplace) | $P_{\text{Lap}}(w) = \dfrac{\text{count}(w) + 1}{N + V}$ | Add-one smoothing | $[0, 1]$ |
| Zipf's Law | $f(r) \propto \dfrac{1}{r^{\alpha}}$ | Rank-frequency relation | $\alpha \approx 1$ empirically |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Laplace smoothing with $V$ (vocabulary size) is **mandatory** for unseen events. Forgetting the $+V$ in the denominator is a common error.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| **Application Domain** | **Use of Lexical Acquisition** |
|------------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Search Engines** | Query expansion, synonym handling, autocomplete |
| **Machine Translation** | Handling OOV (out-of-vocabulary) words, subword tokenization (BPE in Google Translate) |
| **Speech Recognition** | Acoustic-lexical mapping, dynamic vocabularies |
| **Sentiment Analysis** | Learning new slang and domain-specific terms |
| **Information Extraction** | Named-entity induction from raw text |
| **Chatbots / Virtual Assistants** | Continual learning of user-generated vocabulary |

> [!NOTE]
> The subword acquisition mechanism used in BERT (WordPiece) and GPT (BPE) is *directly* a form of lexical acquisition — the system learns a vocabulary of sub-units from corpus statistics rather than a fixed dictionary.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Bayesian Word Acquisition Model

**Problem Setup:** A learner has prior belief $P(h)$ about a hidden lexical property $h$ (e.g., POS category). They observe contextual data $d$ from a corpus. Compute the posterior $P(h \mid d)$.

### Step 1 — State the Prior
The learner begins with a prior distribution over hypotheses. For $V$ possible categories:
$$
P(h_i) = \frac{1}{V}, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, V
$$

This is the **uniform prior** — a reasonable starting point when no information exists.

### Step 2 — Define the Likelihood
The likelihood measures how probable the observed data is under each hypothesis. Assuming conditional independence of context tokens (naive Bayes assumption):
$$
P(d \mid h_i) = \prod_{j=1}^{n} P(c_j \mid h_i)
$$
where $c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_n$ are the surrounding context words.

### Step 3 — Compute the Evidence (Marginal)
By the law of total probability:
$$
P(d) = \sum_{i=1}^{V} P(d \mid h_i) \cdot P(h_i)
$$

This term is independent of $h$ and serves as a normalizing constant.

### Step 4 — Apply Bayes' Rule
$$
P(h_i \mid d) = \frac{P(d \mid h_i) \cdot P(h_i)}{P(d)}
$$

### Step 5 — Decision Rule
The learner picks the most probable hypothesis (Maximum A Posteriori):
$$
h^* = \arg\max_{h_i} P(h_i \mid d) = \arg\max_{h_i} P(d \mid h_i) \cdot P(h_i)
$$
(The denominator $P(d)$ is constant across $h_i$ and can be dropped.)

### Step 6 — Logarithmic Reformulation
To prevent numerical underflow, take the logarithm:
$$
\log P(h_i \mid d) = \log P(h_i) + \sum_{j=1}^{n} \log P(c_j \mid h_i) - \log P(d)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The log-form is the **exact** form used in production NLP pipelines. Students writing only the unsummed form lose a mark.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Pointwise Mutual Information (PMI) for Collocation Acquisition

**Goal:** Determine whether a bigram $(w_1, w_2)$ is a *true collocation* (acquired as a single lexical unit) versus a *chance co-occurrence*.

### Step 1 — Observed Joint Probability
$$
P(w_1, w_2) = \frac{\text{count}(w_1, w_2)}{N}
$$

### Step 2 — Expected Joint Under Independence
If $w_1$ and $w_2$ were statistically independent:
$$
P_{\text{ind}}(w_1, w_2) = P(w_1) \cdot P(w_2) = \frac{\text{count}(w_1)}{N} \cdot \frac{\text{count}(w_2)}{N}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the PMI
$$
\text{PMI}(w_1, w_2) = \log_2 \frac{P(w_1, w_2)}{P(w_1) \cdot P(w_2)}
$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Worked Example
Consider a corpus of $N = 1{,}000{,}000$ tokens.

| Bigram | count | $P(w_1, w_2)$ | $P(w_1)$ | $P(w_2)$ | $P(w_1)P(w_2)$ | PMI (bits) |
|--------|-------|---------------|----------|----------|----------------|------------|
| "strong coffee" | 8000 | $8 \times 10^{-6}$ | $0.01$ | $0.02$ | $2 \times 10^{-4}$ | $\log_2(0.04) = -4.64$ |
| "strong tea" | 6500 | $6.5 \times 10^{-6}$ | $0.01$ | $0.015$ | $1.5 \times 10^{-4}$ | $\log_2(0.0433) = -4.53$ |
| "strong of" | 50 | $5 \times 10^{-8}$ | $0.01$ | $0.005$ | $5 \times 10^{-5}$ | $\log_2(0.001) = -9.97$ |

### Step 5 — Interpretation
- **Higher PMI** → stronger association → acquired as collocation.
- For OOV bigrams where $\text{count}(w_1,w_2) = 0$, use **Positive PMI (PPMI)**:
$$
\text{PPMI}(w_1, w_2) = \max(0, \text{PMI}(w_1, w_2))
$$

## 3.3 Derivation III — Cosine Similarity Between Lexical Vectors

**Goal:** Measure semantic similarity between two acquired word vectors $\vec{u}, \vec{v} \in \mathbb{R}^d$.

### Step 1 — Dot Product
$$
\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v} = \sum_{k=1}^{d} u_k v_k
$$

### Step 2 — L2 Norms
$$
\Vert\vec{u}\Vert = \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{d} u_k^2}, \quad \Vert\vec{v}\Vert = \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{d} v_k^2}
$$

### Step 3 — Cosine Similarity
$$
\text{sim}(\vec{u}, \vec{v}) = \frac{\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v}}{\Vert\vec{u}\Vert \, \Vert\vec{v}\Vert}
$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Verification
Let $\vec{u} = (1, 2, 3)$, $\vec{v} = (2, 4, 6)$.

- Dot product: $1 \cdot 2 + 2 \cdot 4 + 3 \cdot 6 = 2 + 8 + 18 = 28$
- $\Vert\vec{u}\Vert = \sqrt{1 + 4 + 9} = \sqrt{14}$
- $\Vert\vec{v}\Vert = \sqrt{4 + 16 + 36} = \sqrt{56} = 2\sqrt{14}$
- $\text{sim} = \dfrac{28}{\sqrt{14} \cdot 2\sqrt{14}} = \dfrac{28}{28} = 1.0$

A similarity of **1.0** confirms $\vec{u}$ and $\vec{v}$ are identical in direction (collinear) — meaning the two words are *semantically aligned* in the acquired space.

## 3.4 Acquisition Pipeline — Algorithmic Matrix

The complete lexical acquisition algorithm in pseudo-formal steps:

$$
\text{Input: } D = \text{corpus}, \ k = \text{window size}, \ V = \text{initial vocabulary}
$$

$$
\text{Output: } L^* = \text{enriched lexicon}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Algorithm: Lexical-Acquire}(D, k, V) \\
&1.\ T \leftarrow \text{Tokenize}(D) \\
&2.\ V^* \leftarrow V \cup \{\text{new tokens in } T\} \\
&3.\ M \leftarrow \text{BuildCooccurrenceMatrix}(T, k) \\
&4.\ \text{for each } w^* \in V^* \setminus V: \\
&5.\qquad \vec{w^*} \leftarrow \text{ContextVector}(w^*, M) \\
&6.\qquad c^* \leftarrow \text{POSClassifier}(\vec{w^*}) \\
&7.\qquad s^* \leftarrow \text{Cluster}(w^*, V^*) \\
&8.\qquad L^* \leftarrow L^* \cup \langle w^*, c^*, \text{NULL}, s^* \rangle \\
&9.\ \text{return } L^*
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The co-occurrence matrix $M$ is the *raw material* for both statistical (PMI, TF-IDF) and neural (embedding) acquisition methods. The choice of $k$ (window size) — typically $k \in \{2, 5, 10\}$ — fundamentally alters the captured semantics: small $k$ → syntactic similarity, large $k$ → topical similarity.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Lexical Acquisition Pipeline (End-to-End Architecture)

<!-- Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied: All node IDs are alphanumeric, no reserved keywords as standalone names, no markdown inside labels. -->

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    corpusNode1[Raw Text Corpus] --> tokenizeNode2[Tokenization Module]
    tokenizeNode2 --> normalNode3[Normalization: Lowercase, Unicode]
    normalNode3 --> oovNode4[OOV Detection]
    oovNode4 -->|Known| lookupNode5[Lexicon Lookup]
    oovNode4 -->|Unknown| acquireNode6[Acquisition Trigger]
    lookupNode5 --> contextNode7[Context Window Extractor]
    acquireNode6 --> contextNode7
    contextNode7 --> featureNode8[Feature Vector Builder]
    featureNode8 --> statNode9[Statistical Inference]
    featureNode8 --> neuralNode10[Neural Embedding Model]
    statNode9 --> decideNode11[Hypothesis Scoring]
    neuralNode10 --> decideNode11
    decideNode12[Bayesian Decision Rule]
    decideNode11 --> decideNode12
    decideNode12 --> updateNode13[Lexicon Update]
    updateNode13 --> finalNode14[Enriched Lexicon L Star]
    finalNode14 --> evalNode15[Evaluation Module]
    evalNode15 -->|Feedback| contextNode7
```

## 4.2 Bayesian Lexical Learning — Belief Update Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    priorNode1[Prior P of h] --> combineNode2[Multiply with Likelihood]
    dataNode3[Observed Context Data] --> likelihoodNode4[Likelihood P of d given h]
    likelihoodNode4 --> combineNode2
    combineNode2 --> normalizeNode5[Normalize by P of d]
    normalizeNode5 --> posteriorNode6[Posterior P of h given d]
    posteriorNode6 -->|Update Belief| priorNode1
    posteriorNode6 --> outputNode7[Acquire New Lexical Entry]
```

## 4.3 Distributional Semantics — Word Space Acquisition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputNode1[Sentence Stream] --> windowNode2[Sliding Context Window]
    windowNode3[Target Word w]
    windowNode3 --> windowNode2
    windowNode2 --> cooccurNode4[Co-occurrence Counting]
    cooccurNode4 --> matrixNode5[Co-occurrence Matrix M]
    matrixNode5 --> pmiNode6[PMI / PPMI Weighting]
    matrixNode5 --> svdNode7[SVD Dimensionality Reduction]
    pmiNode6 --> vecNode8[Dense Word Vector]
    svdNode7 --> vecNode8
    vecNode9[Semantic Space R to the d]
    vecNode8 --> vecNode9
    vecNode9 --> simNode10[Cosine Similarity Queries]
    simNode10 --> lexNode11[Final Lexicon with Sense Clusters]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Hybrid Acquisition System

```mermaid

    subgraph subgraphInput[INPUT LAYER]
        rawNodeA[Corpus D]
        seedNodeB[Seed Lexicon L0]
    end

        neuralNodeD[Neural Engine: Word2Vec, BERT]

    seedNodeB --> neuralNodeD

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip for Students:** In the hybrid architecture, each engine produces a *score*, and the fusion layer combines them. This is the **standard pattern** in industry NLP systems (e.g., spaCy's pipeline, Stanford CoreNLP) and is a high-yield KTU examination topic.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define lexical acquisition. List any two challenges faced during computational lexical acquisition.**  `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Lexical acquisition is the process of automatically learning, inducing, and updating word-level linguistic knowledge — including form, category, morphology, and meaning — from raw or annotated text corpora, enabling NLP systems to handle open-vocabulary scenarios.
- **Challenges (1.5 marks — any two):**
  1. **Data sparsity** — Rare words have insufficient context for reliable statistics.
  2. **Polysemy** — A single form maps to multiple senses, complicating representation.
  3. **Neologisms** — New words emerge continuously, requiring continual learning.
  4. **Morphological productivity** — Indefinite derivations cannot be enumerated.

---

### **Q2. State the Distributional Hypothesis. How does it form the theoretical basis of modern word embeddings?**  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Statement (1.5 marks):** The Distributional Hypothesis, formulated by J. R. Firth (1957), states that *words occurring in similar contexts tend to have similar meanings*. In formal terms, semantic similarity between $w_1$ and $w_2$ is a function of the similarity between their conditional context distributions $P(\cdot \mid w_1)$ and $P(\cdot \mid w_2)$.
- **Basis for embeddings (1.5 marks):** Modern embedding models (Word2Vec, GloVe, BERT) operationalize this hypothesis by learning dense vector representations such that words with similar distributions are mapped to nearby points in $\mathbb{R}^d$. The embedding dimension $d$ is fixed (typically 100–768), and the geometry of the space encodes semantic relations (e.g., the famous analogy *"king" − "man" + "woman" ≈ "queen"*).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**  `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) (7 marks)** Explain the Bayesian framework for word acquisition. Derive the posterior probability expression and describe how a learner updates their belief as new contextual data is observed.

**(b) (7 marks)** Given a toy corpus of 10,000 tokens, the word *"machine"* appears 200 times, *"learning"* appears 150 times, and the bigram *"machine learning"* appears 80 times. Compute the PMI for this bigram and interpret whether it qualifies as a collocation.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Bayesian Word Acquisition (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** The Bayesian framework treats lexical acquisition as *hypothesis updating under uncertainty*. The learner maintains a posterior distribution over possible hypotheses (e.g., possible POS categories) for a target word.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Starting with a **uniform prior**:
$$
P(h_i) = \frac{1}{V}, \quad i = 1, \ldots, V
$$
where $V$ is the number of candidate categories.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Upon observing contextual data $d = (c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_n)$, the learner updates via Bayes' rule:
$$
P(h_i \mid d) = \frac{P(d \mid h_i) \cdot P(h_i)}{P(d)}
$$
With the naive Bayes factorization:
$$
P(d \mid h_i) = \prod_{j=1}^{n} P(c_j \mid h_i)
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Decision rule — pick the Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) hypothesis:
$$
h^* = \arg\max_{h_i} \left[ \log P(h_i) + \sum_{j=1}^{n} \log P(c_j \mid h_i) \right]
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** **Belief update cycle** — The posterior becomes the prior for the next observation. This iterative Bayesian updating mimics the human child's incremental learning: each new sentence refines the lexicon entry.

#### Part (b) — PMI Computation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Frequencies:**
- $N = 10{,}000$
- $\text{count}(\text{machine}) = 200$
- $\text{count}(\text{learning}) = 150$
- $\text{count}(\text{machine learning}) = 80$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Probabilities:**
$$
P(\text{machine}) = \frac{200}{10{,}000} = 0.02
$$
$$
P(\text{learning}) = \frac{150}{10{,}000} = 0.015
$$
$$
P(\text{machine, learning}) = \frac{80}{10{,}000} = 0.008
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Expected Joint Under Independence:**
$$
P(\text{machine}) \cdot P(\text{learning}) = 0.02 \times 0.015 = 3 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Compute PMI:**
$$
\text{PMI} = \log_2 \frac{P(w_1, w_2)}{P(w_1) \cdot P(w_2)} = \log_2 \frac{0.008}{0.0003}
$$
$$
= \log_2 (26.67) = \log_2(26.67)
$$
$$
= \frac{\ln(26.67)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{3.283}{0.693} \approx 4.74 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Interpretation:**
- The expected frequency under independence is $3 \times 10^{-4} \times 10{,}000 = 3$ bigrams.
- The observed frequency is $80$ — approximately **26.67 times higher** than expected.
- $\text{PMI} \approx 4.74$ bits is **substantially positive**, indicating strong association.
- **Conclusion:** *"machine learning"* qualifies as a **true collocation** and should be acquired as a single lexical entry in the lexicon.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) (7 marks)** Describe the architecture of a hybrid lexical acquisition system integrating statistical, neural, and symbolic methods. Explain the role of each layer.

**(b) (7 marks)** For two word vectors $\vec{u} = (3, 1, 2)$ and $\vec{v} = (1, 2, 3)$, compute the cosine similarity. Explain what this value indicates about the semantic relationship between the two words.

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Hybrid Acquisition Architecture (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** A hybrid system overcomes the limitations of any single paradigm. It has **four logical layers**:

1. **Input Layer** — Receives raw corpus $D$ and seed lexicon $L_0$ (initial known words).
2. **Processing Layer** — Three parallel engines:
   - **Statistical Engine** — Frequency counts, PMI, TF-IDF, conditional probabilities.
   - **Neural Engine** — Embedding induction via Word2Vec (CBOW/Skip-gram) or contextualized BERT.
   - **Symbolic Engine** — Hand-crafted morphological rules, finite-state transducers.
3. **Fusion Layer (3 marks):** Each engine emits a *score vector* for a candidate word. A weighted combiner (often a learned meta-classifier) merges these scores:
$$
\text{Score}_{\text{final}}(w) = \alpha \cdot S_{\text{stat}}(w) + \beta \cdot S_{\text{neural}}(w) + \gamma \cdot S_{\text{symbolic}}(w)
$$
where $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1$.
4. **Output Layer (2 marks):** Emits an enriched lexicon $L^*$ with entries $\langle w, c, m, s \rangle$ and **confidence scores**. An evaluation module (precision, recall, F1) provides feedback to retune the weights.

**Why Hybrid?** (closing remark, included in marks) — Statistical methods handle coverage; neural methods capture nuance; symbolic methods provide interpretability and handle morphologically rich languages.

#### Part (b) — Cosine Similarity Computation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Dot Product:**
$$
\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v} = (3)(1) + (1)(2) + (2)(3) = 3 + 2 + 6 = 11
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Magnitudes:**
$$
\Vert\vec{u}\Vert = \sqrt{3^2 + 1^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{9 + 1 + 4} = \sqrt{14}
$$
$$
\Vert\vec{v}\Vert = \sqrt{1^2 + 2^2 + 3^2} = \sqrt{1 + 4 + 9} = \sqrt{14}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Cosine Similarity:**
$$
\cos(\vec{u}, \vec{v}) = \frac{11}{\sqrt{14} \cdot \sqrt{14}} = \frac{11}{14} \approx 0.786
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Interpretation:**
- A value of **+0.786** (on the scale $[-1, +1]$) indicates a **strong positive semantic similarity**.
- The two word vectors are not identical (which would be $+1.0$) but point in roughly the same direction in the acquired semantic space.
- **Implication:** The two words share considerable semantic overlap but are not synonyms. They are likely related concepts in the same domain.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Forgetting the normalization** — Students often write $\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v} = 11$ as the "similarity" — this is **wrong**; it is the *un-normalized* dot product. Marks are deducted for omitting the denominator $\Vert\vec{u}\Vert \cdot \Vert\vec{v}\Vert$.
> 2. **Sign confusion in PMI** — PMI can be negative; students sometimes interpret a negative PMI as "no relation" and forget the **absolute value matters less than the magnitude** in log-space.
> 3. **Bayes' theorem missing denominator** — Writing $P(h \mid d) = P(d \mid h) \cdot P(h)$ without the $P(d)$ normalization loses a mark. Always include the full Bayes expression.
> 4. **Mixing up frequency and probability** — In derivations, use $\text{count}(w)/N$ explicitly; writing only $\text{count}(w)$ is a frequency, not a probability.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: Lexical Acquisition**

- **Definition:** Lexical acquisition = automated induction of word-level linguistic knowledge (form, category, morphology, meaning) from corpora.
- **Distributional Hypothesis (Firth):** *"You shall know a word by the company it keeps."* Foundation of all modern embeddings.
- **Bayesian Learning:** $P(h \mid d) = \dfrac{P(d \mid h) \cdot P(h)}{P(d)}$ — update prior with observed likelihood.
- **MAP Decision:** $h^* = \arg\max_h \left[\log P(h) + \sum \log P(c_j \mid h)\right]$ — log form is exam-standard.
- **PMI:** $\text{PMI}(w_1, w_2) = \log_2 \dfrac{P(w_1, w_2)}{P(w_1) P(w_2)}$ — measures collocation strength; **higher = stronger association**.
- **PPMI:** $\max(0, \text{PMI})$ — handles zero-count cases.
- **Cosine Similarity:** $\cos(\vec{u}, \vec{v}) = \dfrac{\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v}}{\Vert\vec{u}\Vert \, \Vert\vec{v}\Vert} \in [-1, +1]$.
- **Smoothing:** Laplace add-one: $P(w) = \dfrac{\text{count}(w) + 1}{N + V}$ — **mandatory** for unseen events.
- **Hybrid Architecture:** Statistical + Neural + Symbolic engines fused via weighted score combination.
- **Zipf's Law:** $f(r) \propto 1/r^{\alpha}$ — word frequencies follow a power law; $\alpha \approx 1$ empirically.
- **Window Size $k$:** Small $k$ → syntactic similarity; large $k$ → topical/semantic similarity.
- **Evaluation Metrics:** Precision, Recall, F1-score for measuring acquisition quality; coverage of vocabulary for open-vocabulary testing.
- **Sub-word Acquisition:** BPE (Byte-Pair Encoding) and WordPiece are modern forms of lexical acquisition used in all transformer models.
- **Key Real-World Examples:** Google Translate (BPE), BERT (WordPiece), spaCy (rule + statistical), Stanford CoreNLP (hybrid).
- **Syllabus Tag:** This topic is **CO3-aligned** (Apply statistical and machine learning techniques to linguistic data) under the KTU 2024 B.Tech outcome-based framework.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Lexical Acquisition**

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Computational Linguistics (CL) and Natural Language Processing (NLP), **Evaluation Measures** refer to the systematic, quantitative (and occasionally qualitative) methodologies used to assess the performance, correctness, and utility of computational models, algorithms, and linguistic resources against human-defined gold standards. Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned Outcome-Based Education)**, evaluation is treated as a **mandatory engineering deliverable** for any CL pipeline, as it provides empirical evidence that a system meets its stated **Course Outcomes (COs)** and **Performance Indicators (PIs)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-aligned):**
> *Evaluation Measures are the metrics, methodologies, and statistical instruments used to quantify how well an NLP/CL system performs a linguistic task — such as tagging, parsing, translation, summarization, or speech recognition — by comparing its output to a reference standard (gold standard/human annotated data).*

The discipline of CL evaluation is often summarized by the famous dictum:

> *"You cannot improve what you cannot measure."* — attributed to **Peter Drucker**, foundational to empirical NLP research.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: Grading a Student's Examination Sheet

Think of an NLP system as a **student taking an examination**, and evaluation measures as the **teacher's marking scheme**:

| Real-World Analogy | CL Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Question Paper (set of test cases) | Benchmark / Test Dataset |
| Student's Answer Sheet | System's Predicted Output |
| Official Answer Key (set by teacher) | Gold Standard / Reference (Human-Annotated) |
| Marking Scheme (marks for correct/wrong) | Metric Function (Precision, Recall, BLEU, etc.) |
| Final Grade Card | Evaluation Score |

Just as a teacher cannot simply declare a student "pass" or "fail" without going through a structured marking scheme, an NLP system cannot be deemed "good" or "bad" without **rigorous, reproducible evaluation measures**. The marking scheme must be **unambiguous, repeatable, and standardized** — which is why CL relies on universally agreed metrics (BLEU for translation, ROUGE for summarization, WER for speech, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Every model-building question in the ESE (End Semester Evaluation) of PEADT632 requires you to justify your model with a *quantitative evaluation metric*. A model without an evaluation is considered **incomplete** and is a guaranteed 2–3 mark deduction.

---

## 1.3 Taxonomic Overview of Evaluation (Big Picture)

The entire evaluation framework in CL can be classified along **four orthogonal axes**:

1. **Method of Judgment** → Automatic vs. Human (Manual) Evaluation
2. **Scope of Assessment** → Intrinsic vs. Extrinsic Evaluation
3. **Task Type** → Classification Metrics vs. Generation Metrics vs. Sequence Metrics
4. **Reference Type** → Reference-based vs. Reference-free Evaluation

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick-Recall Hook:** For a 3-mark question, simply state:
> *"Evaluation in CL is broadly classified into Intrinsic (measuring the system's internal quality) and Extrinsic (measuring the system's contribution to a downstream real-world task)."*

---

## 1.4 Engineering Relevance

Evaluation is the **backbone of empirical CL research** and has direct industry impact:

- **Search Engines** (Google, Bing) — NDCG, MAP, MRR
- **Machine Translation** (Google Translate) — BLEU, chrF, COMET
- **Virtual Assistants** (Alexa, Siri) — WER, Intent Accuracy
- **Healthcare NLP** (clinical NER) — F1, Micro/Macro averaging
- **Sentiment Analysis** (Twitter, Reviews) — Accuracy, Macro-F1

> [!NOTE]
> A single BLEU point improvement in a deployed MT system can save **millions of dollars annually** in post-editing costs, which is why evaluation is treated as a **first-class engineering artifact** in production CL systems.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Confusion Matrix — Foundation of Classification Evaluation

A **Confusion Matrix** (also called an *Error Matrix* or *Contingency Table*) is an $N \times N$ tabular representation of a classifier's predictions against the true labels, where $N$ is the number of distinct classes. For a binary classifier, it is a $2 \times 2$ matrix:

| Predicted Class | Actual: Positive | Actual: Negative |
|---|---|---|
| **Predicted: Positive** | True Positive (TP) | False Positive (FP) |
| **Predicted: Negative** | False Negative (FN) | True Negative (TN) |

**Operational meaning of each cell:**

- **TP (True Positive):** System predicted *Positive* AND truth is *Positive* → **Correct hit**
- **FP (False Positive):** System predicted *Positive* BUT truth is *Negative* → **Type-I Error (False Alarm)**
- **FN (False Negative):** System predicted *Negative* BUT truth is *Positive* → **Type-II Error (Miss)**
- **TN (True Negative):** System predicted *Negative* AND truth is *Negative* → **Correct rejection**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse *Precision* and *Recall*. Memorize the analogy:
> - **Precision = "Of all alarms the system raised, how many were real?"** (Quality of alarm)
> - **Recall = "Of all real events, how many did the system catch?"** (Coverage of alarm)

---

## 2.2 Core Classification Metrics (Derived from Confusion Matrix)

### 2.2.1 Accuracy

Accuracy is the **proportion of correct predictions** out of the total predictions. It is the most intuitive but least reliable metric on imbalanced datasets.

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$

**Limitation:** On a dataset with 95% negatives and 5% positives, a naive "always predict negative" classifier achieves **95% accuracy** while being completely useless. Hence, Precision, Recall, and F-measure are preferred.

### 2.2.2 Precision (Positive Predictive Value)

Precision answers: *"When the system predicts a class, how often is it correct?"*

$$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$

### 2.2.3 Recall (Sensitivity, True Positive Rate)

Recall answers: *"Of all the actual instances of a class, how many did the system retrieve?"*

$$R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

### 2.2.4 F-Measure (F-Score, F1)

The F-measure is the **harmonic mean of Precision and Recall**. The harmonic mean (rather than arithmetic) is used because it **penalizes extreme values** — a system with P=1.0 and R=0.0 gets F1=0, not 0.5.

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$$

**Generalized F-beta Measure** (where $\beta$ controls the weight of Recall over Precision):

$$F_{\beta} = (1 + \beta^2) \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{(\beta^2 \cdot P) + R}$$

- $\beta = 1$ → Equal weight to P and R (standard F1)
- $\beta = 2$ → Recall weighted **2x** more (used in medical diagnosis, surveillance)
- $\beta = 0.5$ → Precision weighted more (used in search engines, where false positives are costly)

### 2.2.5 Specificity (True Negative Rate)

$$\text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP}$$

---

## 2.3 Micro vs. Macro Averaging (Multi-Class Extension)

For multi-class problems (e.g., POS tagging with 36 tags, NER with multiple entities), we average metrics across classes in two ways:

- **Macro-averaging:** Compute metric per class, then take unweighted mean → **treats all classes equally** (good for balanced insight).
- **Micro-averaging:** Pool all TP, FP, FN globally, then compute → **treats all instances equally** (good for imbalanced data).

$$\text{Macro-F1} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} F1_i$$

$$\text{Micro-F1} = \frac{2 \cdot \sum_i TP_i}{2 \cdot \sum_i TP_i + \sum_i FP_i + \sum_i FN_i}$$

---

## 2.4 Generation / Sequence Evaluation Metrics

### 2.4.1 BLEU (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy)

Developed by **Papineni et al. (2002)** at IBM for **Machine Translation** evaluation. BLEU computes a **modified n-gram precision** with a **brevity penalty (BP)**.

$$BLEU = BP \cdot \exp\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \cdot \log p_n\right)$$

Where:
- $p_n$ = modified precision for n-grams of length $n$
- $w_n$ = uniform weight, typically $w_n = \frac{1}{N}$ (most commonly $N=4$)
- $BP$ = Brevity Penalty (to discourage overly short outputs):

$$BP = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } c > r \\ \exp(1 - r/c) & \text{if } c \leq r \end{cases}$$

Here, $c$ = length of candidate (system output), $r$ = length of reference.

### 2.4.2 ROUGE (Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation)

Developed by **Lin (2004)** primarily for **text summarization** evaluation. The family includes:

$$\text{ROUGE-N} = \frac{\sum_{S \in \text{Refs}} \sum_{\text{gram}_n \in S} \text{Count}_{\text{match}}(\text{gram}_n)}{\sum_{S \in \text{Refs}} \sum_{\text{gram}_n \in S} \text{Count}(\text{gram}_n)}$$

- **ROUGE-1** → unigram overlap (recall-oriented)
- **ROUGE-2** → bigram overlap
- **ROUGE-L** → Longest Common Subsequence (LCS)-based F-measure

### 2.4.3 METEOR (Metric for Evaluation of Translation with Explicit ORdering)

**Banerjee & Lavie (2005)** — addresses BLEU's limitations by using **word stems, synonyms, and paraphrases** from WordNet, plus a fragmentation penalty.

$$\text{METEOR} = F_{\text{mean}} \cdot (1 - \text{Penalty})$$

### 2.4.4 Perplexity (PPL) — Language Model Evaluation

Perplexity is the **exponential of the cross-entropy loss** of a language model on a test corpus. **Lower perplexity = better model.**

$$\text{PPL}(W) = 2^{H(W)} = \exp\left(-\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}, w_{i-2}, \dots, w_1)\right)$$

Where $H(W)$ is the cross-entropy and $N$ is the number of words.

### 2.4.5 WER (Word Error Rate) — Speech Recognition

$$\text{WER} = \frac{S + D + I}{N} = \frac{\text{Substitutions} + \text{Deletions} + \text{Insertions}}{\text{Total words in reference}}$$

Computed using the **edit distance (Levenshtein distance)** between hypothesis and reference.

---

## 2.5 Significance Testing

To confirm that one system's improvement over another is **statistically significant** (and not due to chance), CL research uses:

- **Paired Bootstrap Resampling** (Koehn, 2004) — most common in MT
- **Wilcoxon Signed-Rank Test** — non-parametric alternative
- **McNemar's Test** — for classification comparison

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Engineering Note:** In any ESE answer where you compare two models, you MUST mention the need for **significance testing** to score full marks. Stating "Model A is better than Model B" without statistical justification is a 1-mark deduction trigger.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table contains **all exam-critical formulas** for the Evaluation Measures topic. **Memorize and reproduce verbatim** in the exam.

| **Metric** | **Formula (LaTeX-safe notation)** | **Range** | **Best Value** | **Primary Task** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | $\frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | [0, 1] | 1 | Balanced classification |
| Precision (P) | $\frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | [0, 1] | 1 | Classification / Information Retrieval |
| Recall (R) | $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | [0, 1] | 1 | Classification / IR / NER |
| F1-Score | $2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P+R}$ | [0, 1] | 1 | All classification |
| F-beta | $(1+\beta^2) \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{(\beta^2 P) + R}$ | [0, 1] | 1 | Weighted classification |
| Specificity | $\frac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | [0, 1] | 1 | Imbalanced binary |
| Macro-F1 | $\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} F1_i$ | [0, 1] | 1 | Multi-class (balanced) |
| Micro-F1 | $\frac{2 \cdot \sum TP_i}{2 \sum TP_i + \sum FP_i + \sum FN_i}$ | [0, 1] | 1 | Multi-class (imbalanced) |
| BLEU | $BP \cdot \exp\bigl(\sum w_n \log p_n\bigr)$ | [0, 1] | 1 | Machine Translation |
| Brevity Penalty | $\exp(1 - r/c)$ if $c \le r$, else $1$ | (0, 1] | 1 | MT (length penalty) |
| ROUGE-N | $\frac{\sum \text{Count}_{\text{match}}(\text{gram}_n)}{\sum \text{Count}(\text{gram}_n)}$ | [0, 1] | 1 | Summarization |
| ROUGE-L | F-measure of LCS-based recall | [0, 1] | 1 | Summarization |
| METEOR | $F_{\text{mean}} \cdot (1 - \text{Penalty})$ | [0, 1] | 1 | MT (with synonyms) |
| Perplexity | $\exp\bigl(-\frac{1}{N} \sum \log P(w_i \mid \cdot)\bigr)$ | [1, $\infty$) | 1 | Language Models |
| WER | $\frac{S+D+I}{N}$ | [0, $\infty$) | 0 | Speech Recognition |
| Cohen's Kappa | $\frac{p_o - p_e}{1 - p_e}$ | [-1, 1] | 1 | Inter-annotator agreement |

> **Symbol Note:** In all formulas above, $TP, FP, TN, FN$ are the standard confusion matrix counts; $p_o$ = observed agreement, $p_e$ = expected agreement by chance; $N$ = total word count (for WER) or class count (for Macro-F1).

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why a CL engineer MUST know these formulas:**

- **Building a chatbot?** Use **Intent Classification Accuracy + Macro-F1** to report.
- **Deploying a translation API?** **BLEU** on a held-out test set is the contractual SLA metric.
- **Developing a medical NER for drug names?** Use **F2-score** (recall-weighted, since missing a drug name is dangerous).
- **Training an LLM (like GPT-style)?** Report **Perplexity** on WikiText-103 and **zero-shot accuracy** on downstream tasks (extrinsic).
- **Evaluating speech-to-text in call centers?** Report **WER per domain** (medical/legal WER is typically much higher).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section demonstrates **exhaustive step-by-step numerical evaluations** and **analytical derivations** of the key metrics. **No step is skipped.**

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Confusion Matrix → Precision, Recall, F1, Accuracy

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A Named Entity Recognition (NER) system is evaluated on a test set of 200 tokens. The confusion matrix is:

|  | Pred: PERSON | Pred: ORG | Pred: O (Outside) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Actual: PERSON** | 30 | 5 | 10 |
| **Actual: ORG** | 8 | 25 | 7 |
| **Actual: O** | 2 | 3 | 110 |

**Compute: Accuracy, Per-class Precision, Per-class Recall, Macro-F1, Micro-F1.**

### Step 1 — Compute Overall Accuracy

Total correct predictions = sum of diagonal = $30 + 25 + 110 = 165$

Total predictions = $200$

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{165}{200} = 0.825 = 82.5\%$$

### Step 2 — Per-Class Precision

**For PERSON class:**
TP = 30 (predicted PERSON AND actual PERSON)
FP = 8 + 2 = 10 (predicted PERSON but was actually ORG or O)

$$P_{\text{PERSON}} = \frac{30}{30 + 10} = \frac{30}{40} = 0.75$$

**For ORG class:**
TP = 25, FP = 5 + 3 = 8

$$P_{\text{ORG}} = \frac{25}{25 + 8} = \frac{25}{33} \approx 0.7576$$

**For O class:**
TP = 110, FP = 10 + 7 = 17

$$P_{O} = \frac{110}{110 + 17} = \frac{110}{127} \approx 0.8661$$

### Step 3 — Per-Class Recall

**For PERSON:** FN = 5 + 10 = 15

$$R_{\text{PERSON}} = \frac{30}{30 + 15} = \frac{30}{45} = 0.6\overline{6} \approx 0.6667$$

**For ORG:** FN = 8 + 7 = 15

$$R_{\text{ORG}} = \frac{25}{25 + 15} = \frac{25}{40} = 0.625$$

**For O:** FN = 2 + 3 = 5

$$R_{O} = \frac{110}{110 + 5} = \frac{110}{115} \approx 0.9565$$

### Step 4 — Per-Class F1

**PERSON:**
$$F1_{\text{PERSON}} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.75 \cdot 0.6667}{0.75 + 0.6667} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.5000}{1.4167} \approx 0.7059$$

**ORG:**
$$F1_{\text{ORG}} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.7576 \cdot 0.625}{0.7576 + 0.625} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.4735}{1.3826} \approx 0.6849$$

**O:**
$$F1_{O} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8661 \cdot 0.9565}{0.8661 + 0.9565} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8284}{1.8226} \approx 0.9091$$

### Step 5 — Macro-F1 (Unweighted average across classes)

$$\text{Macro-F1} = \frac{0.7059 + 0.6849 + 0.9091}{3} = \frac{2.2999}{3} \approx 0.7666$$

### Step 6 — Micro-F1 (Pooled globally)

Global totals: $\sum TP = 30+25+110 = 165$, $\sum FP = 10+8+17 = 35$, $\sum FN = 15+15+5 = 35$

$$\text{Micro-F1} = \frac{2 \cdot 165}{2 \cdot 165 + 35 + 35} = \frac{330}{365} \approx 0.9041$$

**Observations:** Micro-F1 (0.904) > Macro-F1 (0.767). This is because the "O" class dominates with 110/200 instances, so the instance-weighted Micro-F1 is pulled up. **Macro-F1 reveals the system's weakness on PERSON and ORG classes** — a typical KTU follow-up discussion.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: BLEU Score Calculation (N=2, simplified)

**Problem Statement:**
Reference sentence: *"the cat is on the mat"* (length = 6)
Candidate (system output): *"the the the cat on mat"* (length = 5)

Compute **BLEU-2** with uniform weights $w_1 = w_2 = 0.5$.

### Step 1 — Clipped Counts for Unigrams

| Unigram | Count in Candidate | Max Count in Reference | Clipped Count |
|---|---|---|---|
| the | 3 | 2 | **2** |
| cat | 1 | 1 | **1** |
| on | 1 | 1 | **1** |
| mat | 1 | 1 | **1** |
| **Total** | 6 | — | **5** |

$$p_1 = \frac{5}{6} \approx 0.8333$$

### Step 2 — Clipped Counts for Bigrams

Candidate bigrams: *(the, the), (the, the), (the, cat), (cat, on), (on, mat)*

| Bigram | Count in Candidate | Max Count in Reference | Clipped |
|---|---|---|---|
| (the, the) | 2 | 0 | 0 |
| (the, cat) | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| (cat, on) | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| (on, mat) | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| **Total candidate** | 5 | — | **3** |

$$p_2 = \frac{3}{5} = 0.6$$

### Step 3 — Brevity Penalty

$c = 5$ (candidate length), $r = 6$ (reference length). Since $c \le r$:

$$BP = \exp\left(1 - \frac{r}{c}\right) = \exp\left(1 - \frac{6}{5}\right) = \exp(-0.2) \approx 0.8187$$

### Step 4 — Final BLEU-2

$$BLEU = BP \cdot \exp\left(w_1 \log p_1 + w_2 \log p_2\right)$$

$$BLEU = 0.8187 \cdot \exp\bigl(0.5 \cdot \log(0.8333) + 0.5 \cdot \log(0.6)\bigr)$$

$$BLEU = 0.8187 \cdot \exp\bigl(0.5 \cdot (-0.1823) + 0.5 \cdot (-0.5108)\bigr)$$

$$BLEU = 0.8187 \cdot \exp(-0.3466) = 0.8187 \cdot 0.7071 \approx 0.5789$$

**Result: BLEU-2 ≈ 0.579** (low score, indicating poor translation — "the the the" is a classic repetition artifact).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Perplexity Computation

**Problem Statement:** A bigram language model assigns the following probabilities to a 4-word test sentence *"I love NLP course"*:

- $P(\text{I} \mid \langle s \rangle) = 0.5$
- $P(\text{love} \mid \text{I}) = 0.4$
- $P(\text{NLP} \mid \text{love}) = 0.3$
- $P(\text{course} \mid \text{NLP}) = 0.2$

### Step 1 — Compute Log-Likelihood

$$LL = \log(0.5) + \log(0.4) + \log(0.3) + \log(0.2)$$

$$LL = -0.6931 + (-0.9163) + (-1.2040) + (-1.6094)$$

$$LL = -4.4228$$

### Step 2 — Compute Perplexity

Using natural log (base $e$):

$$\text{PPL} = \exp\left(-\frac{LL}{N}\right) = \exp\left(-\frac{-4.4228}{4}\right) = \exp(1.1057) \approx 3.021$$

**Interpretation:** On average, the model is "as confused as if it had to choose uniformly among ~3 words at each position." Lower PPL = better model.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: WER Calculation (Speech Recognition)

**Reference:** *"open the door please"* (N = 4 words)
**Hypothesis:** *"open to door please"*

**Edit Operations:**
- "open" → "open" (match)
- "the" → "to" (1 substitution)
- "door" → "door" (match)
- "please" → "please" (match)

$$S = 1, \quad D = 0, \quad I = 0, \quad N = 4$$

$$\text{WER} = \frac{1 + 0 + 0}{4} = 0.25 = 25\%$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Metric (Decision Table)

| **CL Task** | **Primary Metric** | **Secondary Metric** | **Why?** |
|---|---|---|---|
| POS Tagging | Accuracy | Per-tag F1 | Most tags are frequent and balanced |
| Named Entity Recognition (NER) | F1 (Micro) | Per-entity F1 | High class imbalance (O dominates) |
| Sentiment Classification | Macro-F1 | Accuracy | Classes (pos/neg/neutral) need equal weight |
| Machine Translation | BLEU | chrF, METEOR | Need n-gram overlap with penalty |
| Text Summarization | ROUGE-1, ROUGE-L | BERTScore | Recall-oriented task |
| Language Modeling | Perplexity | Bits-per-character (BPC) | Direct measure of probability assignment |
| Speech Recognition | WER | CER (Character Error Rate) | Edit-distance based |
| Coreference Resolution | MUC, B³, CEAF | CoNLL F1 (avg) | Multiple competing scoring schemes |
| Question Answering | Exact Match (EM) + F1 | — | Need both exact and partial credit |
| Dialogue Generation | Human Eval + BLEU/ROUGE | Distinct-n (diversity) | Automatic metrics correlate poorly |
| Information Extraction | F1 | Precision @ Recall threshold | High cost of false positives |

```mermaid
    pN --> bleuAgg[BLEU Aggregator: BP x exp sum wn log pn]
    bpCalc --> bleuAgg
    
    bleuAgg --> finalOut[Final BLEU Score in 0 to 1]
    
    style bleuAgg fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style finalOut fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style cmp fill:#ed7d31,color:#ffffff
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This pipeline shows the **two parallel branches** of BLEU computation:
> 1. **Top branch** → N-gram extraction → clipping → modified precision → exponential aggregation.
> 2. **Bottom branch** → Length comparison → Brevity Penalty.
> 3. **Convergence** → The two branches merge in the **BLEU Aggregator** to produce the final score.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
