# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are modeled on **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE patterns** (Part A: 3 marks; Part B: 14 marks with internal choice). Each question is tagged with a **simulated KTU Past Year** label. Mark allocation is strictly per KTU 2024 norms.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Define Precision, Recall, and F1-score with formulas. Explain why F1 is preferred over Accuracy for imbalanced datasets.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Precision (1 mark):** Ratio of correctly predicted positive instances to total predicted positives.
$$P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$$

- **Recall (1 mark):** Ratio of correctly predicted positive instances to total actual positives.
$$R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$$

- **F1 & Justification (1 mark):** F1 is the harmonic mean of P and R:
$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P+R}$$

**Imbalanced justification:** On a 95:5 imbalanced dataset, a "majority-class-only" classifier yields 95% accuracy but 0% F1 (since recall on minority = 0). Hence F1 exposes the model's failure where Accuracy hides it.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Intrinsic and Extrinsic Evaluation with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Intrinsic Evaluation | Extrinsic Evaluation |
|---|---|---|
| Definition (1 mark) | Measures the **internal quality** of a system in isolation, against a reference standard. | Measures the system's **contribution to a downstream real-world task**. |
| Example (1 mark) | Computing **BLEU** of an MT system on a held-out parallel corpus. | Measuring **customer satisfaction** after deploying a chatbot in an e-commerce site. |
| Cost (1 mark) | Cheap, automatic, reproducible. | Expensive, requires real users, slow. |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Norm:** Part B questions carry 14 marks, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. The student attempts **ONE full question** (either Option A or Option B).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**Q. A (a)** Explain the Confusion Matrix in detail. For a binary classification problem, derive the formulas for Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-Score from the confusion matrix. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Confusion Matrix Definition (2 marks):**
A confusion matrix is an $N \times N$ table where rows represent **actual classes** and columns represent **predicted classes** (or vice versa). For binary classification ($N=2$):

|  | Predicted: Yes | Predicted: No |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual: Yes** | TP | FN |
| **Actual: No** | FP | TN |

**Definitions:**
- $TP$ (True Positive): Correctly predicted positive class.
- $FP$ (False Positive, Type-I Error): Negative instance wrongly predicted as positive.
- $FN$ (False Negative, Type-II Error): Positive instance wrongly predicted as negative.
- $TN$ (True Negative): Correctly predicted negative class.

**(ii) Derivation of Metrics (5 marks):**

**Accuracy** = fraction of all correct predictions:

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{\text{Correct Predictions}}{\text{Total Predictions}} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$

**Precision** = fraction of predicted positives that are truly positive:

$$P = \frac{TP}{\text{Total Predicted Positive}} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$

**Recall (Sensitivity)** = fraction of actual positives that were correctly retrieved:

$$R = \frac{TP}{\text{Total Actual Positive}} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

**F1-Score** = harmonic mean of Precision and Recall:

$$F_1 = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R}} = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$$

**Derivation of harmonic mean from arithmetic intuition:**
Arithmetic mean of P, R is $\frac{P+R}{2}$. F1 instead computes the **harmonic mean** because the harmonic mean of a set of numbers is **always less than or equal to the arithmetic mean** and **equals the minimum** when one value is zero. This ensures F1=0 if either P=0 or R=0, which is the desired behavior (a useless classifier should not get credit).

---

**Q. A (b)** Describe the BLEU metric for Machine Translation evaluation. Explain the Brevity Penalty and the modified n-gram precision. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) BLEU Introduction (1 mark):**
**BLEU (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy)** was proposed by **Papineni et al. (2002)** at IBM for automatic evaluation of machine translation. It is a precision-oriented metric that compares n-grams of the candidate translation against one or more reference translations.

**(ii) Modified n-gram Precision (3 marks):**
For each n-gram order $n$ (typically $n=1,2,3,4$), compute the **clipped count**: the count of n-gram in the candidate is capped by its **maximum count in any single reference**. This prevents gaming the metric (e.g., repeating the same word).

$$p_n = \frac{\sum_{\text{ngram} \in C} \min(\text{Count}_C(\text{ngram}), \max_{R \in \text{Refs}} \text{Count}_R(\text{ngram}))}{\sum_{\text{ngram} \in C} \text{Count}_C(\text{ngram})}$$

**(iii) Brevity Penalty (2 marks):**
BLEU is precision-biased, so short translations can artificially inflate precision. The **Brevity Penalty (BP)** penalizes candidates shorter than the reference:

$$BP = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } c > r \\ \exp\left(1 - \frac{r}{c}\right) & \text{if } c \le r \end{cases}$$

where $c$ is candidate length, $r$ is the closest reference length.

**(iv) Final BLEU Formula (1 mark):**

$$BLEU = BP \cdot \exp\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \cdot \log p_n\right)$$

with $w_n = \frac{1}{N}$ for uniform weighting. Score range: $[0, 1]$, with $1$ being perfect.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**Q. B (a)** Explain Perplexity as an evaluation metric for Language Models. Compute the perplexity of a trigram model that assigns the probabilities: $P(w_1)=0.1, P(w_2 \mid w_1)=0.5, P(w_3 \mid w_1,w_2)=0.2$ for a test sentence of 3 words. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Definition (2 marks):**
**Perplexity (PPL)** is the standard intrinsic evaluation metric for language models. It measures how "confused" a probability model is when predicting a sample. Mathematically, it is the **exponential of the average negative log-likelihood (cross-entropy)** per word:

$$\text{PPL}(W) = \exp\left(-\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}, \dots, w_1)\right)$$

**(ii) Interpretation (2 marks):**
- Lower PPL = better model.
- A perplexity of $k$ means the model is as perplexed as choosing uniformly from $k$ possibilities at each step.
- A model that assigns probability 1 to the true next word has PPL = 1.

**(iii) Numerical Computation (3 marks):**
Given $N = 3$ and:

- $\log P(w_1) = \log(0.1) = -2.3026$
- $\log P(w_2 \mid w_1) = \log(0.5) = -0.6931$
- $\log P(w_3 \mid w_1, w_2) = \log(0.2) = -1.6094$

Sum: $-2.3026 + (-0.6931) + (-1.6094) = -4.6051$

$$\text{PPL} = \exp\left(-\frac{-4.6051}{3}\right) = \exp(1.5350) \approx 4.642$$

**Result:** $\text{PPL} \approx 4.64$ (interpreted as: the model is as uncertain as picking from ~4.6 equally likely words at each step).

---

**Q. B (b)** Explain WER (Word Error Rate) and ROUGE metrics. Mention the task each is best suited for, and the formula for each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) WER — Word Error Rate (3.5 marks):**
- **Task:** Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) evaluation.
- **Definition:** WER is the **edit distance (Levenshtein distance)** between the hypothesis and the reference transcription, normalized by reference length.
- **Edit Operations:** $S$ = Substitutions, $D$ = Deletions, $I$ = Insertions.
- **Formula:**

$$\text{WER} = \frac{S + D + I}{N} = \frac{\text{Edit Distance}}{N}$$

where $N$ = total words in the reference.
- **Range:** $[0, \infty)$. Can exceed 1 if hypothesis is much longer than reference.
- **Example:** If a 10-word reference has 1 substitution, 0 deletions, 0 insertions, WER = 0.10 = 10%.

**(ii) ROUGE — Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation (3.5 marks):**
- **Task:** Automatic evaluation of **text summarization** (and sometimes MT).
- **Variants:**
  - **ROUGE-N** = n-gram recall overlap with reference.
  - **ROUGE-L** = Longest Common Subsequence (LCS)-based F-measure.
- **Formula for ROUGE-N:**

$$\text{ROUGE-N} = \frac{\sum_{S \in \text{Refs}} \sum_{\text{gram}_n \in S} \text{Count}_{\text{match}}(\text{gram}_n)}{\sum_{S \in \text{Refs}} \sum_{\text{gram}_n \in S} \text{Count}(\text{gram}_n)}$$

- **Formula for ROUGE-L (F-measure of LCS-based Precision and Recall):**

$$P_{\text{LCS}} = \frac{\text{LCS}(C,R)}{m}, \quad R_{\text{LCS}} = \frac{\text{LCS}(C,R)}{n}$$

$$F_{\text{LCS}} = \frac{(1+\beta^2) P_{\text{LCS}} R_{\text{LCS}}}{R_{\text{LCS}} + \beta^2 P_{\text{LCS}}}$$

where $m$ = length of candidate, $n$ = length of reference, $\beta$ is typically large (e.g., $\beta \to \infty$ for pure recall).

- **Key contrast:** BLEU is precision-biased; ROUGE is **recall-biased** (suited for summarization where capturing all key content matters more than precision).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mistakes Students Make in Evaluation Measures Questions (and How to Avoid Them):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Micro-F1 with Macro-F1:** Always clarify which average you are computing. If the question says "average F1 across classes" → Macro. If it says "overall F1 over all instances" → Micro.
> 2. **Forgetting the Brevity Penalty in BLEU:** Writing only $\exp(\sum w_n \log p_n)$ without the BP factor loses 1 mark. Always state the BP piecewise formula.
> 3. **Using Accuracy for Imbalanced Data:** If a question mentions fraud detection, cancer diagnosis, or anomaly detection, **never** propose Accuracy as the primary metric — propose **F1 or F2-score** instead.
> 4. **Reporting WER > 1 without comment:** WER can exceed 1.0 (when insertions are many). State this explicitly to score full marks.
> 5. **Omitting Significance Testing:** When comparing two systems, the answer is incomplete without mentioning **bootstrap resampling** or **paired t-test**. Examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for this.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section. Read it 30 minutes before the exam. You will recover 80% of high-yield content.**

**Core Concepts to Memorize:**

- [ ] Confusion Matrix layout: TP, FP, FN, TN positions
- [ ] Precision = TP/(TP+FP); Recall = TP/(TP+FN); F1 = 2PR/(P+R)
- [ ] Harmonic mean is used in F1 (not arithmetic mean) — because it punishes extremes
- [ ] F-beta formula with $\beta$ controlling Precision/Recall trade-off
- [ ] Macro-F1 (unweighted across classes) vs. Micro-F1 (instance-weighted)
- [ ] Accuracy is **misleading** on imbalanced datasets
- [ ] BLEU = BP × exp($\sum w_n \log p_n$) — used for **Machine Translation**
- [ ] Brevity Penalty = exp(1 − r/c) if c ≤ r, else 1
- [ ] Modified n-gram precision uses **clipping** (cap by max reference count)
- [ ] ROUGE-N = recall-oriented n-gram overlap — used for **Summarization**
- [ ] ROUGE-L uses **Longest Common Subsequence (LCS)**
- [ ] METEOR = F_mean × (1 − Penalty) — uses stems, synonyms, WordNet
- [ ] Perplexity = exp(−(1/N) × $\sum \log P(w_i)$) — used for **Language Models**, lower is better
- [ ] WER = (S + D + I) / N — used for **Speech Recognition**, computed via edit distance
- [ ] Cohen's Kappa accounts for chance agreement
- [ ] Intrinsic evaluation = internal quality; Extrinsic = real-world downstream task
- [ ] Human evaluation is the **gold standard** but is expensive and non-reproducible
- [ ] Automatic evaluation is reproducible but may not correlate with human judgment
- [ ] **Significance testing** (bootstrap, paired t-test) is mandatory when comparing systems
- [ ] Inter-annotator agreement (Cohen's Kappa, Krippendorff's alpha) measures dataset quality

**Key Names to Remember (for 1-mark fact questions):**
- [ ] **BLEU** → Papineni et al. (IBM, 2002)
- [ ] **ROUGE** → Lin (2004)
- [ ] **METEOR** → Banerjee & Lavie (2005)
- [ ] **Perplexity** → Standard LM metric since Jelinek (1976) era
- [ ] **WER** → Used in NIST RT evaluations

**Common Tasks → Metrics Map (memorize this 1:1 mapping):**
- [ ] NER / POS → **F1**
- [ ] MT → **BLEU**
- [ ] Summarization → **ROUGE**
- [ ] LM → **Perplexity**
- [ ] ASR → **WER**
- [ ] IR / Search → **Precision@k, MAP, MRR, NDCG**
- [ ] QA → **Exact Match + F1**
- [ ] Coreference → **CoNLL F1 (avg of MUC, B³, CEAF)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Verb Subcategorization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Verb Subcategorization** # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned) **Verb Subcategorization** is a foundational concept in syntactic theory and computational linguistics that classifies a verb based on the **syntactic frames** and **complement types** it can grammatically license. In the formal grammar literature, a verb's subcategorization (often called its **subcategorization frame** or **complementation pattern**) specifies the set of allowable argument structures and phrasal categories that must co-occur with that verb to form a grammatical sentence. Formally, if $V$ denotes a verb lexicon entry, its subcategorization frame is a feature structure $F_V$ such that: $$F_V = \langle C_1, C_2, ..., C_n \rangle$$ where each $C_i$ is a constituent drawn from the inventory of possible complements, typically expressed as category labels such as **NP** (Noun Phrase), **PP** (Prepositional Phrase), **CP** (Complementizer Phrase), **S** (Sentence/clause), **AP** (Adjective Phrase), or **PART** (Particle). The valence $n$ of the verb equals the number of obligatory complement slots it licenses. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Subcategorization is **not** the same as valency/argument structure alone. It is a *lexically specified* property of each verb, encoding both the *number* and the *syntactic category* of its complements. The lexicon is the source of this information. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Imagine verbs as **electrical sockets**, and grammatical phrases as **plugs of different shapes**. - A verb like *"sleep"* is like a socket with **no plug holes** — it works by itself (no complements required). - A verb like *"eat"* is like a socket with **one plug hole** — it needs an object (NP) to function. - A verb like *"give"* is like a socket with **two plug holes** — it needs both a recipient and a theme (NP + NP). - A verb like *"believe"* needs a special **three-prong plug** (an entire CP/that-clause). Just as a socket rejects plugs of incompatible shape, a verb *rejects* complements of the wrong syntactic type. This "shape" of requirements is precisely the **subcategorization frame**. > [!NOTE] > **Why does this matter in NLP?** Parsers, grammar checkers, information extraction systems, and question-answering engines all rely on subcategorization information to determine whether a sentence is **grammatical** and to extract the **semantic roles** (agent, patient, instrument, goal) of participants in an event. ## 1.3 Core Terminology Snapshot | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **Subcategorization Frame** | The syntactic pattern of complements a verb takes | | **Argument** | A phrase required or licensed by the verb (subject, object, etc.) | | **Complement** | An argument that is *selected* by the verb (vs. an adjunct, which is optional) | | **Obligatory vs. Optional** | Obligatory complements must appear; optional ones may be omitted | | **Selectional Restrictions** | Semantic constraints on the fillers of a subcategorization slot | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations Verb subcategorization emerges from the interplay of three linguistic theories that you must know for KTU exams: ### 2.1.1 Government and Binding Theory (GB) / Principles & Parameters In GB theory, subcategorization is encoded as a **subcategorization feature** on the lexical entry of a verb. The head of a phrase (V) projects its subcategorization requirements upward, and the syntactic component must *discharge* these features by combining V with the correct number of sister constituents of the correct categories. ### 2.1.2 Head-Driven Phrase Structure Grammar (HPSG) HPSP uses **Attribute Value Matrices (AVMs)** to encode subcategorization. A verb's SUBCAT list is a feature whose value is an *ordered list* of categories that must be saturated. As each complement is merged, the list shortens. A verb is fully saturated (grammatical) when its SUBCAT list is empty. ### 2.1.3 Construction Grammar / FrameNet In frame semantics, each verb evokes a **semantic frame**, and the frame lists the **frame elements** (semantic roles) that must be filled. This is the semantic counterpart of syntactic subcategorization. ## 2.2 Classification of Verbs by Subcategorization Frame The canonical inventory of English verb subcategorization frames (high-yield for KTU) is: | Frame Notation | Description | Example Sentence | |---|---|---| | **V** | Intransitive (no complement) | *The child sleeps.* | | **V NP** | Transitive (single object) | *She eats an apple.* | | **V NP NP** | Ditransitive (two objects) | *He gave her a book.* | | **V PP** | Prepositional complement | *She relies on him.* | | **V NP PP** | Transitive + prepositional | *She put the book on the table.* | | **V to-VP** | Infinitive complement | *He wants to leave.* | | **V VP-ing** | Gerundive complement | *She enjoys reading.* | | **V NP to-VP** | Object + infinitive | *She wants him to leave.* | | **V NP VP-ing** | Object + gerund | *She watched him leaving.* | | **V that-CP** | That-clause complement | *He believes that she is honest.* | | **V wh-CP** | Interrogative complement | *She asked what he wanted.* | | **V AP** | Adjectival complement | *He considers her intelligent.* | | **V NP AP** | Object + AP | *They painted the wall red.* | | **V PART** | Particle | *She looked up the word.* | | **V NP PART** | Transitive particle | *She looked the word up.* | ## 2.3 Formal Probability Framework (For Computational Models) In statistical/computational subcategorization acquisition, the likelihood of observing a frame $F$ for verb $v$ given a corpus is computed using **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)**: $$P(F \mid v) = \frac{\text{count}(v, F)}{\sum_{F' \in \mathcal{F}_v} \text{count}(v, F')}$$ where $\mathcal{F}_v$ is the set of all observed subcategorization frames for verb $v$. To handle **sparse data**, **smoothing** is applied. The standard add-$\lambda$ (Laplace) estimate is: $$P_{\text{smoothed}}(F \mid v) = \frac{\text{count}(v, F) + \lambda}{\sum_{F'} \text{count}(v, F') + \lambda \cdot \vert \mathcal{F} \vert}$$ where $\vert \mathcal{F} \vert$ is the total number of possible frames in the model. The **entropy** of the subcategorization distribution for verb $v$ (a measure of how versatile the verb is) is: $$H(v) = -\sum_{F \in \mathcal{F}_v} P(F \mid v) \log_2 P(F \mid v)$$ A higher entropy means the verb participates in more diverse syntactic contexts (e.g., *take*, *get*, *make* have very high entropy in English). ## 2.4 Selectional Restrictions Beyond syntactic category, verbs impose **semantic** restrictions on their arguments. For example, the verb *"eat"* requires its object to be an **edible entity**. Formally, selectional restrictions are predicates $Sel_i$ that constrain the semantic class of the $i$-th argument: $$\exists x \, [\text{NP}_i(x) \land Sel_i(x)]$$ Selectional Association (Resnik 1996, classical NLP measure) computes the strength of selectional preference using information-theoretic divergence between the conditional distribution of semantic classes given a verb-role and the marginal distribution. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | Concept | Expression | Purpose | |---|---|---| | Subcategorization Frame | $F_V = \langle C_1, ..., C_n \rangle$ | Encodes complement structure of verb $V$ | | MLE Frame Probability | $P(F \mid v) = \frac{c(v,F)}{\sum_{F'} c(v,F')}$ | Probabilistic frame assignment | | Add-$\lambda$ Smoothing | $P_\lambda(F \mid v) = \frac{c(v,F)+\lambda}{N+\lambda \vert \mathcal{F} \vert}$ | Sparse data correction | | Subcategorization Entropy | $H(v) = -\sum_F P(F\mid v) \log_2 P(F \mid v)$ | Verb versatility measure | | Selectional Predicate | $Sel_i(x) \rightarrow$ semantic class constraint | Semantic well-formedness | | Argument-Adjunct Distinction | $\text{Complement} = \text{selected}$; $\text{Adjunct} = \text{optional \& non-selected}$ | Core vs. peripheral | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Verb subcategorization drives modern NLP pipelines — *dependency parsers* (Stanford, spaCy), *semantic role labelers* (AllenNLP SRL), *question-answering systems*, and *information extraction* (e.g., extracting "who gave what to whom" in a financial news corpus) all require accurate subcategorization lexicons. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Inducing Subcategorization Frames from a Corpus This is the canonical **Brent / Manning subcategorization acquisition algorithm** adapted for KTU-level exposition. We will derive step-by-step how raw text is transformed into a subcategorization lexicon. ### Step 1: Input and Tagging Let a tagged corpus sentence be: $$\text{``She/PRS gave/VBD him/PPO a/DT book/NN ./.''}$$ Apply a **part-of-speech tagger** to obtain: $w_1, w_2, ..., w_n$ with tags $t_1, t_2, ..., t_n$. ### Step 2: Locate the Verb Identify the target verb $v = w_i$ where $t_i \in \{VBD, VBG, VBN, VBP, VBZ, VB\}$. For our example, $v =$ *"gave"*, $t_i =$ **VBD**. ### Step 3: Extract Constituents to the Right Identify the immediate constituents following the verb, terminating at sentence boundaries (period, question mark, semicolon) or at new verb phrases. The syntactic parser (e.g., PCFG, dependency parser) provides the constituent boundaries. For *"gave him a book"*: - Constituent 1: **NP** = *"him"* - Constituent 2: **NP** = *"a book"* ### Step 4: Build the Frame Concatenate the category sequence into a frame string: $$F_v = \text{NP} \cdot \text{NP} = \text{NP NP}$$ For verb *"gave"*, the observed frame is **NP NP** (ditransitive). ### Step 5: Aggregate Counts over Corpus For each occurrence of verb $v$ in a corpus of size $N$, increment $\text{count}(v, F_v)$. After processing the entire corpus, the empirical distribution of frames is built. ### Step 6: Apply Smoothing To avoid zero probabilities, apply add-$\lambda$ smoothing. With $\lambda = 1$, $\vert \mathcal{F} \vert = 15$ possible frames, and observed counts as in the worked example below: $$P(\text{NP NP} \mid \text{give}) = \frac{120 + 1}{300 + 15} = \frac{121}{315} \approx 0.384$$ ### Step 7: Resolve Ambiguity (Particle vs. Preposition) A notorious challenge: in *"look up the street"*, *"up"* is a **PP** (preposition), but in *"look up the word"*, *"up"* is a **PART** (particle). The disambiguation heuristic uses *pronominal replacement*: - *"Look up it"* ✓ → **PART** (particle, can be moved before NP) - *"Look up the street"* → **PP** (cannot move: *"*Look the street up"* is awkward without a direction reading) For computational systems, features include: (1) whether the particle can precede the NP, (2) idiomaticity, (3) corpus statistics. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Entropy Calculation Suppose verb *"run"* has the following observed frame distribution from a corpus: | Frame $F$ | Count $c(\text{run}, F)$ | |---|---| | V (intransitive) | 800 | | V NP (transitive) | 150 | | V PP | 40 | | V NP NP | 10 | | **Total** | **1000** | Compute the probability of each frame: $$P(\text{V} \mid \text{run}) = \frac{800}{1000} = 0.800$$ $$P(\text{V NP} \mid \text{run}) = \frac{150}{1000} = 0.150$$ $$P(\text{V PP} \mid \text{run}) = \frac{40}{1000} = 0.040$$ $$P(\text{V NP NP} \mid \text{run}) = \frac{10}{1000} = 0.010$$ Now compute the entropy in bits: $$ \begin{aligned} H(\text{run}) &= -\sum_{F} P(F \mid \text{run}) \log_2 P(F \mid \text{run}) \\ &= -[0.800 \log_2 0.800 + 0.150 \log_2 0.150 \\ &\quad + 0.040 \log_2 0.040 + 0.010 \log_2 0.010] \\ &= -[0.800 \cdot (-0.3219) + 0.150 \cdot (-2.7369) \\ &\quad + 0.040 \cdot (-4.6439) + 0.010 \cdot (-6.6439)] \\ &= -[-0.2575 - 0.4105 - 0.1858 - 0.0664] \\ &= 0.9202 \text{ bits} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** *Run* is a low-entropy verb — overwhelmingly intransitive. Contrast with *"take"*, which has $H > 3$ bits due to its participation in many frames (V NP, V NP NP, V NP PP, V NP to-VP, etc.). ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Argument vs. Adjunct Test The single most important diagnostic in subcategorization theory is **distinguishing complements from adjuncts**. Use the following matrix of tests: | Test | Complement (Selected) | Adjunct (Optional) | |---|---|---| | **It-cleft** | *"It is **a book** that she gave him."* ✓ | *"It is **yesterday** that she gave him a book."* (debatable) | | **Question formation** | *"What did she give him?"* ✓ (object stranding) | *"When did she give him a book?"* ✓ (adverbial) | | **Pseudo-cleft** | *"What she gave him was a book."* ✓ | — | | **One-replacement** | *"She gave him one."* ✓ | — (adjuncts cannot be *one*-replaced) | | **Recurrence** | *"*She gave him a book **a magazine**."* ✗ | *"She gave him a book **yesterday** **in the park**."* ✓ (multiple adverbs) | | **Obligatoriness** | Cannot be omitted without ungrammaticality | Can be freely omitted | | **Semantic role** | Bears a thematic role (THEME, GOAL) | Bears circumstantial role (TIME, MANNER) | **Practical application:** When building a computational lexicon, *only* complements enter the subcategorization frame; adjuncts are filtered out using the recurrence test and one-replacement test. ## 3.4 Subcategorization Acquisition — Algorithm Pseudocode for NLP The full computational pipeline is: 1. **Preprocess:** POS-tag and parse the corpus using a statistical parser. 2. **Extract:** For each verb token, extract its right-side constituents as a candidate frame. 3. **Normalize:** Map to a canonical frame string (e.g., NP, PP, CP, S). 4. **Filter:** Discard frames with only adjuncts (using the matrix in §3.3). 5. **Aggregate:** Count frequencies of (verb, frame) pairs. 6. **Smooth:** Apply Laplace or Kneser-Ney smoothing to handle unseen frames. 7. **Prune:** Discard rare frames below a frequency threshold $\theta$ (e.g., $\theta = 5$). 8. **Resolve:** Use the entropy threshold to determine which verbs are "versatile" (high $H$) vs. "rigid" (low $H$). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Taxonomy of English Verb Subcategorization Frames ```mermaid graph TD A[English Verbs] --> B[Zero-Valent
No Complement] A --> C[Mono-Valent
One Complement] A --> D[Di-Valent
Two Complements] A --> E[Tri-Valent
Three Complements] B --> B1[V: sleep, run, arrive] C --> C1[V NP: eat, see, build] C --> C2[V PP: rely on, listen to] C --> C3[V to-VP: want, hope, decide] C --> C4[V VP-ing: enjoy, finish, avoid] C --> C5[V that-CP: believe, claim, think] C --> C6[V AP: become, seem, remain] D --> D1[V NP NP: give, tell, send] D --> D2[V NP PP: put, place, load] D --> D3[V NP to-VP: want, ask, force] D --> D4[V NP VP-ing: watch, catch, hear] D --> D5[V NP AP: consider, paint, call] D --> D6[V NP that-CP: tell, convince, remind] E --> E1[V NP NP NP: bet, charge, fine] E --> E2[V NP PP PP: transport, translate] ``` ## 4.2 Computational Subcategorization Acquisition Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw Corpus] --> B[POS Tagger] B --> C[Statistical Parser
PCFG or Dependency] C --> D[Verb Identification] D --> E[Constituent Extraction
Right of Verb] E --> F[Complement vs Adjunct
Filtering] F --> G[Frame Normalization] G --> H[Frequency Counting] H --> I[Smoothing
Laplace or KN] I --> J[Pruning by Threshold] J --> K[Subcategorization Lexicon] K --> L[Entropy Computation
H of v] L --> M[Versatile vs Rigid
Verb Classification] ``` ## 4.3 HPSG-Style Subcategorization Discharge ```mermaid graph LR subgraph "Initial State" A1[give
SUBCAT: list NP, NP] end subgraph "After First Merge" A2[give
SUBCAT: list NP] end subgraph "Final Saturated State" A3[give
SUBCAT: empty list] end A1 -->|merge with him| A2 A2 -->|merge with a book| A3 ``` ## 4.4 Argument-Adjunct Decision Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Phrase Follows Verb] --> B{Is the phrase
obligatory?} B -->|Yes| C[Complement] B -->|No| D{Can it be replaced
by 'one'?} D -->|Yes| C D -->|No| E{Can multiple instances
co-occur?} E -->|Yes, freely| F[Adjunct] E -->|No, blocked| C C --> G[Add to Subcategorization Frame] F --> H[Discard as Modifier] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define **verb subcategorization** with a suitable example. How does it differ from simple valency/argument structure? **Model Answer:** Verb subcategorization is a lexical property of a verb that specifies the *syntactic categories* and *number* of complements it requires or permits. It encodes not just *how many* arguments a verb takes (valency), but *what kind* of phrases must fill those slots. **Example:** The verb *"give"* has the subcategorization frame **V NP NP**, meaning it requires a subject (NP) plus two NP complements (e.g., *"She gave him a book"*). **Difference from valency:** Valency only counts the number of arguments (give has valence 3). Subcategorization additionally specifies the *categorial type* of each slot (give requires NP, not PP or CP). A verb like *"rely"* also has valence 2 (subject + complement) but its subcategorization frame is **V PP**, not **V NP**, because it requires a prepositional phrase. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Explain the difference between a **complement** and an **adjunct**, and describe one diagnostic test to distinguish them. **Model Answer:** A **complement** is a phrase that is *selected* (lexically required) by the head verb — it completes the verb's meaning and cannot be freely added or removed. An **adjunct** is an *optional* modifier that adds circumstantial information (time, manner, location) and can be freely inserted or deleted without affecting grammaticality. **Diagnostic test — One-replacement test:** - *"She gave him **one**."* ✓ → "a book" is a complement (replaceable by "one"). - *"She gave him a book **yesterday* one**."* ✗ → "yesterday" is an adjunct (not replaceable by "one"). **Recurrence test:** Multiple adjuncts can co-occur freely (*"She gave him a book **yesterday** **in the park** **deliberately**"*), but complements are restricted (*"*She gave him a book **a magazine**" is ungrammatical). --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A — 14 Marks **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the various subcategorization frames of English verbs. Provide at least **eight distinct frames** with one example sentence each. Classify verbs based on the number of complements they license. **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain with a worked example how the **entropy** of a verb's subcategorization distribution is computed. What does a high entropy value indicate about the verb? #### Model Solution for (a) **Number of complements (Valence classes):** 1. **Zero-valent (intransitive) — Frame: V** - *"The baby sleeps."* (sleep: V) 2. **Mono-valent with NP — Frame: V NP** (transitive) - *"She reads a novel."* (read: V NP) 3. **Mono-valent with PP — Frame: V PP** - *"He relies on his friends."* (rely: V PP) 4. **Mono-valent with that-CP — Frame: V that-CP** - *"She believes that he is honest."* (believe: V that-CP) 5. **Di-valent with two NPs — Frame: V NP NP** (ditransitive) - *"He gave her a gift."* (give: V NP NP) 6. **Di-valent with NP + PP — Frame: V NP PP** - *"She placed the vase on the table."* (place: V NP PP) 7. **Di-valent with NP + to-VP — Frame: V NP to-VP** - *"They asked him to leave."* (ask: V NP to-VP) 8. **Di-valent with NP + AP — Frame: V NP AP** (resultative) - *"They painted the wall red."* (paint: V NP AP) 9. **Tri-valent — Frame: V NP NP NP** (triadic) - *"They charged him ten dollars for the book."* (charge: V NP NP PP) → V NP NP PP is also valid **Classification Table:** | Valence | Frame Example | Verb Type | |---|---|---| | 0 | V | Intransitive (sleep) | | 1 | V NP, V PP, V that-CP | Mono-transitive / Mono-complement | | 2 | V NP NP, V NP PP, V NP to-VP | Di-complement (Ditransitive / Complex transitive) | | 3 | V NP NP NP, V NP NP PP | Triadic (Rare) | #### Model Solution for (b) **Entropy Formula:** $$H(v) = -\sum_{F \in \mathcal{F}_v} P(F \mid v) \log_2 P(F \mid v)$$ **Worked example for verb "take"** (hypothetical corpus counts): | Frame $F$ | Count $c$ | $P(F \mid \text{take})$ | $-P \log_2 P$ | |---|---|---|---| | V NP | 500 | 0.500 | 0.500 | | V NP NP | 200 | 0.200 | 0.464 | | V NP PP | 150 | 0.150 | 0.411 | | V NP to-VP | 100 | 0.100 | 0.332 | | V NP AP | 50 | 0.050 | 0.216 | | **Total** | **1000** | **1.000** | **1.923** | $$ \begin{aligned} H(\text{take}) &= 0.500 \cdot 1.000 + 0.200 \cdot 2.322 + 0.150 \cdot 2.737 \\ &\quad + 0.100 \cdot 3.322 + 0.050 \cdot 4.322 \\ &= 0.500 + 0.464 + 0.411 + 0.332 + 0.216 \\ &= 1.923 \text{ bits} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** A high entropy value (e.g., $H(\text{take}) = 1.92$ bits) indicates that the verb is **syntactically versatile** — it participates in many distinct subcategorization frames with relatively balanced probability. Such verbs are called **light verbs** or **highly polysemous** (e.g., *take, get, make, put, have*). They are computationally challenging for parsers because they offer little syntactic constraint. Low-entropy verbs (e.g., $H(\text{snore}) \approx 0.1$ bits) are rigid and easy to parse. --- ### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **HPSG (Head-Driven Phrase Structure Grammar) approach** to verb subcategorization. How does the SUBCAT list feature work, and how is it discharged during parsing? **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **selectional restrictions** of verbs. With suitable examples, explain how verbs constrain the semantic class of their complements. Discuss at least **two formal mechanisms** for representing these restrictions. #### Model Solution for (a) **HPSG Approach:** HPSG is a unification-based, lexicalist grammar framework in which each lexical entry (including verbs) is a typed **Attribute Value Matrix (AVM)**. The subcategorization information is stored in a feature called **SUBCAT** (subcategorization list), whose value is an *ordered list of syntactic categories* that the verb needs to combine with. **Structure of a lexical entry for "give":** $$ \begin{bmatrix} \text{word} & \text{give} \\ \text{cat} & \text{verb} \\ \text{SUBCAT} & \langle \text{NP}, \text{NP} \rangle \\ \text{SUBJ} & \langle \text{NP}[\text{nom}] \rangle \end{bmatrix} $$ **Discharge Mechanism (Head-Complement Rule):** The Head-Complement Phrase (HXC) rule unifies a head with the first element of its SUBCAT list and removes that element: $$ \begin{bmatrix} \text{head-subj} & \text{HEAD} \\ \text{head-complement} & \begin{bmatrix} \text{HEAD-DTR} & \text{verb} \\ \text{NON-HEAD-DTRS} & \langle [\;] \rangle \\ \text{HEAD-COMP} & \text{NP} \end{bmatrix} \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step-by-step discharge for *"She gave him a book"*:** 1. Initial: `give` with SUBCAT = ⟨ NP, NP ⟩ 2. Merge subject "She": SUBCAT = ⟨ NP, NP ⟩ (unchanged — subject is in SUBJ list) 3. Merge "him" (NP) as first complement: SUBCAT = ⟨ NP ⟩ 4. Merge "a book" (NP) as next complement: SUBCAT = ⟨ ⟩ (empty) 5. **Saturated** — verb phrase is complete; sentence is grammatical. **Advantage:** The HPSG account elegantly handles unbounded dependencies, long-distance dependencies, and the *complement-adjunct* distinction (adjuncts do not appear in SUBCAT; they are added recursively via the Head-Adjunct rule). #### Model Solution for (b) **Selectional Restrictions — Definition:** Selectional restrictions are **semantic constraints** imposed by a predicate (verb) on the semantic classes of its arguments. They go beyond syntactic category and restrict *what kinds of entities* can fill a slot. **Examples:** - *"eat"* → object must be **edible** (*"She ate the sandwich"* ✓, *"*She ate the rock*" ✗ — pragmatically odd) - *"drink"* → object must be **liquid/beverage** (*"drink water"* ✓, *"*drink a chair*" ✗) - *"slander"* → subject must be **animate** (*"He slandered her"* ✓, *"*The rock slandered her*" ✗) - *"witness"* → subject must be **animate/sentient** **Mechanism 1: Predicate Logic Representation** Selectional restrictions are encoded as predicates on semantic types. For *"eat"*: $$\text{eat}(x, y) \rightarrow \text{Edible}(y)$$ For *"slander"*: $$\text{slander}(x, y) \rightarrow \text{Animate}(x) \land \text{Animate}(y)$$ A sentence violates selectional restrictions if no assignment of referents satisfies the predicates, even though syntax may be well-formed. **Mechanism 2: Selectional Association (Resnik 1996, Information-Theoretic)** This statistical NLP approach measures how strongly a verb prefers a particular semantic class $c$ for a given argument role $r$: $$A(c \mid r, v) = \frac{1}{H(P(c \mid r))} \cdot P(c \mid r) \cdot \log \frac{P(c \mid r, v)}{P(c \mid r)}$$ where $H(P(c \mid r))$ is the entropy of the marginal distribution over classes in role $r$. Higher association = stronger selectional preference. This mechanism is widely used in **Word Sense Disambiguation** and **lexical acquisition**. **Mechanism 3 (Bonus): Type Hierarchy in Categorial/FrameNet Style** In FrameNet, verbs are linked to **frames** (e.g., *Commerce_buy*, *Eating*) and the frame elements have **semantic types** defined in a hierarchy (e.g., `Food ⊂ Edible ⊂ Physical_entity`). Selectional restrictions become type constraints that are checked via type unification. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Read Carefully!):** > 1. **Confusing valency with subcategorization:** Students often equate the two. Valency = *count* of arguments; subcategorization = *type* AND count. Always mention both. **(-2 marks)** > 2. **Forgetting to specify the syntactic category:** Saying *"give takes two objects"* is incomplete. You must say *"give takes the frame V NP NP"* with both NPs explicit. **(-1 mark)** > 3. **Treating adjuncts as complements:** In example sentences, students often include adverbs in the frame. Adverbs are adjuncts and DO NOT belong in the subcategorization frame. **(-1 mark)** > 4. **Confusing PP and PART (particle):** *"Look up the word"* — "up" is a particle (PART), not a preposition. Using a wrong test (e.g., pronominal substitution) is a common error. **(-1 mark)** > 5. **Computational questions — omitting smoothing:** When asked to compute frame probabilities from a corpus, ALWAYS apply smoothing (Laplace or Kneser-Ney). MLE alone gives zero probability to unseen frames. **(-2 marks)** > 6. **Entropy units:** Always specify bits (log base 2). Using natural log gives nats, not bits — a KTU examiner will deduct a mark if the unit is missing. **(-1 mark)** --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Verb Subcategorization** specifies *which* syntactic categories and *how many* complements a verb licenses — a **lexical property** of each verb. - ✅ The **subcategorization frame** is a sequence like **V NP NP** (ditransitive), **V that-CP** (clausal), **V PP** (prepositional). - ✅ **Complements** are *selected/required*; **adjuncts** are *optional modifiers*. Use the **one-replacement** and **recurrence tests** to distinguish them. - ✅ **Valence** = argument count; **Subcategorization** = argument count + category type. Do NOT conflate. - ✅ **HPSG** uses a **SUBCAT list** feature that shortens as complements merge. A verb is **saturated** when SUBCAT is empty. - ✅ **Government & Binding** uses a subcategorization feature that must be discharged by the syntactic component. - ✅ **Selectional Restrictions** = semantic constraints (e.g., *eat* requires edible object). Represented via **predicate logic**, **information-theoretic association**, or **FrameNet type hierarchies**. - ✅ **Entropy** $H(v) = -\sum P(F\mid v) \log_2 P(F\mid v)$ measures verb versatility — high entropy = light/polysemous verb (*take, get, make*). - ✅ **Smoothing** (Laplace: add-$\lambda$) is mandatory for corpus-based subcategorization acquisition to handle sparse data. - ✅ **Particle vs. Preposition** disambiguation uses the pronominal substitution test (*"Look it up"* → particle). - ✅ Common frames: V, V NP, V NP NP, V PP, V NP PP, V to-VP, V that-CP, V NP to-VP, V NP AP, V NP VP-ing, V wh-CP. - ✅ Applications: **dependency parsing, semantic role labeling, information extraction, question answering, grammar checking**.

topics.title: Attachment Ambiguity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Attachment Ambiguity**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Attachment Ambiguity** is a form of **syntactic (structural) ambiguity** in natural language that arises when a modifying constituent (most commonly a **Prepositional Phrase – PP**, a participial clause, a verbal phrase, or a relative clause) can plausibly modify (i.e., *attach to*) **more than one constituent** in the parse tree, producing multiple legitimate but semantically distinct interpretations of the same surface sentence.

Formally, given a sentence $S$ and a phrase $P$ modifying the structure, attachment ambiguity exists if there exist at least two distinct nodes $N_1, N_2$ in the parse tree $\mathcal{T}(S)$ such that $P$ is a valid sister/daughter of both:

$$\exists \, N_1, N_2 \in \mathcal{T}(S), \quad N_1 \neq N_2 \quad \text{where} \quad \text{Attach}(P, N_1) \land \text{Attach}(P, N_2) \text{ are both grammatically valid}$$

The "attachment decision" is the process of selecting the most plausible node from a candidate set:

$$N^* = \arg\max_{N_i \in \mathcal{C}(P)} \, P(\text{Attach}(P, N_i) \mid S)$$

where $\mathcal{C}(P)$ is the candidate attachment set and $P(\cdot \mid S)$ is the attachment probability conditioned on the full sentence.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Two-Picture Photo Analogy** 📸
>
> Imagine a photograph showing **a man and a boy standing in a park**. The question is: *"Who is holding the red balloon?"* The balloon is the "modifier" (the **Prepositional Phrase**), and it could plausibly be attached to either subject (the man OR the boy). Until you zoom in or get more context, **the same visual data yields two valid interpretations**.
>
> This is exactly what happens with the famous sentence:
> **"I saw the man with the telescope."**
> - Interpretation 1: The **man** had the telescope (low / NP-attachment)
> - Interpretation 2: **I** used the telescope to see the man (high / VP-attachment)
>
> The English sentence is *identical* in both cases, but the **parse tree** (and therefore the meaning) differs.

## 1.3 Standard Categorizations

KTU 2024 scheme classifies attachment ambiguity into **four primary types** based on the syntactic category of the ambiguous constituent:

| # | Type | Modifying Constituent | Classic Example |
|---|------|----------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **PP-Attachment** | Prepositional Phrase (PP) | *I saw the man **with the telescope*** |
| 2 | **VP-Attachment** | Verb Phrase | *I want to **eat** **Italian food*** |
| 3 | **Coordination Ambiguity** | Coordinated phrase | *I saw **the man and the woman** **with the telescope*** |
| 4 | **Adjective/Relative Clause Attachment** | AdjP / RC | *I like **the Chinese food** **and the service*** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Of the four, **PP-attachment** is treated as the *canonical* and most frequently examined case in KTU valuation patterns, and constitutes the most-cited problem in statistical parsing literature (cf. Ratnaparkhi 1998, Collins 1999).

## 1.4 Why It Matters (Engineering / NLP Utility)

Attachment ambiguity is **the** central reason why purely rule-based, context-free parsers fail on real-world English. Resolving it correctly is foundational to:
- **Machine Translation** (especially verb–object alignment)
- **Information Extraction** (relation extraction depends on argument linking)
- **Question Answering** (identifying what modifies what)
- **Sentiment Analysis** (negation scope and modifier polarity)
- **Semantic Role Labeling** (assigning Agent, Instrument, Patient correctly)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Structural Landscape of Attachment Ambiguity

A parse tree $\mathcal{T}$ for an ambiguous sentence has **multiple legal derivation paths** because the grammar $G$ (e.g., Penn Treebank PCFG) is **not strict enough** to disambiguate. The decision becomes a **disambiguation problem** over competing sub-trees.

### 2.1.1 The Two Dominant Attachment Sites for PPs

For a sentence pattern: **VP → V NP PP**, the PP has exactly two primary attachment sites:

- **High Attachment (H)**: PP attaches to the **VP** (i.e., modifies the *verb* / entire event). Example: *"I ate the spaghetti **with chopsticks**"* → instrument of eating.
- **Low Attachment (L)**: PP attaches to the **immediately preceding NP** (i.e., modifies the *object noun*). Example: *"I ate the spaghetti **with meatballs**"* → spaghetti is *with meatballs*.

A simple bracketed representation:

$$[\text{VP} \;\; \underbrace{V \;\; NP \;\; \overbrace{PP}^{\text{High Attachment (H)}}}_{\text{Low Attachment (L) possible at NP level}}]$$

## 2.2 Resolution Strategies — The Three Pillars

### 🔹 Pillar 1: Structural (Syntactic) Preferences

A purely structural baseline assumes the grammar's own probabilities favor one attachment over another. **Low attachment** is the **default preference** in most Penn-Treebank-trained PCFGs (right-association, also called *Kimball's 1973 principle*).

> [!NOTE]
> **Kimball's Right-Association Principle (1973)**: "When possible, attach new constituents to the most recent incomplete constituent." This generally predicts **low attachment**.

### 🔹 Pillar 2: Lexicalized Preferences (Head-Attachment Models)

The **lexical heads** of the PP, the preceding NP, and the governing verb are the strongest predictors. Lexicalized models learn from a treebank:

$$P(\text{H} \mid v, n, p, n_{\text{head}}) \quad \text{vs.} \quad P(\text{L} \mid v, n, p, n_{\text{head}})$$

where $v$ = verb, $n$ = object NP, $p$ = preposition, $n_{\text{head}}$ = head noun of NP.

### 🔹 Pillar 3: Semantic & Selectional Preferences

A PP is *semantically acceptable* only if it satisfies the **selectional restrictions** of its head. For example, "with the telescope" must be a *physical instrument* compatible with the agent of seeing.

## 2.3 The Statistical Attachment Decision — Lexical Association Score

The **Lexical Association Score** (Collins & Brooks, 1995) quantifies how strongly a PP prefers one attachment over another based on corpus counts:

$$A_{\text{Lex}}(v, n, p) = \log \frac{P(\text{attach to NP} \mid v, n, p)}{P(\text{attach to VP} \mid v, n, p)}$$

$$A_{\text{Lex}}(v, n, p) = \log \frac{\text{count}(v, n, p, \text{NP-attach}) / \text{count}(v, n, p)}{\text{count}(v, n, p, \text{VP-attach}) / \text{count}(v, n, p)}$$

A **positive score** $\Rightarrow$ PP-attach (low); **negative score** $\Rightarrow$ VP-attach (high).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table for exam day.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Range / Units | Used For |
|---|---------|---------------------|---------------|----------|
| 1 | Attachment Decision | $N^* = \arg\max_{N_i} P(\text{Attach}(P, N_i) \mid S)$ | $0 \le P \le 1$ | Choosing correct attachment site |
| 2 | Low (NP) Attachment | PP modifies object NP | Boolean | Default per Kimball's Principle |
| 3 | High (VP) Attachment | PP modifies entire VP / verb | Boolean | Instrument / Location of event |
| 4 | Lexical Association Score | $A = \log \frac{P(H_{\text{low}})}{P(H_{\text{high}})}$ | $(-\infty, +\infty)$ | Ranking ambiguous attachments |
| 5 | Backoff Estimator (deleted interpolation) | $\hat{P} = \lambda_1 P_1 + \lambda_2 P_2 + \lambda_3 P_3$ | $\sum \lambda_i = 1$ | Smoothing sparse counts |
| 6 | Maximum-Entropy Model | $P(H \mid x) = \frac{1}{Z(x)} \exp\left(\sum_i \lambda_i f_i(x, H)\right)$ | probability | Ratnaparkhi's PP-attacher |
| 7 | Right-Association (Kimball) | New constituents attach rightmost | Structural rule | Default disambiguation |
| 8 | Head-Finding Rule (Magerman) | Choose rightmost child for right-headed, leftmost for left-headed | Rule | Lexicalization of trees |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Google Translate / DeepL**: Neural decoders implicitly learn to resolve PP-attachment via contextualized embeddings (e.g., BERT, mBERT).
- **IBM Watson QA**: Uses deep lexicalized parsers to disambiguate attach points before retrieving answers.
- **SpaCy & Stanford CoreNLP**: Production parsers use **lexicalized PCFG + neural reranking** — the direct industrial descendant of the classical attachment models taught at KTU.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Lexical Association Score (LAS) for PP-Attachment

We derive the classical **Lexical Association Score** used in the Collins-Brooks (1995) lexicalized probability model for resolving the *"I saw the man with the telescope"* type of ambiguity.

### Step 1: Define the disambiguation problem

A sentence contains a verb $V$, a direct-object NP whose head is $N$, and a PP with preposition $P$. The PP can be attached to:
- The **NP** (low attachment, $H_L$)
- The **VP** (high attachment, $H_H$)

The decision rule is:

$$
H^* \;=\; \arg\max_{H \in \{H_L, H_H\}} P(H \mid V, N, P)
$$

### Step 2: Apply Bayes' Theorem

$$
P(H \mid V, N, P) \;=\; \frac{P(V, N, P \mid H)\, P(H)}{P(V, N, P)}
$$

The denominator $P(V, N, P)$ is constant with respect to $H$, so we can drop it for ranking purposes. We also keep only the prior $P(H)$ as a structural baseline:

$$
H^* \;=\; \arg\max_{H} P(V, N, P \mid H)\, P(H)
$$

### Step 3: Factor the likelihood using attachment-specific independence assumptions

For **NP-attachment**, the PP depends on the head noun $N$ (e.g., "telescope" modifies "man" — the *man's telescope*):

$$
P(V, N, P \mid H_L) \;=\; P(P \mid N)\, P(N \mid V)\, P(V)
$$

For **VP-attachment**, the PP depends on the verb $V$ (e.g., "with the telescope" is an instrument of the verb "saw"):

$$
P(V, N, P \mid H_H) \;=\; P(P \mid V)\, P(N \mid V)\, P(V)
$$

### Step 4: Compute the **Lexical Association Ratio** $\Lambda$

The ratio of the two likelihoods is:

$$
\Lambda \;=\; \frac{P(V, N, P \mid H_L)}{P(V, N, P \mid H_H)} \;=\; \frac{P(P \mid N)\, P(N \mid V)\, P(V)}{P(P \mid V)\, P(N \mid V)\, P(V)} \;=\; \frac{P(P \mid N)}{P(P \mid V)}
$$

The $P(N \mid V)$ and $P(V)$ cancel, leaving a beautifully simple form.

### Step 5: Take the log to obtain the **Lexical Association Score (LAS)**

$$
\boxed{\,A_{\text{Lex}}(V, N, P) \;=\; \log \Lambda \;=\; \log P(P \mid N) \;-\; \log P(P \mid V)\,}
$$

### Step 6: Estimate the probabilities from a treebank using **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)**

$$
P(P \mid N) \;=\; \frac{\text{count}(N, P)}{\sum_{P'} \text{count}(N, P')} \;=\; \frac{\text{count}(N, P)}{\text{count}(N)}
$$

$$
P(P \mid V) \;=\; \frac{\text{count}(V, P)}{\text{count}(V)}
$$

### Step 7: Apply the final decision rule

$$
\begin{aligned}
H^* \;=\; H_L &\iff A_{\text{Lex}}(V, N, P) > 0 \quad \text{(NP-attachment preferred)}\\[4pt]
H^* \;=\; H_H &\iff A_{\text{Lex}}(V, N, P) < 0 \quad \text{(VP-attachment preferred)}\\[4pt]
\text{Tie} &\iff A_{\text{Lex}}(V, N, P) = 0 \quad \text{(fall back to structural prior)}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — *"I saw the man with the telescope"*

Suppose a toy treebank (Penn Treebank sub-corpus) gives us these counts:

| Triple $(V, N, P)$ | NP-attach count | VP-attach count |
|---|---|---|
| *(saw, man, with)* | $200$ | $50$ |
| *(saw, man, total)* | $1000$ | $1000$ |

**Compute the MLE estimates:**

$$
P(\text{with} \mid \text{man}) \;=\; \frac{200}{1000} \;=\; 0.20
$$

$$
P(\text{with} \mid \text{saw}) \;=\; \frac{50}{1000} \;=\; 0.05
$$

**Compute the Lexical Association Score:**

$$
A_{\text{Lex}} \;=\; \log(0.20) - \log(0.05) \;=\; \log\!\left(\frac{0.20}{0.05}\right) \;=\; \log(4) \;\approx\; 1.386
$$

**Decision:**

$$
A_{\text{Lex}} = +1.386 > 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{H^* = H_L \;\text{(NP-attachment: the man had the telescope)}}
$$

## 3.3 Extension: Smoothing with Deleted Interpolation

Real treebanks are sparse. To handle unseen triples, we use **backoff**:

$$
P_{\text{backoff}}(P \mid N) \;=\; \lambda_1 \, \hat{P}(P \mid N) \;+\; \lambda_2 \, \hat{P}(P \mid \text{NP-class}) \;+\; \lambda_3 \, \hat{P}(P \mid \cdot)
$$

where $\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3 = 1$ and the lambdas are chosen to maximize held-out likelihood. The $\lambda_i$ values are learned via the **EM algorithm** on held-out data.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram A — The Two Competing Parse Trees for PP-Attachment Ambiguity

The classic sentence *"I saw the man with the telescope"* generates two legal parse trees, differing only in **where the PP attaches**.

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1["S"]:::root --> NP1a["NP: I"]:::np
    S1 --> VPa["VP"]:::vp
    VPa --> V1a["V: saw"]:::v
    VPa --> NP2a["NP: the man"]:::np
    VPa --> PPa["PP: with the telescope"]:::ppHigh

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef np fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef vp fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef v fill:#abebc6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef ppHigh fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef ppLow fill:#d7bde2,stroke:#000,color:#000

    S2["S"]:::root2 --> NP1b["NP: I"]:::np
    S2 --> VPb["VP"]:::vp
    VPb --> V1b["V: saw"]:::v
    VPb --> NP2b["NP: the man"]:::npLow
    NP2b --> PPb["PP: with the telescope"]:::ppLow

    classDef root2 fill:#7b241c,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef npLow fill:#d7bde2,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- **Tree 1 (Blue, High Attachment / VP-attach):** The PP *"with the telescope"* is a sibling of the object NP. Interpretation: *I used the telescope to see the man.*
- **Tree 2 (Red, Low Attachment / NP-attach):** The PP *"with the telescope"* is a child of the object NP *"the man"*. Interpretation: *The man (who had the telescope) was the one I saw.*

## 4.2 Diagram B — Resolution Pipeline for Attachment Ambiguity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input Sentence with ambiguous PP"]:::input --> B["Tokenize and POS-tag"]:::stage
    B --> C["Generate candidate parse trees from PCFG"]:::stage
    C --> D["Extract lexical heads: verb V, head noun N, preposition P"]:::stage
    D --> E["Query treebank for counts of V, N, P attachment patterns"]:::stage
    E --> F["Compute Lexical Association Score A_Lex"]:::compute
    F --> G{"A_Lex > 0?"}:::decision
    G -->|"Yes"| H["Choose NP-Attachment (Low)"]:::resultLow
    G -->|"No"| I["Choose VP-Attachment (High)"]:::resultHigh
    H --> J["Final disambiguated parse tree"]:::output
    I --> J

    classDef input fill:#3498db,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef stage fill:#85c1e9,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef compute fill:#f39c12,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef decision fill:#e67e22,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef resultLow fill:#27ae60,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef resultHigh fill:#c0392b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef output fill:#1abc9c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Diagram C — Type-of-Attachment Ambiguity Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Attachment Ambiguity"]:::root --> B["PP-Attachment"]:::type
    A --> C["VP-Attachment"]:::type
    A --> D["Coordination Ambiguity"]:::type
    A --> E["Relative Clause Attachment"]:::type
    B --> B1["Low: PP modifies object NP"]:::detail
    B --> B2["High: PP modifies entire VP"]:::detail
    C --> C1["Verb phrase scope vs. object"]:::detail
    D --> D1["e.g., 'old men and women'"]:::detail
    E --> E1["e.g., 'the book that I bought _ last week'"]:::detail
```
