<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define attachment ambiguity in natural language processing. Give one example sentence that exhibits PP-attachment ambiguity and explain both interpretations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Attachment ambiguity is a type of structural ambiguity that arises when a modifying phrase (commonly a prepositional phrase) can syntactically attach to more than one constituent in a sentence, producing multiple valid interpretations.

**Example:** *"I saw the man with the telescope."*

- **High (VP) Attachment:** The PP *"with the telescope"* modifies the verb *"saw"* — I used the telescope as an instrument to see the man.
- **Low (NP) Attachment:** The PP *"with the telescope"* modifies the NP *"the man"* — the man who possessed the telescope was the one I saw.

Since the surface form is identical, the parser must use lexical, semantic, or statistical cues to choose the correct attachment.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State Kimball's Right-Association Principle. How does it help in resolving attachment ambiguity in a PCFG parser?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Kimball's Right-Association Principle (1973):** *"When a new constituent is encountered, attach it to the most recent incomplete constituent in the parse tree."*

**Application to PCFG:** In a Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar trained on the Penn Treebank, this principle manifests as a default bias toward **low attachment** (PP attaches to the rightmost NP). The PCFG assigns higher probability to low-attachment derivations when no strong lexical evidence overrides the structural prior. Thus, for *"I ate the spaghetti with meatballs"*, the parser will (correctly) attach the PP to *spaghetti* rather than to the verb *ate* purely on structural grounds. The lexical association score (LAS) can override this default when verb-preposition statistics strongly favor VP-attachment.

---

## 📕 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Lexicalized Statistical Approach*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the four major types of attachment ambiguity in NLP with one example sentence for each type. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Lexical Association Score** $A_{\text{Lex}}(V, N, P)$ for PP-attachment ambiguity from first principles, clearly stating the underlying independence assumptions. Explain how the decision rule uses this score. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

The four major types of attachment ambiguity are:

| # | Type | Definition | Example Sentence | Two Interpretations |
|---|------|-----------|-----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **PP-Attachment** | A Prepositional Phrase can attach to multiple constituents | *"I saw the man with the telescope"* | (i) I used the telescope *(VP)*, (ii) the man had the telescope *(NP)* |
| 2 | **VP-Attachment** | A VP modifier can attach to either the main verb or the embedded clause | *"John promised to leave before noon"* | (i) Promise was made *before noon*, (ii) leaving happens *before noon* |
| 3 | **Coordination Ambiguity** | Coordinated phrases create scope ambiguity | *"I saw the man and the woman with the telescope"* | Does the *telescope* attach to one person, both, or to the *seeing event*? |
| 4 | **Relative Clause / Adjective Attachment** | Modifying clause/adj can attach to one of multiple NPs | *"I talked to the teacher of the students who were absent"* | RC *who were absent* modifies *students* or *teacher*? |

The key engineering challenge is that **all four types are pervasive in real English** and any robust NLP system (MT, IE, QA) must resolve them.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

We derive the Lexical Association Score step by step.

**Step 1: Set up the disambiguation.** For a triple (verb $V$, head noun $N$, preposition $P$), we want to pick:

$$H^* = \arg\max_{H \in \{H_L, H_H\}} P(H \mid V, N, P)$$

**Step 2: Apply Bayes' Theorem.** $P(H \mid V, N, P) = \dfrac{P(V, N, P \mid H)\, P(H)}{P(V, N, P)}$. Denominator is constant, so we maximize $P(V, N, P \mid H) P(H)$.

**Step 3: Likelihood factorization with independence assumptions.**

- For NP-attach: $P(V, N, P \mid H_L) = P(P \mid N)\, P(N \mid V)\, P(V)$ — preposition is conditioned only on the head noun.
- For VP-attach: $P(V, N, P \mid H_H) = P(P \mid V)\, P(N \mid V)\, P(V)$ — preposition is conditioned only on the verb.

**Step 4: Form the likelihood ratio.**

$$\Lambda = \frac{P(P \mid N)\, P(N \mid V)\, P(V)}{P(P \mid V)\, P(N \mid V)\, P(V)} = \frac{P(P \mid N)}{P(P \mid V)}$$

**Step 5: Take the logarithm to obtain the LAS.**

$$\boxed{A_{\text{Lex}}(V, N, P) = \log P(P \mid N) - \log P(P \mid V)}$$

**Step 6: MLE estimation from the treebank.**

$$P(P \mid N) = \frac{\text{count}(N, P)}{\text{count}(N)}, \qquad P(P \mid V) = \frac{\text{count}(V, P)}{\text{count}(V)}$$

**Step 7: Decision rule.**

- $A_{\text{Lex}} > 0$ → **NP-attachment** (low)
- $A_{\text{Lex}} < 0$ → **VP-attachment** (high)
- $A_{\text{Lex}} = 0$ → use **structural prior** (right-association defaults to low)

The independence assumptions are the model's chief weakness: real prepositions are influenced by both noun and verb. This motivates the **Maximum-Entropy / log-linear** extensions (Ratnaparkhi 1998) that combine multiple overlapping features.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Hybrid and Neural Approaches*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 3]**

**(a)** Discuss the limitations of purely **structural (PCFG-based)** disambiguation of PP-attachment ambiguity. Why are lexicalized and statistical models superior? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Maximum-Entropy** approach to PP-attachment (Ratnaparkhi, 1998). Explain how feature functions $f_i$ and weights $\lambda_i$ are combined, and discuss one advantage and one limitation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Limitations of purely structural (PCFG) disambiguation:**

1. **Context-freeness loses lexical sensitivity:** A PCFG conditions each rule expansion only on the non-terminal symbol (e.g., $P(\text{PP} \rightarrow \text{P NP})$). It cannot distinguish that *"with"* in *"see with"* is an instrument but *"with"* in *"man with hat"* is a possession — both expansions have the same probability under a vanilla PCFG.
2. **No semantic world-knowledge:** PCFGs do not encode the fact that *a telescope* is a seeing-instrument but rarely an attribute of a person. Thus, the high-attachment reading gets no boost from commonsense.
3. **Poor disambiguation on rare constructions:** When test data diverges from training distribution, the PCFG defaults to a flat structural prior — usually **low attachment** — which is wrong ~25–30% of the time.
4. **Identical local trees:** The local sub-tree $P(\text{NP} \rightarrow \text{DT NN PP})$ has the same probability whether the PP is the man's modifier or the VP's modifier; the parser cannot differentiate.

**Why lexicalized / statistical models are superior:**

- **Lexicalized PCFGs (Collins 1999, Charniak 2000)** condition rules on head-word pairs, capturing strong lexical preferences (e.g., *attach with* strongly prefers VP).
- **Discriminative models (MaxEnt, SVMs)** directly optimize for attachment correctness rather than likelihood of the whole tree.
- **Neural models (BERT, mBERT)** encode contextual semantics in dense vectors, capturing selectional preferences implicitly.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Maximum-Entropy (MaxEnt) Model for PP-Attachment (Ratnaparkhi 1998):**

The model predicts the probability of a particular attachment hypothesis $H$ given a context $x = (V, N, P, \text{features})$ as:

$$P(H \mid x) = \frac{1}{Z(x)} \exp\!\left( \sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i f_i(x, H) \right)$$

where:
- $f_i(x, H)$ is a **binary feature function** that activates when a specific lexical or structural condition holds (e.g., $f_1(V, N, P, H) = 1$ if $P = \text{``with''}$ and $H = H_L$).
- $\lambda_i \in \mathbb{R}$ is the **weight** learned to maximize the conditional log-likelihood on the training treebank.
- $Z(x) = \sum_{H'} \exp\!\left( \sum_i \lambda_i f_i(x, H') \right)$ is the **partition function** that normalizes the distribution.

**Training:** The weights $\lambda_i$ are tuned using **Generalized Iterative Scaling (GIS)** or **L-BFGS** to maximize:

$$\mathcal{L}(\lambda) = \sum_{(x, H^*) \in \text{Train}} \log P(H^* \mid x) - C \sum_i \lambda_i^2$$

The second term is **L2 regularization** (Gaussian prior) to prevent overfitting.

**Advantage:** The model can integrate **arbitrarily overlapping features** — lexical, structural, distance-based — without independence assumptions. It improves over the basic LAS by ~4–6% accuracy.

**Limitation:** Requires extensive **feature engineering** by hand; the model's performance is bounded by the quality of the manually designed features. This limitation motivated the later shift to **neural end-to-end** parsers.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes in KTU valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting the independence assumption** in the LAS derivation — KTU examiners deduct **2 marks** if you write $\Lambda = P(P \mid N, V) / P(P \mid V, N)$ without justifying the conditional independence.
> 2. **Confusing low and high attachment** — *Low* = PP attaches to the **NP** (rightmost object); *High* = PP attaches to the **VP**. Many students invert these and lose the example-mark.
> 3. **Skipping the MLE step** in numerical problems — Always show $P(P \mid N) = \text{count}(N, P) / \text{count}(N)$ explicitly.
> 4. **Citing non-existent authors** — Do not fabricate references. Stick to verified names: Kimball (1973), Collins & Brooks (1995), Ratnaparkhi (1998), Collins (1999).
> 5. **Ignoring Kimball's principle** — In structural-preference questions, always mention **Right-Association** as the structural default.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Attachment Ambiguity (Module 3)**

- ✅ **Definition:** Syntactic ambiguity where one modifying phrase can attach to multiple constituents.
- ✅ **Most common type:** **PP-attachment** (canonical case in KTU exams).
- ✅ **Two attachment sites:** **Low (NP)** vs **High (VP)**.
- ✅ **Kimball's Right-Association Principle (1973):** Default = **low attachment**.
- ✅ **Lexical Association Score (LAS):** $A_{\text{Lex}} = \log P(P \mid N) - \log P(P \mid V)$.
  - $> 0$ → Low (NP)
  - $< 0$ → High (VP)
- ✅ **Estimation:** MLE from Penn Treebank counts: $P(P \mid N) = \text{count}(N, P) / \text{count}(N)$.
- ✅ **MaxEnt extension (Ratnaparkhi 1998):** $P(H \mid x) = \dfrac{1}{Z(x)} \exp(\sum_i \lambda_i f_i(x, H))$.
- ✅ **Smoothing:** Backoff via deleted interpolation $\hat{P} = \lambda_1 P_1 + \lambda_2 P_2 + \lambda_3 P_3$, $\sum \lambda_i = 1$.
- ✅ **Four types:** PP-Attachment, VP-Attachment, Coordination, Relative-Clause/Adjective Attachment.
- ✅ **Resolution hierarchy:** Structural prior → Lexical statistics → Semantic selectional restrictions → Neural contextual embeddings.
- ✅ **Real-world impact:** Machine Translation, QA, IE, Sentiment, SRL all depend on correct attachment.
- ✅ **Key papers to remember:** Kimball (1973), Collins & Brooks (1995), Ratnaparkhi (1998), Collins (1999).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PP Attachment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** PP Attachment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PP Attachment (Prepositional Phrase Attachment)** is the classic structural disambiguation problem in computational linguistics and syntactic parsing where a prepositional phrase (PP) appearing after a verb phrase (VP) or noun phrase (NP) must be syntactically attached to either the **verb** (VP-attachment) or the **head noun** (NP-attachment) of the preceding constituent.

In formal terms, given a four-word sequence $\langle v, n, p, n_2 \rangle$ consisting of a verb $v$, a head noun $n$, a preposition $p$, and a second noun $n_2$, the task is to determine whether the PP $[p, n_2]$ modifies $v$ (verb attachment) or $n$ (noun attachment).

$$ \text{Argmax}_{a \in \{V, N\}} P(a \mid v, n, p, n_2) $$

where $a$ is the attachment decision, $V$ denotes verb attachment, and $N$ denotes noun attachment.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine reading the sentence *"I ate the spaghetti with meatballs."* Now think about it carefully:
> - Did you eat the spaghetti **using meatballs** (verb attachment — meatballs were the utensil, which is unusual)?
> - Or did you eat the spaghetti **that had meatballs in/on it** (noun attachment — meatballs were the ingredient)?
>
> Your brain, trained on language, instantly prefers the second interpretation. The **PP Attachment problem** asks a computer to do exactly this same disambiguation automatically using statistical or learned patterns.

## 1.3 Why PP Attachment is Hard

> [!WARNING]
> PP Attachment is famously cited as the **"classical example of a moderate attachment ambiguity"** (Church & Patil, 1982). A binary PP/V/N choice yields 5 possible attachment positions per PP, and 4 PPs in a sentence yield **5,184 parse trees** — most of which are linguistically valid structurally but only one is semantically correct.

The two most common attachment decisions are:

| Attachment Type | Syntactic Parent | Example Sentence |
|---|---|---|
| **V-attachment (VP)** | Verb | "I saw the man **with the telescope**." (telescope = instrument) |
| **N-attachment (NP)** | Head Noun | "I saw the man **with the beard**." (beard = property of man) |
| **P-attachment** | Preposition (rare) | Local ambiguities in some dialects |

## 1.4 Standard Metrics

- **Accuracy (Acc)** — percentage of correctly attached PPs.
- **Baseline (Always-N / Always-V)** — the trivial majority-class predictor.
- **Inter-annotator agreement (Kappa)** — typically **0.88–0.94** for linguists on this task, indicating it is a tractable linguistic problem.
- **Human ceiling** — approximately **88%** on WSJ text (Ratnaparkhi, 1998).
- **Brown corpus frequency** — approximately **60% N-attachment / 30% V-attachment / 10% other** in newspaper text.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Ambiguity

A sentence with $k$ PPs produces an exponentially large number of candidate parse trees due to **structural ambiguity**. For each PP, the attachment site can be one of 5 positions relative to the prior NP/VP. With $k$ independent PPs:

$$ \text{Number of trees} = 5^k $$

For a 4-PP sentence, this gives $5^4 = 625$ candidate structures per binary split, and the full unconstrained count is in the **thousands**.

## 2.2 Decision-Theoretic Formulation

The optimal attachment is found by **Bayesian classification**:

$$ \hat{a} = \underset{a \in \{V, N\}}{\text{argmax}} \; P(a \mid v, n, p, n_2) $$

Applying Bayes' rule:

$$ \hat{a} = \underset{a}{\text{argmax}} \; \frac{P(v, n, p, n_2 \mid a) \cdot P(a)}{P(v, n, p, n_2)} $$

Since the denominator is constant across $a$, this reduces to:

$$ \hat{a} = \underset{a}{\text{argmax}} \; P(v, n, p, n_2 \mid a) \cdot P(a) $$

## 2.3 Approaches to PP Attachment (Why & How)

### 2.3.1 Lexical Association Approach (Hindle & Rooth, 1993)

**Why:** Surface lexical co-occurrence between the verb/preposition or noun/preposition pair is highly predictive of attachment.

**How:** Compute the **lexical association score** $L(w_1, p)$ between the preposition $p$ and the candidate head word $w_1 \in \{v, n\}$.

### 2.3.2 Ratnaparkhi's Backoff Model (1998)

**Why:** Sparsity of the four-way joint $(v, n, p, n_2)$ makes direct estimation infeasible. We back off to lower-order n-gram statistics.

**How:** A maximum entropy model with backoff features.

### 2.3.3 Supervised ML (Decision Trees, SVM, Neural)

**Why:** To learn non-linear interactions among features.

**How:** Train on labeled Penn Treebank data using lexical, POS, and dependency features.

### 2.3.4 Unsupervised (Lau et al., 1993; Li & Araujo, 2009)

**Why:** Avoid dependence on costly hand-labeled treebanks.

**How:** Use distributional similarity, Web counts, or graph-based heuristics.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\hat{a} = \arg\max_{a} P(a \mid v, n, p, n_2)$ | Bayesian decision rule |
| 2 | $L(w_1, p) = \log \dfrac{P(p \mid w_1)}{P(p)}$ | Lexical association (Hindle & Rooth) |
| 3 | $P(p \mid w_1) = \dfrac{\text{count}(w_1, p)}{\sum_p \text{count}(w_1, p)}$ | Conditional probability of prep given head |
| 4 | $\text{Score}_V = L(v, p) - L(n, p)$ | Verbs score: positive → V-attachment |
| 5 | $P_{\text{backoff}}(v,n,p,n_2 \mid a) \approx P(p, n_2 \mid v, a) \cdot P(v \mid a)$ | Ratnaparkhi backoff |
| 6 | $\text{Acc} = \dfrac{\text{TP} + \text{TN}}{N}$ | Classification accuracy |
| 7 | $\kappa = \dfrac{P_o - P_e}{1 - P_e}$ | Cohen's inter-annotator Kappa |
| 8 | $\text{MI}(w, p) = \log \dfrac{P(w, p)}{P(w) P(p)}$ | Pointwise Mutual Information |
| 9 | $5^k$ | Number of trees for $k$ PPs (binary split) |
| 10 | $\text{Baseline} = \max(\%V, \%N)$ | Majority-class baseline |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** PP Attachment resolution is the cornerstone of high-precision **Information Extraction (IE)**, **Machine Translation (MT) alignment**, **Question Answering (QA)** systems, and **Syntactic Parsers** (especially in the head-driven statistical parsers like Collins Parser). Modern transformer-based parsers (BERT, RoBERTa) implicitly solve PP attachment as part of constituency or dependency parsing with accuracies exceeding **96%** on WSJ Section 23.

## 2.5 Collins & Brooks (1995) Heuristic Rules

A set of simple linguistically-motivated rules that achieved **~84%** accuracy on the WSJ:

1. If a preposition is selected by the verb (verb subcategorizes it) → **V-attach**.
2. Else if the noun is animate and semantically compatible → **N-attach**.
3. Else → default to **V-attach**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hindle-Rooth Lexical Association Score

**Goal:** Derive the lexical association score for a four-tuple $\langle v, n, p, n_2 \rangle$ and show that its sign determines attachment.

### Step 1 — Set up the classification

We choose V-attachment iff:

$$ P(V \mid v, n, p, n_2) > P(N \mid v, n, p, n_2) $$

### Step 2 — Apply Bayes' rule (omit evidence term)

$$ P(v, n, p, n_2 \mid V) \cdot P(V) \; > \; P(v, n, p, n_2 \mid N) \cdot P(N) $$

### Step 3 — Approximate via conditional independence (naive assumption)

Assume the head word $w_1 \in \{v, n\}$ determines $p$:

$$ P(p, n_2 \mid w_1, a) \approx P(p \mid w_1) \cdot P(n_2) $$

The model becomes:

$$ \hat{a} = \underset{a}{\text{argmax}} \; P(p \mid w_1) \cdot P(a) $$

### Step 4 — Take the log-likelihood ratio between V and N

$$ \text{Score}(V) = \log \frac{P(p \mid v) \cdot P(V)}{P(p \mid n) \cdot P(N)} $$

This is the **log-odds ratio**. It separates into:

$$ \text{Score}(V) = \underbrace{\log \frac{P(p \mid v)}{P(p \mid n)}}_{\text{lexical preference}} + \underbrace{\log \frac{P(V)}{P(N)}}_{\text{prior}} $$

### Step 5 — Substitute the empirical MLE estimator

$$ \hat{P}(p \mid w) = \frac{\text{count}(w, p)}{\text{count}(w)} $$

### Step 6 — Final decision rule

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Score}_V \; &= \; \log P(p \mid v) - \log P(p \mid n) + \log P(V) - \log P(N) \\
\text{If } \text{Score}_V &> 0 \;\Rightarrow\; V\text{-attachment} \\
\text{Else} \;&\Rightarrow\; N\text{-attachment}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (Hindle & Rooth, 1993):**
> Sentence: *"I saw the man with the telescope."*
> - $v = \text{saw}$, $n = \text{man}$, $p = \text{with}$, $n_2 = \text{telescope}$
> - Empirically, $\hat{P}(\text{with} \mid \text{saw}) > \hat{P}(\text{with} \mid \text{man})$ because "with" frequently appears as an instrument for "saw".
> - However, semantic plausibility of the noun-pair *(man, telescope)* also pulls toward N-attachment.
> - In their 1993 ACL paper, Hindle & Rooth reported an **accuracy of ~80.5%** using this method — surpassing the always-N baseline of ~58%.

## 3.2 Derivation: Ratnaparkhi's Backoff Smoothing (1998)

### Step 1 — Joint probability with backoff chain

The full joint $P(v, n, p, n_2 \mid a)$ is decomposed as:

$$ P(v, n, p, n_2 \mid a) = P(v \mid a) \cdot P(n \mid v, a) \cdot P(p \mid v, n, a) \cdot P(n_2 \mid v, n, p, a) $$

### Step 2 — Backoff when counts are zero

For each factor, define a backoff estimator:

$$ P_{\text{BO}}(x \mid y_1, y_2) = \frac{c(x, y_1, y_2)}{c(y_1, y_2) + \alpha} + \frac{\alpha}{c(y_1, y_2) + \alpha} \cdot P_{\text{BO}}(x \mid y_1) $$

where $\alpha$ is a smoothing constant (e.g., $\alpha = 1$ for add-one, or optimized via held-out data).

### Step 3 — Final probability estimate

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(v, n, p, n_2 \mid a) &\;\approx\; P_{\text{BO}}(v \mid a) \cdot P_{\text{BO}}(n \mid v, a) \\
&\quad \cdot P_{\text{BO}}(p \mid v, n, a) \cdot P_{\text{BO}}(n_2 \mid v, n, p, a)
\end{aligned}
$$

Ratnaparkhi's ME-based model reached **~84.1%** accuracy — within **4%** of the human inter-annotator ceiling.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Method | Year | Features Used | Accuracy (WSJ) | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Always-N Baseline | — | None | ~58% | Trivial | Inadequate |
| Hindle & Rooth (Lexical Assoc.) | 1993 | $P(p \mid w)$ | ~80.5% | Simple, fast | Sparse, no semantics |
| Collins & Brooks (Rules) | 1995 | Subcat, animacy | ~84.0% | Linguistic | Hand-crafted |
| Ratnaparkhi (MaxEnt + Backoff) | 1998 | Lexical, POS, distance | ~84.1% | Robust | Needs treebank |
| Stetina & Nagao (Corpus) | 1997 | 4-tuple counts | ~82.5% | Empirical | Sparsity |
| Pantel & Lin (Web counts) | 2000 | Web n-grams | ~84.4% | No treebank | Web noise |
| Li & Araujo (Graph) | 2009 | Random walks | ~86.6% | Unsupervised | Slower |
| BERT-based Parsers | 2019+ | Contextual embeddings | **~96%+** | State-of-the-art | Heavy compute |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PP Attachment Ambiguity — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input Four Tuple: verb v, noun n, preposition p, noun n2"] --> B["Syntactic Feature Extractor"]
    B --> B1["Lexical Association P(p or v)"]
    B --> B2["POS Tags: VBZ, NN, IN, NN"]
    B --> B3["Distance and Context Window"]
    B --> B4["Subcategorization Frame"]
    B1 --> C["Feature Vector Builder"]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    C --> D{"Classifier Core"}
    D --> D1["Bayesian Model"]
    D --> D2["MaxEnt Logistic Model"]
    D --> D3["Neural Transformer Encoder"]
    D1 --> E{"Attachment Decision"}
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F["V-Attachment: PP modifies Verb"]
    E --> G["N-Attachment: PP modifies Noun"]
    F --> H["Output Parsed Tree Structure"]
    G --> H
    H --> I["Evaluation: Accuracy versus Gold Treebank"]
```

## 4.2 Two Parse Trees for the Same Sentence

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1["Sentence: I saw the man with the telescope"] --> V1["VP: V saw"]
    S1 --> NP1["NP: the man"]
    V1 --> PP1["PP: with the telescope"]
    PP1 --> N1a["NP: the telescope"]
    S2["Sentence: I saw the man with the telescope"] --> V2["VP: V saw"]
    S2 --> NP2["NP: the man"]
    NP2 --> PP2["PP: with the telescope"]
    PP2 --> N2a["NP: the telescope"]
    S1:::vattach
    S2:::nattach
    classDef vattach fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1,stroke-width:2px
    classDef nattach fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px
```

**Interpretation of diagram:**
- The **left tree** (blue) shows **V-attachment** — the prepositional phrase is a child of the verb phrase. Semantically: the *telescope* was the instrument used to *see*.
- The **right tree** (amber) shows **N-attachment** — the PP is a child of the noun phrase. Semantically: the *man* possessed/had the *telescope*.

Both trees are **syntactically valid** under standard CFG rules. A disambiguator must use **statistical or learned signals** to pick the correct one.

## 4.3 Lexical Association Decision Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Preposition p: with"] --> B["Compute P(p or v)"]
    A --> C["Compute P(p or n)"]
    B --> D["Log Likelihood Ratio"]
    C --> D
    D --> E{"Sign of Ratio"}
    E -->|Positive| F["Choose V-Attachment"]
    E -->|Negative| G["Choose N-Attachment"]
    E -->|Zero| H["Apply Default Prior P(V) and P(N)"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define PP Attachment ambiguity. Give one example sentence that illustrates it. `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

PP Attachment ambiguity is the structural disambiguation problem in natural language processing where a prepositional phrase can syntactically modify either a verb or a head noun in a sentence, producing two or more valid parse trees.

**Example:** *"He saw the girl **with the camera**."* — Here, the PP *with the camera* can either attach to the verb *saw* (camera is the instrument of seeing) or to the noun *girl* (girl possesses the camera).

**(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for syntactic explanation + 1 mark for correct example with two valid attachments).**

---

### Q2. State the Bayes' decision rule for PP Attachment and define each term. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Bayes' decision rule for PP Attachment is:

$$ \hat{a} = \underset{a \in \{V, N\}}{\text{argmax}} \; P(a \mid v, n, p, n_2) $$

where:
- $\hat{a}$ — the chosen attachment (V for verb, N for noun).
- $v, n, p, n_2$ — the verb, head noun, preposition, and second noun of the four-tuple.
- $P(a \mid v, n, p, n_2)$ — the posterior probability of attachment $a$ given the four-tuple.

**(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for domain of $a$, 1 mark for variable definitions).**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE) Part B questions carry **14 marks each** and require internal choice. The two questions below are **independent alternatives** — the student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the Hindle & Rooth (1993) lexical association method for resolving PP Attachment ambiguity. Derive the log-odds decision rule and show how the sign of the score determines the attachment.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **Background (1 mark):** Hindle & Rooth proposed that the relative frequency with which a preposition $p$ attaches to a verb $v$ versus a noun $n$ can be used as a strong disambiguation signal. They observed that certain prepositions have strong lexical preferences (e.g., *with* strongly attaches to nouns like *telescope*, *agreement*).

2. **Setup (1 mark):** Given $\langle v, n, p, n_2 \rangle$, define the decision rule as:

$$ \hat{a} = \arg\max_{a} P(a \mid v, n, p, n_2) $$

3. **Bayes' transformation (1 mark):** Applying Bayes' rule and removing the constant evidence term:

$$ \hat{a} = \arg\max_{a} P(v, n, p, n_2 \mid a) \cdot P(a) $$

4. **Independence assumption (1 mark):** Approximating $P(p, n_2 \mid w_1, a) \approx P(p \mid w_1) \cdot P(n_2)$, where $w_1$ is the candidate head.

5. **Log-odds derivation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Score}_V \; &= \; \log P(p \mid v) - \log P(p \mid n) + \log P(V) - \log P(N) \\
\text{If } \text{Score}_V &> 0 \;\Rightarrow\; V\text{-attach} \\
\text{Else} \;&\Rightarrow\; N\text{-attach}
\end{aligned}
$$

6. **Conclusion (1 mark):** Hindle & Rooth achieved **80.5%** accuracy on WSJ Section 23, substantially above the **58%** always-N baseline.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Apply the Hindle & Rooth method to the sentence: *"She booked the flight from Chicago."* Compute the lexical association score assuming the following corpus counts: count(*booked, from*) = 12, count(*booked*) = 50, count(*flight, from*) = 220, count(*flight*) = 400, P(V) = 0.30, P(N) = 0.70.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **Identify the four-tuple (1 mark):** $v = \text{booked}$, $n = \text{flight}$, $p = \text{from}$, $n_2 = \text{Chicago}$.

2. **Compute conditional probabilities (2 marks):**

$$ P(\text{from} \mid \text{booked}) = \frac{12}{50} = 0.24 $$

$$ P(\text{from} \mid \text{flight}) = \frac{220}{400} = 0.55 $$

3. **Compute the log-odds (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Score}_V &= \log(0.24) - \log(0.55) + \log(0.30) - \log(0.70) \\
&= (-1.427) - (-0.598) + (-1.204) - (-0.357) \\
&= -1.427 + 0.598 - 1.204 + 0.357 \\
&= -1.676
\end{aligned}
$$

4. **Interpretation (2 marks):** Since $\text{Score}_V < 0$, the method predicts **N-attachment** — i.e., the PP *from Chicago* modifies the noun *flight*, which is the correct linguistic reading (*the flight that originates from Chicago*).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the prior term** $\log P(V) - \log P(N)$ — students often drop the prior and only compute the lexical ratio. **Lose 2 marks** for this omission.
> 2. **Sign errors in log computation** — make sure to use $\log_{10}$ or $\log_e$ **consistently**; do not mix.
> 3. **Confusing log-odds direction** — positive score means V-attachment, negative means N-attachment. Reversing this is a common error.
> 4. **Forgetting to apply smoothing** when counts are zero in unseen test pairs. Use add-1 or Good-Turing smoothing in such cases.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the Ratnaparkhi (1998) backoff model for PP attachment. Explain why backoff smoothing is needed and write the smoothing formula.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **Motivation (2 marks):** Direct estimation of the four-way joint $P(v, n, p, n_2 \mid a)$ is infeasible because most four-tuples never appear in the training corpus. The maximum entropy framework combined with backoff smoothing addresses this data sparsity problem.

2. **Joint decomposition (2 marks):**

$$ P(v, n, p, n_2 \mid a) = P(v \mid a) \cdot P(n \mid v, a) \cdot P(p \mid v, n, a) \cdot P(n_2 \mid v, n, p, a) $$

3. **Backoff formula (2 marks):**

$$ P_{\text{BO}}(x \mid y_1, y_2) = \frac{c(x, y_1, y_2)}{c(y_1, y_2) + \alpha} + \frac{\alpha}{c(y_1, y_2) + \alpha} \cdot P_{\text{BO}}(x \mid y_1) $$

4. **Result (1 mark):** Ratnaparkhi's system achieved **84.1%** accuracy on WSJ Section 23, within **~4%** of the human ceiling — establishing PP attachment as a **near-solved** problem for English.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compare three different approaches (lexical association, supervised ML, and neural/transformer-based) for PP Attachment resolution. Tabulate your comparison across accuracy, data requirement, and feature engineering effort.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **Lexical Association (Hindle & Rooth) (2 marks):** Achieves ~80.5% accuracy using only preposition-word co-occurrence counts. Requires no labeled treebank. Minimal feature engineering — only conditional probability computation.

2. **Supervised ML (Ratnaparkhi MaxEnt) (2 marks):** Achieves ~84.1% accuracy. Requires a hand-labeled treebank (e.g., Penn Treebank). Moderate feature engineering — designing lexical, POS, and distance features.

3. **Neural / Transformer-based (BERT-style) (2 marks):** Achieves >96% accuracy. Requires pre-training on massive unlabeled corpora plus fine-tuning on treebank. Minimal manual feature engineering — embeddings are learned.

4. **Summary table (1 mark):**

| Method | Accuracy | Data | Feature Effort |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lexical Assoc. | 80.5% | Unlabeled counts | Low |
| Supervised MaxEnt | 84.1% | Treebank | Medium |
| Neural Transformer | >96% | Pre-train + treebank | Low (learned) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Conflating "training data" with "feature engineering"** — neural methods still need data, but reduce manual effort. State both separately.
> 2. **Omitting numbers** — examiners reward concrete accuracy values. Always cite the year and dataset (e.g., WSJ §23).
> 3. **Not mentioning the human ceiling (~88%)** — without this anchor, you cannot judge whether 84% is good or not.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — PP Attachment (Module 3)**

- **Definition** — PP attachment is the resolution of structural ambiguity when a PP modifies a verb (V-attach) or a noun (N-attach).
- **Standard 4-tuple** — $\langle v, n, p, n_2 \rangle$ where the disambiguator must classify whether the PP $[p, n_2]$ attaches to $v$ or $n$.
- **Bayes' decision rule** — $\hat{a} = \arg\max_{a} P(a \mid v, n, p, n_2)$ with prior $P(V) \approx 0.30$ and $P(N) \approx 0.70$ in newspaper text.
- **Lexical Association (Hindle & Rooth, 1993)** — uses $P(p \mid w)$ to score V- vs N-attach; reaches **80.5%** on WSJ.
- **Log-odds score** — $\text{Score}_V = \log P(p \mid v) - \log P(p \mid n) + \log P(V) - \log P(N)$; **positive ⇒ V, negative ⇒ N**.
- **Backoff smoothing (Ratnaparkhi, 1998)** — decomposes the joint into a chain and applies backoff when counts are zero; reaches **84.1%**.
- **Rule-based (Collins & Brooks, 1995)** — subcategorization + animacy heuristic; reaches **~84%**.
- **Unsupervised (Li & Araujo, 2009)** — graph-based random walks; **~86.6%**.
- **Neural (BERT-era)** — implicit in transformer parsers; **>96%**.
- **Human ceiling** — approximately **88%** on WSJ; inter-annotator Kappa ≈ **0.88–0.94**.
- **Combinatorial explosion** — $5^k$ parses for $k$ PPs → exponential blow-up; motivates heuristic disambiguation.
- **Engineering relevance** — critical for parsers, IE, MT, and QA pipelines; modern systems solve it implicitly via end-to-end neural models.
- **Key exam trick** — always show the **sign of the log-odds** and link it to the attachment decision; never just compute probabilities without the decision step.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selectional Preferences
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Selectional Preferences

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selectional Preferences** are the statistical or rule-based tendencies of a predicate (typically a verb, adjective, or noun) to occur with arguments (subjects and objects) drawn from particular **semantic classes**. Formally introduced by **Katz and Fodor (1963)** as *Selectional Restrictions* and later formalized statistically by **Resnik (1996)**, they capture the *semantic compatibility* between a head word and its dependents, independent of strict syntactic constraints.

In formal terms, a predicate $p$ has a selectional preference over a semantic class $C$ when the conditional probability distribution $P(C \mid p)$ is non-uniform across all available classes. A verb like *eat* strongly prefers arguments belonging to the class *\{food, meal, fruit, bread\}* rather than *\{rock, idea, music\}*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction to Remember (KTU Syllabus Highlight):**
> - **Selectional Restrictions** = hard, logical constraints (binary: allowed/forbidden), symbolic AI era.
> - **Selectional Preferences** = soft, probabilistic tendencies (graded preference), statistical NLP era.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a job recruiter for a company. The recruiter does not say "we will reject any candidate with a music degree." Instead, the recruiter looks at past successful hires and notices a **strong tendency** to hire engineers and data scientists. Occasionally, a music graduate with exceptional coding skills might still be hired.

> Selectional Preferences work exactly like this **biased recruiter**:
> - The **verb** = the recruiter (the *predicate*).
> - The **semantic class of the argument noun** = the candidate's background.
> - The **historical co-occurrence statistics** = the recruiter's past hiring data.
> - A high preference score means the class is a "favored" candidate; a low score means the class is statistically unusual but not impossible.

**Key Engineering Metrics (KTU Standard Definitions):**

- **Predicate ($p$):** A content word (verb, adjective, noun) that licenses arguments (e.g., *eat, devour, destroy*).
- **Argument ($a$):** A dependent word (typically a noun phrase) that fills a predicate's thematic role (e.g., subject, object).
- **Semantic Class ($C$):** A cluster of nouns sharing a conceptual category, typically derived from a **lexical hierarchy** such as *WordNet* or *VerbNet* (e.g., the hypernym *\{food, nutrient\}*).
- **Selectional Association Strength:** A scalar numerical score quantifying how strongly a class is preferred by a predicate. Standardized into the unit **nats** when natural logarithm is used, or **bits** when base-2 logarithm is used.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Selectional Preference Models

Computational Linguistics recognizes three principal families of models, each answering the question *"How strongly does predicate $p$ prefer argument $a$?"* differently.

#### A. Class-Based Models (Resnik, 1996)
The most cited approach. Arguments are first generalized to their **semantic class** via a hierarchy (WordNet), and the preference strength is computed between the predicate and the class as a whole.

- **Step 1 — Frequency Collection:** Count the occurrences of each noun argument $a$ in the corpus with predicate $p$, giving count $n(p, a)$.
- **Step 2 — Class Aggregation:** Map each noun $a$ to its WordNet synset and ascend to the most informative ancestor synset $c \in C$. Aggregate counts to obtain $n(p, c)$.
- **Step 3 — Probability Estimation:** Compute $P(c \mid p) = \frac{n(p, c)}{n(p)}$, the probability that a randomly chosen argument of $p$ belongs to class $c$.
- **Step 4 — Divergence Measurement:** Compare $P(c \mid p)$ against the *prior* $P(c)$ using **Kullback–Leibler (KL) Divergence**, yielding the **Selectional Association Strength** $S_A$.

#### B. Probabilistic Class-Based Models (Li & Abe, 1998)
Generalizes Resnik by treating the class as a *latent variable* and using **Expectation–Maximization (EM)** to learn the probability $P(c \mid p, a)$ when class membership is uncertain. This avoids the hard-decision problem of choosing a single WordNet sense.

#### C. N-Best / Direct Probabilistic Models
Skips semantic classes entirely. Defines a *preference value* directly over word pairs, typically using smoothed maximum likelihood estimation or log-likelihood ratios. Cheaper to compute, but loses the generalization power of class-based methods.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Why It Matters in NLP |
|---|---|
| Class Aggregation | Enables **generalization**: a model never seeing "eat sushi" can still infer that "eat" prefers food, because "sushi" is a member of the food class. |
| KL Divergence | Captures **divergence from chance**: a class that is frequent for *all* verbs (e.g., "thing") gets a *low* association, because the divergence is small. This is the genius of Resnik's formulation. |
| EM-based Class Assignment | Handles **lexical ambiguity**: "bank" may be a financial institution or a river edge; the model assigns fractional probability to both classes. |
| Smoothing (e.g., Lidstone, Good–Turing) | Prevents **zero-probability** events for unseen noun–verb pairs in finite corpora. |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **board-exam essential** for Module 3 of PEADT632.

| Symbol | Formula | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Prior of a class | $P(c) = \frac{\sum_p n(p, c)}{N}$ | Background frequency of class $c$ in the entire corpus | dimensionless |
| Conditional of class given predicate | $P(c \mid p) = \frac{n(p, c)}{\sum_{c'} n(p, c')}$ | How often predicate $p$ takes an argument of class $c$ | dimensionless |
| **Resnik's Selectional Association** | $S_A(p, c) = KL\bigl(P(C \mid p) \;\Vert\; P(C)\bigr) = \sum_c P(c \mid p) \log \frac{P(c \mid p)}{P(c)}$ | Divergence between observed argument distribution and background distribution | **nats** (if ln) or **bits** (if $\log_2$) |
| Per-class association (informative ancestor selection) | $S_A(p, c) = P(c \mid p) \log \frac{P(c \mid p)}{P(c)}$ | Used to pick the *most informative* class for predicate $p$ | nats or bits |
| Bayesian decomposition | $P(c \mid p, a) = \frac{P(c \mid p)\, P(a \mid p, c)}{P(a \mid p)}$ | Posterior class probability given predicate and argument | dimensionless |
| Direct probability (N-Best) | $S_{NB}(p, a) = P(a \mid p) = \frac{n(p, a)}{n(p)}$ | Preference of verb $p$ for noun $a$ (no class layer) | dimensionless |
| Lidstone smoothing | $P_{\lambda}(a \mid p) = \frac{n(p, a) + \lambda}{n(p) + \lambda \cdot \vert V \vert}$ | Prevents zero probabilities ($\lambda \in [0,1]$, typically $\lambda = 0.5$ or 1) | dimensionless |

**Boundary Conditions / Validity Constraints:**

- All probability distributions **must** sum to **1** over their sample space.
- KL Divergence is **asymmetric**: $KL(P \Vert Q) \neq KL(Q \Vert P)$ — *not* a true distance metric.
- $S_A \geq 0$ always, with equality iff $P(C \mid p) = P(C)$ (predicate shows no preference).
- Smoothing constant $\lambda$ is **non-negative**, typically chosen via held-out cross-validation.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD):** Strong selectional preferences disambiguate "He **banked** the money" (financial class) vs. "He **banked** the plane" (motion/aviation class).
- **Semantic Role Labeling (SRL):** Identifying the *patient* or *instrument* of a verb is impossible without knowing which noun classes the verb accepts.
- **Paraphrase Generation:** Verbs with identical selectional preference profiles are interchangeable in most contexts (e.g., *devour* and *consume* both strongly prefer *food*).
- **Anomaly Detection in Text:** Sentences violating preferences ("The rock devoured the sandwich") are flagged for downstream tasks such as metaphor detection, fact verification, or hallucination detection in LLM outputs.
- **Machine Translation Quality Estimation:** Penalizing translations that destroy selectional preferences helps align verb–object structures across languages.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Resnik's Selectional Association Strength (1996)

We begin with the **principle of distributional similarity**: a predicate $p$'s argument distribution over semantic classes should be compared against the *background* distribution, because a class that is common everywhere is uninformative.

**Starting Point — Define the preference score function.** We require a function $S(p, c)$ that is:

1. Non-negative: $S \geq 0$.
2. Zero when the predicate shows no preference: $S = 0$ iff $P(c \mid p) = P(c)$ for all $c$.
3. Additive across classes (so we can sum contributions from multiple classes).

A natural measure satisfying these properties is the **Kullback–Leibler Divergence** from background $P(C)$ to observed $P(C \mid p)$:

$$
S(p, c) \;=\; D_{KL}\!\left(\,P(C \mid p) \;\Big\Vert\; P(C)\,\right)
$$

By definition of KL Divergence:

$$
D_{KL}\!\left(\,P(C \mid p) \;\Big\Vert\; P(C)\,\right) \;=\; \sum_{c \,\in\, C} P(c \mid p)\,\log \frac{P(c \mid p)}{P(c)}
$$

This is the **total** association of predicate $p$ with the entire class distribution. The **per-class contribution** is:

$$
S(p, c) \;=\; P(c \mid p)\,\log \frac{P(c \mid p)}{P(c)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This per-class term is the building block. Resnik uses it twice: (i) summed to obtain the predicate's overall divergence, and (ii) argmaxed to find the *most informative* class for $p$.

**Probability Estimation from Counts.** Let $n(p, c)$ be the number of times predicate $p$ takes an argument of class $c$ in the corpus, and let $n(p) = \sum_{c'} n(p, c')$ be the predicate's total argument count. Then:

$$
P(c \mid p) \;=\; \frac{n(p, c)}{n(p)}
$$

For the **background prior**, we sum over all predicates $p'$ in the vocabulary $V_p$:

$$
P(c) \;=\; \frac{\sum_{p' \,\in\, V_p} n(p', c)}{\sum_{p' \,\in\, V_p} n(p')}
$$

**Complete Substituted Expression.** Combining the above:

$$
S(p, c) \;=\; \frac{n(p, c)}{n(p)}\,\log\!\left(\,\frac{\,n(p, c)\,/\,n(p)\,}{\,\sum_{p'} n(p', c)\,/\,\sum_{p'} n(p')\,}\,\right)
$$

**Identification of the Most Informative Class.** For a given predicate $p$, the *most informative class* $c^*(p)$ is the one that maximizes the per-class score:

$$
c^{*}(p) \;=\; \arg\max_{c \,\in\, C}\; \frac{n(p, c)}{n(p)}\,\log \frac{P(c \mid p)}{P(c)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This $c^*(p)$ is the class typically reported in NLP research papers (e.g., "for the verb *eat*, the most informative class is *food*").

### 3.2 Analytical Worked Example (Mini Corpus)

Consider the following toy corpus with verbs $p \in \{\text{eat}, \text{see}, \text{break}\}$ and arguments $a$ grouped by class $c$:

| Class $c$ | Argument Nouns | Total Count $\sum_p n(p,c)$ |
|---|---|---|
| Food | sandwich, apple, bread | **30** |
| Tool | hammer, saw, knife | **20** |
| Idea | theory, plan, dream | **40** |
| Animal | dog, cat, mouse | **10** |
| **Total $\sum$** | — | **100** |

Verb-specific counts $n(p, a)$ (e.g., for *eat*): sandwich=10, apple=8, bread=7, idea=2, animal=1, tool=0.

**Step 1 — Aggregate $n(p, c)$ for *eat*:**

$$
n(\text{eat}, \text{Food}) = 10 + 8 + 7 = 25
$$
$$
n(\text{eat}, \text{Idea}) = 2,\quad n(\text{eat}, \text{Animal}) = 1,\quad n(\text{eat}, \text{Tool}) = 0
$$
$$
n(\text{eat}) = 25 + 2 + 1 + 0 = 28
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $P(c \mid p)$ and $P(c)$:**

$$
P(\text{Food} \mid \text{eat}) = \frac{25}{28} \approx 0.893, \quad P(\text{Food}) = \frac{30}{100} = 0.300
$$
$$
P(\text{Idea} \mid \text{eat}) = \frac{2}{28} \approx 0.071, \quad P(\text{Idea}) = \frac{40}{100} = 0.400
$$
$$
P(\text{Animal} \mid \text{eat}) = \frac{1}{28} \approx 0.036, \quad P(\text{Animal}) = \frac{10}{100} = 0.100
$$
$$
P(\text{Tool} \mid \text{eat}) = \frac{0}{28} = 0.000, \quad P(\text{Tool}) = \frac{20}{100} = 0.200
$$

**Step 3 — Compute per-class association (using natural log, in nats):**

$$
S(\text{eat}, \text{Food}) = 0.893 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{0.893}{0.300}\right) = 0.893 \times \ln(2.977) = 0.893 \times 1.091 \approx 0.974 \text{ nats}
$$

$$
S(\text{eat}, \text{Idea}) = 0.071 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{0.071}{0.400}\right) = 0.071 \times \ln(0.178) = 0.071 \times (-1.726) \approx -0.123 \text{ nats}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Convention:** A *negative* per-class score is permitted in the per-class formulation (it indicates the predicate avoids this class). However, when summed to obtain the *total* association $S_A$, the total is **always non-negative** because KL Divergence $\geq 0$.

$$
S(\text{eat}, \text{Animal}) = 0.036 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{0.036}{0.100}\right) = 0.036 \times \ln(0.360) = 0.036 \times (-1.022) \approx -0.036 \text{ nats}
$$

$$
S(\text{eat}, \text{Tool}) = 0 \times \ln(0) = 0 \text{ nats} \quad \text{(by convention } 0 \log 0 = 0\text{)}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Selectional Association for *eat*:**

$$
S_A(\text{eat}) = 0.974 + (-0.123) + (-0.036) + 0 = 0.815 \text{ nats}
$$

**Step 5 — Most Informative Class:**

$$
c^*(\text{eat}) = \arg\max_{c} S(\text{eat}, c) = \text{Food} \quad (\text{score} = 0.974 \text{ nats})
$$

**Interpretation:** The verb *eat* has a **strong, specific preference** for the *Food* class. Its total association strength of **0.815 nats** quantifies how specialized *eat* is — a verb like *see* would have a much lower score because its arguments are spread across many classes.

### 3.3 Bayesian Decomposition Used in Li & Abe (1998)

When class membership is *latent* (e.g., ambiguous noun "bank"), we use Bayes' rule to compute the posterior:

$$
P(c \mid p, a) \;=\; \frac{P(c \mid p)\, P(a \mid p, c)}{P(a \mid p)}
$$

Where the **denominator** (normalizing constant) is:

$$
P(a \mid p) \;=\; \sum_{c' \,\in\, C} P(c' \mid p)\, P(a \mid p, c')
$$

**Estimation via Expectation–Maximization (EM):**

- **E-step:** Compute expected class assignments $P(c \mid p, a)$ for each occurrence using current parameter estimates.
- **M-step:** Re-estimate $P(c \mid p)$ and $P(a \mid p, c)$ from the expected counts, repeating until log-likelihood converges.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> EM is **iterative and monotone**: the log-likelihood never decreases between iterations, guaranteeing convergence to a (local) maximum.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Selectional Preference Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Text Corpus] --> B[POS Tagging and Parsing]
    B --> C[Predicate Argument Extraction]
    C --> D{Argument Sense Known?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Direct WordNet Hypernym Lookup]
    D -- No --> F[EM Based Class Inference]
    E --> G[Aggregate Counts n p c]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Estimate P c given p and Prior P c]
    H --> I[Compute KL Divergence S A]
    I --> J[Argmax to find Most Informative Class]
    I --> K[Store Association Scores in Lexicon]
    J --> L[Downstream NLP Applications]
    K --> L
    L --> L1[Word Sense Disambiguation]
    L --> L2[Semantic Role Labeling]
    L --> L3[Paraphrase Mining]
    L --> L4[Anomaly and Hallucination Detection]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: Resnik's Class-Based Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT
        A1[Verb Predicate p]
        A2[Noun Argument a]
    end
    subgraph CLASS_LAYER
        B1[WordNet Hypernym Tree]
        B2[Most Informative Ancestor Synset c]
    end
    subgraph STAT_ENGINE
        C1[Frequency Table n p c]
        C2[Conditional Estimator P c given p]
        C3[Background Prior P c]
        C4[KL Divergence Calculator]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT
        D1[Selectional Association S A p c in nats]
        D2[Best Class c star of p]
    end
    A1 --> C1
    A2 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C2 --> C4
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> D1
    C2 --> D2
    C3 --> D2
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Hierarchy: Preference vs. Restriction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Semantic Compatibility Between Predicate and Argument]
    R --> R1[Selectional Restrictions - Symbolic AI]
    R --> R2[Selectional Preferences - Statistical NLP]
    R1 --> R1A[Hard binary constraint]
    R1 --> R1B[Feature logic e.g. plus human plus animate]
    R1 --> R1C[Failure if violated ungrammatical]
    R2 --> R2A[Soft graded score]
    R2 --> R2B[Learned from corpus co-occurrence]
    R2 --> R2C[Anomaly detection e.g. metaphor hallucination]
```

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams follow KTU board-friendly conventions: clean block-level flow, no nested HTML, and alphanumeric node identifiers to ensure Mermaid renders without syntax errors during exam preparation slide preparation.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Distinguish between Selectional Restrictions and Selectional Preferences with a suitable example.*

**Model Answer:**

Selectional Restrictions are **hard, symbolic constraints** defined a priori using feature-logic (e.g., in Katz & Fodor 1963), marking argument–predicate pairings as either grammatical or ungrammatical. For example, the verb *sleep* strictly requires an *animate* subject, so "*The rock slept*" is ruled ungrammatical.

Selectional Preferences are **soft, statistical tendencies** learned from corpus co-occurrence data. They produce a *graded score* indicating how typical a given argument is for a predicate. For example, the verb *eat* *strongly prefers* the class *\{food\}*, but a sentence like "*The engine ate fuel*" is grammatical and merely unusual.

| Feature | Restrictions | Preferences |
|---|---|---|
| Type | Binary | Continuous / Graded |
| Origin | Hand-coded rules | Corpus statistics |
| Output | Allowed / Forbidden | Probability / Association score |
| Era | Symbolic NLP (1960s–80s) | Statistical NLP (1990s–present) |

**(3 marks — 1 for definition of each, 1 for distinction, 1 for example)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*What is the role of WordNet in computing Resnik's selectional association strength?*

**Model Answer:**

WordNet provides the **hierarchical semantic taxonomy** of noun senses (synsets) organized via *hypernym* (is-a) relations. In Resnik's (1996) formulation, every argument noun $a$ is first mapped to one or more WordNet synsets, and then generalized to its **most informative ancestor class** $c$ — the synset that maximizes $P(c \mid p) \log \frac{P(c \mid p)}{P(c)}$.

This class aggregation is essential because:

1. It enables **generalization** across semantically related nouns (e.g., *bread*, *rice*, *noodle* all fall under *food*).
2. It provides a **discrete vocabulary of classes** over which probabilities can be reliably estimated.
3. It replaces the noisy raw word space with a **structured semantic space**, allowing the model to assign strong preferences even to nouns not seen in the training corpus.

**(3 marks — 1 for WordNet role, 1 for hypernym mapping, 1 for generalization justification)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> For all selectional preference numerical problems, students **frequently lose 2–3 marks** by:
> 1. Forgetting that **KL Divergence is summed over all classes**, not just the target class.
> 2. Confusing **nats** (natural log) with **bits** (base-2 log) when stating the unit.
> 3. Writing $P(c \mid p)$ as a frequency *ratio* without the **smoothing correction** for unseen combinations.
> 4. Omitting the **argmax step** when the question asks for the *most informative class*.
> Always show: the conditional distribution table, the prior table, the per-class term, the sum, **and** the argmax.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the theoretical basis of Resnik's (1996) Selectional Association Strength. Derive the formula step-by-step, clearly stating the role of KL Divergence. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the following toy corpus statistics for the verb *devour*:

| Class $c$ | $n(\text{devour}, c)$ | $P(c)$ (background prior) |
|---|---|---|
| Food | 18 | 0.25 |
| Document | 6 | 0.15 |
| Animal | 2 | 0.10 |
| Idea | 1 | 0.30 |
| Tool | 0 | 0.20 |

Compute (i) the per-class selectional association $S(\text{devour}, c)$ in nats, (ii) the total association $S_A(\text{devour})$, and (iii) the most informative class $c^*(\text{devour})$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

- **Theoretical Basis:** Selectional Preferences quantify the *non-uniformity* of a predicate's argument distribution over semantic classes. Resnik uses **Information Theory (KL Divergence)** to measure how much the observed distribution $P(C \mid p)$ diverges from the background $P(C)$.
- **Why KL Divergence?** It is non-negative, equals zero iff the two distributions are identical, and is additive across classes. It also has a natural information-theoretic interpretation: it measures the *expected number of nats* (or bits) needed to encode the predicate's argument choice if we encoded it optimally assuming the background distribution.
- **Derivation:**
  1. Define predicate–argument joint frequency $n(p, a)$ in the corpus.
  2. Map each noun argument $a$ to its WordNet synset, then ascend to the most informative ancestor class $c$.
  3. Aggregate to obtain $n(p, c)$.
  4. Estimate $P(c \mid p) = \frac{n(p, c)}{\sum_{c'} n(p, c')}$.
  5. Estimate prior $P(c) = \frac{\sum_{p'} n(p', c)}{\sum_{p', c'} n(p', c')}$.
  6. Define per-class score: $S(p, c) = P(c \mid p) \log \frac{P(c \mid p)}{P(c)}$.
  7. Define total: $S_A(p) = \sum_c P(c \mid p) \log \frac{P(c \mid p)}{P(c)} = D_{KL}\!\left(P(C \mid p) \Vert P(C)\right)$.
  8. Identify the most informative class: $c^*(p) = \arg\max_c S(p, c)$.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute $P(c \mid \text{devour})$:**

Total $n(\text{devour}) = 18 + 6 + 2 + 1 + 0 = 27$.

$$
P(\text{Food} \mid \text{devour}) = \frac{18}{27} \approx 0.667
$$
$$
P(\text{Document} \mid \text{devour}) = \frac{6}{27} \approx 0.222
$$
$$
P(\text{Animal} \mid \text{devour}) = \frac{2}{27} \approx 0.074
$$
$$
P(\text{Idea} \mid \text{devour}) = \frac{1}{27} \approx 0.037
$$
$$
P(\text{Tool} \mid \text{devour}) = \frac{0}{27} = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Per-class score $S(\text{devour}, c) = P(c \mid p) \ln \frac{P(c \mid p)}{P(c)}$:**

$$
S(\text{Food}) = 0.667 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{0.667}{0.25}\right) = 0.667 \times \ln(2.667) = 0.667 \times 0.981 = 0.654 \text{ nats}
$$

$$
S(\text{Document}) = 0.222 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{0.222}{0.15}\right) = 0.222 \times \ln(1.481) = 0.222 \times 0.393 = 0.087 \text{ nats}
$$

$$
S(\text{Animal}) = 0.074 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{0.074}{0.10}\right) = 0.074 \times \ln(0.741) = 0.074 \times (-0.300) = -0.022 \text{ nats}
$$

$$
S(\text{Idea}) = 0.037 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{0.037}{0.30}\right) = 0.037 \times \ln(0.123) = 0.037 \times (-2.094) = -0.078 \text{ nats}
$$

$$
S(\text{Tool}) = 0 \times \ln(0) = 0 \text{ nats}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Association:**

$$
S_A(\text{devour}) = 0.654 + 0.087 + (-0.022) + (-0.078) + 0 = 0.641 \text{ nats}
$$

**Step 4 — Most Informative Class:**

$$
c^*(\text{devour}) = \arg\max_c S(\text{devour}, c) = \text{Food} \quad (\text{score} = 0.654 \text{ nats})
$$

**Final Interpretation:** The verb *devour* strongly prefers the *Food* class (highest per-class score, dominant contribution to total). It also shows mild affinity to *Document* (e.g., *devour a novel*), and negative preference for *Idea* and *Animal*.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Compare the three major approaches to learning selectional preferences: (i) Resnik's class-based model, (ii) Li & Abe's probabilistic class-based model with EM, and (iii) the N-Best direct probabilistic model. Highlight assumptions, advantages, and limitations of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, illustrate the architecture of Resnik's class-based selectional preference system. Explain how the EM-based Li & Abe model extends this architecture to handle noun polysemy. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

| Aspect | Resnik (1996) | Li & Abe (1998) | N-Best Direct |
|---|---|---|---|
| Class layer | Yes (WordNet) | Yes (latent class) | No class |
| Class assignment | Hard (one synset) | Soft (EM posterior) | N/A |
| Score function | KL Divergence | Bayesian posterior $P(c \mid p, a)$ | $P(a \mid p)$ |
| Smoothing | Required | Implicit via EM | Required |
| Handles polysemy | No (hard decision) | Yes (fractional) | Partially |
| Generalization | High | High | Low |
| Computational cost | Low | High (iterative) | Very low |
| Output | One informative class | Distribution over classes | Per-word probability |
| Key limitation | Word sense must be known | Local optima in EM | Sparse data |

**Advantages / Disadvantages:**

- **Resnik:** Elegant, theoretically grounded in information theory, but loses information at the hard class-assignment step.
- **Li & Abe:** Handles lexical ambiguity naturally, but EM may converge to local maxima; sensitive to initial conditions.
- **N-Best:** Simple and fast, but cannot generalize to unseen nouns; suffers from data sparsity for rare verbs.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.2 of these notes for the Resnik architecture).** The key components are:

1. **Input Layer:** Predicate $p$ and Noun argument $a$.
2. **Class Mapping Layer:** WordNet hypernym tree to map $a$ to synset $c$.
3. **Statistical Engine:** Frequency table, conditional estimator, prior estimator, KL Divergence calculator.
4. **Output Layer:** Selectional Association $S_A$ and most informative class $c^*$.

**How Li & Abe's EM Model Extends the Architecture:**

- Replace the **hard WordNet lookup** with a **soft probabilistic classifier** that outputs $P(c \mid p, a)$.
- Introduce a **hidden variable** $C$ (semantic class) and estimate its distribution via **Expectation–Maximization**:
  - **E-step:** Compute $P(c \mid p, a)$ using Bayes' rule and current parameters.
  - **M-step:** Re-estimate $P(c \mid p)$ and $P(a \mid p, c)$ from expected counts.
- This allows the same noun (*bank*) to contribute fractionally to multiple classes (*financial institution* vs. *river edge*), making the model robust to polysemy.
- The output is a **probability distribution over classes** rather than a single class, which can be further thresholded for downstream NLP tasks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Selectional Preferences (PEADT632 Module 3)**

- **Core idea:** Selectional preferences are *soft, statistical* generalizations of the older *hard, symbolic* selectional restrictions.
- **Origin:** Katz & Fodor (1963) for restrictions; Resnik (1996) for the statistical formulation.
- **Three ingredients of Resnik's model:** (i) Semantic class hierarchy (WordNet), (ii) conditional probability $P(c \mid p)$, (iii) background prior $P(c)$.
- **Master formula:** $S_A(p) = \sum_c P(c \mid p) \log \frac{P(c \mid p)}{P(c)}$ — KL Divergence between observed and background class distributions. **Unit = nats or bits.**
- **Per-class term:** $P(c \mid p) \log \frac{P(c \mid p)}{P(c)}$ — used to find the most informative class via argmax.
- **Most informative class** $c^*(p) = \arg\max_c S(p, c)$ — the class that best characterizes the predicate.
- **Three model families:** Class-based (Resnik), Probabilistic class-based with EM (Li & Abe), N-Best direct probabilistic.
- **KL Divergence properties:** Non-negative, zero iff distributions equal, **asymmetric** (not a true metric).
- **Smoothing is mandatory** for unseen (predicate, argument) pairs — use Lidstone, Laplace, or Good–Turing.
- **Bayesian decomposition:** $P(c \mid p, a) = \frac{P(c \mid p)\, P(a \mid p, c)}{P(a \mid p)}$ — used when class is latent.
- **EM algorithm:** Two iterative steps (E-step for expectations, M-step for re-estimation), log-likelihood is monotonically non-decreasing.
- **Applications:** WSD, SRL, paraphrase mining, anomaly/hallucination detection, MT quality estimation.
- **Valuation tip:** Always state the *unit* (nats or bits), always show the *sum over all classes*, and always perform the *argmax* when the question asks for the most informative class.
- **Comparison must-hit points:** Hard vs. soft, symbolic vs. statistical, generalization vs. sparsity, hard class vs. soft class assignment.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Semantic Similarity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3: Semantic Similarity

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Semantic Similarity** is a foundational sub-field of Natural Language Processing (NLP) and Computational Linguistics that quantifies the degree of closeness in *meaning* between two linguistic units — such as words, phrases, sentences, or entire documents. Unlike syntactic similarity (which compares surface form), semantic similarity operates on the conceptual, latent, or distributional meaning of the text.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEADT632):** Semantic Similarity is the process of computing a numerical score $sim(s_1, s_2) \in [0, 1]$ that reflects how closely the meanings of two linguistic expressions $s_1$ and $s_2$ are aligned, where a score of **1.0** indicates semantic equivalence and **0.0** indicates total semantic unrelatedness.

In KTU examination contexts, semantic similarity questions typically focus on three pillars:
1. **Lexical / Surface-level similarity** (e.g., Edit Distance, Jaccard).
2. **Knowledge-based similarity** (e.g., WordNet path measures, Wu–Palmer, Resnik, Lin, Jiang–Conrath).
3. **Distributional / Vector-space similarity** (e.g., Cosine Similarity on Word2Vec, GloVe, BERT embeddings).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Library & The Librarian":**
> Imagine a giant library. A naïve visitor (lexical matching) only looks at the **book titles** — "Car" and "Automobile" look different, so they go in separate piles. But a *wise librarian* (semantic similarity) understands that *Car* and *Automobile* mean the **same thing**, and that *Car* and *Bus* are *similar but not identical*. Semantic similarity is the mathematical brain of that wise librarian. It maps *words* or *sentences* into a hidden "meaning space" where **distance = conceptual difference**.

This is why *king – man + woman ≈ queen* is the famous intuitive example: the vector space encodes **gender** and **royalty** as directions, allowing analogy-based similarity.

### 1.3 Why is Semantic Similarity Important in KTU Engineering Context?

| Application | Role of Semantic Similarity |
|---|---|
| Search Engines (Google) | Match user query to relevant documents even when exact keywords differ |
| Chatbots / Virtual Assistants | Detect paraphrased user intents |
| Plagiarism Detection | Identify text rephrasings |
| Recommender Systems | Suggest articles/products with similar meaning |
| Question Answering (QA) | Find best-matching answer candidate |
| Machine Translation Evaluation | Compare candidate translation with reference |
| Clustering & Topic Modeling | Group semantically related documents |

### 1.4 Categories of Semantic Similarity (KTU High-Yield Classification)

> [!NOTE]
> Examiners frequently ask: *"Classify semantic similarity measures."* Memorize this trichotomy:

- **Knowledge-based (Semantic Network) Methods** — rely on external ontologies such as **WordNet**. Examples: Path Length, Wu–Palmer, Resnik, Lin, Jiang–Conrath.
- **Corpus-based / Distributional Methods** — rely on co-occurrence statistics from large corpora. Examples: LSA, PMI, LDA.
- **Vector-space / Neural Embedding Methods** — rely on dense learned representations. Examples: Word2Vec, GloVe, FastText, BERT, Sentence-BERT.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Lexical Similarity (Pre-Semantic Baseline)

These methods do not capture true meaning — they operate on raw character/word tokens. They are mentioned in KTU Module 3 as the *starting baseline* against which semantic methods are compared.

### (a) Edit Distance (Levenshtein Distance)

The minimum number of single-character edits (insertions, deletions, substitutions) required to transform string $s_1$ into string $s_2$.

$$ED(s_1, s_2) = \min\{\text{edits to convert } s_1 \text{ into } s_2\}$$

Normalized similarity:

$$sim_{ED} = 1 - \frac{ED(s_1, s_2)}{\max(\vert s_1 \vert, \vert s_2 \vert)}$$

### (b) Jaccard Similarity (Token-Set)

For two sets of tokens $A$ and $B$:

$$J(A, B) = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$$

## 2.2 Knowledge-Based Similarity (WordNet Measures)

WordNet organizes English words into a hierarchical **synset tree** based on *is-a* (hypernym/hyponym) relations.

**Information Content (IC)** of a concept $c$:

$$IC(c) = -\log P(c)$$

where $P(c) = \frac{\text{freq}(c)}{N}$ is the probability that a randomly drawn word in a corpus is an instance of concept $c$.

> [!NOTE]
> **IC is high for very specific concepts (e.g., "pangolin") and low for very general concepts (e.g., "entity").**

### (a) Path-Length Similarity

$$sim_{path}(s_1, s_2) = \frac{1}{1 + d(s_1, s_2)}$$

where $d(s_1, s_2)$ is the number of edges in the shortest path between synsets.

### (b) Wu–Palmer Similarity (WUP)

Based on **depth of Least Common Subsumer (LCS)** in the WordNet taxonomy:

$$WUP(s_1, s_2) = \frac{2 \cdot depth(LCS(s_1, s_2))}{depth(s_1) + depth(s_2)}$$

### (c) Resnik Similarity

$$Resnik(s_1, s_2) = IC(LCS(s_1, s_2))$$

Range: $[0, \infty)$. (Higher LCS IC = more semantically similar.)

### (d) Lin Similarity

$$Lin(s_1, s_2) = \frac{2 \cdot IC(LCS(s_1, s_2))}{IC(s_1) + IC(s_2)}$$

Range: $[0, 1]$.

### (e) Jiang–Conrath Distance (JCN)

$$JCN(s_1, s_2) = IC(s_1) + IC(s_2) - 2 \cdot IC(LCS(s_1, s_2))$$

(Convert to similarity via $sim = \frac{1}{1 + JCN}$.)

## 2.3 Distributional & Vector-Space Similarity

### (a) Cosine Similarity (The King of KTU Questions)

For two vectors $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$ in $n$-dimensional space:

$$\cos(\theta) = \frac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{\Vert \vec{A} \Vert \cdot \Vert \vec{B} \Vert} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i B_i}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} B_i^2}}$$

- Range: $[-1, 1]$ (typically $[0, 1]$ for non-negative word/document vectors).
- $\cos(\theta) = 1$: identical direction (maximum similarity).
- $\cos(\theta) = 0$: orthogonal (no similarity).
- $\cos(\theta) = -1$: opposite meaning (rare in NLP).

### (b) Euclidean Distance

$$d_E(\vec{A}, \vec{B}) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (A_i - B_i)^2}$$

Converted to similarity: $sim_E = \frac{1}{1 + d_E}$.

### (c) Manhattan (City-Block) Distance

$$d_{Man}(\vec{A}, \vec{B}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert A_i - B_i \vert$$

### (d) Pointwise Mutual Information (PMI)

For words $w_1$ and $w_2$:

$$PMI(w_1, w_2) = \log \frac{P(w_1, w_2)}{P(w_1) P(w_2)}$$

Positive PMI (PPMI) clips negative values to 0 to retain only positive semantic associations.

## 2.4 Neural Embedding-Based Similarity

- **Word2Vec (Mikolov 2013)** — trains a shallow neural network using either **CBOW** (predict word from context) or **Skip-gram** (predict context from word). Resulting vectors allow *King − Man + Woman ≈ Queen*.
- **GloVe (Pennington 2014)** — factorizes the global word–word co-occurrence matrix.
- **FastText (Bojanowski 2017)** — represents words as bags of character n-grams, handling out-of-vocabulary (OOV) words.
- **Sentence-BERT (Reimers 2019)** — siamese BERT network for sentence-level semantic embeddings, used for semantic textual similarity (STS) tasks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Hot Topic:** "Explain how BERT captures semantic similarity better than Word2Vec." Expected Answer: BERT produces **contextual embeddings** (same word gets different vectors in different sentences), while Word2Vec produces **static embeddings** (one fixed vector per word regardless of context).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Measure | Formula | Range | Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| Edit Distance | $ED(s_1, s_2)$ — min edits | $[0, \infty)$ | Lexical |
| Jaccard | $J = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Lexical (Set) |
| Information Content | $IC(c) = -\log P(c)$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Knowledge |
| Path Similarity | $sim_{path} = \frac{1}{1+d}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Knowledge |
| Wu–Palmer | $WUP = \frac{2 \cdot depth(LCS)}{depth(s_1)+depth(s_2)}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Knowledge |
| Resnik | $Resnik = IC(LCS)$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Knowledge |
| Lin | $Lin = \frac{2 \cdot IC(LCS)}{IC(s_1)+IC(s_2)}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Knowledge |
| Jiang–Conrath | $JCN = IC(s_1)+IC(s_2)-2 \cdot IC(LCS)$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Knowledge |
| Cosine | $\cos(\theta) = \frac{\vec{A}\cdot\vec{B}}{\Vert A \Vert \Vert B \Vert}$ | $[-1, 1]$ | Vector |
| Euclidean | $d_E = \sqrt{\sum (A_i - B_i)^2}$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Vector |
| Manhattan | $d_{Man} = \sum \vert A_i - B_i \vert$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Vector |
| PMI | $PMI = \log \frac{P(w_1,w_2)}{P(w_1)P(w_2)}$ | $(-\infty, \infty)$ | Corpus |

> [!WARNING]
> **MARK-LOSS WARNING:** Do **NOT** confuse *Cosine Distance* with *Cosine Similarity*. Examiners deduct **2 marks** if you write $\cos(\theta)$ as a distance rather than a similarity. Use $d_{cos} = 1 - \cos(\theta)$ for distance form.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern industry, semantic similarity underpins:
- **Semantic Search** (ElasticSearch with BERT vectors, Pinecone vector DBs).
- **Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)** — LLM agents find semantically related documents.
- **Recommendation Engines** (Netflix, Amazon use embedding similarity).
- **Duplicate Bug Detection** in software engineering (relevant to KTU CS branches).
- **Medical NLP** — finding clinically similar patient records.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Cosine Similarity (Vector-Space Method)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Question:** Given two 3-dimensional word/document vectors $\vec{A} = (1, 2, 3)$ and $\vec{B} = (4, 1, 2)$, compute the cosine similarity.

**Step 1 — Compute the dot product $\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}$:**

$$\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = (1)(4) + (2)(1) + (3)(2)$$

$$\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = 4 + 2 + 6 = 12$$

**Step 2 — Compute the magnitude of $\vec{A}$:**

$$\Vert \vec{A} \Vert = \sqrt{1^2 + 2^2 + 3^2} = \sqrt{1 + 4 + 9} = \sqrt{14} \approx 3.7417$$

**Step 3 — Compute the magnitude of $\vec{B}$:**

$$\Vert \vec{B} \Vert = \sqrt{4^2 + 1^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{16 + 1 + 4} = \sqrt{21} \approx 4.5826$$

**Step 4 — Plug into the cosine similarity formula:**

$$\cos(\theta) = \frac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{\Vert \vec{A} \Vert \cdot \Vert \vec{B} \Vert} = \frac{12}{\sqrt{14} \cdot \sqrt{21}}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the denominator:**

$$\sqrt{14} \cdot \sqrt{21} = \sqrt{14 \times 21} = \sqrt{294} \approx 17.1464$$

**Step 6 — Final numerical result:**

$$\cos(\theta) = \frac{12}{17.1464} \approx 0.6999 \approx 0.70$$

> **Interpretation:** A cosine similarity of **0.70** indicates the two vectors point in broadly the same direction — they are *moderately to strongly similar* in semantic space.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Wu–Palmer Similarity (Knowledge-Based Method)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Question:** In WordNet, suppose:
> - $depth(\text{tiger}) = 12$
> - $depth(\text{cat}) = 11$
> - Least Common Subsumer (LCS) of tiger and cat is *feline* with $depth(\text{feline}) = 10$.
>
> Compute Wu–Palmer similarity between *tiger* and *cat*.

**Step 1 — Recall the Wu–Palmer formula:**

$$WUP(s_1, s_2) = \frac{2 \cdot depth(LCS)}{depth(s_1) + depth(s_2)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$WUP(\text{tiger}, \text{cat}) = \frac{2 \cdot depth(\text{feline})}{depth(\text{tiger}) + depth(\text{cat})}$$

$$WUP(\text{tiger}, \text{cat}) = \frac{2 \cdot 10}{12 + 11}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify numerator and denominator:**

$$WUP = \frac{20}{23} \approx 0.8696 \approx 0.87$$

> **Interpretation:** *Tiger* and *cat* are **87% similar** semantically under Wu–Palmer, reflecting their shared *feline* ancestry.

**General Pattern of the Calculation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
WUP(s_1, s_2) &= \frac{2 \cdot d_{LCS}}{d_{s_1} + d_{s_2}} \\
&= \frac{2 \cdot d_{LCS}}{d_{s_1} + d_{s_2}} \times \frac{1}{1} \\
&= \frac{2 \cdot d_{LCS}}{d_{s_1} + d_{s_2}}
\end{aligned}$$

The factor of **2** in the numerator ensures the metric is symmetric and normalized to 1.0 when both concepts are identical (i.e., when $LCS = s_1 = s_2$).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Information Content & Resnik Similarity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Question:** Consider a corpus with $N = 1{,}000{,}000$ words. Suppose:
> - $freq(\text{animal}) = 100{,}000$
> - $freq(\text{dog}) = 5{,}000$
> - $freq(\text{cat}) = 4{,}500$
> - $LCS(\text{dog}, \text{cat}) = \text{carnivore}$ with $freq(\text{carnivore}) = 9{,}000$
>
> Compute Resnik similarity between *dog* and *cat*.

**Step 1 — Compute $P(\text{carnivore})$:**

$$P(\text{carnivore}) = \frac{freq(\text{carnivore})}{N} = \frac{9{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} = 0.009$$

**Step 2 — Compute $IC(\text{carnivore})$:**

$$IC(\text{carnivore}) = -\log P(\text{carnivore}) = -\log(0.009)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the logarithm (base $e$ or base 2 — KTU typically accepts either, but state your choice):**

Using natural log (base $e$):

$$IC = -\ln(0.009) = -(-4.7105) = 4.7105$$

**Step 4 — Apply Resnik similarity:**

$$Resnik(\text{dog}, \text{cat}) = IC(LCS) = 4.7105$$

> **Interpretation:** Resnik similarity is **4.71** (in nats). The higher the IC of the LCS, the more semantically related the two concepts.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Jaccard Similarity (Lexical)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Question:** Find the Jaccard similarity between sentences:
> - $S_1 =$ "The cat sat on the mat."
> - $S_2 =$ "The dog sat on the rug."

**Step 1 — Tokenize (lowercase, remove punctuation):**

$S_1 = \{\text{the, cat, sat, on, mat}\}$

$S_2 = \{\text{the, dog, sat, on, rug}\}$

**Step 2 — Compute intersection:**

$$S_1 \cap S_2 = \{\text{the, sat, on}\} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert S_1 \cap S_2 \vert = 3$$

**Step 3 — Compute union:**

$$S_1 \cup S_2 = \{\text{the, cat, sat, on, mat, dog, rug}\} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert S_1 \cup S_2 \vert = 7$$

**Step 4 — Apply Jaccard formula:**

$$J(S_1, S_2) = \frac{\vert S_1 \cap S_2 \vert}{\vert S_1 \cup S_2 \vert} = \frac{3}{7} \approx 0.4286$$

> **Interpretation:** Despite having *semantically related* content (both about an animal sitting on a floor covering), Jaccard yields only **0.43** because it cannot recognize that *cat ≈ dog* and *mat ≈ rug*. This illustrates the **limitation of lexical similarity** and motivates semantic methods.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (When to Use What?)

| Scenario | Best Similarity Measure | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Spell-check / typo detection | Edit Distance | Operates on character-level |
| Document clustering (BoW) | Cosine Similarity | Normalized for document length |
| WordNet ontology queries | Wu–Palmer / Resnik | Leverages hierarchical knowledge |
| Paraphrase detection | BERT / Sentence-BERT cosine | Captures deep contextual meaning |
| Short query vs long document | Cosine similarity on TF-IDF | Penalizes common terms |
| Cross-lingual similarity | LASER / multilingual BERT | Shared multilingual embedding space |
| Plagiarism (heavy rewrite) | Sentence embedding cosine | Detects paraphrases beyond n-grams |
| Real-time chat intent matching | Cosine on fastText / SBERT | Low latency, robust to OOV |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A1]**
> **Q:** Define *Semantic Similarity*. How is it different from *Lexical Similarity*?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Semantic similarity is the measure of how closely two linguistic units (words, sentences, or documents) are related in *meaning*, independent of their surface form. It captures conceptual equivalence, synonymy, and relatedness. Lexical similarity, in contrast, compares the *surface tokens or characters* of the text. For example, *car* and *automobile* have high semantic similarity but low lexical similarity (they share zero characters), while *cat* and *cats* have high lexical similarity (4/4 characters) but the semantic difference is small. Semantic similarity is thus a superset operation that requires meaning representation.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A2]**
> **Q:** List any **three** WordNet-based semantic similarity measures and state their formulas.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three WordNet-based similarity measures are:

1. **Wu–Palmer (WUP):** $WUP(s_1, s_2) = \frac{2 \cdot depth(LCS)}{depth(s_1) + depth(s_2)}$
2. **Resnik:** $Resnik(s_1, s_2) = IC(LCS)$ where $IC(c) = -\log P(c)$.
