- **Brown clusters:** hierarchical groupings of words by distributional similarity.
- **Pretrained embeddings** from large unannotated corpora (transfer learning).

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\hat{t}(w) = \arg\max_t\ P(t \mid w)$ | Bayes' decision rule for tagging | Unigram baseline |
| 2 | $P(t \mid w) = \dfrac{P(w \mid t)\,P(t)}{P(w)}$ | Bayes' theorem | Statistical taggers |
| 3 | $\hat{T} = \arg\max_T\ \prod_i P(t_i \mid t_{i-n+1}^{i-1})\,P(w_i \mid t_i)$ | Generative n-gram tagger | HMM-style taggers |
| 4 | $P_{\text{MLE}}(t_i \mid t_{i-1}) = \dfrac{C(t_{i-1}, t_i)}{C(t_{i-1})}$ | Maximum-likelihood bigram estimate | N-gram training |
| 5 | $P_{\text{Laplace}}(t_i \mid t_{i-1}) = \dfrac{C(t_{i-1}, t_i) + 1}{C(t_{i-1}) + \mid T \mid}$ | Add-one smoothing | Smoothing |
| 6 | $w_i = f_1^i \oplus f_2^i \oplus \dots \oplus f_k^i$ | Feature vector for word $i$ | MaxEnt / CRF / Neural |
| 7 | $\text{OOV Rate} = \dfrac{\#\text{Unknown Words}}{\#\text{Total Words}}$ | Out-of-vocabulary rate | Evaluation |
| 8 | $\text{Accuracy} = \dfrac{\#\text{Correct Tags}}{\#\text{Total Words}}$ | Tagger accuracy metric | Evaluation |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX NOTE:** All set-membership and absolute-value bars in the table above use the safe `\mid` operator — never the plain pipe `|` character — to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Role of Tagging Information Sources |
|---|---|
| **Machine Translation** | Disambiguating word senses before alignment |
| **Information Retrieval** | Indexing by noun phrases |
| **Sentiment Analysis** | Distinguishing adjectives/adverbs that carry polarity |
| **Speech Recognition** | Resolving homophones (e.g., `"their"` vs. `"there"`) |
| **Question Answering** | Detecting question types via auxiliary verb patterns |
| **Healthcare NLP** | Identifying drug names, dosages, body parts in clinical notes |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Bayes' Rule to the HMM Tagger

We derive the **fundamental equation of statistical POS tagging** — the equation that a Hidden Markov Model (HMM) tagger actually optimizes.

**Step 1 — The Joint Probability of a Word Sequence and a Tag Sequence**

For a sentence $W = w_1 w_2 \dots w_n$ with a candidate tag sequence $T = t_1 t_2 \dots t_n$, the joint probability is, by the chain rule of probability:

$$
P(W, T) = P(w_1, t_1, w_2, t_2, \dots, w_n, t_n)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Chain Rule (Lexical × Transition)**

We factor the joint into emission and transition components:

$$
P(W, T) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid t_i) \cdot P(t_i \mid t_{i-1})
$$

where we define $P(t_1 \mid t_0) \triangleq P(t_1)$ as the initial tag probability.

**Step 3 — Explanation of Each Term**

- $P(w_i \mid t_i)$ — **emission probability**: the likelihood that word $w_i$ is generated by tag $t_i$.
- $P(t_i \mid t_{i-1})$ — **transition probability**: the probability of moving from tag $t_{i-1}$ to tag $t_i$.

**Step 4 — Apply Bayes' Rule (Why HMM Taggers are Generative)**

The decision rule is to find the tag sequence that maximizes the posterior:

$$
\hat{T} = \underset{T}{\arg\max}\ P(T \mid W) = \underset{T}{\arg\max}\ \frac{P(W \mid T)\,P(T)}{P(W)}
$$

Since $P(W)$ does not depend on $T$, we can drop it:

$$
\hat{T} = \underset{T}{\arg\max}\ P(W \mid T)\,P(T)
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the HMM Factorization**

$$
\hat{T} = \underset{T}{\arg\max}\ \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid t_i) \cdot P(t_i \mid t_{i-1})
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to Log-Space (for numerical stability)**

To prevent underflow when multiplying many small probabilities, take the log:

$$
\hat{T} = \underset{T}{\arg\max}\ \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Big[\,\log P(w_i \mid t_i) + \log P(t_i \mid t_{i-1})\,\Big]
$$

**Step 7 — Practical Estimation (Maximum Likelihood)**

Both terms are estimated from the tagged training corpus:

$$
P(t_i \mid t_{i-1}) = \frac{C(t_{i-1}, t_i)}{C(t_{i-1})}
$$

$$
P(w_i \mid t_i) = \frac{C(w_i, t_i)}{C(t_i)}
$$

**Step 8 — Smoothing (Laplace) for Sparse Data**

To handle unseen n-grams we apply additive smoothing:

$$
P_{\text{Lap}}(t_i \mid t_{i-1}) = \frac{C(t_{i-1}, t_i) + 1}{C(t_{i-1}) + \mid T \mid}
$$

$$
P_{\text{Lap}}(w_i \mid t_i) = \frac{C(w_i, t_i) + 1}{C(t_i) + \mid V \mid}
$$

where $\mid T \mid$ is the tagset size and $\mid V \mid$ is the vocabulary size.

**Step 9 — The Final Decoding Algorithm**

The arg-max in the equation above is found by the **Viterbi dynamic-programming algorithm**, which fills a trellis of size $n \times \mid T \mid$ in $O(n \cdot \mid T \mid^{2})$ time.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Information Source × Tagging Model

The following matrix maps each information source to the type of tagger that consumes it most effectively. This is a **high-yield KTU visualization** that students should memorize.

| Information Source | Tagger Type That Uses It | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Lexical (word identity)** | Unigram tagger, Lookup tagger | Simple, fast | Cannot disambiguate, OOV failure |
| **Morphological (suffix, prefix)** | Regular-expression tagger, MaxEnt, Neural | Best for OOV words | Limited context |
| **Contextual (n-gram tags)** | N-gram tagger, HMM, MEMM, CRF | Captures local syntax | Sparse data |
| **Distributional (co-occurrence)** | Neural BiLSTM, Transformer taggers | Generalizes to rare words | Computationally heavy |
| **External (gazetteer, WordNet)** | Hybrid taggers, knowledge-rich taggers | High-precision NER | Coverage gaps |
| **Syntactic (chunk, parse)** | Constraint-based taggers | Enforces global coherence | Slow, brittle |

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — Fusing Information Sources

**Sentence:** *"`The book on the table is interesting.`"*

| Position $i$ | Word $w_i$ | Lexical Prior $P(\text{NN} \mid w)$ | Morph. Cue (suffix `-k` → no info) | Context $P(\text{NN} \mid t_{i-1}=\text{DT})$ | Combined Score (log) | Predicted Tag |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `the` | high (DT) | — | — | high | **DT** |
| 2 | `book` | ambiguous | none | $P(\text{NN} \mid \text{DT})$ high | favors NN | **NN** |
| 3 | `on` | high (IN) | — | $P(\text{IN} \mid \text{NN})$ high | favors IN | **IN** |
| 4 | `the` | high (DT) | — | $P(\text{DT} \mid \text{IN})$ high | favors DT | **DT** |
| 5 | `table` | high (NN) | — | $P(\text{NN} \mid \text{DT})$ high | favors NN | **NN** |
| 6 | `is` | high (VBZ) | — | $P(\text{VBZ} \mid \text{NN})$ high | favors VBZ | **VBZ** |
| 7 | `interesting` | moderate | `-ing` → adjective (JJ) | $P(\text{JJ} \mid \text{VBZ})$ vs. $P(\text{VBG} \mid \text{VBZ})$ | favors JJ (semantic wins) | **JJ** |

**Outcome:** The correct tag for `"book"` is disambiguated as **NN** because the contextual source (the preceding `DT` strongly selects for nouns) outweighs the ambiguity in the lexical source.

---

### 3.4 Quantitative Evaluation of Information-Source Contributions

The contribution of each source can be isolated by **ablation studies** — removing one source and measuring the drop in accuracy on a test corpus (e.g., Penn Treebank WSJ).

$$
\Delta\text{Acc}(S_k) = \text{Acc}_{\text{full}} - \text{Acc}_{-S_k}
$$

where $\text{Acc}_{-S_k}$ is the accuracy when source $S_k$ is removed. Typical ablations on an English POS tagger:

| Removed Source $S_k$ | Typical $\Delta$ Accuracy Drop |
|---|---|
| Lexical | **−5% to −10%** (largest single drop) |
| Morphological | **−2% to −4%** (largest for OOV) |
| Contextual (bigram/trigram) | **−3% to −6%** |
| Distributional (embeddings) | **−1% to −3%** |
| External (gazetteer) | **−0.5% to −1.5%** (task-specific) |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Information Source Architecture in a Modern Tagger

The following block diagram shows how raw text is transformed through a pipeline of information sources into a final tag sequence. This is the **canonical KTU Module-4 architecture** expected in 14-mark descriptive answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Sentence W = w1 w2 ... wn] --> B[Tokenization Stage]
    B --> C1[Lexical Source Module]
    B --> C2[Morphological Source Module]
    B --> C3[Contextual N-gram Source Module]
    B --> C4[Distributional Embedding Module]
    B --> C5[External Gazetteer Module]
    C1 --> D[Feature Vector Assembler]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    D --> E[Sequence Model Engine]
    E --> F1[HMM Viterbi Decoder]
    E --> F2[CRF Tagger]
    E --> F3[BiLSTM Neural Tagger]
    E --> F4[Transformer Encoder]
    F1 --> G[Tag Sequence T = t1 t2 ... tn]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[Output Tagged Sentence]
```

### 4.2 Information-Source Decision Flow

The following diagram captures the **decision logic** inside a tagger: for each ambiguous word, the tagger consults the available sources, computes a score, and selects the highest-scoring tag.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Word wi at position i] --> Q1{Is wi in Lexicon?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Lex[Use Lexical P of ti over wi]
    Q1 -- No --> OOV[Trigger OOV Handler]
    OOV --> Morph[Extract Morphological Features - Suffix - Prefix - Capitalization]
    Morph --> MorphScore[Compute Morphological Score]
    Lex --> Context[Look at Contextual N-gram P of ti given t i-2, t i-1]
    MorphScore --> Context
    Context --> Dist[Add Distributional Embedding Score]
    Dist --> Ext[Query External Knowledge if NNP or CD]
    Ext --> Fuse[Fuse All Source Scores]
    Fuse --> Argmax[Argmax over all candidate tags t in T]
    Argmax --> Out[Final Tag ti assigned]
```

### 4.3 Information Source Coverage Map

This diagram illustrates the **complementary coverage** of the four primary information sources over the word space. Morphological covers OOV, lexical covers known-words, contextual handles ambiguity, and distributional generalizes across rare words.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph KS[Known Word Space]
        L1[Lexical Source]
    end
    subgraph OS[OOV Word Space]
        M1[Morphological Source]
    end
    subgraph AS[Ambiguous Word Space]
        C1[Contextual Source]
    end
    subgraph RS[Rare Word Space]
        D1[Distributional Source]
    end
    L1 --> F[Fused Decision]
    M1 --> F
    C1 --> F
    D1 --> F
    F --> Tag[Final Tag Output]
```

### 4.4 Generative vs. Discriminative Source Usage

This block diagram contrasts how two major families of taggers use the information sources internally.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GEN[Generative HMM Tagger]
        G1[Estimates P wi over ti] --> G2[Estimates P ti over t i-1]
        G2 --> G3[Joint argmax via Viterbi]
    end
    subgraph DIS[Discriminative CRF Tagger]
        D1[Builds feature vector f wi ti t i-1] --> D2[Learns weights lambda]
        D2 --> D3[Conditional argmax over T given W]
    end
    G3 --> OUT[Tag Sequence]
    D3 --> OUT
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]**
*List the four primary information sources used in Part-of-Speech tagging. For each, give one example of how it helps disambiguate a word's tag.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Lexical Information:** The word `"run"` has a known prior $P(\text{VB} \mid \text{run}) > P(\text{NN} \mid \text{run})$ in most corpora; the tagger consults this prior first.
2. **Morphological / Orthographic Information:** A capitalized unknown word like `"Xylophone"` is highly likely to be a proper noun (NNP) by capitalization cue.
3. **Contextual (N-gram) Information:** The bigram `DT → NN` is one of the highest-frequency transitions in English, so the word after `"the"` is biased toward a noun.
4. **Distributional / Embedding Information:** Two words rarely seen in training but having similar word vectors (e.g., `"tweeting"` and `"posting"`) are likely to share the same tag class (VBG).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is morphological information the most important source for handling Out-of-Vocabulary (OOV) words in a POS tagger?*

**Model Answer:**

OOV words are tokens never observed in the training corpus, so neither the **lexical** nor the **contextual** (n-gram) source can provide reliable statistics for them. However, OOV words almost always carry strong **morphological cues** — suffixes, prefixes, capitalization, and punctuation — that correlate highly with their POS tag.

For example:

- Suffix `-tion` → noun (NN) with probability ≈ 0.97.
- Suffix `-ly` → adverb (RB) with probability ≈ 0.95.
- Suffix `-ing` → verb-gerund (VBG) or adjective (JJ).
- Initial capital letter + non-sentence-initial position → proper noun (NNP).

A regular-expression or suffix-tree component in the tagger can map these patterns to tags with very high accuracy (often **> 90%** for unknown words), making morphology the **single most effective information source for OOV disambiguation**.

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Critical for 14-Mark Answers:**
> Students routinely lose **3–5 marks** on tagging questions by:
> 1. **Confusing information sources with tagging algorithms** — information sources are *inputs*, not models.
> 2. **Omitting the OOV handling strategy** — always mention what happens for unseen words.
> 3. **Failing to write the Bayes' formulation explicitly** — examiners expect the equation $P(T \mid W) \propto P(W \mid T)\,P(T)$ on paper.
> 4. **Ignoring smoothing** — if you mention n-gram probabilities, you **must** mention Laplace or Kneser-Ney smoothing.
> 5. **Not connecting sources to specific disambiguation examples** — generic descriptions score lower than concrete sentence examples.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Information Sources and HMM Formulation

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain in detail the **five major information sources** used in Part-of-Speech tagging. For each source, give a concrete example of a sentence where that source disambiguates a word's tag.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Derive the **HMM tagging equation** from Bayes' theorem and show how the **Viterbi algorithm** uses contextual information to find the optimal tag sequence for the sentence `"The race was close"` with an example trellis of size $4 \times \mid T \mid$.

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The five major information sources used in POS tagging are:

**1. Lexical Information (Word-Identity Source)**

The tagger maintains a table of word-tag frequencies from a labeled training corpus. For each word, it consults the prior probability $P(t \mid w)$.

- **Example:** `"book"` appears as a noun 70% of the time and as a verb 30% in the Brown Corpus. In isolation, the lexical prior alone would (incorrectly) favor a noun for the sentence `"I will book a ticket"` because of the high prior. Hence lexical information alone is insufficient and **must be combined** with other sources.

**2. Morphological / Orthographic Information**

Internal structure of words — affixes, capitalization, digits, hyphens.

- **Example:** In `"The dogs are barking loudly"`, the word `"dogs"` is unambiguously a plural noun (NNS) because of the suffix `-s` following a known stem. Similarly, `"loudly"` is an adverb (RB) because of the suffix `-ly`.

**3. Contextual (N-gram) Information**

Tags of surrounding words strongly constrain the current tag.

- **Example:** In `"I will book a ticket"`, the previous tag `MD` (modal) strongly favors a base-form verb (VB) at the next position. The bigram probability $P(\text{VB} \mid \text{MD})$ is very high (≈ 0.80), flipping the lexical prior of `"book"` from noun to verb.

**4. Distributional / Co-occurrence Information**

Words with similar context vectors tend to have similar tags.

- **Example:** The rare word `"tweeting"` (in 1990s text) has very sparse statistics, but its word-embedding cosine similarity to `"posting"` (high) allows the tagger to transfer the VBG tag with high confidence.

**5. External Knowledge Sources**

Gazetteers, WordNet, named-entity lists, and Brown clusters.

- **Example:** In `"Barack Obama visited Paris"`, the external gazetteer marks `"Barack"` and `"Paris"` as NNP (proper noun), giving the contextual n-gram model a high-confidence initial state for the noun phrase chunk.

**Marks Allocation (Part a — 7 marks):**
- Definition of 5 sources — 3 marks.
- Concrete sentence example for each — 2 marks.
- Linking sources to OOV vs. in-vocabulary handling — 2 marks.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Decision Rule via Bayes' Theorem**

For a sentence $W = w_1 \dots w_n$, the optimal tag sequence is:

$$
\hat{T} = \underset{T}{\arg\max}\ P(T \mid W) = \underset{T}{\arg\max}\ \frac{P(W \mid T)\,P(T)}{P(W)}
$$

**Step 2 — HMM Factorization**

$$
\hat{T} = \underset{T}{\arg\max}\ \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid t_i) \cdot P(t_i \mid t_{i-1})
$$

**Step 3 — Parameters Estimated from Training Data**

$$
P(t_i \mid t_{i-1}) = \frac{C(t_{i-1}, t_i)}{C(t_{i-1})}
$$

$$
P(w_i \mid t_i) = \frac{C(w_i, t_i)}{C(t_i)}
$$

**Step 4 — Worked Example: Tagging `"The race was close"`**

Let the relevant tagset for this sentence be $T = \{\text{DT}, \text{NN}, \text{VBD}, \text{JJ}, \text{VBN}\}$.

| Position $i$ | Word $w_i$ | Possible Tags | Emission $P(w_i \mid t_i)$ | Best Previous Tag $t_{i-1}$ | Transition $P(t_i \mid t_{i-1})$ | Viterbi Score |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | The | DT | 0.95 | `<s>` | $P(\text{DT} \mid \text{<s>}) = 0.30$ | $0.95 \times 0.30 = 0.285$ |
| 2 | race | NN / VB | 0.85 (NN) | DT | $P(\text{NN} \mid \text{DT}) = 0.55$ | $0.285 \times 0.85 \times 0.55 = 0.133$ |
| 3 | was | VBD / VBN | 0.90 (VBD) | NN | $P(\text{VBD} \mid \text{NN}) = 0.10$ | $0.133 \times 0.90 \times 0.10 = 0.012$ |
| 4 | close | JJ / NN / VB | 0.60 (JJ) | VBD | $P(\text{JJ} \mid \text{VBD}) = 0.25$ | $0.012 \times 0.60 \times 0.25 = 0.0018$ |

**Step 5 — The Viterbi Algorithm in Trellis Form**

The Viterbi algorithm fills a **$4 \times 5$ trellis**, where each cell stores the best cumulative log-score and a back-pointer:

$$
v_i(t) = \max_{t'}\ \Big[\,v_{i-1}(t') + \log P(t \mid t') + \log P(w_i \mid t)\,\Big]
$$

Backtrace from the best final cell recovers the optimal tag sequence: **`DT NN VBD JJ`**.

**Marks Allocation (Part b — 7 marks):**
- Bayes' derivation — 2 marks.
- HMM factorization with MLE — 2 marks.
- Worked numerical example / trellis — 3 marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Morphological Source & OOV Handling

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Discuss the **role of morphological information** in POS tagging. With a labeled diagram of suffix rules, show how suffix patterns help in tagging unknown words. Provide at least **five suffix-rule examples** with their corresponding tags and confidence levels.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Compare **statistical (HMM/MaxEnt)** and **neural (BiLSTM/Transformer)** taggers in terms of how they integrate the information sources. Which approach is more robust for **low-resource languages**, and why?

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Role of Morphological Information**

Morphological information is the **primary rescue mechanism** for Out-of-Vocabulary (OOV) words. While lexical information fails for unseen words and contextual n-gram statistics become sparse, the **internal structure** of a word — its prefix, suffix, and orthographic shape — provides reliable tag priors.

**Suffix Rule Table (Common English Patterns)**

| Suffix Pattern | Example Word | Most Likely Tag | Confidence (approx.) |
|---|---|---|---|
| `-tion`, `-sion`, `-ment` | `decision`, `movement` | **NN** (singular noun) | 0.97 |
| `-ly` | `quickly`, `loudly` | **RB** (adverb) | 0.95 |
| `-ing` (as gerund) | `running`, `swimming` | **VBG** | 0.80 |
| `-ing` (as adjective) | `interesting book` | **JJ** | 0.15 |
| `-ed` (regular past tense) | `walked`, `talked` | **VBD** | 0.90 |
| `-s` (plural noun) | `dogs`, `tables` | **NNS** | 0.85 |
| `-s` (3rd-person verb) | `runs`, `eats` | **VBZ** | 0.10 |
| `-er`, `-est` (comparative/superlative) | `bigger`, `biggest` | **JJR / RBR**, **JJS / RBS** | 0.95 |
| `-ous`, `-ive`, `-al` | `famous`, `active` | **JJ** (adjective) | 0.92 |
| `-ize`, `-ate`, `-ify` | `realize`, `activate` | **VB** (base verb) | 0.85 |
| Capitalized (mid-sentence) | `Barack`, `India` | **NNP** | 0.92 |

**Diagrammatic Representation of Suffix-Rule Engine**

```
            ┌──────────────────────┐
            │   OOV Word Input    │
            └──────────┬───────────┘
                       │
            ┌──────────▼───────────┐
            │  Extract Suffix (1-4)│
            └──────────┬───────────┘
                       │
      ┌────────────────┼────────────────┐
      │                │                │
  ┌───▼────┐      ┌────▼────┐      ┌────▼────┐
  │  -tion │      │   -ly   │      │  -ing   │
  │  → NN  │      │  → RB   │      │ → VBG/JJ│
  └────────┘      └─────────┘      └─────────┘
                       │
            ┌──────────▼───────────┐
            │  Assign Most Likely  │
            │   Tag + Confidence  │
            └──────────────────────┘
```

**Marks Allocation (Part a — 7 marks):**
- Explanation of morphological role — 2 marks.
- Suffix rule table with 5+ examples — 3 marks.
- Diagram of suffix-rule engine — 2 marks.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Comparison: Statistical vs. Neural Taggers**

| Dimension | Statistical (HMM / MaxEnt) | Neural (BiLSTM / Transformer) |
|---|---|---|
| **Lexical Source** | Explicit lookup table | Implicitly encoded in embeddings |
| **Morphological Source** | Hand-crafted suffix/prefix rules | Learned via character-level CNN or subword tokens (BPE) |
| **Contextual Source** | N-gram probability tables | Self-attention captures **long-range** dependencies |
| **Distributional Source** | Brown clusters (external) | Pretrained embeddings (BERT, fastText) — *internal* |
| **External Source** | Gazetteers, dictionaries | Transfer learning from large unannotated corpora |
| **Feature Engineering** | Manual, labor-intensive | Automatic via representation learning |
| **Training Data Needed** | **Small** to moderate | **Large** |
| **OOV Handling** | Strong via suffix rules | Strong via subword/character embeddings |
| **Inference Speed** | Fast (Viterbi) | Slower (GPU required) |
| **Low-Resource Performance** | **Better** | Poor without transfer learning |

**Which is More Robust for Low-Resource Languages?**

**Statistical (HMM / MaxEnt) taggers are more robust for low-resource languages** for the following reasons:

1. **Data Efficiency:** HMMs and CRFs can be trained on corpora as small as **5,000–10,000 tagged tokens**, whereas neural taggers typically require **100,000+ tokens** to avoid overfitting.
2. **Interpretability:** Hand-crafted morphological rules (suffix tables) transfer across languages with minimal changes — a Hindi or Malayalam suffix table can be constructed with a small expert effort.
3. **No Need for Pretrained Models:** Languages like **Maltese, Sinhala, or Manipuri** have no large unannotated corpora to pretrain BERT; statistical taggers can be built from scratch.
4. **Cross-lingual Transfer:** Brown clusters trained on a related high-resource language (e.g., Hindi for Marathi) can be reused for low-resource POS tagging.

**Counter-argument:** If a pretrained **multilingual model** (e.g., mBERT, XLM-R) is available, neural taggers win on accuracy. But the **engineering cost of annotation** remains the bottleneck for truly low-resource languages.

**Marks Allocation (Part b — 7 marks):**
- Side-by-side comparison table — 3 marks.
- Discussion of low-resource suitability — 2 marks.
- Justified conclusion with reasoning — 2 marks.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — The Information Sources in Tagging**

- **Definition:** An information source is any signal (lexical, morphological, contextual, distributional, external) that a tagger uses to assign a POS tag.
- **Lexical Source:** Word-tag frequency table; the simplest source; fails on ambiguous and OOV words.
- **Morphological Source:** Suffixes, prefixes, capitalization, digits; **the strongest source for OOV words**.
- **Contextual Source:** N-gram tag probabilities; captures local syntactic constraints; needs smoothing.
- **Distributional Source:** Co-occurrence vectors / embeddings; generalizes across rare words.
- **External Source:** Gazetteers, WordNet, Brown clusters; boosts NER and proper-noun tagging.
- **HMM Tagging Equation:**

$$
\hat{T} = \underset{T}{\arg\max}\ \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid t_i) \cdot P(t_i \mid t_{i-1})
$$

- **Bayes' Decision Rule:** $\hat{t}(w) = \arg\max_{t} P(t \mid w)$.
- **MLE Estimates:** $P(t_i \mid t_{i-1}) = C(t_{i-1}, t_i) / C(t_{i-1})$ and $P(w_i \mid t_i) = C(w_i, t_i) / C(t_i)$.
- **Smoothing is Mandatory:** Always apply Laplace or Kneser-Ney smoothing for n-gram probabilities.
- **Decoding Algorithm:** Viterbi — runs in $O(n \cdot \mid T \mid^{2})$ time using dynamic programming.
- **Baseline Accuracy:** Default unigram tagger ≈ **90%**; state-of-the-art ≈ **97%–98%** on Penn Treebank.
- **For OOV Words:** Always mention morphological handling (suffix rules, capitalization, default NN).
- **Generative vs. Discriminative:** HMM = generative (joint $P(W,T)$); CRF = discriminative (conditional $P(T \mid W)$).
- **Low-Resource Languages:** Statistical taggers (HMM/CRF) outperform neural taggers when training data < 10K tokens.
- **KTU Mark-Saver:** In 14-mark answers, always draw a **block diagram** of the information-source architecture — it alone can earn **2–3 easy marks**.
- **Common Pitfall:** Confusing *information sources* (the inputs) with *tagging algorithms* (the model). Examiners will deduct marks for this.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Markov Model Taggers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Markov Model Taggers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4: Markov Model Taggers

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Markov Model Tagger** is a statistical sequence labeling algorithm used in **Part-of-Speech (POS) Tagging** that assigns grammatical categories (nouns, verbs, adjectives, etc.) to every word in a sentence by modeling the tag sequence as a **Markov stochastic process**. The system estimates the most probable tag sequence $T^* = t_1, t_2, ..., t_n$ for an observed word sequence $W = w_1, w_2, ..., w_n$ using conditional probability distributions derived from annotated training corpora.

The two principal variants in KTU syllabus context are:

- **Markov Chain Tagger (Markov Model Tagger)** — A visible-state stochastic model where the next state depends only on the current state, formalized as the **First-Order Markov Assumption**.
- **Hidden Markov Model (HMM) Tagger** — A doubly-embedded stochastic model where the underlying tag sequence is *hidden* and only the emitted word sequence is *observable*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme, the term "Markov Model Taggers" collectively refers to **n-gram statistical taggers (Unigram, Bigram, Trigram)** built on the **Markov Assumption**, with the **Hidden Markov Model (HMM)** being the most examination-relevant formulation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Weather-to-Clothing Predictor**

Imagine you are a meteorologist who **cannot directly see the weather** (Sunny, Rainy, Cloudy) but can observe what people are **wearing** (shorts, umbrella, jacket). Based on the clothes people wear today, and yesterday's outfit, you predict today's weather.

- **Hidden states** = Actual weather (you don't directly see it).
- **Observations** = Clothes worn (what you see).
- **Transition probabilities** = Probability of weather changing from one day to the next.
- **Emission probabilities** = Probability of a person wearing shorts *given* it's sunny.

In POS tagging, the **hidden states are the grammatical tags** (NN, VB, JJ), and the **observations are the actual words** ("dog", "runs", "fast"). The tagger must *infer* the hidden tag sequence from the visible word sequence — exactly like guessing the weather from clothing.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| KTU Term | Notation | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Markov Assumption** | $P(t_i \mid t_{i-1}, ..., t_1) \approx P(t_i \mid t_{i-1})$ | The future depends only on the present |
| **States** | $S = \{s_1, s_2, ..., s_N\}$ | The set of POS tags |
| **Observations** | $O = \{o_1, o_2, ..., o_M\}$ | The vocabulary of words |
| **Transition Probability** | $A = a_{ij}$ | $P(s_j \mid s_i)$ — probability of moving from one tag to another |
| **Emission Probability** | $B = b_i(o_k)$ | $P(o_k \mid s_i)$ — probability of a word given a tag |
| **Initial Distribution** | $\pi$ | $P(s_i)$ — probability of a tag being first in a sentence |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Markov Assumption — The Heart of the Tagger

The **fundamental problem** with assigning POS tags using raw Bayes' rule is computational intractability. For a sentence of length $n$ with tag set size $T$, the number of possible tag sequences is $T^n$, which explodes combinatorially.

The **Markov Assumption** simplifies this drastically by asserting:

> The probability of the current state depends **only on a limited history** of the previous states.

**Formally (First-Order):**

$$P(t_i \mid t_{i-1}, t_{i-2}, ..., t_1) \approx P(t_i \mid t_{i-1})$$

**Formally (Second-Order / Trigram):**

$$P(t_i \mid t_{i-1}, t_{i-2}, ..., t_1) \approx P(t_i \mid t_{i-1}, t_{i-2})$$

### 2.2 Why and How It Works

- **Why?** Language has strong local dependencies. A verb is more likely to follow a noun phrase than an article, but the dependence drops off rapidly beyond 1–2 preceding tags.
- **How?** By truncating the conditioning context, the joint probability of a tag sequence becomes a product of manageable local probabilities.

The joint probability of a tag sequence under the **bigram Markov assumption** is:

$$P(t_1, t_2, ..., t_n) \approx P(t_1) \cdot \prod_{i=2}^{n} P(t_i \mid t_{i-1})$$

### 2.3 Hidden Markov Model (HMM) Tagger — Components

A complete HMM Tagger is defined as a **5-tuple** $\lambda = (S, O, A, B, \pi)$:

- **$S$** — Finite set of hidden states (POS tags).
- **$O$** — Finite set of observation symbols (words).
- **$A$** — **Transition probability matrix** of size $N \times N$.
- **$B$** — **Emission probability matrix** of size $N \times M$.
- **$\pi$** — **Initial state distribution** vector of size $N$.

### 2.4 The Three Classical Problems of HMM (Board-Favorite!)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently test this 3-problem framework. Memorize it.

| Problem # | Goal | Algorithm Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Problem 1** | Given $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$ and $O$, compute $P(O \mid \lambda)$ | **Forward-Backward Algorithm** |
| **Problem 2** | Given $\lambda$ and $O$, find the best state sequence $Q^*$ | **Viterbi Algorithm** |
| **Problem 3** | Given $O$, learn $A$, $B$, $\pi$ that maximize $P(O \mid \lambda)$ | **Baum-Welch (EM) Algorithm** |

For POS tagging, **Problem 2 (Viterbi)** is the most relevant.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Application in Tagging |
|---|---|---|
| **Markov Assumption** | $P(t_i \mid t_{i-1}^1) \approx P(t_i \mid t_{i-1})$ | Reduces history-dependence |
| **Bigram Tag Sequence Probability** | $P(T) = P(t_1) \prod_{i=2}^{n} P(t_i \mid t_{i-1})$ | Joint tag probability |
| **Bayes' Rule in Tagger** | $T^* = \arg\max_T P(T \mid W) = \arg\max_T P(W \mid T) P(T)$ | Most likely tag sequence |
| **HMM Decomposition** | $P(T \mid W) \propto P(W \mid T) \cdot P(T)$ | Separates emission and transition |
| **Transition Prob.** | $a_{ij} = P(t_j \mid t_i) = \frac{C(t_i, t_j)}{C(t_i)}$ | Tag-to-tag likelihood |
| **Emission Prob.** | $b_i(w_k) = P(w_k \mid t_i) = \frac{C(t_i, w_k)}{C(t_i)}$ | Word-given-tag likelihood |
| **Viterbi Recursion** | $v_t(j) = \max_{i} \left[ v_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij} \cdot b_j(w_t) \right]$ | Best path scoring |
| **Viterbi Backpointer** | $bt_t(j) = \arg\max_{i} \left[ v_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij} \right]$ | Path reconstruction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**: Markov Model Taggers form the foundation of modern NLP systems powering **search engines (Google)**, **voice assistants (Alexa, Siri)**, **machine translation (Google Translate legacy systems)**, **named entity recognition**, and **speech recognition systems**.

### 2.6 Operational Logic Flow of an HMM Tagger

1. **Training Phase**: Use an annotated corpus (e.g., Brown Corpus, Penn Treebank) to compute the entries of matrices $A$ and $B$ via **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)**.
2. **Decoding Phase**: Given a new sentence $W$, apply the **Viterbi Algorithm** to find the optimal tag sequence $T^*$.
3. **Output Phase**: Emit the tag sequence for downstream tasks (parsing, NER, etc.).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Bayes' Rule to the HMM Tagger Equation

We begin with the formal statement of the POS tagging problem. Given a word sequence $W = w_1^n$ and a tag set $T$, we seek:

$$T^* = \arg\max_{T} P(T \mid W)$$

**Step 1**: Apply Bayes' Theorem to the conditional.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(T \mid W) &= \frac{P(W \mid T) \cdot P(T)}{P(W)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual explanation**: Since $P(W)$ is constant across all candidate tag sequences for a fixed input sentence, it does not affect the $\arg\max$ decision. We can safely drop it from the optimization.

**Step 2**: Drop the denominator and rewrite.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T^* &= \arg\max_{T} P(W \mid T) \cdot P(T)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual explanation**: This is the **Noisy Channel Model** of tagging — the tag sequence $T$ is a "hidden cause" that generates the observed word sequence $W$ through a probabilistic "channel" $P(W \mid T)$.

**Step 3**: Decompose $P(W \mid T)$ using the **emission independence assumption** (each word depends only on its own tag, not on neighboring words or tags).

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(W \mid T) &= \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid t_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual explanation**: This is the "Hidden" part of HMM. Each emitted word is conditionally independent of every other word given its own tag.

**Step 4**: Decompose $P(T)$ using the **bigram Markov assumption**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(T) &= P(t_1) \cdot \prod_{i=2}^{n} P(t_i \mid t_{i-1})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual explanation**: The probability of the entire tag sequence is reduced to a product of local tag-transition probabilities. The first tag uses the initial distribution $P(t_1) = \pi(t_1)$.

**Step 5**: Combine Steps 3 and 4 into the **complete HMM tagger equation**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T^* &= \arg\max_{T} \left[ P(t_1) \prod_{i=2}^{n} P(t_i \mid t_{i-1}) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid t_i) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual explanation**: This single equation is the **central object of every HMM POS tagger**. All three HMM problems ultimately revolve around efficiently computing or optimizing this expression.

## 3.2 Worked-Out Example: Viterbi Decoding (Full Calculation)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the most likely Part B (14-mark) question format in KTU ESE. Practice this numerical carefully.

**Given**:
- Tag set: $S = \{N \text{ (Noun)}, V \text{ (Verb)}\}$
- Word sequence to tag: $W = \text{"time flies"}$
- Known parameters:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| $P(N \mid \text{start})$ | 0.7 |
| $P(V \mid \text{start})$ | 0.3 |
| $P(N \mid N)$ | 0.4 |
| $P(V \mid N)$ | 0.6 |
| $P(N \mid V)$ | 0.8 |
| $P(V \mid V)$ | 0.2 |
| $P(\text{time} \mid N)$ | 0.6 |
| $P(\text{time} \mid V)$ | 0.1 |
| $P(\text{flies} \mid N)$ | 0.4 |
| $P(\text{flies} \mid V)$ | 0.9 |

**Task**: Use the **Viterbi Algorithm** to find $T^* = \arg\max_T P(T, W)$.

### Initialization ($t = 1$, word = "time")

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_1(N) &= P(N \mid \text{start}) \cdot P(\text{time} \mid N) = 0.7 \times 0.6 = 0.42 \\
v_1(V) &= P(V \mid \text{start}) \cdot P(\text{time} \mid V) = 0.3 \times 0.1 = 0.03
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual explanation**: The first word's best tag is determined by multiplying the prior probability of the tag with the emission probability of the word.

### Recursion ($t = 2$, word = "flies")

For state $N$ at $t=2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_2(N) &= \max \begin{cases} v_1(N) \cdot P(N \mid N) \cdot P(\text{flies} \mid N) \\ v_1(V) \cdot P(N \mid V) \cdot P(\text{flies} \mid N) \end{cases} \\
v_2(N) &= \max \begin{cases} 0.42 \times 0.4 \times 0.4 = 0.0672 \\ 0.03 \times 0.8 \times 0.4 = 0.0096 \end{cases} \\
v_2(N) &= 0.0672
\end{aligned}
$$

For state $V$ at $t=2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_2(V) &= \max \begin{cases} v_1(N) \cdot P(V \mid N) \cdot P(\text{flies} \mid V) \\ v_1(V) \cdot P(V \mid V) \cdot P(\text{flies} \mid V) \end{cases} \\
v_2(V) &= \max \begin{cases} 0.42 \times 0.6 \times 0.9 = 0.2268 \\ 0.03 \times 0.2 \times 0.9 = 0.0054 \end{cases} \\
v_2(V) &= 0.2268
\end{aligned}
$$

### Termination

$$
\begin{aligned}
T^* &= \arg\max \{ v_2(N), v_2(V) \} = \arg\max \{0.0672, 0.2268\} = V
\end{aligned}
$$

### Backtracking

The best state at $t=2$ is $V$. We look at the backpointer of $v_2(V)$: the best predecessor was $N$ (since $0.42 \times 0.6 > 0.03 \times 0.2$).

**Final Result**:

$$T^* = (N, V) \Rightarrow \text{"time/NOUN flies/VERB"}$$

**Score of optimal path**: $\mathbf{0.2268}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU exams, examiners award **3 marks** for the Viterbi table, **2 marks** for the backpointer matrix, **2 marks** for the recursion formulas, and **2 marks** for the final answer. Show every arithmetic step.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Tagger Variants

| Tagger Type | History Used | Key Formula | Accuracy | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Unigram Tagger** | None (each word independent) | $t_i^* = \arg\max_t P(t \mid w_i)$ | ~90% | Ignores context |
| **Bigram Tagger** | One previous tag | $P(t_i \mid t_{i-1})$ | ~93–95% | Sparse data |
| **Trigram Tagger** | Two previous tags | $P(t_i \mid t_{i-1}, t_{i-2})$ | ~95–96% | Data sparsity worse |
| **HMM Tagger** | Bigram tags + emission | Full HMM equation | ~95–96% | Independence assumption |
| **MEMM / CRF** | Arbitrary features | Log-linear models | ~96–97% | Computationally expensive |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HMM Tagger — Functional Architecture (Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Annotated Training Corpus e.g. Penn Treebank] --> B[Probability Estimation Module]
    B --> C[Transition Matrix A]
    B --> D[Emission Matrix B]
    B --> E[Initial Distribution pi]
    F[New Raw Sentence Input] --> G[Viterbi Decoding Engine]
    C --> G
    D --> G
    E --> G
    G --> H[Optimal Tag Sequence T*]
    H --> I[Downstream NLP Applications]
    I --> J1[Parsing]
    I --> J2[Named Entity Recognition]
    I --> J3[Machine Translation]
    I --> J4[Speech Recognition]
```

## 4.2 Viterbi Trellis Diagram (Mermaid State Graph)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0((START)) --> N1((N: 0.42))
    S0 --> V1((V: 0.03))
    N1 --> N2((N: 0.0672))
    N1 --> V2((V: 0.2268))
    V1 --> N2
    V1 --> V2
    N2 --> END1((END))
    V2 --> END2((END))
    V2 -.Best Path.-> BP[Backpointer: from N1]
```

## 4.3 Markov Model Tagger — Conceptual Layered View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Layer 1: Observable Layer - Words w1 w2 w3 ... wn] --> L2[Layer 2: Hidden Layer - Tags t1 t2 t3 ... tn]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3: Markov Chain Constraint - t_i depends on t_i-1]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4: Emission Constraint - w_i depends on t_i]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5: Output - Maximum Likelihood Path]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]
**State and explain the Markov Assumption used in statistical POS tagging. Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:
- **Definition (1 Mark)**: The Markov Assumption states that the probability of a tag $t_i$ depends only on a limited history of preceding tags:
$$P(t_i \mid t_1, t_2, ..., t_{i-1}) \approx P(t_i \mid t_{i-1})$$
- **First-Order (Bigram) Form (1 Mark)**: The future depends only on the immediate past, reducing the joint probability of a tag sequence to:
$$P(t_1, t_2, ..., t_n) \approx P(t_1) \cdot \prod_{i=2}^{n} P(t_i \mid t_{i-1})$$
- **Necessity (1 Mark)**: Without the assumption, computing $P(T \mid W)$ requires considering all possible tag sequences (exponential in sentence length, $T^n$ combinations). The Markov Assumption makes computation tractable by restricting the conditioning context, and reflects the linguistic fact that grammatical context is largely local.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]
**List and briefly explain the three classical problems of Hidden Markov Models.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 Mark each)**:
1. **Evaluation Problem**: Given the model $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$ and an observation sequence $O$, compute $P(O \mid \lambda)$. *Solved by the Forward-Backward Algorithm.*
2. **Decoding Problem**: Given $\lambda$ and $O$, find the most likely hidden state sequence $Q^*$. *Solved by the Viterbi Algorithm.* — This is the core problem in POS tagging.
3. **Learning Problem**: Given only the observation sequence $O$, find the model parameters $\lambda$ that maximize $P(O \mid \lambda)$. *Solved by the Baum-Welch (Expectation-Maximization) Algorithm.*

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 MARKS — INTERNAL CHOICE)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1 [KTU ESE Style]

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a Hidden Markov Model (HMM) Tagger with a neat block diagram. Define all five parameters of the model. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution**:

An HMM Tagger is a probabilistic sequence model in which the **POS tags form a hidden Markov chain**, and the **words are the observed emissions** from that chain. The architecture consists of three phases:

1. **Training Phase**: An annotated corpus is used to estimate the model parameters.
2. **Decoding Phase**: For a new input sentence, the Viterbi algorithm finds the optimal tag sequence.
3. **Output Phase**: The predicted tags are emitted for downstream applications.

**The Five Parameters of HMM** $\lambda = (S, O, A, B, \pi)$:

| Parameter | Notation | Description |
|---|---|---|
| State set | $S = \{s_1, ..., s_N\}$ | The set of $N$ hidden POS tags |
| Observation set | $O = \{o_1, ..., o_M\}$ | The vocabulary of $M$ words |
| Transition matrix | $A = [a_{ij}]$ | $a_{ij} = P(s_j \mid s_i)$, $N \times N$ matrix |
| Emission matrix | $B = [b_j(k)]$ | $b_j(k) = P(o_k \mid s_j)$, $N \times M$ matrix |
| Initial distribution | $\pi = [\pi_i]$ | $\pi_i = P(s_i \text{ is initial state})$ |

**Diagram**: As shown in SECTION_4 (Block 4.1).

**(b)** Given a small training corpus, compute the transition and emission probabilities for an HMM tagger with tags $S = \{NN, VB\}$ and vocabulary $O = \{\text{"I"}, \text{"see"}, \text{"her"}\}$. Then apply the Viterbi algorithm to find the best tag sequence for the sentence "I see". **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution**:

Assume the following counts from a tiny annotated training corpus:

| Word | Tag | Count |
|---|---|---|
| I | NN | 2 |
| I | VB | 5 |
| see | NN | 1 |
| see | VB | 4 |
| her | NN | 3 |
| her | VB | 2 |

**Tag bigram counts** (assumed):
- $C(\text{start}, NN) = 4$, $C(\text{start}, VB) = 3$
- $C(NN, NN) = 2$, $C(NN, VB) = 5$
- $C(VB, NN) = 1$, $C(VB, VB) = 6$

**Step 1 — Compute Transition Probabilities** (2 Marks):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(NN \mid \text{start}) &= \frac{4}{4+3} = 0.571 \\
P(VB \mid \text{start}) &= \frac{3}{7} = 0.429 \\
P(NN \mid NN) &= \frac{2}{2+5} = 0.286 \\
P(VB \mid NN) &= \frac{5}{7} = 0.714 \\
P(NN \mid VB) &= \frac{1}{7} = 0.143 \\
P(VB \mid VB) &= \frac{6}{7} = 0.857
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Emission Probabilities** (2 Marks):

For tag NN, total count = $C(NN) = 2+1+3 = 6$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{"I"} \mid NN) &= 2/6 = 0.333 \\
P(\text{"see"} \mid NN) &= 1/6 = 0.167 \\
P(\text{"her"} \mid NN) &= 3/6 = 0.500
\end{aligned}
$$

For tag VB, total count = $C(VB) = 5+4+2 = 11$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{"I"} \mid VB) &= 5/11 = 0.455 \\
P(\text{"see"} \mid VB) &= 4/11 = 0.364 \\
P(\text{"her"} \mid VB) &= 2/11 = 0.182
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Viterbi** (3 Marks):

**Initialization** ($t=1$, word = "I"):

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_1(NN) &= P(NN \mid \text{start}) \cdot P(\text{"I"} \mid NN) = 0.571 \times 0.333 = 0.190 \\
v_1(VB) &= P(VB \mid \text{start}) \cdot P(\text{"I"} \mid VB) = 0.429 \times 0.455 = 0.195
\end{aligned}
$$

**Recursion** ($t=2$, word = "see"):

For $v_2(NN)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_2(NN) &= \max \begin{cases} 0.190 \times 0.286 \times 0.167 = 0.00908 \\ 0.195 \times 0.143 \times 0.167 = 0.00466 \end{cases} \\
v_2(NN) &= 0.00908 \quad \text{(from NN)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $v_2(VB)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_2(VB) &= \max \begin{cases} 0.190 \times 0.714 \times 0.364 = 0.04938 \\ 0.195 \times 0.857 \times 0.364 = 0.06083 \end{cases} \\
v_2(VB) &= 0.06083 \quad \text{(from VB)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Termination**:

$$T^* = \arg\max \{0.00908, 0.06083\} = VB$$

**Backtrack**: Best state at $t=1$ was VB (since $0.195 > 0.190$).

**Final Answer**: **"I/VB see/VB"** with score **0.06083**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 [KTU ESE Style]

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **Unigram**, **Bigram**, and **HMM** taggers in terms of methodology, accuracy, and limitations. Which is the most suitable for real-world NLP pipelines? Justify. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution**:

| Aspect | Unigram Tagger | Bigram Tagger | HMM Tagger |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Context** | No context | One previous tag | One previous tag + emission model |
| **Formula** | $P(t \mid w)$ | $P(t_i \mid t_{i-1}) \cdot P(w_i \mid t_i)$ | Full joint $P(W,T)$ via Viterbi |
| **Training** | Simple MLE | MLE on bigrams | MLE on $A$ and $B$ matrices |
| **Accuracy** | ~90% | ~93–95% | ~95–96% |
| **Limitation** | Ignores sequence | Sparse data | Independence assumptions |
| **Unknown Words** | Defaults to most frequent tag | Same issue | Uses emission smoothing |

**Justification (2 Marks)**: The **HMM Tagger** is the most suitable for real-world pipelines because it balances **accuracy, computational efficiency, and robustness to unknown words** (via smoothing techniques like Laplace or deletion-based methods). Modern systems like MEMMs/CRFs surpass HMM, but HMM remains the conceptual foundation.

**(b)** Discuss the **Forward-Backward Algorithm** used in HMM evaluation. Why is direct computation of $P(O \mid \lambda)$ infeasible, and how does the algorithm overcome it? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Why Direct Computation is Infeasible (3 Marks)**:
The probability of an observation sequence $O = o_1 o_2 ... o_T$ given a model $\lambda$ is, by definition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(O \mid \lambda) &= \sum_{Q} P(O, Q \mid \lambda)
\end{aligned}
$$

where the sum is over **all possible state sequences** $Q = q_1 q_2 ... q_T$. If there are $N$ states, there are $N^T$ possible state sequences. For $N=12$ POS tags and $T=10$ words, this is $12^{10} \approx 6.2 \times 10^{10}$ — computationally intractable. A brute-force summation is **exponential** in $T$.

**The Forward Variable $\alpha_t(i)$** (2 Marks):
The forward variable is defined as the probability of observing the partial sequence $o_1 ... o_t$ and being in state $s_i$ at time $t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_t(i) &= P(o_1, o_2, ..., o_t, q_t = s_i \mid \lambda)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Forward Recursion**:

- **Initialization**: $\alpha_1(i) = \pi_i \cdot b_i(o_1)$
- **Recursion**: $\alpha_t(j) = \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij} \right] \cdot b_j(o_t)$
- **Termination**: $P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_T(i)$

**The Backward Variable $\beta_t(i)$** works symmetrically from $t=T$ backwards.

**How it Overcomes Infeasibility (2 Marks)**: By using **dynamic programming**, the algorithm reuses intermediate results and reduces complexity from $O(N^T \cdot T)$ to $O(N^2 \cdot T)$ — a **polynomial-time solution** to an otherwise exponential problem.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**:
> 1. **Forgetting the $\arg\max$ step** in the Viterbi termination — students often compute the max value but forget to identify the *state* that achieves it.
> 2. **Confusing Transition and Emission probabilities** — Transition = tag-to-tag; Emission = word-given-tag. Examiners deduct **2 marks** if these are swapped.
> 3. **Skipping the backpointer table** — Without it, you cannot reconstruct the optimal tag sequence, even with correct Viterbi scores. Always show backpointers.
> 4. **Ignoring Laplace smoothing** — When asked about unknown words, simply writing "default to most frequent tag" loses **1 mark**. Mention at least one smoothing technique (Laplace, Good-Turing, or linear interpolation).
> 5. **Confusing HMM with CRF/MEMM** — KTU 2024 Scheme strictly requires distinguishing generative (HMM) from discriminative (CRF/MEMM) models.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Markov Model Tagger** = statistical POS tagger built on the **Markov Assumption** that limits the conditioning history of tag probabilities.
- **Two flavors**: Visible-state **Markov Chain** (bigram/trigram n-gram taggers) and **Hidden Markov Model (HMM)** — the dominant formulation.
- **HMM Tagger** = 5-tuple $\lambda = (S, O, A, B, \pi)$ where $A$ = transition, $B$ = emission, $\pi$ = initial state distribution.
- **Central equation**: $T^* = \arg\max_T P(T) \cdot P(W \mid T) = \arg\max_T \left[ P(t_1) \prod_{i=2}^n P(t_i \mid t_{i-1}) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^n P(w_i \mid t_i) \right]$
- **Three HMM Problems**: Evaluation (Forward-Backward), Decoding (**Viterbi** — most important for POS), Learning (Baum-Welch).
- **Viterbi Algorithm** = dynamic programming for Problem 2; computes max instead of sum (unlike forward algorithm). Complexity: $O(N^2 \cdot T)$.
- **Limitations to mention in exams**: Markov assumption ignores long-range dependencies; emission independence assumption; sparse-data issues; inability to incorporate rich features (resolved by MEMM/CRF).
- **Smoothing techniques for unknown words**: Laplace (add-one), Good-Turing, linear interpolation, backoff methods.
- **Accuracy benchmarks**: Unigram ~90%, Bigram ~93–95%, HMM ~95–96%, MEMM/CRF ~96–97%, Neural (BERT) ~98%+.
- **Real-world applications**: POS tagging, Named Entity Recognition, Speech Recognition, Bioinformatics (gene prediction), Machine Translation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hidden Markov Model Taggers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hidden Markov Model Taggers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

A **Hidden Markov Model (HMM) Tagger** is a probabilistic sequence model used in Natural Language Processing to assign a Part-of-Speech (POS) tag to every word in a sentence. The model is "hidden" because the true POS tag sequence is never directly observed — only the words (the *emissions* or *observations*) are visible. The tagging problem is therefore reduced to finding the *most probable sequence of hidden states* (tags) that could have generated the observed sequence (words).

Mathematically, an HMM is defined as a 5-tuple:

$$
\lambda = (S,\; O,\; A,\; B,\; \pi)
$$

Where:
- $S = \{s_1, s_2, \dots, s_N\}$ — the finite set of **N** hidden states (POS tags).
- $O = \{o_1, o_2, \dots, o_M\}$ — the finite set of **M** distinct observations (vocabulary words).
- $A = [a_{ij}]$ — the **state transition probability matrix**, where $a_{ij} = P(s_j \mid s_i)$.
- $B = [b_j(k)]$ — the **emission (observation) probability matrix**, where $b_j(k) = P(o_k \mid s_j)$.
- $\pi = [\pi_i]$ — the **initial state distribution**, where $\pi_i = P(s_i)$ is the probability that the first tag is $s_i$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In a *statistical HMM POS tagger*, the **hidden states = POS tags** and the **observations = words**. This single mapping is the foundation for every numerical problem in Module 4. Always state this clearly in your Part-A answers to score full marks.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Weather–Clothes Problem

Imagine you are locked in a basement. You **cannot see the weather** outside (Sunny, Rainy, Cloudy — these are *hidden*). However, you can see what your friend upstairs chooses to **wear** each day (T-shirt, Raincoat, Sweater — these are *visible*). By looking at a sequence of clothes, you try to guess the most likely sequence of weather states.

In a Part-of-Speech (POS) tagger:
- The **weather** = the true POS tag of a word (Noun, Verb, Adjective, …).
- The **clothing** = the actual word appearing in the sentence ("cat", "run", "happy", …).
- Your task = decode the hidden tags from the visible words.

| Domain | Hidden State | Observation |
|---|---|---|
| Weather example | Weather (Sunny/Rainy) | Clothes (T-shirt/Raincoat) |
| **POS Tagging (KTU)** | **POS Tag (N/V/Adj)** | **Word (cat/run/happy)** |

## 1.3 Three Fundamental Problems of HMM (Rabiner, 1989)

The HMM framework is defined by three canonical problems. KTU examiners frequently test these directly:

1. **Problem 1 — Likelihood:** Given model $\lambda$ and observation sequence $O$, compute $P(O \mid \lambda)$. *Solved by the **Forward Algorithm**.*
2. **Problem 2 — Decoding:** Given model $\lambda$ and observation $O$, find the best hidden state sequence. *Solved by the **Viterbi Algorithm**.*
3. **Problem 3 — Learning:** Given observation $O$, learn the parameters $A, B, \pi$. *Solved by the **Baum–Welch Algorithm** (EM).*

> [!NOTE]
> For POS tagging, **Problem 2 (Decoding via Viterbi)** is the operationally central problem — it is the "tagging" step itself. Problems 1 and 3 support model training and probability estimation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Two Independence Assumptions of an HMM Tagger

To make the joint distribution tractable, HMM taggers make two simplifying independence assumptions. KTU values both of these in 7-mark questions.

1. **First-order Markov Assumption (Transition):**
The current tag depends *only* on the immediately previous tag.

$$
P(t_i \mid t_1, t_2, \dots, t_{i-1}) \;\approx\; P(t_i \mid t_{i-1})
$$

2. **Output Independence Assumption (Emission):**
The probability of observing a word depends *only* on the current tag, not on neighbouring words or tags.

$$
P(w_i \mid t_1, \dots, t_i, w_1, \dots, w_{i-1}) \;\approx\; P(w_i \mid t_i)
$$

## 2.2 Joint Probability of a Sentence + Tag Sequence

Using the two assumptions above and the chain rule, the joint probability of an entire tagged sentence $(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n, t_1, t_2, \dots, t_n)$ decomposes as:

$$
P(w_1^n,\; t_1^n) \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid t_i) \cdot P(t_i \mid t_{i-1})
$$

where for $i=1$ we use $P(t_1 \mid t_0) := \pi_{t_1}$. In expanded form:

$$
P(w_1^n,\; t_1^n) \;=\; \pi_{t_1} \cdot b_{t_1}(w_1) \cdot a_{t_1, t_2} \cdot b_{t_2}(w_2) \cdots a_{t_{n-1}, t_n} \cdot b_{t_n}(w_n)
$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Definition |
|---|---|---|
| $\pi_i$ | Initial state probability | $P(t_1 = s_i)$ |
| $a_{ij}$ | Transition probability | $P(t_i = s_j \mid t_{i-1} = s_i)$ |
| $b_j(k)$ | Emission probability | $P(w_i = o_k \mid t_i = s_j)$ |
| $P(O \mid \lambda)$ | Likelihood of observations | Computed by Forward algorithm |
| $V_t(j)$ | Viterbi score (best path score) | $\max_{i} V_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij} \cdot b_j(o_t)$ |
| $B_t(j)$ | Backpointer (best prev. state) | $\arg\max_{i} V_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij} \cdot b_j(o_t)$ |
| $\alpha_t(j)$ | Forward variable | $\sum_i \alpha_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij} \cdot b_j(o_t)$ |
| $\beta_t(i)$ | Backward variable | $\sum_j a_{ij} \cdot b_j(o_{t+1}) \cdot \beta_{t+1}(j)$ |
| $\xi_t(i,j)$ | Posterior of transition at $t$ | $\alpha_t(i) a_{ij} b_j(o_{t+1}) \beta_{t+1}(j) / P(O\mid\lambda)$ |
| $\gamma_t(j)$ | Posterior of state $j$ at $t$ | $\alpha_t(j) \beta_t(j) / P(O \mid \lambda)$ |

> [!TIP]
> In the KTU exam, always show the **arithmetic substitution** of $a_{ij}$ and $b_j(k)$ values from the given table. Examiners award the method mark only when numeric substitution is visible.

## 2.4 Viterbi Algorithm — Operational Logic

The Viterbi algorithm is a **dynamic programming** decoder. The recurrence relation is:

$$
V_t(j) \;=\; \max_{i \,\in\, S} \Big[\, V_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij} \cdot b_j(o_t) \,\Big]
$$

with initialization $V_1(j) = \pi_j \cdot b_j(o_1)$ and a parallel backpointer:

$$
B_t(j) \;=\; \arg\max_{i \,\in\, S} \Big[\, V_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij} \cdot b_j(o_t) \,\Big]
$$

After processing the full sequence of length $n$, the best path probability is $\max_j V_n(j)$ and the best last state is $\arg\max_j V_n(j)$. Back-tracing the backpointers in reverse yields the optimal tag sequence.

## 2.5 Why HMM Taggers Matter in Engineering

HMM taggers form the statistical backbone of many real NLP pipelines:
- **Machine Translation:** POS tags are features used by phrase-based and neural translation systems.
- **Speech Recognition:** HMMs align phonemes to acoustic frames (the classical pre-deep-learning approach).
- **Bioinformatics:** Gene taggers use profile HMMs (e.g., HMMER).
- **Information Extraction:** POS tags help identify named entities, verbs of action, and adjectival modifiers.

## 2.6 Limitations (Frequently Asked in KTU 7-Mark Questions)

| Limitation | Explanation |
|---|---|
| Output independence | Cannot model "the" being more likely before a noun *and* a verb. |
| First-order only | Cannot capture long-range dependencies. |
| Unknown words | Zero emission probability if a word was not in training (mitigated by smoothing / unknown-word handling). |
| Sparse counts | Some tag-tag or tag-word combinations never occur in the corpus. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — Viterbi Decoding (14-Mark Standard)

### 3.1.1 Given Data

A simplified HMM POS tagger is built from a tagged corpus. The tag set is $S = \{\text{N (Noun), V (Verb), D (Determiner)}\}$. The vocabulary is $O = \{\text{the, cat, sat, dog, on, mat}\}$.

**Initial probabilities $\pi$:**

$$
\pi_{\text{D}} = 0.7,\quad \pi_{\text{N}} = 0.2,\quad \pi_{\text{V}} = 0.1
$$

**Transition matrix $A$ (rows = previous tag, columns = next tag):**

| From $\backslash$ To | D | N | V |
|---|---|---|---|
| **D** | 0.0 | **0.9** | 0.1 |
| **N** | 0.1 | 0.4 | **0.5** |
| **V** | 0.2 | 0.6 | 0.2 |

**Emission matrix $B$ (rows = tag, columns = word):**

| Tag | the | cat | sat | dog | on | mat |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **D** | **0.7** | 0.0 | 0.0 | 0.0 | 0.3 | 0.0 |
| **N** | 0.0 | **0.4** | 0.0 | **0.4** | 0.1 | 0.1 |
| **V** | 0.0 | 0.0 | **0.7** | 0.0 | 0.0 | 0.0 |

### 3.1.2 Task

Use the Viterbi algorithm to find the most probable POS tag sequence for the observation sentence:

$$
O = (\text{the, cat, sat, on, mat})
$$

### 3.1.3 Solution — Step 1: Viterbi Initialization ($t = 1$, word = "the")

Initial recurrence: $V_1(j) = \pi_j \cdot b_j(\text{the})$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_1(\text{D}) &= \pi_{\text{D}} \cdot b_{\text{D}}(\text{the}) = 0.7 \times 0.7 = 0.49 \\
V_1(\text{N}) &= \pi_{\text{N}} \cdot b_{\text{N}}(\text{the}) = 0.2 \times 0.0 = 0.0 \\
V_1(\text{V}) &= \pi_{\text{V}} \cdot b_{\text{V}}(\text{the}) = 0.1 \times 0.0 = 0.0
\end{aligned}
$$

Best previous state for each is the only one with non-zero value, so $B_1(\cdot) = \text{N/A}$.

### 3.1.4 Step 2: Recursion at $t = 2$ (word = "cat")

We need $b_j(\text{cat})$: D → 0.0, N → 0.4, V → 0.0.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_2(\text{D}) &= \max_i \big[ V_1(i) \cdot a_{i,\text{D}} \cdot b_{\text{D}}(\text{cat}) \big] \\
              &= \max\big[\,0.49 \times 0.0,\; 0.0 \times 0.1,\; 0.0 \times 0.2\,\big] \cdot 0.0 = 0.0 \\[4pt]
V_2(\text{N}) &= \max_i \big[ V_1(i) \cdot a_{i,\text{N}} \cdot b_{\text{N}}(\text{cat}) \big] \\
              &= \max\big[\,0.49 \times 0.9,\; 0.0 \times 0.4,\; 0.0 \times 0.6\,\big] \times 0.4 \\
              &= 0.441 \times 0.4 = 0.1764 \\
B_2(\text{N}) &= \text{D} \\[4pt]
V_2(\text{V}) &= \max_i \big[ V_1(i) \cdot a_{i,\text{V}} \cdot b_{\text{V}}(\text{cat}) \big] \\
              &= 0.0
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.5 Step 3: Recursion at $t = 3$ (word = "sat")

$b_j(\text{sat})$: D → 0.0, N → 0.0, V → 0.7.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_3(\text{D}) &= 0.0 \\
V_3(\text{N}) &= \max_i \big[ V_2(i) \cdot a_{i,\text{N}} \cdot 0.0 \big] = 0.0 \\
V_3(\text{V}) &= \max_i \big[ V_2(i) \cdot a_{i,\text{V}} \cdot 0.7 \big] \\
              &= \max\big[\,0.0 \times 0.1,\; 0.1764 \times 0.5,\; 0.0 \times 0.2\,\big] \times 0.7 \\
              &= 0.0882 \times 0.7 = 0.06174 \\
B_3(\text{V}) &= \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.6 Step 4: Recursion at $t = 4$ (word = "on")

$b_j(\text{on})$: D → 0.3, N → 0.1, V → 0.0.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_4(\text{D}) &= \max_i \big[ V_3(i) \cdot a_{i,\text{D}} \cdot 0.3 \big] \\
              &= \max\big[\,0.0 \times 0.0,\; 0.0 \times 0.1,\; 0.06174 \times 0.2\,\big] \times 0.3 \\
              &= 0.012348 \times 0.3 = 0.0037044 \\
B_4(\text{D}) &= \text{V} \\[4pt]
V_4(\text{N}) &= \max_i \big[ V_3(i) \cdot a_{i,\text{N}} \cdot 0.1 \big] \\
              &= 0.0 \quad (\text{all } V_3 \text{ values where } a_{i,\text{N}} \neq 0 \text{ yield } 0) \\[4pt]
V_4(\text{V}) &= 0.0
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.7 Step 5: Recursion at $t = 5$ (word = "mat")

$b_j(\text{mat})$: D → 0.0, N → 0.1, V → 0.0.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_5(\text{D}) &= 0.0 \\
V_5(\text{N}) &= \max_i \big[ V_4(i) \cdot a_{i,\text{N}} \cdot 0.1 \big] \\
              &= \max\big[\,0.0,\; 0.0 \times 0.4,\; 0.0037044 \times 0.6\,\big] \times 0.1 \\
              &= 0.00222264 \times 0.1 = 0.000222264 \\
B_5(\text{N}) &= \text{V} \\
V_5(\text{V}) &= 0.0
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.8 Step 6: Termination & Back-Trace

$$
\max_j V_5(j) = V_5(\text{N}) = 0.000222264 \quad\Rightarrow\quad t_5^{*} = \text{N}
$$

Back-tracing through the backpointers:
- $t_5^{*} = \text{N} \;\Rightarrow\; B_5(\text{N}) = \text{V} \;\Rightarrow\; t_4^{*} = \text{V}$
- $t_4^{*} = \text{V} \;\Rightarrow\; B_4(\text{D}) = \text{V}$ — *we needed the D-pointer from $t_4$; backpointer for V at $t_4$*:

Re-checking $V_4(\text{V})$: every candidate path was zero (no $V_3(i) \cdot a_{i,\text{V}}$ was non-zero except $V_3(\text{V}) \cdot a_{\text{V},\text{V}} = 0.06174 \times 0.2 = 0.012348$, but $b_{\text{V}}(\text{on}) = 0$). The backpointer for the path that *led* to V at $t=4$ is still D from the V → ? computation if forced; we record the argmax as $i = \text{V}$ since $0.06174 \times 0.2$ is largest.

Thus $B_4(\text{V}) = \text{V} \;\Rightarrow\; t_3^{*} = \text{V}$
- $t_3^{*} = \text{V} \;\Rightarrow\; B_3(\text{V}) = \text{N} \;\Rightarrow\; t_2^{*} = \text{N}$
- $t_2^{*} = \text{N} \;\Rightarrow\; B_2(\text{N}) = \text{D} \;\Rightarrow\; t_1^{*} = \text{D}$

**Best tag sequence:**

$$
\boxed{\,t^{*} = (\text{D},\; \text{N},\; \text{V},\; \text{P}_{\text{as-verb-context}},\; \text{N}) \;\equiv\; \text{the/D, cat/N, sat/V, on/D, mat/N}\,}
$$

(Where "on" is forced to be a Determiner because no $a_{i,\text{P}}$ exists in our 3-tag system; in richer systems it would be tagged Preposition.)

### 3.1.9 Path Score Summary Table

| $t$ | Word | $V_t(\text{D})$ | $V_t(\text{N})$ | $V_t(\text{V})$ | $B_t$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | the | **0.49** | 0.0 | 0.0 | — |
| 2 | cat | 0.0 | **0.1764** | 0.0 | D→N |
| 3 | sat | 0.0 | 0.0 | **0.06174** | N→V |
| 4 | on | **0.0037044** | 0.0 | 0.0 | V→D |
| 5 | mat | 0.0 | **0.000222264** | 0.0 | V→N |

## 3.2 Derivation of the Forward Algorithm

The forward variable $\alpha_t(j)$ is the probability of being in state $j$ after seeing the first $t$ observations:

$$
\alpha_t(j) = P(o_1, o_2, \dots, o_t,\; t_t = j \mid \lambda)
$$

**Initialization ($t = 1$):**

$$
\alpha_1(j) = \pi_j \cdot b_j(o_1)
$$

**Induction ($t = 2, \dots, n$):**

$$
\alpha_t(j) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij} \cdot b_j(o_t)
$$

**Termination:**

$$
P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_n(j)
$$

**Why this works:** The induction sums over all possible predecessor states $i$. For each $i$, the joint probability of the prefix ending in $i$ is $\alpha_{t-1}(i)$; we then transition to $j$ with $a_{ij}$ and emit $o_t$ with $b_j(o_t)$. Summing over all $i$ gives the total probability of all paths of length $t$ that end in $j$.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Backward Algorithm

The backward variable $\beta_t(i)$ is the probability of generating the *remaining* suffix observations $o_{t+1}, \dots, o_n$ given that we are in state $i$ at time $t$:

$$
\beta_t(i) = P(o_{t+1}, \dots, o_n \mid t_t = i, \lambda)
$$

**Initialization ($t = n$):**

$$
\beta_n(i) = 1
$$

**Induction (running backward):**

$$
\beta_t(i) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} a_{ij} \cdot b_j(o_{t+1}) \cdot \beta_{t+1}(j)
$$

The forward and backward variables combine to give the posterior state probability, which is the cornerstone of the Baum–Welch (EM) re-estimation step.

## 3.4 Maximum Likelihood Estimation of HMM Parameters

Given a tagged training corpus, parameters are estimated by counting (with smoothing to avoid zero probabilities):

$$
\pi_i = \frac{\text{Count}(t_1 = s_i)}{\text{Count}(\text{sentences})}
$$

$$
a_{ij} = \frac{\text{Count}(t_{k-1} = s_i,\; t_k = s_j)}{\text{Count}(t_{k-1} = s_i)}
$$

$$
b_j(v) = \frac{\text{Count}(t_k = s_j,\; w_k = v)}{\text{Count}(t_k = s_j)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For unseen events, KTU answers should mention **Laplace (add-one) smoothing**:
> $\hat{a}_{ij} = \dfrac{\text{Count}(s_i \to s_j) + 1}{\text{Count}(s_i) + N}$ where $N$ is the number of tags.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HMM POS Tagger — Trellis Diagram (Mermaid)

The Viterbi algorithm operates on a *trellis* indexed by time step (words) and state (tags). Each cell stores the best path score; an arrow shows which predecessor state gave that score.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    t1D["t1: D = 0.49"]
    t1N["t1: N = 0.0"]
    t1V["t1: V = 0.0"]
    t2D["t2: D = 0.0"]
    t2N["t2: N = 0.1764"]
    t2V["t2: V = 0.0"]
    t3D["t3: D = 0.0"]
    t3N["t3: N = 0.0"]
    t3V["t3: V = 0.06174"]
    t4D["t4: D = 0.00370"]
    t4N["t4: N = 0.0"]
    t4V["t4: V = 0.0"]
    t5D["t5: D = 0.0"]
    t5N["t5: N = 0.000222"]
    t5V["t5: V = 0.0"]
    t1D --> t2N
    t2N --> t3V
    t3V --> t4D
    t4D --> t5N
```

The bold path (t1D → t2N → t3V → t4D → t5N) is the Viterbi-decoded tag sequence for the sentence *the cat sat on mat*.

## 4.2 HMM Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Training Corpus\n(manually tagged sentences)"]
    B["Parameter Estimator\n(MLE Counts)"]
    C["Transition Matrix A\na_ij = P(t_i \vert t_{i-1})"]
    D["Emission Matrix B\nb_j(k) = P(w_k \vert tag j)"]
    E["Initial Vector pi\npi_i = P(first tag is s_i)"]
    F["HMM Model Lambda = A, B, pi"]
    G["New Sentence\n(un-tagged words)"]
    H["Viterbi Decoder\nDynamic Programming"]
    I["Best Tag Sequence\nargmax P(tags \vert words)"]
    A --> B
    B --> C
    B --> D
    B --> E
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I
```

## 4.3 Forward–Backward Information Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ForwardSweep["Forward Sweep alpha_t"]
        A1["alpha_1 j"] --> A2["alpha_2 j"]
        A2 --> A3["alpha_3 j"]
        A3 --> A4["alpha_n j"]
    end
    subgraph BackwardSweep["Backward Sweep beta_t"]
        B1["beta_n i = 1"] --> B2["beta_n-1 i"]
        B2 --> B3["beta_n-2 i"]
        B3 --> B4["beta_1 i"]
    end
    A4 --> Combine["Combine alpha_t and beta_t"]
    B4 --> Combine
    Combine --> Post["Posterior gamma_t j and xi_t i j"]
    Post --> ReEst["Re-estimate A, B, pi"]
    ReEst -.update.-> ForwardSweep
    ReEst -.update.-> BackwardSweep
```

This block diagram is precisely the *Baum–Welch* (Expectation–Maximization) loop, the standard answer-illustration for 14-mark KTU questions on "how parameters are learned in an unsupervised HMM."
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define an HMM. List its five components. (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> A **Hidden Markov Model** is a probabilistic finite-state machine in which the true state sequence is hidden and only the *emitted observations* are visible. The five components $(\lambda = S, O, A, B, \pi)$ are:
> 1. **$S$** — set of hidden states (POS tags).
> 2. **$O$** — set of observations (vocabulary).
> 3. **$A$** — state transition probability matrix.
> 4. **$B$** — emission probability matrix.
> 5. **$\pi$** — initial state distribution.
> In POS tagging, *states* correspond to tags and *observations* to words. (3 marks)

### Question 2: State and justify the two independence assumptions of an HMM POS tagger. (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**Model Answer:**

1. **First-order Markov assumption:** $P(t_i \mid t_1^{i-1}) \approx P(t_i \mid t_{i-1})$ — the current tag depends only on the previous tag.
2. **Output independence assumption:** $P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1}, t_1^i) \approx P(w_i \mid t_i)$ — a word's emission depends only on the current tag.

*Justification:* These assumptions factor the intractable joint distribution $P(w_1^n, t_1^n)$ into a tractable product, enabling dynamic programming. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) Explain the Viterbi algorithm with the recurrence relation. Why is it preferred over brute-force search? (7 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

The Viterbi algorithm is a dynamic-programming decoder that finds the single best hidden-state sequence for a given observation sequence.

*Recurrence relation:*

$$
V_t(j) = \max_{i \in S}\big[\, V_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij} \cdot b_j(o_t) \,\big], \quad V_1(j) = \pi_j \cdot b_j(o_1)
$$

*Why preferred over brute force:* With $N$ tags and a sentence of length $n$, brute force evaluates $N^n$ paths — infeasible for $N=45$ (Penn Treebank tagset) and $n=20$. Viterbi reduces this to $O(N^2 \cdot n)$ by reusing the optimal substructure: the best path to $(t, j)$ is composed of the best path to $(t-1, i)$ followed by a single transition and emission. (7 marks — 3 marks for recurrence, 2 marks for complexity comparison, 2 marks for "why DP works" justification.)

**(b) Given a small HMM with states = {D, N, V}, observations = {the, cat, sat}, and the following tables, run the Viterbi algorithm to find the best tag sequence for "the cat sat". (7 Marks)**

**Given:**

$$
\pi = (0.7,\; 0.2,\; 0.1)
$$

| $A$ | D | N | V |
|---|---|---|---|
| D | 0.0 | 0.9 | 0.1 |
| N | 0.1 | 0.4 | 0.5 |
| V | 0.2 | 0.6 | 0.2 |

| $B$ | the | cat | sat |
|---|---|---|---|
| D | 0.7 | 0.0 | 0.0 |
| N | 0.0 | 0.4 | 0.0 |
| V | 0.0 | 0.0 | 0.7 |

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Initialization $t=1$ (word = "the"):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_1(\text{D}) &= 0.7 \times 0.7 = 0.49 \\
V_1(\text{N}) &= 0.2 \times 0.0 = 0.0 \\
V_1(\text{V}) &= 0.1 \times 0.0 = 0.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Recursion $t=2$ (word = "cat"):** Emission of "cat" requires $b_{\text{N}}(\text{cat}) = 0.4$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_2(\text{N}) &= \max_i V_1(i) \cdot a_{i,\text{N}} \cdot 0.4 \\
              &= \max\{0.49 \times 0.9,\; 0 \times 0.4,\; 0 \times 0.6\} \times 0.4 \\
              &= 0.441 \times 0.4 = 0.1764
\end{aligned}
$$

Backpointer: $B_2(\text{N}) = \text{D}$. All other cells are 0.

**Recursion $t=3$ (word = "sat"):** Emission of "sat" requires $b_{\text{V}}(\text{sat}) = 0.7$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_3(\text{V}) &= \max_i V_2(i) \cdot a_{i,\text{V}} \cdot 0.7 \\
              &= 0.1764 \times 0.5 \times 0.7 \\
              &= 0.06174
\end{aligned}
$$

Backpointer: $B_3(\text{V}) = \text{N}$.

**Termination and back-trace:** $\max V_3 = V_3(\text{V}) = 0.06174$, last tag is V. Back-tracing: $B_3(\text{V}) = \text{N}$, $B_2(\text{N}) = \text{D}$.

$$
\boxed{\,\text{Best tag sequence: D, N, V (i.e., the/D, cat/N, sat/V) with probability } 0.06174\,}
$$

(7 marks — 2 marks each for $t=1, 2, 3$ and 1 mark for back-trace.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) Derive the Forward algorithm recurrence from first principles. Show how the total likelihood $P(O \mid \lambda)$ is obtained at termination. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Define the **forward variable** $\alpha_t(j) \equiv P(o_1, o_2, \dots, o_t,\; t_t = s_j \mid \lambda)$.

*Base case* ($t = 1$): The probability of seeing $o_1$ and being in state $s_j$ is the product of the initial probability of $s_j$ and the probability of emitting $o_1$ from $s_j$:

$$
\alpha_1(j) = \pi_j \cdot b_j(o_1)
$$

*Inductive step* ($t \ge 2$): To be in state $s_j$ at time $t$ and have observed $o_1 \dots o_t$, we must have been in some predecessor state $s_i$ at $t-1$, transitioned to $s_j$, and emitted $o_t$:

$$
\alpha_t(j) = \Big(\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij}\Big) \cdot b_j(o_t)
$$

The inner sum is the total probability of all paths of length $t-1$ ending in any state, weighted by the transition to $j$.

*Termination:* After processing all $n$ observations, the total probability of the observation sequence is obtained by summing $\alpha_n(j)$ over all possible final states:

$$
P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_n(j)
$$

(7 marks — 2 for base case, 3 for induction with full summation justification, 2 for termination.)

**(b) Explain the Baum–Welch (EM) algorithm for unsupervised HMM parameter learning. Why is closed-form MLE infeasible without tagged data? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

When the training corpus is *untagged* (only words observed), the hidden state sequence is a latent variable and the likelihood $P(O \mid \lambda)$ cannot be maximized in closed form because it is a sum of exponentially many state-sequence paths.

*Expectation step (E-step):* Given current parameters $\lambda^{(k)}$, compute the posterior over hidden states using the forward–backward variables:

$$
\gamma_t(j) = \frac{\alpha_t(j) \beta_t(j)}{P(O \mid \lambda^{(k)})}, \quad \xi_t(i,j) = \frac{\alpha_t(i) a_{ij} b_j(o_{t+1}) \beta_{t+1}(j)}{P(O \mid \lambda^{(k)})}
$$

These are *expected counts* of state occurrences and transitions.

*Maximization step (M-step):* Re-estimate parameters using these expected counts exactly as in MLE:

$$
\hat{\pi}_j = \gamma_1(j), \quad \hat{a}_{ij} = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{n-1} \xi_t(i,j)}{\sum_{t=1}^{n-1} \gamma_t(i)}, \quad \hat{b}_j(v) = \frac{\sum_{t : o_t = v} \gamma_t(j)}{\sum_{t=1}^{n} \gamma_t(j)}
$$

*Why closed-form MLE fails:* Without observed tags, the sufficient statistics for $a_{ij}$ and $b_j(v)$ are *latent* — they must be marginalized over all $N^n$ state sequences, and the resulting likelihood has no analytical maximizer. EM iteratively improves a lower bound (the ELBO) on this intractable likelihood, converging to a local maximum. (7 marks — 3 marks for E-step, 3 for M-step, 1 for justification of intractability.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often confuse **Viterbi** (max-product, finds best path) with the **Forward** algorithm (sum-product, computes total probability). Examiners give **zero credit** if you compute a sum when a max was asked, or vice versa.
> 2. When computing $V_t(j)$ at a non-emittable word (e.g., $b_{\text{D}}(\text{cat}) = 0$), the entire cell becomes **0.0**, *not* the maximum of the rest. The "max" is taken over paths that include the emission.
> 3. In the back-trace, students frequently forget to record the **backpointer** in parallel with $V_t(j)$. Without backpointers, you cannot recover the tag sequence.
> 4. For matrix-based questions, **always restate the row and column headers** ("rows = previous tag, columns = next tag") so the examiner sees the indexing convention.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **HMM = (S, O, A, B, π)**: states, observations, transition, emission, initial.
- **In a POS tagger: states = tags, observations = words.** This is the most exam-critical mapping.
- **Markov assumption 1:** $P(t_i \mid t_1^{i-1}) = P(t_i \mid t_{i-1})$.
- **Output independence:** $P(w_i \mid \text{context}) = P(w_i \mid t_i)$.
- **Joint probability:** $P(w_1^n, t_1^n) = \pi_{t_1} \prod_{i=1}^{n} b_{t_i}(w_i) \cdot a_{t_{i-1}, t_i}$.
- **Viterbi (decoding):** max-product DP, recurrence $V_t(j) = \max_i V_{t-1}(i) a_{ij} b_j(o_t)$.
- **Forward (likelihood):** sum-product DP, recurrence $\alpha_t(j) = \sum_i \alpha_{t-1}(i) a_{ij} b_j(o_t)$.
- **Backward (suffix probability):** $\beta_t(i) = \sum_j a_{ij} b_j(o_{t+1}) \beta_{t+1}(j)$.
- **Baum–Welch = EM**: E-step uses $\gamma_t$ and $\xi_t$; M-step re-estimates $A, B, \pi$.
- **MLE with tagged data:** ratios of co-occurrence counts (with optional Laplace smoothing).
- **Complexity:** Viterbi and Forward are both $O(N^2 \cdot n)$ — far better than $O(N^n)$ brute force.
- **Limitations to mention in answers:** sparse data, unknown words, no long-distance context, output independence.
- **Smoothing technique:** add-one / Laplace for unseen tag-tag or tag-word pairs.
- **Hidden vs. observed:** always state explicitly which quantity is hidden and which is observed; examiners mark this as a separate sub-point.
- **Backpointers:** must be stored *in parallel* with each Viterbi cell to allow back-tracing.
- **Termination:** best path score = $\max_j V_n(j)$; best last tag = $\arg\max_j V_n(j)$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applying HMMs to POS Tagging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Applying HMMs to POS Tagging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — APPLYING HIDDEN MARKOV MODELS TO PART-OF-SPEECH TAGGING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Part-of-Speech (POS) Tagging** is the foundational sequence-labeling task in Computational Linguistics where every word $w_i$ in a sentence $W = w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n$ is assigned a grammatical category $t_i \in T$ from a finite tagset. The joint decision over the entire sentence seeks the optimal tag sequence:

$$T^{*} = \underset{T \in \mathcal{T}^{n}}{\arg\max} \; P(T \mid W)$$

Applying a **Hidden Markov Model (HMM)** to POS tagging means treating the grammatical tags as **hidden Markov states** and the observed words as **emissions** generated by those states. An HMM is formally a 5-tuple:

$$\lambda = (S, \; O, \; A, \; B, \; \pi)$$

where:
- $S = \{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_N\}$ — finite set of **N hidden states** (POS tags)
- $O = \{o_1, o_2, \ldots, o_M\}$ — finite set of **M observation symbols** (vocabulary)
- $A = [a_{ij}]$ — **transition probability matrix** with $a_{ij} = P(s_j \mid s_i)$
- $B = [b_j(k)]$ — **emission (observation) probability matrix** with $b_j(k) = P(o_k \mid s_j)$
- $\pi = [\pi_i]$ — **initial state distribution** with $\pi_i = P(s_i \text{ is initial state})$

> [!IMPORTANT]
