> **KTU Syllabus Anchor — PEADT632 Module 4:** HMMs are the canonical probabilistic generative model for sequence labeling. Students must master the three canonical HMM problems (Evaluation, Decoding, Learning) as they are directly tied to Course Outcome **CO4: Apply statistical models to language processing tasks**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Locked Room" Intuition

Imagine a **locked soundproof room** with a fortune-teller (the **tagger**) who can only emit visible light patterns (the **words** you observe). Outside, you hear nothing about what she is actually doing. Each second she transitions between secret activities — *reading a noun, writing a verb, muttering an adjective* — but you only see a flash of light whose color depends on the activity being performed.

> **You observe:** sequence of light flashes (words)
> **You don't observe:** the activity sequence (tags)
> **You know:** transition tendencies (a noun is often followed by a verb) and emission tendencies (the activity "noun" emits "dog" more often than "the")

Your job is to **infer the most likely secret activity sequence** that explains the light flashes you see. This is exactly the POS-tagging problem solved via HMMs.

> [!NOTE]
> The Markov property states that $P(s_t \mid s_{t-1}, \ldots, s_1) = P(s_t \mid s_{t-1})$ — the future depends only on the present. For POS tagging, this is the *bigram tag assumption*, which is the standard first-order HMM.

### 1.3 Why HMMs Are Suited for POS Tagging

POS tagging exhibits three structural properties that align naturally with HMMs:

1. **Sequence structure** — Tags form a chain respecting word order
2. **Local dependencies** — The tag of the next word strongly depends on the current tag (e.g., determiner $\to$ adjective $\to$ noun)
3. **Indirect observation** — We see words, not tags; tags are latent (hidden)

### 1.4 Standard Tagset and Notation

The most common benchmark tagset is the **Penn Treebank (PTB) tagset** with **45 tags** (e.g., NN, VB, JJ, DT, IN). The vocabulary $|V|$ is typically large (~10,000–100,000 words).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Metric:** For a corpus with $N$ tags and vocabulary $V$, the parameter count of an HMM is $N^2 + N \cdot |V|$. For Penn Treebank this is roughly $45^2 + 45 \cdot 10000 \approx \mathbf{452{,}025}$ parameters, all of which must be estimated from labeled training data.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Canonical HMM Problems (Rabiner, 1989)

Every HMM application — POS tagging included — reduces to solving three problems:

| # | Problem | Mathematical Goal | POS Tagging Equivalent |
|---|---------|-------------------|------------------------|
| **1. Evaluation** | Given $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$ and observation sequence $O$, compute $P(O \mid \lambda)$ | Compute total probability of observed word sequence under the model | Used for **language model perplexity** & likelihood scoring |
| **2. Decoding** | Given $\lambda$ and $O$, find optimal state sequence $S^*$ | $\underset{S}{\arg\max} \; P(S \mid O, \lambda)$ | The **core tagging problem** — solved by **Viterbi Algorithm** |
| **3. Learning** | Given $O$, estimate $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$ to maximize $P(O \mid \lambda)$ | Parameter estimation from (un)labeled data | **Baum-Welch (EM)** for unsupervised, **MLE** for supervised |

### 2.2 Generative Story for POS Tagging

The HMM **generates** a tagged sentence by this stochastic recipe:

1. Sample initial tag $t_1 \sim \pi$
2. For each position $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$:
   - Sample word $w_i \sim B[t_i]$ (emission)
   - Sample next tag $t_{i+1} \sim A[t_i]$ (transition)
3. Joint probability of a tagged sentence:

$$P(w_1, \ldots, w_n, \; t_1, \ldots, t_n) = \pi_{t_1} \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} b_{t_i}(w_i) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n-1} a_{t_i, t_{i+1}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The model is **generative**: it explains how a tagged sentence *could have been produced*. To tag, we must *invert* this process via Bayes' rule.

### 2.3 Bayesian Inversion via Bayes' Rule

By Bayes' rule:

$$P(T \mid W) = \frac{P(W \mid T) \cdot P(T)}{P(W)}$$

Since $P(W)$ is constant across all tag sequences, tagging reduces to:

$$T^{*} = \underset{T}{\arg\max} \; P(W \mid T) \cdot P(T) = \underset{T}{\arg\max} \; \prod_{i=1}^{n} b_{t_i}(w_i) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n-1} a_{t_i, t_{i+1}}$$

The HMM decomposes the joint probability into two components:

- **Transition probability** $a_{t_{i-1}, t_i}$ — captures **syntagmatic** (sequential) tag dependencies
- **Emission probability** $b_{t_i}(w_i)$ — captures **lexical** preferences of each tag

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

| Formula | Expression | Used In | Units / Range |
|---------|-----------|---------|---------------|
| **Initial State Probability** | $\pi_i = P(s_1 = q_i)$ | All problems | $\sum_i \pi_i = 1$ |
| **Transition Probability** | $a_{ij} = P(s_{t+1} = q_j \mid s_t = q_i)$ | Viterbi, Forward | $0 \le a_{ij} \le 1$ |
| **Emission Probability** | $b_j(k) = P(o_t = v_k \mid s_t = q_j)$ | Viterbi, Forward | $0 \le b_j(k) \le 1$ |
| **Joint Sequence Probability** | $P(O, S) = \pi_{s_1} \prod_{t=1}^{T} b_{s_t}(o_t) \prod_{t=1}^{T-1} a_{s_t, s_{t+1}}$ | All problems | $(0, 1]$ |
| **Viterbi Recursion** | $v_t(j) = \max_i \; [v_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij}] \cdot b_j(o_t)$ | Decoding (tagging) | Prob value |
| **Forward Recursion** | $\alpha_t(j) = \left[\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij}\right] \cdot b_j(o_t)$ | Evaluation | Prob value |
| **Backward Recursion** | $\beta_t(i) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} a_{ij} \cdot b_j(o_{t+1}) \cdot \beta_{t+1}(j)$ | Learning (Baum-Welch) | Prob value |
| **Baum-Welch Re-estimation (Transition)** | $\hat{a}_{ij} = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{T-1} \xi_t(i,j)}{\sum_{t=1}^{T-1} \gamma_t(i)}$ | Parameter Learning | Normalized count |
| **Baum-Welch Re-estimation (Emission)** | $\hat{b}_j(k) = \frac{\sum_{t: o_t = v_k} \gamma_t(j)}{\sum_{t=1}^{T} \gamma_t(j)}$ | Parameter Learning | Normalized count |
| **Posterior State Probability** | $\gamma_t(i) = \frac{\alpha_t(i) \beta_t(i)}{P(O \mid \lambda)}$ | Learning | $[0,1]$ |
| **Transition Posterior** | $\xi_t(i,j) = \frac{\alpha_t(i) a_{ij} b_j(o_{t+1}) \beta_{t+1}(j)}{P(O \mid \lambda)}$ | Learning | $[0,1]$ |
| **Laplace (Add-1) Smoothing** | $a_{ij} = \frac{C(s_i \to s_j) + 1}{C(s_i) + N}$ | Smoothing unseen events | Ratio $\in [0,1]$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Numerical Underflow Alert:** Direct multiplication of many small probabilities underflows IEEE-754 doubles within ~30–50 timesteps. Always operate in **log-space** for production taggers:
> $$\log P = \log \pi_{t_1} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log b_{t_i}(w_i) + \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \log a_{t_i, t_{i+1}}$$

### 2.5 The Viterbi Algorithm — Decoding for POS Tagging

Viterbi finds $T^{*} = \arg\max_T P(T \mid W)$ in **$O(N^2 \cdot n)$** time using dynamic programming. It maintains:

- $v_t(j)$ — probability of the best path ending in state $j$ at time $t$
- $bp_t(j)$ — backpointer storing the predecessor state that gave the optimum

**Viterbi Recursion:**

$$v_t(j) = \max_{1 \le i \le N} \big[\, v_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij}\,\big] \cdot b_j(o_t)$$

**Initialization ($t=1$):**

$$v_1(j) = \pi_j \cdot b_j(o_1), \quad bp_1(j) = 0$$

**Termination:**

$$P^{*} = \max_{j} v_n(j), \quad t_n^{*} = \arg\max_{j} v_n(j)$$

**Backtrace:** Recover $t_{n-1}^{*}, t_{n-2}^{*}, \ldots, t_1^{*}$ by following $bp$ pointers from $t_n^{*}$.

### 2.6 The Forward Algorithm — Evaluation

$$\alpha_t(j) = P(o_1, o_2, \ldots, o_t, \; s_t = q_j \mid \lambda)$$

$$\alpha_t(j) = \left[\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij}\right] \cdot b_j(o_t)$$

Final sentence probability: $P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_n(j)$.

### 2.7 The Backward Algorithm

$$\beta_t(i) = P(o_{t+1}, \ldots, o_n \mid s_t = q_i, \lambda)$$

$$\beta_t(i) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} a_{ij} \cdot b_j(o_{t+1}) \cdot \beta_{t+1}(j)$$

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use of HMM-POS Tagger |
|--------------------|-----------------------|
| **Information Extraction** | Tagging precedes NER and relation extraction |
| **Speech Recognition** | POS tags constrain acoustic language models |
| **Machine Translation** | Source-side POS tagging guides word alignment |
| **Sentiment Analysis** | Adjective/verb tags enrich feature engineering |
| **Question Answering** | Syntactic POS patterns detect question types |
| **Corpus Linguistics** | Frequency analysis of grammatical categories |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Bayesian Tagger to HMM Viterbi

**Starting principle:** A POS tagger must find:

$$T^{*} = \underset{t_1, \ldots, t_n}{\arg\max} \; P(t_1, \ldots, t_n \mid w_1, \ldots, w_n)$$

**Step 1 — Apply Bayes' rule** to invert the conditional:

$$P(T \mid W) = \frac{P(W \mid T) \cdot P(T)}{P(W)}$$

**Step 2 — Drop the evidence $P(W)$** since it is constant for all tag sequences:

$$T^{*} = \underset{T}{\arg\max} \; P(W \mid T) \cdot P(T)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the HMM generative factorization** (Markov + observation independence):

$$P(W, T) = P(t_1) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid t_i) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n-1} P(t_{i+1} \mid t_i)$$

Therefore:

$$P(W \mid T) \cdot P(T) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} b_{t_i}(w_i) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n-1} a_{t_i, t_{i+1}} \cdot \pi_{t_1}$$

**Step 4 — Rewrite the arg-max product** by converting to log-space for numerical stability:

$$T^{*} = \underset{T}{\arg\max} \left[ \log \pi_{t_1} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log b_{t_i}(w_i) + \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \log a_{t_i, t_{i+1}} \right]$$

**Step 5 — Recognize that naive enumeration** over $N^n$ tag sequences is intractable. Define the **Viterbi trellis**:

$$v_t(j) = \max_{t_1, \ldots, t_{t-1}} P(t_1, \ldots, t_{t-1}, \; t_t = j, \; w_1, \ldots, w_t \mid \lambda)$$

**Step 6 — Derive the recursion** by conditioning on the previous state:

$$v_t(j) = \max_{i} \Big[\, v_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij}\,\Big] \cdot b_j(w_t)$$

This step uses the Markov property: only the previous state matters for the next transition.

**Step 7 — Track backpointers** so we can recover the optimal sequence:

$$bp_t(j) = \underset{i}{\arg\max} \; v_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — HMM POS Tagger

**Setup:** Consider a miniature tagger for the sentence *"Book the flight"*.

**Tag inventory:** $S = \{N \text{ (Noun)}, \; V \text{ (Verb)}\}$ with $N=2$ tags.

**Vocabulary:** $V = \{\text{book, the, flight, ...}\}$

**Estimated parameters from training:**

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} a_{NN} & a_{NV} \\ a_{VN} & a_{VV} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 & 0.7 \\ 0.8 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\pi = [\pi_N, \pi_V] = [0.6, \; 0.4]$$

**Emission probabilities $b_{\text{tag}}(\text{word})$:**

| | book | the | flight |
|---|---|---|---|
| **N** | 0.5 | 0.4 | 0.1 |
| **V** | 0.3 | 0.0 | 0.0 |

(Note: *"the"* cannot be emitted by Verb in this toy model.)

**Step 1 — Initialization ($t = 1$, word = "book"):**

$$v_1(N) = \pi_N \cdot b_N(\text{book}) = 0.6 \cdot 0.5 = 0.30, \quad bp_1(N) = -$$

$$v_1(V) = \pi_V \cdot b_V(\text{book}) = 0.4 \cdot 0.3 = 0.12, \quad bp_1(V) = -$$

**Step 2 — Recursion at $t = 2$, word = "the":**

$$v_2(N) = \max_{i \in \{N, V\}} \big[\, v_1(i) \cdot a_{iN}\,\big] \cdot b_N(\text{the})$$

From N: $v_1(N) \cdot a_{NN} = 0.30 \cdot 0.3 = 0.090$
From V: $v_1(V) \cdot a_{VN} = 0.12 \cdot 0.8 = 0.096$ ✓

Therefore $v_2(N) = 0.096 \cdot 0.4 = 0.0384, \; bp_2(N) = V$.

$$v_2(V) = \max_i v_1(i) \cdot a_{iV} \cdot b_V(\text{the})$$

From N: $0.30 \cdot 0.7 \cdot 0.0 = 0$
From V: $0.12 \cdot 0.2 \cdot 0.0 = 0$

Therefore $v_2(V) = 0, \; bp_2(V) = -$ (state V is impossible at this point).

**Step 3 — Recursion at $t = 3$, word = "flight":**

$$v_3(N) = \max_i \big[\, v_2(i) \cdot a_{iN}\,\big] \cdot b_N(\text{flight})$$

From N: $v_2(N) \cdot a_{NN} = 0.0384 \cdot 0.3 = 0.01152$ ✓
From V: $v_2(V) \cdot a_{VN} = 0 \cdot 0.8 = 0$

Therefore $v_3(N) = 0.01152 \cdot 0.1 = 0.001152, \; bp_3(N) = N$.

$$v_3(V) = \max_i v_2(i) \cdot a_{iV} \cdot b_V(\text{flight})$$

From N: $0.0384 \cdot 0.7 \cdot 0.0 = 0$
From V: $0 \cdot 0.2 \cdot 0.0 = 0$

Therefore $v_3(V) = 0, \; bp_3(V) = -$.

**Step 4 — Termination:**

$$P^{*} = \max\{v_3(N), v_3(V)\} = 0.001152$$

$$t_3^{*} = N$$

**Step 5 — Backtrace:**

$$t_2^{*} = bp_3(t_3^{*}) = bp_3(N) = N$$
$$t_1^{*} = bp_2(t_2^{*}) = bp_2(N) = V$$

**Final tag sequence:** *(V, N, N)* → **"Book/Verb the/Det flight/Noun"** (using N here as the determiner-like position is oversimplified, but V N N is the Viterbi output). The model correctly identifies *"book"* as a verb given the determiner-like context.

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** The tagger correctly disambiguates *"book"* — when followed by *"the"*, it is a verb; when followed by *"flight"*, it could be noun-verb-noun or verb-noun-noun. The Viterbi path selected $V \to N \to N$.

### 3.3 Derivation: Forward Algorithm Identity

We prove that the forward and backward algorithms together yield $P(O \mid \lambda)$:

$$P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_t(j) \cdot \beta_t(j) \quad \text{for any } t \in \{1, \ldots, n\}$$

**Proof sketch:**

$$\alpha_t(j) \cdot \beta_t(j) = P(O_1^t, s_t = j \mid \lambda) \cdot P(O_{t+1}^n \mid s_t = j, \lambda)$$

$$= P(O, s_t = j \mid \lambda)$$

Summing over all possible $j$:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_t(j) \cdot \beta_t(j) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} P(O, s_t = j \mid \lambda) = P(O \mid \lambda) \quad \blacksquare$$

### 3.4 Derivation: Maximum Likelihood Estimation (Supervised Case)

When we have a labeled corpus with tags, parameters are estimated by **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)**:

**Transition probability** — fraction of times tag $j$ follows tag $i$:

$$a_{ij} = \frac{C(i \to j)}{C(i)} = \frac{\text{count of tag } i \text{ followed by } j}{\text{count of tag } i}$$

**Emission probability** — fraction of times word $w$ appears with tag $j$:

$$b_j(w) = \frac{C(j \to w)}{C(j)} = \frac{\text{count of word } w \text{ tagged as } j}{\text{count of tag } j}$$

**Initial probability:**

$$\pi_i = \frac{C(\text{sentences starting with tag } i)}{\text{total sentences}}$$

**Smoothing (Laplace / Add-1):** Handles unseen tag-tag or tag-word pairs:

$$a_{ij}^{\text{smoothed}} = \frac{C(i \to j) + 1}{C(i) + N}, \quad b_j(w)^{\text{smoothed}} = \frac{C(j \to w) + 1}{C(j) + |V|}$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — HMM Tagger Strengths and Limitations

| Property | Strength | Limitation |
|----------|----------|------------|
| **Inference Speed** | Viterbi is $O(N^2 n)$ — linear in sentence length | Quadratic in tagset size |
| **Observation Independence** | Tractable inference | Cannot model word context (a word's identity depends on neighbors) |
| **Markov Order 1** | Captures local tag patterns | Cannot model long-range dependencies (e.g., subject-verb agreement across 10 words) |
| **Generative Story** | Strong probabilistic foundation | Outperformed by **discriminative** models like **CRF** and **MEMM** |
| **Unknown Words** | Emission smoothing via add-1, suffix-based heuristics | Limited morphology modeling |
| **Parameter Estimation** | Closed-form MLE on labeled data | Need labeled corpus (expensive) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern Context:** Although **Conditional Random Fields (CRFs)** and **BiLSTM** models now dominate POS tagging, the HMM remains the *theoretical foundation* and is still examinable in KTU 2024 Scheme papers. CRFs relax the observation-independence assumption and are *discriminative* ($P(T \mid W)$ directly) rather than generative.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define a Hidden Markov Model (HMM). List its five formal parameters and explain how each maps to the POS tagging problem.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Hidden Markov Model is a doubly stochastic process in which the system being modeled is assumed to be a Markov chain with unobserved (hidden) states and observations that are random variables whose distribution depends on the current hidden state.

**The five formal parameters** $\lambda = (S, O, A, B, \pi)$:

| Parameter | POS Tagging Mapping |
|-----------|---------------------|
| $S$ — set of $N$ hidden states | The set of POS tags (e.g., NN, VB, JJ) |
| $O$ — set of $M$ observations | The vocabulary of words |
| $A = [a_{ij}]$ — transition matrix | Probability of tag $j$ following tag $i$: $P(t_{k+1} \mid t_k)$ |
| $B = [b_j(k)]$ — emission matrix | Probability of word $w_k$ being emitted by tag $j$: $P(w \mid t)$ |
| $\pi = [\pi_i]$ — initial distribution | Probability that sentence starts with tag $i$ |

**(1 mark)** for definition; **(1 mark)** for listing parameters; **(1 mark)** for POS mapping table.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between the generative approach (HMM) and discriminative approach (CRF) for POS tagging.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | HMM (Generative) | CRF (Discriminative) |
|--------|------------------|----------------------|
| Objective | Model $P(W, T)$ jointly, then invert via Bayes | Model $P(T \mid W)$ directly |
| Independence Assumption | Observations conditionally independent given tags | No observation-independence assumption |
| Feature Engineering | Limited to transition + emission | Arbitrary overlapping features |
| Accuracy (typical) | ~93–95% | ~96–97% |
| Training | Closed-form MLE / Baum-Welch | Numerical optimization (L-BFGS) |

**(3 marks)** awarded for correct distinction across all rows.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

**Choose ONE of the following:**

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the three fundamental problems of HMMs with reference to POS tagging. For each problem, name the algorithm that solves it and state its time complexity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the following HMM parameters for a toy tagger with tags $\{N, V\}$ and observations $\{\text{book, the, flight}\}$:

$$\pi = [0.6, \; 0.4], \quad A = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 & 0.7 \\ 0.8 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix}$$

| | book | the | flight |
|---|---|---|---|
| **N** | 0.5 | 0.4 | 0.1 |
| **V** | 0.3 | 0.0 | 0.0 |

Use the Viterbi algorithm to find the most likely tag sequence for the sentence *"Book the flight"*. Show all trellis values $v_t(j)$ and backpointers. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a) [7 marks]**

The three canonical HMM problems (per Rabiner, 1989) are:

**1. Evaluation Problem** — *Given* $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$ and observation sequence $O = o_1, o_2, \ldots, o_n$, *compute* $P(O \mid \lambda)$. **(1 mark)**

- **Algorithm:** Forward (or Forward–Backward)
- **Complexity:** $O(N^2 \cdot n)$
- **POS Tagging Relevance:** Used to compute the total likelihood of a sentence under a tagger model; useful for language-model perplexity and minimum Bayes-risk decoding. **(1 mark)**

**2. Decoding Problem** — *Given* $\lambda$ and $O$, *find* the optimal state sequence $S^{*} = \arg\max_S P(S \mid O, \lambda)$. **(1 mark)**

- **Algorithm:** Viterbi Algorithm (dynamic programming)
- **Complexity:** $O(N^2 \cdot n)$
- **POS Tagging Relevance:** This is *the* tagging problem. Given a sentence, find the single best tag sequence. **(1 mark)**

**3. Learning Problem** — *Given* only observations $O$, *estimate* $\lambda^{*} = \arg\max_\lambda P(O \mid \lambda)$. **(1 mark)**

- **Algorithm:** Baum–Welch (a special case of Expectation-Maximization)
- **Complexity:** $O(N^2 \cdot n)$ per iteration; converges in $k$ iterations
- **POS Tagging Relevance:** Unsupervised HMM training; in practice, POS taggers use **supervised MLE** when labeled data is available. **(1 mark)**

**Synthesis (1 mark):** POS tagging fundamentally reduces to **Problem 2 (Decoding)** via Viterbi. Problems 1 and 3 serve auxiliary roles in likelihood evaluation and unsupervised parameter estimation respectively.

---

##### **MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b) [7 marks]**

We compute the Viterbi trellis step-by-step. Let $t=1$ for "book", $t=2$ for "the", $t=3$ for "flight".

**Initialization ($t=1$, word = "book"):** **(1 mark)**

$$v_1(N) = \pi_N \cdot b_N(\text{book}) = 0.6 \times 0.5 = 0.30, \quad bp_1(N) = -$$

$$v_1(V) = \pi_V \cdot b_V(\text{book}) = 0.4 \times 0.3 = 0.12, \quad bp_1(V) = -$$

**Recursion ($t=2$, word = "the"):** **(2 marks)**

For state $N$:
- From N: $v_1(N) \cdot a_{NN} = 0.30 \times 0.3 = 0.090$
- From V: $v_1(V) \cdot a_{VN} = 0.12 \times 0.8 = 0.096$ ✓

$$v_2(N) = 0.096 \times b_N(\text{the}) = 0.096 \times 0.4 = 0.0384, \quad bp_2(N) = V$$

For state $V$:
- From N: $0.30 \times 0.7 \times b_V(\text{the}) = 0.21 \times 0 = 0$
- From V: $0.12 \times 0.2 \times 0 = 0$

$$v_2(V) = 0, \quad bp_2(V) = -$$

**Recursion ($t=3$, word = "flight"):** **(2 marks)**

For state $N$:
- From N: $v_2(N) \cdot a_{NN} = 0.0384 \times 0.3 = 0.01152$ ✓
- From V: $v_2(V) \cdot a_{VN} = 0 \times 0.8 = 0$

$$v_3(N) = 0.01152 \times b_N(\text{flight}) = 0.01152 \times 0.1 = 0.001152, \quad bp_3(N) = N$$

For state $V$:
- From N: $0.0384 \times 0.7 \times 0 = 0$
- From V: $0 \times 0.2 \times 0 = 0$

$$v_3(V) = 0, \quad bp_3(V) = -$$

**Termination and Backtrace:** **(1 mark)**

$$P^{*} = \max\{v_3(N), v_3(V)\} = 0.001152$$

$$t_3^{*} = N, \quad t_2^{*} = bp_3(N) = N, \quad t_1^{*} = bp_2(N) = V$$

**Final Viterbi tag sequence:** $T^* = (V, N, N)$

The HMM POS tagger tags "Book" as **Verb**, "the" as **Noun** (proxy for determiner in this toy model), and "flight" as **Noun**. The optimal sentence probability is $P^* = 0.001152$.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) procedure for estimating HMM parameters $(A, B, \pi)$ from a labeled POS corpus. Why is smoothing necessary, and write the Laplace (Add-1) smoothing equations for transition and emission probabilities. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the Forward Algorithm recursion for an HMM. Show that the forward variable $\alpha_t(j)$ equals $P(o_1, \ldots, o_t, s_t = q_j \mid \lambda)$. Using the Forward–Backward identity, prove that $P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{j} \alpha_t(j) \beta_t(j)$ for any $t$. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**MLE Estimation Procedure:** **(3 marks)**

Given a labeled corpus $\mathcal{D} = \{(W^{(k)}, T^{(k)})\}_{k=1}^{K}$:

**Initial probabilities:**

$$\pi_i = \frac{\text{Number of sentences starting with tag } i}{\text{Total number of sentences}}$$

**Transition probabilities:**

$$a_{ij} = \frac{\text{Number of times tag } j \text{ follows tag } i}{\text{Number of times tag } i \text{ appears}}$$

**Emission probabilities:**

$$b_j(w) = \frac{\text{Number of occurrences of word } w \text{ tagged as } j}{\text{Number of occurrences of tag } j}$$

These are the MLE estimates that maximize the log-likelihood of the labeled data.

**Why Smoothing is Necessary:** **(2 marks)**

In any finite training corpus, there will be unseen tag-tag and tag-word pairs. Without smoothing:
- An unseen transition $a_{ij}$ becomes **0**, zeroing out entire paths in Viterbi
- An unseen emission $b_j(w_{\text{unknown}})$ becomes **0**, making tagging of unknown words impossible

Hence, even valid sentences can receive zero probability. Smoothing redistributes probability mass to unseen events.

**Laplace (Add-1) Smoothing Equations:** **(2 marks)**

$$a_{ij}^{\text{smoothed}} = \frac{C(i \to j) + 1}{C(i) + N}$$

$$b_j(w)^{\text{smoothed}} = \frac{C(j \to w) + 1}{C(j) + \vert V \vert}$$

where $N$ is the number of tags, $\vert V \vert$ is the vocabulary size.

---

##### **MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Forward Variable Definition:** **(1 mark)**

$$\alpha_t(j) = P(o_1, o_2, \ldots, o_t, \; s_t = q_j \mid \lambda)$$

**Derivation of the Recursion:** **(3 marks)**

By conditioning on the previous state $s_{t-1} = q_i$:

$$\alpha_t(j) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} P(o_1, \ldots, o_{t-1}, s_{t-1} = q_i \mid \lambda) \cdot P(s_t = q_j \mid s_{t-1} = q_i, \lambda) \cdot P(o_t \mid s_t = q_j, \lambda)$$

$$\alpha_t(j) = \left[\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij}\right] \cdot b_j(o_t)$$

This uses the Markov property and the conditional independence of observations given the current state.

**Initialization** ($\alpha_1(j) = \pi_j \cdot b_j(o_1)$) and **Termination** ($P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_j \alpha_n(j)$) complete the algorithm.

**Proof of the Forward–Backward Identity:** **(3 marks)**

By definition:
$$\alpha_t(j) \cdot \beta_t(j) = P(o_1, \ldots, o_t, s_t = q_j \mid \lambda) \cdot P(o_{t+1}, \ldots, o_n \mid s_t = q_j, \lambda)$$

Using the chain rule of probability and the Markov property:
$$= P(o_1, \ldots, o_n, s_t = q_j \mid \lambda) = P(O, s_t = q_j \mid \lambda)$$

Summing over all states $j$:
$$\sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_t(j) \cdot \beta_t(j) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} P(O, s_t = q_j \mid \lambda) = P(O \mid \lambda) \quad \blacksquare$$

This identity holds for any $t \in \{1, \ldots, n\}$ and is the foundation of the Baum–Welch re-estimation formulas.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in HMM POS Tagging Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to drop $P(W)$ in Bayes' rule.** Many students keep the denominator, which is constant — but writing the correct arg-max form earns full marks.
> 2. **Confusing forward ($\alpha$) and Viterbi ($v$) variables.** Forward uses **sum** (marginalization); Viterbi uses **max** (best path). Mixing them is a 2–3 mark deduction.
> 3. **Numerical underflow in raw product.** Always state that log-space computation is necessary for long sentences.
> 4. **No backpointer storage.** Viterbi requires $bp_t(j)$ to recover the path. Reporting only $v_n(j)$ without backtracing loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Treating HMM as discriminative.** Students often write $P(T \mid W)$ as if directly modeled; remember the HMM models $P(W, T)$ and applies Bayes.
> 6. **Skipping MLE counts.** When asked to estimate parameters, write the explicit ratio of counts, not just the formula symbol.
> 7. **Tagset confusion.** Mention the **Penn Treebank (45 tags)** when discussing real-world POS tagging, not just abstract tags.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ HMM for POS tagging = **(hidden)** tag sequence + **(observed)** word sequence + **Markov-1 transition** + **observation independence**
- ✅ Five parameters: $\lambda = (S, O, A, B, \pi)$ — know each symbol's meaning cold
- ✅ **Three HMM problems** = Evaluation (Forward), Decoding (Viterbi), Learning (Baum–Welch)
- ✅ POS tagging is fundamentally the **Decoding** problem: $T^* = \arg\max_T P(T \mid W)$
- ✅ Bayes' rule converts the joint generative model $P(W,T)$ into the discriminative decision $P(T \mid W)$
- ✅ **Viterbi** = DP with max-replacement; **Forward** = DP with sum-replacement
- ✅ Viterbi **complexity** = $O(N^2 \cdot n)$ — linear in sentence length, quadratic in tagset
- ✅ Store **backpointers** $bp_t(j)$ in Viterbi — you cannot backtrace without them
- ✅ Use **log-space** to prevent numerical underflow during probability multiplication
- ✅ **MLE** for supervised training; **Baum–Welch (EM)** for unsupervised training
- ✅ **Laplace (Add-1) smoothing** is mandatory to handle unseen tag-tag and tag-word events
- ✅ HMM is **generative**; CRFs and MEMMs are **discriminative** alternatives (often more accurate)
- ✅ Penn Treebank has **45 tags**; standard tagger accuracy is **~95–97%**
- ✅ Standard metric: total HMM parameters $\approx N^2 + N \cdot \vert V \vert \approx \mathbf{452{,}000}$ for PTB
- ✅ The Markov assumption limits HMMs from modeling long-range dependencies (subject-verb agreement over many tokens)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Probabilistic Context Free Grammars
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Probabilistic Context Free Grammars

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Probabilistic Context Free Grammar (PCFG)** is a five-tuple $G = (N, \Sigma, R, S, P)$ where:

- **$N$** — A finite set of **non-terminal symbols** (syntactic categories such as NP, VP, S).
- **$\Sigma$** — A finite set of **terminal symbols** (lexical items / words), disjoint from $N$.
- **$R$** — A finite set of **production rules** of the form $A \to \alpha$, where $A \in N$ and $\alpha \in (N \cup \Sigma)^*$.
- **$S$** — A designated **start symbol**, with $S \in N$.
- **$P$** — A probability assignment function $P : R \to [0, 1]$ such that for every $A \in N$:

$$\sum_{A \to \alpha \in R} P(A \to \alpha) = 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The sum-to-one constraint is the **defining algebraic property** of any valid PCFG. Every KTU exam answer that fails to mention this axiom is considered incomplete.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Grammar Lottery"

Imagine that every time a non-terminal symbol (say a **Noun Phrase**, NP) needs to "rewrite" itself in a sentence, it does not follow a single rigid rule. Instead, it **draws a lottery ticket** among all its possible expansions. Each rule has a *weight* (its probability) that controls how often that expansion is chosen. 

For example, an English VP is statistically far more likely to expand as `VP → V NP` ("eats the apple") than as `VP → V` ("eats" with no object). The PCFG just formalizes this "weighted lottery" mathematically. If we generate a million sentences and parse them, the *relative frequency* of each rule used converges to its assigned probability.

## 1.3 The Two Central Probabilistic Computations

A PCFG lets us answer two fundamentally different questions for any sentence $w$:

| Computation | Question Answered | Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Viterbi (Best Parse)** | What is the single *most likely* syntactic tree? | $\hat{t} = \arg\max_{t \in T(w)} P(t \mid w)$ |
| **Inside Algorithm (Sentence Probability)** | How likely is the sentence under this grammar? | $P(w) = \sum_{t \in T(w)} P(t)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> Both quantities are required by the **KTU 2024 PEADT632 Module 4 syllabus** and are tested regularly in Part B questions worth 14 marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a PCFG Rule

A PCFG production is a **stochastic pair**:

$$A \xrightarrow{\,p\,} \alpha, \quad p = P(A \to \alpha) \in [0, 1]$$

### Example Mini-Grammar (Toy English)

| Rule ID | Production | Probability |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | S → NP VP | **0.80** |
| 2 | S → VP | **0.20** |
| 3 | NP → Det N | **0.65** |
| 4 | NP → N | **0.35** |
| 5 | VP → V NP | **0.60** |
| 6 | VP → V | **0.40** |
| 7 | Det → the | **0.70** |
| 8 | Det → a | **0.30** |
| 9 | N → man | **0.50** |
| 10 | N → apple | **0.50** |
| 11 | V → eats | **1.00** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification:** For every non-terminal on the LHS, the probabilities of all its productions sum to **1.00** (e.g., $0.80 + 0.20 = 1.0$ for S; $0.65 + 0.35 = 1.0$ for NP). KTU examiners often include a *"Verify the grammar is valid"* sub-question worth 2 marks.

## 2.2 Probability of a Parse Tree

Given a complete parse tree $t$ for sentence $w$, the tree's probability is the **product** of the probabilities of every rule used in the tree:

$$P(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(R_i)$$

where $R_i$ is the $i$-th rule application in tree $t$ and $n$ is the total number of rule applications (i.e., the number of internal nodes).

### Conditional Independence Assumption (The "Naïve" Property)

A PCFG assumes that the probability of a rule application **does not depend on the surrounding context** outside its parent non-terminal. This is called the **context-free independence assumption**:

$$P(A \to \alpha \mid \text{rest of tree}) = P(A \to \alpha)$$

> [!WARNING]
> This is the *biggest weakness* of PCFGs and is the entry point to advanced models like **Lexicalized PCFGs (Charniak 1997)** and **Dependency PCFGs (Klein & Manning 2003)**. KTU Module 4 often concludes with a 3-mark "Discuss the limitations" question that tests exactly this point.

## 2.3 Probability of a String (Disambiguation)

A valid sentence $w$ may have **multiple parse trees** $T(w) = \{t_1, t_2, \dots, t_k\}$. The PCFG assigns a probability to each, and the sentence's total probability is the **sum over all valid trees**:

$$P(w) = \sum_{t \in T(w)} P(t)$$

The tree with the highest probability becomes the **most likely parse**, disambiguating the sentence.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sum_{\alpha} P(A \to \alpha) = 1$ | Sum-to-one axiom per non-terminal | Grammar validation |
| 2 | $P(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(R_i)$ | Probability of a parse tree | Tree scoring |
| 3 | $P(w) = \sum_{t \in T(w)} P(t)$ | Total probability of a string | Disambiguation |
| 4 | $\beta(A, i, j) = P(w_{i+1..j} \mid A)$ | **Inside** probability of deriving span $i+1..j$ from $A$ | CYK + Inside algorithm |
| 5 | $\alpha(A, i, j) = P(w_{1..i}, A \text{ generates } i{+}1..j, w_{j+1..m})$ | **Outside** probability of surrounding context | Inside-Outside algorithm |
| 6 | $P(w) = \beta(S, 0, n)$ | String probability from inside of start symbol | Final answer |
| 7 | $P(A \to BC) = \dfrac{\beta(A, i, j)}{\sum_{k} \beta(A, k, k)}$ | Re-estimation (EM / Inside-Outside) | Grammar induction |
| 8 | $\hat{t} = \arg\max_{t} P(t)$ | Best (Viterbi) parse | Parsing |

> [!NOTE]
> All probabilities are **dimensionless** (pure ratios in $[0, 1]$). They are **not** log-probabilities, so multiplications are *valid* but quickly underflow numerically — in practice, engineers work in log-space (base $e$ or base 2).

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

PCFGs are the **workhorse of statistical parsing** and feed directly into:

- **Constituency parsers** (Stanford Parser, Berkeley Parser).
- **Machine Translation** (syntax-based MT systems).
- **Information Extraction** (rule extraction from parse trees).
- **Grammatical Error Correction** in NLP pipelines.
- **Speech Recognition** language models that incorporate syntactic structure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Computing $P(t)$ for a Full Parse Tree

Consider the sentence $w = \text{``the man eats the apple''}$ and its single parse tree $t$ under the toy grammar above.

### 3.1.1 Tree Structure

$$
S
\;\longrightarrow\; NP \; VP
$$

$$
NP \longrightarrow Det \; N, \quad NP \longrightarrow Det \; N
$$

$$
VP \longrightarrow V \; NP
$$

The terminal leaves (bottom-up) are: `the` — `man` — `eats` — `the` — `apple`.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Rule Application

Each rule used in the tree is multiplied:

$$
P(t) = P(S \to NP\; VP) \cdot P(NP \to Det\; N) \cdot P(Det \to \text{the}) \cdot P(N \to \text{man})
$$
$$
\cdot\, P(VP \to V\; NP) \cdot P(V \to \text{eats}) \cdot P(NP \to Det\; N) \cdot P(Det \to \text{the}) \cdot P(N \to \text{apple})
$$

### 3.1.3 Numerical Substitution

Now substitute the values from the grammar table:

$$
P(t) = (0.80) \cdot (0.65) \cdot (0.70) \cdot (0.50) \cdot (0.60) \cdot (1.00) \cdot (0.65) \cdot (0.70) \cdot (0.50)
$$

Group the terms for clarity:

$$
P(t) = \underbrace{0.80}_{S \to NP\,VP} \cdot \underbrace{(0.65 \cdot 0.70 \cdot 0.50)}_{NP \to Det\,N,\,the,man} \cdot \underbrace{0.60}_{VP \to V\,NP} \cdot \underbrace{1.00}_{V \to eats} \cdot \underbrace{(0.65 \cdot 0.70 \cdot 0.50)}_{NP \to Det\,N,\,the,apple}
$$

Compute each group:

$$
(0.65 \cdot 0.70) = 0.455
$$

$$
(0.455 \cdot 0.50) = 0.2275
$$

Therefore:

$$
P(t) = 0.80 \times 0.2275 \times 0.60 \times 1.00 \times 0.2275
$$

$$
P(t) = 0.80 \times 0.60 \times (0.2275)^2
$$

$$
P(t) = 0.48 \times 0.05175625
$$

$$
\boxed{P(t) = 0.024843}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> If the sentence had **two** valid parse trees $t_1, t_2$, the final answer for the sentence's probability would be $P(w) = P(t_1) + P(t_2)$, **not** a product — a classic trap that costs students 2–3 marks.

## 3.2 Inside Algorithm Derivation (CYK-style)

The **Inside probability** $\beta(A, i, j)$ is defined as the probability that non-terminal $A$ derives the substring $w_{i+1} \dots w_j$.

### 3.2.1 Base Case (length-1 spans)

For a terminal rule $A \to w_{i+1}$:

$$
\beta(A, i, i+1) = P(A \to w_{i+1})
$$

### 3.2.2 Recursive Case (longer spans)

For a binary rule $A \to B\,C$ splitting at position $k$:

$$
\beta(A, i, j) = \sum_{k=i+1}^{j-1} P(A \to B\,C) \cdot \beta(B, i, k) \cdot \beta(C, k, j)
$$

### 3.2.3 Worked Numerical Example

Suppose $w = \text{``a man''}$ (length 2). The grammar has $S \to NP\; VP$ etc., but let us focus only on deriving the substring `a man` from NP.

Spans to compute:

- $\beta(Det, 0, 1) = P(Det \to a) = 0.30$
- $\beta(N, 1, 2) = P(N \to man) = 0.50$
- $\beta(NP, 0, 2) = P(NP \to Det\,N) \cdot \beta(Det, 0, 1) \cdot \beta(N, 1, 2)$

Substitute:

$$
\beta(NP, 0, 2) = 0.65 \times 0.30 \times 0.50 = 0.0975
$$

This single number now represents the probability that the NP covers the entire span `a man`. It will be reused by every parent node (such as S) that has NP as a child.

## 3.3 Comparison Matrix — Inside vs. Viterbi

| Property | Inside Algorithm | Viterbi Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| **Objective** | Sum over *all* parses | Max over *all* parses |
| **Combine step** | $\sum$ (addition) | $\max$ (maximization) |
| **Back-pointers stored?** | No | Yes (one best split per cell) |
| **Output** | $P(w)$ | Best tree $\hat{t}$ |
| **Complexity** | $O(n^3 \cdot \vert N \vert^3)$ | Same |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PCFG Parsing Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Input Sentence w] --> B0[Tokenize w into w1..wn]
    B0 --> C0[Initialize CYK Table T 0..n x 0..n]
    C0 --> D0[For each span length l = 1..n]
    D0 --> E0[For each position i]
    E0 --> F0[For each non-terminal A in N]
    F0 --> G0{A is a pre-terminal?}
    G0 -->|Yes| H0[Set beta A,i,i+1 = P A to wi+1]
    G0 -->|No| I0[For each binary rule A to B C]
    I0 --> J0[For each split k]
    J0 --> K0[Accumulate P A to B C times beta B,i,k times beta C,k,j]
    H0 --> L0[Cell T i,j updated]
    K0 --> L0
    L0 --> M0[All spans processed]
    M0 --> N0[Read beta S,0,n as P w]
    M0 --> O0[Trace back-pointers for Viterbi tree]
    N0 --> P0[Output P w and best parse t-hat]
    O0 --> P0
```

## 4.2 Probability Flow Through a Parse Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS["S  start"] --> nodeNP1["NP  0.65"]
    nodeS --> nodeVP["VP  0.60"]
    nodeNP1 --> nodeDet1["Det  0.70"]
    nodeNP1 --> nodeN1["N  0.50"]
    nodeDet1 --> leaf1["the"]
    nodeN1 --> leaf2["man"]
    nodeVP --> nodeV["V  1.00"]
    nodeVP --> nodeNP2["NP  0.65"]
    nodeV --> leaf3["eats"]
    nodeNP2 --> nodeDet2["Det  0.70"]
    nodeNP2 --> nodeN2["N  0.50"]
    nodeDet2 --> leaf4["the"]
    nodeN2 --> leaf5["apple"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Each internal node in the tree diagram above is labeled with the **rule probability used to expand it**. The total $P(t)$ is the product of all these labels, exactly as derived in Section 3.1.

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart — When to Use Inside vs. Viterbi

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Goal?] --> Q2{Disambiguate one tree}
    Q1 --> Q3{Compute P of w}
    Q2 -->|Yes| R1[Use Viterbi Algorithm]
    Q2 -->|No| R2[Need total likelihood]
    Q3 -->|Yes| R3[Use Inside Algorithm]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Need rule re-estimation}
    Q4 -->|Yes| R4[Use Inside-Outside EM Loop]
    Q4 -->|No| R5[Use CYK for membership only]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a Probabilistic Context Free Grammar (PCFG). State the fundamental probability constraint that every PCFG must satisfy.**

**Model Answer:**

A PCFG is a 5-tuple $G = (N, \Sigma, R, S, P)$ where each rule $A \to \alpha \in R$ is assigned a probability $P(A \to \alpha) \in [0, 1]$. The fundamental constraint is that **for every non-terminal $A \in N$, the probabilities of all its productions must sum to 1**:

$$
\sum_{A \to \alpha \in R} P(A \to \alpha) = 1
$$

This ensures that the grammar forms a valid probability distribution over derivations.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between the probability of a parse tree $P(t)$ and the probability of a sentence $P(w)$ in a PCFG.**

**Model Answer:**

- $P(t)$ is the **product** of the probabilities of all rules used in a *single* parse tree $t$.
- $P(w)$ is the **sum** of $P(t)$ over **all valid parse trees** $T(w)$ that yield $w$.

Mathematically:

$$
P(t) = \prod_{i} P(R_i) \qquad\qquad P(w) = \sum_{t \in T(w)} P(t)
$$

$P(t)$ ranks individual trees, while $P(w)$ is the total generative likelihood of the string.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Consider the PCFG with the following rules. The non-terminals are $\{S, NP, VP\}$, terminals are $\{a, b, c\}$, and $S$ is the start symbol.

| Rule | Probability |
|---|---|
| $S \to NP\; VP$ | 0.7 |
| $S \to VP$ | 0.3 |
| $NP \to a$ | 0.4 |
| $NP \to b$ | 0.6 |
| $VP \to c$ | 0.5 |
| $VP \to NP\; VP$ | 0.5 |

Compute the probability of the sentence $w = \text{``a c''}$ by summing over **all possible parse trees**. **Show every intermediate step.** (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the **Inside Algorithm** for PCFGs. Write down the recursive formula for $\beta(A, i, j)$ and clearly state the base case. Briefly state its time complexity. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a)

The sentence $w = a\; c$ has length 2. We must enumerate all derivations rooted at $S$ that produce this string.

**Tree 1 ($t_1$): $S \to NP\; VP$, then $NP \to a$, $VP \to c$**

- Uses rules: $S \to NP\,VP$, $NP \to a$, $VP \to c$.
- Probabilities: $0.7, 0.4, 0.5$.

$$
P(t_1) = 0.7 \times 0.4 \times 0.5 = 0.14
$$

**Tree 2 ($t_2$): $S \to VP \to c$, but $VP$ must also produce $a$ first** — Not possible because $VP \to c$ produces only a single terminal and cannot yield the string $a\,c$ by itself.

**Tree 3 ($t_3$): $S \to VP \to NP\; VP \to a\; c$**

- Uses rules: $S \to VP$, $VP \to NP\,VP$, $NP \to a$, $VP \to c$.
- Probabilities: $0.3, 0.5, 0.4, 0.5$.

$$
P(t_3) = 0.3 \times 0.5 \times 0.4 \times 0.5 = 0.03
$$

**Total sentence probability:**

$$
P(w) = P(t_1) + P(t_3) = 0.14 + 0.03 = \boxed{0.17}
$$

#### Model Solution for (b)

The **Inside Algorithm** computes $\beta(A, i, j)$, the total probability that non-terminal $A$ derives the substring $w_{i+1} \dots w_j$.

**Base case (span of length 1):**

$$
\beta(A, i, i+1) = P(A \to w_{i+1})
$$

**Recursive case (span of length $> 1$):** For each binary rule $A \to B\,C$ and each split point $k$ with $i < k < j$:

$$
\beta(A, i, j) = \sum_{k=i+1}^{j-1} P(A \to B\,C) \cdot \beta(B, i, k) \cdot \beta(C, k, j)
$$

**Final answer extraction:**

$$
P(w) = \beta(S, 0, n)
$$

**Time complexity:** $O(n^3 \cdot \vert N \vert^3)$ where $n$ is sentence length and $\vert N \vert$ is the number of non-terminals.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Given the parse forest for sentence $w = \text{``the dog''}$ containing exactly two trees $t_1$ and $t_2$ with $P(t_1) = 0.012$ and $P(t_2) = 0.008$:

1. Compute $P(w)$.
2. Identify the **Viterbi parse** and justify.
3. State which tree a PCFG-based parser would output and why. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss **three major limitations** of standard PCFGs. For each limitation, briefly suggest an extension or a more advanced model that addresses it. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a)

**1. Sentence probability:**

$$
P(w) = P(t_1) + P(t_2) = 0.012 + 0.008 = \boxed{0.020}
$$

**2. Viterbi parse:**

The Viterbi (best) parse is the tree with the **highest** probability:

$$
\hat{t} = \arg\max_{t \in \{t_1, t_2\}} P(t) = t_1 \quad \text{since } P(t_1) = 0.012 > 0.008 = P(t_2)
$$

**3. Parser output:** A standard **PCFG constituency parser** would output $t_1$ because parsers are typically designed to recover the *single most probable* parse tree (the Viterbi tree), not the sum over all trees.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Limitation 1 — Lack of lexical conditioning:**
PCFGs condition only on non-terminals, ignoring the actual words. This causes the model to incorrectly prefer an attachment based on structure rather than semantics (e.g., the classical *prepositional phrase attachment* ambiguity).
*Extension:* **Lexicalized PCFGs (Collins, Charniak)** attach head words to rules and condition on them.

**Limitation 2 — Context-free independence assumption:**
The probability of a rule is assumed independent of its surrounding tree context, which is unrealistic.
*Extension:* **Discriminative parsers** and **neural PCFGs (e.g., RNNG, Dyer et al.)** relax this assumption.

**Limitation 3 — Sparse data / unseen rules:**
A vanilla PCFG cannot handle words or constructions absent from training data (zero-probability problem).
*Extension:* **Smoothing techniques** (e.g., lexical smoothing, Witten-Bell) or **subword embeddings** in neural variants.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. In Part B (a) numerical questions, **always** show the rule probabilities multiplied step-by-step. Writing only the final number loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. In limitation questions, students often *list* drawbacks without *naming an extension*. KTU's model answer scheme allocates 1 mark per named extension — omitting it costs full marks.
> 3. Do not confuse $P(t)$ (product, one tree) with $P(w)$ (sum, all trees). Mixing them up is the most common board-exam error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- PCFG = **CFG + rule probabilities** with the **sum-to-one axiom** per non-terminal.
- **Tree probability** = **product** of all rule probabilities used.
- **String probability** = **sum** of tree probabilities over all valid parses.
- **Inside algorithm** $\beta(A, i, j)$ computes total probability of a span.
- **Outside algorithm** $\alpha(A, i, j)$ is its dual and is needed for **EM-based grammar learning** (Inside-Outside algorithm).
- **Viterbi parse** = tree with **maximum** $P(t)$, not maximum $P(w)$.
- **Best parse ≠ most probable derivation of $w$** — the Viterbi tree is the most probable *single tree*, while $P(w)$ sums over all.
- **Complexity** of both Inside and Viterbi is **$O(n^3)$** in sentence length.
- **Key weakness:** context-free independence — no lexical/semantic conditioning.
- **Modern extensions:** Lexicalized PCFGs, Dependency PCFGs, Neural PCFGs (RNNG), and Transformer-based parsers.
- **Engineering tip:** Always work in **log-space** to avoid floating-point underflow when multiplying many small probabilities.
- **Parse disambiguation** is the *primary practical application* of PCFGs in real-world NLP systems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Some Features of PCFGs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Some Features of PCFGs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of PCFGs

A **Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar (PCFG)** is a five-tuple $(N, T, S, R, P)$ where:

- $N$ is a finite set of **non-terminal symbols**
- $T$ is a finite set of **terminal symbols**
- $S \in N$ is a designated **start symbol**
- $R$ is a finite set of **production rules** of the form $A \rightarrow \beta$ where $A \in N$ and $\beta \in (N \cup T)^*$
- $P$ is a function that assigns a **probability** to each rule such that for every non-terminal $A$:

$$\sum_{A \rightarrow \beta \in R} P(A \rightarrow \beta) = 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** A PCFG extends a Context-Free Grammar (CFG) by attaching a probability to every production rule. The probabilities of all rules expanding a given non-terminal must sum to **1**. This ensures the PCFG defines a valid probability distribution over the (possibly infinite) set of parse trees and strings.

## 1.2 What are "Features" of PCFGs?

In the context of KTU Module 4, "**Some Features of PCFGs**" refers to the characteristic **mathematical properties**, **probability computation mechanisms**, and **inherent modelling assumptions** of PCFGs. These features dictate how probabilities are derived, how trees and strings are scored, and what the grammar can and cannot capture.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Restaurant Menu with Prices**
> Imagine a CFG as a recipe book describing *which* ingredients (terminals) can be combined into *which* dishes (non-terminals). A PCFG is that same recipe book, but with **prices (probabilities) attached to each ingredient combination step**. For instance, "Pizza → Dough + Sauce + Cheese" might be a common (high probability) rule, while "Pizza → Caviar + Gold Leaf" is rare (low probability).
> The **features of PCFGs** are the rules of this pricing system: how to compute the total price of a complete dish (probability of a parse tree), how to know if a dish is fairly priced (normalization), and the assumption that the choice of sauce is **independent** of the choice of topping (the famous independence assumption we will study next).

## 1.4 The Key Probability Metrics

Three metrics are central to PCFGs:

1. **Probability of a parse tree $t$ for a string $s$** — denoted $P(t, s)$
2. **Probability of a string $s$** — denoted $P(s)$, obtained by summing over all valid parse trees
3. **Most likely parse tree** — $\arg\max_t P(t \mid s)$, found via algorithms like the **CKY (Cocke-Kasami-Younger)** algorithm with Viterbi scoring

## 1.5 Outline of PCFG Features Discussed

The five high-yield features of PCFGs that KTU expects students to master are:

- **Feature 1:** The *decomposition formula* for $P(t, s)$ using rule probabilities
- **Feature 2:** The *derivation probability* summed over complete left-most derivations
- **Feature 3:** The *prior probability of a string* $P(s)$ via sum over all parse trees
- **Feature 4:** The *conditional independence assumption* (subtree independence)
- **Feature 5:** *Limitations* of standard PCFGs (motivation for lexicalized PCFGs)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Feature 1: Probability of a String-Parse Tree Pair

Given a parse tree $t$ for a string $s$, $t$ contains a multiset of rule applications $\{r_1, r_2, \ldots, r_n\}$. The probability of the tree is the **product** of the probabilities of all rules used to build it:

$$P(t, s) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(r_i)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a product?** Each rule application is treated as an **independent event** (the central PCFG assumption). The joint probability of independent events is the product of their individual probabilities. This makes the score of a tree decomposable into local, context-free rule scores.

## 2.2 Feature 2: Probability of a Left-Most Derivation

A *left-most derivation* is a sequence of rule applications where at each step the **left-most non-terminal** is expanded. The probability of a derivation $d = (r_1, r_2, \ldots, r_n)$ is also the product of rule probabilities:

$$P(d) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(r_i)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equivalence Theorem (KTU Favourite):** For a PCFG, there is a **one-to-one correspondence** between parse trees and left-most derivations. Therefore:
>
> $$P(t, s) = P(d)$$
>
> where $d$ is the unique left-most derivation producing $t$ for $s$.

## 2.3 Feature 3: Prior Probability of a String

The probability of a string $s$ is the sum of the probabilities of **all parse trees** that generate $s$. If $T(s)$ is the set of all valid parse trees for $s$:

$$P(s) = \sum_{t \in T(s)} P(t, s)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** This formula is the backbone of language modelling in NLP, speech recognition (acoustic model rescoring), and machine translation. Computing $P(s)$ tells the system how "grammatical" or "expected" a sentence is in the language.

## 2.4 Feature 4: The Conditional Independence Assumption

The most discussed feature of PCFGs is the **independence assumption**: the choice of how to expand a non-terminal $A$ depends **only on $A$** and on **no other context**.

**Formal Statement:** Given any internal node $A$ in a parse tree, the subtree rooted at $A$ is **conditionally independent** of the rest of the tree given the label $A$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** Students often think PCFGs capture context because they extend CFGs. **They do not.** A PCFG rule $A \rightarrow B\ C$ does not condition on the parent of $A$, the siblings of $A$, or the words under $A$. This is the *price* of keeping inference tractable (polynomial in $n^3$ via CKY).

## 2.5 Feature 5: Normalization of PCFG

A PCFG is a valid probabilistic model if and only if the rule probabilities form a **conditional probability distribution** for every non-terminal. This guarantees:

$$\sum_{\beta} P(A \rightarrow \beta) = 1 \quad \forall A \in N$$

Consequence: $P(s)$ summed over **all possible strings** $s \in T^*$ equals **1**, i.e., the PCFG defines a proper probability distribution over the language.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Tree-String Joint Probability | $P(t, s) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(r_i)$ | Product over rules in $t$ |
| 2 | Derivation Probability | $P(d) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(r_i)$ | $d$ is a left-most derivation |
| 3 | Equivalence | $P(t, s) = P(d)$ | One-to-one tree/derivation map |
| 4 | String Probability | $P(s) = \sum_{t \in T(s)} P(t, s)$ | Sum over all parse trees |
| 5 | Normalization Constraint | $\sum_{\beta} P(A \rightarrow \beta) = 1$ | For every $A \in N$ |
| 6 | CKY Inside Probability | $\alpha_{i,j}(A) = P(A \Rightarrow^* w_i \ldots w_j)$ | $i, j$ span indices |
| 7 | CKY Recursion | $\alpha_{i,j}(A) = \sum_{k} \sum_{B, C} P(A \rightarrow B\,C) \cdot \alpha_{i,k}(B) \cdot \alpha_{k+1,j}(C)$ | For $i \le k < j$ |
| 8 | Conditional Independence | $P(t_1, t_2 \mid A) = P(t_1 \mid A) P(t_2 \mid A)$ | Subtrees under $A$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas power **syntactic parsers** in real-world NLP pipelines (e.g., constituency parsers used in sentiment analysis, information extraction, and grammar checking tools). The independence assumption enables $O(n^3)$ CKY parsing, which is the reason PCFGs remained practical before deep learning.

## 2.7 Why Study These Features?

- They explain **why** PCFGs work and **where** they break.
- They motivate advanced models: **Lexicalized PCFGs (Collins 1997, 1999)**, **Probabilistic Lexicalized Tree-Adjoining Grammars (LTAG)**, and **Dependency PCFGs**.
- KTU frequently tests the *derivation* formulas and the *independence* property in Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Toy PCFG

Let the PCFG be defined as:

| Rule | Probability |
|------|-------------|
| $S \rightarrow NP\ VP$ | $1.0$ |
| $NP \rightarrow Det\ N$ | $0.6$ |
| $NP \rightarrow Det\ Adj\ N$ | $0.4$ |
| $VP \rightarrow V\ NP$ | $1.0$ |
| $Det \rightarrow the$ | $0.7$ |
| $Det \rightarrow a$ | $0.3$ |
| $N \rightarrow boy$ | $0.5$ |
| $N \rightarrow girl$ | $0.5$ |
| $Adj \rightarrow big$ | $0.6$ |
| $Adj \rightarrow small$ | $0.4$ |
| $V \rightarrow sees$ | $1.0$ |

### Derivation 3.1.1 — Compute $P(t, s)$ for the sentence "the boy sees a girl" with the obvious parse tree.

**Step 1 — Identify the parse tree $t$.** The unique parse tree expands:

$$S \Rightarrow NP\ VP \Rightarrow Det\ N\ V\ Det\ N \Rightarrow the\ boy\ sees\ a\ girl$$

**Step 2 — Enumerate the rules used in $t$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_1 &: S \rightarrow NP\ VP \quad (P = 1.0) \\
r_2 &: NP \rightarrow Det\ N \quad (P = 0.6) \\
r_3 &: VP \rightarrow V\ NP \quad (P = 1.0) \\
r_4 &: Det \rightarrow the \quad (P = 0.7) \\
r_5 &: N \rightarrow boy \quad (P = 0.5) \\
r_6 &: Det \rightarrow a \quad (P = 0.3) \\
r_7 &: N \rightarrow girl \quad (P = 0.5)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Tree-String Probability formula.** Compute the product:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(t, s) &= \prod_{i=1}^{7} P(r_i) \\
&= 1.0 \times 0.6 \times 1.0 \times 0.7 \times 0.5 \times 0.3 \times 0.5 \\
&= 0.6 \times 0.7 \times 0.5 \times 0.3 \times 0.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply stepwise.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.6 \times 0.7 &= 0.42 \\
0.42 \times 0.5 &= 0.21 \\
0.21 \times 0.3 &= 0.063 \\
0.063 \times 0.5 &= 0.0315
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:**

$$P(t, s) = 0.0315$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The probability of producing the parse tree yielding "the boy sees a girl" under this PCFG is **0.0315** (≈ 3.15%). This is the joint probability of the string *and* the specific syntactic structure chosen.

---

### Derivation 3.1.2 — Compute $P(s)$ for "the boy sees a girl" by summing over all parse trees.

**Step 1 — Enumerate all valid parse trees $T(s)$.** The sentence has 2 valid parse trees corresponding to the two expansions of $NP$:

- $T_1$: $NP \rightarrow Det\ N$ for both NPs (no adjectives)
- $T_2$: $NP \rightarrow Det\ N$ for subject, $NP \rightarrow Det\ Adj\ N$ for object

(Note: $NP \rightarrow Det\ Adj\ N$ cannot apply to *subject* "the boy" because that branch would require an adjective between "the" and "boy", which is not in the string. Thus, only the object NP can be the adjectival one.)

**Step 2 — Compute $P(t_1, s)$ — both NPs use $NP \rightarrow Det\ N$.** Rules:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_1 &: S \rightarrow NP\ VP \quad (1.0) \\
r_2 &: NP \rightarrow Det\ N \quad (0.6) \\
r_3 &: VP \rightarrow V\ NP \quad (1.0) \\
r_4 &: Det \rightarrow the \quad (0.7) \\
r_5 &: N \rightarrow boy \quad (0.5) \\
r_6 &: Det \rightarrow a \quad (0.3) \\
r_7 &: N \rightarrow girl \quad (0.5)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(t_1, s) &= 1.0 \times 0.6 \times 1.0 \times 0.7 \times 0.5 \times 0.3 \times 0.5 \\
&= 0.0315
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $P(t_2, s)$ — object NP uses $NP \rightarrow Det\ Adj\ N$.** New rules added for adjectival expansion:

- $r_2' : NP \rightarrow Det\ N$ (subject, $P = 0.6$)
- $r_8 : Adj \rightarrow small$ or $r_8' : Adj \rightarrow big$ ... but in the string "a girl", there is **no adjective**. Therefore $t_2$ **is not a valid tree** for this exact string.

> [!NOTE]
> **Correction:** For the string "the boy sees a girl", only $T_1$ is a valid parse tree, so $P(s) = P(t_1, s) = 0.0315$.

**Step 3 (revised) — Use a string with two valid trees, "the small boy sees a girl".**

- $T_1$: $NP_{subj} \rightarrow Det\ Adj\ N$ with "small"; $NP_{obj} \rightarrow Det\ N$ with "girl".
- $T_2$: $NP_{subj} \rightarrow Det\ N$ — *invalid* (cannot skip "small")

Actually only $T_1$ is valid here. **Let us use a richer example: "the big boy sees the small girl".** Both NPs are ambiguous.

- $T_1$: subject = "big boy" via $Det\ Adj\ N$; object = "small girl" via $Det\ Adj\ N$.
- $T_2$: subject = "big boy" via $Det\ Adj\ N$; object = "girl" via $Det\ N$ — invalid (string has "small").
- $T_3$: subject = "boy" via $Det\ N$ — invalid (string has "big").

So only $T_1$ is valid in this string as well.

**A truly ambiguous case:** Consider the string "the boy sees a big girl". Here the subject is "the boy" (unambiguous) and the object can be parsed as:

- $T_1$: $NP_{obj} \rightarrow Det\ Adj\ N$ with "big" → $P(t_1, s)$ uses $r: NP \rightarrow Det\ Adj\ N$ ($0.4$).
- $T_2$: $NP_{obj} \rightarrow Det\ N$ with "girl" and "big" as a separate modifier → *invalid without an extra $AdjP$ rule.*

Hence, for our toy grammar, $P(s) = 0.0315$ for "the boy sees a girl".

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** When asked to compute $P(s)$, **always enumerate every valid parse tree first**, then sum. Do *not* assume uniqueness. A common student error is to stop after the first tree.

---

### Derivation 3.1.3 — Verify the Independence Assumption

**Step 1 — State the assumption.** Subtrees under the same parent non-terminal are conditionally independent. For a rule $A \rightarrow B\ C\ D$ with subtrees $t_B, t_C, t_D$:

$$P(t_B, t_C, t_D \mid A) = P(t_B \mid A) \cdot P(t_C \mid A) \cdot P(t_D \mid A)$$

**Step 2 — Demonstrate with a numerical check.** Consider the subtrees under $S$ in our example:

- Subtree 1 (NP spanning "the boy"): probability $0.6 \times 0.7 \times 0.5 = 0.21$.
- Subtree 2 (VP spanning "sees a girl"): probability $1.0 \times 0.3 \times 0.5 = 0.15$.

**Step 3 — Multiply assuming independence under $S$:**

$$
P(t_1, t_2 \mid S) = P(t_1 \mid S) \cdot P(t_2 \mid S)
$$

Since the rule $S \rightarrow NP\ VP$ has probability $1.0$, the joint probability is the product of subtree probabilities:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(t, s) &= P(S \rightarrow NP\ VP) \cdot P(t_1 \mid S) \cdot P(t_2 \mid S) \\
&= 1.0 \times 0.21 \times 0.15 \\
&= 0.0315
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** This matches the direct product from Derivation 3.1.1, confirming the conditional independence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The PCFG says that **the choice of the subject NP is independent of the choice of the VP**, given that the parent is $S$. This is computationally efficient but linguistically unrealistic — a constraint like subject-verb agreement (*boys see* vs *boy sees*) cannot be captured.

---

### Derivation 3.1.4 — Verify Normalization

**Step 1 — Sum rules expanding $NP$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{\beta} P(NP \rightarrow \beta) &= P(NP \rightarrow Det\ N) + P(NP \rightarrow Det\ Adj\ N) \\
&= 0.6 + 0.4 \\
&= 1.0 \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum rules expanding $Det$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{\beta} P(Det \rightarrow \beta) &= P(Det \rightarrow the) + P(Det \rightarrow a) \\
&= 0.7 + 0.3 \\
&= 1.0 \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum rules expanding $N$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{\beta} P(N \rightarrow \beta) &= P(N \rightarrow boy) + P(N \rightarrow girl) \\
&= 0.5 + 0.5 \\
&= 1.0 \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Every non-terminal's rules sum to $1.0$, confirming the PCFG is **properly normalized**.

---

### Derivation 3.1.5 — Limitation: Why Standard PCFGs Fail

Consider the sentences:

- (a) "the boy sees the boy" — Subject=NP, Object=NP, both lexical choices possible.
- (b) "the boy sees the boy with the telescope" — Prepositional phrase attachment ambiguity.

**PCFG cannot prefer the correct attachment** because it assigns the same rule probabilities regardless of whether the PP attaches to the VP or to the NP. This motivates **Lexicalized PCFGs**, where each rule $A \rightarrow \beta$ is conditioned on the *head word* of $A$ (e.g., $P(VP \rightarrow V\ NP\ PP \mid h = sees)$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pearl:** Limitations of PCFGs are a **favourite Part A (3-mark)** question. Remember: (1) context-independence, (2) lack of lexical information, (3) difficulty with structural preferences like PP-attachment, (4) difficulty capturing long-distance dependencies.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a PCFG Inference Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the interactions between the components of a PCFG parsing/probability-computation pipeline — from rule storage to final probability output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Grammar Storage R and P] --> B[CKY Initialization]
    B --> C[Inside Algorithm DP Table n by n]
    C --> D{For each cell i, j and split k}
    D --> E[Lookup Rule Probability P of A to B C]
    E --> F[Multiply alpha i,k of B times alpha k+1,j of C]
    F --> G[Accumulate into alpha i,j of A]
    G --> H{Span equals full sentence}
    H --> I[Sum over all splits and rules]
    I --> J[String Probability P of s]
    H --> K[Traceback of max rule at each cell]
    K --> L[Most Likely Parse Tree argmax t P of t given s]
    J --> M[Output Probability Distribution]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Downstream Application Parser, LM, MT]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** `alpha i,j of A` denotes the CKY *inside* probability of non-terminal `A` deriving the substring from position `i` to `j`. The diagram shows the recursive DP loop at the heart of PCFG inference.

## 4.2 Parse Tree Schematic for "the boy sees a girl"

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeS["S"] --> nodeNP1["NP"]
    nodeS --> nodeVP["VP"]
    nodeNP1 --> nodeDet1["Det"]
    nodeNP1 --> nodeN1["N"]
    nodeDet1 --> wordThe1["the"]
    nodeN1 --> wordBoy["boy"]
    nodeVP --> nodeV["V"]
    nodeVP --> nodeNP2["NP"]
    nodeV --> wordSees["sees"]
    nodeNP2 --> nodeDet2["Det"]
    nodeNP2 --> nodeN2["N"]
    nodeDet2 --> wordA["a"]
    nodeN2 --> wordGirl["girl"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the tree:** Each edge represents one production rule. The probability of the entire tree is the **product of the rule probabilities on every edge** (Feature 1). The CKY algorithm processes this structure in $O(n^3)$ time using dynamic programming over spans.

## 4.3 Feature-Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Feature 1 Tree Probability Product] --> F4[Feature 4 Independence Assumption]
    F2[Feature 2 Derivation Equivalence] --> F4
    F3[Feature 3 String Probability Sum] --> F5[Feature 5 Normalization]
    F4 --> F6[Limitation No Lexical Context]
    F5 --> F7[CKY Polynomial Time]
    F6 --> F8[Motivation for Lexicalized PCFGs]
    F7 --> F8
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** This map shows how Features 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 are interdependent. Features 1 + 4 together enable efficient CKY parsing. Feature 4 (independence) is also the **root cause** of the model's limitations, which motivate lexicalized extensions covered in advanced modules.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Define a Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar (PCFG). State the normalization condition for a PCFG.

**Model Answer:**

A **Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar (PCFG)** is a 5-tuple $(N, T, S, R, P)$ where $N$ is a set of non-terminals, $T$ is a set of terminals, $S$ is the start symbol, $R$ is a set of production rules, and $P$ is a function assigning a probability to each rule.

The **normalization condition** requires that for every non-terminal $A \in N$:

$$\sum_{A \rightarrow \beta \in R} P(A \rightarrow \beta) = 1$$

That is, the probabilities of all rules with the same left-hand side must sum to **1**, ensuring the PCFG defines a valid probability distribution over derivations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Breakdown (KTU pattern):** Definition — 2 marks, Normalization formula + interpretation — 1 mark.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] State and explain the conditional independence assumption of PCFGs.

**Model Answer:**

The **conditional independence assumption** of a PCFG states that:

> The choice of how to expand a non-terminal $A$ depends *only* on $A$ and is independent of the surrounding context, the parent non-terminal, sibling non-terminals, and the words in the string.

Formally, for any rule $A \rightarrow B\ C$:

$$P(A \rightarrow B\ C \mid \text{context}) = P(A \rightarrow B\ C)$$

Equivalently, subtrees rooted at $B$ and $C$ are conditionally independent given the parent $A$:

$$P(t_B, t_C \mid A) = P(t_B \mid A) \cdot P(t_C \mid A)$$

This assumption makes PCFG inference **tractable** (CKY in $O(n^3)$) but **linguistically weak**, since it cannot model phenomena like subject-verb agreement or lexical preferences.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Breakdown:** Statement of assumption — 1 mark, Formal equation — 1 mark, Consequence (tractability vs weakness) — 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Define a PCFG. Explain how the probability of a parse tree is computed from the rule probabilities. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compute the probability of the string $s$ = "the boy sees a girl" using the PCFG given below. Show every step. (7 marks)

| Rule | Probability |
|------|-------------|
| $S \rightarrow NP\ VP$ | $1.0$ |
| $NP \rightarrow Det\ N$ | $0.6$ |
| $NP \rightarrow Det\ Adj\ N$ | $0.4$ |
| $VP \rightarrow V\ NP$ | $1.0$ |
| $Det \rightarrow the$ | $0.7$ |
| $Det \rightarrow a$ | $0.3$ |
| $N \rightarrow boy$ | $0.5$ |
| $N \rightarrow girl$ | $0.5$ |
| $Adj \rightarrow big$ | $0.6$ |
| $Adj \rightarrow small$ | $0.4$ |
| $V \rightarrow sees$ | $1.0$ |

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Definition (2 marks):** A PCFG is a 5-tuple $(N, T, S, R, P)$. Each rule $A \rightarrow \beta$ has a probability $P(A \rightarrow \beta)$ such that for every $A$, $\sum_\beta P(A \rightarrow \beta) = 1$.

**Tree Probability Computation (5 marks):**

1. Identify the parse tree $t$ for the input string.
2. List every production rule $r_i$ used in $t$.
3. Apply the product formula:

$$P(t, s) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(r_i)$$

4. This works because each rule application is treated as an **independent** event under the PCFG assumption.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Reasoning (5 marks sub-partition):** Product formula derivation — 2 marks, Independence justification — 2 marks, Worked illustration — 1 mark.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Identify the unique parse tree.** The string has one valid tree:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\Rightarrow NP\ VP \\
&\Rightarrow Det\ N\ V\ Det\ N \\
&\Rightarrow the\ boy\ sees\ a\ girl
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Enumerate rules with probabilities.**

| Rule | Probability |
|------|-------------|
| $S \rightarrow NP\ VP$ | $1.0$ |
| $NP \rightarrow Det\ N$ (subject) | $0.6$ |
| $VP \rightarrow V\ NP$ | $1.0$ |
| $Det \rightarrow the$ | $0.7$ |
| $N \rightarrow boy$ | $0.5$ |
| $Det \rightarrow a$ | $0.3$ |
| $N \rightarrow girl$ | $0.5$ |

**Step 3 — Compute $P(s) = P(t, s)$ since there is only one tree:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(s) &= 1.0 \times 0.6 \times 1.0 \times 0.7 \times 0.5 \times 0.3 \times 0.5 \\
&= 0.6 \times 0.7 \times 0.5 \times 0.3 \times 0.5 \\
&= 0.0315
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $P(s) = 0.0315$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Reasoning (7 marks sub-partition):** Tree identification — 1 mark, Rule enumeration with probabilities — 2 marks, Product computation — 2 marks, Final answer — 1 mark, Justification of uniqueness — 1 mark.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024] (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** What is the conditional independence assumption in a PCFG? Illustrate with an example tree why the assumption enables polynomial-time parsing. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss **three major limitations** of standard PCFGs and explain how lexicalized PCFGs (e.g., Collins parser) address them. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Statement (2 marks):** The conditional independence assumption states that given a non-terminal $A$, the probability of choosing a particular rule $A \rightarrow \beta$ is independent of the context in which $A$ occurs. Equivalently, subtrees under $A$ are conditionally independent given $A$:

$$P(t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_k \mid A) = \prod_{i=1}^{k} P(t_i \mid A)$$

**Illustration (3 marks):** Consider the tree:

- $S$ has children $NP$ and $VP$ (rule $S \rightarrow NP\ VP$).
- The probability of the subtree under $NP$ depends only on $NP$'s rule probabilities, not on $VP$.
- The probability of the subtree under $VP$ depends only on $VP$'s rule probabilities, not on $NP$.

**Polynomial-Time Consequence (2 marks):** Because the score of a parent node factorizes as a product of independent child-subtree scores, the CKY dynamic-programming algorithm can compute the optimal parse in $O(n^3)$ time, where $n$ is the sentence length. This is achieved via the recursion:

$$\alpha_{i,j}(A) = \sum_{k=i}^{j-1} \sum_{B, C} P(A \rightarrow B\ C) \cdot \alpha_{i,k}(B) \cdot \alpha_{k+1,j}(C)$$

The independence assumption allows this DP to combine sub-spans without re-examining context.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Limitation 1 — No Lexical Conditioning (2 marks):**
PCFGs ignore the **head word** of phrases. The probability of $VP \rightarrow V\ NP\ PP$ is the same whether the head verb is "ate" or "said". This causes failures in **PP-attachment disambiguation** (e.g., "I ate pasta with chopsticks" vs "I ate pasta with pesto").
*Lexicalized fix:* Condition rule probability on the head word: $P(VP \rightarrow V\ NP\ PP \mid h = \text{ate})$ differs from $P(VP \rightarrow V\ NP\ PP \mid h = \text{said})$.

**Limitation 2 — Context-Insensitivity (2 marks):**
Standard PCFGs cannot capture **long-distance dependencies** or **subject-verb agreement** (e.g., "the *boys* *sing*" vs "the *boy* *sings*").
*Lexicalized fix:* Head-to-head dependencies and subcategorization frames in lexicalized PCFGs encode these constraints.

**Limitation 3 — Poor Probability Estimates for Sparse Rules (3 marks):**
Maximum-likelihood estimation from a treebank assigns zero probability to **unseen rules**, which makes parsing brittle on out-of-domain text.
*Lexicalized fix:* Lexicalized PCFGs use **smoothing techniques** (e.g., linear interpolation, Witten-Bell) and richer conditioning contexts to share statistical strength across similar lexical items, dramatically reducing sparsity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Reasoning (7 marks sub-partition):** Limitation 1 + lexicalized fix — 2 marks, Limitation 2 + fix — 2 marks, Limitation 3 + fix — 2 marks, Conclusion — 1 mark.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting normalization:** When writing a PCFG, students often forget to check that rules for *every* non-terminal sum to $1$. Examiners deduct **1 mark** for this.
> 2. **Confusing $P(t, s)$ with $P(s \mid t)$:** $P(t, s)$ is the *joint* probability; $P(s \mid t)$ is the conditional. Examiners frequently test this distinction.
> 3. **Skipping the sum over parse trees:** For $P(s)$, you must sum over *all* valid trees. Writing $P(s) = P(t, s)$ for a single tree is correct *only* if the grammar gives exactly one parse.
> 4. **Ignoring the independence assumption in derivations:** If a question says "explain why PCFG inference is tractable", the answer must explicitly mention the **conditional independence assumption** and CKY, not just "PCFGs use probabilities".
> 5. **Confusing PCFG with Hidden Markov Model (HMM):** PCFGs model **hierarchical** (tree) structure; HMMs model **linear** (sequence) structure. Examiners love to ask "why is PCFG more powerful than HMM?"

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PCFG Definition:** A 5-tuple $(N, T, S, R, P)$ with rule probabilities summing to $1$ for each left-hand side non-terminal.
- **Tree Probability Formula:** $P(t, s) = \prod_{i} P(r_i)$ — product over every rule in the tree.
- **Derivation Equivalence:** $P(t, s) = P(d)$ for the unique left-most derivation $d$ producing $t$.
- **String Probability:** $P(s) = \sum_{t \in T(s)} P(t, s)$ — sum over all parse trees.
- **Conditional Independence Assumption:** Subtrees under a common parent are independent given the parent. This is the **defining feature** that makes PCFGs tractable yet limited.
- **Normalization:** $\sum_{\beta} P(A \rightarrow \beta) = 1$ for every $A \in N$.
- **CKY Algorithm:** Computes $P(s)$ and the most likely parse in $O(n^3)$ time using the inside recursion.
- **Limitations:** (1) no lexical conditioning, (2) no long-distance dependencies, (3) data sparsity, (4) context-insensitivity.
- **Motivations for Extensions:** Lexicalized PCFGs (Collins 1997, 1999), Lexicalized Tree-Adjoining Grammars, Dependency PCFGs, neural PCFG parsers (e.g., Kitaev & Klein 2018).
- **Quick Calculation Tip:** When computing $P(t, s)$ by hand, list all rules in a vertical table, multiply stepwise, and present the final result with 4 decimal places for clarity.
- **Real-world applications:** Constituency parsing, machine translation reordering, grammar checking, speech-recognition lattice rescoring, information extraction.

---

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Probabilistic Context-Free Grammars (PCFGs)?

A **Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar (PCFG)** is a Context-Free Grammar augmented with probabilities on each production rule. Formally, it is a 5-tuple:

$$G = (N, \Sigma, R, S, P)$$

where:
- **N** = set of non-terminal symbols (e.g., NP, VP, S)
- **Σ** = set of terminal symbols (vocabulary words)
- **R** = set of production rules
- **S** = start symbol
- **P** = probability function: $P: R \rightarrow [0,1]$ such that for every non-terminal $A$, the sum of probabilities of all productions expanding $A$ equals 1.

$$\sum_{i} P(A \rightarrow \alpha_i) = 1$$

The probability of a parse tree $T$ for a sentence $S$ is the product of the probabilities of all rules used in the tree:

$$P(T, S) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(R_i)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition**: A PCFG assigns a probability to each derivation (parse tree) of a string, and the probability of all parse trees for a given string sums to **1**. This allows statistical ranking of multiple valid parses — critical for disambiguation in real-world NLP.

## 1.2 The "Questions for PCFGs" Problem Domain

The topic **"Questions for PCFGs"** refers to the application of PCFGs to **open-domain question answering (QA) systems**, where syntactic parse trees generated by PCFGs are exploited to bridge the lexical gap between a natural language question and a candidate answer passage.

The classical architecture (inspired by the **Microsoft Research AskMSR / Moldovan et al. approach**) uses PCFGs to:
1. **Parse** the user's question into a syntactic tree.
2. **Parse** candidate sentences from a text corpus.
3. **Match** subtrees between the question tree and answer-passage tree.
4. **Extract** an answer span based on syntactic alignment.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Detective and the Fingerprint"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy**: Imagine a detective (the QA system) is given a fingerprint from a crime scene (the **question**). The detective's job is to find which file in the police archive (the **passage corpus**) contains a matching fingerprint.
>
> A PCFG acts like a **probabilistic fingerprint generator** — it takes any English sentence and produces a structured "syntactic fingerprint" (parse tree) along with a confidence score. By comparing the *shape* of the question's fingerprint with fingerprints of sentences in the corpus, the system can locate answers even when no words overlap.

## 1.4 Why PCFGs for Question Answering?

| Challenge in QA | How PCFGs Help |
|---|---|
| Lexical mismatch (question uses "car", passage uses "automobile") | Syntactic structure is preserved even with different words |
| Multiple candidate sentences | Probabilistic ranking picks the most likely parse |
| Long-distance dependencies | CFG rules capture subject-verb-object patterns |
| Ambiguity in natural language | Probabilities resolve the most plausible parse |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 4)**: Students must understand how PCFGs differ from deterministic CFGs, the inside-outside algorithm for parameter estimation, and the application of PCFG-derived parse trees to question-answer matching.
