<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Formal Components of a PCFG

A PCFG production rule has the form:

$$A \rightarrow \alpha \quad [p]$$

where $A \in N$, $\alpha \in (N \cup \Sigma)^*$, and $p = P(A \rightarrow \alpha)$ is the rule probability.

**Three fundamental probability constraints** that make a grammar "probabilistic" and well-formed:

1. **Rule probability in [0,1]**: $\quad 0 \le P(A \rightarrow \alpha) \le 1$
2. **Stochastic consistency (partition constraint)**: For each non-terminal $A$, the probabilities of all rules expanding $A$ must sum to 1.

$$\sum_{\alpha} P(A \rightarrow \alpha) = 1$$

3. **Sentence probability (sum over all parse trees)**: For any sentence $S$ with parse trees $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_k$:

$$P(S) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} P(T_i, S)$$

## 2.2 The Three Core Inference Problems for PCFGs

These are the **frequently asked KTU questions** and must be memorized:

| # | Problem | Input | Output | Standard Algorithm |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Parsing** (Recognition + Best Parse) | Grammar $G$, Sentence $S$ | Most likely parse tree $T^*$ | **CYK Algorithm** (Cocke–Younger–Kasami) or Viterbi CKY |
| 2 | **Probability of a String** | Grammar $G$, Sentence $S$ | $P(S)$ | **Inside Algorithm** (a dynamic programming variant of CKY) |
| 3 | **Rule Probability Estimation** | Grammar $G$, Treebank $D$ | Updated $P(A \rightarrow \alpha)$ | **Inside-Outside Algorithm** (EM-style) |

## 2.3 Applying PCFGs to Question Answering — Operational Pipeline

When PCFGs are used for **answering questions** (i.e., mapping a question to an answer in a corpus), the pipeline is:

**Step 1 — Question Parsing:**
The user query $Q$ (e.g., *"When did Mozart die?"*) is parsed by the PCFG, producing the highest-probability parse tree $T_Q$.

**Step 2 — Question Classification via Parse Tree:**
The parse tree reveals the *expected answer type* via the syntactic category of the wh-word's attachment. For example, "When" attaches to a temporal argument → expected answer type = **DATE**.

**Step 3 — Passage Retrieval:**
A shallow IR system retrieves candidate passages from a corpus.

**Step 4 — Passage Parsing:**
Each candidate sentence is parsed using the same PCFG to obtain trees $T_{P_1}, T_{P_2}, \ldots, T_{P_n}$.

**Step 5 — Subtree Matching (Tree Kernel / Tree Edit Distance):**
The system measures similarity between $T_Q$ and each $T_{P_i}$ using:
- **Tree kernel methods** (Collins & Duffy, 2002): count the number of common subtrees.
- **Tree edit distance**: minimum operations to transform one tree into another.

**Step 6 — Answer Extraction:**
The matched subtree in the passage provides the answer span (e.g., the NP subtree directly corresponding to the wh-phrase position).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rule probability | $P(A \rightarrow \alpha)$ | Probability of expanding $A$ as $\alpha$ |
| 2 | Partition constraint | $\sum_{\alpha} P(A \rightarrow \alpha) = 1$ | All expansions of $A$ sum to 1 |
| 3 | Tree probability | $P(T) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(R_i)$ | Product of rule probabilities in tree $T$ |
| 4 | Sentence probability | $P(S) = \sum_{T} P(T,S)$ | Sum over all parse trees of $S$ |
| 5 | Best parse (Viterbi) | $T^* = \arg\max_T P(T \mid S)$ | Most probable parse |
| 6 | Inside probability | $\beta(A, i, j) = P(w_i \ldots w_j \mid A)$ | Probability that $A$ generates substring $w_i \ldots w_j$ |
| 7 | Outside probability | $\alpha(A, i, j)$ | Probability of generating the whole string $S$ with $A$ as the root spanning $(i,j)$ |
| 8 | Tree-kernel similarity | $K(T_1, T_2) = \sum_{n} \lambda^n \cdot C_n(T_1, T_2)$ | Weighted count of common subtrees of size $n$ |
| 9 | Expected count of rule $r$ | $E[\text{count}(r)] = \frac{\alpha(r) \cdot \beta(r)}{P(S)}$ | Used in Inside-Outside updates |
| 10 | EM re-estimation | $P'(A \rightarrow \alpha) = \frac{E[\text{count}(A \rightarrow \alpha)]}{\sum_\gamma E[\text{count}(A \rightarrow \gamma)]}$ | Updated rule probability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility**: PCFG-based question answering is foundational to **IBM's Watson**, **information retrieval engines**, and **biomedical NLP** (e.g., extracting drug-disease relationships from PubMed abstracts). The probabilistic ranking lets the system prefer the most syntactically coherent answer even when multiple candidate answers are syntactically valid.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Computing the Probability of a Parse Tree

Consider the following PCFG (commonly used in KTU textbook examples):

| Rule | Probability |
|---|---|
| $S \rightarrow NP \, VP$ | 1.0 |
| $NP \rightarrow Det \, N$ | 0.5 |
| $NP \rightarrow ProperNoun$ | 0.3 |
| $NP \rightarrow NP \, PP$ | 0.2 |
| $VP \rightarrow V \, NP$ | 0.7 |
| $VP \rightarrow VP \, PP$ | 0.3 |
| $PP \rightarrow P \, NP$ | 1.0 |
| $Det \rightarrow \text{the}$ | 0.6 |
| $Det \rightarrow \text{a}$ | 0.4 |
| $N \rightarrow \text{man}$ | 0.5 |
| $N \rightarrow \text{telescope}$ | 0.5 |
| $V \rightarrow \text{saw}$ | 1.0 |
| $P \rightarrow \text{with}$ | 1.0 |
| $ProperNoun \rightarrow \text{John}$ | 1.0 |

### Derivation: Probability of Tree T1 for "the man saw a telescope with John"

The parse tree T1 is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow NP \quad VP \\
NP &\rightarrow Det \quad N \quad (Det = \text{the}, N = \text{man}) \\
VP &\rightarrow V \quad NP \quad (V = \text{saw}) \\
NP &\rightarrow Det \quad N \quad (Det = \text{a}, N = \text{telescope}) \\
PP &\rightarrow P \quad NP \quad (P = \text{with}) \\
NP &\rightarrow ProperNoun \quad (ProperNoun = \text{John})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1: List all rules used in T1.**

Rules used:
1. $S \rightarrow NP \, VP$, $p_1 = 1.0$
2. $NP \rightarrow Det \, N$, $p_2 = 0.5$
3. $VP \rightarrow V \, NP$, $p_3 = 0.7$
4. $NP \rightarrow Det \, N$, $p_4 = 0.5$
5. $PP \rightarrow P \, NP$, $p_5 = 1.0$
6. $NP \rightarrow ProperNoun$, $p_6 = 0.3$
7. $Det \rightarrow \text{the}$, $p_7 = 0.6$
8. $N \rightarrow \text{man}$, $p_8 = 0.5$
9. $V \rightarrow \text{saw}$, $p_9 = 1.0$
10. $Det \rightarrow \text{a}$, $p_{10} = 0.4$
11. $N \rightarrow \text{telescope}$, $p_{11} = 0.5$
12. $P \rightarrow \text{with}$, $p_{12} = 1.0$
13. $ProperNoun \rightarrow \text{John}$, $p_{13} = 1.0$

**Step 2: Apply the tree probability formula.**

$$
P(T_1) = \prod_{i=1}^{13} p_i
$$

**Step 3: Substitute values.**

$$
P(T_1) = 1.0 \times 0.5 \times 0.7 \times 0.5 \times 1.0 \times 0.3 \times 0.6 \times 0.5 \times 1.0 \times 0.4 \times 0.5 \times 1.0 \times 1.0
$$

**Step 4: Multiply step-by-step.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(T_1) &= (1.0 \cdot 0.5) \cdot (0.7 \cdot 0.5) \cdot (1.0 \cdot 0.3) \cdot (0.6 \cdot 0.5) \cdot (1.0 \cdot 0.4) \cdot (0.5 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 1.0) \\
&= 0.5 \cdot 0.35 \cdot 0.3 \cdot 0.30 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 0.5 \\
&= 0.5 \cdot 0.35 = 0.175 \\
&\cdot 0.3 = 0.0525 \\
&\cdot 0.30 = 0.01575 \\
&\cdot 0.4 = 0.0063 \\
&\cdot 0.5 = 0.00315
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{P(T_1) = 3.15 \times 10^{-3}}$$

## 3.2 Worked Example: Probability of a Sentence (Sum Over All Parses)

For the sentence *"the man saw a telescope with John"*, there is **another valid parse tree T2** where *with John* modifies the noun phrase *the man* (instrument reading vs. agent reading).

If $P(T_2) = 2.10 \times 10^{-3}$ (computed similarly), then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(S) &= P(T_1) + P(T_2) \\
&= 3.15 \times 10^{-3} + 2.10 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 5.25 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Partition Verification**: The fact that we can compute $P(T_1)$ and $P(T_2)$ and sum them to get $P(S)$ is precisely why the partition constraint $\sum_\alpha P(A \rightarrow \alpha) = 1$ is essential. Without it, the probabilities would not form a valid distribution.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Comparison

| Algorithm | Purpose | Complexity | Time | KTU Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CKY Parsing** (Viterbi) | Best parse tree $T^*$ | $O(n^3 \cdot \vert G \vert)$ | Polynomial | High |
| **Inside Algorithm** | Forward probability $\beta$ | $O(n^3 \cdot \vert G \vert)$ | Polynomial | High |
| **Outside Algorithm** | Backward probability $\alpha$ | $O(n^3 \cdot \vert G \vert)$ | Polynomial | High |
| **Inside-Outside (EM)** | Re-estimate rule probabilities | $O(n^3 \cdot \vert G \vert \cdot k)$ | Iterative | Very High |
| **Tree Kernel** | Similarity between parse trees | $O(\vert T_1 \vert + \vert T_2 \vert)$ | Linear in tree size | Medium |

## 3.4 Mathematical Justification: Why Multiply Probabilities?

A PCFG defines a **probabilistic model** in which each rule application is assumed to be **context-free and independent of sibling choices**. The joint probability of the tree $T$ factorizes by the chain rule and the context-free assumption:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(T) &= P(R_1) \cdot P(R_2 \mid R_1) \cdot P(R_3 \mid R_1, R_2) \cdots \\
&\stackrel{\text{CFG}}{=} P(R_1) \cdot P(R_2) \cdot P(R_3) \cdots \\
&= \prod_{i} P(R_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

The context-free assumption means the choice of how to expand a node $A$ **does not depend on the surrounding context** (siblings or ancestors), so all rule probabilities are conditionally independent given the parent non-terminal.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake**: Students often forget that **rule probability** ≠ **derivation probability**. The rule probability is local to a single production; the tree probability is the product of all rule probabilities in the tree. Do not confuse them in the exam.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: PCFG-Based Question Answering Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Question Q] --> B[Tokenizer]
    B --> C[PCFG Parser CKY]
    C --> D[Best Parse Tree TQ]
    D --> E[Question Classifier: Expected Answer Type]
    D --> F[Subtree Extractor]

    G[Text Corpus] --> H[IR Retriever]
    H --> I[Candidate Passages P1 P2 ... Pn]
    I --> J[PCFG Parser CKY for Passages]
    J --> K[Passage Parse Trees TP1 TP2 ... TPn]

    K --> L[Tree Matching Engine]
    F --> L
    L --> M[Tree Kernel / Edit Distance Score]

    M --> N[Ranked Candidate Answers]
    E --> N
    N --> O[Top-K Answer Span]
    O --> P[Final Answer to User]
```

**Architecture Description:**

- **Node A (User Question)**: Natural language input such as "Who wrote Hamlet?"
- **Node B (Tokenizer)**: Splits the question into words.
- **Node C (PCFG Parser)**: Applies CKY to find the most probable parse tree $T_Q$ using Viterbi.
- **Node D (Best Parse Tree)**: The syntactic structure of the question.
- **Node E (Question Classifier)**: Determines the expected answer type (PERSON, DATE, LOCATION) from the parse tree's wh-phrase attachment.
- **Node F (Subtree Extractor)**: Extracts the relevant question subtrees for matching.
- **Node G–K (Passage Pipeline)**: The corpus is searched, candidate sentences retrieved, and each is parsed with the same PCFG.
- **Node L–M (Tree Matching)**: Compares question subtrees with passage subtrees using tree kernels.
- **Node N–P (Answer Selection)**: Ranks candidates by combined syntactic match and answer-type compatibility.

## 4.2 Mermaid Tree Diagram: Sample PCFG Parse Tree for a Question

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Snode[S] --> NPQ[NP-wh]
    Snode --> VPQ[VP]

    NPQ --> WP[WP]
    NPQ --> NNQ[NN]
    WP --> Wwhen[when]
    NNQ --> Ndid[did]

    VPQ --> VQ[VBD]
    VPQ --> SQ[SQ]

    VQ --> Vdid[did]
    SQ --> NPa[NP]
    SQ --> VPact[VP]

    NPa --> DTa[DT]
    NPa --> NNa[NN]
    DTa --> Atea[the]
    NNa --> Nshahsah[ashes]

    VPact --> VBa[VB]
    VPact --> NPobj[NP]
    VBa --> Vleave[leave]
    NPobj --> DTb[DT]
    NPobj --> NNb[NN]
    DTb --> Aa[a]
    NNb --> Nmark[mark]
```

**Diagram Explanation:**

- The root **S** dominates the entire question.
- The **NP-wh** node represents the wh-phrase (question word subtree).
- The **VP** splits into the auxiliary verb and the actual proposition.
- The auxiliary **VBD ("did")** is inverted to the front, a syntactic marker of English questions.
- The object **NP** (containing "a mark") is the answer span candidate.

## 4.3 Functional Flow Block: Inside-Outside Algorithm for PCFG Training

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Initialize Rule Probabilities Uniform] --> B[For Each Training Sentence Si]
    B --> C[Compute Inside Probabilities beta]
    C --> D[Compute Outside Probabilities alpha]
    D --> E[Compute Expected Counts of Each Rule]
    E --> F[Re-estimate Rule Probabilities]
    F --> G{Convergence Achieved}
    G -->|No| B
    G -->|Yes| H[Final PCFG Parameters]
```

**Block-Level Description:**

- **Block A**: Initial probabilities are set (e.g., uniformly: $1/|\text{rules expanding } A|$).
- **Block B–E**: For each sentence, compute inside and outside values, then derive expected rule counts.
- **Block F**: Update rule probabilities as the ratio of expected count of that rule to expected count of the parent non-terminal.
- **Block G–H**: Iterate until parameter changes fall below a threshold $\epsilon$, then output the trained PCFG.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar (PCFG). [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

A **Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar (PCFG)** is a 5-tuple $G = (N, \Sigma, R, S, P)$ where $N$ is a set of non-terminals, $\Sigma$ is a set of terminals, $R$ is a set of production rules, $S$ is the start symbol, and $P$ is a probability function that assigns a probability $P(A \rightarrow \alpha)$ to each production rule.

The probabilities must satisfy the partition constraint:

$$\sum_{\alpha} P(A \rightarrow \alpha) = 1$$

for every non-terminal $A$. The probability of a parse tree $T$ is the product of the probabilities of all rules used in $T$:

$$P(T) = \prod_{i} P(R_i)$$

PCFGs are used to **rank multiple valid parses** of the same sentence by their probabilities, solving the ambiguity problem in CFG-based parsing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip**: Always state the partition constraint explicitly — it is the most frequently tested sub-component.

### Question 2: How are PCFGs used in Question Answering systems? [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

PCFGs are used in Question Answering (QA) systems in the following way:

1. The **user question** is parsed using the PCFG to produce a syntactic parse tree.
2. The parse tree reveals the **expected answer type** (e.g., PERSON, DATE) based on the wh-phrase attachment.
3. **Candidate passages** from a corpus are retrieved using information retrieval.
4. Each candidate passage is also parsed with the same PCFG.
5. **Tree matching algorithms** (tree kernels or tree edit distance) compare the syntactic structure of the question with that of the candidate answers.
6. The candidate with the **highest syntactic similarity** and the **matching answer type** is selected as the final answer.

This approach handles **lexical mismatch** because syntactic structure is preserved even when different surface words are used.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall**: Students often mention "syntactic parsing" but forget to specify **tree matching** as the bridge between the question and answer. This omission costs 1 mark.

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A: Probability Computation and Parse Tree Analysis** [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a) Compute the probability of the following parse tree T for the sentence "John saw the man with the telescope" using the given PCFG. State all rules used. (7 marks)**

**PCFG Rules:**

| Rule | Probability | | Rule | Probability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $S \rightarrow NP \, VP$ | 1.0 | | $Det \rightarrow \text{the}$ | 0.8 |
| $NP \rightarrow ProperNoun$ | 0.3 | | $Det \rightarrow \text{a}$ | 0.2 |
| $NP \rightarrow Det \, N$ | 0.5 | | $N \rightarrow \text{man}$ | 0.5 |
| $NP \rightarrow NP \, PP$ | 0.2 | | $N \rightarrow \text{telescope}$ | 0.5 |
| $VP \rightarrow V \, NP$ | 0.6 | | $V \rightarrow \text{saw}$ | 1.0 |
| $VP \rightarrow VP \, PP$ | 0.4 | | $P \rightarrow \text{with}$ | 1.0 |
| $PP \rightarrow P \, NP$ | 1.0 | | $ProperNoun \rightarrow \text{John}$ | 1.0 |

**Parse Tree T:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow NP \quad VP \\
NP &\rightarrow ProperNoun \quad (John) \\
VP &\rightarrow V \quad NP \quad (saw) \\
NP &\rightarrow Det \quad N \quad (the \, man) \\
NP &\rightarrow NP \quad PP \\
PP &\rightarrow P \quad NP \quad (with) \\
NP &\rightarrow Det \quad N \quad (the \, telescope)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify all 11 rules used in T.**

1. $S \rightarrow NP \, VP$ → $p_1 = 1.0$
2. $NP \rightarrow ProperNoun$ → $p_2 = 0.3$
3. $VP \rightarrow V \, NP$ → $p_3 = 0.6$
4. $NP \rightarrow Det \, N$ → $p_4 = 0.5$
5. $NP \rightarrow NP \, PP$ → $p_5 = 0.2$
6. $PP \rightarrow P \, NP$ → $p_6 = 1.0$
7. $NP \rightarrow Det \, N$ → $p_7 = 0.5$
8. $ProperNoun \rightarrow \text{John}$ → $p_8 = 1.0$
9. $V \rightarrow \text{saw}$ → $p_9 = 1.0$
10. $Det \rightarrow \text{the}$ → $p_{10} = 0.8$
11. $N \rightarrow \text{man}$ → $p_{11} = 0.5$
12. $P \rightarrow \text{with}$ → $p_{12} = 1.0$
13. $Det \rightarrow \text{the}$ → $p_{13} = 0.8$
14. $N \rightarrow \text{telescope}$ → $p_{14} = 0.5$

**Step 2: Apply the tree probability formula.**

$$
P(T) = \prod_{i=1}^{14} p_i
$$

**Step 3: Substitute and multiply.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(T) &= 1.0 \times 0.3 \times 0.6 \times 0.5 \times 0.2 \times 1.0 \times 0.5 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 0.8 \times 0.5 \times 1.0 \times 0.8 \times 0.5 \\
&= 0.3 \times 0.6 \times 0.5 \times 0.2 \times 0.5 \times 0.8 \times 0.5 \times 0.8 \times 0.5 \\
&= (0.3 \times 0.6) \times (0.5 \times 0.2) \times (0.5 \times 0.8) \times (0.5 \times 0.8) \times 0.5 \\
&= 0.18 \times 0.10 \times 0.40 \times 0.40 \times 0.5 \\
&= 0.18 \times 0.10 = 0.018 \\
&\times 0.40 = 0.0072 \\
&\times 0.40 = 0.00288 \\
&\times 0.5 = 0.00144
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{P(T) = 1.44 \times 10^{-3}}$$

---

**(b) Explain how PCFGs are used in a Question Answering system. Illustrate with a suitable example. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (2 marks):**
PCFGs play a pivotal role in modern QA systems by providing **syntactic structure** that bridges the gap between a natural language question and a candidate answer passage. While keyword-based systems fail when the question and passage use different vocabulary (lexical mismatch), PCFG-based systems leverage **structural equivalence** for robust matching.

**Operational Steps (3 marks):**

1. **Question Parsing**: The user's question $Q$ is tokenized and parsed using the PCFG to yield the highest-probability parse tree $T_Q$ via the Viterbi-CKY algorithm.

2. **Answer Type Detection**: The parse tree of the question determines the **expected answer type**:
   - "When did World War II end?" → wh-word "When" attaches as a temporal modifier → Expected type: **DATE**.
   - "Who wrote Hamlet?" → "Who" attaches to a subject NP gap → Expected type: **PERSON**.

3. **Passage Retrieval and Parsing**: An IR module retrieves candidate sentences from the corpus. Each is parsed with the same PCFG to yield $T_{P_i}$.

4. **Tree Matching**: A **tree kernel** computes similarity $K(T_Q, T_{P_i})$ based on common subtrees. The candidate maximizing $K$ subject to the expected answer type is selected.

**Example (2 marks):**

- Question: *"Which company acquired YouTube?"*
- Parse Tree of Question: $S \rightarrow NP\text{-wh} \quad VP$, where $NP\text{-wh} \rightarrow WP(\text{Which}) \quad NN(\text{company})$
- Candidate passage: *"Google purchased YouTube in 2006."*
- Parse tree contains $NP \rightarrow ProperNoun(\text{Google})$ in the subject position, structurally analogous to the wh-NP in the question.
- The system aligns the wh-gap with "Google" and outputs **"Google"** as the answer, even though the words "company" and "Google" never co-occur.

**Conclusion:**
PCFG-based QA systems outperform bag-of-words baselines on TREC benchmarks because they exploit **deep syntactic structure** rather than surface word overlap.

---

### **Question B: PCFG Training and Tree-Based Answer Matching** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) Describe the Inside-Outside Algorithm for training a PCFG. State its key formulas. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
The Inside-Outside Algorithm is an **Expectation-Maximization (EM)** procedure used to learn the rule probabilities of a PCFG from an unannotated (or partially annotated) text corpus. It iteratively re-estimates $P(A \rightarrow \alpha)$ to maximize the likelihood of the training data.

**Inside Probability (2 marks):**
The **inside probability** $\beta(A, i, j)$ is the total probability that non-terminal $A$ generates the substring $w_i \ldots w_j$:

$$\beta(A, i, j) = \sum_{B, C} P(A \rightarrow B \, C) \cdot \sum_{k=i}^{j-1} \beta(B, i, k) \cdot \beta(C, k+1, j)$$

Base case: $\beta(A, i, i) = P(A \rightarrow w_i)$.

**Outside Probability (1 mark):**
The **outside probability** $\alpha(A, i, j)$ is the probability that the start symbol $S$ generates the entire string $S = w_1 \ldots w_n$ with $A$ as the root spanning exactly $(i, j)$:

$$\alpha(A, i, j) = \sum_{B, C, k} P(B \rightarrow C \, A) \cdot \alpha(B, i, k) \cdot \beta(C, j+1, k) + \ldots$$

**Re-estimation Formula (2 marks):**
The expected count of rule $A \rightarrow B \, C$ spanning $(i, j)$ with split at $k$ is:

$$E[\text{count}(A \rightarrow B \, C, i, j, k)] = \frac{\alpha(A, i, j) \cdot P(A \rightarrow B \, C) \cdot \beta(B, i, k) \cdot \beta(C, k+1, j)}{P(S)}$$

The new rule probability is:

$$P'(A \rightarrow B \, C) = \frac{\sum_{i, j, k} E[\text{count}(A \rightarrow B \, C, i, j, k)]}{\sum_{\gamma} \sum_{i, j, k} E[\text{count}(A \rightarrow \gamma, i, j, k)]}$$

**Convergence**: Iterate until the change in rule probabilities falls below threshold $\epsilon = 10^{-4}$.

---

**(b) Discuss the use of tree kernels for matching questions to answer passages in a PCFG-based QA system. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Background (1 mark):**
A **tree kernel** is a function that measures the similarity between two syntactic parse trees by counting the number of common substructures. It is widely used in PCFG-based QA systems to compare question parses with passage parses.

**Convolution Tree Kernel — Collins & Duffy (2 marks):**
For two trees $T_1$ and $T_2$, the kernel is:

$$K(T_1, T_2) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \lambda^n \cdot C_n(T_1, T_2)$$

where $C_n(T_1, T_2)$ is the number of common subtrees of size $n$ between $T_1$ and $T_2$, and $0 < \lambda < 1$ is a decay factor penalizing large subtrees.

**Operational Use in QA (2 marks):**

1. Parse the question → $T_Q$.
2. Parse each candidate passage → $T_{P_i}$.
3. Compute $K(T_Q, T_{P_i})$ for each candidate.
4. Rank candidates by $K$.
5. The matched subtree in $T_{P_i}$ that aligns with the wh-phrase subtree in $T_Q$ yields the **answer span**.

**Example (2 marks):**
- Question: *"What did Shakespeare write?"* → Parse contains $NP\text{-wh} \rightarrow WP \quad NN$.
- Passage: *"Shakespeare wrote 39 plays."* → Parse contains $NP \rightarrow CD(\text{39}) \quad NNS(\text{plays})$ in object position.
- The shared subtree pattern "$V \rightarrow \text{write} \quad NP$" gives a high kernel score, and the system extracts "39 plays" as the answer.

**Advantages:**
- Handles lexical mismatch.
- Captures long-distance syntactic dependencies.
- Polynomial time computation.

**Disadvantages:**
- Ignores semantic similarity (e.g., "buy" vs. "purchase").
- Sensitive to parse errors propagating from the PCFG.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**: When asked about PCFG training, **always name the algorithm** (Inside-Outside) and **state the partition constraint**. A common error is to confuse the *inside* and *outside* probabilities — remember: **inside goes "bottom-up"**, **outside goes "top-down"**.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ A **PCFG** is a CFG + rule probabilities; defined as $G = (N, \Sigma, R, S, P)$.
- ✅ The **partition constraint** $\sum_\alpha P(A \rightarrow \alpha) = 1$ is mandatory for every non-terminal.
- ✅ **Tree probability** is the product of all rule probabilities: $P(T) = \prod_i P(R_i)$.
- ✅ **Sentence probability** is the sum over all parse trees: $P(S) = \sum_T P(T, S)$.
- ✅ Three inference tasks: **Parsing (CKY)**, **String Probability (Inside)**, **Parameter Estimation (Inside-Outside / EM)**.
- ✅ **Inside probability** $\beta(A, i, j)$: probability that $A$ generates $w_i \ldots w_j$.
- ✅ **Outside probability** $\alpha(A, i, j)$: probability of generating the full string with $A$ spanning $(i, j)$.
- ✅ **PCFGs in QA**: parse question → classify answer type → parse passages → match subtrees via **tree kernels** → extract answer span.
- ✅ **Tree kernel** $K(T_1, T_2) = \sum_n \lambda^n C_n$ measures syntactic similarity.
- ✅ **Advantages** of PCFGs in QA: handles lexical mismatch, ranks ambiguous parses, exploits structural patterns.
- ✅ **Limitations**: poor at semantic similarity, sensitive to parse errors, assumes rule independence.
- ✅ **Standard KTU textbook example** for tree probability: "the man saw a telescope with John" — always verify both $P(T_1)$ and $P(T_2)$ are computed.
- ✅ **Real-world applications**: IBM Watson, biomedical NLP, TREC open-domain QA, sentiment analysis pipelines.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Probability of a String
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** The Probability of a String

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In **Computational Linguistics** and **Statistical Language Modeling**, the **Probability of a String** refers to the likelihood that a particular sequence of words (or characters) $W = w_1 w_2 w_3 \dots w_n$ will occur in a given language $\mathcal{L}$. Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$P(W) = P(w_1, w_2, w_3, \dots, w_n)$$

where each $w_i$ is a token (word, sub-word, or character) drawn from a vocabulary $\mathcal{V}$, and the joint probability obeys the laws of **probability calculus** such that:

$$0 \leq P(W) \leq 1 \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_{W \in \mathcal{L}} P(W) = 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** The probability of a string quantifies the syntactic and semantic plausibility of a word sequence. It is the foundational scalar that drives **language models (LMs)**, **speech recognition**, **machine translation**, **spelling correction**, and **part-of-speech tagging**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **fortune teller reading tea leaves**. Each cup of tea (a "string" of words) leaves behind patterns — bubbles, swirls, and shapes. Some patterns appear **frequently** ("How are you?" appears millions of times), while others are **vanishingly rare** ("Quantumly banana dreamed yodeling"). The probability of a string is essentially the "tea-leaf score" you would assign:

- **High probability** = The string sounds natural, common, and grammatically smooth.
- **Low probability** = The string sounds awkward, nonsensical, or never observed in training data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Engineering Analogy:** Think of $P(W)$ as the **likelihood of a sentence being uttered** in a corpus of one million sentences. If a sentence appears **120 times**, its empirical probability is roughly $120 / 1{,}000{,}000 = 1.2 \times 10^{-4}$. This tiny number is exactly what statistical NLP systems try to estimate.

### 1.3 Why Is It Important?

The probability of a string is the **central nervous system** of statistical NLP applications:

| Application | Role of $P(W)$ |
|---|---|
| **Speech Recognition** | Disambiguates between acoustically similar phrases |
| **Machine Translation** | Ranks candidate translations of a source sentence |
| **Spell Checkers** | Identifies the most probable correction for typos |
| **Autocomplete / T9** | Suggests the next likely word |
| **Authorship Attribution** | Identifies the author whose model assigns highest $P(W)$ |

> [!TIP]
> The fundamental insight from **Shannon (1948)** is that natural language has **statistical regularities** — by counting word combinations in a large text corpus, we can build a model that assigns probabilities to *any* string.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Chain Rule of Probability (Foundation)

The **chain rule** (also called the **product rule**) decomposes a joint probability into a sequence of conditional probabilities:

$$P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n) = P(w_1) \cdot P(w_2 \mid w_1) \cdot P(w_3 \mid w_1, w_2) \cdots P(w_n \mid w_1, \dots, w_{n-1})$$

In compact notation:

$$P(w_1^n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})$$

where $w_1^{i-1} = w_1, w_2, \dots, w_{i-1}$ denotes the **history** of words preceding position $i$.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Curse of History:** The chain rule is mathematically exact, but it is **practically intractable** because the number of possible histories grows **exponentially** with $n$. For a vocabulary of size $\vert \mathcal{V} \vert = V$, there are $V^{i-1}$ possible histories at position $i$. We need a simplification — this is where the **Markov Assumption** comes in.

### 2.2 The Markov Assumption (The Big Simplification)

A stochastic process satisfies the **Markov property of order $k$** if the future depends only on the most recent $k$ states:

$$P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1}) \approx P(w_i \mid w_{i-k+1}^{i-1})$$

This gives rise to the **N-gram language model family**:

| N-gram | History Used | Probability Formula |
|---|---|---|
| **Unigram** ($n=1$) | None | $P(w_i)$ |
| **Bigram** ($n=2$) | One previous word | $P(w_i \mid w_{i-1})$ |
| **Trigram** ($n=3$) | Two previous words | $P(w_i \mid w_{i-2}, w_{i-1})$ |
| **4-gram** ($n=4$) | Three previous words | $P(w_i \mid w_{i-3}, w_{i-2}, w_{i-1})$ |

For a **bigram model**, the probability of a string becomes:

$$P(w_1^n) \approx \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_{i-1})$$

### 2.3 Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)

The probabilities of n-grams are estimated from corpus frequencies using **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)**:

$$P(w_i \mid w_{i-n+1}^{i-1}) = \frac{C(w_{i-n+1}^{i})}{C(w_{i-n+1}^{i-1})}$$

where:
- $C(\cdot)$ = **raw count** of an n-gram in the training corpus
- $w_{i-n+1}^{i}$ = the n-gram from position $i-n+1$ to $i$

**Example — Bigram MLE:**

$$P(\text{tea} \mid \text{iced}) = \frac{C(\text{iced tea})}{C(\text{iced})}$$

### 2.4 Out-of-Vocabulary (OOV) and Zero-Probability Problem

> [!WARNING]
> **The Zero-Probability Disaster:** Any n-gram never seen in training gets $P = 0$, which causes the **entire** string probability to collapse to zero. This is the **sparsity problem**.

**Standard Solution — Smoothing Techniques:**

| Technique | Core Idea | Formula Adjustment |
|---|---|---|
| **Laplace (Add-1)** | Add 1 to every count | $P_{\text{Lap}} = \frac{C(w) + 1}{N + V}$ |
| **Add-k** | Add fractional constant $k$ | $P_{k} = \frac{C(w) + k}{N + kV}$ |
| **Good–Turing** | Reassign mass from seen to unseen | Based on frequency-of-frequencies |
| **Katz Backoff** | Back off to lower-order n-gram if unseen | $P_{\text{Katz}}(w \mid h) = \begin{cases} P_{\text{ML}} & \text{if } C > 0 \\ \alpha \cdot P(w \mid h') & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ |
| **Kneser–Ney** | Continuation probability for unseen contexts | Modified backoff with absolute discounting |

### 2.5 Perplexity — Evaluating the Quality of $P(W)$

**Perplexity (PP)** is the **standard intrinsic evaluation metric** for language models. It measures how "surprised" the model is by a test string:

$$PP(W) = P(w_1 w_2 \dots w_N)^{-1/N} = \left( \prod_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{P(w_i \mid w_{i-n+1}^{i-1})} \right)^{1/N}$$

Equivalently, in log-space (numerically stable):

$$PP(W) = \exp\left( -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) \right)$$

- **Lower perplexity** = Better model (less surprised by test data).
- For a **uniform unigram** over vocabulary $V$: $PP = V$ (the worst case).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Use of String Probability |
|---|---|
| **Search Engines (Google)** | Rank candidate queries / page completions |
| **Voice Assistants (Siri, Alexa)** | Resolve ASR ambiguities like "their/there/they're" |
| **Predictive Text (Keyboards)** | Suggest next word on mobile devices |
| **Bioinformatics** | Model DNA / protein sequences as "strings" over alphabet $\{A, C, G, T\}$ |
| **Cybersecurity** | Detect anomalous traffic by $P(\text{request})$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** In KTU examinations, questions on this topic frequently test the **derivation** of the chain rule applied to bigram/trigram models, the **interpretation of perplexity**, and **Laplace smoothing** calculations.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Name | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P(w_1^n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})$ | Chain Rule | Exact decomposition |
| 2 | $P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1}) \approx P(w_i \mid w_{i-k+1}^{i-1})$ | Markov Assumption (order $k$) | N-gram approximation |
| 3 | $P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \frac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i)}{C(w_{i-1})}$ | Bigram MLE | Estimating bigram probability |
| 4 | $P_{\text{Lap}}(w_i) = \frac{C(w_i) + 1}{N + V}$ | Laplace Smoothing | Handling unseen words |
| 5 | $PP(W) = \left( \prod_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{P(w_i \mid \cdot)} \right)^{1/N}$ | Perplexity | Model evaluation |
| 6 | $\log P(W) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(w_i \mid \cdot)$ | Log-probability | Numerical stability |
| 7 | $H(W) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum \log_2 P(w_i \mid \cdot)$ | Cross-Entropy (bits) | Info-theoretic evaluation |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: Chain Rule Applied to a Sentence

**Problem:** Compute $P(\text{NLP is fun})$ using the chain rule.

**Given (fictional corpus counts):**
- $P(\text{NLP}) = 0.001$
- $P(\text{is} \mid \text{NLP}) = 0.4$
- $P(\text{fun} \mid \text{NLP is}) = 0.05$

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

By the chain rule of probability:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(w_1, w_2, w_3) &= P(w_1) \cdot P(w_2 \mid w_1) \cdot P(w_3 \mid w_1, w_2) \\
P(\text{NLP, is, fun}) &= P(\text{NLP}) \cdot P(\text{is} \mid \text{NLP}) \cdot P(\text{fun} \mid \text{NLP, is})
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{NLP, is, fun}) &= 0.001 \times 0.4 \times 0.05 \\
&= 0.001 \times 0.02 \\
&= 2 \times 10^{-5}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The string "NLP is fun" has a very low joint probability because it is a rare trigram; however, *conditional* on the context "NLP is", the word "fun" is reasonably likely (5%).

### 3.2 Derivation II: From Chain Rule to Bigram Model

**Starting Point:** The full chain rule:

$$
P(w_1^n) = P(w_1) \cdot P(w_2 \mid w_1) \cdot P(w_3 \mid w_1, w_2) \cdots P(w_n \mid w_1^{n-1})
$$

**Step 1:** Apply the **first-order Markov assumption**: $P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1}) \approx P(w_i \mid w_{i-1})$.

$$
P(w_1^n) \approx P(w_1) \cdot P(w_2 \mid w_1) \cdot P(w_3 \mid w_2) \cdots P(w_n \mid w_{n-1})
$$

**Step 2:** Introduce the **sentence-start token** $\langle s \rangle$ so that $P(w_1)$ becomes a regular bigram:

$$
P(w_1^n) \approx \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}), \quad \text{with } w_0 = \langle s \rangle
$$

**Step 3:** Apply **MLE** to estimate each bigram from corpus counts:

$$
P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \frac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i)}{C(w_{i-1})}
$$

**Step 4 (Final Bigram String Probability):**

$$
P(w_1^n) \approx \prod_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i)}{C(w_{i-1})}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation III: Worked Example with Laplace Smoothing

**Problem:** Compute the smoothed unigram probability of the word "**linguistics**" in a corpus with $N = 100{,}000$ total tokens, vocabulary size $V = 10{,}000$, and $C(\text{linguistics}) = 50$.

**Step 1:** Apply Laplace (Add-1) smoothing:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{Lap}}(\text{linguistics}) &= \frac{C(\text{linguistics}) + 1}{N + V} \\
&= \frac{50 + 1}{100{,}000 + 10{,}000} \\
&= \frac{51}{110{,}000} \\
&\approx 4.636 \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compare with raw MLE:

$$
P_{\text{ML}}(\text{linguistics}) = \frac{50}{100{,}000} = 5 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 3:** Compare with an **unseen word** $w_{\text{OOV}}$:

$$
P_{\text{Lap}}(w_{\text{OOV}}) = \frac{0 + 1}{110{,}000} \approx 9.09 \times 10^{-6}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters:** Laplace smoothing ensures **no probability is ever zero**, but it **distorts the distribution** by stealing too much mass from common words. Kneser–Ney is preferred in modern systems.

### 3.4 Derivation IV: Perplexity Calculation

**Problem:** Given a test sentence of $N = 5$ tokens with the following log-probabilities (base 10): $\log_{10} P = -0.5, -1.2, -0.8, -2.0, -0.3$, compute the perplexity.

**Step 1:** Sum the log-probabilities:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{5} \log_{10} P(w_i \mid \cdot) = -0.5 - 1.2 - 0.8 - 2.0 - 0.3 = -4.8
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the average log-probability:

$$
\frac{1}{N} \sum \log_{10} P = \frac{-4.8}{5} = -0.96
$$

**Step 3:** Compute perplexity (convert log-base):

$$
\begin{aligned}
PP &= 10^{-(-0.96)} = 10^{0.96} \\
&\approx 9.12
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The model is, on average, as "confused" as if it had to choose uniformly from ~9 words at each step. Lower is better.

### 3.5 Derivation V: Connection to Entropy

The **cross-entropy** $H(P, Q)$ of the true distribution $P$ under model $Q$ is:

$$
H(P, Q) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log_2 Q(w_i \mid \cdot)
$$

And perplexity is just the exponential of the cross-entropy:

$$
PP = 2^{H(P, Q)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The lower bound of perplexity is bounded below by the **true entropy** of the language — a limit that Claude Shannon estimated at roughly **1.3 bits per character** for English (i.e., perplexity $\approx 2.46$).

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Chain Rule (Exact) | Bigram (Markov-1) | Trigram (Markov-2) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | Highest | Moderate | High |
| **Data Required** | Astronomical | Large | Very large |
| **Parameters** | $V^n$ | $V^2$ | $V^3$ |
| **Sparsity** | Catastrophic | Severe | Extreme |
| **Used in Practice** | Theoretical | Sometimes | Yes (classical) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Probability of a String Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input String W = w1 w2 w3 ... wn] --> B[Tokenization & Vocabulary Mapping]
    B --> C[Add Sentence Boundary Tokens]
    C --> D[Apply N-gram Decomposition]
    D --> E[Markov Assumption Selection]
    E --> F[Lookup MLE Counts from Corpus]
    F --> G[Apply Smoothing if Zero Count]
    G --> H[Multiply All Conditional Probabilities]
    H --> I[Output P W as Scalar]
    I --> J[Log-space Conversion for Stability]
    J --> K[Final Probability or Perplexity]
```

### 4.2 Flowchart: Decision Logic for Smoothing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Query Probability P wi given history] --> B{Count of ngram in corpus?}
    B -- Count greater than 0 --> C[Use MLE Estimate]
    B -- Count equals 0 --> D{Smoothing Strategy?}
    D -- Laplace --> E[Apply Add 1 Formula]
    D -- Good Turing --> F[Use Frequency of Frequency 1]
    D -- Katz Backoff --> G[Back off to lower ngram]
    D -- Kneser Ney --> H[Use Continuation Probability]
    C --> I[Final Probability]
    E --> I
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture: Language Model Stack

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph corpus[Training Corpus Layer]
        T1[Raw Text] --> T2[Tokenization]
        T2 --> T3[Count Ngrams]
    end
    subgraph model[Model Layer]
        T3 --> M1[Build Probability Tables]
        M1 --> M2[Smoothing Module]
        M2 --> M3[Backoff Logic]
    end
    subgraph inference[Inference Layer]
        M3 --> I1[Input Sentence]
        I1 --> I2[Compute Joint Probability]
        I2 --> I3[Return P W or Perplexity]
    end
```

### 4.4 Schematic: Information Loss in Markov Approximation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Full History w1 to w i-1] -- Exact --> E[P wi given full history]
    A -- Markov 1 --> B[P wi given w i-1]
    A -- Markov 2 --> C[P wi given w i-2 w i-1]
    A -- Unigram --> D[P wi]
    E --- X[Information Loss grows as context shrinks]
    B --- X
    C --- X
    D --- X
```

### 4.5 Schematic: Probability Propagation in a Bigram Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Start Token] -- P w1 given s --> W1[w1 NLP]
    W1 -- P w2 given w1 --> W2[w2 is]
    W2 -- P w3 given w2 --> W3[w3 fun]
    W3 -- P END given w3 --> E[End Token]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**  
Define the *probability of a string* in the context of statistical language modeling. Why is it computationally intractable to compute it exactly using the chain rule?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The probability of a string $W = w_1 w_2 \dots w_n$ is the joint probability $P(W) = P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n)$, representing the likelihood that this exact word sequence occurs in a language.

By the **chain rule**:

$$P(w_1^n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})$$

This is **intractable** because at each position $i$, the number of possible histories $w_1^{i-1}$ grows as $V^{i-1}$, where $V$ is the vocabulary size. For a sentence of length 20 with $V = 100{,}000$, we would need to estimate an astronomical number of parameters from a finite corpus — a problem known as the **curse of dimensionality**. Hence, the **Markov assumption** is applied to reduce the history to a fixed window of $k$ words.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**  
What is **perplexity**? State the formula and explain its interpretation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Perplexity (PP)** is the standard intrinsic evaluation metric for a language model. It measures how "confused" the model is when predicting a test string.

$$PP(W) = \left( \prod_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{P(w_i \mid w_{i-1})} \right)^{1/N}$$

**Interpretation:**
- It represents the **effective branching factor** of the model — the average number of choices the model is uncertain about at each step.
- A **lower perplexity** indicates a better model (less surprised by unseen test data).
- A unigram model over a vocabulary of size $V$ with uniform probabilities has $PP = V$, the worst case.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`**

**(a)** Explain the **chain rule of probability** in the context of language modeling. Show how the **Markov assumption** leads to a bigram model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a tiny corpus:  
`<s> I love NLP </s> <s> I love deep learning </s> <s> I study NLP </s> <s> deep learning is fun </s>`  
Compute the **bigram probability** of the sentence "**I love NLP**" using MLE. Show all steps. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Chain Rule and Markov Assumption (7 marks):**

The **chain rule** decomposes the joint probability of a string into a product of conditional probabilities:

$$
P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_1, w_2, \dots, w_{i-1})
$$

Each factor $P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})$ requires conditioning on **all** previous words. This is **exact** but **infeasible** because the number of unique histories explodes exponentially with sentence length.

**The Markov Assumption (Order 1)** states that the probability of a word depends *only* on the immediately preceding word:

$$
P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1}) \approx P(w_i \mid w_{i-1})
$$

Applying this to the chain rule:

$$
P(w_1^n) \approx \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_{i-1})
$$

By introducing the sentence-start token $\langle s \rangle$ (so $w_0 = \langle s \rangle$), we obtain a uniform formulation:

$$
P(w_1^n) \approx P(w_1 \mid \langle s \rangle) \cdot P(w_2 \mid w_1) \cdots P(w_n \mid w_{n-1})
$$

Using **MLE**:

$$
P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \frac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i)}{C(w_{i-1})}
$$

This is the **bigram language model**. It reduces parameter space from $V^n$ to $V^2$, making estimation tractable at the cost of ignoring longer-range dependencies.

---

**(b) Computing P("I love NLP") with Bigram MLE (7 marks):**

**Step 1: List all bigrams and their counts from the corpus.**

| Bigram | Count $C$ |
|---|---|
| $\langle s \rangle$ I | 3 |
| I love | 2 |
| I study | 1 |
| love NLP | 1 |
| love deep | 1 |
| study NLP | 1 |
| NLP $\langle /s \rangle$ | 2 |
| deep learning | 2 |
| learning $\langle /s \rangle$ | 1 |
| learning is | 1 |
| is fun | 1 |
| fun $\langle /s \rangle$ | 1 |

**Step 2: Identify required unigram counts.**

- $C(\langle s \rangle) = 4$ (four sentences, so four start tokens)
- $C(\text{I}) = 3$ (appears three times)
- $C(\text{love}) = 2$ (appears twice)

**Step 3: Compute each bigram probability.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{I} \mid \langle s \rangle) &= \frac{C(\langle s \rangle, \text{I})}{C(\langle s \rangle)} = \frac{3}{4} = 0.75 \\
P(\text{love} \mid \text{I}) &= \frac{C(\text{I, love})}{C(\text{I})} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.6667 \\
P(\text{NLP} \mid \text{love}) &= \frac{C(\text{love, NLP})}{C(\text{love})} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the joint probability of "I love NLP".**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{I love NLP}) &= P(\text{I} \mid \langle s \rangle) \times P(\text{love} \mid \text{I}) \times P(\text{NLP} \mid \text{love}) \\
&= 0.75 \times \frac{2}{3} \times 0.5 \\
&= 0.75 \times 0.3333 \\
&= 0.25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $P(\text{I love NLP}) = 0.25$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`**

**(a)** Explain the **zero-probability problem** in n-gram language models. Describe **Laplace (Add-1) smoothing** and **Katz backoff** as two solutions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A unigram language model is built on a corpus of $N = 50{,}000$ tokens with vocabulary size $V = 5{,}000$. The word "**computational**" occurs $C = 200$ times. Compute: (i) the MLE probability, (ii) the Add-1 (Laplace) probability, and (iii) the Add-0.5 probability. Comment on the results. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Zero-Probability Problem and Smoothing Solutions (7 marks):**

**The Zero-Probability Problem:**  
In a maximum-likelihood n-gram model, any n-gram that never appeared in the training corpus is assigned $P = 0$. Since the joint probability is a **product** of conditional probabilities, even **one** unseen n-gram makes the **entire string probability vanish** ($P(W) = 0$). This is catastrophic for any downstream application (e.g., a translation system rejecting a perfectly good sentence).

**Solution 1: Laplace (Add-1) Smoothing**  
Pretend we saw every n-gram at least once. The MLE formula is modified as:

$$
P_{\text{Lap}}(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \frac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i) + 1}{C(w_{i-1}) + V}
$$

**Pros:** Simple, guarantees no zero probabilities.  
**Cons:** Steals too much probability mass from common events; degrades model accuracy.

**Solution 2: Katz Backoff**  
A two-tier strategy:
1. If the n-gram was seen in training → use the **discounted MLE** (Good–Turing).
2. If unseen → **back off** to the $(n-1)$-gram model, scaled by a backoff weight $\alpha$:

$$
P_{\text{Katz}}(w \mid h) =
\begin{cases}
P^*(w \mid h) & \text{if } C(h, w) > 0 \\
\alpha(h) \cdot P_{\text{Katz}}(w \mid h') & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

where $h'$ is the shortened history (e.g., dropping the earliest word).

**Pros:** Preserves the integrity of frequent n-grams while gracefully handling rare ones.  
**Cons:** More complex; requires careful tuning of discount parameters.

---

**(b) Worked Numerical Example (7 marks):**

**Given:** $N = 50{,}000$, $V = 5{,}000$, $C(\text{computational}) = 200$.

**(i) MLE Probability:**

$$
P_{\text{ML}}(\text{computational}) = \frac{C(\text{computational})}{N} = \frac{200}{50{,}000} = 0.004 = 4 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**(ii) Add-1 (Laplace) Probability:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{Lap}}(\text{computational}) &= \frac{C(\text{computational}) + 1}{N + V} \\
&= \frac{200 + 1}{50{,}000 + 5{,}000} \\
&= \frac{201}{55{,}000} \\
&\approx 3.655 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Add-0.5 Probability:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{k=0.5}(\text{computational}) &= \frac{C(\text{computational}) + 0.5}{N + 0.5 \cdot V} \\
&= \frac{200 + 0.5}{50{,}000 + 0.5 \times 5{,}000} \\
&= \frac{200.5}{52{,}500} \\
&\approx 3.819 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Commentary:**  
- The MLE estimate ($4.0 \times 10^{-3}$) is the **highest** because it uses no smoothing and assumes all probability mass is in observed events.  
- Laplace smoothing **reduces** this probability by spreading mass across all $5{,}000$ unseen words, depressing the common word slightly.  
- Add-0.5 lies **between** MLE and Laplace — it is a softer adjustment.  
- The relative differences are small here, but for very rare words (count $\leq 5$) the divergence becomes significant, and Add-0.5 typically yields better perplexity than Add-1.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the start token** $\langle s \rangle$ when computing $P(w_1)$ — this leads to an incorrect first bigram factor.
> 2. **Misapplying Laplace smoothing** — students often add 1 to the *numerator only* and forget to update the *denominator* with $+V$. Both must be adjusted.
> 3. **Confusing unigram and bigram MLE formulas** — a unigram uses total tokens $N$ in the denominator; a bigram uses $C(w_{i-1})$.
> 4. **Not converting perplexity to the correct base** — some textbooks use natural log, some use log-base-2, some log-base-10. Always state the base explicitly.
> 5. **Ignoring boundary conditions** — questions on the probability of a string in KTU often include the $\langle /s \rangle$ end token; omitting it changes the answer.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — The Probability of a String**

- **Definition:** $P(W) = P(w_1^n)$ is the joint probability of a word sequence.
- **Chain Rule:** $P(w_1^n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})$ — exact but intractable.
- **Markov Assumption (order $k$):** Approximate $P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1}) \approx P(w_i \mid w_{i-k+1}^{i-1})$.
- **N-gram Models:** Unigram ($n=1$), Bigram ($n=2$), Trigram ($n=3$), 4-gram ($n=4$).
- **MLE for Bigrams:** $P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \frac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i)}{C(w_{i-1})}$.
- **Sentence Start Token:** $w_0 = \langle s \rangle$ to make all bigrams consistent.
- **Zero-Probability Problem:** Unseen n-grams collapse the joint probability.
- **Smoothing Techniques:** Laplace (Add-1), Add-k, Good–Turing, Katz Backoff, Kneser–Ney.
- **Perplexity Formula:** $PP(W) = \left( \prod_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{P(w_i \mid \cdot)} \right)^{1/N}$ — **lower is better**.
- **Cross-Entropy Connection:** $PP = 2^{H(P, Q)}$.
- **Shannon's Limit:** English entropy $\approx 1.3$ bits/char → lower-bound perplexity $\approx 2.46$.
- **Always work in log-space** to avoid underflow: $\log P(W) = \sum_i \log P(w_i \mid \cdot)$.
- **Boundary tokens** $\langle s \rangle$ and $\langle /s \rangle$ must be included for proper probability normalization.
- **Engineering Applications:** Speech recognition, machine translation, autocomplete, spell check, bioinformatics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Using Inside Probabilities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Using Inside Probabilities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inside Probability** $\alpha(i, j, A)$ is defined for a **Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar (PCFG)** as the total probability of generating the terminal string spanning positions $i$ through $j$ (inclusive) in a sentence, starting from a specific nonterminal $A$ and using all possible sub-derivations.

Mathematically, for a sentence $w = w_1 w_2 \ldots w_n$ of length $n$ and a nonterminal $A \in N$:

$$
\alpha(i, j, A) = P(A \overset{*}{\Rightarrow} w_i w_{i+1} \ldots w_j)
$$

The term **"inside"** refers to the fact that we are computing the probability of the substring that lies **inside** the span of nonterminal $A$ — i.e., the terminals generated entirely within the subtree rooted at $A$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine you are a detective trying to figure out how a particular phrase in a sentence was formed. The "inside probability" tells you, *"Given a grammar rule, how likely is it that this exact chunk of words was produced by that rule?"*

Think of it like a **tree of possibilities**:
- A sentence can be built in many different ways (many parse trees).
- Each nonterminal (like a `NP` — Noun Phrase) can produce a span of words through many different rule expansions.
- **Inside probability sums up all those different ways** to produce a particular substring.

**Another analogy:** Consider a factory assembly line. Each machine (nonterminal) can produce a specific product (terminal string) using several different recipes (rule applications). The inside probability is essentially the **total success rate** of one machine producing a particular product, summed across all recipes.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Anchored to KTU 2024 Module Outcomes

| Term | Notation | Meaning |
|------|----------|---------|
| Nonterminal | $A, B, C$ | Grammar symbols (e.g., S, NP, VP) |
| Terminal | $a, b, w_k$ | Actual words in the sentence |
| Sentence Span | $[i, j]$ | Word positions from $i$ to $j$ |
| Span Length | $l = j - i + 1$ | Number of words covered |
| Start Symbol | $S$ | Root of the parse tree |
| Span Length = 1 | Base Case | Lexical/single word derivation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Inside probabilities are a foundational component of the **CKY (Cocke–Kasami–Younger) parsing algorithm** and the **Inside-Outside Algorithm** used for unsupervised PCFG parameter estimation. Both are examinable topics in the KTU ESE (End Semester Examination).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — How Inside Probability is Computed

The computation follows a **bottom-up dynamic programming** approach, similar to filling a triangular table in the CKY algorithm:

**Step 1 — Base Case (Spans of length 1):**
For each word $w_i$ and each rule of the form $A \rightarrow w_i$:

$$
\alpha(i, i, A) = P(A \rightarrow w_i)
$$

**Step 2 — Recursive Case (Spans of length $l \geq 2$):**
For each split point $k$ where $i \leq k < j$, and for each rule of the form $A \rightarrow B \, C$:

$$
\alpha(i, j, A) = \sum_{k=i}^{j-1} \sum_{B, C \in N} P(A \rightarrow B \, C) \cdot \alpha(i, k, B) \cdot \alpha(k+1, j, C)
$$

**Step 3 — The "Why" Behind the Logic:**
- We consider **all possible binary splits** of the span $[i, j]$ at position $k$.
- For each split, we combine the inside probability of the **left sub-span** (rooted at $B$) and the **right sub-span** (rooted at $C$).
- We multiply by the rule probability $P(A \rightarrow B\,C)$ and sum across all rule choices and split points.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize this table — these are the most frequently tested formulas in KTU ESE for this topic.**

| # | Formula | Description | Span Length |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | $\alpha(i, i, A) = P(A \rightarrow w_i)$ | Base case for terminal rule | $l = 1$ |
| 2 | $\alpha(i, j, A) = \sum_{k=i}^{j-1} \sum_{B, C} P(A \rightarrow B\,C) \cdot \alpha(i, k, B) \cdot \alpha(k+1, j, C)$ | Recursive case for binary rule | $l \geq 2$ |
| 3 | $P(w_1 \ldots w_n) = \alpha(1, n, S)$ | Total probability of sentence (using start symbol) | Full span |
| 4 | $P(w_1 \ldots w_n) = \sum_{A \in N} \alpha(1, n, A)$ | Equivalent expression summing all root nonterminals | Full span |
| 5 | $P(A \rightarrow B\,C \text{ used in } [i, j] \text{ with split } k) = \dfrac{P(A \rightarrow B\,C) \cdot \alpha(i, k, B) \cdot \alpha(k+1, j, C)}{\alpha(i, j, A)}$ | Posterior rule probability | Inside ratio |
| 6 | $P(A \text{ spans } [i, j]) = \dfrac{\beta(i, j, A) \cdot \alpha(i, j, A)}{P(w)}$ | Marginal nonterminal span probability | Full derivation |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Inside probabilities are deployed extensively in:

- **Machine Translation (MT):** Google Translate, Microsoft Translator, and OpenNMT systems use PCFG-based reordering models where inside scores help rank translation hypotheses.
- **Speech Recognition:** Acoustic and language model combination uses CFG-like structures with probabilistic scoring.
- **Bioinformatics:** RNA secondary structure prediction (e.g., **pseudoknot removal** in Covariance Models) uses inside algorithms analogous to PCFG inside probabilities.
- **Information Extraction:** Constituency parsing of web text for downstream tasks (sentiment analysis, question answering).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary condition to remember:** A nonterminal $A$ that **cannot** derive the substring $w_i \ldots w_j$ will have $\alpha(i, j, A) = 0$. This sparsity is what makes CKY tractable on real grammars.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Inside Probability Recursion

We derive the inside probability formula from first principles using the probabilistic chain rule and the assumption of a probabilistic context-free grammar (PCFG).

**Step 1 — Define the Goal**
We want the probability that a nonterminal $A$ derives the substring $w_i \ldots w_j$:

$$
P(A \overset{*}{\Rightarrow} w_i \ldots w_j)
$$

**Step 2 — Decompose by First Rule Applied**
The first rule applied in the derivation must be of the form $A \rightarrow \beta$ for some right-hand side $\beta$. By the law of total probability:

$$
P(A \overset{*}{\Rightarrow} w_i \ldots w_j) = \sum_{A \rightarrow \beta} P(A \rightarrow \beta) \cdot P(\beta \overset{*}{\Rightarrow} w_i \ldots w_j)
$$

**Step 3 — Handle the Terminal Case**
If $\beta = w_i$ and the span is a single word ($i = j$):

$$
P(w_i \overset{*}{\Rightarrow} w_i) = 1
$$

Therefore:

$$
\alpha(i, i, A) = \sum_{A \rightarrow w_i} P(A \rightarrow w_i)
$$

**Step 4 — Handle the Binary Case**
If $\beta = B \, C$ and the span covers multiple words, we must choose a split point $k$ such that $B$ derives $w_i \ldots w_k$ and $C$ derives $w_{k+1} \ldots w_j$:

$$
P(B\,C \overset{*}{\Rightarrow} w_i \ldots w_j) = \sum_{k=i}^{j-1} P(B \overset{*}{\Rightarrow} w_i \ldots w_k) \cdot P(C \overset{*}{\Rightarrow} w_{k+1} \ldots w_j)
$$

This relies on the **independence assumption** of PCFGs — the two sub-derivations are independent given the nonterminals $B$ and $C$.

**Step 5 — Combine**
Substituting back:

$$
\alpha(i, j, A) = \sum_{A \rightarrow B\,C} P(A \rightarrow B\,C) \cdot \sum_{k=i}^{j-1} \alpha(i, k, B) \cdot \alpha(k+1, j, C)
$$

Rearranging the summations:

$$
\alpha(i, j, A) = \sum_{k=i}^{j-1} \sum_{B, C} P(A \rightarrow B\,C) \cdot \alpha(i, k, B) \cdot \alpha(k+1, j, C)
$$

This is the **canonical inside probability recurrence**.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU ESE Standard)

**Problem Setup:**
Consider the PCFG with the following rules:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow NP \, VP \quad [0.8] \\
S &\rightarrow VP \quad [0.2] \\
NP &\rightarrow \text{John} \quad [0.5] \\
NP &\rightarrow \text{Mary} \quad [0.5] \\
VP &\rightarrow \text{runs} \quad [1.0]
\end{aligned}
$$

Sentence: $w_1 w_2 =$ "John runs"

**Step 1 — Compute Base Case (length 1 spans)**

For position $i = 1$, word = "John":

$$
\alpha(1, 1, NP) = P(NP \rightarrow \text{John}) = 0.5
$$

For position $i = 2$, word = "runs":

$$
\alpha(2, 2, VP) = P(VP \rightarrow \text{runs}) = 1.0
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Full Span (length 2)**

For span $[1, 2]$ with $S$ as root, the only split is $k = 1$:

$$
\alpha(1, 2, S) = \sum_{B, C} P(S \rightarrow B\,C) \cdot \alpha(1, 1, B) \cdot \alpha(2, 2, C)
$$

The applicable rule is $S \rightarrow NP\,VP$ with $B = NP, C = VP$:

$$
\alpha(1, 2, S) = P(S \rightarrow NP\,VP) \cdot \alpha(1, 1, NP) \cdot \alpha(2, 2, VP)
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\alpha(1, 2, S) = 0.8 \times 0.5 \times 1.0 = 0.4
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the Result**

$$
P(\text{"John runs"}) = \alpha(1, 2, S) = 0.4
$$

This is the total probability of the sentence "John runs" under the given PCFG, computed using the inside algorithm.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Multi-Rule Worked Example

Now consider a more complex PCFG with multiple binary rules:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow NP\,VP \quad [0.7] \\
S &\rightarrow NP\,NP \quad [0.3] \\
NP &\rightarrow \text{John} \quad [0.6] \\
NP &\rightarrow \text{Mary} \quad [0.4] \\
VP &\rightarrow \text{laughs} \quad [1.0]
\end{aligned}
$$

Sentence: $w_1 w_2 =$ "Mary laughs"

**Step 1 — Base Cases**

$$
\alpha(1, 1, NP) = P(NP \rightarrow \text{Mary}) = 0.4
$$

$$
\alpha(2, 2, VP) = P(VP \rightarrow \text{laughs}) = 1.0
$$

**Step 2 — Recursive Case for $S$**

Only one split point $k = 1$. We consider both rules $S \rightarrow NP\,VP$ and $S \rightarrow NP\,NP$:

$$
\alpha(1, 2, S) = P(S \rightarrow NP\,VP) \cdot \alpha(1, 1, NP) \cdot \alpha(2, 2, VP) + P(S \rightarrow NP\,NP) \cdot \alpha(1, 1, NP) \cdot \alpha(2, 2, NP)
$$

For the second term, $\alpha(2, 2, NP) = 0$ because the word "laughs" is not in NP's terminal rules. So:

$$
\alpha(1, 2, S) = (0.7 \times 0.4 \times 1.0) + (0.3 \times 0.4 \times 0) = 0.28 + 0 = 0.28
$$

**Step 3 — Total Sentence Probability**

$$
P(\text{"Mary laughs"}) = 0.28
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The sparsity property — $\alpha(2, 2, NP) = 0$ — is what makes inside probability computation efficient. In real-world PCFGs with thousands of rules, most cells in the CKY chart become zero, reducing the effective computation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of the Inside Algorithm

The following Mermaid block diagram maps out the complete flow of how inside probabilities are computed bottom-up and subsequently used.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Sentence w1 w2 ... wn] --> B[Input: PCFG Rules with Probabilities]
    B --> C[Initialize CKY Chart Table]
    C --> D[Fill Base Cases: span length = 1]
    D --> E{Loop over span lengths L = 2 to n}
    E --> F[Loop over starting positions i = 1 to n-L+1]
    F --> G[Compute j = i + L - 1]
    G --> H[Loop over split points k = i to j-1]
    H --> I[Loop over grammar rules A to B C]
    I --> J[Compute: rule_prob x alpha(i,k,B) x alpha(k+1,j,C)]
    J --> K[Accumulate Sum into alpha(i,j,A)]
    K --> L{All k done?}
    L -->|No| H
    L -->|Yes| M{All i for this L done?}
    M -->|No| F
    M -->|Yes| N{L equals n?}
    N -->|No| E
    N -->|Yes| O[Output: alpha 1,n,S = Total Sentence Probability]
    O --> P[Application 1: CKY Parsing]
    O --> Q[Application 2: Inside Outside Re-estimation]
    O --> R[Application 3: Best Parse Tree Selection]
```

### 4.2 Tree Structure Visualization — How Inside Probability Sums Over Subtrees

The following diagram illustrates how inside probability accumulates contributions from all subtrees spanning a given substring.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Nonterminal A at span i,j] --> Split[Choose split point k]
    Split --> LeftSub[Nonterminal B at span i,k]
    Split --> RightSub[Nonterminal C at span k+1,j]
    LeftSub --> Lrule1[B to beta1 with prob p1]
    LeftSub --> Lrule2[B to beta2 with prob p2]
    LeftSub --> Lrule3[B to beta3 with prob p3]
    RightSub --> Rrule1[C to gamma1 with prob q1]
    RightSub --> Rrule2[C to gamma2 with prob q2]
    Lrule1 --> Lterm[Terminals w_i ... w_k]
    Lrule2 --> Lterm
    Lrule3 --> Lterm
    Rrule1 --> Rterm[Terminals w_k+1 ... w_j]
    Rrule2 --> Rterm
```

### 4.3 CKY Chart Structure (Triangular Dynamic Programming Table)

The following diagram shows the table structure used to store inside probabilities during CKY-style computation for a 4-word sentence.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cell11[alpha 1,1] --- cell22[alpha 2,2] --- cell33[alpha 3,3] --- cell44[alpha 4,4]
    cell12[alpha 1,2] --- cell23[alpha 2,3] --- cell34[alpha 3,4]
    cell13[alpha 1,3] --- cell24[alpha 2,4]
    cell14[alpha 1,4 S only]
```

| Span Coverage | Cells Filled |
|---------------|--------------|
| Length 1 | $\alpha(1,1), \alpha(2,2), \alpha(3,3), \alpha(4,4)$ |
| Length 2 | $\alpha(1,2), \alpha(2,3), \alpha(3,4)$ |
| Length 3 | $\alpha(1,3), \alpha(2,4)$ |
| Length 4 | $\alpha(1,4)$ — total sentence probability |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the table:** Each cell $\alpha(i, j, A)$ represents the inside probability for a specific nonterminal $A$ at span $[i, j]$. Multiple nonterminals can occupy the same cell (in practice, we maintain a list per cell). The algorithm fills the table **bottom-up by increasing span length**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define inside probability in a PCFG. Write its recurrence relation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Inside probability $\alpha(i, j, A)$ is the total probability that nonterminal $A$ derives the terminal substring $w_i \ldots w_j$ in one or more steps.

**Recurrence relation:**

$$
\alpha(i, i, A) = \sum_{A \rightarrow w_i} P(A \rightarrow w_i)
$$

$$
\alpha(i, j, A) = \sum_{k=i}^{j-1} \sum_{B, C} P(A \rightarrow B\,C) \cdot \alpha(i, k, B) \cdot \alpha(k+1, j, C)
$$

The base case applies when span length equals 1 (single terminal), and the recursive case handles binary rule expansions by summing over all split points.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**How is the total probability of a sentence computed using inside probabilities?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The total probability of a sentence $w_1 w_2 \ldots w_n$ under a PCFG is the inside probability of the **start symbol $S$** spanning the entire sentence:

$$
P(w_1 w_2 \ldots w_n) = \alpha(1, n, S)
$$

Alternatively, since any nonterminal could theoretically generate the full sentence (though in practice only $S$ does so meaningfully):

$$
P(w_1 w_2 \ldots w_n) = \sum_{A \in N} \alpha(1, n, A)
$$

This quantity is computed by the CKY algorithm in $O(n^3 \cdot \vert G \vert)$ time, where $\vert G \vert$ is the size of the grammar.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** Consider the following PCFG and compute the inside probabilities for the sentence "she eats":

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow NP\,VP \quad [1.0] \\
NP &\rightarrow \text{she} \quad [0.6] \\
NP &\rightarrow \text{tasty} \quad [0.4] \\
VP &\rightarrow \text{eats} \quad [1.0]
\end{aligned}
$$

Show all steps clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain how inside probabilities are used in the **Inside-Outside algorithm** for PCFG parameter re-estimation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Initialize and Identify Base Spans**
Sentence: $w_1 = $ "she", $w_2 = $ "eats", $n = 2$.

**Step 2 — Base Cases (length 1)**

For position $i = 1$, word "she":

$$
\alpha(1, 1, NP) = P(NP \rightarrow \text{she}) = 0.6
$$

For position $i = 2$, word "eats":

$$
\alpha(2, 2, VP) = P(VP \rightarrow \text{eats}) = 1.0
$$

No other base-case values are non-zero (e.g., $\alpha(1,1,VP) = 0$, $\alpha(2,2,NP) = 0$).

**Step 3 — Recursive Case for Span [1, 2]**

Only split point is $k = 1$. We apply the rule $S \rightarrow NP\,VP$:

$$
\alpha(1, 2, S) = P(S \rightarrow NP\,VP) \cdot \alpha(1, 1, NP) \cdot \alpha(2, 2, VP)
$$

Substituting:

$$
\alpha(1, 2, S) = 1.0 \times 0.6 \times 1.0 = 0.6
$$

**Step 4 — Final Answer**

$$
P(\text{"she eats"}) = \alpha(1, 2, S) = 0.6
$$

---

**Model Answer (b) — 7 marks:**

The **Inside-Outside algorithm** is an **Expectation-Maximization (EM)** procedure used to learn PCFG rule probabilities from unannotated (unparsed) text corpora. Inside probabilities play a central role in the **Expectation (E) step**.

**E-Step (using Inside Probabilities):**
For each rule $A \rightarrow B\,C$ and each span $[i, j]$ with split point $k$, the expected count of that rule being used is:

$$
E[\text{count of } A \rightarrow B\,C \text{ at } [i, j, k]] = \frac{P(A \rightarrow B\,C) \cdot \alpha(i, k, B) \cdot \alpha(k+1, j, C) \cdot \beta(i, j, A)}{P(w)}
$$

where $\beta(i, j, A)$ is the corresponding **outside probability** (probability of generating the rest of the sentence given that $A$ spans $[i, j]$).

**M-Step (Parameter Re-estimation):**
The new rule probability is computed as:

$$
P_{\text{new}}(A \rightarrow B\,C) = \frac{\text{Expected count of rule usage}}{\text{Expected count of } A \text{ in any rule}}
$$

**Role of Inside Probabilities:**
- They quantify how strongly each nonterminal contributes to generating each substring.
- They enable computation of the **expected rule counts** without enumerating all possible parse trees.
- This is essential for **unsupervised grammar induction**, a key application in computational linguistics research.

**Termination:** The EM iterations continue until the change in rule probabilities (or total log-likelihood of the corpus) falls below a threshold.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Define **inside probability** and **outside probability** in a PCFG. Write the complete inside probability recurrence relation with all base cases and recursive cases. Explain the independence assumption used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the following PCFG, compute the inside probability table (CKY chart) for the sentence "a b" using the rules:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow NP\,VP \quad [0.9] \\
NP &\rightarrow a \quad [1.0] \\
VP &\rightarrow b \quad [1.0]
\end{aligned}
$$

What is $P(\text{"a b"})$? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer (a) — 7 marks:**

**Inside Probability $\alpha(i, j, A)$:**
The total probability that nonterminal $A$ derives the substring $w_i \ldots w_j$ in zero or more steps.

**Outside Probability $\beta(i, j, A)$:**
The probability that the start symbol $S$ derives the entire sentence, given that $A$ derives the substring $w_i \ldots w_j$. Mathematically:

$$
\beta(i, j, A) = P(S \overset{*}{\Rightarrow} w_1 \ldots w_{i-1} \, A \, w_{j+1} \ldots w_n)
$$

**Complete Inside Probability Recurrence:**

Base case (terminal rule):

$$
\alpha(i, i, A) = \sum_{A \rightarrow w_i} P(A \rightarrow w_i)
$$

Recursive case (binary rule):

$$
\alpha(i, j, A) = \sum_{k=i}^{j-1} \sum_{B, C \in N} P(A \rightarrow B\,C) \cdot \alpha(i, k, B) \cdot \alpha(k+1, j, C)
$$

**Independence Assumption:**
The PCFG assumes that once a rule $A \rightarrow B\,C$ is applied, the derivations of the left child $B$ and the right child $C$ are **conditionally independent** of each other given the nonterminals. This is why the inside probability factors as a product $\alpha(i, k, B) \cdot \alpha(k+1, j, C)$ — we can multiply the probabilities of the two sub-derivations because they do not influence each other.

This assumption is what makes PCFGs tractable and enables efficient dynamic programming. The trade-off is reduced modeling power compared to lexicalized or dependency-based models.

---

**Model Answer (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Identify Sentence Components**
Sentence: $w_1 = $ "a", $w_2 = $ "b", $n = 2$.

**Step 2 — Base Cases (length 1)**

For position $i = 1$, word "a":

$$
\alpha(1, 1, NP) = P(NP \rightarrow a) = 1.0
$$

For position $i = 2$, word "b":

$$
\alpha(2, 2, VP) = P(VP \rightarrow b) = 1.0
$$

All other single-span inside probabilities are 0.

**Step 3 — Compute Full Span [1, 2] for $S$**

Only split point is $k = 1$. Applying rule $S \rightarrow NP\,VP$:

$$
\alpha(1, 2, S) = P(S \rightarrow NP\,VP) \cdot \alpha(1, 1, NP) \cdot \alpha(2, 2, VP)
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\alpha(1, 2, S) = 0.9 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 = 0.9
$$

**Step 4 — CKY Chart Summary**

| Cell | Value |
|------|-------|
| $\alpha(1, 1, NP)$ | $1.0$ |
| $\alpha(1, 1, VP)$ | $0$ |
| $\alpha(2, 2, NP)$ | $0$ |
| $\alpha(2, 2, VP)$ | $1.0$ |
| $\alpha(1, 2, S)$ | $0.9$ |
| $\alpha(1, 2, NP)$ | $0$ |
| $\alpha(1, 2, VP)$ | $0$ |

**Step 5 — Final Answer**

$$
P(\text{"a b"}) = \alpha(1, 2, S) = 0.9
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> Students commonly lose marks in inside probability questions for the following reasons:
>
> 1. **Forgetting the base case:** Failing to explicitly compute $\alpha(i, i, A)$ for length-1 spans before tackling larger spans. **Always start from the bottom of the CKY chart.**
>
> 2. **Missing the sum over split points:** Writing only one term of the sum $\sum_{k=i}^{j-1}$ instead of the full summation. For spans of length $> 2$, there are **multiple split points** that must all be considered.
>
> 3. **Ignoring zero values:** Failing to set $\alpha(i, j, A) = 0$ when no applicable rule exists. This leads to incorrect values in the CKY chart.
>
> 4. **Confusing inside with outside probability:** Inside probability is **for the span $[i,j]$ under $A$**, while outside probability is **for everything outside $[i,j]$ given $A$**. Mixing them up is a common error.
>
> 5. **Not showing the final sentence probability explicitly:** The question often asks for $P(w_1 \ldots w_n)$, which requires you to state the final value of $\alpha(1, n, S)$ clearly as the answer.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to multiply rule probability:** Many students write $\alpha(i, k, B) \cdot \alpha(k+1, j, C)$ but forget to multiply by $P(A \rightarrow B\,C)$ — this leads to a **2-mark penalty minimum**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Inside probability** $\alpha(i, j, A)$ = total probability of $A$ deriving the substring $w_i \ldots w_j$.
- **Base case** is for span length 1: $\alpha(i, i, A) = P(A \rightarrow w_i)$.
- **Recursive case** involves a **double summation**: over split points $k$ and over pairs of nonterminals $(B, C)$.
- **Total sentence probability** is $\alpha(1, n, S)$ where $S$ is the start symbol.
- Inside probability is computed **bottom-up** (smallest spans first) using dynamic programming.
- It is a key component of the **CKY parsing algorithm** (membership and probability) and the **Inside-Outside algorithm** (EM training).
- The **independence assumption** of PCFGs allows the inside probability to factor as a product of two sub-derivation probabilities.
- **Time complexity:** $O(n^3 \cdot \vert G \vert)$ for sentence length $n$ and grammar size $\vert G \vert$.
- **Space complexity:** $O(n^2 \cdot \vert N \vert)$ for the CKY chart.
- Sparse grammars (many rules yielding $\alpha = 0$) make the algorithm efficient in practice.
- Inside probability works for **binary branching grammars** (CNF form); for non-CNF grammars, the recursion generalizes to handle n-ary rules.
- **Connection to linguistics:** Constituency parsing in natural language processing (Penn Treebank, Stanford Parser) relies on PCFGs and inside-style computations.
- **Be careful with indexing:** The split at $k$ means $B$ covers $[i, k]$ and $C$ covers $[k+1, j]$ — a one-position overlap is a common indexing bug.
- **KTU 2024 frequently tests:** definition (3 marks), numerical computation (7-10 marks), and application explanation (4-7 marks). Master all three question types.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Using Outside Probabilities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Using Outside Probabilities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Probabilistic Context-Free Grammars (PCFGs)** and the **Inside–Outside Algorithm**, the **Outside Probability** of a non-terminal $A$ spanning the substring $w_i \dots w_j$ is defined as the probability that the grammar $G$ generates the entire string $w_1 \dots w_n$, *given* that the non-terminal $A$ is responsible for generating the substring between positions $i$ and $j$. In standard notation:

$$\beta(A, i, j) = P\big( w_1 \dots w_{i-1}, \, A \Rightarrow^* w_i \dots w_j, \, w_{j+1} \dots w_n \,\big)$$

The corresponding **Inside Probability** (recap) is:

$$\alpha(A, i, j) = P(A \Rightarrow^* w_i \dots w_j)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary condition (KTU Board Standard):** $\beta(S, 1, n) = 1$ where $S$ is the distinguished start symbol. This anchors the recursion: everything *outside* the topmost span contributes probability 1, because there is nothing "above" the start symbol.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The product $\alpha(A, i, j) \cdot \beta(A, i, j)$ gives the joint probability of the complete string *and* the event that $A$ covers span $(i,j)$. Dividing by $P(w_1^n) = \alpha(S, 1, n)$ yields the **marginal probability** of that constituent — this is the foundation of statistical parsing, lexicalized grammar re-estimation, and unsupervised grammar induction.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are a **detective reconstructing a jigsaw puzzle of a sentence**, where each word is a piece and each grammar rule is a possible way of grouping pieces.

- The **Inside Probability** $\alpha(A, i, j)$ answers: *"How likely is it that this group of pieces (positions $i$ to $j$) was glued together by label $A$?"* — i.e., probability of an *internal* cluster.
- The **Outside Probability** $\beta(A, i, j)$ answers the complementary question: *"Given that pieces $i$ to $j$ were glued together as $A$, how likely is it that the rest of the puzzle (everything *outside* this cluster) gets assembled correctly around it?"*

Together, they let you compute: *"How probable is this particular way of grouping the pieces, in the context of the whole puzzle?"* — which is exactly the marginal probability of a constituent.

> [!TIP]
> A simple, memorable mnemonic: **INSIDE = "I built my little block"**, **OUTSIDE = "Did the rest of the world get built correctly around my block?"**

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Value / Role |
|---|---|---|
| $\alpha(\cdot)$ | Inside probability | Recursion in $O(n^3 \cdot \vert G \vert)$ |
| $\beta(\cdot)$ | Outside probability | Recursion in $O(n^3 \cdot \vert G \vert)$ |
| $n$ | Sentence length (in tokens) | 1 to $N$ |
| $\vert G \vert$ | Grammar size (number of rules) | Static parameter |
| $P(w_1^n)$ | Sentence probability under $G$ | $= \alpha(S, 1, n)$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic: How Outside Probabilities Are Computed

The outside probability $\beta(A, i, j)$ is computed via **dynamic programming**, working *outward-in* — i.e., we first initialize $\beta(S, 1, n) = 1$, then propagate probabilities from larger spans down to smaller ones. The key recurrence is constructed by considering every rule in the grammar that has $A$ as one of its children.

### Step-by-step Intuition

1. **Anchor the top:** $\beta(S, 1, n) = 1$. The start symbol $S$ has *no parent* — nothing exists outside it.
