2. **Find a parent:** For any other non-terminal $A$ spanning $(i, j)$, look for every rule $B \rightarrow X_1 \dots X_k$ such that one of the $X_r$ equals $A$.
3. **Identify siblings:** The siblings $X_1, \dots, X_{r-1}, X_{r+1}, \dots, X_k$ must already have their inside probabilities computed for their respective sub-spans.
4. **Combine three factors** for each candidate parent rule:
    - The **outside probability** of the parent $B$ at the parent's span.
    - The **rule probability** $P(B \rightarrow X_1 \dots X_k)$.
    - The **inside probabilities** of all sibling children.
5. **Sum over all such parents, rules, and splits** — this yields $\beta(A, i, j)$.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use the formulas below directly when solving Part B problems. The CNF (Chomsky Normal Form) version is the most commonly tested variant in KTU exams.

| # | Quantity | Formula (CNF) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Inside base | $\alpha(A, i, i) = P(A \rightarrow w_i)$ | For preterminal rules |
| 2 | Inside recurse | $\alpha(A, i, j) = \displaystyle\sum_{B, C} \sum_{k=i}^{j-1} P(A \rightarrow BC) \cdot \alpha(B, i, k) \cdot \alpha(C, k+1, j)$ | Binary rules only |
| 3 | Outside base | $\beta(S, 1, n) = 1$ | Start symbol only |
| 4a | Outside recurse (A = left child) | $\beta(A, i, j) = \displaystyle\sum_{B, C} \sum_{k=j+1}^{n} P(B \rightarrow AC) \cdot \beta(B, i, k) \cdot \alpha(C, j+1, k)$ | $A$ is the **first** child of $B$ |
| 4b | Outside recurse (A = right child) | $\beta(A, i, j) = \displaystyle\sum_{B, C} \sum_{k=1}^{i-1} P(B \rightarrow CA) \cdot \beta(B, k, j) \cdot \alpha(C, k, i-1)$ | $A$ is the **second** child of $B$ |
| 5 | Sentence prob. | $P(w_1^n) = \alpha(S, 1, n)$ | Used as normalizer |
| 6 | Marginal prob. of constituent | $P(A, i, j \mid w_1^n) = \dfrac{\alpha(A, i, j) \cdot \beta(A, i, j)}{\alpha(S, 1, n)}$ | Most important "use" |
| 7 | Expected count of rule $A \rightarrow BC$ | $c(A \rightarrow BC) = \dfrac{1}{P(w_1^n)} \displaystyle\sum_{i, j, k} \alpha(B, i, k) \cdot P(A \rightarrow BC) \cdot \alpha(C, k+1, j) \cdot \beta(A, i, j)$ | Used in EM re-estimation |

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters

- **Unsupervised Grammar Induction (EM):** Outside probabilities, combined with inside, drive the *Expectation* step of the Inside–Outside algorithm. Rule probabilities are re-estimated without treebank annotation.
- **Statistical Parsing:** Marginal probabilities $\frac{\alpha \cdot \beta}{P(w)}$ let us rank competing parses, extract $k$-best constituents, and compute parse entropy.
- **Language Modeling for PCFGs:** Outside probabilities are essential when computing sentence perplexity under a PCFG.
- **Speech & MT Alignment:** Analogous forward–backward computations in HMMs and word-alignment models are direct extensions of the inside–outside paradigm.

> [!NOTE]
> A common KTU pitfall: confusing $\alpha$ (inside) and $\beta$ (outside) directionality. Remember: **inside builds from leaves up**, **outside flows from root down**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Outside Recurrence (General Case)

Let $G = (N, \Sigma, R, S)$ be a PCFG, with $N$ non-terminals, $\Sigma$ terminals, $R$ rules, and $S$ the start symbol. Let $w_1 \dots w_n$ be the input string.

**Definition (Outside Probability):**

$$\beta(A, i, j) = P\big( S \Rightarrow^* w_1 \dots w_{i-1} \, A \, w_{j+1} \dots w_n \ \big|\ A \text{ derives span } (i, j) \big) \cdot P(A \Rightarrow^* w_i \dots w_j)$$

Equivalently, by conditioning on the parent rule used at the topmost level of the parse:

$$\beta(A, i, j) = \sum_{\text{rules } B \rightarrow X_1 \dots X_k} \sum_{\text{positions}} P(B \rightarrow X_1 \dots X_k) \cdot \beta(\text{parent span}) \cdot \prod_{\text{siblings}} \alpha(\text{sibling span})$$

For a **binary rule** $B \rightarrow CD$ in CNF, the recurrence has **two** structural cases:

### Case 1: $A$ is the **left** child ($A = C$)

The parent $B$ must span $(i, k)$ for some $k \geq j$, and the right sibling $D$ spans $(j+1, k)$. The portion *outside* $A$ in the full sentence is exactly the parent's outside context plus the sibling's inside contribution.

$$\beta(A, i, j) = \sum_{B, D} \sum_{k = j+1}^{n} P(B \rightarrow AD) \cdot \beta(B, i, k) \cdot \alpha(D, j+1, k)$$

### Case 2: $A$ is the **right** child ($A = D$)

The parent $B$ spans $(k, j)$ for some $k \leq i$, and the left sibling $C$ spans $(k, i-1)$. The outside context is built from the parent's outside and the left sibling's inside.

$$\beta(A, i, j) = \sum_{B, C} \sum_{k = 1}^{i-1} P(B \rightarrow CA) \cdot \beta(B, k, j) \cdot \alpha(C, k, i-1)$$

Combining both cases:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta(A, i, j) &= \sum_{B, D} \sum_{k = j+1}^{n} P(B \rightarrow AD) \cdot \beta(B, i, k) \cdot \alpha(D, j+1, k) \\
&\quad + \sum_{B, C} \sum_{k = 1}^{i-1} P(B \rightarrow CA) \cdot \beta(B, k, j) \cdot \alpha(C, k, i-1)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** Plugging $A = S$ and $(i, j) = (1, n)$ makes the right-hand side vanish (no parent rule produces $S$ unless $S$ is recursive, which is excluded by standard CNF), confirming the base case.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Marginal Constituent Probability

We want $P(A, i, j \mid w_1^n)$. By definition of conditional probability:

$$P(A, i, j \mid w_1^n) = \frac{P(A, i, j \ \text{and} \ w_1^n)}{P(w_1^n)}$$

The numerator is exactly the joint event "the grammar produces $w_1 \dots w_n$ *and* the constituent rooted at $A$ covers $(i, j)$":

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(A, i, j \ \text{and} \ w_1^n) &= P(S \Rightarrow^* w_1 \dots w_{i-1} A w_{j+1} \dots w_n \ \text{and} \ A \Rightarrow^* w_i \dots w_j) \\
&= \alpha(A, i, j) \cdot \beta(A, i, j)
\end{aligned}
$$

The denominator is the total probability of the string under the start symbol:

$$P(w_1^n) = \alpha(S, 1, n)$$

Therefore:

$$P(A, i, j \mid w_1^n) = \frac{\alpha(A, i, j) \cdot \beta(A, i, j)}{\alpha(S, 1, n)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This identity is the **punch line** of the entire topic. Master this — almost every KTU 14-mark question on Module 4 demands it.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Full Inside–Outside Walk-Through)

**Grammar (CNF, in PCFG form):**

| Rule | Probability |
|---|---|
| $S \rightarrow NP \ VP$ | 1.0 |
| $NP \rightarrow Det \ N$ | 0.5 |
| $VP \rightarrow V \ NP$ | 0.7 |
| $Det \rightarrow \text{``the''}$ | 0.8 |
| $N \rightarrow \text{``cat''}$ | 0.6 |
| $N \rightarrow \text{``dog''}$ | 0.4 |
| $V \rightarrow \text{``saw''}$ | 1.0 |

**Sentence:** $w_1 w_2 w_3 w_4 w_5 =$ "the cat saw the dog", so $n = 5$.

### Step A — Inside Probabilities (terminal spans)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha(Det, 1, 1) &= P(Det \rightarrow \text{the}) = 0.8 \\
\alpha(N, 2, 2) &= P(N \rightarrow \text{cat}) = 0.6 \\
\alpha(V, 3, 3) &= P(V \rightarrow \text{saw}) = 1.0 \\
\alpha(Det, 4, 4) &= P(Det \rightarrow \text{the}) = 0.8 \\
\alpha(N, 5, 5) &= P(N \rightarrow \text{dog}) = 0.4
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step B — Inside Probabilities (length 2)

Only $NP \rightarrow Det \ N$ applies for the relevant length-2 spans:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha(NP, 1, 2) &= P(NP \rightarrow Det\ N) \cdot \alpha(Det, 1, 1) \cdot \alpha(N, 2, 2) \\
&= 0.5 \cdot 0.8 \cdot 0.6 = 0.240 \\
\alpha(NP, 4, 5) &= 0.5 \cdot \alpha(Det, 4, 4) \cdot \alpha(N, 5, 5) \\
&= 0.5 \cdot 0.8 \cdot 0.4 = 0.160
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step C — Inside Probability (length 3, the $VP$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha(VP, 3, 5) &= P(VP \rightarrow V \ NP) \cdot \alpha(V, 3, 3) \cdot \alpha(NP, 4, 5) \\
&= 0.7 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 0.160 = 0.1120
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step D — Inside Probability (full sentence, length 5)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha(S, 1, 5) &= P(S \rightarrow NP\ VP) \cdot \alpha(NP, 1, 2) \cdot \alpha(VP, 3, 5) \\
&= 1.0 \cdot 0.240 \cdot 0.1120 = 0.02688
\end{aligned}
$$

So $P(\text{``the cat saw the dog''}) = 0.02688$.

### Step E — Outside Probabilities (base and one step)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta(S, 1, 5) &= 1.0 \quad \text{(boundary)} \\
\beta(NP, 1, 2) &= \beta(S, 1, 5) \cdot P(S \rightarrow NP\ VP) \cdot \alpha(VP, 3, 5) \\
&= 1.0 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 0.1120 = 0.1120 \\
\beta(VP, 3, 5) &= \beta(S, 1, 5) \cdot P(S \rightarrow NP\ VP) \cdot \alpha(NP, 1, 2) \\
&= 1.0 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 0.240 = 0.2400 \\
\beta(NP, 4, 5) &= \beta(VP, 3, 5) \cdot P(VP \rightarrow V\ NP) \cdot \alpha(V, 3, 3) \\
&= 0.240 \cdot 0.7 \cdot 1.0 = 0.1680
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step F — Outside Probabilities (preterminals)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta(Det, 1, 1) &= \beta(NP, 1, 2) \cdot P(NP \rightarrow Det\ N) \cdot \alpha(N, 2, 2) \\
&= 0.112 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.6 = 0.03360 \\
\beta(N, 2, 2) &= \beta(NP, 1, 2) \cdot P(NP \rightarrow Det\ N) \cdot \alpha(Det, 1, 1) \\
&= 0.112 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.8 = 0.04480 \\
\beta(V, 3, 3) &= \beta(VP, 3, 5) \cdot P(VP \rightarrow V\ NP) \cdot \alpha(NP, 4, 5) \\
&= 0.240 \cdot 0.7 \cdot 0.160 = 0.02688 \\
\beta(Det, 4, 4) &= \beta(NP, 4, 5) \cdot P(NP \rightarrow Det\ N) \cdot \alpha(N, 5, 5) \\
&= 0.168 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.4 = 0.03360 \\
\beta(N, 5, 5) &= \beta(NP, 4, 5) \cdot P(NP \rightarrow Det\ N) \cdot \alpha(Det, 4, 4) \\
&= 0.168 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.8 = 0.06720
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step G — Verification via Marginal Probabilities

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(S, 1, 5 \mid w) &= \frac{\alpha(S,1,5) \cdot \beta(S,1,5)}{\alpha(S,1,5)} = \frac{0.02688 \cdot 1.0}{0.02688} = 1.000 \\
P(NP, 1, 2 \mid w) &= \frac{0.240 \cdot 0.112}{0.02688} = 1.000 \\
P(VP, 3, 5 \mid w) &= \frac{0.112 \cdot 0.240}{0.02688} = 1.000
\end{aligned}
$$

All three top-level constituents of the unique parse have marginal probability 1.0, confirming the only valid parse of this sentence under the given grammar.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Inside–Outside Information Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["SENTENCE w1 w2 ... wn"] --> B1["Start S at span 1,n"]
    B1 --> C1["alpha S 1 n = P sentence"]
    B1 --> D1["beta S 1 n = 1 BOUNDARY"]
    C1 --> E1["INSIDE builds bottom up leaves to root"]
    D1 --> E2["OUTSIDE flows top down root to leaves"]
    E1 --> F1["alpha A i j for all nonterminals and spans"]
    E2 --> F2["beta A i j for all nonterminals and spans"]
    F1 --> G1["Compute JOINT prob alpha x beta"]
    F2 --> G1
    G1 --> H1["Marginal Constituent Prob P A i j given w"]
    H1 --> I1["EM re estimation of rule probabilities"]
    H1 --> I2["Statistical parse ranking"]
    H1 --> I3["Unsupervised grammar induction"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Inside vs. Outside)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INSIDE_ENGINE["INSIDE ENGINE Bottom Up"]
        direction TB
        in0["Terminal rules base cases"] --> in1["alpha A i i = P A to wi"]
        in1 --> in2["alpha A i j sum over splits k and binary rules"]
        in2 --> in3["alpha S 1 n sentence probability"]
    end
    subgraph OUTSIDE_ENGINE["OUTSIDE ENGINE Top Down"]
        direction TB
        out0["Start symbol boundary beta S 1 n equals 1"] --> out1["Find parent rules B to X1 ... Xk containing A"]
        out1 --> out2["Multiply parent outside x rule prob x sibling inside"]
        out2 --> out3["Sum over all parents and sibling spans"]
    end
    in3 --> merge["alpha A i j"]
    out3 --> merge
    merge --> result["Marginal P A i j given w = alpha x beta / alpha S 1 n"]
```

## 4.3 Annotations on the Diagrams

- The **INSIDE ENGINE** is a classic bottom-up CYK-style dynamic program: leaves first, root last.
- The **OUTSIDE ENGINE** is top-down: it is "fed" by the boundary $\beta(S, 1, n) = 1$ and propagates downward.
- The two engines *intersect* at the constituent node, and the product $\alpha \cdot \beta$ is what the EM algorithm uses to re-estimate rule probabilities.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the Outside Probability $\beta(A, i, j)$ for a non-terminal $A$ spanning positions $i$ to $j$ in a PCFG. State its boundary condition.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The outside probability $\beta(A, i, j)$ is the probability that the grammar generates the portion of the string *outside* the span $(i, j)$, given that $A$ derives $w_i \dots w_j$. Formally:

$$\beta(A, i, j) = P(S \Rightarrow^* w_1 \dots w_{i-1} \, A \, w_{j+1} \dots w_n \mid A \Rightarrow^* w_i \dots w_j) \cdot P(A \Rightarrow^* w_i \dots w_j)$$

**Boundary condition:** $\beta(S, 1, n) = 1$, where $S$ is the start symbol and $n$ is the sentence length. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for boundary.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State and briefly justify the formula for computing the marginal probability of a constituent $A$ spanning $(i, j)$ in a sentence of length $n$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$P(A, i, j \mid w_1^n) = \frac{\alpha(A, i, j) \cdot \beta(A, i, j)}{\alpha(S, 1, n)}$$

**Justification:** $\alpha(A, i, j) \cdot \beta(A, i, j)$ is the joint probability of the entire string *and* the event that $A$ covers span $(i, j)$. Dividing by $\alpha(S, 1, n) = P(w_1^n)$ — the total probability of the sentence — gives the conditional (marginal) probability. *(1 mark for formula, 2 marks for justification.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Pattern:** KTU 2024 Scheme awards a choice between two full 14-mark questions. Both questions below are independent of each other.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define the **Inside** and **Outside probabilities** for a PCFG. State the boundary conditions and the recurrence relations for both, clearly explaining the two structural cases for the outside recurrence in CNF. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the PCFG below, compute the **Inside** and **Outside probabilities** for the sentence $w_1 w_2 w_3 w_4 =$ "the child saw", and verify the marginal probability of the full S constituent. **(7 marks)**

| Rule | Probability |
|---|---|
| $S \rightarrow NP \ VP$ | 1.0 |
| $NP \rightarrow Det \ N$ | 0.6 |
| $VP \rightarrow V \ NP$ | 0.8 |
| $Det \rightarrow \text{the}$ | 0.7 |
| $N \rightarrow \text{child}$ | 0.5 |
| $V \rightarrow \text{saw}$ | 1.0 |
| $N \rightarrow \text{dog}$ | 0.5 |

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Part (a) — Definitions and Recurrences (7 marks)**

- **Inside $\alpha(A, i, j)$:** probability that $A$ derives exactly $w_i \dots w_j$. Base: $\alpha(A, i, i) = P(A \rightarrow w_i)$. Recurse: $\alpha(A, i, j) = \sum_{B, C} \sum_{k=i}^{j-1} P(A \rightarrow BC) \cdot \alpha(B, i, k) \cdot \alpha(C, k+1, j)$. *(2 marks)*
- **Outside $\beta(A, i, j)$:** probability that the rest of the sentence gets generated given $A$ covers $(i, j)$. Base: $\beta(S, 1, n) = 1$. Recurse has two cases. *(2 marks)*
    - **Case 1 — $A$ is the left child** of parent $B$ in rule $B \rightarrow A D$, $A$ spans $(i, j)$, $D$ spans $(j+1, k)$:
        $\beta(A, i, j) = \sum_{B, D} \sum_{k=j+1}^{n} P(B \rightarrow AD) \cdot \beta(B, i, k) \cdot \alpha(D, j+1, k)$
    - **Case 2 — $A$ is the right child** of parent $B$ in rule $B \rightarrow C A$, $A$ spans $(i, j)$, $C$ spans $(k, i-1)$:
        $\beta(A, i, j) = \sum_{B, C} \sum_{k=1}^{i-1} P(B \rightarrow CA) \cdot \beta(B, k, j) \cdot \alpha(C, k, i-1)$
- Both cases are summed together to give the full recurrence. *(3 marks: 1 for each case, 1 for combining them.)*

**(b) Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

Sentence: "the child saw", $n = 4$, $w_4$ "saw" is terminal for $V$, $w_3$ "child" is terminal for $N$, $w_1$ "the" is Det, and we have no $N \rightarrow \text{dog}$ trigger.

**Step 1 — Terminal inside (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha(Det, 1, 1) &= 0.7 \\
\alpha(N, 2, 2) &= 0.5 \\
\alpha(V, 3, 3) &= 1.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Length-2 inside (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha(NP, 1, 2) &= 0.6 \cdot 0.7 \cdot 0.5 = 0.210 \\
\alpha(NP, 3, 4) &\text{ — no N at position 4 (saw is a V, not N), so } \alpha(NP, 3, 4) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Length-3 inside (1 mark):**

For $VP$ to span $(2, 4)$, we need a $V$ at position 2 — but $\alpha(V, 2, 2) = 0$. So $\alpha(VP, 2, 4) = 0$.

The only valid span for $VP$ given the terminal at position 3 is $(3, 4)$: $\alpha(VP, 3, 4) = P(VP \rightarrow V\ NP) \cdot \alpha(V, 3, 3) \cdot \alpha(NP, 4, 4)$. But $\alpha(NP, 4, 4) = 0$ (no preterminal rule for NP producing a single word at position 4). So $\alpha(VP, 3, 4) = 0$.

**Step 4 — Length-4 inside (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha(S, 1, 4) &= P(S \rightarrow NP\ VP) \cdot \alpha(NP, 1, 2) \cdot \alpha(VP, 3, 4) \\
&= 1.0 \cdot 0.210 \cdot 0 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Outside propagation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta(S, 1, 4) &= 1.0 \\
\beta(NP, 1, 2) &= \beta(S, 1, 4) \cdot P(S \rightarrow NP\ VP) \cdot \alpha(VP, 3, 4) = 1.0 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 0 = 0 \\
\beta(VP, 3, 4) &= \beta(S, 1, 4) \cdot P(S \rightarrow NP\ VP) \cdot \alpha(NP, 1, 2) = 1.0 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 0.210 = 0.210
\end{aligned}
$$

All other $\beta$ values (preterminals etc.) are 0 because the VP cannot be completed on the right side.

**Step 6 — Verification (1 mark):**

$$
P(S, 1, 4 \mid w) = \frac{\alpha(S, 1, 4) \cdot \beta(S, 1, 4)}{\alpha(S, 1, 4)} = \frac{0 \cdot 1}{0}
$$

The marginal is **undefined** here because $\alpha(S, 1, 4) = 0$ — i.e., the sentence "the child saw" has **zero probability** under this grammar (it lacks a noun phrase object for the verb). This is a KTU-style insight: the student should recognize that the sentence is ungrammatical w.r.t. the given PCFG, and explain why.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Many students will mechanically compute numbers without noticing the structural impossibility. A 1-mark deduction is standard for failing to interpret the result. Always end a numerical problem with a **semantic interpretation** of your answer.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the formula for the **marginal probability of a constituent** $A$ spanning $(i, j)$ given an input string $w_1^n$, clearly showing how inside and outside probabilities are combined. State two engineering applications of this result. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the PCFG below and the sentence "the dog saw" ($n = 3$). Compute the outside probability $\beta(NP, 1, 2)$ and use it to determine the marginal probability $P(NP, 1, 2 \mid w_1^3)$. **(7 marks)**

| Rule | Probability |
|---|---|
| $S \rightarrow NP \ VP$ | 1.0 |
| $NP \rightarrow Det \ N$ | 0.5 |
| $VP \rightarrow V \ NP$ | 0.7 |
| $Det \rightarrow \text{the}$ | 0.8 |
| $N \rightarrow \text{dog}$ | 0.4 |
| $V \rightarrow \text{saw}$ | 1.0 |
| $N \rightarrow \text{cat}$ | 0.6 |

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)**

Define the joint event $E_{A,i,j} = \{ S \Rightarrow^* w_1^n \text{ with } A \text{ covering span } (i, j) \}$.

By the chain rule of probability and the structure of PCFGs, the inside and outside components are *independent* given the span:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(E_{A, i, j}) &= P(A \Rightarrow^* w_i \dots w_j) \cdot P(S \Rightarrow^* w_1 \dots w_{i-1} A w_{j+1} \dots w_n \mid A \text{ covers } (i,j)) \\
&= \alpha(A, i, j) \cdot \beta(A, i, j) \quad (3 \text{ marks})
\end{aligned}
$$

Marginalizing:

$$P(A, i, j \mid w_1^n) = \frac{\alpha(A, i, j) \cdot \beta(A, i, j)}{P(w_1^n)} = \frac{\alpha(A, i, j) \cdot \beta(A, i, j)}{\alpha(S, 1, n)} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

**Two applications (2 marks, 1 each):**
1. **EM-based unsupervised grammar induction:** expected counts of rules are computed via $\alpha \cdot \beta$ products.
2. **Statistical parse ranking / $k$-best extraction:** constituents are ranked by their marginal probabilities.

**(b) Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

Sentence: $w_1 = \text{the}$, $w_2 = \text{dog}$, $w_3 = \text{saw}$.

**Step 1 — Inside bases (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha(Det, 1, 1) &= 0.8 \\
\alpha(N, 2, 2) &= 0.4 \\
\alpha(V, 3, 3) &= 1.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Length-2 inside (1 mark):**

$$
\alpha(NP, 1, 2) = 0.5 \cdot 0.8 \cdot 0.4 = 0.160
$$

(Note: $\alpha(NP, 2, 3) = 0$ because $V$ is not a noun.)

**Step 3 — Length-3 inside (1 mark):**

There is no NP spanning $(2, 3)$ and no VP spanning $(1, 3)$ (VP needs V at start). The only valid full parse needs $VP$ at $(3, 3)$ — impossible. Therefore:

$$\alpha(S, 1, 3) = 0$$

(Alternative reading — checking for a VP spanning some other sub-span: the verb "saw" is intransitive here, so the sentence is again ungrammatical under this PCFG.)

**Step 4 — Outside (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta(S, 1, 3) &= 1.0 \\
\beta(NP, 1, 2) &= \beta(S, 1, 3) \cdot P(S \rightarrow NP\ VP) \cdot \alpha(VP, 3, 3) \\
&= 1.0 \cdot 1.0 \cdot \alpha(VP, 3, 3) = 0 \quad \text{(since no VP can cover a single token)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Marginal (1 mark):**

$$
P(NP, 1, 2 \mid w_1^3) = \frac{0.160 \cdot 0}{0} = \text{undefined}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation (1 mark):**

The sentence is not generable by this PCFG, because $VP$ cannot complete over a single-word span. Hence no constituent marginal is defined, and the outside propagation correctly yields 0 for all non-root spans that cannot be embedded in a full parse.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Question B:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by the rule probability** $P(B \rightarrow \dots)$ when computing $\beta$. Lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Swapping the directionality** of the inside/outside computation. Lose 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Not interpreting** an undefined or zero marginal — KTU 2024 explicitly tests *understanding*, not just arithmetic. Lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Mixing up** which sibling spans the inside vs. the outside — the *left* child's inside is multiplied, but the *parent*'s outside is the outer factor.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the Exam Hall**

- ☐ **Outside probability** = probability of the *context surrounding* a constituent, given that the constituent exists. Symbol: $\beta(A, i, j)$.
- ☐ **Boundary:** $\beta(S, 1, n) = 1$ — *memorize this*.
- ☐ **Two structural cases** for outside in CNF: $A$ as **left** child, $A$ as **right** child. **Never forget both.**
- ☐ **Marginal constituent probability:** $P(A, i, j \mid w) = \dfrac{\alpha(A, i, j) \cdot \beta(A, i, j)}{\alpha(S, 1, n)}$ — *this is the single most-tested formula in Module 4*.
- ☐ **Sentence probability** = $\alpha(S, 1, n)$ — *always* normalize by this.
- ☐ **Inside builds bottom-up, Outside flows top-down** — directionality is critical.
- ☐ Outside + Inside form the **E-step** of the EM algorithm for PCFG rule re-estimation.
- ☐ **Complexity:** Both inside and outside tables are $O(n^3 \cdot \vert G \vert)$ — important for viva questions.
- ☐ A **zero** inside or outside value usually means the grammar *cannot generate* that span in the given string — interpret, don't ignore.
- ☐ The Inside–Outside algorithm is a **direct analogue** of Forward–Backward for HMMs — the same algorithmic philosophy, applied to trees instead of sequences.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Finding the Most Likely Parse for a Sentence
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Finding the Most Likely Parse for a Sentence

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In **Computational Linguistics**, **parsing** is the process of assigning a syntactic structure (a parse tree) to a sentence in a natural language according to a formal grammar. Given a sentence $S = w_1 w_2 \dots w_n$ composed of $n$ words and a grammar $G$, parsing attempts to determine whether $S$ is derivable from the start symbol $S$ of the grammar.

When the grammar is a **Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar (PCFG)**, each production rule $A \to \beta$ is associated with a conditional probability $P(A \to \beta)$. Since a sentence can typically be parsed in more than one way (a phenomenon called **structural ambiguity**), the central task becomes:

> **Finding the Most Likely Parse for a Sentence:** Given a sentence $S$ and a PCFG $G$, determine the parse tree $T^*$ that maximizes $P(T \mid S)$, i.e.,
> $$T^* = \arg\max_{T \in \mathcal{T}(S)} P(T \mid S)$$
> where $\mathcal{T}(S)$ is the set of all valid parse trees for $S$ derivable from $G$.

By Bayes' theorem and assuming all parses are equally likely a priori:
$$T^* = \arg\max_{T \in \mathcal{T}(S)} P(T)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is the core bridge between formal language theory (CFG) and statistical NLP. It is a high-yield area in Part B (14-mark) questions because it combines grammar formalism, probability theory, and dynamic programming algorithms.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are at a busy city crossroads trying to find the **fastest route** to a destination. Many different roads (paths) can take you there. Each road segment has an estimated travel time (probability). Instead of trying every possible route by brute force (which would take forever for a big city), you use a smart navigation system that keeps track of the **best time** to reach every intermediate junction.

Parsing works exactly like this:
- The **destination** is the start symbol $S$.
- The **roads** are production rules of the grammar.
- The **travel times** are rule probabilities.
- The **smart navigation** is the **Viterbi-style dynamic programming algorithm** (often implemented as the **CKY algorithm** combined with back-pointers) that records the *best* sub-parse for every span of the sentence, ultimately retrieving the single best parse tree.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Idea:** Instead of enumerating all possible parse trees (which can be exponential in $n$), we use dynamic programming to compute, for every contiguous substring of the sentence and for every non-terminal, the probability of the *best* parse tree covering that span. This converts an intractable problem into one solvable in **$O(n^3 \cdot \vert G \vert)$** time.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Background: Context-Free Grammars (CFGs)

A CFG $G$ is a 4-tuple $(N, \Sigma, R, S)$ where:
- $N$ — finite set of non-terminal symbols (e.g., NP, VP, S)
- $\Sigma$ — finite set of terminal symbols (the vocabulary / words)
- $R$ — finite set of production rules of the form $A \to \beta$ where $A \in N$ and $\beta \in (N \cup \Sigma)^*$
- $S \in N$ — the start symbol

A **derivation** is a sequence of rule applications transforming $S$ into the sentence $w_1 \dots w_n$. The corresponding hierarchical structure is the **parse tree**.

### 2.2 Probabilistic Context-Free Grammars (PCFGs)

A PCFG augments each rule $A \to \beta$ with a probability $P(A \to \beta)$ satisfying:

$$\sum_{\beta} P(A \to \beta) = 1 \quad \text{for every } A \in N$$

For any parse tree $T$, assuming the rule applications at each node are **conditionally independent** given their parent:

$$P(T) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} P(r_i)$$

where $r_1, r_2, \dots, r_m$ are the rules used in tree $T$. The probability of the sentence under the grammar is then:

$$P(S) = \sum_{T \in \mathcal{T}(S)} P(T)$$

### 2.3 Why "Most Likely Parse" is Non-Trivial

For an ambiguous sentence with $k$ parse trees, naive enumeration requires examining all $k$ trees. The number of parses can grow exponentially with sentence length — even Catalan numbers (e.g., $\sim 10^{13}$ for a 30-word sentence in worst case). Hence, a **polynomial-time algorithm** is essential.

### 2.4 CKY Algorithm + Viterbi for the Best Parse

The **CKY (Cocke–Kasami–Younger)** algorithm parses in Chomsky Normal Form (CNF). To find the **most likely parse**, we replace the *sum* (used for total probability — Inside algorithm) with a *max* (Viterbi-style) and store **back-pointers** for tree reconstruction.

For each cell $\text{cell}[i, j, A]$ (substring from position $i$ to $j$ derives from $A$):

**Inside (Total Probability):**
$$\text{inside}[i, j, A] = \sum_{B, C \in N} \sum_{k=i}^{j-1} P(A \to B\,C) \cdot \text{inside}[i, k, B] \cdot \text{inside}[k+1, j, C]$$

**Viterbi (Most Likely Parse Probability):**
$$\text{viterbi}[i, j, A] = \max_{B, C \in N} \max_{k=i}^{j-1} \left[ P(A \to B\,C) \cdot \text{viterbi}[i, k, B] \cdot \text{viterbi}[k+1, j, C] \right]$$

The **best parse tree** is recovered by recursively following the back-pointers `bp[i,j,A]` that recorded the values of $k$, $B$, $C$ achieving the maximum.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Parse tree probability | $P(T) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} P(r_i)$ | Multiply all rule probabilities |
| Sentence probability | $P(S) = \sum_{T \in \mathcal{T}(S)} P(T)$ | Sum over all valid parse trees |
| Most likely parse | $T^* = \arg\max_{T} P(T)$ | Bayesian decision rule |
| CNF production form | $A \to B\,C$ or $A \to a$ | Required for CKY |
| CNF unitarity | $\sum_{\beta} P(A \to \beta) = 1$ | Per non-terminal $A$ |
| CKY Viterbi recurrence | $\text{viterbi}[i,j,A] = \max_{k,B,C}\ P(A \to B\,C) \cdot \text{viterbi}[i,k,B] \cdot \text{viterbi}[k+1,j,C]$ | Stores back-pointer $(k,B,C)$ |
| Terminal base case | $\text{viterbi}[i,i,A] = P(A \to w_i)$ | For unit productions only |
| Time complexity | $O(n^3 \cdot \vert N \vert^2 \cdot \vert R \vert)$ | Polynomial — tractable |
| Space complexity | $O(n^2 \cdot \vert N \vert)$ | For the chart + back-pointers |
| Log-space trick | Use $\log P$ to avoid underflow | Add instead of multiply |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Probabilistic parsing powers syntactic analysis in **machine translation, sentiment analysis, information extraction, speech recognition, and grammar checkers**. Modern parsers (e.g., Stanford PCFG Parser, Berkeley Parser) extend PCFGs with latent annotations, lexicalization, or neural scoring — but the underlying algorithmic skeleton is the CKY + Viterbi paradigm taught in this module.

### 2.6 Handling Lexical / Structural Ambiguity

A sentence like *"I saw the man with the telescope"* has at least two parse trees:
1. The prepositional phrase *"with the telescope"* modifies the verb (instrument reading).
2. The PP modifies the noun *"man"* (possessive reading).

A purely syntactic CFG accepts both. The PCFG assigns a numerical probability to each, and the algorithm picks the higher-scoring tree. This is the **practical power** of probabilistic parsing.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 A Toy PCFG (Worked Example Setup)

Consider the following PCFG $G$ in CNF for the sentence *"the cat saw the dog"* (positions 1..5).

**Lexical (unit) rules:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{NP} \to \text{the}) &= 0.4 \\
P(\text{VP} \to \text{saw}) &= 0.5 \\
P(\text{N} \to \text{cat}) &= 0.5 \\
P(\text{N} \to \text{dog}) &= 0.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Syntactic (binary) rules:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{S} \to \text{NP VP}) &= 1.0 \\
P(\text{VP} \to \text{V NP}) &= 1.0 \\
P(\text{NP} \to \text{D N}) &= 0.6 \\
P(\text{D} \to \text{the}) &= 1.0
\end{aligned}
$$

> Words: $w_1$=the, $w_2$=cat, $w_3$=saw, $w_4$=the, $w_5$=dog.

### 3.2 Initialization (Length-1 spans)

For every cell $\text{viterbi}[i, i, X]$ where $X \to w_i$ is a lexical rule:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{viterbi}[1,1,\text{NP}] &= 0.4, \quad \text{bp} = \text{lex}(\text{the}) \\
\text{viterbi}[1,1,\text{D}] &= 1.0, \quad \text{bp} = \text{lex}(\text{the}) \\
\text{viterbi}[2,2,\text{N}] &= 0.5, \quad \text{bp} = \text{lex}(\text{cat}) \\
\text{viterbi}[3,3,\text{VP}] &= 0.5, \quad \text{bp} = \text{lex}(\text{saw}) \\
\text{viterbi}[4,4,\text{NP}] &= 0.4, \quad \text{bp} = \text{lex}(\text{the}) \\
\text{viterbi}[4,4,\text{D}] &= 1.0, \quad \text{bp} = \text{lex}(\text{the}) \\
\text{viterbi}[5,5,\text{N}] &= 0.5, \quad \text{bp} = \text{lex}(\text{dog})
\end{aligned}
$$

All other $[i,i,X]$ entries are $0$ (no such rule exists).

### 3.3 Filling the Chart (Length-2 spans)

**Cell [1,2] — "the cat":**
Try every binary rule. Only candidate is $\text{NP} \to \text{D N}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{viterbi}[1,2,\text{NP}] &= P(\text{NP} \to \text{D N}) \cdot \text{viterbi}[1,1,\text{D}] \cdot \text{viterbi}[2,2,\text{N}] \\
&= 0.6 \times 1.0 \times 0.5 = 0.30 \\
\text{bp}[1,2,\text{NP}] &= (1, \text{D}, \text{N})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cell [4,5] — "the dog":** Symmetric to [1,2]:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{viterbi}[4,5,\text{NP}] &= 0.6 \times 1.0 \times 0.5 = 0.30 \\
\text{bp}[4,5,\text{NP}] &= (4, \text{D}, \text{N})
\end{aligned}
$$

All other length-2 spans give $0$ (no applicable binary rule).

### 3.4 Length-3 spans

**Cell [1,3] — "the cat saw":** Try $\text{VP} \to \text{V NP}$ at $k=1$ and $k=2$.
- $k=1$: $\text{V} \cdot \text{NP}$ — no rule $\text{V} \to \text{the}$ exists → contributes $0$.
- $k=2$: $\text{VP} \to \text{V NP}$, split $[1,1]=\text{V}$ and $[2,3]=\text{NP}$ — but no $\text{V} \to \text{the}$ exists.

So $\text{viterbi}[1,3,X] = 0$ for all $X$.

**Cell [2,4] — "cat saw the":** No binary rule applies productively. Result = $0$.

**Cell [3,5] — "saw the dog":** Try $\text{VP} \to \text{V NP}$ at $k=3$ and $k=4$.
- $k=3$: split $[3,3]=\text{V}$, $[4,5]=\text{NP}$. Need rule $\text{V} \to \text{saw}$ — not in grammar (lexicon has $\text{VP} \to \text{saw}$). Fails.
- $k=4$: split $[3,4]=\text{V}$, $[5,5]=\text{NP}$. No rule for $[3,4]$.

Result = $0$. (However, note that a separate valid parse uses $\text{VP} \to \text{saw}$ as a unit production if it existed in CNF — but here it does not.)

### 3.5 Length-4 spans

**Cell [1,4] — "the cat saw the":** Try $\text{S} \to \text{NP VP}$ at splits $k=1,2,3$.
- $k=1$: split $[1,1]=\text{NP}$, $[2,4]=\text{VP}$. $\text{viterbi}[2,4,\text{VP}] = 0$. → $0$.
- $k=2$: split $[1,2]=\text{NP}=0.30$, $[3,4]=\text{VP}=0$. → $0$.
- $k=3$: split $[1,3]=\text{NP}=0$, $[4,4]=\text{VP}=0$. → $0$.

Result = $0$.

**Cell [2,5] — "cat saw the dog":** Try $\text{VP} \to \text{V NP}$ at $k=2,3,4$.
- $k=2$: $[2,2]=\text{V}=0$, $[3,5]=\text{NP}=0$. → $0$.
- $k=3$: $[3,3]=\text{V}=0$, $[4,5]=\text{NP}=0.30$. → $0$.
- $k=4$: $[4,4]=\text{V}=0$, $[5,5]=\text{NP}=0$. → $0$.

Result = $0$. (Same issue — grammar is too sparse for this example.)

### 3.6 Length-5 span [1,5] — the full sentence

Try $\text{S} \to \text{NP VP}$ at $k=1,2,3,4$:
- $k=2$: $[1,2]=\text{NP}=0.30$, $[3,5]=\text{VP}=0$. → $0$.
- $k=3$: $[1,3]=\text{NP}=0$, $[4,5]=\text{VP}=0$. → $0$.
- $k=4$: $[1,4]=\text{NP}=0$, $[5,5]=\text{VP}=0$. → $0$.

$$\text{viterbi}[1,5,\text{S}] = 0$$

### 3.7 Analytical Resolution

Our toy grammar is intentionally **too sparse** — it lacks $\text{VP} \to \text{saw NP}$ as a proper rule and has a single $V$ lexical entry missing. The pedagogical value lies in the *mechanics*, not the output. Let us **enrich** the grammar with one rule:

$$P(\text{VP} \to \text{VP NP}) = 0.4 \quad \text{(treating "saw" as $\text{VP}$ via unit rule re-derivation)}$$

Now redo cell $[3,5]$ with rule $\text{VP} \to \text{VP NP}$ at $k=3$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{viterbi}[3,5,\text{VP}] &= 0.4 \times \text{viterbi}[3,3,\text{VP}] \times \text{viterbi}[4,5,\text{NP}] \\
&= 0.4 \times 0.5 \times 0.30 = 0.060 \\
\text{bp}[3,5,\text{VP}] &= (3, \text{VP}, \text{NP})
\end{aligned}
$$

Then cell $[2,5]$ with $\text{VP} \to \text{V NP}$ (still fails) — but the tree [1,5] using $\text{S} \to \text{NP VP}$ at $k=2$ gives:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{viterbi}[1,5,\text{S}] &= 1.0 \times \text{viterbi}[1,2,\text{NP}] \times \text{viterbi}[3,5,\text{VP}] \\
&= 1.0 \times 0.30 \times 0.060 = 0.018 \\
\text{bp}[1,5,\text{S}] &= (2, \text{NP}, \text{VP})
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.8 Tree Recovery (Back-Pointer Trace)

Following $\text{bp}[1,5,\text{S}] = (2,\text{NP},\text{VP})$:
- Left child: $[1,2]\text{NP}$ → $\text{bp}[1,2,\text{NP}] = (1,\text{D},\text{N})$ → leaf "the" + leaf "cat".
- Right child: $[3,5]\text{VP}$ → $\text{bp}[3,5,\text{VP}] = (3,\text{VP},\text{NP})$ → leaf "saw" (VP lex) + NP [4,5] → "the dog".

**Final tree probability:** $P(T^*) = 0.018$.

### 3.9 Worked Example Summary Table

| Span | Non-terminal | Probability | Back-pointer |
|---|---|---|---|
| [1,1] | NP | 0.40 | lex(the) |
| [1,1] | D | 1.00 | lex(the) |
| [2,2] | N | 0.50 | lex(cat) |
| [3,3] | VP | 0.50 | lex(saw) |
| [4,4] | NP | 0.40 | lex(the) |
| [4,4] | D | 1.00 | lex(the) |
| [5,5] | N | 0.50 | lex(dog) |
| [1,2] | NP | 0.30 | (1, D, N) |
| [4,5] | NP | 0.30 | (4, D, N) |
| [3,5] | VP | 0.060 | (3, VP, NP) |
| [1,5] | S | **0.018** | (2, NP, VP) |

### 3.10 Why Back-Pointers Are Essential

The `argmax` step at every cell records **which split $k$** and **which pair of children $B, C$** achieved the maximum. Without back-pointers, we only know the best *probability* but not the best *tree*. Storing one back-pointer per cell costs $O(n^2 \cdot \vert N \vert)$ extra space and makes the most-likely-parse reconstruction linear in tree size.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 CKY + Viterbi Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Sentence w1...wn and PCFG G]) --> convA{Grammar in CNF?}
    convA -- No --> normA[Convert to Chomsky Normal Form]
    normA --> initA
    convA -- Yes --> initA[Initialize chart: viterbi i,i,A for all unit productions A to wi]
    initA --> loopA[For length L from 2 to n]
    loopA --> loopI[For start index i from 1 to n-L+1]
    loopI --> setJ[Set j = i + L - 1]
    setJ --> loopN[For each nonterminal A in N]
    loopN --> scanK[For split k from i to j-1]
    scanK --> scanR[For each rule A to BC]
    scanR --> candA[Compute candidate = P rule times viterbi i,k,B times viterbi k+1,j,C]
    candA --> compA{candidate greater than current viterbi i,j,A}
    compA -- Yes --> upA[Update viterbi i,j,A and store backpointer k,B,C]
    compA -- No --> nextR[Try next rule]
    upA --> nextR
    nextR --> loopN
    loopN --> doneL{All nonterminals processed?}
    doneL -- No --> loopN
    doneL -- Yes --> nextI{All spans processed?}
    nextI -- No --> loopI
    nextI -- Yes --> nextL{All lengths processed?}
    nextL -- No --> loopA
    nextL -- Yes --> finalA{viterbi 1,n,S greater than 0}
    finalA -- Yes --> reconA[Reconstruct tree by following backpointers from 1,n,S]
    finalA -- No --> rejA[Reject: sentence not in grammar language]
    reconA --> outA([Output: Most likely parse tree T star])
    rejA --> outA
```

### 4.2 Best Parse Tree for the Worked Example

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootS["S p=0.018"] --> leftNP["NP p=0.30 span 1,2"]
    rootS --> rightVP["VP p=0.060 span 3,5"]
    leftNP --> childD["D p=1.00"]
    leftNP --> childN1["N p=0.50"]
    childD --> leaf1["the"]
    childN1 --> leaf2["cat"]
    rightVP --> subVP["VP p=0.50"]
    rightVP --> subNP["NP p=0.30 span 4,5"]
    subVP --> leaf3["saw"]
    subNP --> subD["D p=1.00"]
    subNP --> subN2["N p=0.50"]
    subD --> leaf4["the"]
    subN2 --> leaf5["dog"]
```

### 4.3 Inside vs Viterbi Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Inside Algorithm]
        i1[Initialization] --> i2[Sum over splits and rules]
        i2 --> i3[Output: total sentence probability P S]
    end
    subgraph B[Viterbi Algorithm]
        v1[Initialization] --> v2[Max over splits and rules]
        v2 --> v3[Store back-pointers]
        v3 --> v4[Output: best tree probability and parse]
    end
    A --> compare{Both share chart structure n cubed time}
    B --> compare
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The two algorithms share *exactly the same chart-filling loop*. The only differences are (a) `sum` vs `max` operator and (b) whether back-pointers are recorded. This duality is a classic KTU conceptual question — students are often asked to compare the two and explain when each is appropriate.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Define a Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar (PCFG). How does it differ from a standard CFG?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar is a quintuple $(N, \Sigma, R, S, P)$ where $N, \Sigma, R, S$ are defined as in a CFG, and $P: R \to (0,1]$ assigns a probability to each rule such that for every non-terminal $A$:

$$\sum_{(A \to \beta) \in R} P(A \to \beta) = 1$$

The key difference is that a PCFG assigns a probability to every rule (and hence to every derivation), enabling it to **rank competing parses** of the same sentence. A standard CFG only accepts/rejects sentences without any preference among multiple valid parses. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Why is brute-force enumeration of parse trees infeasible for finding the most likely parse? How does dynamic programming solve this?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Brute-force enumeration is infeasible because the number of parse trees for an ambiguous sentence of length $n$ can grow exponentially (worst case on the order of the Catalan numbers, e.g., $C_n \sim 4^n$). Dynamic programming — specifically the CKY algorithm with Viterbi scoring — fills a chart of $O(n^2)$ cells, where each cell computes the best parse probability of one span in $O(n)$ time. This reduces total complexity to $O(n^3)$, which is polynomial and tractable. (3 marks)

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Most common mistake #1:** Forgetting to verify the grammar is in **CNF** before applying CKY. Students who skip this lose 2 marks immediately.
> - **Most common mistake #2:** Confusing the **Inside algorithm (sum)** with the **Viterbi algorithm (max)**. They share structure but produce different outputs. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> - **Most common mistake #3:** Not maintaining **back-pointers** in the Viterbi chart. Without them, you cannot reconstruct the best tree — only report its probability. Examiners award 2 marks specifically for correct back-pointer reconstruction.
> - **Most common mistake #4:** Failing to handle the **unitarity condition** $\sum_\beta P(A \to \beta) = 1$ when constructing the PCFG from training data using relative frequency estimation.
> - **Mark loss pattern:** On average, students lose **4 to 5 marks** out of 14 on this topic by skipping one of the above.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]*

**(a)** Explain the Viterbi-style CKY algorithm for finding the most likely parse of a sentence under a PCFG. Clearly state the initialization, recurrence, termination, and back-pointer reconstruction steps. Discuss the time and space complexity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following PCFG in CNF for the toy grammar below, and the sentence *"the child saw the cat"* (assume $w_1$=the, $w_2$=child, $w_3$=saw, $w_4$=the, $w_5$=cat).

**Lexical rules:**
$P(\text{D} \to \text{the}) = 1.0$, $P(\text{N} \to \text{child}) = 0.6$, $P(\text{N} \to \text{cat}) = 0.6$, $P(\text{V} \to \text{saw}) = 1.0$.

**Binary rules:**
$P(\text{NP} \to \text{D N}) = 0.8$, $P(\text{VP} \to \text{V NP}) = 1.0$, $P(\text{S} \to \text{NP VP}) = 1.0$.

(i) Initialize the length-1 chart cells. (2 marks)
(ii) Fill the cells needed to identify the best parse for the full sentence. (3 marks)
(iii) Reconstruct the best parse tree using back-pointers and report $P(T^*)$. (2 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(a) [7 marks]**

**Initialization (base case):** For each position $i$ from $1$ to $n$, for each non-terminal $A$ with a unit production $A \to w_i$:
$$\text{viterbi}[i,i,A] = P(A \to w_i), \quad \text{bp}[i,i,A] = (\text{lex}, w_i)$$
All other $\text{viterbi}[i,i,A] = 0$. (1 mark)

**Recurrence (inductive step):** For spans of length $L = 2, 3, \dots, n$, with $j = i + L - 1$:
$$\text{viterbi}[i,j,A] = \max_{k, B, C} \left[ P(A \to BC) \cdot \text{viterbi}[i,k,B] \cdot \text{viterbi}[k+1,j,C] \right]$$
The split point $k$ ranges over $i \le k < j$, and $(B, C)$ over pairs derivable from rule $A \to BC$. (2 marks)

**Back-pointer storage:** Whenever the max improves $\text{viterbi}[i,j,A]$, update:
$$\text{bp}[i,j,A] \leftarrow (k, B, C)$$
(1 mark)

**Termination:** If $\text{viterbi}[1, n, S] > 0$, the sentence is accepted and the best parse probability is $P(T^*) = \text{viterbi}[1, n, S]$. Otherwise, the sentence is rejected. (1 mark)

**Tree reconstruction:** Recursively expand from root $(1, n, S)$ using $\text{bp}$. (1 mark)

**Complexity:** Time $O(n^3 \cdot \vert N \vert^2 \cdot \vert R \vert)$, Space $O(n^2 \cdot \vert N \vert)$ for chart + back-pointers. (1 mark)

**(b) [7 marks]**

**(i) Length-1 initialization (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{viterbi}[1,1,\text{D}] &= 1.0 \quad (\text{the}) \\
\text{viterbi}[2,2,\text{N}] &= 0.6 \quad (\text{child}) \\
\text{viterbi}[3,3,\text{V}] &= 1.0 \quad (\text{saw}) \\
\text{viterbi}[4,4,\text{D}] &= 1.0 \quad (\text{the}) \\
\text{viterbi}[5,5,\text{N}] &= 0.6 \quad (\text{cat})
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Cells needed for full sentence (3 marks):**

Cell [1,2] "the child" — only $\text{NP} \to \text{D N}$ applies:
$$\text{viterbi}[1,2,\text{NP}] = 0.8 \times 1.0 \times 0.6 = 0.48, \quad \text{bp} = (1, \text{D}, \text{N})$$

Cell [4,5] "the cat":
$$\text{viterbi}[4,5,\text{NP}] = 0.8 \times 1.0 \times 0.6 = 0.48, \quad \text{bp} = (4, \text{D}, \text{N})$$

Cell [3,5] "saw the cat" — $\text{VP} \to \text{V NP}$ at $k=3$:
$$\text{viterbi}[3,5,\text{VP}] = 1.0 \times \text{viterbi}[3,3,\text{V}] \times \text{viterbi}[4,5,\text{NP}] = 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 0.48 = 0.48$$
$$\text{bp}[3,5,\text{VP}] = (3, \text{V}, \text{NP})$$

Cell [1,5] — $\text{S} \to \text{NP VP}$ at $k=2$:
$$\text{viterbi}[1,5,\text{S}] = 1.0 \times \text{viterbi}[1,2,\text{NP}] \times \text{viterbi}[3,5,\text{VP}] = 1.0 \times 0.48 \times 0.48 = 0.2304$$
$$\text{bp}[1,5,\text{S}] = (2, \text{NP}, \text{VP})$$

**(iii) Tree reconstruction (2 marks):**

Following back-pointers from $(1,5,S)$:
- Split at $k=2$ → Left NP at [1,2] and Right VP at [3,5].
- NP at [1,2] splits at $k=1$ → D(1,1)="the" + N(2,2)="child".
- VP at [3,5] splits at $k=3$ → V(3,3)="saw" + NP(4,5).
- NP at [4,5] splits at $k=4$ → D(4,4)="the" + N(5,5)="cat".

$$P(T^*) = 0.2304$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]*

**(a)** Define structural ambiguity in natural language. With a suitable example, illustrate how a PCFG assigns probabilities to disambiguate competing parses. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the Inside algorithm for computing the total probability of a sentence under a PCFG. Compare it with the Viterbi (most likely parse) algorithm, clearly highlighting (i) the operator used, (ii) the chart contents, (iii) whether back-pointers are stored, and (iv) the type of output produced. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) [7 marks]**

**Structural ambiguity** (1 mark) is the linguistic phenomenon in which a single sentence admits **two or more valid syntactic parse trees** under the same grammar. It arises from how constituents can be grouped or attached — for example, prepositional-phrase (PP) attachment, coordination scope, or noun-verb ambiguity.

**Classic example (1 mark):** *"I saw the man with the telescope."*

**Two competing parses (2 marks):**
1. **Instrument reading:** "with the telescope" attaches to the VP — I used the telescope to see the man.
2. **Modifier reading:** "with the telescope" attaches to the NP — I saw the man who was holding the telescope.

**PCFG disambiguation (3 marks):** The grammar encodes probabilities for attachment preferences learned from a treebank. Let:

$P(\text{VP} \to \text{V NP PP}) = 0.7$
$P(\text{NP} \to \text{NP PP}) = 0.3$

The instrument reading multiplies by $0.7$; the modifier reading by $0.3$. Hence, the PCFG selects the **instrument reading** as the most likely parse with higher probability. This demonstrates the **empirical power** of PCFGs in resolving real-world ambiguity. (3 marks)

**(b) [7 marks]**

**Inside algorithm definition (3 marks):** The Inside algorithm computes the *total* probability that a non-terminal $A$ derives the substring $w_i \dots w_j$ by summing over **all** possible sub-parses:

$$\beta[i,j,A] = \sum_{B,C} \sum_{k=i}^{j-1} P(A \to BC) \cdot \beta[i,k,B] \cdot \beta[k+1,j,C]$$

Base case: $\beta[i,i,A] = P(A \to w_i)$.

The total sentence probability is $P(S) = \beta[1,n,S]$.

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Inside Algorithm | Viterbi Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Operator | $\sum$ (sum) | $\max$ (maximum) |
| (ii) Chart contents | Total probabilities only | Best-probability + back-pointers |
| (iii) Back-pointers | Not stored | Stored for tree reconstruction |
| (iv) Output | $P(S)$ — one scalar | Best tree $T^*$ + $P(T^*)$ |

The two algorithms share the same chart-filling loop (same time complexity $O(n^3)$) but differ in their *aggregation semantics*. The Inside algorithm answers "How likely is this sentence?"; the Viterbi algorithm answers "What is the single best parse?". (1 mark)

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Parse tree probability** = product of all rule probabilities used in the tree.
- [ ] **Sentence probability** = sum over all valid parse trees of their individual probabilities.
- [ ] **Most likely parse** = argmax over parse trees; requires dynamic programming (Viterbi-CKY) due to exponential naive cost.
- [ ] **CKY requires CNF**: all rules must be of form $A \to BC$ or $A \to a$. Convert grammar first if needed.
- [ ] **Initialization**: length-1 cells use unit (lexical) rule probabilities.
- [ ] **Recurrence**: Viterbi uses $\max$ over splits and binary rules; Inside uses $\sum$.
- [ ] **Back-pointers** are mandatory for tree recovery — store $(k, B, C)$ whenever $\max$ is updated.
- [ ] **Time complexity** is $O(n^3 \cdot \vert N \vert^2 \cdot \vert R \vert)$ — polynomial and tractable.
- [ ] **Log-space trick**: replace products by sums of $\log$ probabilities to avoid floating-point underflow.
- [ ] **Structural ambiguity** is the motivating problem: PP-attachment, coordination scope, etc.
- [ ] **Unitarity condition**: $\sum_\beta P(A \to \beta) = 1$ must hold for every non-terminal $A$ in any valid PCFG.
- [ ] **Rule probability estimation** from a treebank: $P(A \to \beta) = \frac{\text{Count}(A \to \beta)}{\text{Count}(A)}$.
- [ ] **Modern extensions**: lexicalized PCFGs, latent annotations (Berkeley Parser), neural PCFGs — all inherit the CKY + Viterbi skeleton.
- [ ] **Exam tip**: Always state both the probability *and* the back-pointer when filling chart cells in a worked example.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parsing for Disambiguation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Parsing for Disambiguation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Parsing for Disambiguation** is the computational process of assigning the single most plausible (or all ranked) syntactic structures to a sentence that admits more than one valid parse tree under a given grammar, by leveraging probabilistic, lexical, statistical, or constraint-based disambiguation strategies.

In formal terms, given an input sentence $W = w_1 w_2 \dots w_n$ and a Context-Free Grammar (CFG) $G = (N, \Sigma, R, S)$, multiple parse trees $\mathcal{T}_1, \mathcal{T}_2, \dots, \mathcal{T}_k$ may be valid. Parsing for disambiguation aims to select:

$$\hat{T} = \arg\max_{T \in \mathcal{T}(G,W)} \; \text{Score}(T, W)$$

where $\text{Score}(T, W)$ is computed via probabilistic models (e.g., **PCFG**), lexical dependencies (e.g., **Lexicalized Parsers**), or discriminative rerankers.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> Think of **Parsing for Disambiguation** like solving a **multiple-choice crossword puzzle** where many words legally fit the grid, but only one word makes the whole sentence "click." A naive parser returns *every* legal crossword fill (all valid trees), but disambiguation acts like a **linguistic judge** who reads the entire sentence, checks word-substitution habits, and picks the fill that best matches the natural meaning. Just as a crossword expert looks at *across* clues for context, a disambiguating parser uses **lexical preferences**, **attachment frequencies**, and **semantic plausibility** to break ties.

### 1.3 Sources of Structural Ambiguity in Natural Language

| Ambiguity Type | Plain Description | Canonical Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Attachment Ambiguity** | Where does a modifier attach? | "I saw the man with the telescope." (PP *with the telescope* modifies *saw* or *man*?) |
| **Coordination Ambiguity** | Which conjuncts share scope? | "old men and women" (old applies to *men* only, or both?) |
| **NP/VP Bracketing Ambiguity** | How are phrases grouped? | "Russian history teacher" — history of Russia, or teacher of Russian history? |
| **Comma Ambiguity** | Restrictive vs. non-restrictive reading | "The student, who was late, complained." |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PEADT632):** The module emphasizes *Probabilistic Context-Free Grammars (PCFGs)*, *Lexicalized Parsing*, and *Statistical Reranking* as the three principal pillars of disambiguation in modern statistical parsing.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Disambiguation Pipeline — Six Logical Phases

1. **Lexical Tokenization & POS Tagging** — Each word $w_i$ receives a part-of-speech tag $t_i$. Disambiguation begins at the *lexical* level because many structural ambiguities cascade from ambiguous POS tags.
2. **Lexicon Lookup** — Words retrieve their probabilistic categories. Ambiguous words activate multiple grammar rules (e.g., *book* as noun or verb).
3. **CFG Skeleton Expansion** — The parser enumerates candidate parse trees consistent with the CFG. For an English sentence of length $n$, the count can grow as $\mathcal{O}(c^n)$ for a constant $c \geq 1$.
4. **Probabilistic Scoring (PCFG)** — Each tree is assigned a probability derived from rule frequencies in a treebank.
5. **Lexicalization & Head Propagation** — Each non-terminal inherits the *lexical head* from its children (Magerman 1995, Collins 1996), giving trees like $\text{NP(teacher)}$ vs. $\text{NP(history)}$ to distinguish preferences.
6. **Discriminative Reranking** — A second-stage classifier reranks the top-$k$ parses using global features (e.g., n-gram dependencies, non-local relations).

### 2.2 Why PCFGs Disambiguate

A **PCFG** is a 5-tuple $(N, \Sigma, R, S, P)$ where $P$ assigns a probability to each rule $r \in R$ such that:

$$\forall A \in N: \quad \sum_{(A \to \alpha) \in R} P(A \to \alpha) = 1$$

The probability of a complete tree $T$ factorises as the product of all its rule probabilities:

$$P(T) = \prod_{r_i \in T} P(r_i)$$

The *Why*: Because every sentence-tree pair $(W, T)$ is decomposed into independent local decisions, the model can **score trees without enumerating semantics**, simply by multiplying grammar-rule frequencies observed in annotated corpora (e.g., the **Penn Treebank**).

### 2.3 The *How* — Algorithm Family Tree

- **Chart Parsers** (CYK, Earley) — Dynamic programming stores sub-parses in a chart to avoid re-computation.
- **Probabilistic CKY** — Bottom-up chart; each cell stores the *best* (or *Viterbi*) score.
- **Inside-Outside Algorithm** — Estimates PCFG parameters via EM.
- **Lexicalized Models** (Collins 1997, Charniak 2000) — Condition rule probabilities on lexical head-word pairs $h$.
- **Discriminative Rerankers** (Charniak & Johnson 2005) — Use arbitrary global features over $n$-best parse lists.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Box — Memorize for ESE**

| # | Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Boundary / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P(T) = \prod_{r \in T} P(r)$ | Tree probability under PCFG | Strict product over all production rules |
| 2 | $P(W) = \sum_{T \in \mathcal{T}(W)} P(T,W)$ | Marginal likelihood of a string | Computed via Inside algorithm |
| 3 | $P(A \to BC) = \dfrac{C(A \to BC)}{C(A)}$ | MLE estimate of rule probability | $C(\cdot)$ = treebank count |
| 4 | $\alpha(i,j,A) = P(A \Rightarrow^* w_i \dots w_j)$ | **Inside probability** | Computed bottom-up, complexity $\mathcal{O}(n^3 \vert R \vert)$ |
| 5 | $\beta(i,j,A) = P(S \Rightarrow^* w_1 \dots w_{i-1} A w_{j+1} \dots w_n)$ | **Outside probability** | Computed top-down |
| 6 | $\hat{T} = \arg\max_T P(T \mid W)$ | Best parse (Viterbi) | Computed via max-product over chart |
| 7 | $P(h \mid P, c)$ | Collins Model 1 head probability | $h$ = head word, $P$ = parent, $c$ = direction (left/right) |
| 8 | $\text{perplexity} = 2^{H(P)}$ | Branching entropy of grammar | Lower is more deterministic |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** PCFG-based disambiguation underpins *machine translation alignment*, *information extraction pipelines*, *voice-assistant intent parsing*, and *grammar checkers* in tools like Grammarly and LanguageTool.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Viterbi CKY Parsing on a Toy PCFG

**Setup.** Consider the lexicon and grammar below, trained on a tiny treebank.

**Lexicon (POS → words):**
- Det → {the}
- N → {man, telescope, saw}
- V → {saw}
- P → {with}

**Probabilistic Rules (with MLE counts):**
- S → NP VP: count 8/10 ⇒ $P = 0.8$
- S → VP: count 2/10 ⇒ $P = 0.2$
- NP → Det N: count 6/10 ⇒ $P = 0.6$
- NP → NP PP: count 4/10 ⇒ $P = 0.4$
- VP → V NP: count 3/5 ⇒ $P = 0.6$
- VP → VP PP: count 2/5 ⇒ $P = 0.4$
- PP → P NP: count 1.0
- Det → the: count 1.0
- N → man: count 0.30
- N → telescope: count 0.30
- N → saw: count 0.40
- V → saw: count 1.0
- P → with: count 1.0

**Sentence:** $W$ = "saw the man with the telescope"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic step by step** — we use the **CKY algorithm** in CNF (Chomsky Normal Form), filling a triangular chart $\text{cell}(i,j)$ with the Viterbi score of the best sub-tree spanning $w_i \dots w_j$.

#### Step 1: Lexical (Terminal) Cells

For each word position $i$, populate $\text{cell}(i,i+1)$ with the lexically-tagged probabilities.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{cell}(1,2) &: V \to \text{saw} = 1.0,\quad N \to \text{saw} = 0.40 \\
\text{cell}(2,3) &: Det \to \text{the} = 1.0 \\
\text{cell}(3,4) &: N \to \text{man} = 0.30 \\
\text{cell}(4,5) &: P \to \text{with} = 1.0 \\
\text{cell}(5,6) &: Det \to \text{the} = 1.0 \\
\text{cell}(6,7) &: N \to \text{telescope} = 0.30
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Explanation:** Each diagonal cell records the probability that the word at that position instantiates that POS tag, taken directly from the lexicon MLEs.

#### Step 2: Length-2 Spans (NP, VP, PP discovery)

For each adjacent pair $(i,i+2)$ apply binary rules $A \to BC$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{cell}(1,3) : \;& VP \to V\; NP : 0.6 \times 1.0 \times 0.6 = 0.360 \quad [\text{Det N cell 2,3 stays 0.6}] \\
\text{cell}(3,5) : \;& NP \to Det\; N : 0.6 \times 1.0 \times 0.30 = 0.180 \\
\text{cell}(4,6) : \;& NP \to Det\; N : 0.6 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 = 0.600 \quad [\text{the telescope}] \\
\text{cell}(5,7) : \;& NP \to Det\; N : 0.6 \times 1.0 \times 0.30 = 0.180
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Explanation:** The Viterbi recurrence is $\text{score}(i,j,A) = \max_{A \to BC,\,k} \; P(A \to BC) \cdot \text{score}(i,k,B) \cdot \text{score}(k,j,C)$. We tried every split $k$ and picked the maximum.

#### Step 3: Length-3 Spans (Disambiguation of PP-attachment)

At $(1,4)$ — span "saw the man":

$$
\begin{aligned}
VP \to V\; NP :\;& 0.6 \times 1.0 \times 0.180 = 0.108 \\
S  \to NP\; VP :\;& \text{requires NP at }(1,2)\text{ — absent. No path.}
\end{aligned}
$$

At $(4,7)$ — span "with the telescope":

$$
\begin{aligned}
PP \to P\; NP :\;& 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 0.180 = 0.180 \\
NP \to NP\; PP :\;& \text{requires NP at }(4,5)\text{ — absent.}
\end{aligned}
$$

At $(3,7)$ — span "man with the telescope" (NP with PP):

$$
\begin{aligned}
NP \to NP\; PP :\;& 0.4 \times 0.180 \times 0.180 = 0.01296 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4: Final Cell $(1,7)$ — Two Competing Trees

**Tree T1 (PP attaches to NP — *man with the telescope*):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S \to NP\; VP \;&:\; 0.8 \times 0.01296 \times 0.108 = 0.001120
\end{aligned}
$$

**Tree T2 (PP attaches to VP — *saw with the telescope*):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S \to NP\; VP \;&:\; 0.8 \times 0.180 \times \big[0.4 \times 0.108 \times 0.180\big] \\
&:\; 0.8 \times 0.180 \times 0.007776 = 0.001120 \quad \text{(approx.)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Comparison result (with the simplified corpus):** Both attachments produce comparable Viterbi scores, **demonstrating exactly the structural ambiguity problem**. In a real treebank, the **PP-VP attachment** would typically receive a higher conditional probability $P(\text{PP} \mid \text{VP, with})$ because instrument-PPs strongly favour VP attachment.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Inside-Outside Re-estimation

The **Inside probability** $\alpha(i,j,A)$ is computed bottom-up by dynamic programming:

$$
\alpha(i,j,A) = \sum_{A \to BC} \; \sum_{k=i+1}^{j-1} \; P(A \to BC) \cdot \alpha(i,k,B) \cdot \alpha(k,j,C)
$$

with base case $\alpha(i,i+1,A) = P(A \to w_i)$.

The **Outside probability** $\beta(i,j,A)$ is computed top-down:

$$
\beta(i,j,A) = \sum_{B \to AC} P(B \to AC)\beta(i,k,B)\alpha(j,k,C) + \sum_{B \to CA} P(B \to CA)\alpha(k,i,C)\beta(j,k,B)
$$

**Re-estimation of rule probabilities (EM-M step):**

$$
\hat{P}(A \to BC) = \frac{\text{Expected count of rule } A \to BC}{\text{Expected count of } A} = \frac{\sum_{i,k,j} \frac{\beta(i,j,A)\;P(A \to BC)\;\alpha(i,k,B)\;\alpha(k,j,C)}{P(W)}}{\sum_{i,j}\beta(i,j,A)\,\alpha(i,j,A)\,/\,P(W)}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The numerator accumulates *fractional* rule uses across all derivations consistent with $W$, and the denominator normalises by the total fractional time the parent $A$ spans a sub-constituent. The result is the *Maximum-Likelihood Estimate* under the E-step's posterior.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Disambiguation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[RAW SENTENCE INPUT] --> B0[TOKENIZER]
    B0 --> C0[POS TAGGER HMM OR CRF]
    C0 --> D0[LEXICAL LOOKUP MULTIPLE RULES]
    D0 --> E0[CFG SKELETON GENERATOR]
    E0 --> F0[CHART BUILDER CKY OR EARLEY]
    F0 --> G0[PCFG SCORING MODULE]
    G0 --> H0[LEXICALIZED HEAD PROPAGATION]
    H0 --> I0[N BEST LIST OF TREES]
    I0 --> J0[DISCRIMINATIVE RERANKER FEATURES]
    J0 --> K0[BEST PARSE TREE OUTPUT]
    K0 --> L0[DOWNSTREAM SEMANTIC PARSER]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each block represents a processing stage. The `CHART BUILDER` enumerates all candidate parses; the `PCFG SCORING MODULE` assigns probabilities; the `LEXICALIZED HEAD PROPAGATION` injects word-specific preferences (defeating PP-attachment ties); the `DISCRIMINATIVE RERANKER` re-orders the n-best list using global features such as sibling n-gram dependencies.

### 4.2 PCFG Rule Probability Computation — Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M0[TREEBANK CORPUS] --> M1[RULE EXTRACTION COUNTER]
    M1 --> M2[PARENT COUNT C OF A]
    M1 --> M3[CHILD COUNT C OF A RIGHT ARROW BC]
    M2 --> M4[DIVISION OPERATION]
    M3 --> M4
    M4 --> M5[STORED PROBABILITIES TABLE]
    M5 --> M6[VITERBI CKY PARSER]
```

> **Explanation:** The Penn Treebank provides millions of annotated parse trees. From these, the `RULE EXTRACTION COUNTER` tallies how often each production $A \to BC$ appears and how often $A$ appears in any rule. Dividing gives the MLE probability which is then loaded into the parser's rule table.

### 4.3 Lexicalization Walk-Through

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X1[NP DET ADJECTIVES NOUN] --> X2[HEAD FINDER RULES COLLINS 1996]
    X2 --> X3[NP HEAD NOUN WORD]
    X3 --> X4[NP NOUN ANNOTATED AS HISTORY]
    X3 --> X5[NP NOUN ANNOTATED AS TEACHER]
    X4 --> X6[LEXICALIZED PCFG RULES]
    X5 --> X6
    X6 --> X7[DISAMBIGUATION BY HEAD PAIR FREQUENCY]
```

> **Explanation:** Each NP is *annotated* with its head word, transforming rule `NP → Det Adj N` into many lexicalized micro-rules such as `NP(history) → Det Adj N(history)` and `NP(teacher) → Det Adj N(teacher)`. The model can now distinguish a *history teacher* (teacher who teaches history) from a *Russian history* teacher (teacher of Russian history) because the head-pair `(history, teacher)` has a different empirical frequency than `(russian, history)`.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]**
*Define **Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar (PCFG)**. Mention any two limitations of vanilla PCFGs in handling structural disambiguation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **PCFG** is a CFG augmented with a probability distribution $P$ over its production rules such that for every non-terminal $A \in N$:

$$\sum_{\alpha} P(A \to \alpha) = 1$$

The probability of a complete parse tree $T$ is the product $\prod_{r \in T} P(r)$. **Limitations:** (i) It fails to capture **lexical dependencies** — the same rule `VP → V NP` is scored identically regardless of whether the verb is *ate* or *believes*; (ii) It cannot model **structural preferences** between siblings, so attachment ambiguities are often broken arbitrarily without contextual word cues. These limitations motivated **Lexicalized PCFGs** and **Discriminative Rerankers**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
*Differentiate between **attachment ambiguity** and **coordination ambiguity** with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Attachment Ambiguity:** Arises when a phrase (typically a PP or adverbial) can syntactically attach to more than one position in the parse tree. *Example:* "I shot the elephant with the gun" — the PP *with the gun* can attach to the verb *shot* (instrument reading) or to the NP *the elephant* (modifier reading).
- **Coordination Ambiguity:** Arises when conjunctions (and, or) create uncertainty about the scope of coordinated phrases. *Example:* "old men and women" — *old* may coordinate with *men* only, or with the conjoined NP *men and women* (yielding old women as well).

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT** confuse *Viterbi decoding* with *posterior decoding* (Inside algorithm). Viterbi picks the single best parse; Inside computes the total probability mass.
> - **Do NOT** state the CKY algorithm requires grammar in CNF without mentioning the conversion step (e.g., binarisation of unit productions).
> - **Lexicalized parsers** must mention **head-percolation rules** explicitly to earn full marks.
> - For derivations, examiners often deduct marks for **missing intermediate steps** — always show the full chart cell computation.

---

#### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **Probabilistic CKY algorithm** for parsing. How does it disambiguate between competing parse trees for the sentence "Book the flight through Houston"? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

The **CKY (Cocke-Kasami-Younger) algorithm** is a bottom-up dynamic-programming chart parser that runs in $\mathcal{O}(n^3 \vert R \vert)$ on a CNF grammar. The probabilistic version maintains for each cell $(i,j)$ a *Viterbi score*:

$$
\text{Viterbi}(i,j,A) = \max_{A \to BC,\, k} \; P(A \to BC) \cdot \text{Viterbi}(i,k,B) \cdot \text{Viterbi}(k,j,C)
$$

**Disambiguation application to "Book the flight through Houston":**

1. **Lexical tagging ambiguity:** *Book* is tagged both Noun and Verb. The CKY chart stores both candidates with their respective MLEs, e.g., $P(N \to \text{book})=0.0001$ vs. $P(V \to \text{book})=0.02$.
2. **PP-attachment ambiguity:** *through Houston* can attach to NP (*the flight through Houston*) or VP (*book through Houston*).
3. **Viterbi selection:** The algorithm picks the split that maximises the product of rule probabilities, automatically favouring the reading with the highest rule-probability product. In treebank statistics, **PP → P NP** attaching to **NP** is typically the dominant reading for a *flight through Houston*.
4. **Final parse:** The cell $(1,5, S)$ retains the maximum over both candidate trees, breaking the tie in favour of the higher-probability derivation.

**(b)** Describe the **Inside-Outside algorithm** for unsupervised PCFG parameter estimation. State the re-estimation formula and outline one iteration. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

The **Inside-Outside algorithm** is the **EM algorithm specialised to PCFGs**, used when no annotated treebank is available. It alternates two steps:

**E-step (Expectation):** Compute the expected counts of each rule given the current parameters.

*Inside pass* (bottom-up, $\mathcal{O}(n^3 \vert R \vert)$):

$$
\alpha(i,j,A) = \sum_{A \to BC} \sum_{k=i+1}^{j-1} P(A \to BC)\,\alpha(i,k,B)\,\alpha(k,j,C)
$$

*Outside pass* (top-down):

$$
\beta(i,j,A) = \sum_{B \to AC,\, k} P(B \to AC)\beta(i,k,B)\alpha(j,k,C) + \sum_{B \to CA,\, k} P(B \to CA)\alpha(k,i,C)\beta(j,k,B)
$$

**M-step (Maximisation):** Re-estimate rule probabilities:

$$
\hat{P}(A \to BC) = \frac{\sum_{i,j,k} \frac{\beta(i,j,A)\,P(A \to BC)\,\alpha(i,k,B)\,\alpha(k,j,C)}{P(W)}}{\sum_{i,j} \frac{\beta(i,j,A)\,\alpha(i,j,A)}{P(W)}}
$$

**One iteration outline:** (i) Initialise $P$ uniformly (or with random small values). (ii) Run Inside to fill $\alpha$. (iii) Run Outside to fill $\beta$. (iv) Accumulate fractional rule counts. (v) Re-normalise. (vi) Repeat until log-likelihood $\log P(W)$ converges. Disambiguation emerges because rules consistent with the observed corpus accumulate higher probabilities across iterations.

---

#### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**

**(a)** Discuss the role of **lexicalized parsing** in disambiguation. Explain the **head-percolation rules** used in the Collins parser with examples. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Role in disambiguation:** Vanilla PCFGs assign identical scores to all parses differing only in lexical content. Lexicalized parsers (Magerman 1995; Collins 1996, 1997) attach the *head word* of each constituent to its non-terminal label, yielding annotated categories like $\text{NP}_{\text{teacher}}$, $\text{VP}_{\text{saw}}$. This lets the model capture **lexicalized dependencies** such as the strong preference for the verb *saw* to take NP-complements rather than PP-complements, breaking PP-attachment ties.

**Head-percolation rules (Collins 1996 example):**

| Rule | Head Selection |
|---|---|
| S → NP VP | Head = head(VP) |
| VP → V NP | Head = head(V) |
| VP → VP PP | Head = head(VP) |
| NP → Det N | Head = head(N) |
| NP → NP PP | Head = head(NP) |
| PP → P NP | Head = head(NP) |

**Example application:** In *"Russian history teacher"*, the head-percolation table identifies *teacher* as the head of the outer NP and *history* as the head of the inner NP. The lexicalized model then scores the configuration $\text{NP}(\text{teacher}) \to \text{NP}(\text{history})\; \text{NP}(\text{teacher})$ by conditioning on the head pair `(history, teacher)`, which has a higher empirical frequency than `(russian, history)` for an attributive-noun reading.

**(b)** Explain **discriminative reranking** for n-best parse lists. What global features does the reranker use that the base parser cannot? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Reranking paradigm (Charniak & Johnson 2005):** A first-stage PCFG/Lexicalized parser produces an **n-best list** of the top 50–1000 candidate parses. A second-stage **discriminative classifier** (e.g., a MaxEnt or perceptron model) then re-orders this list using *rich global features* that would be intractable to incorporate into the base parser's dynamic program.

**Global features used by the reranker:**

1. **Sibling n-gram statistics** — frequencies of consecutive sibling-POS sequences anywhere in the tree.
2. **Long-distance head-modifier dependencies** — pairs of words separated by intervening material.
3. **Right-branching bias indicators** — count of right-branching vs. left-branching structures.
4. **Tree-shape features** — depth, number of NP-branching points, unary-chain length.
5. **Lexicalized bigram head pairs** — $(h_{\text{parent}}, h_{\text{child}})$ from non-adjacent subtrees.

**Why the base parser cannot use these:** The base parser's dynamic-programming decomposition assumes *context-free* independence among sub-constituents. A feature like "rightmost-child's POS of NP depends on NP's head word three levels up" violates the independence assumption and would force the parser out of polynomial time. Reranking sidesteps this by *scoring*, not *enumerating*, the candidate trees.

---

### 5.7 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Parsing for Disambiguation**

- **PCFG probability = product of rule probabilities** of a complete tree.
- **CKY algorithm** requires grammar in **Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)**; runs in $\mathcal{O}(n^3 \vert R \vert)$.
- **Viterbi decoding** picks the single best parse; **Inside** computes total probability of a string.
- **Inside-Outside** is the EM algorithm for PCFGs; iteratively refines rule probabilities without a treebank.
- **Lexicalization** (Magerman, Collins) attaches the *head word* to each non-terminal, enabling word-specific preferences.
- **Head-percolation rules** are deterministic tables that pick the head of a phrase from among its children.
- **Discriminative reranking** uses a second-stage classifier on n-best lists with global, non-local features.
- **Structural ambiguity** sources: *attachment*, *coordination*, *NP/VP bracketing*, *comma scope*.
- **Lexicalized PCFGs** overcome the *independence assumption failure* of vanilla PCFGs.
- **Penn Treebank** is the canonical training corpus; contains ~40,000 annotated English sentences.
- **Branch entropy / perplexity** measures grammar determinism — lower means easier disambiguation.
- **CYK restriction** — all rules of form $A \to BC$ or $A \to a$; binarisation may be required for original CFG.
- **Beam search** is often used in practice to prune the chart for long sentences.
- **Rerankers typically add 0.5–2.0% F1 improvement** on top of strong lexicalized parsers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parsing Model versus Language Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT632 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Parsing Model versus Language Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Language Model (LM)

A **Language Model** is a probability distribution over sequences of words (or tokens, sub-words, characters) drawn from a vocabulary $V$. Formally, given a sentence (word sequence) $W = w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n$, the language model assigns a probability:

$$P(W) = P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n)$$

The model answers the question: *"How likely is this sentence to occur in natural language?"*

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Language Model)**
> A statistical or neural system that estimates the joint probability of word sequences, enabling tasks such as next-word prediction, text generation, speech recognition, and machine translation.

### 1.1.1 Intuitive Analogy — Language Model

Imagine a child who has listened to **millions of sentences** being spoken aloud (from radio, television, books, classroom). When asked to complete the sentence:
*"The cat sat on the ..."*
the child immediately suggests *"mat"*, *"sofa"*, *"roof"* — and **almost certainly never** suggests *"universe of integer partitions"*.

The child has internalized a **probability distribution over word sequences**. That internal distribution *is* a language model. The model does not care about grammar rules — it just knows, statistically, which word sequences feel "natural."

In engineering terms, every autocomplete on your smartphone, every predictive text suggestion, every chatbot reply is powered by a language model.

## 1.2 Parsing Model (PM)

A **Parsing Model** is a system that assigns a **syntactic structure** (constituency tree, dependency tree, or graph) to a given input sentence. The model answers a fundamentally different question: *"What is the grammatical structure of this sentence?"*

Formal output:
- **Constituency parsing**: a phrase-structure tree $T$ governed by a Context-Free Grammar (CFG).
- **Dependency parsing**: a set of head-dependent arcs $\{(h, d, \text{rel})\}$ connecting words.

The parsing model selects the structure that best explains the sentence, often by maximizing:

$$\hat{T} = \arg\max_{T \in \mathcal{T}(S)} \; \text{Score}(T, S)$$

where $\mathcal{T}(S)$ is the set of all valid parses for sentence $S$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Parsing Model)**
> A computational model (probabilistic, neural, or rule-based) that maps a surface sentence to an explicit syntactic representation — typically a constituency tree (CFG / PCFG) or a dependency graph — exposing NP, VP, PP, S and other grammatical constituents and their head-dependent relationships.

### 1.2.1 Intuitive Analogy — Parsing Model

Picture an English schoolteacher diagramming a sentence on the blackboard:

```
[The cat]NP  [sat [on [the mat]NP]PP]VP
```

She is **not** judging how *probable* the sentence is. She is identifying the **grammatical role of each word** — who is the subject, what is the verb, which prepositional phrase modifies what, etc.

That act of structural decomposition — exposing the *hidden skeleton* of a sentence — is exactly what a parsing model performs, only at machine scale over millions of sentences.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Exams**
> - **L**anguage Model → predicts **L**ikelihood of word sequences.
> - **P**arsing Model → exposes **P**hrase-structure / dependencies.

## 1.3 Why Compare Them?

Both models consume the same input — a raw sentence — but they emit radically different outputs:

| Aspect | Language Model | Parsing Model |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Output | Probability $P(W)$ | Syntactic tree / graph |
| Captures | Fluency, co-occurrence | Grammar, structure |
| Granularity | Sequential / token-level | Hierarchical / structural |
| Typical training | Unlabeled text (self-supervised) | Annotated treebanks |
| Example | GPT, BERT (causal/masked LM) | PCFG parser, Stanford Dependency Parser |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake**
> Students often claim "BERT is a parser" or "GPT understands grammar." BERT and GPT are **language models**. A separate parsing head, fine-tuned on a treebank, is needed to obtain explicit syntactic trees.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Language Model — Operational Theory

A language model factorizes the joint probability using the **chain rule of probability**:

$$P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_1, w_2, \dots, w_{i-1})$$
